topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Chemical Engineering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🏭 Introduction to Chemical Engineering

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Chemical Engineering** is the branch of engineering that applies the principles of **physics**, **chemistry**, **mathematics**, **biology**, and **economics** to design, develop, produce, and use chemicals, materials, energy, and processes in a safe, economical, and environmentally sustainable manner. It transforms raw materials into useful products through well-defined physical and chemical changes at an industrial scale.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, **Process Calculations (PCCHT205)** forms the foundational bedrock upon which all subsequent chemical engineering courses (Mass Transfer, Heat Transfer, Reaction Engineering, Process Control) are built. It teaches the student to perform **material and energy balances** on chemical process systems — the very first step before any design, control, or optimization can be attempted.

### 🧠 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are baking a cake at home. You follow a *recipe* — 200 g flour, 100 g sugar, 2 eggs, 50 mL milk. The recipe is essentially a **material balance**: the total mass that enters (ingredients) must equal the total mass that leaves (cake + steam + any residue). The oven temperature and baking time form a simple **energy balance**.

Now scale this up by a factor of **10,000,000**: a petroleum refinery processes **500,000 barrels of crude oil per day** through distillation, cracking, and reforming units to produce gasoline, diesel, kerosene, and LPG. A **chemical engineer** is the person who designs the *recipe* (the process), the *kitchen* (the plant), and ensures everything runs **safely, economically, and continuously** without the cake burning or the kitchen catching fire.

| Term | Home Kitchen | Chemical Plant |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Recipe | Cooking instructions | **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** |
| Ingredients | Raw materials (flour, sugar) | **Feed streams** (crude oil, natural gas) |
| Final dish | Cake | **Products** (gasoline, polymers, fertilizers) |
| Oven | Cooking appliance | **Reactor, Distillation Column, Heat Exchanger** |
| Leftovers / smoke | Waste / steam | **By-products / emissions** |
| You | Cook | **Chemical Engineer** |

> [!NOTE] **Why This Matters for PCCHT205**
> Every calculation you will perform in this course — whether it is a simple unit conversion, a material balance on a mixer, or an energy balance on a reactor — is the *engineering translation* of the kitchen recipe into rigorous mathematical language. **There are no hidden assumptions in the universe**: matter is conserved, energy is conserved, and these two truths govern every process.

---

## 1.2 Historical Evolution of Chemical Engineering

The discipline of Chemical Engineering was **not** born fully formed. It evolved through three distinct philosophical eras, each marking a deeper level of abstraction and rigor.

### Era 1 — The Industrial Chemistry Period (Pre-1900)

Early chemical manufacturing (soap, sulfuric acid, dyes, gunpowder) was carried out by **industrial chemists** who relied heavily on empirical rules and trial-and-error. There was no formal engineering methodology. The focus was on *what* reaction to perform, with little regard for *how* to perform it efficiently at scale.

### Era 2 — The Unit Operations Revolution (1900 – 1960)

In **1915**, the American chemist **Arthur Amos Noyes** formally proposed the revolutionary idea that *every* chemical process, regardless of the industry, can be broken down into a sequence of discrete physical operations — **distillation, drying, filtration, evaporation, crystallization, mixing, heat exchange**, and so on. His student, **Walker, Lewis, and McAdams**, codified this in the landmark textbook *Principles of Chemical Engineering* (1923).

> [!IMPORTANT] **The Unit Operations Concept**
> A **Unit Operation** is a physical change (no chemical reaction involved) applied to a material stream to transform it from one state to another. Common examples include:
> - **Distillation** (separation by boiling point difference)
> - **Absorption** (gas scrubbing by a liquid)
> - **Filtration** (solid-liquid separation)
> - **Drying** (moisture removal)
> - **Crystallization** (solid formation from solution)
> - **Heat Exchange** (energy transfer between streams)
>
> A **Unit Process** is a chemical change — a reaction — such as oxidation, reduction, polymerization, nitration, sulfonation, or fermentation.

### Era 3 — Transport Phenomena & Modern Chemical Engineering (1960 – Present)

In the 1960s, **R. Byron Bird, Warren E. Stewart, and Edwin N. Lightfoot** published *Transport Phenomena* (1960), which unified **momentum, heat, and mass transfer** under a common mathematical framework of differential equations. This gave chemical engineers the ability to derive *fundamental* design equations from first principles rather than relying solely on empirical correlations. Modern chemical engineering also embraces **process safety**, **process control**, **computational modeling**, **sustainability**, and **biochemical engineering**.

### Timeline Summary

| Year | Milestone | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1824 | First Chemical Engineering course, **Polytechnique, Paris** | Birth of formal education |
| 1888 | First ChemE dept., **MIT, USA** | Institutionalization |
| 1915 | Noyes proposes **Unit Operations** | Conceptual foundation |
| 1923 | *Principles of Chemical Engineering* published | First textbook |
| 1960 | Bird, Stewart, Lightfoot — *Transport Phenomena* | First-principles design |
| 1990s | **Computer-Aided Process Engineering (CAPE)** | Digital revolution |
| 2020s | AI, sustainability, circular economy | Current frontiers |

---

## 1.3 Scope of Chemical Engineering

The scope of chemical engineering is far broader than its name suggests. Less than **5% of chemical engineers** actually work in *traditional* chemical plants. The breadth of application includes:

- **Petroleum Refining & Petrochemicals** — crude oil to fuels and plastics
- **Pharmaceuticals & Biotechnology** — drug formulation, fermentation, vaccines
- **Food Processing** — dairy, brewing, edible oils, packaged foods
- **Environmental Engineering** — wastewater treatment, air pollution control, carbon capture
- **Energy** — batteries, fuel cells, solar cells, hydrogen economy
- **Materials Science** — polymers, ceramics, composites, nanomaterials
- **Electronics** — semiconductor processing, ultra-pure chemicals
- **Nuclear Engineering** — fuel processing, radioactive waste handling
- **Pulp & Paper, Textiles, Paints, Adhesives**
- **Consulting, Banking, Patent Law, Management** — non-traditional roles

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📐 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemical Engineering Approach to Problem Solving

A chemical engineer tackles any problem — whether it is designing a new plant, troubleshooting an existing unit, or scaling up a lab experiment — by following a systematic **seven-step methodology** (sometimes called the *McCabe-Thiele-Harriott* approach, though the framework is universal):

### Step-by-Step Logical Framework

- **Step 1 — Draw a Flowchart / Block Diagram** of the process. Mark every input stream, output stream, and unit operation. Use a labeled rectangle for each unit, and labeled arrows for each stream. This single drawing clarifies 80% of the problem.

- **Step 2 — Choose a Basis of Calculation.** Select a convenient reference quantity — usually **100 kg of feed**, **1000 mol of product**, **1 hour of operation**, or **1 kmol of a key component** — and express all other quantities relative to this basis.

- **Step 3 — Label All Streams.** Assign known compositions, flow rates, temperatures, and pressures to every stream. Unknowns are clearly marked with symbols ($F$, $P$, $W$, $x_i$, $y_j$, etc.).

- **Step 4 — Write the Material Balance Equations.** Apply the **Law of Conservation of Mass** to each unit and to the entire process. The governing equation is:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} \pm \text{Generation / Consumption}$$

For a **steady-state** (no accumulation) process with **no chemical reaction**:

$$\text{Input} = \text{Output}$$

- **Step 5 — Write the Energy Balance Equations (if required).** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to compute heating, cooling, work, or heat of reaction requirements. For a steady-state process:

$$\Delta H = Q - W_s$$

where $\Delta H$ is the enthalpy change of the streams, $Q$ is the heat added, and $W_s$ is the shaft work done by the system.

- **Step 6 — Solve the System of Equations.** Use algebra, simultaneous equations, or matrix methods to find the unknowns. Always check that the **number of independent equations equals the number of unknowns** — a critical KTU examination pitfall.

- **Step 7 — Verify and Interpret the Results.** Perform dimensional checks, sanity checks, and confirm physical plausibility. A negative flow rate or a composition greater than 1.0 indicates an error in formulation.

> [!NOTE] **Why This Methodology is Universal**
> This seven-step framework is identical in spirit whether you are balancing a CSTR, designing a distillation column, or sizing a wastewater treatment plant. The KTU examiner will often award **partial credit** if your flowchart and basis of calculation are clearly stated, even if the final algebra has an arithmetic slip.

---

## 2.2 The Role of a Chemical Engineer

A chemical engineer acts as the **bridge between the laboratory chemist (who discovers reactions) and the mechanical engineer (who builds equipment)**. The core competencies include:

- **Process Design** — selecting the sequence of unit operations, equipment, and operating conditions
- **Process Simulation** — using software like Aspen Plus, HYSYS, DWSIM, or CHEMCAD to model flowsheets
- **Process Economics** — capital cost, operating cost, payback period, net present value (NPV)
- **Process Safety** — HAZOP studies, relief valve sizing, runaway reaction analysis
- **Process Control** — PID controllers, feedback loops, model predictive control
- **Plant Operations & Troubleshooting** — daily running of chemical plants
- **R&D and Scale-up** — taking a lab discovery to industrial production

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Tip**
> When asked *"What is the role of a chemical engineer?"* in a 3-mark KTU question, always list **at least 5 distinct roles** with one-line descriptions. Avoid vague generalities like *"designs plants"* — specify *what kind* of design, *what* economic analysis, *what* safety methodology.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Foundational Equations

The following table consolidates every foundational equation a KTU student must internalize before proceeding to material and energy balance problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | When to Use |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Conservation of Mass (Steady State)** | $\displaystyle\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$ | $F$ = mass flow rate | kg/h or kmol/h | All material balance problems |
| 2 | **Conservation of Mass (Unsteady State)** | $\displaystyle\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ | $m$ = mass holdup | kg | Tank filling/draining |
| 3 | **Component Mass Balance** | $\displaystyle\sum F_{\text{in}} x_{i,\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}} x_{i,\text{out}}$ | $x_i$ = mass or mole fraction | dimensionless | Multi-component systems |
| 4 | **Mole Fraction Relation** | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = 1.0$ | $x_i$ = mole fractions | dimensionless | Composition closure |
| 5 | **Density Definition** | $\displaystyle\rho = \frac{m}{V}$ | $\rho$ = density, $m$ = mass, $V$ = volume | kg/m³ | Volume ↔ mass conversion |
| 6 | **Specific Gravity** | $\displaystyle SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{substance}}}{\rho_{\text{water @ 4°C}}}$ | — | dimensionless | Density expressed relative to water |
| 7 | **Volumetric Flow Rate** | $\displaystyle\dot{V} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho}$ | $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow rate | m³/s or L/min | Converting mass to volume flow |
| 8 | **Ideal Gas Law** | $\displaystyle PV = nRT$ | $P$ = pressure, $V$ = volume, $n$ = moles, $T$ = absolute temperature | Pa, m³, mol, K | Gaseous systems |
| 9 | **Universal Gas Constant** | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | — | — | SI calculations |
| 10 | **Mole-Mass Conversion** | $\displaystyle m = n \cdot M$ | $M$ = molar mass | kg/kmol or g/mol | Moles ↔ mass |
| 11 | **Average Molecular Weight of Mixture** | $\displaystyle M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i} y_i \, M_i$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction | kg/kmol | Gas mixture properties |
| 12 | **Mass Fraction to Mole Fraction** | $\displaystyle y_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_{j} w_j / M_j}$ | $w_i$ = mass fraction | — | Composition conversion |
| 13 | **Energy Balance (Steady State)** | $\displaystyle\sum H_{\text{in}} = \sum H_{\text{out}} + Q$ | $H$ = enthalpy, $Q$ = heat | kW or kJ/h | Energy balance problems |
| 14 | **First Law of Thermodynamics** | $\displaystyle\Delta U = Q - W$ | $\Delta U$ = internal energy change, $W$ = work | J or kJ | Closed-system energy analysis |
| 15 | **STP Conditions** | $T = 273.15$ K, $P = 101.325$ kPa | — | — | Standard reference for gas calculations |
| 16 | **Dimensional Consistency Rule** | All terms in any equation must have **identical units** | — | — | Every problem, always |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical KTU Pitfall**
> The most common mark-losing error in KTU examinations is mixing **mass basis** and **mole basis** in the same balance. Choose one basis (preferably **mass**, since most raw materials are weighed in mass), express all compositions in **mass fractions**, and apply the mass balance equation $\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$ with full consistency. If the problem gives data in **mole %**, convert to **mass %** *once, upfront*, and then proceed entirely on a mass basis.

---

## 2.4 Units, Dimensions, and the SI System

### The Seven SI Base Units

| Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Length | meter | m |
| Mass | kilogram | kg |
| Time | second | s |
| Temperature | kelvin | K |
| Amount of substance | mole | mol |
| Electric current | ampere | A |
| Luminous intensity | candela | cd |

### Derived Units Used in Chemical Engineering

| Derived Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol | In Base Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Force | newton | N | kg·m·s⁻² |
| Energy / Heat | joule | J | kg·m²·s⁻² |
| Power | watt | W | kg·m²·s⁻³ = J/s |
| Pressure | pascal | Pa | kg·m⁻¹·s⁻² = N/m² |
| Volume | cubic meter | m³ | m³ |
| Density | kilogram per cubic meter | kg/m³ | kg·m⁻³ |

### Common Non-SI Units and Their Conversions

| Quantity | Non-SI Unit | Equivalent in SI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure | 1 atm | $1.01325 \times 10^5$ Pa |
| Pressure | 1 bar | $1.0 \times 10^5$ Pa |
| Pressure | 1 torr / 1 mmHg | 133.322 Pa |
| Energy | 1 cal | 4.184 J |
| Energy | 1 BTU | 1055.06 J |
| Power | 1 hp | 745.7 W |
| Volume | 1 gallon (US) | 3.785 L = 0.003785 m³ |
| Mass | 1 lb | 0.4536 kg |
| Length | 1 ft | 0.3048 m |
| Temperature | $t$ °C | $T$ K = $t + 273.15$ |

> [!NOTE] **Dimensional Analysis — The Detective Tool**
> Dimensional analysis uses the principle that physical equations must be **dimensionally homogeneous** to either *check the validity* of a derived equation, or *derive* a relationship between variables when the underlying physics is not fully understood. The classic example is the **Buckingham $\Pi$ theorem**, which states that if a physical system has $n$ variables involving $k$ fundamental dimensions, it can be completely described by $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Molecular Weight of a Gas Mixture

Consider a gas mixture containing $n$ components with mole fractions $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$ and corresponding molar masses $M_1, M_2, \dots, M_n$ (in kg/kmol). We wish to derive an expression for the **average (or mean) molar mass** $M_{\text{avg}}$ of the mixture.

### Line-by-Line Derivation

**Step 1 — Choose a basis.** Take exactly **1 kmol of the total mixture** as the basis. This is a convenient choice because, by definition, the mole fraction gives the number of moles of each component per mole of total mixture.

**Step 2 — Express the moles of each component.** From the definition of mole fraction:

$$n_i = y_i \times n_{\text{total}} = y_i \times 1 \text{ kmol} = y_i \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Express the mass of each component.** The mass of component $i$ is the product of its moles and its molar mass:

$$m_i = n_i \cdot M_i = y_i \cdot M_i \quad \text{[kmol × kg/kmol = kg]}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the masses of all components.** The total mass of the 1 kmol of mixture is:

$$m_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of molar mass.** The average molar mass is the total mass divided by the total number of moles (which is 1 kmol by our basis choice):

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i}{1}$$

$$\boxed{M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \, M_i}$$

> [!NOTE] **Validity Check**
> If the mixture is a pure component (i.e., $y_1 = 1$ and all other $y_i = 0$), the formula correctly reduces to $M_{\text{avg}} = 1 \times M_1 = M_1$, as expected. The formula is **linear** in mole fractions and is valid for **ideal gas mixtures** where the components do not interact chemically.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion from Mass Fraction to Mole Fraction

Often in KTU problems, feed compositions are given as **mass fractions** ($w_i$), but the problem requires you to work on a **mole basis** (e.g., for gas-phase reactions). The conversion formula is derived below.

### Line-by-Line Derivation

**Step 1 — Choose a basis of 1 kg of total mixture.** The mass of component $i$ is then $m_i = w_i \times 1 = w_i$ kg.

**Step 2 — Convert mass to moles for each component.** Using $n_i = m_i / M_i$:

$$n_i = \frac{w_i}{M_i} \quad \text{[kg / (kg/kmol) = kmol]}$$

**Step 3 — Total moles in the mixture.** Sum over all components:

$$n_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{w_i}{M_i}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the definition of mole fraction.** $y_i = n_i / n_{\text{total}}$:

$$y_i = \frac{\displaystyle\frac{w_i}{M_i}}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{w_j}{M_j}}$$

$$\boxed{y_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j / M_j}}$$

The reverse conversion (mole fraction to mass fraction) follows an identical structure with $M_i$ and $1/M_i$ interchanged:

$$w_i = \frac{y_i M_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j M_j}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Pure Concept Application

> [!IMPORTANT] **Worked Example for Conceptual Mastery**
> A dry flue gas from a furnace has the following composition by volume (mole %): **CO₂ = 14%**, **O₂ = 6%**, **N₂ = 80%**. Calculate:
> 1. The **average molecular weight** of the flue gas.
> 2. The **density of the flue gas** at **1.5 atm** and **200 °C**.
> 3. The **mass of 1 kmol** of this gas.

### Complete Step-by-Step Solution

**Part (a) — Average Molecular Weight**

Since volume % = mole % for ideal gases, the mole fractions are:

$$y_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.14, \quad y_{\text{O}_2} = 0.06, \quad y_{\text{N}_2} = 0.80$$

The atomic / molecular weights required are:
- $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 12 + 2(16) = 44$ kg/kmol
- $M_{\text{O}_2} = 2(16) = 32$ kg/kmol
- $M_{\text{N}_2} = 2(14) = 28$ kg/kmol

Apply the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{avg}} &= \sum_{i} y_i M_i \\
&= (0.14)(44) + (0.06)(32) + (0.80)(28) \\
&= 6.16 + 1.92 + 22.40 \\
&= 30.48 \text{ kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) — Density of the Flue Gas**

Using the ideal gas law: $PV = nRT \Rightarrow \rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$

Convert given conditions to SI units:
- $P = 1.5 \text{ atm} = 1.5 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 1.5199 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $T = 200 + 273.15 = 473.15 \text{ K}$
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- $M_{\text{avg}} = 30.48 \text{ kg/kmol} = 0.03048 \text{ kg/mol}$

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{PM}{RT} = \frac{(1.5199 \times 10^5)(0.03048)}{(8.314)(473.15)} \\
&= \frac{4632.8}{3933.6} \\
&= 1.178 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c) — Mass of 1 kmol of Flue Gas**

By definition of molar mass, 1 kmol of this gas weighs exactly $M_{\text{avg}}$:

$$m = 1 \text{ kmol} \times 30.48 \text{ kg/kmol} = 30.48 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE] **Sanity Check**
> Air has a molecular weight of approximately 28.97 kg/kmol. Our flue gas is slightly heavier (30.48 kg/kmol) because of the CO₂ content. This physical intuition confirms our calculation is correct.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — How to Choose the Basis of Calculation

| Scenario | Recommended Basis | Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Liquid-phase process with mass flows given | **100 kg of feed** (or 1000 kg/h) | Avoids decimal manipulation |
| Gas-phase reaction with mole flows given | **100 kmol of feed** | Aligns with stoichiometry |
| Combustion problem with air-fuel ratio | **100 kg of fuel** | Mass is conserved, moles are not |
| Recycle / purge problem | **1 kmol of recycle** or **100 kg of fresh feed** | Minimizes unknowns |
| Batch process with cycle time | **1 batch** (e.g., 1 batch = 1 hour of operation) | Natural reference |
| Crystallization / drying with moisture | **1 kg of dry solid** | Removes the variable water content |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Exam Heuristic**
> When in doubt, choose a **mass basis of 100 kg or 1000 kg**. This is the most common KTU convention and the most forgiving when arithmetic slips occur. Always **declare the basis in the first line** of your answer and refer to it consistently throughout. Examiners explicitly award marks for this step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Seven-Step Process Engineering Methodology (Flowchart)

The following Mermaid diagram captures the universal seven-step problem-solving methodology that every KTU Process Calculations answer should follow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([KTU Process Problem Encountered]) --> stepA1[Step 1: Draw the Flowchart\nBlock diagram of all unit operations]
    stepA1 --> stepA2[Step 2: Choose a Basis of Calculation\n100 kg feed or 100 kmol]
    stepA2 --> stepA3[Step 3: Label All Streams\nFlow rates, compositions, T, P]
    stepA3 --> check1{Need Material\nBalance?}
    check1 -- YES --> stepA4a[Step 4a: Write Mass Balances\nOn each unit and overall]
    check1 -- NO --> check2{Need Energy\nBalance?}
    stepA4a --> check2
    check2 -- YES --> stepA4b[Step 4b: Write Energy Balances\nFirst Law of Thermodynamics]
    check2 -- NO --> stepA5
    stepA4b --> stepA5[Step 5: Solve the Equations\nAlgebra or matrix methods]
    stepA5 --> stepA6[Step 6: Verify Results\nDimensional check and sanity check]
    stepA6 --> stepA7[Step 7: State Final Answer\nWith proper units and basis]
    stepA7 --> endA([Solution Complete])

    style startA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style endA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style stepA1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style stepA2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style stepA3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style stepA4a fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style stepA4b fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style stepA5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style stepA6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style stepA7 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style check1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style check2 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Architecture of Chemical Engineering Knowledge

The following diagram shows how **Process Calculations** sits at the foundation of the entire chemical engineering curriculum, supporting all advanced subjects.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph foundation ["FOUNDATION LAYER"]
        mathA[Engineering Mathematics]
        phyA[Engineering Physics]
        chemA[Engineering Chemistry]
    end

    subgraph core ["CORE LAYER (S2 B.Tech)"]
        pccal["PCCHT205 Process Calculations\nMaterial + Energy Balances"]
        thermoA[Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics]
        fmecA[Fluid Mechanics]
    end

    subgraph intermediate ["INTERMEDIATE LAYER (S3-S4)"]
        htxA[Heat Transfer]
        mtxA[Mass Transfer]
        reactA[Chemical Reaction Engineering]
        ctrlA[Process Control]
    end

    subgraph advanced ["ADVANCED LAYER (S5-S8)"]
        desA[Process Equipment Design]
        plantA[Plant Design and Economics]
        safeA[Process Safety and HSE]
        simA[Process Simulation]
    end

    mathA --> pccal
    phyA --> pccal
    chemA --> pccal
    mathA --> thermoA
    mathA --> fmecA

    pccal --> htxA
    pccal --> mtxA

    pccal --> ctrlA

    thermoA --> desA

    mtxA --> desA
    reactA --> desA
    ctrlA --> simA

    desA --> plantA
    desA --> safeA
    simA --> plantA

    style pccal fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    style foundation fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px

```

---

## 4.3 The Unit Operations vs Unit Processes Classification Map

A clean architecture diagram distinguishing **physical changes** (Unit Operations) from **chemical changes** (Unit Processes), as introduced in Section 1.2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[Raw Material Feed] --> processA{Physical or\nChemical Change?}

    processA -- PHYSICAL CHANGE --> uoBlock[UNIT OPERATIONS]
    processA -- CHEMICAL CHANGE --> upBlock[UNIT PROCESSES]

    subgraph uoBlock [" "]
        uo1[Distillation]
        uo2[Absorption]
        uo3[Filtration]
        uo4[Drying]
        uo5[Crystallization]
        uo6[Evaporation]
        uo7[Mixing]
        uo8[Heat Exchange]
    end

    subgraph upBlock [" "]
        up1[Oxidation]
        up2[Reduction]
        up3[Polymerization]
        up4[Nitration]
        up5[Sulfonation]
        up6[Fermentation]
        up7[Cracking]
        up8[Reforming]
    end

    uoBlock --> finalProdA[Purified / Separated Product]
    upBlock --> finalProdB[New Chemical Compound]

    style feedA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style processA fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style uoBlock fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    style upBlock fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px
    style finalProdA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style finalProdB fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Generic Material Balance Block Diagram Template

This is the **universal block diagram** a KTU student should draw as the first step of every material balance problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    in1[F1\nStream 1] -- x1 --> blockA[Unit Operation]
    in2[F2\nStream 2] -- x2 --> blockA
    in3[F3\nStream 3] -- x3 --> blockA
    blockA -- y1 --> out1[P1\nProduct]
    blockA -- y2 --> out2[P2\nBy-product]
    blockA -- y3 --> out3[P3\nWaste / Recycle]

    style blockA fill:#FFB74D,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style in1 fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style in2 fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style in3 fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style out1 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style out2 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    style out3 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Convention used in the diagram:**
- $F_1, F_2, F_3$ = Feed stream flow rates (kg/h)
- $x_1, x_2, x_3$ = Compositions of component $i$ in feeds
- $P_1, P_2, P_3$ = Product stream flow rates (kg/h)
- $y_1, y_2, y_3$ = Compositions of component $i$ in products
- The **orange block** represents the unit operation (mixer, reactor, separator, etc.)

> [!NOTE] **How to Use This Template**
> For any KTU problem, replace the generic stream labels with the actual streams given. Write the balance $\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$ overall, and $\sum F_{\text{in}} x_{i,\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}} y_{i,\text{out}}$ for each component. This single visual is worth **at least 1–2 marks** out of 14 in a typical KTU question, as it demonstrates organized thinking.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Differentiate between Unit Operations and Unit Processes. Give two examples of each.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Unit Operations | Unit Processes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Nature of change** | Physical change (no reaction) | Chemical change (reaction involved) |
| **Governing principles** | Momentum, heat, mass transfer laws | Reaction kinetics, thermodynamics |
| **Examples (any 2)** | Distillation, Filtration, Drying, Crystallization, Evaporation, Absorption | Oxidation, Polymerization, Nitration, Fermentation, Cracking, Sulfonation |

**1 mark** for the correct definition of each, **0.5 mark** each for examples.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**State the Law of Conservation of Mass. Write the general material balance equation in words and explain the significance of the "Accumulation" term.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Law of Conservation of Mass**
> *"Mass can neither be created nor destroyed in any physical or chemical process. The total mass of all inputs to a system must equal the total mass of all outputs plus any accumulation within the system."*

**General Material Balance Equation:**

$$\text{Input} - \text{Output} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}$$

Where:
- **Input** — mass entering the system boundary
- **Output** — mass leaving the system boundary
- **Generation** — mass produced by chemical reaction inside the system (zero for inert components)
- **Accumulation** — change of mass held up inside the system with time

**Significance of the Accumulation term:**
- If **Accumulation = 0** → process is at **steady state** (most common case in KTU problems)
- If **Accumulation ≠ 0** → process is at **unsteady state** (e.g., a tank being filled or drained)

**1 mark** for the statement, **1 mark** for the equation, **1 mark** for the significance discussion.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme)**
> Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. KTU uses internal choice — the student may answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are provided below for parallel practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **seven-step methodology** for solving chemical engineering process calculation problems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas mixture has the following composition by volume: **CH₄ = 30%, C₂H₆ = 20%, CO₂ = 50%**. Calculate the:
- (i) Average molecular weight of the mixture
- (ii) Density of the mixture at **2.0 atm** and **300 K**
- (iii) Mass of **500 L** of the mixture at the same conditions **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

The seven-step methodology is universal in chemical engineering. The student should describe each step clearly:

1. **Draw the Flowchart** — Visual representation of all units and streams. **(1 mark)**
2. **Choose a Basis** — Select 100 kg feed, 100 kmol, 1 hour, etc. **(1 mark)**
3. **Label All Streams** — Assign flow rates, compositions, T, P to each. **(1 mark)**
4. **Write Material and Energy Balances** — Apply conservation laws to each unit. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Solve the Equations** — Algebra or matrix methods. **(1 mark)**
6. **Verify Results** — Dimensional check, physical plausibility. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **State the Final Answer** — With proper units and basis reference. **(1 mark)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Mole fractions (since volume % = mole % for ideal gases): $y_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.30$, $y_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 0.20$, $y_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.50$.

**Molar masses:**
- $M_{\text{CH}_4} = 12 + 4(1) = 16$ kg/kmol
- $M_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 2(12) + 6(1) = 30$ kg/kmol
- $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 12 + 2(16) = 44$ kg/kmol

**(i) Average Molecular Weight** (1.5 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{avg}} &= \sum y_i M_i \\
&= (0.30)(16) + (0.20)(30) + (0.50)(44) \\
&= 4.8 + 6.0 + 22.0 \\
&= 32.8 \text{ kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Density at 2.0 atm and 300 K** (3 marks)

Convert to SI units:
- $P = 2.0 \times 1.01325 \times 10^5 = 2.0265 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $T = 300$ K
- $M_{\text{avg}} = 0.0328$ kg/mol

Using the ideal gas law in the form $\rho = PM / RT$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{PM}{RT} = \frac{(2.0265 \times 10^5)(0.0328)}{(8.314)(300)} \\
&= \frac{6646.9}{2494.2} \\
&= 2.665 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Mass of 500 L at the same conditions** (2.5 marks)

Convert volume: $V = 500 \text{ L} = 0.500 \text{ m}^3$

$$m = \rho \times V = 2.665 \times 0.500 = 1.3325 \text{ kg}$$

Or alternatively using $m = \dfrac{PVM}{RT}$ directly:

$$m = \frac{(2.0265 \times 10^5)(0.500)(0.0328)}{(8.314)(300)} = 1.3325 \text{ kg}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $M_{\text{avg}} = 32.8$ kg/kmol
- (ii) $\rho = 2.665$ kg/m³
- (iii) $m = 1.333$ kg

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Define **Chemical Engineering**. Discuss the **role and scope** of chemical engineering in modern industry with at least **five** distinct application areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel oil has the following composition by mass: **C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 3%, ash = 1%**. It is burned completely with **20% excess air**. Calculate the:
- (i) Kilograms of air required per **100 kg of oil**
- (ii) Composition of the flue gas (in mole % dry basis) **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
Chemical Engineering is the discipline that applies the principles of chemistry, physics, mathematics, biology, and economics to design, develop, produce, and use chemicals, materials, and energy in a safe, economical, and sustainable manner. It converts raw materials into useful products through controlled physical and chemical changes at industrial scale.

**Scope and Application Areas (5 marks, 1 mark each for any 5):**

1. **Petroleum Refining and Petrochemicals** — Conversion of crude oil into gasoline, diesel, kerosene, LPG, and feedstock for plastics, synthetic rubber, and detergents.

2. **Pharmaceuticals and Biotechnology** — Manufacturing of drugs, vaccines, antibiotics, and biological products using fermentation, extraction, crystallization, and drying.

3. **Food Processing** — Production of dairy products, edible oils, beverages, packaged foods involving pasteurization, evaporation, and freeze-drying.

4. **Environmental Engineering** — Treatment of industrial wastewater, flue-gas desulfurization, carbon capture and storage, and solid waste management.

5. **Energy Sector** — Design of fuel cells, lithium-ion batteries, hydrogen production, solar cell fabrication, and nuclear fuel processing.

6. **Materials Science** — Synthesis of polymers, ceramics, composites, and nanomaterials for use in aerospace, automotive, and electronics industries.

7. **Electronic Materials** — Production of ultra-pure silicon wafers, photoresists, and specialty gases for semiconductor manufacturing.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Write combustion reactions (1.5 marks)**

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$
$$\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical oxygen requirement per 100 kg oil**

- For C: $\dfrac{84}{12} = 7.0$ kmol C → requires 7.0 kmol O₂
- For H₂: $\dfrac{12}{2} = 6.0$ kmol H₂ → requires $6.0 \times 0.5 = 3.0$ kmol O₂
- For S: $\dfrac{3}{32} = 0.09375$ kmol S → requires 0.09375 kmol O₂

Total theoretical O₂ = $7.0 + 3.0 + 0.09375 = 10.09375$ kmol

**Step 3 — Air composition** (1 mark)

Air = 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by mole. For 1 kmol O₂, air supplied = $1/0.21 = 4.762$ kmol air, containing 3.762 kmol N₂.

**Step 4 — With 20% excess air** (1 mark)

$$\text{Actual O}_2 \text{ supplied} = 1.20 \times 10.09375 = 12.1125 \text{ kmol}$$

$$\text{Total air} = 4.762 \times 12.1125 = 57.680 \text{ kmol air}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of air** (1 mark)

Average M of air = 28.97 kg/kmol.

$$m_{\text{air}} = 57.680 \times 28.97 = 1671.2 \text{ kg air per 100 kg oil}$$

Or equivalently, **16.71 kg air per kg oil**.

**Step 6 — Flue gas composition (dry basis)** (2.5 marks)

Components in dry flue gas (water removed):

| Component | Moles (kmol) |
| :--- | :--- |
| CO₂ from C | 7.0 |
| SO₂ from S | 0.09375 |
| Excess O₂ | $12.1125 - 10.09375 = 2.01875$ |
| N₂ from air | $3.762 \times 12.1125 = 45.567$ kmol |
| **Total dry** | $7.0 + 0.09375 + 2.01875 + 45.567 = 54.679$ kmol |

Mole percentages (dry basis):

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,\text{CO}_2 &= \frac{7.0}{54.679} \times 100 = 12.80\% \\
\%\,\text{SO}_2 &= \frac{0.09375}{54.679} \times 100 = 0.17\% \\
\%\,\text{O}_2 &= \frac{2.01875}{54.679} \times 100 = 3.69\% \\
\%\,\text{N}_2 &= \frac{45.567}{54.679} \times 100 = 83.34\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **16.71 kg air per kg oil** (or 1671 kg per 100 kg oil)
- (ii) Dry flue gas: **CO₂ = 12.80%, SO₂ = 0.17%, O₂ = 3.69%, N₂ = 83.34%**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum w_i M_i$ (mass-fraction-weighted) instead of $\sum y_i M_i$ (mole-fraction-weighted). This is **wrong** — mass fractions give the **average molar mass** only via the inverse formula. **Always check whether the given composition is mass-based or mole-based.**
> - **Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert °C to K in the ideal gas law. A student writing $T = 300$ when given $t = 27$ °C will lose **2 to 3 marks** instantly.
> - **Mistake 3:** Converting atm to Pa incorrectly (some students use 1 atm = 10⁵ Pa, losing a small but significant accuracy). Use the **exact** 1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa.
> - **Mistake 4:** Not declaring the basis of calculation. Examiners explicitly look for *"Basis: 100 kg of oil"* as the first line. **No basis = 0.5 to 1 mark penalty.**
> - **Mistake 5:** In combustion problems, forgetting the **excess air** adjustment. The N₂ in the flue gas must reflect the **total** air (theoretical + excess), not just the theoretical air.
> - **Mistake 6:** Not labelling the units in the final answer. "16.71" without "kg air/kg oil" is incomplete and will cost a mark.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chemical Engineering** is the application of chemistry, physics, mathematics, and economics to design, produce, and use chemicals, materials, and energy at industrial scale. **(Definition — must be memorized verbatim for KTU)**

- **Unit Operations** = physical changes (distillation, drying, filtration, absorption, crystallization, evaporation, mixing, heat exchange). **Unit Processes** = chemical changes (oxidation, reduction, polymerization, nitration, sulfonation, fermentation, cracking, reforming).

- **Arthur Amos Noyes (1915)** introduced the Unit Operations concept; **Bird, Stewart, Lightfoot (1960)** introduced Transport Phenomena. Both are KTU-favorite historical facts.

- The **seven-step methodology** is the universal problem-solving framework: (1) Draw flowchart, (2) Choose basis, (3) Label streams, (4) Write material/energy balances, (5) Solve equations, (6) Verify, (7) State answer.

- **Conservation of Mass:** $\sum F_{\text{in}} = \sum F_{\text{out}}$ for steady state; $\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ for unsteady state.

- **Average molecular weight** is a **mole-fraction-weighted** average: $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$ (in kg/kmol).

- **Mass-fraction to mole-fraction conversion:** $y_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j w_j / M_j}$

- **Ideal Gas Law:** $PV = nRT$ with $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³, $T$ in K, $n$ in mol.

- **Density of ideal gas:** $\rho = PM/RT$ (in kg/m³ when $M$ is in kg/mol).

- **Basis of calculation** is non-negotiable in KTU answers. Common safe choices: 100 kg feed, 100 kmol feed, 1 hour of operation, 1 batch.

- **SI Units** (kg, m, s, K, mol, A, cd) are preferred; conversions to non-SI units (atm, °C, cal, BTU, lb) are routinely tested.

- **Combustion rule:** theoretical O₂ = kmol C + 0.5 × kmol H₂ + kmol S (per 100 kg of fuel). Air supplied = (1 + excess fraction) × theoretical air.

- **Air composition** is always 21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by mole (or 23.2% O₂ and 76.8% N₂ by mass). The molar ratio of N₂:O₂ in air is **3.762 : 1**.

- **Dimensional consistency** is the first check on any calculation. If the units don't match, the answer cannot be right.

- **Dry flue gas** excludes water vapor; **wet flue gas** includes it. The composition must be specified accordingly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Process Industry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Chemical Process Industry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Chemical Process Industry (CPI)** is the broad segment of the manufacturing sector that transforms raw materials (crude oil, minerals, agricultural products, air, water, and natural gas) into useful industrial, agricultural, and consumer chemical products through a sequence of physical changes (unit operations) and chemical changes (unit processes). In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Process Calculations (PCCHT205), the term is used to describe the *engineered system* whose mass and energy flows must be quantitatively analyzed using material and energy balances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1, PCCHT205):**
> *“The Chemical Process Industry comprises those manufacturing units in which a planned series of physical, chemical, or biochemical transformations are carried out on raw feedstocks to obtain finished products of higher economic and utility value.”*

Key terminology you will see repeatedly in KTU questions:

| Term | KTU Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Feedstock** | The raw material entering the first unit of a process plant |
| **Product** | The desired output stream from the plant |
| **By-product** | A valuable stream produced simultaneously with the main product |
| **Recycle Stream** | A portion of the product or downstream stream returned to an upstream unit |
| **Bypass Stream** | A portion of a stream diverted around a unit to combine downstream |
| **Purge Stream** | A bleed removed from a recycle loop to prevent accumulation of inerts |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Kitchen is a Mini Chemical Plant

Imagine a South Indian kitchen preparing *Appam and Stew*:

1. Raw rice, coconut, vegetables (analogous to **feedstock**).
2. Soaking, grinding, fermenting (analogous to **mixing, size reduction, reaction — unit operations & unit processes**).
3. The cooker (analogous to a **reactor/heat exchanger**).
4. Steam, odour, water vapour (analogous to **waste/by-product streams**).
5. The final Appam and Stew (analogous to the **product stream**).
6. The leftover coconut milk unused? It can be poured back into the next batch — that is a **recycle stream**.
7. Excess steam escaping out of the pressure cooker lid — that is a **purge**.

A chemical engineer simply scales this kitchen concept to **tons per day** of chemicals, applies thermodynamics, kinetics, and rigorous **mass & energy bookkeeping** — and that entire bookkeeping exercise is what PCCHT205 teaches.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Every CPI plant, regardless of whether it makes sulphuric acid, ammonia, petrol, or paracetamol, is fundamentally a *system of inputs, transformations, and outputs*. The Process Calculations course is the art of writing *exact mathematical equations* for those inputs and outputs.

## 1.3 Scope of the Chemical Process Industry

The CPI is conventionally divided into the following major branches, which frequently appear in KTU Module 1 short-answer questions:

1. **Inorganic Chemicals Industry** — acids (H₂SO₄, HNO₃, HCl), alkalis (NaOH, Na₂CO₃), fertilizers (urea, DAP, ammonia), industrial gases (O₂, N₂, H₂, Cl₂).
2. **Organic Chemicals Industry** — petrochemicals (ethylene, propylene, benzene, toluene, xylene), polymers (PE, PP, PVC, PET), synthetic fibres, dyes, explosives.
3. **Pharmaceutical & Fine Chemicals Industry** — APIs, intermediates, specialty chemicals, soaps & detergents.
4. **Food & Biochemical Industry** — sugar, starch, brewery, dairy, oils & fats, fermentation products.
5. **Metallurgical & Materials Industry** — iron & steel, aluminium, cement, glass, ceramics.
6. **Petroleum Refining & Petrochemicals** — crude oil distillation, catalytic cracking, reforming, alkylation.
7. **Pulp, Paper & Natural Products Industry** — paper, rayon, rubber, wood chemicals.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Module 1 Part A questions often test whether you can *classify* a given product into the correct branch of CPI. Memorize at least 2 examples per branch.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Chemical Process Plant

A modern chemical process plant, irrespective of its end product, contains the following **functional blocks**. Understanding this architecture is essential because every Process Calculations problem ultimately reduces to writing balances across one or more of these blocks.

1. **Raw Material Storage and Preparation Section** — feed tanks, crushers, screens, dryers.
2. **Reactor / Conversion Section** — where chemical change occurs (the heart of the plant).
3. **Separation / Purification Section** — distillation, extraction, absorption, filtration, drying, crystallization.
4. **Recycle / Recompression Section** — unconverted reactants and valuable by-products are returned upstream.
5. **Heat & Power Integration Section** — heat exchangers, reboilers, condensers, furnaces, turbines.
6. **Product Storage and Packaging Section** — final storage, drumming, bottling.
7. **Effluent Treatment Section** — to handle waste water, gases, and solids.

## 2.2 Unit Operations vs Unit Processes

This distinction is a *favourite KTU Part A (3-mark) question*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Operation** — A physical change in which the chemical identity of the substance is preserved (e.g., distillation of ethanol from water — no new molecule is formed).
> **Unit Process** — A chemical change in which the molecular structure of the reactant is altered (e.g., esterification of acetic acid with ethanol to give ethyl acetate).

| Classification | Type of Change | Examples (often asked in KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| Unit Operation | Physical | Distillation, Evaporation, Drying, Filtration, Centrifugation, Crushing, Mixing, Leaching, Crystallization, Humidification, Absorption, Adsorption, Membrane separation |
| Unit Process | Chemical | Alkylation, Cracking, Polymerization, Hydrogenation, Oxidation, Nitration, Sulphonation, Halogenation, Fermentation, Esterification, Hydrolysis |

## 2.3 Types of Flow Diagrams Used in the CPI

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiners frequently ask:** *“Differentiate between a Block Flow Diagram (BFD), a Process Flow Diagram (PFD), and a Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID).”*

### 2.3.1 Block Flow Diagram (BFD)

- Simplest representation.
- Each **major process step** is shown as a single rectangular block.
- Streams are arrows with mass flow labels.
- Used in the **conceptual / preliminary design** stage.
- No equipment internals, no controls, no minor streams.

### 2.3.2 Process Flow Diagram (PFD)

- More detailed than BFD.
- Shows **all major equipment items** (reactors, distillation columns, heat exchangers, pumps, compressors) with proper symbols.
- Shows **all major process streams** with flow rate, temperature, pressure, and composition.
- Includes **energy integration** information (heat duty, work input/output).
- Excludes minor piping and instrumentation details.

### 2.3.3 Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)

- The most detailed schematic.
- Includes **every pipe, every valve, every instrument, every controller loop**.
- Shows the **interlock logic** and safety devices.
- Used in **detailed engineering, construction, and operations**.
- Cannot be prepared until the PFD is finalised.

## 2.4 Classification of Chemical Processes by Operating Mode

| Mode | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch** | All reactants charged at t = 0, products withdrawn at end of cycle | Pharmaceutical API, dyes |
| **Continuous (Steady-State)** | Streams enter and leave continuously; conditions invariant with time | Ammonia, petroleum refining |
| **Semi-Batch (Semi-Continuous)** | One reactant charged initially, the other fed slowly | Fermentation, emulsion polymerization |
| **Transient (Unsteady)** | Conditions change with time but no discrete batches | Plant start-up, shut-down, changeover |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU Process Calculations, **steady-state continuous processes** are the default assumption unless stated otherwise.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet for Module 1 (Process Industry Basics)

The formulas below are those that Process Calculations builds upon. They are introduced in Module 1 and re-used in every later module.

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass fraction of component *i* | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{\sum_j m_j}$ | dimensionless | $\sum_i w_i = 1$ |
| 2 | Mole fraction of component *i* | $x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum_j n_j}$ | dimensionless | $\sum_i x_i = 1$ |
| 3 | Average molecular weight of mixture | $M_{avg} = \sum_i x_i \, M_i = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}}$ | g/mol or kg/kmol | Useful in gas/liquid systems |
| 4 | Density of mixture | $\dfrac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \sum_i \dfrac{w_i}{\rho_i}$ | kg/m³ | Mass-fraction weighted |
| 5 | Molarity (M) | $M = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{V_{solution}}$ | mol/L | Temperature dependent |
| 6 | Molality (m) | $m = \dfrac{n_{solute}}{m_{solvent}\,(kg)}$ | mol/kg | Temperature independent |
| 7 | Normality (N) | $N = n \, \times \, M$ | eq/L | $n$ = n-factor (acidity / basicity) |
| 8 | Mass flow → Molar flow conversion | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{M_{avg}}$ | kmol/s | Foundation of gas-balance problems |
| 9 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | SI: P in Pa, V in m³, T in K, n in kmol, **R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** | Foundation of Module 2 gas-law problems |
| 10 | Standard conditions (IUPAC) | $T = 273.15\ \text{K},\quad P = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ | — | Used to convert Nm³ to kmol |
| 11 | STP (older convention) | $T = 273.15\ \text{K},\quad P = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ | — | Same numerical volume = 22.414 m³/kmol |
| 12 | NTP (older convention) | $T = 293.15\ \text{K},\quad P = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$ | — | Volume = 24.054 m³/kmol |
| 13 | Conversion (X) of limiting reactant | $X = \dfrac{\text{moles reacted}}{\text{moles fed}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le X \le 1$ |
| 14 | Yield (Y) | $Y = \dfrac{\text{moles of product formed}}{\text{theoretical maximum moles of product}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le Y \le 1$ |
| 15 | Selectivity (S) | $S = \dfrac{\text{desired product formed}}{\text{undesired product formed}}$ | dimensionless | $S \ge 0$ |

> [!TIP]
> Notice the appearance of the universal gas constant **R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)** and **R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)**. KTU papers accept both, but always write the **units explicitly** to score the easy 1 mark on dimensional consistency.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The framework above is not merely academic. A CPI engineer in industry uses:

- A **P&ID** as a legal document for plant construction and safety audits.
- A **BFD** for managerial communication and feasibility studies.
- A **Material Balance (Module 2)** to size reactors, separators, and storage tanks.
- An **Energy Balance (Module 3)** to size heat exchangers, fired heaters, and refrigeration units.
- **Process economics (Module 5)** to compute cost of manufacture and payback period.

> [!NOTE]
> Almost every Chapter downstream of Module 1 in PCCHT205 is built on the *concepts defined in this very first module*. Treat Module 1 as the alphabet of the entire course.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **mathematical backbone** of Module 1 concepts so that you can answer *any* derivation- or numerical-style question in the KTU 2024 ESE.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Mass Fraction and Mole Fraction

This is a **favourite 7-mark derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme because it is a closed-book derivable result.

Let a mixture contain $C$ components. For component $i$:

- Mass of $i$ in a basis of **1 kg of mixture** = $w_i$ kg
- Number of moles of $i$ = $\dfrac{w_i}{M_i}$ kmol
- Total moles of mixture = $\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}$ kmol

By definition of mole fraction:

$$
x_i = \dfrac{\dfrac{w_i}{M_i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} \dfrac{w_j}{M_j}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for $w_i$:

$$
w_i = \dfrac{x_i \, M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{C} x_j \, M_j}
$$

**Verification (using a 2-component system):**

For a binary mixture with components 1 and 2:

$$
w_1 = \dfrac{x_1 M_1}{x_1 M_1 + x_2 M_2}, \quad w_2 = \dfrac{x_2 M_2}{x_1 M_1 + x_2 M_2}
$$

Sum of mass fractions:

$$
w_1 + w_2 = \dfrac{x_1 M_1 + x_2 M_2}{x_1 M_1 + x_2 M_2} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

Sum of mole fractions:

$$
x_1 + x_2 = \dfrac{\dfrac{w_1}{M_1} + \dfrac{w_2}{M_2}}{\dfrac{w_1}{M_1} + \dfrac{w_2}{M_2}} = 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Average Molecular Weight of a Gas Mixture

Consider a gas mixture of $C$ components, total mass $m$ kg, total moles $n$ kmol.

The mass of component $i$ is:

$$
m_i = n_i \, M_i = x_i \, n \, M_i
$$

The total mass is:

$$
m = \sum_{i=1}^{C} m_i = n \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i M_i
$$

But by definition, average molecular weight is:

$$
M_{avg} = \dfrac{m}{n}
$$

Therefore:

$$
M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} x_i \, M_i
$$

**Alternative form (mass-fraction basis):**

Starting from $x_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j w_j / M_j}$:

$$
M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{C} \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}}
$$

**Sanity check (2-component, air-like system):**

Take 21 % O₂ ($M = 32$ kg/kmol) and 79 % N₂ ($M = 28$ kg/kmol) by moles:

$$
M_{air} = 0.21 \times 32 + 0.79 \times 28 = 6.72 + 22.12 = 28.84 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

The accepted textbook value is **28.97 kg/kmol** (the small difference is due to Ar and CO₂). This confirms the validity of the derivation.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material Balance on a Single Unit (Steady State)

This is the foundation equation that Modules 2, 3, 4, 5 will repeatedly use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Material Balance Equation (KTU Module 2 syllabus statement, but introduced conceptually in Module 1):**

$$
\underbrace{\text{Accumulation}}_{\text{change inside system}} = \underbrace{\text{Input}}_{\text{in}} - \underbrace{\text{Output}}_{\text{out}} + \underbrace{\text{Generation}}_{\text{produced}} - \underbrace{\text{Consumption}}_{\text{reacted}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons}
$$

**For a steady-state process** (no accumulation, $\dfrac{dm_{sys}}{dt} = 0$):

$$
\dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{gen} - \dot{m}_{cons} = 0
$$

Or, equivalently:

$$
\dot{m}_{in} + \dot{m}_{gen} = \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{cons}
$$

**For a non-reactive physical unit** (no generation, no consumption, e.g. a heat exchanger or pump):

$$
\dot{m}_{in} = \dot{m}_{out}
$$

**For a reactive unit** with stoichiometry, generation and consumption are linked by the **reaction coordinate (extent of reaction, ξ)**:

$$
\dot{n}_{i,out} = \dot{n}_{i,in} + \sum_{j=1}^{R} \nu_{i,j} \, \xi_j
$$

where $\nu_{i,j}$ is the stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$ in reaction $j$ (negative for reactants, positive for products), and $\xi_j$ is the **extent of reaction $j$** in kmol/s.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Conversion, Yield, and Selectivity Relationships

Consider the elementary reaction:

$$
aA + bB \longrightarrow pP + qQ
$$

with side reaction $aA + b'B' \longrightarrow rR$ producing an undesired product $R$.

**Definitions:**

$$
X_A = \dfrac{n_{A,in} - n_{A,out}}{n_{A,in}}
$$

$$
Y_P = \dfrac{n_{P,out}}{n_{P,theoretical}} = \dfrac{n_{P,out}}{(n_{A,in}/a) \, \cdot \, p}
$$

Wait — for one mole of A consumed, $p/a$ moles of P are theoretically possible. So:

$$
Y_P = \dfrac{n_{P,out}}{(n_{A,in} - n_{A,out}) \cdot (p/a)} = \dfrac{n_{P,out}}{\Delta n_A \cdot (p/a)}
$$

**Selectivity of P with respect to R:**

$$
S_{P/R} = \dfrac{n_{P,out}}{n_{R,out}} \cdot \dfrac{r}{p}
$$

Where the factor $\dfrac{r}{p}$ normalizes to a *per-mole-of-A* basis.

**Fundamental relationship:**

$$
\text{Overall Conversion Efficiency} = X_A \times Y_P
$$

The fraction of the feed A that ends up as the desired product P is:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{n_{P,out}}{n_{P,theoretical, max\ from\ feed\ A}} = X_A \times Y_P
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Ideal Gas and Standard Volume Conversions

The ideal gas law:

$$
PV = nRT
$$

At **STP (101.325 kPa, 273.15 K)**:

$$
V_{molar} = \dfrac{RT}{P} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 273.15}{101.325} = 22.414 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol} = 22.414\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}
$$

At **NTP (101.325 kPa, 293.15 K)**:

$$
V_{molar} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 293.15}{101.325} = 24.054 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol} = 24.054\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}
$$

**Conversion formula (any P, T → STP):**

$$
n = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{P_{STP} \, V_{STP}}{R \, T_{STP}}
$$

Solving for $V_{STP}$:

$$
V_{STP} = V \times \dfrac{P}{P_{STP}} \times \dfrac{T_{STP}}{T}
$$

Where the subscript *STP* refers to the chosen standard conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU papers often give a gas volume at non-standard conditions and ask for the **moles**. Always reduce the gas to standard conditions first using the above ratio, *then* divide by 22.414 m³/kmol.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Aligned with KTU 2024 Numerical Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, 3-mark type):**
> A flue gas has the following analysis by volume: CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 8 %, N₂ = 80 %. Calculate (a) the average molecular weight, and (b) the density at 1 atm and 600 K. (R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)).

**Solution:**

Given mole fractions = volume fractions (for ideal gas):

- $x_{CO_2} = 0.12$, $M_{CO_2} = 44$ g/mol
- $x_{O_2} = 0.08$, $M_{O_2} = 32$ g/mol
- $x_{N_2} = 0.80$, $M_{N_2} = 28$ g/mol

**(a) Average molecular weight:**

$$
M_{avg} = \sum_i x_i M_i
$$

$$
M_{avg} = (0.12 \times 44) + (0.08 \times 32) + (0.80 \times 28)
$$

$$
M_{avg} = 5.28 + 2.56 + 22.40 = 30.24 \text{ g/mol} = 30.24 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**(b) Density at 1 atm, 600 K:**

From ideal gas law, $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$:

$$
\rho = \dfrac{(1 \text{ atm}) \times (30.24 \text{ g/mol})}{(0.0821 \text{ L·atm/(mol·K)}) \times (600 \text{ K})}
$$

$$
\rho = \dfrac{30.24}{49.26} = 0.614 \text{ g/L} = 0.614 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 pattern always gives you the gas constant explicitly so that you cannot get confused about units. The trick is **dimensional consistency**: every term in the numerator and denominator must reduce to the desired final unit.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — How to Choose a Calculation Basis

| Problem Type | Recommended Basis | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| All compositions given in **mass %** | 100 kg of feed (or 1 kg) | Mass fractions sum to 1 |
| All compositions in **mole %** (or vol % for gases) | 100 kmol of feed | Mole fractions sum to 1 |
| Single component asked in **kg** or **kmol** | 1 kmol of limiting reactant | Stoichiometry is mole-based |
| Reactor sizing / extent of reaction | 1 kmol of feed | $\xi$ is naturally in kmol |
| Gas volumes given | 1 Nm³ (or convert to moles first) | Gas law is mole-based |
| Distillation column with mass flowrates | 100 kg feed | Tray hydraulics depend on mass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Rule of Thumb:** "A wrong basis almost always produces a numerically correct but physically meaningless answer." Always **state your basis explicitly** at the top of every numerical solution — it is worth **half a mark** in KTU valuation.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Typical Chemical Process Plant

The Mermaid diagram below maps the *interactions between* the major blocks of a chemical process plant. This block-level functional architecture is exactly the type of schematic KTU Module 1 questions ask you to draw.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    RawMat[Raw Material Storage and Preparation]
    Reactor[Reactor Section with Unit Processes]
    SepA[Primary Separation e.g. Distillation]
    SepB[Secondary Separation e.g. Extraction]
    RecycleLoop[Recycle Compressor and Recycle Stream]
    PurgeLine[Purge Stream to Prevent Inert Buildup]
    HX[Heat Exchanger Network]
    ProductSt[Product Storage and Packaging]
    ETP[Effluent Treatment Plant]
    Utility[Utilities Steam Cooling Water Power]

    RawMat -->|Feed Stream| Reactor
    Reactor -->|Effluent Stream| ETP
    Reactor -->|Crude Product Stream| SepA
    SepA -->|Overhead Distillate| SepB
    SepA -->|Bottom Residue| ETP
    SepB -->|Purified Product| ProductSt
    SepB -->|Unreacted Feed| RecycleLoop
    RecycleLoop --> PurgeLine
    PurgeLine --> ETP
    RecycleLoop -->|Recycle Feed| Reactor
    HX -.->|Steam Supply| Reactor
    HX -.->|Cooling Water Supply| SepA
    Utility -.->|Power| Reactor
    Utility -.->|Power| RecycleLoop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every arrow is a stream. Solid arrows carry process material; dotted arrows carry utilities (energy). The `RecycleLoop` and `PurgeLine` together form the **closed-loop feedback structure** that distinguishes a real chemical plant from a textbook "once-through" process.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between a unit operation and a unit process with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Operation** is a physical transformation in which the chemical identity of the substance remains unchanged. **Example 1:** Distillation of a benzene–toluene mixture (no change in molecular structure). **Example 2:** Filtration of a slurry to separate solids from liquid.
>
> **Unit Process** is a chemical transformation in which a new chemical species is produced by a chemical reaction. **Example 1:** Nitration of benzene to form nitrobenzene. **Example 2:** Hydrogenation of vegetable oil to form vanaspati ghee.
>
> **Key differentiator:** Unit operations follow the laws of physics (mass transfer, heat transfer, momentum transfer). Unit processes follow the laws of chemical kinetics and thermodynamics.

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: List the different types of flow diagrams used in the chemical process industry. How does a P&ID differ from a PFD?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The CPI uses three main types of flow diagrams, in increasing order of detail:

1. **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** — single block per major process step, used at conceptual stage.
2. **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** — all major equipment, all major streams, with temperatures, pressures, flow rates, compositions, and energy duties; used in basic engineering.
3. **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** — every pipe, every valve, every instrument, every controller loop, every safety device; used in detailed engineering, construction, and operations.

**Difference between P&ID and PFD:**

| Feature | PFD | P&ID |
|---|---|---|
| All minor streams | No | Yes |
| Valve types and sizes | No | Yes |
| Instrumentation & control loops | No | Yes (with ISA tag numbers) |
| Equipment internals | Simplified | Full mechanical detail |
| Pipe classes and insulation | No | Yes |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark questions, **marks are forfeited** in three predictable ways: (1) failing to **state the calculation basis**, (2) writing a numerical answer **without units**, and (3) omitting the **assumption of steady state** in reactive systems. Avoid these traps and you will gain at least 2–3 free marks.

---

### **Question B-A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**The chlorination of methane occurs by the following reactions:**

$$
\text{CH}_4 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{HCl} \quad (1)
$$

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{Cl} + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{HCl} \quad (2)
$$

$$
\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CHCl}_3 + \text{HCl} \quad (3)
$$

$$
\text{CHCl}_3 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow \text{CCl}_4 + \text{HCl} \quad (4)
$$

**The feed to the reactor is 30 % CH₄ and 70 % Cl₂ by volume. 60 % of the Cl₂ fed is converted to HCl. The product stream contains CH₃Cl, CH₂Cl₂, CHCl₃, and CCl₄ in the mole ratio 5 : 3 : 2 : 1, and unreacted CH₄ and Cl₂. Calculate:**

**(a) The analysis of the product stream in mole percent and the conversion of CH₄. [7 marks]**
**(b) If the total product flow is 100 kmol/h, calculate the feed rates in kmol/h and the mass flowrate of the product stream. [7 marks]**

---

#### **Solution (a) — Product Analysis and Conversion [7 marks]**

**Basis:** 1 kmol of feed.

**Feed composition (mole basis = volume basis for ideal gases):**

- $n_{CH_4, in} = 0.30$ kmol
- $n_{Cl_2, in} = 0.70$ kmol

**Cl₂ converted:**

$$
n_{Cl_2, reacted} = 0.60 \times 0.70 = 0.42 \text{ kmol}
$$

**HCl produced:** Since every mole of Cl₂ converted produces 1 mole of HCl:

$$
n_{HCl, out} = 0.42 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Distribution of Cl₂ among the four chlorinated products:**

Let the mole ratio of CH₃Cl : CH₂Cl₂ : CHCl₃ : CCl₄ = 5 : 3 : 2 : 1.
Total ratio units = 5 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 11.

Moles of each chlorinated product:

$$
n_{CH_3Cl} = 5k, \quad n_{CH_2Cl_2} = 3k, \quad n_{CHCl_3} = 2k, \quad n_{CCl_4} = k
$$

**Chlorine atom balance:** Each product consumes a different number of Cl₂ molecules (1, 2, 3, 4 respectively). The HCl produced = (Cl atoms in) − (Cl atoms still in chlorinated product).

Total Cl atoms in (from Cl₂): $2 \times 0.42 = 0.84$ kmol of Cl atoms.

Cl atoms in products:

- CH₃Cl: $1 \times 5k = 5k$
- CH₂Cl₂: $2 \times 3k = 6k$
- CHCl₃: $3 \times 2k = 6k$
- CCl₄: $4 \times k = 4k$
- HCl: $1 \times 0.42 = 0.42$

Cl atom balance:

$$
5k + 6k + 6k + 4k + 0.42 = 0.84
$$

$$
21k = 0.42
$$

$$
k = 0.02
$$

Therefore:

- $n_{CH_3Cl} = 5 \times 0.02 = 0.10$ kmol
- $n_{CH_2Cl_2} = 3 \times 0.02 = 0.06$ kmol
- $n_{CHCl_3} = 2 \times 0.02 = 0.04$ kmol
- $n_{CCl_4} = 1 \times 0.02 = 0.02$ kmol

**Unreacted Cl₂:**

$$
n_{Cl_2, out} = 0.70 - 0.42 = 0.28 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Unreacted CH₄:** Use C-atom balance.

C in: $1 \times 0.30 = 0.30$ kmol.
C in products: $1 \times 0.10 + 1 \times 0.06 + 1 \times 0.04 + 1 \times 0.02 = 0.22$ kmol.

$$
n_{CH_4, out} = 0.30 - 0.22 = 0.08 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Total out:** $0.10 + 0.06 + 0.04 + 0.02 + 0.42 + 0.28 + 0.08 = 1.00$ kmol ✓

**Product analysis (mole %):**

| Component | Moles | Mole % |
|---|---|---|
| CH₃Cl | 0.10 | 10 |
| CH₂Cl₂ | 0.06 | 6 |
| CHCl₃ | 0.04 | 4 |
| CCl₄ | 0.02 | 2 |
| HCl | 0.42 | 42 |
| Cl₂ | 0.28 | 28 |
| CH₄ | 0.08 | 8 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | **100** |

**Conversion of CH₄:**

$$
X_{CH_4} = \dfrac{n_{CH_4, in} - n_{CH_4, out}}{n_{CH_4, in}} = \dfrac{0.30 - 0.08}{0.30} = \dfrac{0.22}{0.30} = 0.7333 = 73.33\ \%
$$

---

#### **Solution (b) — Feed Rates and Mass Flowrate [7 marks]**

**Given:** Total product flow = 100 kmol/h.
**Our basis was 1 kmol of feed → 1 kmol of product (since total moles are conserved when only HCl and chlorinated products form and Cl₂ is partially consumed).**

Therefore: **Feed rate = 100 kmol/h**

**Component feed rates (multiplying each by 100):**

- $F_{CH_4} = 100 \times 0.30 = 30$ kmol/h
- $F_{Cl_2} = 100 \times 0.70 = 70$ kmol/h

**Mass flowrate of product stream** (using molecular weights):

- $M_{CH_3Cl} = 50.5$ kg/kmol
- $M_{CH_2Cl_2} = 85$ kg/kmol
- $M_{CHCl_3} = 119.5$ kg/kmol
- $M_{CCl_4} = 154$ kg/kmol
- $M_{HCl} = 36.5$ kg/kmol
- $M_{Cl_2} = 71$ kg/kmol
- $M_{CH_4} = 16$ kg/kmol

Mass flow of each component (per 100 kmol/h of product):

$$
\dot{m}_{CH_3Cl} = 10 \times 50.5 = 505 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{CH_2Cl_2} = 6 \times 85 = 510 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{CHCl_3} = 4 \times 119.5 = 478 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{CCl_4} = 2 \times 154 = 308 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{HCl} = 42 \times 36.5 = 1533 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{Cl_2} = 28 \times 71 = 1988 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{CH_4} = 8 \times 16 = 128 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Total mass flowrate of product:**

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = 505 + 510 + 478 + 308 + 1533 + 1988 + 128 = 5450 \text{ kg/h}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall in this question:** Students often forget that for this reaction, the *total number of moles changes* (4 reactants → 4 products per elementary step, but here net it is conserved because every Cl₂ consumed yields one HCl and one product in steps 2–4, and step 1 yields one CH₃Cl and one HCl). Always verify with a **total mole balance** before assuming F_feed = F_product.

---

### **Question B-B. [14 Marks, Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**A continuous evaporator is used to concentrate a fruit juice from 12 % total solids (by mass) to 28 % total solids (by mass).**

**(a) Draw a Process Flow Diagram and write the steady-state material balance equations. Identify clearly the unknown variables. [7 marks]**
**(b) For a feed rate of 5000 kg/h, calculate the kg/h of concentrated juice produced and the kg/h of water evaporated. [7 marks]**

---

#### **Solution (a) — PFD Sketch and Material Balances [7 marks]**

**PFD description (textual since drawing is on answer sheet):**

A feed stream $F$ (12 % solids, 88 % water) enters the evaporator. A steam supply (utility, not counted in mass balance) supplies the heat of vaporization. The product stream $P$ (28 % solids, 72 % water) leaves the evaporator as concentrated juice. A vapour stream $V$ (pure water, by assumption of negligible entrainment) leaves overhead and is condensed (the condenser is a separate unit, not within the evaporator material balance boundary).

**Unknowns:** $F$, $P$, $V$ — three unknowns.

**Steady-state balances:**

1. **Total mass balance:** $F = P + V$
2. **Component (solids) balance:** $0.12 F = 0.28 P$
3. **Component (water) balance:** $0.88 F = 0.72 P + V$

(Note: equations 1 and 3 are not independent — any two of the three are sufficient.)

#### **Solution (b) — Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Basis:** 1 h of operation. Given $F = 5000$ kg/h.

**From the solids balance:**

$$
P = \dfrac{0.12 \times 5000}{0.28} = \dfrac{600}{0.28} = 2142.86 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**From the total mass balance:**

$$
V = F - P = 5000 - 2142.86 = 2857.14 \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Verification using the water balance:**

Water in feed: $0.88 \times 5000 = 4400$ kg/h.
Water in product: $0.72 \times 2142.86 = 1542.86$ kg/h.
Water in vapour: $4400 - 1542.86 = 2857.14$ kg/h ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner pitfall:** Many students forget to **draw the PFD** in part (a) and lose 2 marks. The KTU 2024 scheme values *graphical representation* as much as the algebraic equations. A neatly labelled box with three streams is enough — perfection is not required, but presence is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist the night before the KTU exam to consolidate Module 1.**

- [ ] The CPI transforms **raw materials → intermediates → products** using **unit operations (physical)** and **unit processes (chemical)**.
- [ ] **Always state the calculation basis** at the start of any numerical problem (1 kmol, 100 kg, 1 h, etc.).
- [ ] **Mass fractions sum to 1**; **mole fractions sum to 1**; both are dimensionless.
- [ ] The conversion formulae: $w_i = \dfrac{x_i M_i}{\sum_j x_j M_j}$ and $x_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j w_j / M_j}$.
- [ ] Average molecular weight: $M_{avg} = \sum_i x_i M_i$ (mole-fraction basis).
- [ ] For gas mixtures, **mole % = volume %** (Avogadro's law).
- [ ] **Ideal gas law:** $PV = nRT$ with $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) for SI or $0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K) for CGS.
- [ ] **STP** = 22.414 m³/kmol; **NTP** = 24.054 m³/kmol. Use the value the question gives.
- [ ] **BFD < PFD < P&ID** in increasing level of detail.
- [ ] **Batch, Continuous (Steady-state), Semi-batch, Transient** are the four operating modes.
- [ ] **Conversion (X)**, **Yield (Y)**, and **Selectivity (S)** are the three KPI's of reactor performance.
- [ ] Recycle and purge are essential to a real chemical plant — they appear in almost every later module.
- [ ] **Steady-state assumption** simplifies the general material balance to: Input + Generation = Output + Consumption.
- [ ] **Always include units** in the final answer — KTU deducts ½ mark per missing unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 is the foundation.** Mastering the *terminology* and *conventions* in this module saves you from making silly mistakes in the more complex material and energy balance modules that follow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unit Operations and Unit Processes, Units and Dimensions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Unit Operations and Unit Processes, Units and Dimensions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Unit Operations

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
A **Unit Operation** is a basic step in a chemical process in which a physical change is effected (without any change in chemical composition) and which is common to many chemical industries. The concept was pioneered by **Arthur D. Little (1916)** and systematized by **Walker, Lewis, McAdams, and Gilliland**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Unit Operations involve ONLY physical changes — no chemical reaction, no change in chemical identity, no new substances formed. Examples: distillation, filtration, evaporation, drying, crystallization, heat exchange, fluid transport.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine you have a cup of saltwater. To get pure water, you can **boil** it (evaporation), **condense** the steam (condensation), or **freeze** it partially. None of these steps *chemically alters* the water or salt — you still have H₂O and NaCl. These are all **unit operations**. The salt never "becomes" something else.

**Examples of Unit Operations (Industry-Standard Classification):**

| Category | Specific Operations |
|----------|---------------------|
| Fluid Mechanics | Fluid transport, filtration, mixing, sedimentation |
| Heat Transfer | Heat exchange, evaporation, condensation, boiling |
| Mass Transfer | Distillation, absorption, drying, extraction, leaching, crystallization |
| Mechanical | Size reduction, screening, conveying |

---

### 1.2 Unit Processes

**Formal Definition:**
A **Unit Process** is one in which a definite chemical change is effected, involving the transformation of molecular structure, formation of new compounds, and change in chemical identity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction from Unit Operations:** Unit Processes change the *chemistry* (molecular bonds rearrange). Unit Operations change only the *physical state* (phase, size, temperature, pressure).

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Take a piece of wood and burn it. The wood (cellulose) becomes ash, CO₂, water vapor, and heat. The *molecules themselves rearranged* — this is a **unit process** (specifically, **combustion**). In contrast, *chopping* the wood into small pieces is a **unit operation** (size reduction) — the wood is still wood.

**Common Unit Processes:**
- **Oxidation** (e.g., H₂S → SO₂)
- **Reduction** (e.g., Fe₂O₃ → Fe)
- **Nitration** (e.g., benzene → nitrobenzene)
- **Esterification** (acid + alcohol → ester)
- **Polymerization** (monomer → polymer)
- **Cracking, Alkylation, Hydrolysis, Hydrogenation**

---

### 1.3 Units and Dimensions

**Definitions:**
- **Unit:** A reference standard of measurement (e.g., **meter, kilogram, second**). The magnitude of a physical quantity is expressed as a number times a unit.
- **Dimension:** The qualitative nature of a physical quantity (e.g., Length **[L]**, Mass **[M]**, Time **[t]**). Dimensions are independent of the chosen unit system.

**Primary (Fundamental) Dimensions in Chemical Engineering:**

| Dimension | SI Symbol | SI Unit | Symbol |
|-----------|-----------|---------|--------|
| Mass | M | kilogram | kg |
| Length | L | metre | m |
| Time | t (or T) | second | s |
| Temperature | T | kelvin | K |
| Amount of substance | N (or n) | mole | mol |
| Electric current | I (or A) | ampere | A |
| Luminous intensity | $I_v$ | candela | cd |

**Why this matters:** KTU Process Calculations requires **SI units as the standard**, but in the chemical industry (especially in the USA), **CGS, FPS, and CGS-metric** systems are still used. Therefore, **unit conversion** and **dimensional analysis** are non-negotiable skills.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The most common marks-loss area in Module 1 is unit inconsistency. Always reduce to base SI units before any calculation, and verify dimensional homogeneity of your final equation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic: Why Two Categories?

The division into **Unit Operations** and **Unit Processes** arose because most chemical processes, however complex, can be broken into a finite set of these building blocks. This **reductionist approach** allows:

1. **Transferability** — A heat exchanger designed for an oil refinery works similarly in a dairy plant.
2. **Standardization** — Design equations (e.g., heat transfer coefficient) apply universally.
3. **Modular teaching** — Each block can be studied, designed, and optimized independently.

### 2.2 SI Base Units, Derived Units & Multiples

**SI Derived Units (with Special Names) — Critical for KTU:**

| Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Symbol |
|----------|---------|---------|--------|
| Force | $M \cdot L / t^2$ | newton | N = kg·m/s² |
| Energy / Work | $F \cdot L$ | joule | J = N·m |
| Power | $E / t$ | watt | W = J/s |
| Pressure | $F / A$ | pascal | Pa = N/m² |
| Frequency | $1/t$ | hertz | Hz = 1/s |
| Volume (derived) | $L^3$ | cubic metre | m³ |
| Density (derived) | $M / L^3$ | kg/m³ | — |
| Viscosity (dynamic) | $\mu$ | pascal-second | Pa·s |
| Surface tension | $\gamma$ | newton per metre | N/m |
| Heat capacity | — | joule per kelvin | J/K |

**SI Prefixes (Memorize — Direct KTU 3-Mark Question):**

| Prefix | Symbol | Factor | Prefix | Symbol | Factor |
|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| tera | T | $10^{12}$ | centi | c | $10^{-2}$ |
| giga | G | $10^{9}$ | milli | m | $10^{-3}$ |
