| mega | M | $10^{6}$ | micro | µ | $10^{-6}$ |
| kilo | k | $10^{3}$ | nano | n | $10^{-9}$ |
| deci | d | $10^{-1}$ | pico | p | $10^{-12}$ |

### 2.3 Dimensional Homogeneity Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dimensional Homogeneity Theorem (Rayleigh's Principle):** An equation is dimensionally homogeneous if and only if the dimensions of every term on both sides of the equation are identical. Only *dimensionless* constants or pure numbers (like π, e) may appear without dimensions.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. Express every physical quantity in terms of fundamental dimensions **[M, L, t, T]**.
2. For each additive term in the equation, ensure identical dimensions.
3. Multiply dimensionless groups freely; **add** or **subtract** only like-dimensioned terms.

### 2.4 Buckingham Pi (π) Theorem

**Formal Statement:** If a physical phenomenon involves **n** physical variables, expressible in terms of **k** fundamental dimensions, then the phenomenon can be described completely by a set of **(n − k)** independent dimensionless groups (called **π groups**).

**Algorithm (Standard KTU Method):**
1. List all **n** variables involved.
2. Determine the number of fundamental dimensions **k** (usually 3, 4, or 5).
3. Compute the number of π groups: **P = n − k**.
4. Choose **k** repeating variables (must collectively contain all dimensions and be dimensionally independent — no derived unit like Force, which is $M L t^{-2}$).
5. Form the π groups by combining each non-repeating variable with the repeating variables raised to unknown exponents.
6. Solve the simultaneous equations for the exponents.
7. Write final functional relationship: $f(\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_{P}) = 0$

> [!NOTE]
> **Choice of Repeating Variables — KTU Trap:** Never choose a dependent variable (e.g., the quantity you're trying to predict) as a repeating variable. Always include the dependent variable in the non-repeating list.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Dimension |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Dimensional form of a quantity | $[Q] = M^a L^b t^c T^d$ | — |
| 2 | Dimensional homogeneity | $[LHS] = [RHS]$ | — |
| 3 | Number of π groups | $P = n - k$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Density | $\rho = m/V$ | kg/m³ → $[M L^{-3}]$ |
| 5 | Specific gravity | $SG = \rho_{sub} / \rho_{water}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Force | $F = ma$ | N → $[M L t^{-2}]$ |
| 7 | Pressure | $P = F/A$ | Pa → $[M L^{-1} t^{-2}]$ |
| 8 | Energy | $E = F \cdot d$ | J → $[M L^2 t^{-2}]$ |
| 9 | Power | $P = E/t$ | W → $[M L^2 t^{-3}]$ |
| 10 | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu$ | Pa·s → $[M L^{-1} t^{-1}]$ |
| 11 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s → $[L^2 t^{-1}]$ |
| 12 | Surface tension | $\gamma = F/L$ | N/m → $[M t^{-2}]$ |
| 13 | Heat capacity | $C_p$ | J/(kg·K) → $[L^2 t^{-2} T^{-1}]$ |
| 14 | Thermal conductivity | $k$ | W/(m·K) → $[M L t^{-3} T^{-1}]$ |
| 15 | Mass flow rate | $\dot m$ | kg/s → $[M t^{-1}]$ |
| 16 | Volumetric flow rate | $\dot V$ | m³/s → $[L^3 t^{-1}]$ |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- Dimensional analysis lets us design scaled models (e.g., aerodynamic testing of cars in wind tunnels at 1/10 scale).
- It allows us to combine variables into **fewer dimensionless groups**, making experimental work tractable.
- It catches algebraic errors — if a formula gives a final answer with wrong dimensions, it is **wrong by definition**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Dimensional Formula of Pressure

**Starting Physical Definition:**
Pressure is defined as force per unit area.

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute force in terms of mass and acceleration.**
By Newton's Second Law, $F = m \cdot a$, where acceleration is rate of change of velocity:

$$F = m \cdot a = m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = m \cdot \frac{L}{t^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back into the pressure equation.**

$$P = \frac{m \cdot a}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Replace each quantity with its fundamental dimensions.**
- Mass $m \rightarrow M$
- Acceleration $a \rightarrow L \cdot t^{-2}$
- Area $A \rightarrow L^2$

**Step 4 — Simplify the exponent algebra.**

$$P = \frac{M \cdot L \cdot t^{-2}}{L^2} = M \cdot L^{1-2} \cdot t^{-2} = M \cdot L^{-1} \cdot t^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Final dimensional form.**

$$[P] = M L^{-1} t^{-2}$$

**Step 6 — Confirm dimensional homogeneity check.**
A standard atmospheric pressure is 101325 Pa. In fundamental SI: $kg \cdot m^{-1} \cdot s^{-2}$. Substituting into our derived expression: $1 \cdot 1^{-1} \cdot 1^{-2} = 1$. The dimensions match.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Dimensional Formula of Energy (Work)

**Starting Definition:**

$$E = F \cdot d$$

Force from Newton's Second Law (derived above): $[F] = M L t^{-2}$
Displacement: $[d] = L$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$[E] = [F] \cdot [d] = (M L t^{-2}) \cdot (L)$$

**Simplify:**

$$[E] = M \cdot L^{1+1} \cdot t^{-2} = M L^2 t^{-2}$$

**Conversion logic:** Energy is dimensionally "mass times length squared divided by time squared" — the same form as **kinetic energy** $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$, which has the same dimensions as we just derived. This is a self-consistency check.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Dimensional Formula of Power

**Starting Definition:**

$$P_{wr} = \frac{E}{t}$$

**Substitute energy and time dimensions:**

$$[P_{wr}] = \frac{M L^2 t^{-2}}{t} = M L^2 t^{-3}$$

**Logical interpretation:** Power has one extra "1/t" compared to energy. This is intuitive because power measures how *fast* energy is delivered per unit time.

---

### 3.4 Complete Worked Example: Buckingham Pi Theorem

**Problem Statement:**
The pressure drop $\Delta P$ in a fluid flowing through a pipe depends on: pipe diameter $D$, pipe length $L$, fluid velocity $v$, fluid density $\rho$, and fluid viscosity $\mu$. Use Buckingham Pi theorem to obtain the dimensionless groups.

**Step 1 — Identify all variables.**

$$n = 6 \text{ variables: } \Delta P, D, L, v, \rho, \mu$$

**Step 2 — Identify fundamental dimensions.**
Each variable's dimensional form:
- $[\Delta P] = M L^{-1} t^{-2}$
- $[D] = L$
- $[L] = L$
- $[v] = L t^{-1}$
- $[\rho] = M L^{-3}$
- $[\mu] = M L^{-1} t^{-1}$

Fundamental dimensions present: $M, L, t$, so $k = 3$.

**Step 3 — Number of π groups.**

$$P = n - k = 6 - 3 = 3$$

So we expect **3 dimensionless groups**.

**Step 4 — Choose repeating variables.**
Standard choice: $D, v, \rho$. These collectively contain $M, L, t$ and are dimensionally independent.

**Step 5 — Form the π groups.**
The non-repeating variables are: $\Delta P, L, \mu$.

**π₁ (with $\Delta P$):**

$$\pi_1 = \Delta P \cdot D^a \cdot v^b \cdot \rho^c$$

Substitute dimensions:

$$M^0 L^0 t^0 = (M L^{-1} t^{-2}) \cdot (L)^a \cdot (L t^{-1})^b \cdot (M L^{-3})^c$$

Expand:

$$M^0 L^0 t^0 = M^{1+c} \cdot L^{-1+a+b-3c} \cdot t^{-2-b}$$

Equate exponents:
- $M$: $1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$
- $t$: $-2 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$
- $L$: $-1 + a + b - 3c = 0 \Rightarrow -1 + a - 2 - 3(-1) = 0 \Rightarrow a = 0$

Therefore:

$$\pi_1 = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho v^2}$$

This is the **Euler number** (or pressure coefficient).

**π₂ (with $L$):**

$$\pi_2 = L \cdot D^a \cdot v^b \cdot \rho^c$$

$$M^0 L^0 t^0 = (L) \cdot (L)^a \cdot (L t^{-1})^b \cdot (M L^{-3})^c$$

Expand:

$$= M^c \cdot L^{1+a+b-3c} \cdot t^{-b}$$

Equate exponents:
- $M$: $c = 0$
- $t$: $-b = 0 \Rightarrow b = 0$
- $L$: $1 + a + 0 - 0 = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$

Therefore:

$$\pi_2 = \frac{L}{D}$$

This is the **L/D ratio** (geometric similarity parameter).

**π₃ (with $\mu$):**

$$\pi_3 = \mu \cdot D^a \cdot v^b \cdot \rho^c$$

$$M^0 L^0 t^0 = (M L^{-1} t^{-1}) \cdot (L)^a \cdot (L t^{-1})^b \cdot (M L^{-3})^c$$

Expand:

$$= M^{1+c} \cdot L^{-1+a+b-3c} \cdot t^{-1-b}$$

Equate exponents:
- $M$: $1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$
- $t$: $-1 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$
- $L$: $-1 + a + b - 3c = 0 \Rightarrow -1 + a - 1 + 3 = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$

Therefore:

$$\pi_3 = \frac{\mu}{\rho v D} = \frac{1}{Re}$$

This is the **inverse of the Reynolds number**.

**Step 6 — Final functional relationship:**

$$f\left(\frac{\Delta P}{\rho v^2}, \frac{L}{D}, \frac{1}{Re}\right) = 0$$

Or equivalently:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho v^2} = \phi\left(\frac{L}{D}, Re\right)$$

This is the classical **Fanning/Darcy friction factor equation** used universally in chemical engineering pipe-flow design.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Unit Operations vs Unit Processes

| Feature | Unit Operation | Unit Process |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| Type of change | Physical | Chemical |
| Molecular rearrangement | None | Yes |
| New chemical species formed | No | Yes |
| Energy source (typical) | Mechanical / thermal | Chemical (reaction enthalpy) |
| Governing equations | Mass/heat/momentum transfer | Reaction kinetics, stoichiometry |
| Examples | Distillation, drying, filtration | Oxidation, nitration, polymerization |
| Scale-up driver | Transport phenomena | Reaction engineering (kinetics) |
| Treated in course (ChE B.Tech) | Transport Processes, Separation | Reaction Engineering |

---

### 3.6 Unit Conversion Master Derivation

**Conversion between unit systems is done by multiplying by unit ratios that equal 1.**

**Example:** Convert 1 atm to SI (Pa).

$$1 \text{ atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

To convert 5 atm:

$$5 \text{ atm} \times \frac{1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}}{1 \text{ atm}} = 5.06625 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

**Conversion logic:** The "atm" unit cancels, leaving only "Pa". Always label the units in the numerator and denominator of your conversion factor — never write a "naked number".

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart — Unit Operations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chemical Process Plant] --> B{Type of Step?}
    B -- Physical Change --> C[UNIT OPERATIONS]
    B -- Chemical Change --> D[UNIT PROCESSES]
    C --> E[Fluid Mechanics Ops]
    C --> F[Heat Transfer Ops]
    C --> G[Mass Transfer Ops]
    C --> H[Mechanical Ops]
    E --> E1[Fluid Transport]
    E --> E2[Filtration]
    E --> E3[Mixing]
    F --> F1[Heat Exchange]
    F --> F2[Evaporation]
    F --> F3[Condensation]
    G --> G1[Distillation]
    G --> G2[Absorption]
    G --> G3[Drying]
    G --> G4[Extraction]
    H --> H1[Size Reduction]
    H --> H2[Screening]
    H --> H3[Conveying]
    D --> D1[Oxidation]
    D --> D2[Reduction]
    D --> D3[Nitration]
    D --> D4[Polymerization]
    D --> D5[Esterification]
    D --> D6[Cracking]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: Dimensional Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[List n Physical Variables] --> P1[Identify Fundamental Dimensions k]
    P1 --> P2[Compute P equals n minus k]
    P2 --> P3[Select k Repeating Variables]
    P3 --> P4[Form P Pi Groups via Exponent Equations]
    P4 --> P5[Solve Simultaneous Equations for Exponents]
    P5 --> P6[Write Pi Groups]
    P6 --> P7[Express Functional Relationship f of Pi 1 to Pi P equals 0]
    P7 --> Out1[Experimentally Correlate Dimensionless Groups]
    Out1 --> Eng[Scaled Up Engineering Design]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map: SI Base Units and Derived Units

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Base[SI BASE UNITS] --> M[Mass: kg]
    Base --> Lg[Length: m]
    Base --> Tm[Time: s]
    Base --> Tp[Temperature: K]
    Base --> Mol[Amount: mol]
    Base --> I[Current: A]
    M --> Der[DERIVED UNITS]
    Lg --> Der
    Tm --> Der
    Tp --> Der
    Mol --> Der
    Der --> F[Force: N equals kg m s to power minus 2]
    Der --> E[Energy: J equals N m]
    Der --> Pr[Power: W equals J s to power minus 1]
    Der --> Pa[Pressure: Pa equals N m to power minus 2]
    Der --> H[Frequency: Hz equals s to power minus 1]
    Der --> V[Volume: m cubed]
    Der --> Rho[Density: kg per m cubed]
    Der --> Mu[Viscosity: Pa s]
    Der --> St[Surface Tension: N per m]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Analogy Diagram: Operation vs Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Salt[Raw Salt Water Mixture] --> Dist[Distillation Unit Operation]
    Dist --> FW[Pure Water]
    Dist --> DS[Dry Salt]
    Mon[Monomer Molecules] --> Pol[Polymerization Unit Process]
    Pol --> Ploy[Polymer Product]
    Pol --> Heat[Heat Released Reaction Exothermic]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between Unit Operations and Unit Processes with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark definition + 1 mark difference + 1 mark examples):**

| Aspect | Unit Operation | Unit Process |
|--------|---------------|--------------|
| Definition | Step involving only physical change | Step involving definite chemical change |
| New substances | Not formed | New chemical species formed |
| Driving force | Concentration, temperature, pressure gradient | Chemical reaction (molecular rearrangement) |
| Example 1 | Distillation (separation by boiling point) | Oxidation of SO₂ to SO₃ in H₂SO₄ manufacture |
| Example 2 | Filtration (solid-liquid separation) | Polymerization of ethylene to polyethylene |

**Key line for full marks:** *Unit operations change only the physical state (phase, size, temperature), while unit processes change the chemical identity of the species by breaking and forming molecular bonds.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**State the Buckingham Pi theorem. What is the significance of choosing "repeating variables"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Theorem (1.5 marks):** If a physical phenomenon depends on $n$ physical variables involving $k$ fundamental dimensions, the relationship between them can be expressed in terms of $(n - k)$ independent dimensionless groups (π terms).

**Significance of repeating variables (1.5 marks):**
- The $k$ repeating variables must collectively contain **all $k$ fundamental dimensions**.
- They must be **dimensionally independent** (no π group of one can be formed from the others).
- The **dependent variable** (the one to be predicted) must **NOT** be a repeating variable.
- They serve as a basis to which each non-repeating variable is non-dimensionalized.

---

### PART B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Dimensional Analysis Application**

**(a)** [7 Marks] The drag force $F_D$ on a sphere moving through a viscous fluid is known to depend on: viscosity $\mu$, density $\rho$, velocity $v$, and diameter $D$. Using Buckingham Pi theorem, find the dimensionless groups.

**(b)** [7 Marks] An experiment shows that the force is given by $F_D = K \mu^a v^b D^c$. Determine the values of $a$, $b$, $c$, and $K$ by dimensional analysis.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Buckingham Pi (7 marks):**

Variables: $F_D, \mu, \rho, v, D$ → $n = 5$

Dimensions:
- $[F_D] = M L t^{-2}$
- $[\mu] = M L^{-1} t^{-1}$
- $[\rho] = M L^{-3}$
- $[v] = L t^{-1}$
- $[D] = L$

Fundamental dimensions: $M, L, t$ → $k = 3$

Number of π groups: $P = 5 - 3 = 2$

Choose repeating variables: $\rho, v, D$ (dimensionally independent, contain all three base dimensions).

**Form π₁ (with $F_D$):**
$$\pi_1 = F_D \cdot \rho^a \cdot v^b \cdot D^c$$

$$M^0 L^0 t^0 = (M L t^{-2}) \cdot (M L^{-3})^a \cdot (L t^{-1})^b \cdot (L)^c$$

Exponent equations:
- $M$: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
- $t$: $-2 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -2$
- $L$: $1 - 3a + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + 3 - 2 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -2$

$$\pi_1 = \frac{F_D}{\rho v^2 D^2}$$

This is the **Drag Coefficient** $C_D$.

**Form π₂ (with $\mu$):**
$$\pi_2 = \mu \cdot \rho^a \cdot v^b \cdot D^c$$

$$M^0 L^0 t^0 = (M L^{-1} t^{-1}) \cdot (M L^{-3})^a \cdot (L t^{-1})^b \cdot (L)^c$$

Exponent equations:
- $M$: $1 + a = 0 \Rightarrow a = -1$
- $t$: $-1 - b = 0 \Rightarrow b = -1$
- $L$: $-1 - 3a + b + c = 0 \Rightarrow -1 + 3 - 1 + c = 0 \Rightarrow c = -1$

$$\pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho v D} = \frac{1}{Re}$$

This is the **inverse of Reynolds number**.

**Final relationship:**
$$\boxed{C_D = \phi(Re) \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{F_D}{\rho v^2 D^2} = \phi\left(\frac{\rho v D}{\mu}\right)}$$

**Part (b) — Determine Exponents and K (7 marks):**

Given: $F_D = K \mu^a v^b D^c$

Substitute dimensions:
$$M L t^{-2} = K (M L^{-1} t^{-1})^a (L t^{-1})^b (L)^c$$

$$M L t^{-2} = K \cdot M^a \cdot L^{-a+b+c} \cdot t^{-a-b}$$

Equate exponents:
- $M$: $1 = a \Rightarrow a = 1$
- $t$: $-2 = -a - b \Rightarrow -2 = -1 - b \Rightarrow b = 1$
- $L$: $1 = -a + b + c \Rightarrow 1 = -1 + 1 + c \Rightarrow c = 1$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{F_D = K \mu v D}$$

**Interpretation:** $K$ is a dimensionless constant, experimentally determined (e.g., for low Reynolds number Stokes' flow, $K = 3\pi$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Unit Systems and Conversions**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the following with SI units: (i) Dynamic viscosity, (ii) Surface tension, (iii) Power, (iv) Pressure, (v) Density, (vi) Energy, (vii) Frequency.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A chemical plant produces 5000 kg/h of a product. Convert this flow rate to: (i) kg/s, (ii) tonnes/day, (iii) lb/h, (iv) g/min. Given: 1 tonne = 1000 kg, 1 lb = 0.4536 kg.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Definitions and Units (7 marks, 1 mark each):**

| Quantity | Definition | SI Unit | Dimensional Form |
|----------|-----------|---------|------------------|
| Dynamic viscosity $\mu$ | Measure of internal friction in a fluid; tangential force per unit area per unit velocity gradient | Pa·s (or N·s/m²) | $M L^{-1} t^{-1}$ |
| Surface tension $\gamma$ | Force per unit length acting perpendicular to a line on the surface | N/m | $M t^{-2}$ |
| Power $P$ | Rate of doing work or transferring energy | W (or J/s) | $M L^2 t^{-3}$ |
| Pressure $P$ | Normal force per unit area | Pa (or N/m²) | $M L^{-1} t^{-2}$ |
| Density $\rho$ | Mass per unit volume of a substance | kg/m³ | $M L^{-3}$ |
| Energy $E$ | Capacity to do work | J (or N·m) | $M L^2 t^{-2}$ |
| Frequency $f$ | Number of cycles per unit time | Hz (or 1/s) | $t^{-1}$ |

**Part (b) — Unit Conversions (7 marks, 1.75 marks each):**

Given: $5000 \text{ kg/h}$

**(i) kg/s:**
$$5000 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1 \text{ h}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 1.389 \text{ kg/s}$$

**(ii) tonnes/day:**
$$5000 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{24 \text{ h}}{1 \text{ day}} \times \frac{1 \text{ tonne}}{1000 \text{ kg}} = 120 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**(iii) lb/h:**
$$5000 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1 \text{ lb}}{0.4536 \text{ kg}} = 11022.93 \text{ lb/h}$$

**(iv) g/min:**
$$5000 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{1 \text{ kg}} \times \frac{1 \text{ h}}{60 \text{ min}} = 83333.33 \text{ g/min}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully to Avoid 3-5 Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit cancellation must be EXPLICIT in your answer sheet.** Writing "1.389" without "kg/s" earns zero. Units are part of the answer, not optional labels.
> 2. **Conversion factors must be correctly placed in numerator/denominator** — many students accidentally invert them. Always write "× something / something" and let units cancel symbolically.
> 3. **In Buckingham Pi problems, do NOT choose the dependent variable (the one you are predicting) as a repeating variable.** If you do, you will get the wrong set of π groups and lose 4+ marks.
> 4. **Show all exponent equations clearly** — partial marks are awarded for setting up the system, not just the final answer.
> 5. **A "dimensionless" group must have zero net exponent for EVERY fundamental dimension** — verify each one before finalizing.
> 6. **Don't confuse "Unit Operation" with "Unit Process"** — definitions are routinely swapped by students, costing full marks on direct questions.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- [x] **Unit Operation** = Physical change only; **Unit Process** = Chemical change.
- [x] **SI base dimensions** for chemical engineering: $M, L, t, T, N$ (mass, length, time, temperature, amount of substance).
- [x] **Dimensional homogeneity** requires every additive term in an equation to have the same dimensions.
- [x] **Buckingham Pi theorem**: $P = n - k$ dimensionless groups, where $n$ is the number of variables and $k$ is the number of fundamental dimensions.
- [x] **Repeating variables** must: contain all dimensions, be dimensionally independent, and exclude the dependent variable.
- [x] **Standard SI derived units** to memorize: Force (N), Energy (J), Power (W), Pressure (Pa), Frequency (Hz).
- [x] **SI prefixes**: giga (G, $10^9$), mega (M, $10^6$), kilo (k, $10^3$), milli (m, $10^{-3}$), micro (µ, $10^{-6}$), nano (n, $10^{-9}$).
- [x] **Unit conversions** must show explicit cancellation of intermediate units.
- [x] **Dimensional formula of pressure**: $M L^{-1} t^{-2}$. **Energy**: $M L^2 t^{-2}$. **Power**: $M L^2 t^{-3}$. **Viscosity**: $M L^{-1} t^{-1}$.
- [x] **Real-world applications**: drag force on spheres, pipe flow friction, heat transfer correlations, fluidization, sedimentation — all rely on dimensionless groups.
- [x] **Common KTU traps**: (a) mixing up Operation vs Process; (b) forgetting that 1 atm = 101325 Pa; (c) not verifying final dimensionless groups; (d) not labeling units in numerical answers.
- [x] **Module 1 motto:** "Every equation tells a dimensional story — read it before solving."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion of Units
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conversion of Units

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Unit Conversion** is the process of multiplying or dividing a physical quantity by a numerical factor (called a **conversion factor**) to express the same quantity in a different system of units, without altering its physical magnitude or dimensional integrity.

In **Process Calculations (PCCHT205)**, unit conversion is the foundational skill upon which all mass balances, energy balances, and reactor design calculations are built. Every measurable quantity in chemical engineering — mass, moles, volume, pressure, temperature, energy, and flow rate — must be expressed in **dimensionally consistent units** before it enters a governing equation such as the ideal gas law, Bernoulli's equation, or a mass balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> *"Units, Dimensions, and Conversions — SI, CGS, FPS, and MKS systems; Fundamental and Derived units; Conversion of units for mass, length, time, area, volume, force, pressure, energy, power, temperature, and amount of substance."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Currency Exchange" Model

Imagine you have **₹100** in your pocket. The physical "value" you possess is identical whether you express it as:
- **₹100** (Indian Rupees)
- **$1.20** (US Dollars, assuming ₹83.33 = $1)
- **€1.10** (Euros)
- **1.42** (in some hypothetical gold-standard units)

The money is the same. Only the *numerical label* changes via a **conversion factor**. Unit conversion in chemical engineering is the **exact same idea** — only we exchange *physical units* (kg ↔ lb, K ↔ °C, Pa ↔ atm) instead of currencies.

The governing principle, known as the **Dimensional Homogeneity Principle**, states:

> *An equation is dimensionally valid only if the units on both sides of the equals sign are identical.*

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants to Memorize

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ or $n_A$ | $\mathbf{6.022 \times 10^{23}}$ | entities/mol |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | $\mathbf{8.314}$ | J/(mol·K) |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | $\mathbf{273.15}$ | K |
| Standard Pressure | $P_{std}$ | $\mathbf{101.325}$ | kPa |
| Standard Molar Volume | $V_m$ | $\mathbf{22.414}$ | L/mol (at STP) |
| 1 atm equivalent | — | $\mathbf{101.325}$ | kPa |
| 1 calorie | cal | $\mathbf{4.184}$ | J (exact) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Almost every numerical problem in PCCHT205 begins with a "given data" block in messy mixed units (e.g., *"A gas at 30 psia and 80°F..."*). Your **first step is always to convert everything to a consistent SI or CGS set** before applying any formula. Examiners often test this conversion skill independently for 3–7 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic of Unit Conversion — Step by Step

The conversion of any unit $U_1$ into another unit $U_2$ follows a strict, three-step logical protocol:

1. **Identify the given quantity** and its associated unit (e.g., 5 miles).
2. **Establish the equivalence relation** between the two units (e.g., 1 mile = 1.60934 km). This equivalence is itself a *conversion factor* that numerically equals **unity** (1).
3. **Multiply the given quantity by the conversion factor**, written as a fraction so that the unwanted unit cancels dimensionally, leaving only the desired unit.

This dimensional cancellation is the **"railway track"** of unit conversion. If the unit you wish to remove appears in both numerator and denominator, you can cancel it just like an algebraic variable.

### 2.1.1 The "Factor Label" Method (Dimensional Analysis)

The **Factor-Label Method** (also called the **Unit-Factor Method** or **Dimensional Analysis**) is the standard KTU-expected technique. It works because multiplying any quantity by a ratio equal to 1 leaves the quantity unchanged.

$$
Q_{new} = Q_{old} \times \left(\frac{U_2}{U_1}\right) \times \left(\frac{U_3}{U_2}\right) \times \cdots
$$

Each bracketed ratio is a *conversion factor*, and the unwanted units cancel pairwise along the chain.

## 2.2 Systems of Units

A **system of units** is a coherent set of base units from which all other units are derived. Chemical engineers encounter four major systems:

| System | Length | Mass | Time | Force | Used In |
|--------|--------|------|------|-------|---------|
| **SI** (Système International) | m | kg | s | N | Global standard, KTU default |
| **CGS** (Centimeter-Gram-Second) | cm | g | s | dyne | Older scientific literature |
| **FPS** (Foot-Pound-Second) | ft | lb | s | poundal | UK / US engineering legacy |
| **MKS** (Meter-Kilogram-Second) | m | kg | s | kgf | Older European engineering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024, the **SI system is the mandatory base** for all final answers. However, the *given* data in the question may be in *any* system — your job is to convert.

### 2.2.1 Fundamental (Base) SI Units

The **seven SI base units** are the indivisible building blocks of all other units:

| Quantity | SI Base Unit | Symbol |
|----------|--------------|--------|
| Length | meter | m |
| Mass | kilogram | kg |
| Time | second | s |
| Amount of substance | mole | mol |
| Temperature | kelvin | K |
| Electric current | ampere | A |
| Luminous intensity | candela | cd |

### 2.2.2 Derived SI Units

Derived units are formed by **multiplying or dividing** base units. Some have special names:

| Derived Quantity | Formula | SI Name | Symbol |
|------------------|---------|---------|--------|
| Force | kg·m/s² | newton | N |
| Pressure | N/m² | pascal | Pa |
| Energy | N·m | joule | J |
| Power | J/s | watt | W |
| Frequency | 1/s | hertz | Hz |
| Volume (SI accepted) | dm³ | liter | L |
| Molar mass | kg/mol | — | kg/mol |

## 2.3 Temperature Conversion — A Special Case

Temperature conversions are **NOT** simple multiplication. They involve a **shift** (offset) as well as a **scale factor**, because the zero points of Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin differ.

$$
T_K = T_{°C} + 273.15
$$

$$
T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9}\left(T_{°F} - 32\right)
$$

$$
T_K = \frac{5}{9}\left(T_{°F} - 32\right) + 273.15
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU error: applying a *ratio* conversion to temperature (e.g., multiplying °C by 1.8 to get °F). The correct relationship is $°F = 1.8 \times °C + 32$. **Always remember the "+32" offset.**

## 2.4 Chemical Engineering–Specific Conversions

The four most heavily tested unit-conversion classes in PCCHT205 are:

### 2.4.1 Mass ↔ Moles ↔ Molecules

$$
n = \frac{m}{M} \quad \text{and} \quad N = n \times N_A
$$

where $n$ = moles, $m$ = mass, $M$ = molar mass, $N$ = number of molecules, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol.

### 2.4.2 Volume ↔ Moles (Ideal Gas)

$$
PV = nRT \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \frac{PV}{RT}, \quad V = \frac{nRT}{P}
$$

Always ensure $P$ is in **Pa (or atm)**, $V$ in **m³ (or L)**, $T$ in **K**, and $R$ is **consistent with those units**.

### 2.4.3 Concentration Units

| Quantity | Definition | Symbol | Typical Units |
|----------|------------|--------|---------------|
| Molarity | moles solute per liter solution | M | mol/L |
| Molality | moles solute per kg solvent | m | mol/kg |
| Normality | equivalents per liter solution | N | eq/L |
| Mass fraction | mass of component / total mass | $w_i$ | dimensionless |
| Mole fraction | moles of component / total moles | $x_i$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> $1 \, M = 1 \, \text{mol/L} = 1000 \, \text{mol/m}^3$ in SI units. KTU questions frequently test this exact 1000× conversion.

### 2.4.4 Pressure Conversion Map

$$
1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar} = 760 \, \text{torr} = 760 \, \text{mmHg} = 14.696 \, \text{psia}
$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Conversion | Formula | Notes |
|---|------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Length: in → m | $1 \, \text{in} = 0.0254 \, \text{m}$ (exact) | Imperial → SI |
| 2 | Length: ft → m | $1 \, \text{ft} = 0.3048 \, \text{m}$ (exact) | Imperial → SI |
| 3 | Mass: lb → kg | $1 \, \text{lb} = 0.453592 \, \text{kg}$ (exact) | Avoirdupois pound |
| 4 | Mass: ton → kg | $1 \, \text{metric ton} = 1000 \, \text{kg}$ | — |
| 5 | Volume: gal → L | $1 \, \text{US gal} = 3.78541 \, \text{L}$ | US gallon (not UK!) |
| 6 | Volume: ft³ → m³ | $1 \, \text{ft}^3 = 0.0283168 \, \text{m}^3$ | Cubic factor |
| 7 | Force: lbf → N | $1 \, \text{lbf} = 4.44822 \, \text{N}$ | — |
| 8 | Pressure: psi → Pa | $1 \, \text{psi} = 6894.76 \, \text{Pa}$ | lbf/in² → N/m² |
| 9 | Energy: BTU → J | $1 \, \text{BTU} = 1055.06 \, \text{J}$ | — |
| 10 | Energy: cal → J | $1 \, \text{cal} = 4.184 \, \text{J}$ (exact) | Thermochemical calorie |
| 11 | Power: hp → W | $1 \, \text{hp} = 745.7 \, \text{W}$ | Mechanical horsepower |
| 12 | Temperature: °F → °C | $T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9}(T_{°F} - 32)$ | Offset required |
| 13 | Temperature: °C → K | $T_K = T_{°C} + 273.15$ | Offset required |
| 14 | Moles: g → mol | $n = \frac{m}{M}$ | $M$ in g/mol |
| 15 | Gas: P, V, T, n | $PV = nRT$ | Mind unit consistency for $R$ |
| 16 | Density: water at 4°C | $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 1 \, \text{g/cm}^3$ | Reference value |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In a **petrochemical refinery**, raw feedstock flow rates arrive as *barrels per day* (bbl/day), reactor temperatures are logged in *°F*, and pump discharge pressures are in *psig* — all must be seamlessly converted into SI (m³/s, K, Pa gauge) before the process simulation software (Aspen Plus, HYSYS) can solve the mass and energy balances. A single unit-conversion error in a heat exchanger can mis-size it by **30%**, costing crores in real capital.

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial standards like **ISA-5.1** (instrumentation symbols) and **API MPMS** (petroleum measurement) mandate SI units for new designs, but historical plant data and US-supplied equipment often arrive in legacy units — making conversion an everyday plant-engineer skill.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Temperature Has Both an Offset and a Scale

Temperature scales **Celsius (°C)**, **Fahrenheit (°F)**, and **Kelvin (K)** all measure the same physical quantity — average kinetic energy of molecules — but they choose **different zero points** and **different slope sizes**.

At standard atmospheric pressure:
- Water **freezes** at $0 \, °C = 32 \, °F = 273.15 \, K$
- Water **boils** at $100 \, °C = 212 \, °F = 373.15 \, K$

The number of degree units between freezing and boiling:
- Celsius: $212 - 32 = 180$ divisions
- Fahrenheit: $100 - 0 = 100$ divisions

This gives the **slope ratio** $\frac{180}{100} = \frac{9}{5}$ between Fahrenheit divisions and Celsius divisions.

Now we derive the °F → °C formula step by step.

**Step 1:** Let $T_C$ and $T_F$ be the same temperature on the two scales.

**Step 2:** Write the linear relation:

$$
\frac{T_F - 32}{180} = \frac{T_C - 0}{100}
$$

This is the statement: *the fractional distance of the temperature from the freezing point is the same on both scales.*

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply:

$$
100 \times (T_F - 32) = 180 \times T_C
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $T_C$:

$$
T_C = \frac{100}{180} \times (T_F - 32) = \frac{5}{9} \times (T_F - 32)
$$

**Final derived formula:**

$$
\boxed{T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9}\left(T_{°F} - 32\right)}
$$

**Step 5:** To convert °C to Kelvin, just shift the origin by 273.15:

$$
\boxed{T_K = T_{°C} + 273.15}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Conversion Between Pressure Units Using "1 atm" as the Hub

**Given definitions:**
- $1 \, \text{atm} = 760 \, \text{torr}$ (exact, by definition of torr)
- $1 \, \text{atm} = 14.696 \, \text{psia}$ (approximately, since 1 atm = 101.325 kPa and 1 psi = 6.89476 kPa)
- $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$ (exact)

**Derive the conversion 1 psi = ? kPa:**

$$
1 \, \text{atm} = 14.696 \, \text{psia} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}
$$

Therefore:

$$
1 \, \text{psi} = \frac{101.325 \, \text{kPa}}{14.696} = 6.8948 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Derive 1 bar → 1 atm:**

Since $1 \, \text{bar} = 100 \, \text{kPa}$ (exact) and $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$:

$$
1 \, \text{bar} = \frac{100}{101.325} \, \text{atm} = 0.98692 \, \text{atm}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — A Full KTU-Style Conversion Problem

**Problem (KTU-style, 14 marks):**
*A chemical plant stores 250 gallons of a liquid coolant (specific gravity = 1.25) at a temperature of 95°F. The coolant is loaded onto a tanker whose maximum capacity is 1.2 m³. The driver is told that the destination ambient temperature may drop to 5°C. The density of the coolant changes with temperature according to:*
$$
\rho = \rho_{20} \left[1 - 0.0007 (T - 20)\right] \quad \text{with } T \text{ in } °C
$$
*Determine:*
*(a) The mass of the coolant stored (in kg).*
*(b) Whether the entire load will fit in the tanker at 5°C, showing all unit conversion steps.*

### Solution

#### Part (a): Mass of the Coolant Stored

**Step 1 — Convert volume from US gallons to m³:**

We use the chain of conversion factors:

$$
1 \, \text{US gal} = 3.78541 \, \text{L} = 3.78541 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
V = 250 \, \text{gal} \times \left(\frac{3.78541 \, \text{L}}{1 \, \text{gal}}\right) \times \left(\frac{10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3}{1 \, \text{L}}\right)
$$

$$
V = 250 \times 3.78541 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3 = 0.946353 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Find the density of the coolant at storage temperature (95°F):**

First, convert 95°F to °C:

$$
T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9}(95 - 32) = \frac{5}{9} \times 63 = 35 \, °C
$$

Specific gravity (SG) of 1.25 means density is 1.25 times that of water at 4°C. Reference density of water is $\rho_{water, 4°C} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$.

So density at 20°C:

$$
\rho_{20} = 1.25 \times 1000 = 1250 \, \text{kg/m}^3
$$

Now apply the temperature-correction formula at $T = 35 \, °C$:

$$
\rho_{35} = 1250 \times \left[1 - 0.0007 \times (35 - 20)\right]
$$

$$
\rho_{35} = 1250 \times \left[1 - 0.0007 \times 15\right]
$$

$$
\rho_{35} = 1250 \times \left[1 - 0.0105\right]
$$

$$
\rho_{35} = 1250 \times 0.9895
$$

$$
\rho_{35} = 1236.875 \, \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass:**

$$
m = \rho \times V = 1236.875 \times 0.946353
$$

$$
m = 1170.41 \, \text{kg}
$$

**Answer (a):** The mass of coolant stored is **$m \approx 1170.41 \, \text{kg}$**.

#### Part (b): Will the Load Fit at 5°C?

**Step 1 — Compute the density at 5°C:**

$$
\rho_5 = 1250 \times \left[1 - 0.0007 \times (5 - 20)\right]
$$

$$
\rho_5 = 1250 \times \left[1 - 0.0007 \times (-15)\right]
$$

$$
\rho_5 = 1250 \times \left[1 + 0.0105\right]
$$

$$
\rho_5 = 1250 \times 1.0105
$$

$$
\rho_5 = 1263.125 \, \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the new volume required for the same mass (mass is conserved):**

$$
V_5 = \frac{m}{\rho_5} = \frac{1170.41}{1263.125}
$$

$$
V_5 = 0.92663 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to the tanker capacity (1.2 m³):**

$$
V_5 = 0.92663 \, \text{m}^3 \; < \; 1.2 \, \text{m}^3 \; \text{(tanker capacity)}
$$

**Answer (b):** **Yes, the entire load will fit**, with a safety margin of $1.2 - 0.9266 = 0.2734 \, \text{m}^3$ (≈22.8% spare volume).

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight:** Cooling a liquid generally *increases* its density (the molecules slow down and pack tighter), which means a fixed mass occupies a *smaller* volume. The numerical answer confirms this: the volume at 5°C is slightly less than at 35°C.

## 3.4 Conversion-Table Analytical Matrix

Use this quick-reference matrix when solving KTU problems. Pick the *row* of the *source* unit and the *column* of the *target* unit; multiply the source value by the factor in the cell.

**Pressure Conversion Matrix (Source × Factor = Target):**

| From \ To | Pa | kPa | bar | atm | psi | mmHg |
|-----------|-----|------|------|------|------|-------|
| 1 Pa | 1 | $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-5}$ | $9.869 \times 10^{-6}$ | $1.450 \times 10^{-4}$ | $7.501 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| 1 kPa | $10^{3}$ | 1 | $0.01$ | $9.869 \times 10^{-3}$ | $0.1450$ | $7.501$ |
| 1 bar | $10^{5}$ | $100$ | 1 | $0.9869$ | $14.504$ | $750.06$ |
| 1 atm | $101325$ | $101.325$ | $1.01325$ | 1 | $14.696$ | $760$ |
| 1 psi | $6894.76$ | $6.8948$ | $0.06895$ | $0.06805$ | 1 | $51.715$ |
| 1 mmHg | $133.32$ | $0.13332$ | $1.333 \times 10^{-3}$ | $1.316 \times 10^{-3}$ | $0.01934$ | 1 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Unit Conversion Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end logic of solving any unit-conversion problem** in a chemical engineering context — the same workflow examiners expect you to demonstrate in your answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read given quantity and its unit] --> B[Identify target unit required by the formula or problem]
    B --> C[Lookup or derive the equivalence factor between the two units]
    C --> D[Write the equivalence as a dimensionless ratio equal to ONE]
    D --> E[Multiply the given quantity by the ratio in factor-label form]
    E --> F[Cancel unwanted units along numerator and denominator]
    F --> G[Check dimensional homogeneity: only target unit survives]
    G --> H{Is the final unit in SI or as requested by the problem?}
    H -- NO --> I[Perform an intermediate conversion via a hub unit like SI base or 1 atm]
    I --> E
    H -- YES --> J[State the final numerical answer with the correct unit and significant figures]
    J --> K[END: Validate by sanity check e.g. order of magnitude]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reading Guide:** The diamond node **H** is the *decision gate* — if your unit is *not* yet in SI, you cycle back to step **E** through a *hub* unit (the SI base is the most reliable hub for chain conversions). This is the only safe way to handle multi-step unit changes without losing a factor.

## 4.2 Schematic: Hierarchy of Unit Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SI[Système International SI - Globally mandated]
    CGS[Centimeter Gram Second CGS - Scientific legacy]
    FPS[Foot Pound Second FPS - US engineering]
    MKS[Meter Kilogram Second MKS - European legacy]
    SI --> M1[meter m]
    SI --> M2[kilogram kg]
    SI --> M3[second s]
    SI --> M4[kelvin K]
    SI --> M5[mole mol]
    CGS --> N1[centimeter cm]
    CGS --> N2[gram g]
    CGS --> N3[second s]
    FPS --> O1[foot ft]
    FPS --> O2[pound lb]
    FPS --> O3[second s]
    MKS --> P1[meter m]
    MKS --> P2[kilogram kg]
    MKS --> P3[second s]
    M1 -.->|0.01| N1
    M2 -.->|0.001| N2
    O1 -.->|0.3048| M1
    O2 -.->|0.453592| M2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted lines represent *fixed conversion ratios* between the base units of different systems. These ratios are **exact** (e.g., $1 \, \text{in} = 0.0254 \, \text{m}$) or **measured constants** that the SI committee maintains.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Chemical-Engineering Quantity Conversions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MASS[Mass m kg] -->|divide by Molar Mass M kg per kmol| MOLES[Moles n kmol]
    MOLES -->|multiply by N_A 6.022e26 entities per kmol| MOLECULES[Molecules N dimensionless]
    MOLES -->|multiply by Molar Volume V_m m3 per kmol| VOLGAS[Gas Volume V m3 at STP]
    MASS -->|divide by Density rho kg per m3| VOLUME[Liquid Volume V m3]
    MOLES -->|multiply by M and divide by V| MOLARITY[Concentration M mol per L]
    MASS -->|divide by Total Mass| MF[Mass Fraction w_i]
    MOLES -->|divide by Total Moles| XF[Mole Fraction x_i]
    MASS -->|multiply by g and divide by Molecular Weight| WT[Weight in N or lbf]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This block diagram is the **master roadmap** for Module 1 of PCCHT205. Any KTU numerical that involves "convert 500 g of H₂SO₄ into moles, molecules, and liters of 2M solution" requires you to *traverse* this map, applying the correct conversion factor at each arrow.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. Define the term "conversion factor" as used in unit conversion. Give two examples relevant to chemical engineering. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
A *conversion factor* is a numerical ratio (equal to unity) that expresses the equivalence between two different units of the same physical quantity. It is constructed from a known equivalence relation so that the unwanted units cancel algebraically.

**Examples in chemical engineering:**
1. Pressure: $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$. Therefore, the conversion factor from atm to kPa is $\left(\frac{101.325 \, \text{kPa}}{1 \, \text{atm}}\right)$.
2. Mass–Moles: $1 \, \text{kmol of H}_2\text{O} = 18.015 \, \text{kg}$. The conversion factor from mass to moles is $\left(\frac{1 \, \text{kmol}}{18.015 \, \text{kg}}\right)$.

### Q2. State the principle of dimensional homogeneity. Why is it essential in chemical engineering calculations? **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **principle of dimensional homogeneity** states that in any valid physical equation, every term separated by "+", "−", or "=" must have the **same fundamental dimensions** (and therefore the same units).

It is essential in chemical engineering because:
1. It allows engineers to **detect errors** in derived equations by checking whether the units on both sides match.
2. It enables the **Factor-Label method** for unit conversion by ensuring units cancel in a well-defined algebraic manner.
3. It guarantees that **scale-up** of a process (e.g., from lab to plant) preserves the underlying physics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Conversion Problem
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Statement:**
A reactor operates at a pressure of **30 psia** and a temperature of **540 °R**. The feed stream contains **15 lb** of nitrogen gas (N₂, molecular weight = 28 lb/lbmol).

**Calculate:**
**(a)** The temperature in **°C** and the pressure in **kPa**. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** The volume occupied by the nitrogen in **m³** at the given conditions, using the ideal gas law with $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Temperature and pressure conversion.

**Step 1 — Convert 540 °R (Rankine) to °C.**

We use the relation:

$$
T_{°F} = T_{°R} - 459.67
$$

Therefore:

$$
T_{°F} = 540 - 459.67 = 80.33 \, °F
$$

Now convert °F → °C:

$$
T_{°C} = \frac{5}{9}\left(80.33 - 32\right) = \frac{5}{9} \times 48.33 = 26.85 \, °C
$$

**Step 2 — Convert 30 psia to kPa.**

$$
P = 30 \, \text{psia} \times \left(\frac{101.325 \, \text{kPa}}{14.696 \, \text{psia}}\right)
$$

$$
P = 30 \times 6.8948 = 206.84 \, \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert pressure to Pa and temperature to K (for use with $R = 8.314$):**

$$
P = 206.84 \times 10^3 \, \text{Pa} = 2.0684 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}
$$

$$
T = 26.85 + 273.15 = 300.00 \, \text{K}
$$

**Answer (a):** $T = 26.85 \, °C = 300.00 \, K$; $P = 206.84 \, \text{kPa} = 2.0684 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$

**Part (b):** Volume of nitrogen using $PV = nRT$.

**Step 1 — Compute the number of moles from the given mass (15 lb of N₂).**

We need to convert the mass from lb to kg, and use molar mass in g/mol (or kg/kmol).

$$
m = 15 \, \text{lb} \times \left(\frac{0.453592 \, \text{kg}}{1 \, \text{lb}}\right) = 6.8039 \, \text{kg}
$$

Molar mass of N₂ = $28 \, \text{g/mol} = 28 \, \text{kg/kmol}$.

$$
n = \frac{m}{M} = \frac{6.8039 \, \text{kg}}{28 \, \text{kg/kmol}} = 0.2430 \, \text{kmol} = 243.0 \, \text{mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$:**

$$
V = \frac{nRT}{P}
$$

$$
V = \frac{243.0 \, \text{mol} \times 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} \times 300.00 \, \text{K}}{2.0684 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
243.0 \times 8.314 \times 300.00 = 6.0610 \times 10^5 \, \text{J} = 6.0610 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa·m}^3
$$

(because $1 \, \text{J} = 1 \, \text{Pa·m}^3$)

Therefore:

$$
V = \frac{6.0610 \times 10^5}{2.0684 \times 10^5} = 2.930 \, \text{m}^3
$$

**Answer (b):** $V = 2.93 \, \text{m}^3$ of nitrogen at the given conditions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Conversion Problem
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Statement:**
A solution of **NaOH** has a concentration of **2.5 N** (normality). The molecular weight of NaOH is **40 g/mol**, and the density of the solution is **1.10 g/cm³**.

**Determine:**
**(a)** The molarity, mass fraction of NaOH, and the mass of NaOH present in **5 liters** of the solution. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** Express the density in **kg/m³** and **lb/ft³**, and determine the volume in **ft³** occupied by the 5-liter sample. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** Concentration conversions.

**Step 1 — Normality to Molarity for NaOH.**

For NaOH, the *n-factor* (equivalents per mole) = 1 (NaOH provides exactly one OH⁻ ion). Therefore:

$$
M = \frac{N}{\text{n-factor}} = \frac{2.5}{1} = 2.5 \, \text{M} = 2.5 \, \text{mol/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of NaOH in 5 L of solution.**

$$
n_{NaOH} = M \times V = 2.5 \, \text{mol/L} \times 5 \, \text{L} = 12.5 \, \text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{NaOH} = n \times M_w = 12.5 \, \text{mol} \times 40 \, \text{g/mol} = 500 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass fraction of NaOH.**

Mass of 5 L of solution:

$$
m_{soln} = V \times \rho = 5000 \, \text{cm}^3 \times 1.10 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 5500 \, \text{g}
$$

Mass fraction:

$$
w_{NaOH} = \frac{500}{5500} = 0.0909 = 9.09\%
$$

**Answer (a):** Molarity = 2.5 M, Mass fraction = 0.0909, Mass of NaOH = 500 g.

**Part (b):** Density and volume conversions.

**Step 1 — Convert 1.10 g/cm³ to kg/m³:**

$$
1 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
\rho = 1.10 \times 1000 = 1100 \, \text{kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert 1.10 g/cm³ to lb/ft³:**

Use the chain:

$$
1 \, \text{g} = \frac{1}{453.592} \, \text{lb} = 2.2046 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{lb}
$$

$$
1 \, \text{cm}^3 = \left(\frac{1}{30.48}\right)^3 \, \text{ft}^3 = 3.5315 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{ft}^3
$$

Therefore:

$$
1 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = \frac{2.2046 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{lb}}{3.5315 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{ft}^3} = 62.428 \, \text{lb/ft}^3
$$

So:

$$
\rho = 1.10 \times 62.428 = 68.67 \, \text{lb/ft}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of 5 L in ft³:**

$$
5 \, \text{L} = 5000 \, \text{cm}^3 = 5000 \times 3.5315 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{ft}^3 = 0.17657 \, \text{ft}^3
$$

**Answer (b):** $\rho = 1100 \, \text{kg/m}^3 = 68.67 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$; $V = 0.1766 \, \text{ft}^3$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature offsets forgotten:** Converting °F to °C without subtracting 32 first is the single most common 1-mark loss in PCCHT205.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $R$:** Using $R = 0.0821 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$ when the problem gives $P$ in Pa and $V$ in m³ — *all three* variables ($P$, $V$, $T$) must be consistent with the chosen $R$ value.
> 3. **Molar mass units:** Forgetting to convert g/mol to kg/mol (or vice versa) when $m$ and $M$ are in different unit systems leads to a factor of 1000 error in $n$.
> 4. **Normality vs. Molarity:** Molarity = Normality / n-factor, where n-factor depends on the specific reaction. For acids, it's the number of H⁺ donated; for bases, the number of OH⁻; for salts, the total positive/negative charge.
> 5. **Significant figures:** Final answer should match the precision of the *least precise* given quantity. Writing $V = 2.93 \, \text{m}^3$ when inputs have only 2 sig figs is acceptable; writing $V = 2.930203 \, \text{m}^3$ is not — it implies false precision.
> 6. **1 atm ≠ 1 bar:** $1 \, \text{bar} = 100 \, \text{kPa}$ exactly, but $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$. Forgetting this difference introduces a 1.3% error in pressure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Unit conversion is the foundation** of every calculation in PCCHT205; always convert all data to a consistent system (preferably SI) before applying any formula.
- **Factor-Label method** is the gold standard — write the conversion as a ratio equal to 1 and cancel units dimensionally.
- **Seven SI base units** to remember: **m, kg, s, mol, K, A, cd**.
- **Pressure hub:** $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar} = 760 \, \text{mmHg} = 14.696 \, \text{psi}$.
- **Temperature conversion ALWAYS involves an offset** — never a simple ratio. °F → °C needs *both* the $\frac{5}{9}$ scale and the $-32$ shift.
- **Mole conversions** are the gateway to stoichiometry: $n = m/M$, $N = n \cdot N_A$, $V = nRT/P$ (ideal gas).
- **Concentration trio:** Molarity (M, mol/L) ↔ Molality (m, mol/kg solvent) ↔ Normality (N, eq/L). They are NOT equal; M and N coincide only when n-factor = 1.
- **MKS ≠ SI:** MKS is a *subset* of SI (it lacks mol, A, cd). Always default to full SI for chemical engineering.
- **Density of water at 4°C = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³ = 62.43 lb/ft³** — anchor value for SG-based problems.
- **Watch significant figures** in your final answer; preserve the precision of the *least* precise given data.
- **Universal Gas Constant $R$ has 3 forms**, each tied to specific unit combinations:
  - $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} = 0.0821 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)} = 0.08314 \, \text{L·bar/(mol·K)}$
- **Avogadro's Number** is a dimensionless multiplier that bridges the macroscopic (moles) and microscopic (atoms/molecules) worlds: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{entities/mol}$.
- **For KTU ESE:** Always *show* the conversion factors explicitly in your answer sheet — do not skip them, even if they look obvious. Examiners award *method marks* for the conversion path itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion of Equations - Problems; Composition of Solids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conversion of Equations - Problems; Composition of Solids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Conversion of Equations — Formal Definition

In Chemical Process Calculations, every chemical stream — be it a feedstock, intermediate, or product — can be described using **multiple, mutually compatible units of composition**. The phrase *"Conversion of Equations"* refers to the **systematic mathematical procedure** by which the composition of a mixture (gas, liquid, or solid) is transformed from one system of expressing concentration into another, without altering the physical mass balance of the system.

The major composition systems covered in KTU Module 1 are:

1. **Mass fraction / Weight fraction ($w_i$)** — dimensionless, on a 0–1 scale or 0–100 % scale.
2. **Mole fraction ($y_i$ or $x_i$)** — dimensionless, ratio of moles.
3. **Mass percent (wt %)** — grams of component per 100 g of mixture.
4. **Mole percent (mol %)** — moles per 100 moles of mixture.
5. **Parts per million (ppm)** and **parts per billion (ppb)** — used for trace components.
6. **Concentration (mass/volume)** — g/L or kg/m³.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Conversion between *weight basis* and *mole basis* forms the **single most repeated numerical pattern** in the ESE (End Semester Evaluation) for PCCHT205. Mastery of the *average molecular weight* $M_{avg}$ is non-negotiable.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Why Do We Convert?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Classroom Currency Exchange"**
> Imagine your classroom has two currencies: "Marks" (out of 100, like weight %) and "Rank" (1st, 2nd, 3rd..., like mole fraction). A student who scores **90 marks** is not the same as the **1st ranker** if total marks differ between students. To compare fairly, you must convert marks → percentage (weight basis) OR convert ranks → proportion of class strength (mole basis). In chemical engineering, **mass basis** is like marks (depends on how heavy each molecule is), and **mole basis** is like rank (depends on how many molecules, regardless of weight). Engineers often *need* moles because **chemical reactions happen molecule-to-molecule**, while *material balances on plant* are easier in mass units.

### 1.3 Composition of Solids — Formal Definition

The phrase **Composition of Solids** in Module 1 specifically addresses how solid mixtures (ores, coal, fertilizers, food grains, sludges, catalysts, etc.) are described. The two principal sub-topics are:

| Concept | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Moisture Content (MC)** | Mass of water present per unit mass of **wet solid** or **dry solid**. |
| **Dry Basis vs. Wet Basis** | Two different reference frames for reporting solid composition. |
| **Proximate Analysis of Coal** | Empirical split into Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, Ash. |
| **Ultimate Analysis** | Elemental breakdown (C, H, O, N, S, Ash). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constant Reminder:** The molar mass of water is **$M_{H_2O} = 18.015$ kg/kmol**. The molar mass of dry air is **$M_{air} = 28.97$ kg/kmol**. Always carry 3 significant figures for exam precision.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Logic Behind Conversions

The entire conversion machinery rests on **two pillars**:

**Pillar 1 — Conservation of Mass (or moles):**  
The *absolute* mass (or moles) of component *i* does not change when we re-express the composition. Only the *denominator* changes.

**Pillar 2 — Average Molecular Weight ($M_{avg}$):**  
This is the *bridge* between mass basis and mole basis.

$$M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \cdot M_i \quad \text{[kg/kmol]}$$

where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component *i* and $M_i$ is its molar mass.

### 2.2 Conversion Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Derivation

To convert **mass fraction ($w_i$) → mole fraction ($y_i$)**:

1. Assume a **basis**: e.g., 1 kg (or 100 kg) of mixture.
2. Mass of component *i* = $w_i \times$ basis mass.
3. Moles of component *i* = $\dfrac{w_i}{M_i}$ per kg of mixture.
4. Total moles = $\sum \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}$.
5. Mole fraction $y_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum w_j / M_j}$.

The **inverse conversion** (mole → mass) uses:

$$w_i = \dfrac{y_i \cdot M_i}{\sum y_j \cdot M_j}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table. It carries 60–70 % of Module 1 marks.**

| # | Conversion | Formula | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass frac → Mole frac | $y_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j (w_j / M_j)}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Mole frac → Mass frac | $w_i = \dfrac{y_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_j (y_j \cdot M_j)}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Average Mol. Wt. (mass basis) | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_j (w_j / M_j)}$ | kg/kmol |
| 4 | Average Mol. Wt. (mole basis) | $M_{avg} = \sum_j y_j \cdot M_j$ | kg/kmol |
| 5 | wt % → Mole % | $\text{mol\%}_i = \dfrac{(\text{wt\%}_i / M_i)}{\sum_j (\text{wt\%}_j / M_j)} \times 100$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | mol % → wt % | $\text{wt\%}_i = \dfrac{(\text{mol\%}_i \cdot M_i)}{\sum_j (\text{mol\%}_j \cdot M_j)} \times 100$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | ppm (mass) → mole frac | $y_i = \dfrac{(\text{ppm}/10^6)}{M_i} \cdot M_{avg}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Moisture content (wet basis) | $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_w + m_d} \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Moisture content (dry basis) | $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_w}{m_d} \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Wet basis → Dry basis | $w_{db,i} = \dfrac{w_{wb,i}}{1 - w_{wb,water}}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Dry basis → Wet basis | $w_{wb,i} = w_{db,i} \cdot (1 - w_{wb,water})$ | dimensionless |
| 12 | Proximate coal sum check | Moisture + VM + FC + Ash = 100 % | % |
| 13 | Ultimate coal sum check | C + H + O + N + S + Ash = 100 % | % |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Reactor design** requires **mole basis** because reaction stoichiometry is governed by molecular count.
- **Heat & material balance on plants** uses **mass basis** because flow meters measure kg/hr, not kmol/hr.
- **Emission regulations** are quoted in **ppm or mg/Nm³** — both are mass-style trace units.
- **Coal-fired boiler design** uses *ultimate analysis* (for stoichiometric air) and *proximate analysis* (for combustion efficiency).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall Warning:** Students commonly confuse whether $w_i$ is on a *dry* or *wet* basis. Always read the problem statement for the word **"as-received," "air-dried," "oven-dried,"** or **"dry basis"**. This single word changes the entire calculation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation — Mass Fraction to Mole Fraction

Let us take a general mixture of $n$ components. Assume an arbitrary **basis of 1 kg of total mixture**.

- Mass of component *i* in 1 kg of mixture = $w_i$ kg.
- Number of moles of component *i* = $\dfrac{w_i}{M_i}$ kmol.
- Total moles in 1 kg of mixture = $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{w_j}{M_j}$ kmol.

By definition of mole fraction:

$$
y_i = \dfrac{\text{moles of } i}{\text{total moles}} = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j / M_j}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by the basis mass (1 kg) does **not change the ratio**, confirming the formula is basis-independent.

### 3.2 Inverse Derivation — Mole Fraction to Mass Fraction

Starting from the definition of mass fraction in a basis of 1 kmol of mixture:

- Moles of component *i* = $y_i$ kmol.
- Mass of component *i* = $y_i \cdot M_i$ kg.
- Total mass = $\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j \cdot M_j$ kg.

Therefore:

$$
w_i = \dfrac{y_i \cdot M_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j \cdot M_j}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solved Conversion Problems

> [!NOTE]
> Each problem below is solved **exhaustively**. No step is skipped. Model these solutions for your exam answer sheets.

---

#### Problem 3.3.A — Flue Gas Mole-to-Mass Conversion

**Statement:** A flue gas has the following composition on a mole basis: CO₂ = 14 %, CO = 1 %, O₂ = 5 %, N₂ = 80 %. Calculate (a) the average molecular weight, (b) the composition on a mass basis (wt %).

**Given:** Mole percent of CO₂, CO, O₂, N₂.  
**Molar masses (kg/kmol):** CO₂ = 44, CO = 28, O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28.

**Basis:** 100 kmol of flue gas.

**Step 1 — Tabulate moles and compute mass of each:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass of CO}_2 &= 14 \times 44 = 616 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mass of CO}   &= 1 \times 28 = 28 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mass of O}_2   &= 5 \times 32 = 160 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Mass of N}_2   &= 80 \times 28 = 2240 \text{ kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass and average molecular weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total mass} &= 616 + 28 + 160 + 2240 = 3044 \text{ kg} \\
M_{avg} &= \dfrac{3044 \text{ kg}}{100 \text{ kmol}} = 30.44 \text{ kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Weight percent of each:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{wt\% CO}_2 &= \dfrac{616}{3044} \times 100 = 20.24 \% \\
\text{wt\% CO}   &= \dfrac{28}{3044} \times 100 = 0.92 \% \\
\text{wt\% O}_2   &= \dfrac{160}{3044} \times 100 = 5.26 \% \\
\text{wt\% N}_2   &= \dfrac{2240}{3044} \times 100 = 73.58 \% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $20.24 + 0.92 + 5.26 + 73.58 = 100.00$ % ✓

---

#### Problem 3.3.B — Weight Basis to Mole Basis (Aqueous Solution)

**Statement:** An aqueous solution contains 25 wt % H₂SO₄ and 75 wt % H₂O. Find (a) the mole fraction of each, (b) average molecular weight, (c) composition in mol %.

**Given:** wt % H₂SO₄ = 25 %, wt % H₂O = 75 %.  
**Molar masses:** H₂SO₄ = 98 kg/kmol, H₂O = 18 kg/kmol.

**Basis:** 100 kg of solution.

**Step 1 — Mass and moles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2SO_4} &= 25 \text{ kg}, \quad n_{H_2SO_4} = \dfrac{25}{98} = 0.2551 \text{ kmol} \\
m_{H_2O} &= 75 \text{ kg}, \quad n_{H_2O} = \dfrac{75}{18} = 4.1667 \text{ kmol} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total moles and mole fractions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= 0.2551 + 4.1667 = 4.4218 \text{ kmol} \\
x_{H_2SO_4} &= \dfrac{0.2551}{4.4218} = 0.0577 \\
x_{H_2O} &= \dfrac{4.1667}{4.4218} = 0.9423 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Mole percent (multiply by 100):**

$$
\text{mol\% H}_2\text{SO}_4 = 5.77 \%, \quad \text{mol\% H}_2\text{O} = 94.23 \%
$$

**Step 4 — Average molecular weight:**

$$
M_{avg} = \dfrac{100 \text{ kg}}{4.4218 \text{ kmol}} = 22.62 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Cross-check using $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$:**

$$
M_{avg} = (0.0577)(98) + (0.9423)(18) = 5.6546 + 16.9614 = 22.62 \text{ kg/kmol} \ \checkmark
$$

---

#### Problem 3.3.C — Solid Composition: Wet Basis vs Dry Basis

**Statement:** A wet solid contains 30 % water (wet basis). Find (a) water content on a dry basis, (b) if 100 kg of this wet solid is dried to 10 % moisture (wet basis), how much water was removed?

**Step 1 — Wet basis to dry basis conversion:**

Wet basis: $w_{wb, water} = 0.30$, so dry solid fraction = $1 - 0.30 = 0.70$ kg dry solid / kg wet solid.

$$
w_{db, water} = \dfrac{w_{wb, water}}{1 - w_{wb, water}} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ or } 42.86 \%
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of dry solid in 100 kg of wet feed:**

$$
m_{dry} = 100 \times 0.70 = 70 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Final wet solid at 10 % moisture (wet basis):**

Dry solid fraction = $1 - 0.10 = 0.90$.

$$
\text{Total final mass} = \dfrac{70}{0.90} = 77.78 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Water removed:**

$$
\text{Water removed} = 100 - 77.78 = 22.22 \text{ kg}
$$

**Cross-check using mass balance:**  
Initial water = 30 kg, final water = 77.78 − 70 = 7.78 kg. Water removed = 30 − 7.78 = 22.22 kg ✓

---

#### Problem 3.3.D — Proximate Analysis of Coal

**Statement:** The proximate analysis of a coal sample is: Moisture 5 %, Volatile Matter 30 %, Fixed Carbon 50 %, Ash 15 %. If 1000 kg of this coal is fired with 30 % excess air, calculate the actual air supplied (in kmol).

**Step 1 — Dry coal composition (after moisture removal):**

Mass of dry coal = 1000 × (1 − 0.05) = 950 kg.

On dry basis: VM = 30/95 = 31.58 %, FC = 50/95 = 52.63 %, Ash = 15/95 = 15.79 %.

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric oxygen requirement (using ultimate analysis assumed for typical bituminous coal):**

For Module 1 problems, the *combustion equation* is typically simplified as:

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2, \quad 4\text{H} + \text{O}_2 \to 2\text{H}_2\text{O}, \quad \text{S} + \text{O}_2 \to \text{SO}_2
$$

For this problem, we directly use the **assumption that fixed carbon and volatile matter burn stoichiometrically** with O₂.

**Step 3 — Mass of FC and VM burned:**

Mass of FC = 1000 × 0.50 = 500 kg → kmol C = $500/12 = 41.67$ kmol.  
This requires 41.67 kmol O₂.

Mass of VM (assume as CH₄-equivalent, M = 16 kg/kmol) = 1000 × 0.30 = 300 kg → kmol = 300/16 = 18.75 kmol CH₄-equivalent.  
Combustion: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O → requires 2 × 18.75 = 37.5 kmol O₂.

**Step 4 — Total stoichiometric O₂:**

$$
\text{O}_2^{stoich} = 41.67 + 37.5 = 79.17 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical air (air is 21 % O₂ by volume):**

$$
\text{Air}^{stoich} = \dfrac{79.17}{0.21} = 377.0 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 6 — Actual air with 30 % excess:**

$$
\text{Air}^{actual} = 1.30 \times 377.0 = 490.1 \text{ kmol}
$$

---

#### Problem 3.3.E — ppm Concentration Conversion

**Statement:** A wastewater stream contains 250 ppm (by mass) of benzene. Express this concentration as (a) mass fraction, (b) mole fraction (assume M_benzene = 78 kg/kmol, M_water = 18 kg/kmol).

**Step 1 — Mass fraction:**

$$
w_{C_6H_6} = \dfrac{250}{10^6} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles per kg basis:**

Basis = 1 kg of wastewater.  
Mass of benzene = $2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ kg → moles = $\dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{78} = 3.205 \times 10^{-6}$ kmol.  
Mass of water ≈ 1 kg → moles = $1/18 = 0.05556$ kmol.

**Step 3 — Mole fraction:**

$$
x_{C_6H_6} = \dfrac{3.205 \times 10^{-6}}{0.05556 + 3.205 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 5.77 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Equivalent ppm (mole basis):**

$$
\text{ppm (mole)} = 5.77 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^6 = 57.7 \text{ ppm-mol}
$$

**Insight:** For heavy organics in water, *mass ppm* is always numerically **larger** than *mole ppm* because the heavy molecule dilutes to fewer moles.

---

#### Problem 3.3.F — Complete Coal Combustion with Orsat Composition

**Statement:** A coal has the following ultimate analysis: C = 72 %, H = 5 %, O = 8 %, N = 1.5 %, S = 1 %, Ash = 12.5 %. It is burned with 20 % excess air. Calculate (a) O₂ required per kg of coal, (b) Orsat analysis of flue gas.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ requirement (basis: 100 kg coal):**

For each element on 100 kg coal basis:

- C: 72 kg → $72/12 = 6$ kmol → O₂ required = 6 kmol.
- H₂ (from H): $5 \times 2 = 10$ kg H₂ → $10/2 = 5$ kmol H₂ → O₂ = $5/2 = 2.5$ kmol.
- S: 1 kg → $1/32 = 0.03125$ kmol → O₂ = 0.03125 kmol.
- O already present in coal: 8 kg → $8/32 = 0.25$ kmol O₂ (subtract).

**Step 2 — Net stoichiometric O₂:**

$$
\text{O}_2^{stoich} = 6 + 2.5 + 0.03125 - 0.25 = 8.281 \text{ kmol per 100 kg coal}
$$

Or per kg coal = 0.08281 kmol O₂/kg coal.

**Step 3 — Theoretical air:**

$$
\text{Air}^{stoich} = \dfrac{8.281}{0.21} = 39.43 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 4 — Actual air (20 % excess):**

$$
\text{Air}^{actual} = 1.20 \times 39.43 = 47.32 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 5 — Flue gas composition (Orsat basis — excludes excess O₂ surplus as dry gas):**

Dry products per 100 kg coal (excluding moisture):

- CO₂ = 6 kmol
- SO₂ = 0.03125 kmol
- N₂ from air = $47.32 \times 0.79 = 37.38$ kmol
- N₂ from coal = $1.5/28 = 0.0536$ kmol → Total N₂ = 37.43 kmol
- Excess O₂ = $0.20 \times 8.281 = 1.656$ kmol
- H₂O = 5 kmol (but Orsat basis excludes H₂O)

**Step 6 — Orsat (dry) composition:**

Total dry flue gas = $6 + 0.03125 + 37.43 + 1.656 = 45.12$ kmol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 &= \dfrac{6}{45.12} \times 100 = 13.30 \% \\
\text{SO}_2 &= \dfrac{0.03125}{45.12} \times 100 = 0.07 \% \\
\text{N}_2 &= \dfrac{37.43}{45.12} \times 100 = 82.96 \% \\
\text{O}_2 &= \dfrac{1.656}{45.12} \times 100 = 3.67 \% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum check:** $13.30 + 0.07 + 82.96 + 3.67 = 100.00$ % ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Composition Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Composition Data] --> B{Reference Basis?}
    B -- Mass Basis --> C[Apply w_i and Sum check]
    B -- Mole Basis --> D[Apply y_i and Sum check]
    C --> E[Compute M_avg via sum w_i/M_i]
    D --> F[Compute M_avg via sum y_i M_i]
    E --> G[Target: Mole Fraction y_i]
    F --> H[Target: Mass Fraction w_i]
    G --> I[Final Composition in mol %]
    H --> J[Final Composition in wt %]
    I --> K[Output for Reactor Design]
    J --> L[Output for Plant Material Balance]
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture — Solid Composition Reporting Frameworks

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wet Solid Sample] --> B[Weigh Wet Mass m_wet]
    B --> C{Oven Dry at 105 C}
    C -- Yes --> D[Weigh Dry Mass m_dry]
    C -- No --> E[Report as Wet Basis]
    D --> F[Compute m_water = m_wet - m_dry]
    F --> G[Moisture Wet Basis: m_w / m_wet x 100]
    F --> H[Moisture Dry Basis: m_w / m_dry x 100]
    G --> I[Plant Operations Report]
    H --> J[Drying Equipment Sizing Report]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Coal Analysis Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Coal Sample] --> B[Proximate Analysis]
    A --> C[Ultimate Analysis]
    B --> B1[Moisture]
    B --> B2[Volatile Matter]
    B --> B3[Fixed Carbon]
    B --> B4[Ash]
    C --> C1[Carbon]
    C --> C2[Hydrogen]
    C --> C3[Oxygen]
    C --> C4[Nitrogen]
    C --> C5[Sulfur]
    C --> C6[Ash]
    C --> D[Stoichiometric Air Calculation]
    B --> E[Combustion Efficiency]
    D --> F[Orsat Flue Gas Composition]
```

### 4.4 Block Architecture — Solving a Typical KTU Module 1 Numerical

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Exam Question]) --> Step1[Identify Basis]
    Step1 --> Step2[Tabulate Given: w_i or y_i and M_i]
    Step2 --> Step3{Conversion Direction?}
    Step3 -- w to y --> StepA1[Compute w_i / M_i for each]
    Step3 -- y to w --> StepB1[Compute y_i x M_i for each]
    StepA1 --> StepA2[Sum to get total moles per kg]
    StepB1 --> StepB2[Sum to get total mass per kmol]
    StepA2 --> StepA3[Divide numerator by sum to get y_i]
    StepB2 --> StepB3[Divide numerator by sum to get w_i]
    StepA3 --> StepA4[Convert to mol percent if needed]
    StepB3 --> StepB4[Convert to wt percent if needed]
    StepA4 --> Verify[Sum Check = 100 percent]
    StepB4 --> Verify
    Verify --> End([Final Answer])
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to use these diagrams in exam:** If a question asks you to "explain the steps," you can sketch the flow of the relevant block diagram in your answer sheet for **1–2 bonus marks** under the "presentation" criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Define *mole fraction* and *mass fraction*. State the formula to convert mass fraction to mole fraction for a multi-component mixture.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Mole fraction ($y_i$):** Ratio of the number of moles of component *i* to the total number of moles of all components in the mixture. It is dimensionless and lies between 0 and 1. **(1 mark)**
- **Mass fraction ($w_i$):** Ratio of the mass of component *i* to the total mass of the mixture. Also dimensionless and lies between 0 and 1. **(1 mark)**
- **Conversion formula:** $y_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (w_j / M_j)}$ where $M_i$ is the molar mass of component *i*. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
Distinguish between *wet basis* and *dry basis* moisture content of a solid. Why is dry basis preferred in dryer design?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Wet basis MC:** $MC_{wb} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{water} + m_{dry}} \times 100\%$. Mass of water per unit mass of *wet* solid. **(1 mark)**
- **Dry basis MC:** $MC_{db} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{m_{dry}} \times 100\%$. Mass of water per unit mass of *dry* solid. **(1 mark)**
- **Dry basis preference:** As drying proceeds, the dry solid mass remains constant whereas the wet basis denominator keeps shrinking, distorting rates. Dry basis gives a true measure of remaining water per unit of invariant dry solid, which is essential for mass-transfer calculations in dryer design. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**  
(a) A gaseous mixture has the following composition by volume: CH₄ = 30 %, C₂H₆ = 20 %, C₃H₈ = 10 %, CO₂ = 25 %, N₂ = 15 %. Calculate: (i) average molecular weight, (ii) composition in weight percent, (iii) density of the mixture at 1.2 atm and 35 °C. **[7 marks]**

(b) A wet solid ore contains 18 % moisture (wet basis). It is dried in a rotary dryer. After drying, the moisture content reduces to 3 % (wet basis). Calculate: (i) moisture content on dry basis initially and finally, (ii) mass of water removed per 1000 kg of original wet ore. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Given:** Mole % = vol % (ideal gas). Molar masses (kg/kmol): CH₄ = 16, C₂H₆ = 30, C₃H₈ = 44, CO₂ = 44, N₂ = 28.

**Basis:** 100 kmol of mixture.

**Step 1 — Tabulate masses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{CH_4} &= 30 \times 16 = 480 \text{ kg} \\
m_{C_2H_6} &= 20 \times 30 = 600 \text{ kg} \\
m_{C_3H_8} &= 10 \times 44 = 440 \text{ kg} \\
m_{CO_2} &= 25 \times 44 = 1100 \text{ kg} \\
m_{N_2} &= 15 \times 28 = 420 \text{ kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass and average molecular weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total mass} &= 480 + 600 + 440 + 1100 + 420 = 3040 \text{ kg} \\
M_{avg} &= \dfrac{3040}{100} = 30.40 \text{ kg/kmol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Weight percent:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{wt\% CH}_4 &= \dfrac{480}{3040} \times 100 = 15.79 \% \\
\text{wt\% C}_2\text{H}_6 &= \dfrac{600}{3040} \times 100 = 19.74 \% \\
\text{wt\% C}_3\text{H}_8 &= \dfrac{440}{3040} \times 100 = 14.47 \% \\
\text{wt\% CO}_2 &= \dfrac{1100}{3040} \times 100 = 36.18 \% \\
\text{wt\% N}_2 &= \dfrac{420}{3040} \times 100 = 13.82 \% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum check: $15.79 + 19.74 + 14.47 + 36.18 + 13.82 = 100.00$ % ✓

**Step 4 — Density using ideal gas law:**

$$
\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}
$$

With $P = 1.2$ atm, $T = 35 + 273 = 308$ K, $M_{avg} = 30.40$ kg/kmol, $R = 0.08206$ atm·m³/(kmol·K):

$$
\rho = \dfrac{1.2 \times 30.40}{0.08206 \times 308} = \dfrac{36.48}{25.27} = 1.443 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Initial moisture on dry basis:**

$$
MC_{db,initial} = \dfrac{18}{100 - 18} \times 100 = \dfrac{18}{82} \times 100 = 21.95\%
$$

**Step 2 — Final moisture on dry basis:**

$$
MC_{db,final} = \dfrac{3}{100 - 3} \times 100 = \dfrac{3}{97} \times 100 = 3.093\%
$$

**Step 3 — Dry ore mass (constant during drying):**

Basis: 1000 kg wet ore → dry ore = $1000 \times 0.82 = 820$ kg.

**Step 4 — Initial water mass:**

$$
m_{w,initial} = 1000 - 820 = 180 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Final water mass:**

Final wet mass = $820 / 0.97 = 845.36$ kg. Final water = $845.36 - 820 = 25.36$ kg.

**Step 6 — Water removed:**

$$
m_{w,removed} = 180 - 25.36 = 154.64 \text{ kg}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Internal Choice Set B]**  
(a) The ultimate analysis of a coal is: C = 78 %, H = 5 %, N = 1 %, S = 2 %, O = 6 %, Ash = 8 %. The coal is burned with 25 % excess air. Calculate: (i) the stoichiometric air required per kg of coal, (ii) the Orsat analysis of the flue gas (dry basis). **[7 marks]**

(b) A liquid mixture contains 40 wt % ethanol (C₂H₅OH) and 60 wt % water. Convert this composition to (i) mole fraction, (ii) mole percent, (iii) average molecular weight, and (iv) kg-moles of mixture per 1000 kg of mixture. **[7 marks]**

---

**Solution to Part (a):**

**Basis:** 100 kg coal.

**Molar masses:** C = 12, H₂ = 2, S = 32, O₂ = 32, N₂ = 28 kg/kmol.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ for each element:**

- C + O₂ → CO₂: 78 kg C = $78/12 = 6.5$ kmol C → O₂ required = 6.5 kmol.
- H₂ + 0.5 O₂ → H₂O: 5 kg H = $5/1 = 5$ kmol H atoms = $5/2 = 2.5$ kmol H₂ → O₂ required = 1.25 kmol.
- S + O₂ → SO₂: 2 kg S = $2/32 = 0.0625$ kmol → O₂ required = 0.0625 kmol.
- O already present in coal = 6 kg → $6/32 = 0.1875$ kmol O₂ (subtract).

**Step 2 — Net stoichiometric O₂:**

$$
\text{O}_2^{stoich} = 6.5 + 1.25 + 0.0625 - 0.1875 = 7.625 \text{ kmol per 100 kg coal}
$$

Per kg coal = 0.07625 kmol O₂/kg coal.

**Step 3 — Theoretical air:**

$$
\text{Air}^{stoich} = \dfrac{7.625}{0.21} = 36.31 \text{ kmol per 100 kg coal}
$$

**Per kg coal: 0.3631 kmol air/kg coal.**

**Step 4 — Actual air (25 % excess):**

$$
\text{Air}^{actual} = 1.25 \times 36.31 = 45.39 \text{ kmol per 100 kg coal}
$$

**Step 5 — Flue gas dry composition:**

- CO₂ produced = 6.5 kmol.
- SO₂ produced = 0.0625 kmol.
- N₂ from air = $45.39 \times 0.79 = 35.86$ kmol.
- N₂ from coal = $1/28 = 0.0357$ kmol → Total N₂ = 35.89 kmol.
- Excess O₂ = $0.25 \times 7.625 = 1.906$ kmol.
- H₂O formed = 2.5 kmol (excluded from Orsat).

**Step 6 — Total dry flue gas:**

$$
\text{Total dry} = 6.5 + 0.0625 + 35.89 + 1.906 = 44.36 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 7 — Orsat (mole %) composition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 &= \dfrac{6.5}{44.36} \times 100 = 14.65 \% \\
\text{SO}_2 &= \dfrac{0.0625}{44.36} \times 100 = 0.14 \% \\
\text{N}_2 &= \dfrac{35.89}{44.36} \times 100 = 80.91 \% \\
\text{O}_2 &= \dfrac{1.906}{44.36} \times 100 = 4.30 \% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum: $14.65 + 0.14 + 80.91 + 4.30 = 100.00$ % ✓

---

**Solution to Part (b):**

**Molar masses:** Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) = 46 kg/kmol, Water = 18 kg/kmol.

**Basis:** 100 kg of mixture.

**Step 1 — Moles of each:**

$$
n_{ethanol} = \dfrac{40}{46} = 0.8696 \text{ kmol}
$$

$$
n_{water} = \dfrac{60}{18} = 3.3333 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 2 — Total moles:**

$$
n_{total} = 0.8696 + 3.3333 = 4.2029 \text{ kmol}
$$

**Step 3 — Mole fractions:**

$$
x_{ethanol} = \dfrac{0.8696}{4.2029} = 0.2069
$$

$$
x_{water} = \dfrac{3.3333}{4.2029} = 0.7931
$$

**Step 4 — Mole percent:**

$$
\text{mol\% ethanol} = 20.69\%, \quad \text{mol\% water} = 79.31\%
$$

**Step 5 — Average molecular weight:**

$$
M_{avg} = \dfrac{100 \text{ kg}}{4.2029 \text{ kmol}} = 23.79 \text{ kg/kmol}
$$

**Cross-check:** $M_{avg} = (0.2069)(46) + (0.7931)(18) = 9.517 + 14.276 = 23.79$ kg/kmol ✓

**Step 6 — kmol per 1000 kg:**

$$
n = \dfrac{1000}{23.79} = 42.03 \text{ kmol}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark Loss Hotspots):**
> 
> 1. **Basis ambiguity:** Students often pick 1 kg for one step and 100 kg for another in the same problem. **Fix:** Declare basis ONCE at the top and stick to it.
> 2. **Forgetting the "−O" correction:** Coal contains bound oxygen. Always subtract it from the stoichiometric O₂ demand. **(–2 marks lost if missed)**
> 3. **Orsat ≠ Wet analysis:** Orsat is *dry* flue gas. Never include H₂O in Orsat mole %. **(–1 mark lost)**
> 4. **Mixing dry/wet basis:** A student sees "5 % moisture" and divides by dry mass, but the data is on wet basis. Always check the basis wording. **(–2 marks lost)**
> 5. **Not carrying units:** The KTU answer key deducts up to **0.5 marks per answer** if kmol is written as "mol" or kg is written as "g". Carry SI units throughout.
> 6. **Not summing to 100 %:** Examiners reward the verification line. Write "Sum check = 100.00 % ✓" at the end.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Module 1: Conversion of Equations & Composition of Solids):**

- [ ] **Mass fraction** is dimensionless; **Mole fraction** is dimensionless; both sum to **1** (or 100 %).
- [ ] The *bridge formula* between mass and mole is the **average molecular weight** $M_{avg}$.
- [ ] $M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum (w_i / M_i)}$ (when starting from mass basis).
- [ ] $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ (when starting from mole basis).
- [ ] For an ideal gas, **volume % = mole % = pressure %**.
- [ ] For liquids and solids, volume % is **not** mole % — convert via density and molar mass.
- [ ] **ppm (mass)** → $w_i = \text{ppm} / 10^6$.
