- [ ] **Wet basis MC** divides by *wet* mass; **dry basis MC** divides by *dry* mass.
- [ ] **Conversion:** $w_{db} = w_{wb} / (1 - w_{wb, water})$.
- [ ] **Proximate analysis** sums Moisture + VM + FC + Ash = 100 %.
- [ ] **Ultimate analysis** sums C + H + O + N + S + Ash = 100 %.
- [ ] **Stoichiometric O₂ for coal** = $\dfrac{m_C}{12} + \dfrac{m_H}{4} + \dfrac{m_S}{32} - \dfrac{m_O}{32}$ kmol per kg basis.
- [ ] **Theoretical air** = $O_2^{stoich} / 0.21$ (since dry air is 21 % O₂ by volume).
- [ ] **Excess air %** = $\dfrac{\text{Actual air} - \text{Theoretical air}}{\text{Theoretical air}} \times 100$.
- [ ] **Orsat analysis** = dry flue gas composition (excludes H₂O).
- [ ] Always perform a **sum-check** (composition must total 100 %) to catch arithmetic errors.
- [ ] Carry **SI units** (kg, kmol, m³, K) consistently throughout every calculation.
- [ ] When a problem says "as fired" or "as received," treat as **wet basis**.
- [ ] When a problem says "moisture-free" or "dry," treat as **dry basis**.
- [ ] Density of mixture (ideal gas) = $\dfrac{P M_{avg}}{RT}$ — uses kg/m³ if P in kPa and R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K).

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 1 Notes — Conversion of Equations & Composition of Solids**  
> You have now covered the two foundational numerical pillars of PCCHT205. Practise at least **8–10 numerical problems** of the type shown in Section 3.3 and Section 5.2 before your ESE. The conversion of equations is *mechanical* — it is solved by **practice**, not memorization.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Chemical Process Calculations**, the **Weight Percent** (also called **Mass Percent** or **Percent by Mass**) of a component in a mixture or solution is formally defined as the mass of that component divided by the total mass of the mixture, expressed as a percentage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Weight Percent* of a component $i$ in a solution, denoted $w_i$ (or wt %), is the number of mass units of component $i$ present in 100 mass units of the solution. It is a dimensionless quantity (although it carries the implicit units of mass/mass × 100%).

The mathematical expression is:

$$w_i \;(\%) = \frac{m_i}{m_{solution}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $m_i$ = mass of the component $i$
- $m_{solution} = m_{solution} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} m_j$ = total mass of the solution (sum of masses of all components)

The corresponding **weight fraction** (also called **mass fraction**), denoted $x_{w,i}$, is simply the decimal form:

$$x_{w,i} = \frac{m_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} m_j}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Property of Weight Fractions**
> The sum of the weight fractions of all components in a mixture is always equal to **unity (1)**:
>
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{w,i} = 1 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 100\%$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are preparing **chicken soup** for a dinner party. You drop **20 g of salt** into a large pot that ends up containing **1000 g of total soup** (salt + water + vegetables + chicken). To describe how "salty" the soup is, you would say it contains 20 g of salt per 1000 g of soup, or equivalently, **2 g of salt per 100 g of soup**. That "2 per 100" measure is precisely the **weight percent (2 wt %)** of salt.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Weight percent answers the question — "Out of every 100 kg of mixture, how many kg are the component of interest?"* This is the most common way industries report compositions for liquids because balances and scales directly measure mass.

## 1.3 Distinction Between Weight Percent and Other Concentration Units

| Concentration Term | Definition | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Weight Percent (wt %)** | Mass of solute per 100 mass units of solution | Liquids, solids, slurries |
| **Volume Percent (vol %)** | Volume of solute per 100 volume units of solution | Gases, liquid mixtures (ideal) |
| **Mole Percent (mol %)** | Moles of solute per 100 moles of mixture | Gases, reactions, VLE |
| **Molarity (M)** | Moles of solute per litre of solution | Lab analysis, aqueous solutions |
| **Molality (m)** | Moles of solute per kg of solvent | Colligative properties, electrochemistry |
| **ppm / ppb** | Parts per million / billion by mass | Trace impurities, environmental |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** For liquid solutions in chemical engineering practice, *weight percent* and *mole percent* are the two most frequently encountered basis-of-composition notations. Master the conversion between them — it is tested almost every semester.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The concept of weight percent rests on three foundational principles that every KTU student must internalize:

- **Principle 1 — Mass Conservation:** The total mass of a solution is the exact arithmetic sum of the masses of its individual components. No mass is created or destroyed during simple mixing (neglecting chemical reaction).
- **Principle 2 — Basis of Calculation:** Chemical engineers choose a convenient basis (e.g., 100 kg of solution, 1 kg of solute, 1 m³ of mixture) to simplify numerical work. The *basis* does not change the *composition*.
- **Principle 3 — Linearity with Mass:** Because weight percent is a *ratio of two masses*, it is independent of the total quantity considered. A 5 wt % NaOH solution has 5 kg NaOH per 100 kg solution, **or** 50 g NaOH per 1000 g solution — the proportion is invariant.

## 2.2 Composition of Multi-Component Solutions

For a solution containing $n$ components with masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$:

$$m_{total} = m_1 + m_2 + \cdots + m_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

The weight percent of component $i$ becomes:

$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{m_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} m_j} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why divide by the sum (and not by the solvent mass)?**
> Weight percent is defined on the *total solution* mass, not on the solvent mass. This distinguishes it from molality, which is based on the *solvent* mass. Confusing these two is a common KTU error.

## 2.3 Conversion: Weight Percent ↔ Mole Fraction

This is the **single most-tested conversion** in KTU Module 1. The procedure requires the **molar mass** of every component.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Assume a convenient basis — say 100 mass units of solution.
2. Each component $i$ contributes $w_i$ mass units.
3. Convert each mass to moles: $n_i = m_i / M_i = w_i / M_i$.
4. Total moles: $n_{total} = \sum_i (w_i / M_i)$.
5. Mole fraction: $x_i = n_i / n_{total} = (w_i / M_i) \Big/ \sum_j (w_j / M_j)$.

Compactly:

$$x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (w_j / M_j)}$$

The inverse (mole fraction → weight percent) uses the **mean molecular weight** $M_{avg}$:

$$w_i = \frac{x_i \, M_i}{M_{avg}}, \qquad M_{avg} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} x_j M_j$$

Equivalently, using weight fractions directly:

$$x_i = \frac{x_{w,i}/M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_{w,j}/M_j)}$$

## 2.4 Mean (Average) Molecular Weight of a Solution

The **mean molecular weight** $M_{avg}$ of a multi-component solution is the mass of one mole of the solution.

From mole fractions:

$$M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i M_i$$

From weight fractions (the more common form when wt % is given):

$$M_{avg} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( x_{w,i} / M_i \right)}$$

## 2.5 Parts Per Million (ppm) and Parts Per Billion (ppb) — Trace Concentrations

For very dilute solutions, weight percent becomes inconvenient (e.g., 0.0001 wt %). The same mass ratio is expressed as:

$$\text{ppm}_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{solution}} \times 10^{6} = w_i \times 10^{4}$$

$$\text{ppb}_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{solution}} \times 10^{9} = w_i \times 10^{7}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick conversion:** $1 \text{ wt \%} = 10{,}000 \text{ ppm} = 10^{7} \text{ ppb}$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}} \times 100$ | Weight percent of component $i$ | wt % (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $x_{w,i} = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}}$ | Weight (mass) fraction of $i$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sum_i x_{w,i} = 1$ | Closure condition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $x_i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum_j (w_j / M_j)}$ | wt % → mole fraction | dimensionless |
| 5 | $w_i = \dfrac{x_i M_i}{M_{avg}}$ | mole fraction → wt % | wt % |
| 6 | $M_{avg} = \sum_i x_i M_i$ | Mean mol. wt (from mol frac) | g/mol or kg/kmol |
| 7 | $M_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i (x_{w,i}/M_i)}$ | Mean mol. wt (from wt frac) | g/mol or kg/kmol |
| 8 | $\text{ppm} = w \times 10^{4}$ | wt % → ppm | ppm |
| 9 | $\text{ppb} = w \times 10^{7}$ | wt % → ppb | ppb |
| 10 | $m_{total} = \sum_i m_i$ | Additivity of mass | kg, g, lb, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Engineering Utility:** Weight percent is the *lingua franca* of industrial formulations — paint recipes, polymer blends, pharmaceutical syrups, petroleum assays (e.g., "sulphur content = 2.3 wt %"), and food chemistry all report compositions in wt %. The chemical engineer must be able to convert wt % ↔ mole fraction ↔ molarity without hesitation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion from Weight Fraction to Mole Fraction

**Statement:** Given a binary liquid mixture with components $A$ and $B$ having weight fractions $x_{w,A}$ and $x_{w,B}$ and molar masses $M_A$ and $M_B$, derive the mole fraction $x_A$.

**Starting premise:** Take a basis of **1 kg of solution**.

- Mass of $A$: $m_A = x_{w,A} \times 1$ kg $= x_{w,A}$ kg
- Mass of $B$: $m_B = x_{w,B} \times 1$ kg $= x_{w,B}$ kg

**Step 1 — Convert mass to moles** using $n = m / M$:

$$n_A = \frac{x_{w,A}}{M_A} \quad \text{(kmol of A per kg of solution, if } M \text{ in kg/kmol)}$$

$$n_B = \frac{x_{w,B}}{M_B} \quad \text{(kmol of B per kg of solution)}$$

**Step 2 — Total moles:**

$$n_{total} = n_A + n_B = \frac{x_{w,A}}{M_A} + \frac{x_{w,B}}{M_B}$$

**Step 3 — Mole fraction of A** is the ratio of moles of A to total moles:

$$x_A = \frac{n_A}{n_{total}} = \frac{\dfrac{x_{w,A}}{M_A}}{\dfrac{x_{w,A}}{M_A} + \dfrac{x_{w,B}}{M_B}}$$

**Step 4 — Generalise to $n$ components:**

$$
x_i = \frac{x_{w,i}/M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_{w,j}/M_j)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The denominator $\sum (x_{w,j}/M_j)$ is the reciprocal of the **mean molecular weight** $M_{avg}$. So equivalently:
>
> $$x_i = x_{w,i} \cdot \frac{M_{avg}}{M_i}$$
>
> This shortcut is invaluable for quick KTU problem-solving.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mean Molecular Weight from Weight Fractions

**Statement:** Prove that $M_{avg} = 1 \big/ \sum (x_{w,i}/M_i)$.

**Starting premise:** 1 kg of solution contains masses $x_{w,1}, x_{w,2}, \ldots, x_{w,n}$ kg of the respective components.

**Step 1 — Total moles in 1 kg of solution:**

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{x_{w,i}}{M_i}$$

**Step 2 — Mean molecular weight is the mass (1 kg) divided by total moles:**

$$
M_{avg} = \frac{1 \text{ kg}}{n_{total}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_{w,i}/M_i)}
$$

**Step 3 — Confirm by expanding** for a binary mixture:

$$
M_{avg} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{x_{w,A}}{M_A} + \dfrac{x_{w,B}}{M_B}} = \frac{M_A M_B}{x_{w,B} M_A + x_{w,A} M_B}
$$

This is the well-known **lever-rule harmonic mean** form.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Binary Liquid

> **Problem (KTU-style):** An aqueous solution of ethanol is reported as **12 wt % ethanol**. Calculate the mole fraction of ethanol. Given: $M_{ethanol} = 46 \text{ g/mol}$, $M_{water} = 18 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Solution:**

Take a basis of **100 g of solution**.

$$
m_{ethanol} = 12 \text{ g}, \quad m_{water} = 88 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 1 — Convert each mass to moles:**

$$
n_{ethanol} = \frac{12}{46} = 0.2609 \text{ mol}
$$

$$
n_{water} = \frac{88}{18} = 4.8889 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Total moles:**

$$
n_{total} = 0.2609 + 4.8889 = 5.1498 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Mole fraction of ethanol:**

$$
x_{ethanol} = \frac{0.2609}{5.1498} = 0.0507
$$

**Step 4 — Mole fraction of water:**

$$
x_{water} = 1 - 0.0507 = 0.9493
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** Because water is much lighter per mole (18 g/mol) than ethanol (46 g/mol), the **mass % of water (88%) is far larger than its mole % (94.9%)** — wait, actually the mole % is *also* larger because water is more abundant. The conversion direction is correct. Notice that a small wt % of a *heavy* molecule gives an even smaller mol %.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Three-Component System

> **Problem:** A solvent blend contains 40 wt % acetone, 35 wt % methanol, and 25 wt % water. Calculate the mole fraction of each component and the mean molecular weight. Given: $M_{acetone} = 58 \text{ g/mol}$, $M_{methanol} = 32 \text{ g/mol}$, $M_{water} = 18 \text{ g/mol}$.

**Solution:**

**Basis:** 100 g of solution.

| Component | wt | Mass (g) | $M$ (g/mol) | Moles (mol) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acetone | 0.40 | 40 | 58 | 0.6897 |
| Methanol | 0.35 | 35 | 32 | 1.0938 |
| Water | 0.25 | 25 | 18 | 1.3889 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | **100** | — | **3.1724** |

**Step 1 — Mole fractions:**

$$
x_{acetone} = \frac{0.6897}{3.1724} = 0.2174
$$

$$
x_{methanol} = \frac{1.0938}{3.1724} = 0.3448
$$

$$
x_{water} = \frac{1.3889}{3.1724} = 0.4378
$$

**Check:** $0.2174 + 0.3448 + 0.4378 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 2 — Mean molecular weight:**

$$
M_{avg} = \sum x_i M_i = (0.2174)(58) + (0.3448)(32) + (0.4378)(18)
$$

$$
M_{avg} = 12.61 + 11.03 + 7.88 = 31.52 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Alternative check (using weight-fraction formula):**

$$
M_{avg} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.40}{58} + \dfrac{0.35}{32} + \dfrac{0.25}{18}} = \frac{1}{0.006897 + 0.010938 + 0.013889} = \frac{1}{0.031724}
$$

$$
M_{avg} = 31.52 \text{ g/mol} \quad \checkmark
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — ppm Conversion

> **Problem:** The maximum allowable lead (Pb) content in drinking water is **0.010 mg/L** in a water of density 1.00 g/mL. Express this limit in (a) wt % and (b) ppm.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert to consistent units.** Per 1 L of water = 1000 mL = 1000 g of solution.

$$
m_{Pb} = 0.010 \text{ mg} = 0.010 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ g}
$$

$$
m_{solution} = 1000 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass fraction:**

$$
x_{w,Pb} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}{1000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to wt % and ppm:**

$$
w_{Pb} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 100 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\text{ppm}_{Pb} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{6} = 0.010 \text{ ppm}
$$

$$
\text{ppb}_{Pb} = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{9} = 10 \text{ ppb}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Realisation:** $1 \text{ mg/L}$ in water of density 1 g/mL is *exactly* **1 ppm**. This is a celebrated KTU shortcut for dilute aqueous systems.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Composition Reporting

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Liquid Mixture or Solution] --> B{Reporting Basis}
    B --> C[Mass / Weight Basis]
    B --> D[Volume Basis]
    B --> E[Mole Basis]
    B --> F[Particle Number Basis]
    C --> C1[Weight Fraction xw]
    C --> C2[Weight Percent wt%]
    C --> C3[ppm / ppb]
    D --> D1[Volume Fraction]
    D --> D2[Volume Percent]
    D --> D3[Molarity mol per L]
    E --> E1[Mole Fraction x]
    E --> E2[Mole Percent mol%]
    E --> E3[Molality mol per kg solvent]
    F --> F1[Number Concentration]
    C2 --> G{Conversion Needed?}
    E1 --> G
    G --> H[Use Molar Masses of each component]
    H --> I[Compute Mavg]
    I --> J[Final Required Basis]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Weight Percent Conversion Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Input: wt percent of all components] --> stage1[Stage 1: Choose basis - 100 kg solution]
    stage1 --> stage2[Stage 2: Extract mass of each component]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3: Look up molar mass Mi from data table]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 4: Compute moles ni = mi divided by Mi]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 5: Sum total moles]
    stage5 --> stage6[Stage 6: Mole fraction xi = ni divided by ntotal]
    stage6 --> stage7[Stage 7: Compute Mavg equals 1 over sum xw divided by Mi]
    stage7 --> output1[Output: Mole fraction and mean molecular weight]
    output1 --> verify{Sum of xi equals 1?}
    verify -->|Yes| done[Validated result]
    verify -->|No| debug[Recheck arithmetic and basis]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Weight Percent vs Mole Percent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What physical information do you have?] --> Q2{Balances on a reactor?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| MP[Use Mole Percent mol%]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Weighing on a scale or balance?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| WP[Use Weight Percent wt%]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Volumetric flow or liquid level?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| VP[Use Volume Percent or Molarity]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Trace contaminant below 100 ppm?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| PPM[Use ppm or ppb]
    Q5 -->|No| WP
```

## 4.4 Information-Flow Schematic — Mass Balance with Weight Percent

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Input[Feed Stream]
        F1[Total Mass mF in kg]
        F2[Weight percent wA of solute]
    end
    subgraph Compute[Calculation Block]
        C1[mA equals wA times mF divided by 100]
        C2[msolvent equals mF minus mA]
    end
    subgraph Output[Result]
        R1[Mass of solute mA in kg]
        R2[Mass of solvent in kg]
    end
    F1 --> C1
    F2 --> C1
    C1 --> R1
    F1 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> R2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The block-level schematics above are designed to mirror the **mental algorithm** the KTU examiner expects a student to follow while writing the solution. They are *not* physical process flows — they are decision trees for solving numerical problems.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define *weight percent* and *weight fraction*. How are the two related? Give one industrial example where weight percent is the preferred composition unit.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Weight percent of a component in a solution is the mass of that component expressed per 100 mass units of the solution. It is a dimensionless ratio scaled by 100.
- **Relation (1 mark):** Weight fraction = (weight percent) / 100, or equivalently, weight percent = weight fraction × 100.
- **Industrial example (1 mark):** Sulphur content in crude oil is reported as wt % (e.g., "Assam crude has 0.2 wt % sulphur") because sulphur is measured by mass-based analytical techniques like ASTM D4294.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** A solution contains 5.0 g of NaCl dissolved in 95.0 g of water. Calculate (a) the weight fraction of NaCl and (b) the mole fraction of NaCl. ($M_{NaCl} = 58.5$ g/mol, $M_{water} = 18$ g/mol)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Weight fraction (1 mark):** $x_{w,NaCl} = \dfrac{5.0}{5.0 + 95.0} = \dfrac{5.0}{100} = 0.050$
- **Moles (1 mark):** $n_{NaCl} = \dfrac{5.0}{58.5} = 0.0855$ mol; $n_{water} = \dfrac{95.0}{18} = 5.2778$ mol; $n_{total} = 5.3633$ mol
- **Mole fraction (1 mark):** $x_{NaCl} = \dfrac{0.0855}{5.3633} = 0.01594$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **ONE** of the following (a) and (b) combinations. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a)** A nitric acid solution has a concentration of **63 wt % HNO₃** and density of **1.40 g/cm³**. Calculate:
> 1. The mole fraction of HNO₃ in the solution.
> 2. The molarity (M) of the solution in mol/L.
> 3. The mean molecular weight of the solution.
>
> ($M_{HNO_3} = 63$ g/mol, $M_{water} = 18$ g/mol)
>
> **(b)** Discuss, with examples, the relative advantages of using **weight percent** over **mole percent** for representing compositions in (i) petroleum refining and (ii) food and pharmaceutical industries. Convert a **500 ppm** aqueous impurity concentration into wt % and ppb.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 marks]

**Given:** 63 wt % HNO₃, $\rho = 1.40$ g/cm³ = 1400 g/L, $M_{HNO_3} = 63$ g/mol, $M_{water} = 18$ g/mol.

**Basis:** 100 g of solution.

**Step 1 — Extract masses (1 mark):**

$$m_{HNO_3} = 63 \text{ g}, \quad m_{water} = 100 - 63 = 37 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2 — Moles (1 mark):**

$$n_{HNO_3} = \frac{63}{63} = 1.000 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{water} = \frac{37}{18} = 2.0556 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Total moles and mole fraction of HNO₃ (1 mark):**

$$n_{total} = 1.000 + 2.0556 = 3.0556 \text{ mol}$$

$$x_{HNO_3} = \frac{1.000}{3.0556} = 0.3273$$

**Step 4 — Mean molecular weight (1 mark):**

$$M_{avg} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.63}{63} + \dfrac{0.37}{18}} = \frac{1}{0.010000 + 0.020556} = \frac{1}{0.030556} = 32.726 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Molarity (2 marks):**

Mass of 1 L of solution = $1.40 \times 1000 = 1400$ g.

Moles of HNO₃ per litre = $\dfrac{1400 \times 0.63}{63} = \dfrac{882}{63} = 14.0$ mol.

$$\boxed{M_{HNO_3} = 14.0 \text{ mol/L}}$$

**Step 6 — Final answer (1 mark for statement):**

$$\boxed{x_{HNO_3} = 0.3273, \quad M_{avg} = 32.73 \text{ g/mol}, \quad \text{Molarity} = 14.0 \text{ M}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Petroleum refining — advantages of wt % (2 marks):**

- Crude oil assays report sulphur, metals (V, Ni), and asphaltene contents in wt % because these are measured by gravimetric and XRF techniques that give mass fractions directly.
- Trade contracts for crude oil are settled by mass (barrel × density), so wt % seamlessly interfaces with pricing and mass-balance closure.

**(ii) Food and pharmaceutical industries — advantages of wt % (2 marks):**

- Recipes, FDA labelling, and pharmacopoeia (USP/IP) specify active ingredient content in wt % or wt/wt %, because formulations are weighed on balances, not counted in moles.
- Regulatory limits (e.g., "salt < 2 wt % in bread") are mass-based for legal traceability.

**Conversion of 500 ppm (1 mark + 2 marks = 3 marks):**

$$
w = \frac{\text{ppm}}{10^{4}} = \frac{500}{10{,}000} = 0.05 \text{ wt \%}
$$

$$
\text{ppb} = \text{ppm} \times 1000 = 500 \times 1000 = 5 \times 10^{5} \text{ ppb}
$$

$$\boxed{w = 0.05 \text{ wt \%}, \quad \text{ppb} = 5 \times 10^{5} = 500{,}000}$$

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a)** An aqueous solution contains three solutes: **NaOH (10 wt %)**, **Na₂CO₃ (5 wt %)**, and **NaCl (3 wt %)**. Calculate:
> 1. The mole fraction of each solute and of water.
> 2. The mean molecular weight of the solution.
>
> ($M_{NaOH} = 40$, $M_{Na_2CO_3} = 106$, $M_{NaCl} = 58.5$, $M_{water} = 18$ g/mol)
>
> **(b)** A plant discharge stream must have a **phenol content below 1.0 ppm (mass basis)**. If the stream has density 1.02 g/cm³ and the average daily flow is 250 m³/day, calculate the **maximum allowable mass of phenol** discharged per day in grams. Comment on why such regulatory limits are expressed in ppm rather than wt %.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]

**Basis:** 100 g of solution.

| Component | wt | Mass (g) | $M$ (g/mol) | Moles |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NaOH | 0.10 | 10 | 40 | 0.2500 |
| Na₂CO₃ | 0.05 | 5 | 106 | 0.04717 |
| NaCl | 0.03 | 3 | 58.5 | 0.05128 |
| Water | 0.82 | 82 | 18 | 4.5556 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | **100** | — | **4.9040** |

**Step 1 — Mole fractions (3 marks):**

$$x_{NaOH} = \frac{0.2500}{4.9040} = 0.05098$$

$$x_{Na_2CO_3} = \frac{0.04717}{4.9040} = 0.009619$$

$$x_{NaCl} = \frac{0.05128}{4.9040} = 0.01046$$

$$x_{water} = \frac{4.5556}{4.9040} = 0.9290$$

**Check:** $0.05098 + 0.00962 + 0.01046 + 0.9290 \approx 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 2 — Mean molecular weight (2 marks):**

$$M_{avg} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.10}{40} + \dfrac{0.05}{106} + \dfrac{0.03}{58.5} + \dfrac{0.82}{18}}$$

$$M_{avg} = \frac{1}{0.002500 + 0.0004717 + 0.0005128 + 0.045556} = \frac{1}{0.049040}$$

$$\boxed{M_{avg} = 20.39 \text{ g/mol}}$$

**Step 3 — Statement of results (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{x_{NaOH} = 0.0510,\; x_{Na_2CO_3} = 0.0096,\; x_{NaCl} = 0.0105,\; x_{water} = 0.9290,\; M_{avg} = 20.39 \text{ g/mol}}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** 1.0 ppm (mass), $\rho = 1.02$ g/cm³ = 1020 kg/m³, flow $Q = 250$ m³/day.

**Step 1 — Daily mass of stream (1 mark):**

$$m_{stream} = \rho \times Q = 1020 \times 250 = 255{,}000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of phenol (2 marks):**

$$\text{ppm} = \frac{m_{phenol}}{m_{stream}} \times 10^{6}$$

$$m_{phenol} = \frac{1.0 \times 255{,}000}{10^{6}} = 0.255 \text{ kg/day} = 255 \text{ g/day}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on ppm vs wt % (4 marks):**

- **Magnification of small numbers:** 1.0 ppm corresponds to 0.0001 wt %. Expressing trace limits as wt % involves leading zeros and decimal places that are error-prone in field reporting; "1 ppm" is more intuitive.
- **Scale invariance across streams of different densities:** ppm by mass is independent of stream composition to first order, allowing a single regulatory number to apply across various industrial effluents.
- **International regulatory convention:** WHO, EPA, BIS, and ISI standards universally use ppm (mg/L for water) for contaminants, making global compliance easier.
- **Ease of analytical reporting:** Modern analytical instruments (ICP-MS, GC-MS) report concentrations directly in ppm or ppb, removing conversion overhead at the reporting stage.

$$\boxed{m_{phenol,\,max} = 255 \text{ g/day}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Marks Are Lost**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the closure check:** Always verify $\sum x_i = 1$ and $\sum x_{w,i} = 1$. Examiners award 1 mark for this validation step.
> 2. **Confusing wt % with wt/wt %:** Some texts (pharmacopoeia) write "w/w" instead of wt %. They are *identical*. Do not lose marks by treating them as different.
> 3. **Molarity requires density:** The single most common KTU mistake is computing molarity from wt % alone, without multiplying by density to get mass per litre. Full marks require: $\text{Molarity} = \dfrac{\rho \times w}{M_{solute}} \times 10$ (when $\rho$ in g/cm³ and $M$ in g/mol).
> 4. **ppm conversion factor wrong:** $1 \text{ wt \%} = 10^{4}$ ppm, **not** $10^{6}$ ppm. The factor $10^{6}$ converts *mass fraction* to ppm.
> 5. **Basis not stated:** Always start the solution with "**Basis: 100 kg (or 100 g) of solution**" — examiners specifically look for this line.
> 6. **Unitless answer with units:** If a question asks for mole fraction, write the answer as a pure number (e.g., 0.327), not "0.327 mol".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weight Percent Module**

- **Definition:** wt % of $i$ = (mass of $i$ / total mass of solution) × 100. Dimensionless, but conventionally suffixed with "%" or "wt %".
- **Closure property:** $\sum x_{w,i} = 1$ and $\sum w_i = 100\%$ — always.
- **Conversion identity:** $x_i = \dfrac{x_{w,i} / M_i}{\sum_j (x_{w,j} / M_j)}$ — requires molar masses of *every* component.
- **Mean molecular weight (from wt fractions):** $M_{avg} = 1 \big/ \sum (x_{w,i}/M_i)$.
- **Mean molecular weight (from mol fractions):** $M_{avg} = \sum x_i M_i$.
- **Shortcut:** $x_i = x_{w,i} \cdot M_{avg} / M_i$ — fastest KTU-solving formula.
- **ppm / ppb:** $1 \text{ wt \%} = 10^{4} \text{ ppm} = 10^{7} \text{ ppb}$. Aqueous: $1 \text{ mg/L} \approx 1 \text{ ppm}$.
- **Molarity = $\rho \times w / M \times 10$** (with $\rho$ in g/cm³, $M$ in g/mol) — the three-parameter link between wt %, density, and molarity.
- **Always state the basis** — 100 g or 100 kg of solution is the most common and safest.
- **Additivity of mass:** $m_{total} = \sum m_i$ is a strict constraint; never violate it.
- **Sanity-check with closure:** $\sum x_i$ must equal 1 within rounding — if not, redo the arithmetic.
- **Industrial contexts favouring wt %:** petroleum assays, polymer blends, food labelling, pharmaceutical formulations, metallurgical assays, environmental compliance (ppm).
- **Industrial contexts favouring mol %:** vapour–liquid equilibrium, reaction stoichiometry, gas-phase reactions, combustion calculations.
- **Common pitfall — Volume percent is NOT weight percent** unless the solution is ideal and densities are equal; avoid cross-substitution.

---

### End of Module 1 — Liquids and Solutions: Weight Percent
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mole Percent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mole Percent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Chemical Engineering Process Calculations, a **mixture composition** can be expressed using different bases: mole, mass, or volume. The **Mole Percent** (often denoted as $y_i$ for gases or $x_i$ for liquids) of a component $i$ in a mixture is defined as the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the total number of moles of all components present in the mixture, multiplied by **100**.

Mathematically, if a mixture contains $n$ components with $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_n$ moles respectively, the mole percent of component $i$ is expressed as:

$$\text{Mole \% of } i = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Mole percent is the most fundamental basis of composition in chemical engineering calculations. It is preferred over mass percent in reactor design, vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE), and gas-phase reactions because chemical reactions occur on a *mole* basis (stoichiometry is governed by moles, not by mass).

## 1.2 Relationship with Mole Fraction

The **Mole Fraction** ($x_i$) is the ratio of moles of component $i$ to the total moles, expressed as a dimensionless fraction (0 to 1). Mole percent is simply the mole fraction expressed on a percentage scale (0 to 100).

$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum n_j} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{Mole \% } i = x_i \times 100$$

By definition, the mole fractions of all components in a mixture must sum to unity:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 1 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\text{Mole \% } i) = 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Rule:** The mole percent of every component in the mixture, when summed, **must equal exactly 100%**. If the sum deviates, it indicates either a calculation error or unaccounted components (e.g., inert gases, moisture).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a classroom with **100 students**. Suppose 40 students are from Kerala, 35 from Tamil Nadu, and 25 from Karnataka. The **mole percent** is essentially this same idea but applied to "particles" (atoms/molecules) instead of students.

- **"100 students"** = the **100% mole basis** (a convenient total).
- **"40 students"** = the **40 mole %** of one particular group (e.g., N₂ molecules in air).

Just as a teacher calculates the class percentage of each state by `group size / total students × 100`, a chemical engineer calculates the mole percent of a substance by `moles of that substance / total moles × 100`.

A more chemically intuitive analogy: imagine you have a jar containing **100 marbles total**, where 78 are red (N₂), 21 are blue (O₂), and 1 is white (Ar). The mole percent of red marbles is 78%. Similarly, dry air is **78 mol% N₂, 21 mol% O₂, and 1 mol% Ar** — this is the same as saying out of every 100 molecules of dry air, 78 are nitrogen, 21 are oxygen, and 1 is argon.

## 1.4 Special Properties for Gases (KTU High-Yield)

For **ideal gas mixtures**, a powerful identity holds true at constant temperature and pressure:

| Property | Relation to Mole Percent |
|----------|--------------------------|
| **Volume percent** | $\text{Vol \%} = \text{Mole \%}$ (Amagat's Law for ideal gases) |
| **Pressure percent** | $\text{Pressure \%} = \text{Mole \%}$ (Dalton's Law for ideal gases) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equivalence is the reason gas analyses from instruments (like Orsat analysers) report compositions directly in **mole percent**, and why engineers can use gas mole fractions interchangeably with volume fractions at the same $T$ and $P$.

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Used

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \;\text{J/(mol·K)} = 0.0821 \;\text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP):** $T = 273.15 \;\text{K}$, $P = 1 \;\text{atm} = 101.325 \;\text{kPa}$
- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \;\text{molecules/mol}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

To compute or interpret mole percent in any chemical engineering problem, follow this logical hierarchy:

1. **Identify the basis.** Decide whether the mixture is liquid, solid, or gas. For liquids/solids, mass basis is often measured first, so conversion is required. For gases, mole percent is often measured directly.
2. **Convert mass to moles.** If the given data is in mass ($m_i$ in kg or g) or mass percent, convert each component to moles using the relation $n_i = m_i / M_i$, where $M_i$ is the molecular weight of component $i$ in **g/mol** or **kg/kmol**.
3. **Sum the moles.** Compute $\Sigma n_i = n_1 + n_2 + \ldots + n_N$.
4. **Apply the formula.** Calculate the mole fraction $x_i = n_i / \Sigma n_j$, then multiply by **100** to get mole percent.
5. **Verify the closure constraint.** Confirm that all mole percents sum to 100% (or all mole fractions sum to 1.00).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "per mole" and not "per kilogram"?** Stoichiometry — the foundation of reaction engineering — is built on mole balances, not mass balances. A reaction like $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ tells us the *mole* ratios, so any reactor, combustor, or distillation column must work with mole-based compositions for the governing equations to be valid.

## 2.2 Conversion Between Mole Percent and Mass Percent

This is one of the most frequently tested concepts in KTU exams. The bridge between the two is the **average molecular weight** of the mixture, $M_{avg}$.

### Step A: Mole Percent → Mass Percent

$$\text{Mass \% of } i = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} (x_j \cdot M_j)} \times 100 = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100$$

### Step B: Mass Percent → Mole Percent

$$\text{Mole \% of } i = \frac{(w_i / M_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} (w_j / M_j)} \times 100$$

where $w_i$ is the mass fraction of component $i$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------------|
| 1 | $x_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$ | Mole fraction of component $i$ | Dimensionless; $0 \le x_i \le 1$ |
| 2 | $\text{Mole \% } i = x_i \times 100$ | Mole percent (definition) | Percentage |
| 3 | $\sum x_i = 1$ | Closure (sum of mole fractions) | Must always hold |
| 4 | $\sum (\text{Mole \%}) = 100$ | Closure (sum of mole percents) | Must always hold |
| 5 | $n_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}$ | Mass-to-mole conversion | $m$ in g, $M$ in g/mol |
| 6 | $M_{avg} = \sum (x_i \cdot M_i)$ | Average molecular weight of mixture | g/mol or kg/kmol |
| 7 | $\text{Mass \% } i = \dfrac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100$ | Mole% → Mass% conversion | Valid for any mixture |
| 8 | $\text{Mole \% } i = \dfrac{(w_i / M_i)}{\sum (w_j / M_j)} \times 100$ | Mass% → Mole% conversion | Valid for any mixture |
| 9 | $\text{Mol \%} = \text{Vol \%} = \text{Pressure \%}$ | For ideal gas mixtures | At same $T$ and $P$ |
| 10 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}$ | Density of ideal gas mixture | $M_{avg}$ must be in kg/m³ basis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Avoid the `\|` notation** in handwritten answers. KTU evaluators prefer writing "Mole %" explicitly, and always show units. Use $\vert x_i \vert$ in LaTeX if you must denote "magnitude of $x_i$".

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reactor Design:** Mole balances form the basis of the mole balance equation $\frac{dN_i}{dt} = F_{i,in} - F_{i,out} + r_i V$, where $F_i$ are molar flow rates — derived directly from mole percent.
- **Combustion Calculations:** Flue gas analysis from Orsat apparatus gives mole percents of CO₂, O₂, CO, N₂. Engineers use this to compute the **air–fuel ratio** and **excess air**.
- **Distillation / VLE:** Raoult's law ($y_i P = x_i P_i^{sat}$) uses mole fractions in both liquid ($x_i$) and vapor ($y_i$) phases.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Stack emissions and greenhouse gas reporting are mandated in **mole percent** (or ppm-mole) for regulatory compliance.
- **Petroleum Refining:** Natural gas trade is on a *mole* (or *standard cubic meter*) basis, not on mass.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Mole Fraction to Average Molecular Weight

The average (or mean) molecular weight of a mixture is the mass of one mole of the mixture. We derive it as follows.

**Step 1:** Consider 1 mole of mixture total. Then the moles of component $i$ in this 1-mole basis is exactly equal to its mole fraction $x_i$.

**Step 2:** The mass of component $i$ present in 1 mole of mixture is therefore:
$$m_i = (\text{moles of } i) \times (\text{molar mass of } i) = x_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 3:** The total mass of 1 mole of mixture is the sum of masses of all components:
$$M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot M_i$$

**Step 4:** This gives the final compact form:
$$M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i \cdot M_i) \quad \text{(g/mol or kg/kmol)}$$

This is a **rigorous thermodynamic identity** that holds for any mixture (ideal or non-ideal) at any composition.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Percent of Component $i$ from Mole Percent

**Step 1:** Start from the definition of mass fraction:
$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{\sum m_j}$$

**Step 2:** Express the mass of each component using moles and molecular weight:
$$w_i = \frac{n_i M_i}{\sum n_j M_j}$$

**Step 3:** Divide numerator and denominator by the total moles $\sum n_j$ (which is a non-zero scalar):
$$w_i = \frac{(n_i / \sum n_j) \cdot M_i}{\sum (n_j / \sum n_j) \cdot M_j} = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{\sum (x_j \cdot M_j)} = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}}$$

**Step 4:** Convert mass fraction to mass percent by multiplying by 100:
$$\text{Mass \% of } i = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100 = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{\sum (x_j \cdot M_j)} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The numerator $x_i \cdot M_i$ represents the mass contribution of component $i$ per mole of mixture. Dividing by $M_{avg}$ normalises this against the total mass per mole, yielding a dimensionless mass fraction.

## 3.3 Derivation: Mole Percent from Mass Percent

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of mole fraction:
$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$$

**Step 2:** Express moles using $n_i = m_i / M_i$:
$$x_i = \frac{m_i / M_i}{\sum (m_j / M_j)}$$

**Step 3:** Express mass in terms of mass fraction: $m_i = w_i \cdot (\text{total mass})$. Since the total mass cancels from numerator and denominator:
$$x_i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum (w_j / M_j)}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mole percent by multiplying by 100:
$$\text{Mole \% of } i = \frac{w_i / M_i}{\sum (w_j / M_j)} \times 100$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Detailed)

**Problem:** A gas mixture has the following composition by mole: CH₄ = 60%, C₂H₆ = 25%, N₂ = 15%. Calculate:
(a) The average molecular weight of the mixture.
(b) The mass percent of each component.
(c) The density of the mixture at 25 °C and 2 atm.

**Given data:**
- Mole fractions: $x_{CH_4} = 0.60$, $x_{C_2H_6} = 0.25$, $x_{N_2} = 0.15$
- Molecular weights: $M_{CH_4} = 16.04 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{C_2H_6} = 30.07 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{N_2} = 28.01 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \;\text{K}$, $P = 2 \;\text{atm}$

**Solution to (a): Average Molecular Weight**

$$M_{avg} = \sum (x_i \cdot M_i)$$

$$M_{avg} = (0.60 \times 16.04) + (0.25 \times 30.07) + (0.15 \times 28.01)$$

\begin{aligned}
M_{avg} &= 9.624 + 7.5175 + 4.2015 \\
M_{avg} &= 21.343 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}

**Solution to (b): Mass Percent of Each Component**

Using $\text{Mass \% } i = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100$:

For CH₄:
$$\text{Mass \% CH}_4 = \frac{0.60 \times 16.04}{21.343} \times 100 = \frac{9.624}{21.343} \times 100 = 45.09\%$$

For C₂H₆:
$$\text{Mass \% C}_2\text{H}_6 = \frac{0.25 \times 30.07}{21.343} \times 100 = \frac{7.5175}{21.343} \times 100 = 35.23\%$$

For N₂:
$$\text{Mass \% N}_2 = \frac{0.15 \times 28.01}{21.343} \times 100 = \frac{4.2015}{21.343} \times 100 = 19.69\%$$

**Verification:** $45.09 + 35.23 + 19.69 = 100.01\% \approx 100\%$ ✓ (rounding)

**Solution to (c): Density of the Mixture**

Using the ideal gas law applied to the mixture, $PV = nRT$. With $n = m / M_{avg}$ and $\rho = m/V$:

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}$$

Convert to consistent SI-like units: $P = 2 \;\text{atm}$, $M_{avg} = 21.343 \;\text{g/mol}$, $R = 0.0821 \;\text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$, $T = 298.15 \;\text{K}$.

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{2 \times 21.343}{0.0821 \times 298.15} = \frac{42.686}{24.477}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = 1.744 \;\text{g/L} = 1.744 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from this Example:** Even though methane is 60 mol% of the mixture, it is only ~45 mass%. This is because C₂H₆ and N₂ have higher molecular weights, so they contribute more mass per mole. **Mole% ≠ Mass%** except when all $M_i$ are equal.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What

| Scenario | Given Data | Use This Conversion | Reason |
|----------|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| Combustion analysis | Volume % from Orsat | Read directly as Mole % | Ideal gas law: $V \propto n$ |
| Liquid mixture | Mass % from lab scale | Convert via $n = m/M$ | Mass is directly weighed |
| Air composition | Mole % given | Volume % = Mole % directly | At constant $T, P$ |
| Ideal gas partial pressures | Partial pressure data | $y_i = P_i / P_{total}$ | Dalton's Law |
| Density calculations | Mole % given | Use $M_{avg} = \sum x_i M_i$ | Required for ideal gas density |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Composition-Conversion Architecture (Block Flow)

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** for converting between different composition bases — a critical workflow in KTU 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given Composition Basis] --> B{Basis Type}
    B -->|Mass percent| C[Convert mass to moles: n_i = m_i over M_i]
    B -->|Volume percent Gas| D[Volume percent equals Mole percent for ideal gases]
    B -->|Mole percent| E[Direct use: x_i = n_i over sum of n_j]
    C --> F[Compute Mole Fraction x_i]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute Average Molecular Weight M_avg = sum of x_i times M_i]
    G --> H{Target Output}
    H -->|Mass percent required| I[Mass percent i = x_i times M_i over M_avg times 100]
    H -->|Density of gas mixture| J[rho = P times M_avg over R times T]
    H -->|Partial pressures| K[Partial Pressure p_i = y_i times P_total]
    H -->|Molar flow rate| L[F_i = y_i times F_total]
```

## 4.2 Mole Percent — Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Number of Moles n_i] --> N2[Total Moles sum n_j]
    N2 --> N3[Mole Fraction x_i]
    N3 --> N4[Mole Percent equals x_i times 100]
    N3 --> N5[Average M_wt equals sum x_i M_i]
    N5 --> N6[Mass Percent of i]
    N3 --> N7[Volume Percent Gas only]
    N3 --> N8[Pressure Percent Gas only]
    N1 --> N9[Mass equals n_i times M_i]
    N9 --> N5
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Identifying Problem Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start of Composition Problem] --> P2{State of Mixture}
    P2 -->|Gas at low P| P3[Use Ideal Gas Assumptions]
    P2 -->|Liquid or solid| P4[Convert via Molecular Weights]
    P3 --> P5{Gas data given as}
    P5 -->|Volume or pressure| P6[Mole percent equals volume percent equals pressure percent]
    P5 -->|Partial pressures| P7[Compute y_i = p_i over P_total times 100]
    P4 --> P8[Convert mass to moles first: n_i = m_i over M_i]
    P8 --> P9[Compute x_i = n_i over sum n_j]
    P9 --> P10[Compute M_avg = sum x_i M_i]
    P10 --> P11[Convert back to mass percent if required]
    P6 --> P12[Answer Ready]
    P7 --> P12
    P11 --> P12
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams?** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, 14-mark questions often integrate 3–4 sub-concepts (e.g., mole% to mass%, then density, then partial pressure). The block diagram helps you **structure your answer** and ensures you don't skip intermediate steps that fetch partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define mole percent. How is it different from mass percent? Under what conditions is mole percent equal to volume percent for a gas mixture?**

**Model Answer:**

**Mole Percent:** The mole percent of a component in a mixture is the number of moles of that component expressed as a percentage of the total number of moles of all components in the mixture.

$$\text{Mole \% } i = \frac{n_i}{\sum n_j} \times 100$$

**Difference from Mass Percent:**
- Mole percent is based on the *number of molecules* (moles), while mass percent is based on the *weight* of each component.
- Mass percent requires a conversion through molecular weight: $\text{Mass \% } i = \frac{x_i M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100$.
- For ideal gases, mole% = volume% (Amagat's Law), but this identity does **not** hold for mass%.

**Condition for equality with volume percent:** For an **ideal gas mixture at constant temperature and pressure**, the volume occupied by each gas is directly proportional to its number of moles (Avogadro's hypothesis). Therefore:
$$\text{Mole \%} = \text{Volume \%} \quad \text{(ideal gas, constant } T, P\text{)}$$

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A flue gas contains 12 mol% CO₂, 8 mol% O₂, and 80 mol% N₂. Calculate the average molecular weight of the mixture.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $x_{CO_2} = 0.12$, $x_{O_2} = 0.08$, $x_{N_2} = 0.80$
**Molecular weights:** $M_{CO_2} = 44.01 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{O_2} = 32.00 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{N_2} = 28.01 \;\text{g/mol}$

**Formula:** $M_{avg} = \sum (x_i \cdot M_i)$

\begin{aligned}
M_{avg} &= (0.12 \times 44.01) + (0.08 \times 32.00) + (0.80 \times 28.01) \\
&= 5.2812 + 2.5600 + 22.4080 \\
&= 30.2492 \;\text{g/mol} \approx 30.25 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}

**Verification:** $0.12 + 0.08 + 0.80 = 1.00$ ✓

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define mole fraction and mole percent. Derive the relationship between mole percent and mass percent for a multicomponent mixture. State clearly the assumption, if any.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A gaseous mixture has the following composition by volume: CH₄ = 40%, C₂H₆ = 30%, and CO₂ = 30%. Calculate:
  (i) The average molecular weight of the mixture.
  (ii) The mass percent of each component.
  (iii) The density of the mixture at 100 °C and 1.5 atm.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation**

**Mole Fraction ($x_i$):** The mole fraction of component $i$ is the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the total number of moles of all components in the mixture. It is dimensionless, with values between 0 and 1.

$$x_i = \frac{n_i}{n_1 + n_2 + \ldots + n_N} = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j}$$

**Mole Percent:** It is the mole fraction expressed as a percentage:
$$\text{Mole \% } i = x_i \times 100 = \frac{n_i}{\sum n_j} \times 100$$

**Derivation of Mole% → Mass% Relationship:**

Consider 1 mole of the mixture as the basis. Then moles of component $i$ = $x_i$.

The mass of component $i$ in this 1 mole basis is:
$$m_i = x_i \cdot M_i$$

Total mass of 1 mole of mixture (i.e., the average molecular weight):
$$M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i M_i$$

The mass fraction $w_i$ of component $i$:
$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{\sum m_j} = \frac{x_i M_i}{\sum x_j M_j}$$

**Assumption:** The mixture is treated as an ideal mixture (no volume change on mixing, applicable to ideal gases or ideal solutions).

Converting to mass percent by multiplying by 100:
$$\boxed{\text{Mass \% } i = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{\sum (x_j \cdot M_j)} \times 100 = \frac{x_i \cdot M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100}$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given (volume basis → mole basis for ideal gas):**
- $x_{CH_4} = 0.40$, $x_{C_2H_6} = 0.30$, $x_{CO_2} = 0.30$
- $M_{CH_4} = 16.04 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{C_2H_6} = 30.07 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{CO_2} = 44.01 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $T = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \;\text{K}$, $P = 1.5 \;\text{atm}$

**(i) Average Molecular Weight:**

\begin{aligned}
M_{avg} &= (0.40 \times 16.04) + (0.30 \times 30.07) + (0.30 \times 44.01) \\
&= 6.416 + 9.021 + 13.203 \\
&= 28.640 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}

**(ii) Mass Percent of Each Component:**

Using $\text{Mass \% } i = \frac{x_i M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100$:

For CH₄:
$$\text{Mass \% CH}_4 = \frac{0.40 \times 16.04}{28.640} \times 100 = \frac{6.416}{28.640} \times 100 = 22.40\%$$

For C₂H₆:
$$\text{Mass \% C}_2\text{H}_6 = \frac{0.30 \times 30.07}{28.640} \times 100 = \frac{9.021}{28.640} \times 100 = 31.50\%$$

For CO₂:
$$\text{Mass \% CO}_2 = \frac{0.30 \times 44.01}{28.640} \times 100 = \frac{13.203}{28.640} \times 100 = 46.10\%$$

**Verification:** $22.40 + 31.50 + 46.10 = 100.00\%$ ✓

**(iii) Density at 100 °C and 1.5 atm:**

Using the ideal gas law, $PV = nRT$, and $\rho = m/V$ with $n = m/M_{avg}$:

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}$$

Using $R = 0.0821 \;\text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$:
$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{1.5 \times 28.640}{0.0821 \times 373.15} = \frac{42.960}{30.635} = 1.402 \;\text{g/L}$$

Converting to SI units: $\rho_{mix} = 1.402 \;\text{kg/m}^3$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is mole percent? Explain with a suitable example. Show that for an ideal gas mixture, mole percent is equal to volume percent as well as pressure percent.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A liquid mixture contains 40 mass% ethanol (C₂H₅OH) and 60 mass% water (H₂O). Calculate:
  (i) The mole fraction and mole percent of each component.
  (ii) The average molecular weight of the mixture.
  (iii) The density of the resulting vapor if the liquid is completely vaporised at 200 °C and 1 atm (assume ideal gas behaviour).

---

### **Model Answer to Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Theoretical Explanation**

**Mole Percent:** As defined, mole percent is the number of moles of a component expressed as a percentage of the total number of moles of all components in the mixture.

**Example:** Air is approximately 78 mol% N₂, 21 mol% O₂, and 1 mol% Ar. This means that for every 100 molecules of dry air, 78 are nitrogen, 21 are oxygen, and 1 is argon.

**Proof of Mole% = Vol% = Pressure% for Ideal Gases:**

**Step 1 — Volume percent (Amagat's Law of Additive Volumes):** For an ideal gas mixture at constant $T$ and $P$, the total volume equals the sum of partial volumes:
$$V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 + \ldots + V_N$$

From Avogadro's hypothesis, the partial volume $V_i$ is proportional to the moles $n_i$ at constant $T, P$:
$$\frac{V_i}{V_{total}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = x_i$$

Multiplying by 100:
$$\text{Vol \% } i = \text{Mole \% } i \quad \text{(ideal gas, constant } T, P\text{)} \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Pressure percent (Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures):** For an ideal gas mixture, the partial pressure of component $i$ is:
$$p_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} \cdot P_{total} = y_i \cdot P_{total}$$

Therefore:
$$\frac{p_i}{P_{total}} = y_i = x_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Pressure \% } i = \text{Mole \% } i \;\checkmark$$

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Given:**
- Mass percent: $w_{ethanol} = 40\%$, $w_{water} = 60\%$
- $M_{ethanol} = 46.07 \;\text{g/mol}$, $M_{water} = 18.02 \;\text{g/mol}$
- $T = 200 + 273.15 = 473.15 \;\text{K}$, $P = 1 \;\text{atm}$

**Basis: 100 g of mixture.**
- Mass of ethanol = 40 g, Mass of water = 60 g.

**(i) Mole Fraction and Mole Percent:**

Moles of ethanol: $n_{ethanol} = \dfrac{40}{46.07} = 0.8682 \;\text{mol}$

Moles of water: $n_{water} = \dfrac{60}{18.02} = 3.3296 \;\text{mol}$

Total moles: $n_{total} = 0.8682 + 3.3296 = 4.1978 \;\text{mol}$

Mole fraction of ethanol:
$$x_{ethanol} = \frac{0.8682}{4.1978} = 0.2068$$

Mole percent of ethanol:
$$\text{Mole \% ethanol} = 0.2068 \times 100 = 20.68\%$$

Mole fraction of water:
$$x_{water} = \frac{3.3296}{4.1978} = 0.7932$$

Mole percent of water:
$$\text{Mole \% water} = 0.7932 \times 100 = 79.32\%$$

**Verification:** $20.68 + 79.32 = 100.00\%$ ✓

**(ii) Average Molecular Weight:**

$$M_{avg} = (0.2068 \times 46.07) + (0.7932 \times 18.02)$$

\begin{aligned}
M_{avg} &= 9.5273 + 14.2955 \\
&= 23.823 \;\text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}

**Cross-check using total mass / total moles:**
$$M_{avg} = \frac{\text{Total Mass}}{\text{Total Moles}} = \frac{100 \;\text{g}}{4.1978 \;\text{mol}} = 23.821 \;\text{g/mol} \;\checkmark$$

**(iii) Density of Vapor at 200 °C and 1 atm:**

Using $\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}$ with $R = 0.0821 \;\text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$:

$$\rho = \frac{1 \times 23.823}{0.0821 \times 473.15} = \frac{23.823}{38.846} = 0.6132 \;\text{g/L}$$

Converting: $\rho = 0.6132 \;\text{kg/m}^3$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert mass % → mole basis first.** A common mistake is to plug mass % directly into the mole % formula, producing wrong $M_{avg}$ and wrong density. Always go mass → moles → mole fraction.
> 2. **Mixing up units of $R$:** Using $R = 8.314$ (SI) with $P$ in atm and $M$ in g/mol gives dimensionally inconsistent density. Match units: either $R = 0.0821 \;\text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$ with $P$ in atm, or $R = 8.314 \;\text{J/(mol·K)}$ with $P$ in Pa and $M$ in kg/kmol.
> 3. **Assuming Mole% = Mass%.** They are equal **only** when all $M_i$ are equal (e.g., isotope mixtures). For dissimilar molecules, always convert.
> 4. **Omitting the closure check.** Evaluators award partial marks if the final sum does not equal 100%. Always show the verification: $\sum x_i = 1$ or $\sum (\text{Mole \%}) = 100\%$.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the assumption** of ideal gas behaviour when equating volume % to mole %. KTU 2024 scheme questions explicitly demand stating assumptions for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mole Percent (Module 1)**

- **Definition:** Mole percent of $i$ = $\dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j} \times 100 = x_i \times 100$
- **Mole fraction closure:** $\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 1$ — must always hold; verify in every problem.
- **Mole percent closure:** $\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\text{Mole \%}) = 100\%$ — must always hold; verify in every problem.
- **Mole–mass conversion identity:** $\text{Mass \% } i = \dfrac{x_i M_i}{\sum (x_j M_j)} \times 100 = \dfrac{x_i M_i}{M_{avg}} \times 100$
- **Reverse conversion:** $\text{Mole \% } i = \dfrac{w_i / M_i}{\sum (w_j / M_j)} \times 100$
- **Average molecular weight:** $M_{avg} = \sum x_i M_i$ — the master bridge between mole and mass compositions.
- **Ideal gas identity (CRITICAL):** At constant $T$ and $P$, **Mole % = Volume % = Pressure %** (Avogadro's hypothesis + Dalton's + Amagat's).
- **Density of ideal gas mixture:** $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R \cdot T}$ — use this whenever density is asked from mole percent data.
- **Reactor / stoichiometry link:** Always work in *moles* (not mass) for reaction balancing, equilibrium constants, and conversion calculations.
- **Use Avogadro's number $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$** when the problem involves number of *molecules* (instead of moles) — convert using $n = N / N_A$.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Never assume mole% = mass% for mixtures of different compounds.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Always state the *ideal gas* assumption before equating mole% and volume% — KTU evaluators penalise for unstated assumptions.
- **Time saver in exams:** For 14-mark questions, structure your answer into clear sub-parts (definition → formula → calculation → verification) — mirrors the KTU marking scheme and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molarity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Molarity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Molarity (M)**, also called **Molar Concentration**, is defined as the number of **moles of solute** dissolved per **litre of solution**. It is a measure of the concentration of a chemical species in a solution in terms of amount of substance per unit volume of solution.

Mathematically, Molarity is expressed as:

$$M = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{V_{\text{solution (L)}}}$$

Where:
- $n_{\text{solute}}$ = number of moles of solute (in **mol**)
- $V_{\text{solution}}$ = volume of the entire solution (in **L**)

The official SI unit of molarity is **mol/m³**, but the most commonly used practical unit is **mol/L**, often abbreviated as **M** (capital M for molar).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Party Punch Bowl"

Imagine you are hosting a party and preparing a large bowl of fruit punch. The recipe says: *"Add 10 lemons (solute) to 5 litres of total punch (solution)."* 

If you had instead prepared 10 litres of punch, you would have to add **20 lemons** to keep the same "lemon strength." This "lemon strength" — the number of lemons **per litre of total punch** — is exactly what **molarity** represents. The solute (lemons) is dissolved in the solvent, and the volume is measured for the **entire solution**, not just the solvent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Often Confused):**
> - Molarity uses **volume of solution** (not volume of solvent).
> - This is why molarity **changes slightly with temperature** — the solution expands or contracts, changing the total volume.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Reference Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Unit | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Avogadro's Number ($N_A$) | $6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ | Number of particles per mole |
| Standard Molar Volume (STP) | $22.414 \text{ L/mol}$ | For ideal gases at 0 °C, 1 atm |
| Standard Molar Volume (SATP) | $24.0 \text{ L/mol}$ | For ideal gases at 25 °C, 1 bar |
| Density of pure water (25 °C) | $\mathbf{0.997 \text{ g/mL}}$ | Approximated as **1 g/mL** in dilute solutions |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In problems where density is not given, assume the dilute aqueous solution behaves like pure water with density $\rho \approx 1 \text{ g/mL}$. This is a very common assumption in textbook problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" Behind Molarity

To master molarity problems, you must understand the chain of reasoning involved:

1. **Identify the solute and solvent.** The solute is the species being dissolved (typically the smaller quantity); the solvent is the dissolving medium (typically water in chemical engineering).
2. **Convert mass of solute to moles.** Use the relation $n = \dfrac{w}{MW}$, where $w$ is the mass in grams and $MW$ is the molecular weight (g/mol).
3. **Convert solution volume to litres.** Since 1 L = 1000 mL = 1000 cm³, ensure units are consistent.
4. **Apply the formula:** Divide moles by litres to get molarity.
5. **Track significant figures.** KTU typically expects 3–4 significant figures in final answers.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all** high-yield formulas required for KTU Module 1 problems on Molarity. The pipe `$\vert$` is used in math mode to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{n}{V_{\text{L}}}$ | Fundamental definition (mol/L) |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{w}{MW \times V_{\text{L}}}$ | From mass of solute $w$ (in g) and volume in L |
| 3 | $M = \dfrac{w \times 1000}{MW \times V_{\text{mL}}}$ | When volume is given in **mL** |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{10 \times \rho \times \%w/w}{MW}$ | From mass percent ($\rho$ in g/mL) |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{10 \times \%w/v}{MW}$ | From mass per volume percent |
| 6 | $M = \dfrac{x_A \times \rho_{\text{soln}}}{x_A \cdot MW_A + x_B \cdot MW_B}$ | From mole fraction $x_A$ (solute) |
| 7 | $M = \dfrac{m \times \rho}{1 + m \times MW}$ | From molality $m$ (mol/kg), $\rho$ in g/mL |
| 8 | $M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$ | Dilution / concentration formula |
| 9 | $M_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{M_1 V_1 + M_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$ | Mixing two solutions of same solute |
| 10 | $N = M \times \text{valence factor}$ | Molarity $\leftrightarrow$ Normality conversion |
| 11 | $\text{g/L} = M \times MW$ | Mass concentration from molarity |

Where:
- $w$ = mass of solute in grams
- $MW$ = molecular weight of solute (g/mol)
- $V_{\text{L}}$ = volume in litres
- $V_{\text{mL}}$ = volume in millilitres
- $\rho$ = density of solution (g/mL)
- $\%w/w$ = mass percent of solute
- $\%w/v$ = mass per 100 mL of solution
- $x_A$ = mole fraction of solute
- $m$ = molality
- $N$ = normality

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Molarity is the workhorse concentration unit in **virtually every chemical engineering domain**:

- **Reaction Stoichiometry & Reactor Design:** Reactor sizing (CSTR, PFR) requires concentration terms in mol/L for rate equations. The rate law $r = k C_A^n$ uses molar concentration.
- **Titrimetric / Volumetric Analysis:** Acid-base, redox, and complexometric titrations use molarity (and normality) for quantitative analysis of feedstocks, products, and pollutants.
- **Process Safety:** Corrosive and reactive chemical handling (e.g., H₂SO₄, NaOH) requires precise molar concentration for corrosion-rate calculations and material selection.
- **Pharmaceutical & Biotechnology Industries:** Buffer preparation, fermentation media, and drug formulations rely on accurate molar concentrations (often at mM or µM levels).
- **Effluent Treatment:** BOD, COD, and nutrient (N, P) concentrations in wastewater are commonly reported in mol/L or its derivatives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Molarity is **temperature-dependent** because solution volume expands or contracts with temperature. In precise industrial applications (e.g., reference standards), **molality (m)** is preferred because it depends only on mass, not volume.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Mass and Volume (The Foundational Case)

Starting from the basic definition and substituting moles $n = \dfrac{w}{MW}$:

$$M = \frac{n}{V_{\text{L}}}$$

Substitute $n = \dfrac{w}{MW}$:

$$M = \frac{w / MW}{V_{\text{L}}} = \frac{w}{MW \times V_{\text{L}}}$$

If volume is given in **mL**, we convert: $V_{\text{L}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{mL}}}{1000}$. Substituting:

$$M = \frac{w}{MW \times (V_{\text{mL}}/1000)} = \frac{w \times 1000}{MW \times V_{\text{mL}}}$$

This is the most frequently applied form in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From Mass Percent (%w/w)

Let the solution have:
- Mass percent = $\%w/w$ (i.e., $g$ of solute per $100 \text{ g}$ of solution)
- Density of solution = $\rho$ (g/mL)
- Consider a basis of $100 \text{ g}$ of solution (basis of 100 g is the standard trick for % problems).

**Step 1:** Mass of solute in 100 g solution = $\%w/w$ grams.

**Step 2:** Volume of 100 g solution = $\dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{density}} = \dfrac{100}{\rho}$ mL $= \dfrac{100}{1000 \times \rho}$ L.

**Step 3:** Moles of solute = $\dfrac{\%w/w}{MW}$.

**Step 4:** Molarity = $\dfrac{\text{moles}}{\text{Volume in L}}$:

$$M = \frac{\%w/w / MW}{100/(1000\rho)} = \frac{\%w/w \times 1000 \times \rho}{100 \times MW}$$

Simplifying the constant $\dfrac{1000}{100} = 10$:

$$M = \frac{10 \times \rho \times \%w/w}{MW}$$

Where $\rho$ is in g/mL and the resulting $M$ is in mol/L. This is **Formula #4** in the cheat sheet.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: From Molality (m)

Let:
- $m$ = molality (mol solute per kg solvent)
- $\rho$ = density of solution (g/mL or equivalently kg/L)
- $MW$ = molecular weight of solute (g/mol)
- $n$ = moles of solute (basis: $n = 1$ mol for simplicity)

**Step 1:** Mass of solute = $n \times MW = 1 \times MW = MW$ grams.

**Step 2:** Molality definition: $m = \dfrac{n}{\text{kg of solvent}}$, so mass of solvent = $\dfrac{n}{m} = \dfrac{1}{m}$ kg = $\dfrac{1000}{m}$ grams.

**Step 3:** Total mass of solution = $MW + \dfrac{1000}{m}$ grams.

**Step 4:** Volume of solution = $\dfrac{\text{mass}}{\text{density}} = \dfrac{MW + 1000/m}{\rho \times 1000}$ L (converting g to kg in denominator, since $\rho$ is g/mL = kg/L).

**Step 5:** Molarity = $\dfrac{\text{moles}}{\text{volume}}$:

$$M = \frac{1}{\left(MW + 1000/m\right)/(\rho \times 1000)} = \frac{1000 \times \rho}{1000 \times MW \times m / 1000 + 1000}$$

Wait — let me re-derive cleanly. Volume in L:

$$V = \frac{MW + 1000/m}{1000 \rho}$$

(Note: dividing grams by (g/mL × 1000) = g/(g/L) gives L.)

So:

$$M = \frac{1}{V} = \frac{1000 \rho}{MW + 1000/m}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $m$:

$$M = \frac{1000 \rho \cdot m}{m \cdot MW + 1000}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by 1000:

$$M = \frac{\rho \cdot m}{1 + (m \cdot MW)/1000}$$

Hmm, this is messy. Let me restart with cleaner notation.

**Clean Derivation (basis: 1 kg of solvent = 1000 g):**

**Step 1:** For 1 kg of solvent, moles of solute = $m$ (by definition of molality).

**Step 2:** Mass of solute = $m \times MW$ grams = $\dfrac{m \times MW}{1000}$ kg.

**Step 3:** Total mass of solution = $1 + \dfrac{m \times MW}{1000}$ kg.

**Step 4:** Volume of solution (L) = $\dfrac{\text{total mass (kg)}}{\rho \text{ (kg/L)}}$:

$$V = \frac{1 + m \cdot MW/1000}{\rho}$$

**Step 5:** Molarity = $\dfrac{\text{moles solute}}{\text{volume (L)}}$:

$$M = \frac{m}{\left(1 + m \cdot MW/1000\right)/\rho} = \frac{m \cdot \rho}{1 + m \cdot MW/1000}$$

Multiplying top and bottom by 1000:

$$M = \frac{1000 \, m \, \rho}{1000 + m \cdot MW}$$

Where $\rho$ is in **kg/L** (numerically same as g/mL). This is the correct standard form (also written as $M = \dfrac{m \rho}{1 + m \cdot MW/1000}$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Special case — Dilute aqueous solutions:** When $m$ is very small, $m \cdot MW \ll 1000$, so $M \approx m \cdot \rho$. For water at 25 °C, $\rho \approx 1 \text{ kg/L}$, giving $M \approx m$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: From Mole Fraction

Let $x_A$ = mole fraction of solute, $x_B = 1 - x_A$ = mole fraction of solvent, $MW_A$, $MW_B$ are the respective molecular weights.

**Step 1:** Consider a basis of $(n_A + n_B) = 1$ total mole of solution.
- Moles of solute: $n_A = x_A$
- Moles of solvent: $n_B = 1 - x_A = x_B$

**Step 2:** Mass of solution = $n_A \cdot MW_A + n_B \cdot MW_B = x_A \cdot MW_A + x_B \cdot MW_B$ grams (per total mole).

**Step 3:** Volume of solution (L) = $\dfrac{\text{mass (g)}}{1000 \times \rho \text{ (g/mL)}}$:

$$V = \frac{x_A \cdot MW_A + x_B \cdot MW_B}{1000 \, \rho}$$

**Step 4:** Molarity = $\dfrac{n_A}{V}$:

$$M = \frac{x_A}{(x_A \cdot MW_A + x_B \cdot MW_B)/(1000 \rho)} = \frac{1000 \, \rho \cdot x_A}{x_A \cdot MW_A + x_B \cdot MW_B}$$

Where $\rho$ is in g/mL. This is **Formula #6** in the cheat sheet.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A solution of H₂SO₄ has a mass percent of 49% and density 1.15 g/mL. Calculate its molarity. (MW of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol)

**Solution using Formula #4:**

Given: $\%w/w = 49$, $\rho = 1.15 \text{ g/mL}$, $MW = 98 \text{ g/mol}$.

$$M = \frac{10 \times \rho \times \%w/w}{MW} = \frac{10 \times 1.15 \times 49}{98}$$

$$M = \frac{563.5}{98} = 5.75 \text{ mol/L}$$

**Verification by first principles:** Take 100 g of solution.
- Mass of H₂SO₄ = 49 g → Moles = $49/98 = 0.5$ mol.
- Volume of solution = $100 \text{ g} / 1.15 \text{ g/mL} = 86.96$ mL $= 0.08696$ L.
- Molarity = $0.5 / 0.08696 = 5.75$ mol/L. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Molarity in the Concentration Universe

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **concentration conversion ecosystem**, showing how Molarity (M) interlinks with every other major concentration unit. This is a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow, mapping how each formula connects to the others.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Molarity M mol per L] --> B[Normality N]
    A --> C[Mass Concentration g per L]
    A --> D[Molality m mol per kg]
    A --> E[Mass Percent w per w]
    A --> F[Mole Fraction xA]
    A --> G[Mass per Volume percent w per v]

    B --> B1[N equals M times valence factor]
    C --> C1[g per L equals M times MW]
    D --> D1[M equals 1000 m rho over 1000 plus m MW]
    E --> E1[M equals 10 rho percent w per w over MW]
    F --> F1[M equals 1000 rho xA over xA MWA plus xB MWB]
    G --> G1[M equals 10 percent w per v over MW]

    H[Pure Substance Data MW, rho] --> A
    I[Temperature T] --> J[Volume Change]
    J --> A
    I --> K[Mass Invariant]
    K --> D

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style D fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style G fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style I fill:#a5a5a5,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style J fill:#bfbfbf,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    style K fill:#bfbfbf,stroke:#fff,color:#000
```

**How to read this diagram:**

- The **central node A** (Molarity) is the hub. Every concentration conversion funnels through it.
- The **blue nodes (B–G)** are the six most common concentration siblings of Molarity.
- The **annotation nodes (B1–G1)** are the exact conversion formulas (matching the cheat sheet).
- The **grey nodes (H, I, J, K)** are the **boundary conditions** that affect Molarity:
  - **H**: Pure substance properties ($MW$, $\rho$) are required inputs.
  - **I & J**: Temperature changes volume → **Molarity is temperature-sensitive**.
  - **K**: Mass is temperature-insensitive → **Molality is temperature-stable**.

## 4.2 Decision Tree — "Which Formula Should I Use?"

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Given data type] --> Q1{Volume of solution given?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, in mL or L| P1[Use M equals w times 1000 over MW times VmL]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Mass percent given?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, with density| P2[Use M equals 10 rho percent over MW]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Molality given?}
    Q3 -->|Yes, with density| P3[Use M equals 1000 m rho over 1000 plus m MW]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Mole fraction given?}
    Q4 -->|Yes, with density| P4[Use M equals 1000 rho xA over xA MWA plus xB MWB]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Normality given?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| P5[Use M equals N over valence factor]
    Q5 -->|No| P6[Re-examine problem statement]

    style S1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style Q5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style P1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P3 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P4 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P5 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style P6 fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **molarity**. The label on a reagent bottle reads "**0.5 M Na₂CO₃**". How many grams of Na₂CO₃ (MW = 106 g/mol) are required to prepare 250 mL of this solution?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Molarity is the number of moles of solute dissolved per litre of solution, expressed in mol/L.

**Calculation (1 mark):** Given $M = 0.5 \text{ mol/L}$, $V = 250 \text{ mL} = 0.25 \text{ L}$, $MW = 106 \text{ g/mol}$.

$$n = M \times V = 0.5 \times 0.25 = 0.125 \text{ mol}$$

$$w = n \times MW = 0.125 \times 106 = 13.25 \text{ g}$$

**∴ Mass of Na₂CO₃ required = 13.25 g.**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State **two differences between molarity and molality**. Why is molarity commonly used in volumetric analysis?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Molarity (M) | Molality (m) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition basis | Moles of solute per **litre of solution** | Moles of solute per **kg of solvent** |
| Temperature dependence | **Depends** on temperature (volume changes) | **Independent** of temperature (mass invariant) |

**Why molarity in volumetric analysis (1 mark):** Volumetric analysis involves measuring volumes of solutions using burettes, pipettes, and standard flasks. Since the procedures involve direct volume measurements, molarity (which is defined per unit volume) provides the most direct and convenient concentration measure. The simple dilution formula $M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$ makes calculations straightforward during titration.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **molarity and mass percent** of an aqueous solution. State clearly all assumptions and units. (7 marks)

**(b)** A commercial HCl solution is labeled as **37% w/w** with a density of **1.19 g/mL**. Calculate:
   - (i) The molarity of the solution.
   - (ii) The volume of this stock solution required to prepare **500 mL of 0.5 M HCl** for laboratory use. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Let:
- $\%w/w$ = mass percent of solute (g solute / 100 g solution)
- $\rho$ = density of solution (g/mL)
- $MW$ = molecular weight of solute (g/mol)
- $M$ = molarity (mol/L)

**Step 1:** Choose a basis of **100 g of solution** (standard trick).

**Step 2:** Mass of solute = $\%w/w$ grams.

**Step 3:** Moles of solute:
$$n = \frac{\%w/w}{MW}$$

**Step 4:** Volume of solution:
$$V_{\text{mL}} = \frac{\text{mass}}{\text{density}} = \frac{100}{\rho} \text{ mL}$$

$$V_{\text{L}} = \frac{100}{1000 \times \rho} = \frac{1}{10 \rho} \text{ L}$$

**Step 5:** Apply definition of molarity:
$$M = \frac{n}{V_{\text{L}}} = \frac{\%w/w / MW}{1/(10\rho)} = \frac{10 \, \rho \, \%w/w}{MW}$$

$$\boxed{M = \frac{10 \, \rho \, \%w/w}{MW}}$$

Where $\rho$ is in g/mL and the resulting $M$ is in mol/L.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $\%w/w = 37$, $\rho = 1.19 \text{ g/mL}$, $MW_{\text{HCl}} = 36.5 \text{ g/mol}$, final volume $V_2 = 500 \text{ mL}$, final molarity $M_2 = 0.5 \text{ M}$.

**(i) Molarity of stock solution (3 marks):**
$$M_1 = \frac{10 \times 1.19 \times 37}{36.5} = \frac{440.3}{36.5} = 12.06 \text{ mol/L}$$

**(ii) Volume of stock needed (4 marks):** Using dilution formula $M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$:
$$V_1 = \frac{M_2 \times V_2}{M_1} = \frac{0.5 \times 500}{12.06} = \frac{250}{12.06} = 20.73 \text{ mL}$$

**∴ The required stock volume = 20.73 mL.**

*(Practical procedure: Take 20.73 mL of the concentrated HCl and dilute to 500 mL with distilled water in a volumetric flask.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat flowchart and a worked example, explain how to **prepare a standard solution of given molarity** from a solid solute in the laboratory. (7 marks)

**(b)** The molality of an aqueous KOH solution is **5.5 m** and its density is **1.25 g/mL**. Calculate:
   - (i) The molarity of the solution.
   - (ii) The mole fraction of KOH in the solution.
   - (MW of KOH = 56 g/mol, MW of H₂O = 18 g/mol) (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Preparation of Standard Solution from Solid (7 marks):**

**Procedure (flowchart):**

1. **Calculate** the mass of solute required: $w = M \times V_{\text{L}} \times MW$.
2. **Weigh** the calculated mass accurately on an analytical balance.
3. **Transfer** the solid quantitatively to a clean **volumetric flask** of the desired volume (using a funnel).
4. **Dissolve** the solid in a small amount of distilled water; swirl gently.
5. **Make up the volume** to the calibration mark with distilled water.
6. **Stopper and invert** the flask several times to ensure homogeneity.
7. **Label** the flask with solution name, concentration, and date.

**Worked Example:** Prepare 250 mL of 0.1 M Na₂CO₃ solution.
- $w = 0.1 \times 0.25 \times 106 = 2.65 \text{ g}$
- Weigh 2.65 g of anhydrous Na₂CO₃ → transfer to 250 mL volumetric flask → dissolve → make up to mark.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Given: $m = 5.5 \text{ mol/kg}$, $\rho = 1.25 \text{ g/mL} = 1.25 \text{ kg/L}$, $MW_{\text{KOH}} = 56 \text{ g/mol}$, $MW_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 18 \text{ g/mol}$.

**(i) Molarity (3 marks):** Using the molality-to-molarity formula:

$$M = \frac{1000 \, m \, \rho}{1000 + m \cdot MW}$$

$$M = \frac{1000 \times 5.5 \times 1.25}{1000 + 5.5 \times 56} = \frac{6875}{1000 + 308} = \frac{6875}{1308} = 5.258 \text{ mol/L}$$

**(ii) Mole fraction of KOH (4 marks):**

Basis: 1 kg of solvent (water) = 1000 g of water.
- Moles of KOH = 5.5 (by definition of molality).
- Moles of H₂O = $1000 / 18 = 55.56$ mol.
- Total moles = $5.5 + 55.56 = 61.06$ mol.

$$x_{\text{KOH}} = \frac{n_{\text{KOH}}}{n_{\text{total}}} = \frac{5.5}{61.06} = 0.0901$$

**∴ Molarity = 5.258 mol/L and $x_{\text{KOH}}$ = 0.0901.**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes that Cost Marks in Molarity Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Mix-up Trap:** Using $V$ in mL directly in $M = w/(MW \times V)$ without converting to L. Always remember: $V_{\text{L}} = V_{\text{mL}} / 1000$, OR equivalently use $M = (w \times 1000)/(MW \times V_{\text{mL}})$.
> 2. **Molecular Weight Error:** Forgetting to multiply atomic weights by stoichiometric coefficients (e.g., for H₂SO₄: use $2(1) + 32 + 4(16) = 98$, not $1 + 32 + 64$).
> 3. **Density Omission:** Converting mass percent to molarity **without** including the density $\rho$ in the formula $M = 10\rho\%w/w/MW$. The factor of $\rho$ often swings answers by 10–25%.
> 4. **Dilution vs. Mixing Confusion:** For dilution (adding pure solvent), use $M_1V_1 = M_2V_2$. For mixing two solutions of **same solute**, use $M_{\text{mix}} = (M_1V_1 + M_2V_2)/(V_1+V_2)$. These are different problems!
> 5. **Sign / Direction Error:** When the question asks "how much stock to take," the answer is the *smaller* volume (since you're diluting). When it asks "what final volume to prepare," it is the *larger* volume. Read the question carefully.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Molarity = moles of solute ÷ litres of solution** (the only defining equation you must memorize verbatim).
- ✅ **1 L = 1000 mL** — always convert volumes to L before computing, OR use the alternate form $M = (w \times 1000)/(MW \times V_{\text{mL}})$.
- ✅ **Molecular weight (MW)** is the sum of atomic weights with stoichiometric coefficients — verify for ionic compounds (e.g., NaCl = 58.5 g/mol, not 58).
