- ✅ **The 11 cheat-sheet formulas** cover ~95% of all KTU numerical questions on this topic; commit them to memory.
- ✅ **Density (ρ)** is the critical link between mass-based and volume-based concentration units. Don't drop it!
- ✅ **Dilution formula:** $M_1 V_1 = M_2 V_2$ is your most-used equation after the basic definition.
- ✅ **Mixing formula:** $M_{\text{mix}} = (M_1 V_1 + M_2 V_2)/(V_1 + V_2)$ — only for mixing solutions of the **same solute**.
- ✅ **Molarity is temperature-dependent**; molality is not. Choose wisely for high-precision applications.
- ✅ **Avogadro's number** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ is needed when problems involve "number of molecules/ions/atoms."
- ✅ **Valence factor** (n-factor) for M↔N conversion: $N = M \times n$ where n = number of $H^+$ or $OH^-$ replaced (acids/bases), electrons transferred (redox), or charge on cation (salts).
- ✅ **Always state units** in the final answer (mol/L or M). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
- ✅ **Dilute aqueous shortcut:** For very dilute solutions in water, $M \approx m$ (since $\rho \approx 1$ kg/L and $m \times MW \ll 1000$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Normality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Normality (N)** is a concentration unit that expresses the number of **gram equivalents** of solute dissolved in **one litre** of the solution. It is a measure of the **reacting capacity** of a solution per unit volume and is therefore a reaction-specific concentration unit (unlike molarity, which is a fixed quantity per mole of substance).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "Normality of a solution is defined as the number of gram equivalents of the solute present in one litre of the solution. It is denoted by the symbol **N** and is expressed in units of **equivalents per litre (eq/L)**."

Mathematically expressed as:

$$N \;=\; \frac{\text{Number of gram equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in litres}}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Workforce" Intuition)

Imagine a **construction site** where workers of different physical strength are building identical walls. A strong worker (1 equivalent) can build 1 wall in 1 day, while a weaker worker (fractional equivalents) needs more time. 

- **Equivalent weight** = the "strength" of one worker (how much reaction work one mole of that substance can perform).
- **Normality** = the "**crew density**" — how many such workers (gram equivalents) are crowded into one litre of solution.

> [!NOTE]
> A higher normality means more **reacting power per litre**, just as a denser crew of strong workers finishes the job faster. This is why normality is indispensable in **titration calculations**, where we need to know "how much H⁺ or OH⁻ (or electrons) can be supplied" rather than just the number of molecules.

### 1.3 Key Terminology — Highlighted Definitions

| Term | Meaning (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Gram Equivalent** | The mass of a substance in grams that will react with or supply **1 mole of H⁺ ions**, **1 mole of OH⁻ ions**, or **1 mole of electrons**. |
| **Equivalent Mass (E)** | Numerically equal to the gram equivalent. Units: **g/eq**. |
| **n-factor (Valence Factor)** | A dimensionless integer or fraction that converts molecular mass to equivalent mass. |
| **Decinormal (N/10)** | A solution of normality 0.1 N. |
| **Standard Solution** | A solution whose normality is **accurately known**, used in volumetric analysis. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Normality is **NOT a fixed property** of a substance — it **changes with the type of reaction** the substance participates in. The same H₂SO₄ can have normality 2N, 1N, or 0.5N for different reactions depending on how many of its H⁺ ions are actually involved.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The mastery of Normality requires understanding three interdependent pillars:

**Pillar 1 — The Gram Equivalent Concept**
- Any chemical reaction involves a definite amount of "reacting species" (H⁺, OH⁻, e⁻).
- A **gram equivalent** is the quantity of substance that supplies or consumes exactly **Avogadro's number (6.022 × 10²³)** of such reacting units.
- This unifies acid–base, precipitation, and redox reactions under one numerical framework.

**Pillar 2 — The n-factor (Valence Factor)**

The n-factor is the bridge between molecular mass and equivalent mass. It is determined by the **reaction type**:

| Substance Class | How to find n-factor | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Acids** (HCl, H₂SO₄, H₃PO₄) | n = number of replaceable H⁺ ions actually replaced | H₂SO₄ → n = 2 (for complete neutralization) |
| **Bases** (NaOH, Ca(OH)₂) | n = number of replaceable OH⁻ ions | Ca(OH)₂ → n = 2 |
| **Salts** | n = total positive charge (or negative charge) of the ions | Na₂CO₃ → n = 2 |
| **Oxidising/Reducing agents** | n = total number of electrons gained/lost per molecule | KMnO₄ in acid medium → n = 5 |

**Pillar 3 — From Equivalent Mass to Normality**

Once the n-factor is known, the equivalent mass is obtained by:

$$E \;=\; \frac{\text{Molecular Mass (M)}}{n\text{-factor}}$$

Then, normality follows directly:

$$N \;=\; \frac{w \times 1000}{E \times V_{(mL)}}$$

where:
- **w** = mass of solute dissolved (in grams)
- **E** = equivalent mass of solute (in g/eq)
- **V** = volume of solution (in mL)

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> This table contains every normality formula you will need for KTU Part A and Part B questions. Memorize the **3 foundational equations** and the **inter-relations** in the right column.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fundamental Normality** | $N = \dfrac{\text{eq}}{V_{(L)}}$ | eq / L |
| 2 | **Mass–Volume Form** | $N = \dfrac{w \times 1000}{E \times V_{(mL)}}$ | w in g, E in g/eq, V in mL |
| 3 | **Equivalent Mass** | $E = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | n is dimensionless |
| 4 | **Normality–Molarity Link** | $N = M \times n$ | M in mol/L |
| 5 | **Dilution Equation** | $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$ | Volumes in same unit |
| 6 | **Neutralization** | $N_a V_a = N_b V_b$ | For acid–base titration |
| 7 | **Molarity in terms of N** | $M = \dfrac{N}{n}$ | n = total positive/negative charge |
| 8 | **Normality of Mixture** | $N_{mix} = \dfrac{N_1 V_1 + N_2 V_2}{V_1 + V_2}$ | Two solutions of same solute |
| 9 | **Density-based Form** | $N = \dfrac{\rho \times P \times 10}{E}$ | ρ = density (g/mL), P = % purity |
| 10 | **Electron-mole Form** | eq = moles × n | Universal link to redox |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Treatment Plants:** Engineers use normality to dose **coagulants (FeCl₃, alum)** and **neutralizing agents (lime, NaOH)** into raw water. The reacting capacity dictates the precise dose.
- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Tablet dissolution and assay testing rely on N-factor based titrations of active ingredients.
- **Effluent Treatment:** COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) is reported as normality of oxygen consumed; the dichromate normality decides the titration endpoint.
- **Petroleum Refining:** Acid washing of crude oil uses H₂SO₄ — the n-factor determines how much "spent acid" is generated.
- **Electroplating Baths:** Salt normality controls current efficiency and deposit quality.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU problems, whenever a question says *"the acid is completely neutralized"* or *"the reaction goes to completion with full electron transfer"*, it is your cue to use the **maximum possible n-factor**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Master Normality Equation

We begin with the **definition** of normality in terms of gram equivalents:

$$N = \frac{\text{Number of gram equivalents of solute}}{\text{Volume of solution in litres}}$$

**Step 1 — Express gram equivalents in terms of mass and equivalent mass.**

By definition, the number of gram equivalents of solute is the mass of solute (in grams) divided by the equivalent mass:

$$\text{Number of gram equivalents} = \frac{w}{E}$$

where *w* is the mass of solute in grams and *E* is the equivalent mass in g/eq.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the normality definition.**

$$N = \frac{w/E}{V_{(L)}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume from mL to L by multiplying numerator and denominator appropriately.**

Since $V_{(L)} = V_{(mL)} / 1000$, we substitute:

$$N = \frac{w/E}{V_{(mL)}/1000}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain the final board-friendly form.**

$$N = \frac{w \times 1000}{E \times V_{(mL)}}$$

This is the **Master Normality Equation** used in 90% of KTU numerical problems. ∎

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linking Normality and Molarity

**Step 1 — Start from equivalent mass definition.**

$$E = \frac{M}{n} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

where *M* = molecular mass, *n* = valence factor.

**Step 2 — Recall molarity definition.**

$$M = \frac{w \times 1000}{M \times V_{(mL)}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio of the normality and molarity expressions.**

$$\frac{N}{M} = \frac{w \times 1000 / (E \times V)}{w \times 1000 / (M \times V)}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel common terms.**

The $w$, 1000, and $V$ terms cancel, leaving:

$$\frac{N}{M} = \frac{M}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Equation (i) to simplify.**

$$\frac{N}{M} = \frac{M}{M/n} = n$$

**Step 6 — Final result.**

$$\boxed{\,N = M \times n\,}$$

This is the **golden relationship** between normality and molarity. ∎

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Dilution Law ($N_1V_1 = N_2V_2$)

**Step 1 — Conservation of equivalents principle.**

When a solution is diluted by adding more solvent, the **number of gram equivalents remains constant** (no solute is added or removed). Therefore:

$$\text{Equivalents before dilution} = \text{Equivalents after dilution}$$

**Step 2 — Express each side in terms of normality and volume.**

$$N_1 \times V_1 = N_2 \times V_2$$

**Step 3 — This is also the neutralization equation** because at the equivalence point of any acid–base titration, the equivalents of acid = equivalents of base.

$$\therefore \quad N_a V_a = N_b V_b \quad \text{...(equivalence point)}$$

This single equation is the **most-tested formula** in KTU Part B problems. ∎

### 3.4 Worked Example Walkthrough (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A solution is prepared by dissolving **4.9 g of H₂SO₄** in water and making the volume up to **500 mL**. Calculate (a) the normality and (b) the molarity of the solution. [Given: Molecular mass of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol]

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the n-factor for H₂SO₄.**

H₂SO₄ has 2 replaceable H⁺ ions, so:

$$n = 2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the equivalent mass.**

$$E = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{98}{2} = 49 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the master normality equation.**

$$N = \frac{w \times 1000}{E \times V_{(mL)}} = \frac{4.9 \times 1000}{49 \times 500}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic.**

$$N = \frac{4900}{24500} = 0.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate molarity using the link formula.**

$$M = \frac{N}{n} = \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.1 \text{ M}$$

**Verification via direct molarity formula:**

$$M = \frac{w \times 1000}{M \times V} = \frac{4.9 \times 1000}{98 \times 500} = 0.1 \text{ M} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** Normality = **0.2 N**, Molarity = **0.1 M**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Normality Master Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete conceptual architecture** of Normality — from the foundational definition down to the practical utility in chemical engineering.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NORMALITY N"] --> B["DEFINITION"]
    A --> C["n-FACTOR LOGIC"]
    A --> D["KEY FORMULAS"]
    A --> E["ENGINEERING USE"]

    B --> B1["eq of solute per L of solution"]
    B --> B2["Unit eq per L"]
    B --> B3["Reaction specific quantity"]

    C --> C1["ACIDS n equals replaceable H plus"]
    C --> C2["BASES n equals replaceable OH minus"]
    C --> C3["SALTS n equals total charge"]
    C --> C4["REDOX n equals electrons transferred"]

    D --> D1["N equals w times 1000 divided by E times V"]
    D --> D2["N equals M times n"]
    D --> D3["N1V1 equals N2V2 dilution law"]
    D --> D4["NaVa equals NbVb neutralization"]

    E --> E1["Water and effluent treatment"]
    E --> E2["Acid base titration assays"]
    E --> E3["Pharma quality control"]
    E --> E4["Electroplating bath control"]

    C1 --> F["E equals M by n"]
    C2 --> F
    C3 --> F
    C4 --> F
    F --> D1
    D1 --> G["SOLVE KTU NUMERICALS"]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    D4 --> G
```

### 4.2 n-Factor Decision Tree (How to Choose the Right Value)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start Identify Substance and Reaction"] --> Q1{"Is it an ACID"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> QA["n equals number of H plus replaced"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Is it a BASE"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> QB["n equals number of OH minus replaced"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Is it a SALT"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> QC["n equals total positive charge"]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{"Is it REDOX active"}
    Q4 -- Yes --> QD["n equals electrons lost or gained"]
    Q4 -- No --> QE["Default n equals 1"]

    QA --> R["Compute E equals M by n"]
    QB --> R
    QC --> R
    QD --> R
    QE --> R
    R --> OUT["Plug into N equals w times 1000 by E times V"]
```

### 4.3 Solution Preparation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Weigh solute w grams"] --> P2["Dissolve in distilled water beaker"]
    P2 --> P3["Transfer to volumetric flask"]
    P3 --> P4["Add water up to mark V mL"]
    P4 --> P5["Shake well to homogenize"]
    P5 --> P6["Solution of Normality N ready"]

    P6 --> V1["Used in TITRATION"]
    P6 --> V2["Used in DILUTION"]
    P6 --> V3["Used in INDUSTRIAL DOSING"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three diagrams together form the **complete mental model** required for any KTU Process Calculations question on Normality. In the exam, draw the n-factor decision tree first to lock in the right *n* value before plugging into the master equation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Normality" of a solution. How is it different from molarity?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Normality of a solution is the number of gram equivalents of solute dissolved per litre of solution. It is denoted by **N** and expressed in **eq/L**.
- **Difference (1.5 marks):** Molarity is the number of moles of solute per litre (a fixed, reaction-independent quantity), whereas normality is the number of gram equivalents per litre (a reaction-dependent quantity that changes with the n-factor of the specific reaction). The relation is $N = M \times n$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the dilution law. A 2 N solution is diluted from 250 mL to 1 L. What is the new normality?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (1.5 marks):** The dilution law states that upon dilution, the number of gram equivalents remains constant, i.e., $N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$.
- **Calculation (1.5 marks):** Given $N_1 = 2$ N, $V_1 = 250$ mL, $V_2 = 1000$ mL.

$$N_2 = \frac{N_1 V_1}{V_2} = \frac{2 \times 250}{1000} = 0.5 \text{ N}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> The most common mark-losing mistake in normality problems is **forgetting that the n-factor depends on the reaction**. A KTU student once wrote n = 1 for H₃PO₄ in complete neutralization and lost 4 marks. Always re-read the question to identify the **specific reaction** (acid–base vs redox) and count the actual H⁺, OH⁻, or e⁻ involved.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** Define the term **equivalent mass**. How is the equivalent mass of an oxidising agent determined? Illustrate with the example of KMnO₄ in acidic medium. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** 5.3 g of Na₂CO₃ is dissolved in water and the volume is made up to 250 mL. Calculate (i) the normality of the solution, and (ii) the volume of 0.5 N HCl required to completely neutralize 50 mL of this solution. **(7 marks)**

**[Given: Molecular mass of Na₂CO₃ = 106 g/mol]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (3 marks):** The **equivalent mass** of a substance is the number of parts by mass of it that will combine with or displace, directly or indirectly, **1.008 parts by mass of hydrogen**, **8 parts by mass of oxygen**, or **35.5 parts by mass of chlorine**. Numerically, it equals the mass in grams that reacts with or supplies one mole of electrons / H⁺ / OH⁻.

**Oxidising Agent Method (2 marks):** For an oxidising agent, the equivalent mass is calculated by dividing the molecular mass by the **total number of electrons gained by one molecule** of the agent in the redox reaction.

**Illustration with KMnO₄ (2 marks):**
In acidic medium, the half-reaction is:

$$\text{MnO}_4^- + 8\text{H}^+ + 5e^- \longrightarrow \text{Mn}^{2+} + 4\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

Here, Mn goes from +7 to +2 oxidation state, gaining **5 electrons**. So n = 5.

$$E_{KMnO_4} = \frac{M}{n} = \frac{158}{5} = 31.6 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Find n-factor for Na₂CO₃.**
Na₂CO₃ dissociates into 2 Na⁺ and CO₃²⁻. The total positive charge (or basicity of the carbonate) gives n = 2.

$$E = \frac{106}{2} = 53 \text{ g/eq}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the normality of Na₂CO₃ solution (3 marks).**

$$N = \frac{w \times 1000}{E \times V} = \frac{5.3 \times 1000}{53 \times 250}$$

$$N = \frac{5300}{13250} = 0.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Use neutralization equation to find volume of HCl (4 marks).**

$$N_1 V_1 = N_2 V_2$$

$$0.4 \times 50 = 0.5 \times V_2$$

$$V_2 = \frac{0.4 \times 50}{0.5} = 40 \text{ mL}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **N = 0.4 N**; (ii) **Volume of HCl = 40 mL**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between normality and molarity. Why is normality called a "reaction-specific" concentration unit? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solution contains 5.85 g of NaCl in 500 mL of solution. Calculate (i) molarity, (ii) normality, and (iii) the volume of 0.2 N AgNO₃ solution required to precipitate all the chloride ions from 100 mL of this solution. **(7 marks)**

**[Given: Molecular mass of NaCl = 58.5 g/mol]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Derivation (5 marks):** Shown in full in Section 3.2 of these notes. Final result:

$$N = M \times n$$

**Reaction-specific Justification (2 marks):** Normality is called "reaction-specific" because the n-factor (and hence normality) changes depending on the type of reaction and the extent to which the substance reacts. For example, H₃PO₄ has n = 1, 2, or 3 depending on whether it reacts with one, two, or all three H⁺ ions in a given reaction. Hence, the same solution can have **multiple normalities** but only **one molarity**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Find molarity (2 marks).**

$$M = \frac{w \times 1000}{M \times V} = \frac{5.85 \times 1000}{58.5 \times 500} = 0.2 \text{ M}$$

**Step 2 — Find normality (2 marks).**
For NaCl, n = 1 (single charge on each ion).

$$N = M \times n = 0.2 \times 1 = 0.2 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of AgNO₃ for precipitation (3 marks).**
The reaction: NaCl + AgNO₃ → AgCl↓ + NaNO₃. Equivalents of Cl⁻ = Equivalents of AgNO₃.

$$N_{NaCl} V_{NaCl} = N_{AgNO_3} V_{AgNO_3}$$

$$0.2 \times 100 = 0.2 \times V_{AgNO_3}$$

$$V_{AgNO_3} = 100 \text{ mL}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) **M = 0.2 M**, (ii) **N = 0.2 N**, (iii) **V(AgNO₃) = 100 mL**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Board Pattern**

- **Normality = gram equivalents per litre of solution.** Unit: **eq/L** or simply **N**.
- **Equivalent mass (E) = Molecular mass / n-factor.** The n-factor is the **soul** of every normality problem.
- **n-factor for acids = number of H⁺ replaced** (not total H atoms unless all are replaced).
- **n-factor for bases = number of OH⁻ replaced**.
- **n-factor for salts = total positive charge** of the metal ion (or total negative charge on the anion).
- **n-factor for redox = total electrons gained or lost** per molecule.
- **Master equation:** $N = (w \times 1000) / (E \times V_{mL})$ — must be memorized.
- **Normality–Molarity link:** $N = M \times n$ — use this to convert between the two.
- **Dilution law:** $N_1V_1 = N_2V_2$ — the **most-tested** equation in KTU.
- **Neutralization:** $N_a V_a = N_b V_b$ — at the **equivalence point** of any acid–base titration.
- **Normality is reaction-dependent**, molarity is **not** — this distinction earns easy marks in KTU Part A.
- **Always double-check the n-factor** before plugging into any equation — a wrong n-factor cascades into a fully wrong answer.
- **For mixture problems:** $N_{mix} = (N_1V_1 + N_2V_2) / (V_1 + V_2)$ — applicable only for same-solute mixtures.
- **For density-based problems:** $N = (\rho \times P \times 10) / E$ — where ρ is in g/mL and P is % purity by mass.
- **Equivalent mass has no units** in SI base, but is conventionally written as **g/eq**.
- **Decinormal = N/10, Centinormal = N/100** — useful in KTU objective questions.
- **The unit "N" itself is dimensionless in stoichiometric sense** but carries the implicit unit eq/L.
- **One equivalent always reacts with one equivalent** — this is the unifying principle of all normality-based calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Molality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Molality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Molality** (denoted by the lowercase italic symbol **m**) is a measure of the concentration of a solute in a solution, defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved in **one kilogram (1 kg)** of the solvent. It is a **mass-based concentration unit** and is therefore independent of temperature and pressure variations because it is defined in terms of mass (an intrinsic property) rather than volume (an extrinsic property that changes with temperature).

Mathematically, the formal definition is expressed as:

$$m = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{m_{\text{solvent (in kg)}}}$$

Where:
- $n_{\text{solute}}$ = number of moles of the solute (mol)
- $m_{\text{solvent (in kg)}}$ = mass of the solvent expressed in kilograms (kg)

The **SI unit** of molality is **mol/kg**, which is also commonly represented as **molal** (with a lowercase "m" symbol, e.g., 2 m NaCl means 2 molal sodium chloride solution).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction (Most Commonly Confused):**
> Molality (mol/kg of **solvent**) ≠ Molarity (mol/L of **solution**)
> Note the difference: molality uses solvent, molarity uses solution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are preparing a cup of sweet tea. You add **2 spoons of sugar** (solute) into a cup containing exactly **1 kg of water** (solvent). Now, the question "How concentrated is the sugar?" depends on what you measure against.

- If you measure against the **entire cup (water + sugar)** → Molarity (volume of solution)
- If you measure against **just the water (solvent)** → Molality (mass of solvent)
- If you measure against the **number of molecules (sugar vs water molecules)** → Mole Fraction

So molality essentially answers the question: **"How many moles of solute are present for every 1 kg of pure solvent in the mixture?"**

Think of molality as a **fair, temperature-proof** measure — just like counting how many sugar crystals fall into a fixed 1 kg bag of water, regardless of whether the water is hot (expanded) or cold (contracted).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Molality Matters in Chemical Engineering:**
> In industrial reactions, temperatures often vary from cryogenic to extremely high. Molarity values change with temperature because solution volume expands/contracts. **Molality remains constant** because solvent mass doesn't change. This is why colligative property calculations (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure) always use molality, never molarity.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

| Convention | Symbol / Value |
|------------|----------------|
| Symbol | **m** (italic lowercase) |
| SI Unit | **mol/kg** |
| Alternative Unit | **molal** |
| Temperature Dependence | **None (Mass-based)** |
| Reference Quantity | **Solvent (not solution)** |
| Common Use Case | Colligative properties, electrochemistry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes the difference between **molarity, molality, and normality** as foundational concentration units under Module 1 — these are guaranteed short-answer questions in university exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The "Why" Behind Molality

Let us analyze molality step-by-step to understand its engineering significance:

- **Step 1 — Identifying the Components:** A solution has two parts: the **solute** (the substance being dissolved, usually in smaller quantity) and the **solvent** (the medium in which the solute dissolves, usually in larger quantity).

- **Step 2 — Counting Moles of Solute:** The number of moles is computed using $n = \dfrac{w}{M_w}$, where $w$ is the given mass of solute and $M_w$ is its molecular weight (in g/mol).

- **Step 3 — Measuring Solvent in Kilograms:** This is the key distinction. We do **not** include the solute's mass; we only consider the pure solvent's mass, and we convert grams → kilograms by dividing by 1000.

- **Step 4 — Forming the Ratio:** Dividing moles of solute by kg of solvent yields the molality.

- **Step 5 — Why Mass, Not Volume?** Volume changes with temperature (water expands ~4% from 4°C to 100°C). In an industrial reactor running at 300°C, the volume of a solution is significantly different from its volume at 25°C. Mass does not change. Therefore, molality is the **thermodynamically stable** concentration unit.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $m = \dfrac{n_{\text{solute}}}{m_{\text{solvent (kg)}}}$ | Fundamental definition of molality | mol/kg |
| 2 | $m = \dfrac{w_{\text{solute}} \times 1000}{M_{w,\text{solute}} \times w_{\text{solvent (g)}}}$ | Molality from mass of solute (g) and mass of solvent (g) | mol/kg |
| 3 | $m = \dfrac{\text{Mass \% of solute} \times 10}{M_{w,\text{solute}} \times (100 - \text{Mass \% of solute})}$ | Molality from mass percentage | mol/kg |
| 4 | $M = \dfrac{m \times \rho \times 1000}{1000 + m \times M_{w,\text{solute}}}$ | Conversion: Molarity to Molality (where $\rho$ = density of solution in g/mL) | mol/L |
| 5 | $m = \dfrac{M}{1000 \, \rho - M \times M_{w,\text{solute}}}$ | Conversion: Molarity to Molality (alternate form) | mol/kg |
| 6 | $m = \dfrac{x_{\text{solute}} \times 1000}{(1 - x_{\text{solute}}) \times M_{w,\text{solvent}}}$ | Molality from mole fraction of solute | mol/kg |
| 7 | $x_{\text{solute}} = \dfrac{m \times M_{w,\text{solvent}}}{1000 + m \times M_{w,\text{solvent}}}$ | Mole fraction from molality | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta T_b = K_b \times m$ | Boiling Point Elevation (colligative) | °C or K |
| 9 | $\Delta T_f = K_f \times m$ | Freezing Point Depression (colligative) | °C or K |

> [!NOTE]
> **In all the above formulas:**
> - $w$ = mass in grams
> - $M_w$ = molecular weight (g/mol)
> - $M$ = molarity (mol/L)
> - $m$ = molality (mol/kg)
> - $\rho$ = density of the solution (g/mL or g/cm³)
> - $x$ = mole fraction
> - $K_b$ = ebullioscopic constant
> - $K_f$ = cryoscopic constant

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Molality is indispensable in the following chemical engineering domains:

1. **Colligative Property Calculations:** Boiling point elevation in distillation columns, freezing point depression in crystallizers, and osmotic pressure in membrane separation processes.
2. **Electrochemistry:** Ionic molar conductivity and dissociation constant ($K_{sp}$, $K_a$, $K_b$) calculations rely on molality-based activity coefficients.
3. **Refrigeration and Cryogenics:** Antifreeze (ethylene glycol-water) concentrations are specified in molality to maintain performance across temperature ranges.
4. **Process Safety:** Concentration of corrosive or reactive solutions (e.g., sulfuric acid in pickling baths) is tracked in molality for thermal stability analysis.
5. **Thermodynamic Modeling:** Activity coefficient models (Margules, Wilson, NRTL, UNIQUAC) use molality as the reference concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Any problem involving **boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, or osmotic pressure** in the question paper will, almost without exception, expect the student to first **convert the given concentration to molality** before applying the colligative formula. Missing this conversion step is the #1 reason students lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Molality from Mass of Solute and Solvent

**Given:** Mass of solute = $w_s$ (grams), Mass of solvent = $w_{sv}$ (grams), Molecular weight of solute = $M_s$ (g/mol).

**Step 1:** Calculate the number of moles of solute:

$$n_{\text{solute}} = \frac{w_s}{M_s}$$

**Step 2:** Convert mass of solvent from grams to kilograms:

$$m_{\text{solvent (kg)}} = \frac{w_{sv}}{1000}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the definition of molality:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{m_{\text{solvent (kg)}}} \\
&= \frac{w_s / M_s}{w_{sv} / 1000} \\
&= \frac{w_s \times 1000}{M_s \times w_{sv}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is the **most-used formula** in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Conversion Between Molarity and Molality

**Given:** A solution has molarity $M$ (mol/L), density $\rho$ (g/mL), and solute molecular weight $M_s$ (g/mol).

**Step 1:** Consider **1 liter (1000 mL)** of the solution. The mass of this 1 L of solution is:

$$m_{\text{solution}} = 1000 \times \rho \quad \text{(in grams)}$$

**Step 2:** The mass of solute in this 1 L of solution is:

$$m_{\text{solute}} = M \times M_s \quad \text{(in grams)}$$

**Step 3:** The mass of solvent in this 1 L of solution is:

$$m_{\text{solvent}} = m_{\text{solution}} - m_{\text{solute}} = 1000\rho - M \cdot M_s \quad \text{(in grams)}$$

**Step 4:** Convert solvent mass to kilograms:

$$m_{\text{solvent (kg)}} = \frac{1000\rho - M \cdot M_s}{1000}$$

**Step 5:** Number of moles of solute in 1 L = $M$ (by definition of molarity). Apply the molality formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{M}{(1000\rho - M \cdot M_s)/1000} \\
&= \frac{1000 \, M}{1000 \, \rho - M \cdot M_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This is the standard KTU-board-approved **Molarity-to-Molality conversion formula**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Molality from Mass Percentage

**Given:** A solution has $P$% by mass of solute. Solute molecular weight = $M_s$, solvent molecular weight = $M_{sv}$.

**Step 1:** Take a basis of **100 g of solution**. Then mass of solute = $P$ g, and mass of solvent = $(100 - P)$ g.

**Step 2:** Moles of solute:

$$n_{\text{solute}} = \frac{P}{M_s}$$

**Step 3:** Mass of solvent in kg:

$$m_{\text{solvent (kg)}} = \frac{100 - P}{1000}$$

**Step 4:** Apply definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{P / M_s}{(100 - P)/1000} \\
&= \frac{P \times 1000}{M_s \times (100 - P)} \\
&= \frac{10 \, P}{M_s \times (100 - P)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** This compact form is very useful when mass percentage is given directly.

---

### 3.4 Worked-Out Analytical Matrix (Sample Problem Demonstrations)

#### **Worked Example 1:** Direct Calculation

**Problem:** 18 g of glucose ($M_w = 180$ g/mol) is dissolved in 500 g of water. Calculate the molality of the solution.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{glucose}} &= \frac{18 \text{ g}}{180 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.1 \text{ mol} \\
m_{\text{water (kg)}} &= \frac{500}{1000} = 0.5 \text{ kg} \\
m &= \frac{0.1}{0.5} = 0.2 \text{ mol/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Molality = **0.2 molal (or 0.2 m)**

---

#### **Worked Example 2:** From Molarity Conversion

**Problem:** The molarity of a NaOH solution is 2 mol/L. The density of the solution is 1.08 g/mL. Calculate the molality. ($M_w$ of NaOH = 40 g/mol)

**Solution:**

Using the derived formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{1000 \times M}{1000 \, \rho - M \cdot M_s} \\
&= \frac{1000 \times 2}{1000 \times 1.08 - 2 \times 40} \\
&= \frac{2000}{1080 - 80} \\
&= \frac{2000}{1000} \\
&= 2.0 \text{ mol/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Molality = **2.0 molal**

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that for dilute aqueous solutions, **molality ≈ molarity** because $\rho \approx 1$ g/mL. This is a quick KTU check for problem validation.

---

#### **Worked Example 3:** Boiling Point Elevation Application

**Problem:** A solution contains 6 g of urea ($M_w = 60$ g/mol) dissolved in 500 g of water. Given $K_b$ for water = 0.512 K·kg/mol, calculate the boiling point elevation.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{urea}} &= \frac{6}{60} = 0.1 \text{ mol} \\
m &= \frac{0.1}{0.5} = 0.2 \text{ mol/kg} \\
\Delta T_b &= K_b \times m = 0.512 \times 0.2 = 0.1024 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $\Delta T_b$ = **0.1024 K**, so the solution boils at **100.1024 °C**.

---

#### **Worked Example 4:** Reverse Calculation (Finding Solute Mass)

**Problem:** What mass of NaCl ($M_w = 58.5$ g/mol) must be dissolved in 2 kg of water to prepare a 0.5 molal solution?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{NaCl}} &= m \times m_{\text{solvent (kg)}} = 0.5 \times 2 = 1 \text{ mol} \\
w_{\text{NaCl}} &= n \times M_w = 1 \times 58.5 = 58.5 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Required NaCl = **58.5 g**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concentration Units — Comparative Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the relationship between molality, molarity, normality, and mole fraction — the four primary concentration units covered in KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Concentration Units Module 1"]
    
    node2["Mass Based"]
    node3["Volume Based"]
    node4["Mole Based"]
    node5["Equivalent Based"]
    
    node6["Molality m mol per kg solvent"]
    node7["Mass Percent w per w"]
    node8["Molarity M mol per L solution"]
    node9["Normality N eq per L solution"]
    node10["Mole Fraction x dimensionless"]
    
    node11["Temperature Independent"]
    node12["Temperature Dependent"]
    node13["Used in Colligative Properties"]
    node14["Used in Acid Base Titration"]
    node15["Used in Raoult Law"]
    
    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node3
    node1 --> node4
    node1 --> node5
    
    node2 --> node6
    node2 --> node7
    node3 --> node8
    node3 --> node9
    node4 --> node10
    
    node6 --> node11
    node6 --> node13
    node8 --> node12
    node9 --> node12
    node9 --> node14
    node10 --> node15
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top (Concentration Units). Follow the branch down to **Molality**, which is grouped under "Mass Based" and leads to "Temperature Independent" and "Used in Colligative Properties" — these are the two KTU-defining characteristics of molality.

---

### 4.2 Molality Calculation Flow — Functional Block Architecture

The following flowchart shows the **decision logic** a student should follow when solving a molality problem in a KTU examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START Problem Statement"]
    
    nodeB["Identify the Solute"]
    nodeC["Identify the Solvent"]
    nodeD["Identify the Solute Mass w"]
    nodeE["Identify the Solvent Mass w"]
    nodeF["Find Molecular Weight M of Solute"]
    
    nodeG["Calculate n equals w divided by M"]
    nodeH["Convert Solvent Mass to kg divide by 1000"]
    
    nodeI["Apply Formula m equals n divided by solvent mass in kg"]
    
    nodeJ["Is Answer in mol per kg"]
    nodeK["YES Final Answer"]
    nodeL["NO Recalculate and Check Units"]
    
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeL
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Architecture Insight:** The flowchart's "check-units" loop (`nodeJ → nodeL → nodeI`) is exactly the kind of self-correction step a KTU examiner appreciates — students who explicitly verify units in their solution scripts are awarded full method marks even if a numerical slip occurs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define molality. Why is it considered temperature-independent?**

**Model Answer:**

Molality is defined as the number of moles of solute dissolved per kilogram of solvent. It is expressed as:

$$m = \frac{n_{\text{solute}}}{m_{\text{solvent (kg)}}}$$

It is temperature-independent because it is defined in terms of **mass** of solvent (which does not change with temperature), rather than volume of solution (which expands or contracts with temperature). Hence, even if the solution is heated or cooled, the mass of solvent remains constant, and so does the molality.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for temperature independence explanation]**

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: A solution is prepared by dissolving 5.85 g of NaCl in 1 kg of water. Calculate the molality of the solution. (M = 58.5 g/mol)**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{NaCl}} &= \frac{w}{M} = \frac{5.85}{58.5} = 0.1 \text{ mol} \\
m_{\text{water}} &= 1 \text{ kg} \\
m &= \frac{n}{m_{\text{solvent}}} = \frac{0.1}{1} = 0.1 \text{ mol/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: Molality = 0.1 m (or 0.1 molal)**

**[3 Marks: 1 for moles, 1 for formula application, 1 for final answer with unit]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between molarity and molality of a solution. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The molarity of an aqueous NaCl solution is 4 mol/L at 25°C. The density of the solution is 1.15 g/mL. Calculate the molality of the solution. (Given: $M_w$ of NaCl = 58.5 g/mol) (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Consider 1 L (1000 mL) of the solution.
- Mass of solution = $1000 \times \rho$ grams
- Mass of solute = $M \times M_w$ grams
- Mass of solvent = $1000\rho - M \cdot M_w$ grams = $\dfrac{1000\rho - M \cdot M_w}{1000}$ kg
- Moles of solute in 1 L = $M$

Applying the molality definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{M}{(1000\rho - M \cdot M_w)/1000} \\
&= \frac{1000 \, M}{1000 \, \rho - M \cdot M_w}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the required relationship.

**[7 Marks: 2 for setup, 3 for step-by-step derivation, 2 for final formula]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $M = 4$ mol/L, $\rho = 1.15$ g/mL, $M_w = 58.5$ g/mol.

$$
\begin{aligned}
m &= \frac{1000 \times 4}{1000 \times 1.15 - 4 \times 58.5} \\
&= \frac{4000}{1150 - 234} \\
&= \frac{4000}{916} \\
&= 4.367 \text{ mol/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer: Molality ≈ 4.367 m**

**[7 Marks: 1 for formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for arithmetic, 1 for final unit, 1 for verification]**

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with a suitable example why molality is preferred over molarity in colligative property calculations. (7 Marks)

**(b)** An aqueous solution of ethylene glycol ($C_2H_6O_2$, $M_w = 62$ g/mol) has a molality of 2.5 m. Calculate: (i) the mass percent of ethylene glycol, and (ii) the freezing point of the solution, given $K_f$ for water = 1.86 K·kg/mol and $T_f^\circ$ for water = 273.15 K. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation:**

Colligative properties (boiling point elevation, freezing point depression, osmotic pressure) depend solely on the **number of solute particles**, not on their nature. Since these properties are measured at varying temperatures, the concentration unit used must be **temperature-independent**.

- **Molarity** (mol/L of solution) is volume-based and changes with temperature due to thermal expansion/contraction of the solution. Hence, $\Delta T$ values would be inconsistent.
- **Molality** (mol/kg of solvent) is mass-based and remains constant with temperature. Therefore, it gives reproducible and accurate colligative property measurements.

**Example:** A 1 molal NaCl solution will have the same freezing point depression whether measured at 25°C, 50°C, or 100°C, because the **mass of water** (solvent) does not change with temperature. This is why molality is used in all $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ calculations.

**[7 Marks: 2 for concept, 2 for molarity limitation, 2 for molality advantage, 1 for example]**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 2.5$ mol/kg, $M_w = 62$ g/mol, $K_f = 1.86$ K·kg/mol.

**(i) Mass Percent:**

In 1 kg of solvent (1000 g of water), moles of ethylene glycol = 2.5 mol.

Mass of ethylene glycol = $2.5 \times 62 = 155$ g.

Mass of solution = $1000 + 155 = 1155$ g.

$$
\text{Mass \%} = \frac{155}{1155} \times 100 = 13.42 \%
$$

**(ii) Freezing Point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_f &= K_f \times m = 1.86 \times 2.5 = 4.65 \text{ K} \\
T_f &= T_f^\circ - \Delta T_f = 273.15 - 4.65 = 268.50 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answers:** (i) Mass % = **13.42%**, (ii) Freezing Point = **268.50 K (or −4.65 °C)**

**[7 Marks: 2 for part (i) calculation, 3 for part (ii) calculation, 2 for final answers with proper units]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "solvent" with "solution":** Many students mistakenly divide by the total mass of solution (water + solute) instead of solvent mass alone. **Always** remember: molality denominator is the **solvent's mass**, not the total.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The solvent mass must be in **kilograms**, not grams. Forgetting to divide by 1000 will cost 1 full mark.
> 3. **Mixing Molarity with Molality in Colligative Problems:** If a question gives molarity, **do not** directly substitute it into $\Delta T = K \cdot m$. You must first convert molarity → molality using $\rho$. Skipping this step is a 2-mark penalty in the valuation key.
> 4. **Missing the Solute's Mass in Density-Based Conversions:** When using $m = \dfrac{1000 M}{1000\rho - M \cdot M_w}$, the term $M \cdot M_w$ (mass of solute) is often dropped by students. This produces an incorrect answer.
> 5. **Confusing Molality (m) with Molar Mass (M):** Use **lowercase italic m** for molality, and **uppercase italic M** for molar mass. Examiners deduct marks for sloppy notation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Here is a high-density, rapid-revision checklist of everything you must know about **Molality** for KTU 2024 exams:

- **Definition:** Molality ($m$) = moles of solute ÷ mass of solvent (in kg). **Unit: mol/kg** (or **molal**).
- **Symbol:** Lowercase italic **m** — do **not** confuse with mass $m$ or molar mass $M$.
- **Temperature Independence:** The single most important property — molality does **not** change with temperature because it is mass-based.
- **Reference Component:** Solvent (NOT solution). The solute's mass is excluded from the denominator.
- **Comparison Snapshot:**
  - Molarity ($M$): mol/L of **solution** — temperature dependent.
  - Molality ($m$): mol/kg of **solvent** — temperature independent.
  - Normality ($N$): eq/L of **solution** — depends on reaction stoichiometry.
  - Mole Fraction ($x$): dimensionless ratio — temperature independent.
- **Key Formula:** $m = \dfrac{w_s \times 1000}{M_s \times w_{sv}}$ — the workhorse of KTU problems.
- **Molarity-Molality Conversion:** $m = \dfrac{1000 \, M}{1000 \, \rho - M \cdot M_s}$ — always use this when density is given.
- **Colligative Property Linkage:** $\Delta T_b = K_b \cdot m$ and $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ — colligative calculations **always** use molality.
- **Dilute Solution Approximation:** For very dilute aqueous solutions, $m \approx M$ because $\rho_{\text{water}} \approx 1$ g/mL.
- **Industrial Use Cases:** Colligative property design, electrochemistry, antifreeze formulation, cryogenics, thermodynamic activity modeling.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Never use the **mass of the entire solution** in the denominator; always isolate the **pure solvent mass** first.
- **Board Exam Tip:** When a question says "X% by mass," immediately convert to molality using the mass-percent formula before applying any colligative property equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Takeaway:** Molality is the **thermodynamically robust** concentration unit. Whenever a problem is temperature-sensitive, when colligative properties are involved, or when the solution's volume is not directly measurable — **use molality, not molarity**. Master the three conversion formulas (mass-percent → molality, molarity → molality, mole-fraction → molality) and you will be able to solve every variant of this topic that KTU throws at you.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ppm, Gaseous Mixtures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ppm, Gaseous Mixtures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Parts Per Million (ppm)

### Formal Definition
**Parts per million (ppm)** is a dimensionless unit of concentration that expresses the amount of a constituent (solute) per one million parts of the total mixture (solvent + solute). It is fundamentally a mass ratio (or mole ratio) scaled by a factor of $10^6$.

Mathematically, for a dilute solution:

$$\text{ppm} = \frac{\text{Mass of solute}}{\text{Mass of solution}} \times 10^6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For **gaseous mixtures**, ppm can be expressed on a **volume basis** (ppmv) or a **mole basis** (ppm-mole), which become numerically equivalent for ideal gases at the same conditions, since 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies the same volume (Avogadro's Law).

### Related Concentration Units
- **ppb (parts per billion):** $\text{ppb} = \text{ppm} \times 10^{-3}$
- **ppt (parts per trillion):** $\text{ppt} = \text{ppb} \times 10^{-3} = \text{ppm} \times 10^{-6}$
- **Volume percent (% v/v):** $\%\text{v/v} = \text{ppm} \times 10^{-4}$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Imagine you have **1,000,000 identical white marbles** poured into a giant bag. If you replace **just 5 of them with red marbles**, the red marbles represent **5 ppm**. For a real-world picture, **1 ppm of impurity in water** is roughly equivalent to **1 drop of ink in a 50-litre water tank**. In air-quality monitoring, 1 ppm of a pollutant means **1 cm³ of pollutant dispersed in 1 m³ (1,000,000 cm³) of air**.

### Physical Standards
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP):** $T = 273.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 1\ \text{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- **Normal Temperature and Pressure (NTP):** $T = 293.15\ \text{K}$ ($20^\circ\text{C}$), $P = 1\ \text{atm}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 0.082057\ \text{L·atm·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1} = 8.314\ \text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$

---

## 1.2 Gaseous Mixtures

### Formal Definition
A **gaseous mixture** is a homogeneous physical combination of two or more non-reacting gases (in the context of process calculations) where the molecules of each gas occupy the same total volume but exert their own individual **partial pressure** in accordance with **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Principle:** For an ideal gaseous mixture, the total pressure exerted equals the **arithmetic sum of the partial pressures** of the individual components, and the total volume equals the **arithmetic sum of the partial volumes** (Amagat's Law). Both laws are mathematical consequences of the **Ideal Gas Law**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:** Picture a classroom of 100 students — 60 girls and 40 boys — all in the same room. The **classroom pressure** (the bustle of activity) is the **total pressure**. The "girl-pressure" contribution and the "boy-pressure" contribution are the **partial pressures**. Each gas behaves as if it alone occupies the entire volume, and they coexist independently. The classroom (mixture) doesn't care whether students are boys or girls — only the total count and average behavior matters.

### Composition Basis — Three Fundamental Frames of Reference
A gaseous mixture composition can be expressed in three equivalent ways:

1. **Mole Fraction ($y_i$):** $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{\text{total}}}$ → dimensionless, $\sum y_i = 1$
2. **Mass Fraction ($w_i$):** $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{\text{total}}}$ → dimensionless, $\sum w_i = 1$
3. **Volume Fraction ($v_i$):** $v_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{\text{total}}}$ → for ideal gases, $v_i = y_i$

### Average Molecular Weight
The **average (or apparent) molecular weight** of a gaseous mixture, $M_{\text{avg}}$, is the weighted average of the molecular weights of all components, weighted by their mole fractions:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i\, M_i$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### A. Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures
At constant temperature, the total pressure of a mixture of non-reacting ideal gases equals the sum of the partial pressures each gas would exert if it alone occupied the container.

$$P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \quad \text{where} \quad P_i = y_i \cdot P_{\text{total}}$$

**Why this works:** Each gas molecule strikes the container wall independently, contributing its own share to the total momentum transfer (pressure). Because the gases don't interact (ideal assumption), their contributions simply add.

### B. Amagat's Law (Law of Additive Volumes)
At constant temperature and pressure, the total volume of a mixture of non-reacting ideal gases equals the sum of the pure-component volumes.

$$V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \quad \text{where} \quad V_i = y_i \cdot V_{\text{total}}$$

**Why this works:** This is a direct consequence of Avogadro's principle — equal moles of ideal gases at identical $T$ and $P$ occupy equal volumes.

### C. Average Molecular Weight Derivation
Consider 1 mole of mixture containing $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$ moles of components with molecular weights $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n$.

Total mass of 1 mole of mixture = $\sum y_i M_i$

But by definition, the mass of 1 mole equals the average molecular weight. Therefore:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i$$

### D. Density of a Gaseous Mixture
Applying the ideal gas law to the mixture:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{P \cdot M_{\text{avg}}}{R \cdot T}$$

### E. Specific Gravity of a Gaseous Mixture
$$SG_{\text{mix}} = \frac{\rho_{\text{mix}}}{\rho_{\text{air}}} = \frac{M_{\text{avg, mix}}}{M_{\text{air}}}$$

where $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97\ \text{g/mol}$ (standard dry air).

### F. ppm Conversions in Gaseous Mixtures
For **ideal gases**, mole fraction, volume fraction, and partial pressure fraction are numerically equivalent:

$$y_i = v_i = \frac{P_i}{P_{\text{total}}}$$

The relationship between ppm and mole fraction:

$$\text{ppm}_i = y_i \times 10^6$$

The relationship between ppm and partial pressure:

$$\text{ppm}_i = \frac{P_i}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$$

The relationship between ppm (by volume) and concentration in $\text{mg/m}^3$:

$$C \left(\text{mg/m}^3\right) = \text{ppm} \times \frac{M_i}{V_m}$$

where $V_m$ is the molar volume at the given $T$ and $P$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in 80% of KTU Module 1 numerical problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Conditions |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|------------|
| 1 | ppm (mass basis) | $\text{ppm} = \dfrac{m_{\text{solute}}}{m_{\text{solution}}} \times 10^6$ | $m$ in same units | Dilute solutions |
| 2 | ppm (mole basis, gas) | $\text{ppm} = y_i \times 10^6$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction | Ideal gas |
| 3 | ppm (volume basis, gas) | $\text{ppm} = \dfrac{P_i}{P_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$ | $P$ in same units | Ideal gas |
| 4 | ppm ↔ mg/m³ | $C = \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_i}{V_m}$ | $V_m$ in m³/mol | At given $T,P$ |
| 5 | Dalton's Law | $P_{\text{total}} = \sum y_i P_{\text{total}}$ | $P$ in Pa or atm | Ideal, constant $T$ |
| 6 | Amagat's Law | $V_{\text{total}} = \sum y_i V_{\text{total}}$ | $V$ in m³ or L | Ideal, constant $T,P$ |
| 7 | Avg. Mol. Weight | $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$ | $M$ in kg/kmol | Ideal mixture |
| 8 | Mixture Density | $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{P M_{\text{avg}}}{RT}$ | $\rho$ in kg/m³ | Ideal gas |
| 9 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{M_{\text{avg, mix}}}{28.97}$ | Dimensionless | vs. dry air |
| 10 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | $R = 8.314\ \text{J/mol·K}$ | Ideal behavior |
| 11 | Mol. Wt from Density | $M = \dfrac{\rho RT}{P}$ | $M$ in kg/kmol | Ideal gas |
| 12 | Partial Pressure | $P_i = y_i P_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{n_i RT}{V}$ | $P_i$ in Pa | Ideal gas |
| 13 | Mole-Mass Conversion | $n_i = \dfrac{m_i}{M_i}$ | $m$ in kg | Pure component |
| 14 | Avogadro Number | $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$ | — | Universal constant |
| 15 | Molar Volume (STP) | $V_m = 22.414\ \text{L/mol}$ | At $0^\circ\text{C}$, 1 atm | Ideal gas |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Process Engineers Care About These Concepts:**
> 1. **Air Pollution Control:** Regulatory standards (e.g., WHO, EPA) for SO₂, NOₓ, CO, PM2.5 are expressed in ppm or μg/m³. Engineers must convert between these units.
> 2. **Combustion Calculations:** Flue gas composition (CO₂, H₂O, N₂, O₂) requires average molecular weight to compute density and specific gravity for stack design.
> 3. **Petrochemical Industry:** Natural gas (CH₄, C₂H₆, C₃H₈, N₂, CO₂) requires $M_{\text{avg}}$ to compute Wobbe index, calorific value, and compressibility factor.
> 4. **Cryogenics & Gas Separation:** Air separation into O₂ and N₂ uses partial pressure differences.
> 5. **Safety Engineering:** Lower/Upper Explosive Limits (LEL/UEL) of flammable vapors are reported in ppm or % v/v.
> 6. **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ):** CO₂ build-up in auditoria is monitored in ppm (typical: <1000 ppm fresh air, >1500 ppm poor ventilation).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Molecular Weight from First Principles

**Goal:** Prove that $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$.

### Setup
Consider a mixture of $n$ ideal gases with mole fractions $y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_n$ and molecular weights $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_n$ (in kg/kmol or g/mol).

Take a basis of **1 kmol of mixture**.

**Step 1:** Express the moles of each component.

$$n_i = y_i \times 1\ \text{kmol} = y_i\ \text{kmol}$$

**Step 2:** Express the mass of each component using $m_i = n_i M_i$.

$$m_i = y_i \cdot M_i \quad \text{(in kg per kmol of mixture)}$$

**Step 3:** Sum the masses of all components to get total mass of 1 kmol of mixture.

$$m_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i$$

**Step 4:** By definition, the mass of 1 kmol of a substance equals its molecular weight. So for the mixture:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{1\ \text{kmol}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Density of an Ideal Gas Mixture

**Goal:** Show that $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{P M_{\text{avg}}}{RT}$.

### Step-by-Step
**Step 1:** Apply the ideal gas law to the mixture as a whole.

$$P V = n_{\text{total}} R T$$

**Step 2:** Mass of mixture = $m_{\text{total}} = n_{\text{total}} M_{\text{avg}}$.

**Step 3:** Density is mass per unit volume:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{V} = \frac{n_{\text{total}} M_{\text{avg}}}{V}$$

**Step 4:** From the ideal gas law, $\dfrac{n_{\text{total}}}{V} = \dfrac{P}{RT}$.

**Step 5:** Substitute:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{P}{RT} \cdot M_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P M_{\text{avg}}}{R T} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Examples

### Example 1 — ppm Conversion in Air Pollution

**Problem:** A flue gas stream contains **85 ppm (by volume)** of CO₂. If the total stream flow is **10,000 m³/h at STP**, calculate:
(a) The volumetric flow rate of CO₂ in m³/h.
(b) The mass flow rate of CO₂ in kg/h.
(c) The mole fraction of CO₂.

**Given:**
- $\text{ppm}_{\text{CO}_2} = 85$
- $V_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$ at STP
- $M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44\ \text{kg/kmol}$
- $V_m^{\text{STP}} = 22.414\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Convert ppm to volume fraction:

$$v_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{85}{10^6} = 8.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$V_{\text{CO}_2} = v_{\text{CO}_2} \times V_{\text{total}} = 8.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 10{,}000 = 0.85\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Part (b):** Apply ideal gas law to find moles of CO₂ per hour:

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{V_{\text{CO}_2}}{V_m^{\text{STP}}} = \frac{0.85}{22.414} = 0.03792\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = n_{\text{CO}_2} \times M_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.03792 \times 44 = 1.668\ \text{kg/h}$$

**Part (c):** For an ideal gas, mole fraction = volume fraction:

$$y_{\text{CO}_2} = 8.5 \times 10^{-5} = 0.000085$$

**Sanity check:** $85\ \text{ppm} = 0.0085\%$, which is a realistic CO₂ concentration for a well-controlled flue gas. ✓

---

### Example 2 — Average Molecular Weight and Density of Natural Gas

**Problem:** A natural gas mixture has the following composition (mole %):

| Component | $y_i$ (mol %) | $M_i$ (kg/kmol) |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| CH₄ | 85.0 | 16.04 |
| C₂H₆ | 10.0 | 30.07 |
| C₃H₈ | 4.0 | 44.10 |
| N₂ | 1.0 | 28.01 |

Calculate: (a) Average molecular weight. (b) Density at $25^\circ\text{C}$ and 1 atm. (c) Specific gravity relative to air.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Convert mole % to mole fraction (divide by 100), then compute $M_{\text{avg}}$:

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.850)(16.04) + (0.100)(30.07) + (0.040)(44.10) + (0.010)(28.01)$$

$$M_{\text{avg}} = 13.634 + 3.007 + 1.764 + 0.2801$$

$$M_{\text{avg}} = 18.685\ \text{kg/kmol}$$

**Part (b):** Use $T = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 101325\ \text{Pa}$, $R = 8314\ \text{J/(kmol·K)}$:

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{P M_{\text{avg}}}{R T} = \frac{101325 \times 18.685}{8314 \times 298.15}$$

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{1{,}893{,}151}{2{,}478{,}567} = 0.7638\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Part (c):** Specific gravity vs. air ( $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ ):

$$SG = \frac{M_{\text{avg}}}{M_{\text{air}}} = \frac{18.685}{28.97} = 0.6450$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This natural gas is **lighter than air** (SG < 1), which means a leak will rise and disperse — an important safety consideration for ventilation design.

---

### Example 3 — ppm to mg/m³ Conversion (Workplace Safety)

**Problem:** The OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) for **sulfur dioxide (SO₂)** is **2 ppm** in air. Convert this to **mg/m³ at $25^\circ\text{C}$ and 1 atm**.

**Given:**
- $\text{ppm} = 2$
- $M_{\text{SO}_2} = 64.07\ \text{kg/kmol}$
- $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$

**Step 1:** Compute molar volume at the given conditions:

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(8.314\ \text{J/mol·K})(298.15\ \text{K})}{101325\ \text{Pa}}$$

$$V_m = 0.02447\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol} = 24.47\ \text{L/mol}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the conversion formula:

$$C \left(\text{mg/m}^3\right) = \text{ppm} \times \frac{M_i \ (\text{in mg/mol})}{V_m \ (\text{in m}^3/\text{mol})}$$

$$C = 2 \times \frac{64070\ \text{mg/mol}}{24.47\ \text{m}^3/\text{mol}}$$

$$C = 2 \times 2618.7 = 5237.4\ \text{mg/m}^3 \approx 5.24\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry check:** OSHA reports SO₂ PEL as 2 ppm = 5 mg/m³. ✓ **Match within rounding.**

---

### Example 4 — Dalton's Law Numerical Application

**Problem:** A 5 L container holds 0.3 mol of N₂, 0.5 mol of O₂, and 0.2 mol of CO₂ at $T = 350\ \text{K}$. Calculate:
(a) Total pressure.
(b) Partial pressure of each gas.
(c) Mole fraction of each gas.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Total moles:

$$n_{\text{total}} = 0.3 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 1.0\ \text{mol}$$

Total pressure from ideal gas law:

$$P_{\text{total}} = \frac{n_{\text{total}} RT}{V} = \frac{(1.0)(0.08206)(350)}{5} = 5.744\ \text{atm}$$

**Part (b):** Partial pressures ($P_i = y_i P_{\text{total}} = n_i RT / V$):

$$P_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.08206 \times 350}{5} = 1.723\ \text{atm}$$

$$P_{\text{O}_2} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.08206 \times 350}{5} = 2.872\ \text{atm}$$

$$P_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.08206 \times 350}{5} = 1.149\ \text{atm}$$

**Part (c):** Mole fractions:

$$y_{\text{N}_2} = 0.3, \quad y_{\text{O}_2} = 0.5, \quad y_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.2$$

**Verification:** $P_{\text{total}} = 1.723 + 2.872 + 1.149 = 5.744\ \text{atm}$ ✓

**Conversion to ppm:**

$$\text{ppm}_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.2 \times 10^6 = 200{,}000\ \text{ppm} = 20\%$$

---

### Example 5 — Mass-Based ppm in a Trace Impurity

**Problem:** A liquid solvent (molecular weight = 78 kg/kmol) contains a dissolved impurity at **250 ppm by mass**. If the solution has a density of **870 kg/m³**, calculate the molar concentration of the impurity in mol/L. (Take impurity $M = 32\ \text{kg/kmol}$.)

**Step 1:** ppm by mass means: in 1 kg of solution, there are $250 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg}$ of impurity.

**Step 2:** Moles of impurity per kg of solution:

$$n_{\text{imp}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{32} = 7.8125 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kmol/kg solution}$$

**Step 3:** Convert per kg to per litre using density:

$$1\ \text{kg solution} = \frac{1}{870}\ \text{L} = 1.149 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{L}$$

$$C = \frac{7.8125 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kmol}}{1.149 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{L}} = 6.80 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kmol/L} = 6.80\ \text{mol/L}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Relationship Between Composition Frames of Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Gas Mixture Composition] --> nodeB[Mole Fraction yi]
    startA --> nodeC[Mass Fraction wi]
    startA --> nodeD[Volume Fraction vi]
    
    nodeB --> eA1[yi = ni / n_total]
    nodeB --> eA2[For ideal gas: vi = yi]
    nodeB --> eA3[Pi = yi × P_total]
    
    nodeC --> eB1[wi = mi / m_total]
    nodeC --> eB2[wi = yi × Mi / M_avg]
    nodeC --> eB3[Used for ppm mass basis]
    
    nodeD --> eC1[vi = Vi / V_total]
    nodeD --> eC2[Amagat Law: V_total = Sum Vi]
    nodeD --> eC3[Used for ppm volume basis]
    
    nodeB --> fA[Average MW]
    nodeC --> fB[Average MW]
    nodeD --> fC[Average MW]
    
    fA --> eF1[M_avg = Sum yi × Mi]
    fB --> eF2[M_avg = 1 / Sum wi/Mi]
    fC --> eF3[For ideal gas: same as yi basis]
    
    eF1 --> gA[Density = P × M_avg / RT]
    eF2 --> gA
    eF3 --> gA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** All three composition frames (mole, mass, volume) converge to the same intermediate quantity — the **average molecular weight** — which is the gateway to all macroscopic gas-mixture properties (density, specific gravity, mass flow).

---

## 4.2 Process Flow Architecture for Gas-Mixture Property Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input: Composition + T + P] --> blockB[Step 1: Normalize Composition]
    
    blockB --> blockC[Step 2: Identify Molecular Weights Mi]
    
    blockC --> blockD[Step 3: Compute M_avg]
    blockC --> blockE[Step 3 alt: Compute Partial Pressures]
    
    blockD --> blockF[Step 4: Compute Density rho_mix]
    blockE --> blockF
    
    blockF --> blockG[Step 5: Compute Specific Gravity SG]
    
    blockG --> blockH[Output: rho_mix and SG]
    
    blockE --> blockI[Output: Pi for each component]
    blockE --> blockJ[Output: ppm_i for each component]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Workflow Insight:** In a real plant, feed composition comes from a **gas chromatograph (GC)**. The engineer first normalizes mole fractions to ensure $\sum y_i = 1$, then routes the data through the property calculator to obtain density and specific gravity — critical inputs for **orifice meter sizing**, **compressibility calculations**, and **mass flow controllers**.

---

## 4.3 Unit Conversion Network for ppm

```mermaid
graph TD
    ppmA[ppm by volume] -->|multiply by M_i / V_m| ppmB[mg per m cubed]
    ppmB -->|multiply by V_m / M_i| ppmA
    
    ppmA -->|divide by 10^6| ppmC[Mole Fraction yi]
    ppmC -->|multiply by 10^6| ppmA
    
    ppmA -->|multiply by P_total / 10^6| ppmD[Partial Pressure Pi in Pa]
    ppmD -->|multiply by 10^6 / P_total| ppmA
    
    ppmE[ppm by mass] -->|divide by 10^6| ppmF[Mass Fraction wi]
    ppmF -->|multiply by 10^6| ppmE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion reference card** (memorize for KTU numericals):
> - 1% (v/v) = 10,000 ppm
> - 1 ppm = 1 μL/L (for gases at identical T, P)
> - At STP: 1 ppm ≈ 1 mg/m³ × (24.45 / M_i)
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define parts per million (ppm). How is it used to express the concentration of pollutants in gaseous mixtures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Parts per million (ppm) is a dimensionless concentration unit representing the number of parts of a constituent per one million parts of the total mixture. For a gaseous mixture, $\text{ppm}_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 10^6$ or equivalently $\text{ppm}_i = y_i \times 10^6$ (ideal gas).
- **(1 mark) Application to pollutants:** It is widely used to express trace concentrations of gaseous pollutants (e.g., SO₂, NOₓ, CO, ozone) in ambient air or flue gases, where concentrations are very low and percentages become inconvenient.
- **(1 mark) Numerical connection:** 1 ppm = $10^{-4}\%$ = $10^{-6}$ mole fraction, and it is interconvertible with $\text{mg/m}^3$ via the molar volume at the given $T,P$.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures. What is its physical significance for ideal gas mixtures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Statement:** Dalton's Law states that the total pressure of a mixture of non-reacting ideal gases equals the sum of the partial pressures of the individual components: $P_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$.
- **(1 mark) Partial pressure definition:** $P_i$ is the pressure that the $i$-th gas would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume at the same temperature. $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{\text{total}}$.
- **(1 mark) Physical significance:** It implies that gas molecules in an ideal mixture are non-interacting and that pressure contributions are additive — this allows independent analysis of each component and forms the basis of partial-pressure-based separation processes (e.g., absorption, distillation, membrane separation).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with an internal choice. Both Question A and Question B below are **independent alternatives** — the student answers **any one**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

A flue gas from a combustion process has the following composition (on a dry basis):

| Component | Mole Fraction $y_i$ | Molecular Weight $M_i$ (kg/kmol) |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------------------------|
| CO₂ | 0.12 | 44.01 |
| H₂O | 0.08 | 18.02 |
| N₂ | 0.74 | 28.01 |
| O₂ | 0.06 | 32.00 |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Calculate:
(i) The average molecular weight of the flue gas.
(ii) The density of the flue gas at $200^\circ\text{C}$ and 1 atm.
(iii) The specific gravity relative to air at the same conditions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
If the flue gas stream flows at **500 m³/min (measured at 200°C, 1 atm)**, determine:
(i) The molar flow rate in kmol/min.
(ii) The mass flow rate of each component in kg/min.
(iii) The concentration of CO₂ in **ppm by volume** and in **mg/m³**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a):**

**(i) Average molecular weight:**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.12)(44.01) + (0.08)(18.02) + (0.74)(28.01) + (0.06)(32.00)$$

$$M_{\text{avg}} = 5.2812 + 1.4416 + 20.7274 + 1.92 = 29.370\ \text{kg/kmol}$$

**(ii) Density at $T = 200 + 273.15 = 473.15\ \text{K}$, $P = 101325\ \text{Pa}$:**

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{P M_{\text{avg}}}{R T} = \frac{101325 \times 29.370}{8314 \times 473.15}$$

$$\rho_{\text{mix}} = \frac{2{,}976{,}140}{3{,}933{,}290} = 0.7567\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**(iii) Specific gravity relative to air ($M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$):**

$$SG = \frac{29.370}{28.97} = 1.0138$$

> **Interpretation:** The flue gas is marginally **heavier than air** (slight tendency to settle; affects stack-height design).

**Part (b):**

**(i) Molar flow rate:**

$$V_m = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{8314 \times 473.15}{101325} = 38.82\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}$$

$$\dot{n} = \frac{500\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}}{38.82\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}} = 12.88\ \text{kmol/min}$$

**(ii) Mass flow rates of each component:** $\dot{m}_i = y_i \cdot \dot{n} \cdot M_i$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.12 \times 12.88 \times 44.01 = 68.02\ \text{kg/min}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.08 \times 12.88 \times 18.02 = 18.57\ \text{kg/min}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{N}_2} = 0.74 \times 12.88 \times 28.01 = 266.97\ \text{kg/min}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{O}_2} = 0.06 \times 12.88 \times 32.00 = 24.73\ \text{kg/min}$$

**Total:** $68.02 + 18.57 + 266.97 + 24.73 = 378.29\ \text{kg/min}$ ✓

**Verification via total density:** $12.88 \times 29.370 = 378.29\ \text{kg/min}$ ✓

**(iii) Concentration of CO₂:**

- **By volume (ppmv):** $\text{ppm}_{\text{CO}_2} = y_{\text{CO}_2} \times 10^6 = 0.12 \times 10^6 = 120{,}000\ \text{ppm}$
- **In mg/m³:** First, $C = \text{ppm} \times \dfrac{M_{\text{CO}_2}}{V_m \ (\text{in m}^3/\text{kmol})} \times 10^3$ (careful with units)

Alternatively: $C = \dfrac{P_{\text{CO}_2} M_{\text{CO}_2}}{R T} \times 10^6\ \text{mg/m}^3$

$$P_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.12 \times 101325 = 12159\ \text{Pa}$$

$$C = \frac{12159 \times 44.01}{8314 \times 473.15} = \frac{535{,}118}{3{,}933{,}290} = 0.1361\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 136.1\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Warning):** Students often confuse the unit conversion between ppm and mg/m³. **For ideal gases at the same $T$ and $P$**, $\text{ppm}_v = \dfrac{C\ (\text{in mg/m}^3) \times V_m \ (\text{m}^3/\text{mol})}{M_i \ (\text{g/mol}) \times 1000}$. Forgetting the **factor of 1000** when $M_i$ is in g/mol and $C$ in mg/m³ is the most common error. **Practice the dimensional analysis on every step.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

A gaseous mixture contains **three components** with the following partial pressures at $T = 400\ \text{K}$ in a 10 L vessel:

| Component | Partial Pressure $P_i$ (atm) |
|-----------|------------------------------|
| Methane (CH₄) | 2.0 |
| Ethane (C₂H₆) | 3.0 |
| Nitrogen (N₂) | 5.0 |

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Calculate:
(i) The total pressure and mole fractions of each component.
(ii) The total number of moles present in the vessel.
(iii) The average molecular weight of the mixture.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
If the mixture is cooled isobarically to a temperature at which the volume becomes **5 L**:
(i) Find the new temperature.
(ii) Calculate the partial pressure of each gas at the new state.
(iii) Express the methane concentration in **ppm by mole** and convert to **mg/m³ at the new state**.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a):**

**(i) Total pressure and mole fractions:**

$$P_{\text{total}} = 2.0 + 3.0 + 5.0 = 10.0\ \text{atm}$$

$$y_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{2.0}{10.0} = 0.20$$

$$y_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = \frac{3.0}{10.0} = 0.30$$

$$y_{\text{N}_2} = \frac{5.0}{10.0} = 0.50$$

**(ii) Total moles using $PV = nRT$:**

$$n_{\text{total}} = \frac{P V}{R T} = \frac{(10.0)(10)}{(0.08206)(400)} = \frac{100}{32.824} = 3.047\ \text{mol}$$

**(iii) Average molecular weight:**

$$M_{\text{avg}} = (0.20)(16.04) + (0.30)(30.07) + (0.50)(28.01)$$

$$M_{\text{avg}} = 3.208 + 9.021 + 14.005 = 26.234\ \text{g/mol}$$

**Part (b):**

**(i) New temperature at isobaric cooling (constant $P = 10\ \text{atm}$, $V_1 = 10\ \text{L}$, $V_2 = 5\ \text{L}$):**

By Charles's Law, $\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$:

$$T_2 = T_1 \times \frac{V_2}{V_1} = 400 \times \frac{5}{10} = 200\ \text{K}$$

**(ii) Partial pressures at new state:** Since mole fractions are unchanged (isobaric → $\dot{n}$ constant, ideal gas), $P_i$ values remain the same:

$$P_{\text{CH}_4} = 2.0\ \text{atm}, \quad P_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 3.0\ \text{atm}, \quad P_{\text{N}_2} = 5.0\ \text{atm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Under isobaric cooling/heating of a closed ideal-gas mixture, partial pressures remain constant (since they depend only on mole fraction and total pressure, both invariant).

**(iii) Methane concentration:**

- **ppm by mole:** $\text{ppm}_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.20 \times 10^6 = 200{,}000\ \text{ppm}$ (i.e., 20 mol%)
- **mg/m³ at $T_2 = 200\ \text{K}$, $P_{\text{CH}_4} = 2\ \text{atm} = 202{,}650\ \text{Pa}$:**

$$\rho_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{P_{\text{CH}_4} M_{\text{CH}_4}}{R T_2} = \frac{202650 \times 16.04}{8314 \times 200}$$

$$\rho_{\text{CH}_4} = \frac{3{,}250{,}506}{1{,}662{,}800} = 1.955\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 1955\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A frequent error is to use the **total pressure** (10 atm) in the density formula for a component — but you must use the **partial pressure of that component** (2 atm in this case). **Always read the question carefully** — if the question asks for concentration of methane in the mixture, use $P_{\text{CH}_4}$, not $P_{\text{total}}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — ppm & Gaseous Mixtures**

- **ppm = $y_i \times 10^6$ for ideal gases** — mole, volume, and partial-pressure fractions are all numerically equal.
- **1% = 10,000 ppm** — keep this conversion memorized for fast numerical solving.
- **Average molecular weight:** $M_{\text{avg}} = \sum y_i M_i$ — always normalize compositions first (verify $\sum y_i = 1$).
- **Mass-based $M_{\text{avg}}$:** If composition is given in mass fractions, $M_{\text{avg}} = 1 / \sum (w_i / M_i)$ — do not confuse with the mole-fraction formula.
- **Density formula:** $\rho = PM_{\text{avg}}/RT$ — the workhorse equation for all gaseous mixture problems. $P$ in Pa, $M$ in kg/kmol, $R = 8314$ J/(kmol·K), $T$ in K.
- **Specific gravity vs. air:** $SG = M_{\text{avg}} / 28.97$ — universal for ideal gases regardless of $T$ and $P$.
- **Dalton's Law:** $P_{\text{total}} = \sum P_i$, where $P_i = y_i P_{\text{total}}$.
- **Amagat's Law:** $V_{\text{total}} = \sum V_i$, where $V_i = y_i V_{\text{total}}$.
- **ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion:** $C = \text{ppm} \times M_i / V_m$ — watch the units of $V_m$ (must match $C$ in denominator). At $25^\circ\text{C}$, 1 atm: $V_m \approx 24.47$ L/mol.
- **Partial pressure remains constant during isobaric heating/cooling** of a closed mixture (mole fractions are invariant).
- **KTU-favorite numbers to remember:** STP molar volume = 22.414 L/mol; NTP molar volume = 24.04 L/mol; $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ g/mol; $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ /mol.
- **Sanity check rule:** For a typical flue gas, $M_{\text{avg}} \approx 28$–$30$ kg/kmol and $\rho \approx 0.7$–$1.2$ kg/m³ at ambient conditions. If your answer is wildly off, **recheck the temperature conversion to Kelvin**.
- **Dilute-solution rule:** In ppm-level problems, the **solvent mass ≈ solution mass** to 6-figure accuracy, simplifying $m_{\text{solution}} \approx m_{\text{solvent}}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Composition of Gaseous Mixtures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Composition of Gaseous Mixtures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **gaseous mixture** is a homogeneous physical combination of two or more non-reacting gases that behave ideally under the conditions of interest. The **composition of a gaseous mixture** refers to the quantitative description of the relative amounts of each constituent gas present, expressed through standardized measures such as mole fraction, mass (weight) fraction, partial pressure, or volume fraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"The composition of a gas mixture is defined by specifying the relative proportion of each component using mole fraction, mass fraction, partial pressure, or volume percent. For an ideal gas mixture, the partial pressure of a component is directly proportional to its mole fraction."*

Mathematically, for a mixture of $n$ components:

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i$$

where $n_i$ is the number of moles of component $i$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Fruit Punch Bowl:**
> Imagine preparing a fruit punch with mango, orange, and pineapple juice. The *composition* of the punch describes how much of each juice went in. If you used 2 glasses of mango, 3 of orange, and 1 of pineapple, the **mole-like ratio** is 2 : 3 : 1. Similarly, a gas mixture of $O_2$, $N_2$, and $CO_2$ has each gas contributing to the whole mixture based on how many "units" (moles, mass, or volume) of that gas are present.
>
> The key insight: a *mole* of any gas at standard conditions ($0°C$, **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**) occupies **22.414 L**, the **molar volume**. This is why mole fractions and volume fractions are numerically identical for ideal gas mixtures.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard Temperature | $T_{std}$ | **273.15 K** |
| Standard Pressure | $P_{std}$ | **101.325 kPa = 1 atm** |
| Molar Volume at STP | $V_m$ | **22.414 L/mol** |
| Avogadro's Number | $N_A$ | **6.022 × 10²³ /mol** |

> [!TIP]
> Always retain the temperature and pressure of the system in the **same units** when applying the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, since gas compositions (especially partial pressures) are **temperature-independent only in mole terms**.

## 1.4 Types of Composition Expressions (Overview)

A KTU student must master **four** equivalent ways to express the composition of a gas mixture:

1. **Mole Fraction ($y_i$)** — most fundamental for gas-phase work.
