2. **Mass (Weight) Fraction ($w_i$)** — used in material balances and combustion.
3. **Partial Pressure ($p_i$ or $P_i$)** — used in thermodynamic equilibrium.
4. **Volume Fraction / Volume Percent (%)** — used in flue gas analysis and pollution monitoring.

Each expression is interconvertible, and the choice depends on the engineering problem context.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mole Fraction ($y_i$)

The **mole fraction** of component $i$ in a gas mixture is the ratio of the number of moles of that component to the total number of moles of all components:

$$y_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} n_j} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}}$$

- **Range:** $0 \le y_i \le 1$
- **Key property:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = 1$ (closure relation).
- **For ideal gas mixtures:** $y_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}}$ (volume fraction) — this is because at fixed $T$ and $P$, moles are directly proportional to volume (Avogadro's principle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why moles for gases?** Gases react in simple integer mole ratios (Gay-Lussac's law of combining volumes). The mole fraction is, therefore, the most chemically meaningful measure.

## 2.2 Mass (Weight) Fraction ($w_i$)

The **mass fraction** of component $i$ is the ratio of the mass of that component to the total mass of the mixture:

$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} m_j} = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}}$$

- **Range:** $0 \le w_i \le 1$
- **Closure:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i = 1$
- **Link to mole fraction:** $w_i = y_i \cdot \dfrac{M_i}{M_{avg}}$, where $M_i$ is the molecular weight of component $i$ and $M_{avg}$ is the average molecular weight of the mixture.

## 2.3 Average Molecular Weight ($M_{avg}$)

The **average molecular weight** (also called *mean molar mass*) of a gas mixture is the total mass divided by the total number of moles:

$$M_{avg} = \frac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $M_{avg}$ is **NOT** a simple arithmetic mean of molecular weights — it is a **mole-fraction-weighted mean**. Using the mass-fraction-weighted form gives a different (incorrect) result, which is a common KTU pitfall.

## 2.4 Partial Pressure & Dalton's Law

**Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** (1801): The total pressure of a non-reacting ideal gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of the individual components.

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i$$

For component $i$:

$$p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$$

This holds rigorously for **ideal gases** because each gas behaves independently, exerting the same pressure it would exert if it alone occupied the volume.

> [!TIP]
> **Amagat's Law** (the volumetric counterpart): $V_{total} = \sum V_i$, where $V_i$ is the *partial volume* of gas $i$. For ideal gases, $V_i = y_i \cdot V_{total}$, and partial volume is the volume the gas would occupy at $T$ and $P_{total}$.

## 2.5 Density of a Gas Mixture

The **density** ($\rho$) of an ideal gas mixture is given by:

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R T}$$

where $P$ and $T$ are the system pressure and temperature. The mixture behaves as a single ideal gas with molecular weight $M_{avg}$.

## 2.6 Specific Gravity (Relative Density) of a Gas Mixture

The **specific gravity** of a gas mixture (with reference to air, $M_{air} = 28.97$ g/mol):

$$SG_{mix} = \frac{M_{avg}}{M_{air}} = \frac{M_{avg}}{28.97}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$ | Mole fraction definition | All systems |
| 2 | $w_i = \dfrac{m_i}{m_{total}}$ | Mass fraction definition | All systems |
| 3 | $\sum y_i = \sum w_i = 1$ | Closure relation | All systems |
| 4 | $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ | Average molecular weight | Ideal gas mixture |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{M_{avg}} = \sum \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}$ | Reciprocal form | Useful when $w_i$ given |
| 6 | $w_i = y_i \cdot \dfrac{M_i}{M_{avg}}$ | Mass from mole fraction | Ideal gas mixture |
| 7 | $y_i = w_i \cdot \dfrac{M_{avg}}{M_i}$ | Mole from mass fraction | Ideal gas mixture |
| 8 | $p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | Dalton's law (partial pressure) | Ideal gas mixture |
| 9 | $V_i = y_i \cdot V_{total}$ | Amagat's law (partial volume) | Ideal gas mixture |
| 10 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{P M_{avg}}{R T}$ | Mixture density | Ideal gas |
| 11 | $SG_{mix} = \dfrac{M_{avg}}{28.97}$ | Specific gravity (air reference) | Ideal gas vs air |
| 12 | $y_i = \dfrac{p_i / M_i}{\sum p_j / M_j}$ | Mole fraction from partial pressures | Ideal gas mixture |

> [!WARNING]
> Formula 12 is **critical** for KTU problems: if partial pressures are given directly, the mole fraction is the *pressure-weighted-by-reciprocal-molecular-weight* average, **not** $p_i / P_{total}$. The latter is valid only if all components have the same molecular weight, which is rarely the case.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

- **Combustion engineering:** Flue gas analysis (Orsat apparatus) gives mole % of $CO_2$, $O_2$, $CO$, $N_2$ → used to compute air-fuel ratio.
- **Air separation:** Cryogenic distillation of air exploits slight differences in boiling points of $O_2$ and $N_2$; composition tracking is essential.
- **Petrochemical industry:** Natural gas sweetening, reformer gas composition analysis.
- **Environmental engineering:** Stack gas monitoring for $SO_2$, $NO_x$, particulates.
- **Refrigeration:** Refrigerant blends (zeotropic/azeotropic) require precise composition control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Molecular Weight from Mole Fractions

**Starting point:** The total mass of a gas mixture is the sum of the masses of its components.

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

The mass of each component can be expressed as moles times molecular weight:

$$m_i = n_i M_i$$

Substituting:

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i M_i$$

By definition, the average molecular weight is total mass divided by total moles:

$$M_{avg} = \frac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i M_i}{n_{total}}$$

Factoring out $n_{total}$ from the denominator of each term:

$$M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} M_i$$

Recognizing $\dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}} = y_i$ (mole fraction):

$$\boxed{M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i M_i}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Average Molecular Weight from Mass Fractions

Starting again from $M_{avg} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{n_{total}}$, but now we express $n_i$ in terms of $m_i$:

$$n_i = \frac{m_i}{M_i}$$

So:

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{m_i}{M_i}$$

Therefore:

$$M_{avg} = \frac{m_{total}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{m_i}{M_i}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $m_{total}$:

$$M_{avg} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{m_i / m_{total}}{M_i}}$$

Since $w_i = m_i / m_{total}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{M_{avg}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{w_i}{M_i}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Conversion Between Mole and Mass Fractions

Starting from:

$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}} = \frac{n_i M_i}{n_{total} M_{avg}} = \left(\frac{n_i}{n_{total}}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{M_i}{M_{avg}}\right)$$

Recognizing $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$:

$$\boxed{w_i = y_i \cdot \frac{M_i}{M_{avg}}}$$

Inverting:

$$\boxed{y_i = w_i \cdot \frac{M_{avg}}{M_i}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Dalton's Law from the Ideal Gas Law

Apply the ideal gas law to component $i$ in the mixture volume $V$ at temperature $T$:

$$p_i V = n_i R T \implies p_i = \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

Apply the ideal gas law to the whole mixture:

$$P_{total} V = n_{total} R T \implies P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V}$$

Dividing the two equations:

$$\frac{p_i}{P_{total}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = y_i$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{p_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}}$$

Summing over all components:

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i = P_{total} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i \quad \text{(which is consistent with } \sum y_i = 1\text{)}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example — Mole to Mass Fraction Conversion

**Problem (KTU Style):** A gas mixture has the following mole fractions: $y_{CH_4} = 0.30$, $y_{C_2H_6} = 0.50$, $y_{N_2} = 0.20$. Calculate the average molecular weight and the mass fraction of each component.

**Step 1 — Identify molecular weights:**
- $M_{CH_4} = 12.01 + 4(1.008) = 16.04$ g/mol
- $M_{C_2H_6} = 2(12.01) + 6(1.008) = 30.07$ g/mol
- $M_{N_2} = 2(14.01) = 28.02$ g/mol

**Step 2 — Compute the average molecular weight:**

$$M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.30)(16.04) + (0.50)(30.07) + (0.20)(28.02)$$

$$M_{avg} = 4.812 + 15.035 + 5.604 = 25.451 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each mass fraction using $w_i = y_i \cdot (M_i / M_{avg})$:**

For $CH_4$:

$$w_{CH_4} = 0.30 \cdot \frac{16.04}{25.451} = 0.30 \cdot 0.6302 = 0.1891$$

For $C_2H_6$:

$$w_{C_2H_6} = 0.50 \cdot \frac{30.07}{25.451} = 0.50 \cdot 1.1815 = 0.5908$$

For $N_2$:

$$w_{N_2} = 0.20 \cdot \frac{28.02}{25.451} = 0.20 \cdot 1.1010 = 0.2202$$

**Step 4 — Verify the closure of mass fractions:**

$$w_{CH_4} + w_{C_2H_6} + w_{N_2} = 0.1891 + 0.5908 + 0.2202 = 1.0001 \approx 1.000 \checkmark$$

The minor deviation of $0.0001$ is due to rounding.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Partial Pressure Calculation

**Problem:** A 5.0 L vessel contains 2.0 mol of $O_2$ and 8.0 mol of $N_2$ at $T = 300$ K. Calculate:
(a) The mole fraction of $O_2$.
(b) The partial pressure of $O_2$.
(c) The total pressure of the mixture.

**Step 1 — Total moles:**

$$n_{total} = 2.0 + 8.0 = 10.0 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Mole fraction of $O_2$:**

$$y_{O_2} = \frac{2.0}{10.0} = 0.20$$

**Step 3 — Total pressure from ideal gas law:**

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V} = \frac{(10.0)(0.08206)(300)}{5.0}$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{246.18}{5.0} = 49.236 \text{ atm}$$

(Here $R = 0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K) was used for unit consistency.)

**Step 4 — Partial pressure of $O_2$ via Dalton's law:**

$$p_{O_2} = y_{O_2} \cdot P_{total} = 0.20 \cdot 49.236 = 9.847 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 5 — Verify by direct calculation:**

$$p_{O_2} = \frac{n_{O_2} R T}{V} = \frac{(2.0)(0.08206)(300)}{5.0} = 9.847 \text{ atm} \checkmark$$

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Expression

| Given Data Type | Required Output | Recommended Path |
|---|---|---|
| Mole fractions $y_i$ | Mass fractions $w_i$ | Compute $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$, then $w_i = y_i M_i / M_{avg}$ |
| Mass fractions $w_i$ | Mole fractions $y_i$ | Compute $1/M_{avg} = \sum w_i/M_i$, then $y_i = w_i M_{avg}/M_i$ |
| Mole fractions $y_i$ | Partial pressures $p_i$ | Multiply $y_i$ by total pressure |
| Partial pressures $p_i$ | Mole fractions $y_i$ | Use $y_i = (p_i/M_i) / \sum (p_j/M_j)$ — **NOT** $p_i/P_{total}$ unless all $M_i$ equal |
| Volume percent | Mole percent | Numerically identical for ideal gases |
| Mass percent | Mass fraction | Divide percent by 100 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Composition Framework — Master Map

The following block-level functional architecture maps the relationships among the four core composition expressions for an ideal gas mixture. Each block represents a computation pathway, and the arrows show the dependency between operations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Mixture Input Data] --> B{Data Type?}
    B -- Moles ni --> C[Compute n_total = sum of ni]
    B -- Masses mi --> D[Compute m_total = sum of mi]
    B -- Partial Pressures pi --> E[Compute P_total = sum of pi]
    B -- Volumes Vi --> F[Note: V_total = sum of Vi]

    C --> G[Mole Fractions yi = ni / n_total]
    D --> H[Mass Fractions wi = mi / m_total]
    E --> I[Partial Fractions pi / P_total]
    F --> J[Volume Fractions Vi / V_total = yi for ideal gases]

    G --> K[Compute M_avg = sum yi Mi]
    H --> L[Compute 1 / M_avg = sum wi / Mi]
    K --> M[Cross Conversion: wi = yi Mi / M_avg]
    L --> N[Cross Conversion: yi = wi M_avg / Mi]

    G --> O[Partial Pressures pi = yi P_total]
    G --> P[Density rho = P M_avg / RT]
    K --> P
    P --> Q[Specific Gravity SG = M_avg / 28.97]

    M --> R[Final Composition Set]
    N --> R
    I --> R
    J --> R
    O --> R

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style R fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style L fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style M fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style N fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Conversion Logic Block — Mole ↔ Mass Flowchart

The next diagram shows the decision pathway for converting between mole-based and mass-based composition measures.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input [Input Stage]
        A1[Mole Fractions yi] --> A2[Known Molecular Weights Mi]
        A2 --> B1[Compute M_avg = sum yi Mi]
    end

    subgraph CrossCalc [Cross Calculation]
        B1 --> C1[For each i, compute wi = yi x Mi / M_avg]
        C1 --> D1[Verify sum of wi = 1]
    end

    subgraph ReversePath [Reverse Path]
        E1[Mass Fractions wi] --> E2[Compute 1/M_avg = sum wi/Mi]
        E2 --> F1[For each i, compute yi = wi x M_avg / Mi]
        F1 --> G1[Verify sum of yi = 1]
    end

    D1 --> H[Output: Full Composition in Both Bases]
    G1 --> H

    style Input fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style CrossCalc fill:#FFF5E6,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    style ReversePath fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#990033,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Dalton's Law — Pressure Contribution Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ideal Gas Mixture in Container V at T] --> B[Component 1: Gas A]
    A --> C[Component 2: Gas B]
    A --> D[Component n: Gas N]

    B --> B1[Exerts Partial Pressure pA]
    C --> C1[Exerts Partial Pressure pB]
    D --> D1[Exerts Partial Pressure pN]

    B1 --> S[Total Pressure P_total]
    C1 --> S
    D1 --> S

    S --> S1[Daltons Law: P_total = pA + pB + ... + pN]
    S1 --> S2[Each pi = yi x P_total]
    S2 --> S3[Sum of yi = 1 ALWAYS]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style S fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each component gas in the mixture acts *independently*, contributing its own partial pressure. The total pressure is the *linear sum* of these independent contributions. This is the physical essence of Dalton's law and is the foundation of all gas-mixture calculations in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following terms with respect to a gaseous mixture:**
(a) Mole fraction
(b) Mass fraction
(c) Partial pressure

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Mole fraction ($y_i$):** The ratio of the number of moles of a particular component to the total number of moles of all components in the mixture.
$$y_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}}, \quad \sum y_i = 1$$

(b) **Mass fraction ($w_i$):** The ratio of the mass of a particular component to the total mass of the mixture.
$$w_i = \frac{m_i}{m_{total}}, \quad \sum w_i = 1$$

(c) **Partial pressure ($p_i$):** The pressure that a component of a gas mixture would exert if it alone occupied the volume of the mixture at the same temperature.
$$p_i = y_i P_{total}$$

(Each term carries 1 mark — definition + formula; this is the KTU 3-mark pattern.)

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Dalton's law of partial pressures. For an ideal gas mixture containing $N_2$, $O_2$, and $CO_2$ with partial pressures 0.60 atm, 0.20 atm, and 0.05 atm respectively, determine the total pressure and the mole fraction of $O_2$.**

**Model Answer:**

Dalton's Law: *The total pressure exerted by a non-reacting gaseous mixture is the sum of the partial pressures of the individual components.*
$$P_{total} = \sum p_i$$

For the given data:
$$P_{total} = 0.60 + 0.20 + 0.05 = 0.85 \text{ atm}$$

Mole fraction of $O_2$:
$$y_{O_2} = \frac{p_{O_2}}{P_{total}} = \frac{0.20}{0.85} = 0.2353$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Sub-parts (a) and (b) typically carry 7 marks each.

### Question B1 (Option A) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A gas mixture contains $30\%$ $CO_2$, $40\%$ $N_2$, and $30\%$ $O_2$ by volume. Calculate:
(i) The mole fraction of each component.
(ii) The average molecular weight of the mixture.
(iii) The mass fraction of each component.

**(b)** A container of volume 10 L holds 0.5 mol of $CH_4$ and 0.3 mol of $C_2H_6$ at 350 K. Calculate:
(i) The total pressure of the mixture.
(ii) The partial pressure of each component.
(iii) The density of the mixture in g/L.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Volume → Mole → Mass Composition

**Step 1:** For ideal gases, volume percent = mole percent. Hence:
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.30$
- $y_{N_2} = 0.40$
- $y_{O_2} = 0.30$

**Step 2 — Average molecular weight:**

Using $M_{CO_2} = 44.01$ g/mol, $M_{N_2} = 28.02$ g/mol, $M_{O_2} = 32.00$ g/mol:

$$M_{avg} = (0.30)(44.01) + (0.40)(28.02) + (0.30)(32.00)$$

$$M_{avg} = 13.203 + 11.208 + 9.600 = 34.011 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass fractions** using $w_i = y_i M_i / M_{avg}$:

$$w_{CO_2} = \frac{(0.30)(44.01)}{34.011} = \frac{13.203}{34.011} = 0.3882$$

$$w_{N_2} = \frac{(0.40)(28.02)}{34.011} = \frac{11.208}{34.011} = 0.3295$$

$$w_{O_2} = \frac{(0.30)(32.00)}{34.011} = \frac{9.600}{34.011} = 0.2823$$

**Verification:** $0.3882 + 0.3295 + 0.2823 = 1.0000$ ✓

#### Part (b) — Pressure, Partial Pressure, and Density

**Step 1 — Total moles:**
$$n_{total} = 0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2 — Total pressure** (using $R = 0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K)):
$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V} = \frac{(0.8)(0.08206)(350)}{10}$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{22.977}{10} = 2.2977 \text{ atm}$$

**Step 3 — Mole fractions:**
$$y_{CH_4} = \frac{0.5}{0.8} = 0.625, \quad y_{C_2H_6} = \frac{0.3}{0.8} = 0.375$$

**Step 4 — Partial pressures:**
$$p_{CH_4} = (0.625)(2.2977) = 1.4361 \text{ atm}$$

$$p_{C_2H_6} = (0.375)(2.2977) = 0.8616 \text{ atm}$$

**Verification:** $1.4361 + 0.8616 = 2.2977$ atm ✓

**Step 5 — Average molecular weight:**
$$M_{avg} = (0.625)(16.04) + (0.375)(30.07) = 10.025 + 11.276 = 21.301 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 6 — Density:**
$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{P \cdot M_{avg}}{R T} = \frac{(2.2977)(21.301)}{(0.08206)(350)} = \frac{48.946}{28.721} = 1.7042 \text{ g/L}$$

(Alternatively, $\rho = m_{total}/V = [(0.5)(16.04) + (0.3)(30.07)]/10 = [8.02 + 9.021]/10 = 1.7041$ g/L ✓)

---

### Question B1 (Option B) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** The dry flue gas from a combustion process has the following composition by volume: $CO_2 = 12\%$, $O_2 = 5\%$, $N_2 = 83\%$. Calculate:
(i) The average molecular weight of the flue gas.
(ii) The mass of each component in 100 kmol of flue gas.
(iii) The density of the flue gas at 1.2 atm and 400 K.

**(b)** A gas mixture has mass fractions: $w_{H_2} = 0.10$, $w_{CH_4} = 0.20$, $w_{CO} = 0.30$, $w_{N_2} = 0.40$. Calculate:
(i) The average molecular weight.
(ii) The mole fraction of each component.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Flue Gas Analysis

**Step 1 — Volume % = Mole %:**
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.12$, $y_{O_2} = 0.05$, $y_{N_2} = 0.83$

**Step 2 — Average molecular weight:**

Using $M_{CO_2} = 44.01$ g/mol, $M_{O_2} = 32.00$ g/mol, $M_{N_2} = 28.02$ g/mol:

$$M_{avg} = (0.12)(44.01) + (0.05)(32.00) + (0.83)(28.02)$$

$$M_{avg} = 5.2812 + 1.6000 + 23.2566 = 30.1378 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of each component in 100 kmol:**

In 100 kmol of flue gas, moles of each component = $100 \times y_i$ kmol.

Mass = moles × molecular weight:

- Mass of $CO_2 = (100)(0.12)(44.01) = 528.12$ kg
- Mass of $O_2 = (100)(0.05)(32.00) = 160.00$ kg
- Mass of $N_2 = (100)(0.83)(28.02) = 2325.66$ kg

**Verification:** Total mass = $528.12 + 160.00 + 2325.66 = 3013.78$ kg
Average MW = $3013.78 / 100 = 30.1378$ g/mol ✓

**Step 4 — Density at 1.2 atm, 400 K:**

Convert $P$ to kPa: $P = 1.2 \times 101.325 = 121.59$ kPa.

$$\rho = \frac{P M_{avg}}{R T} = \frac{(121.59 \text{ kPa})(30.1378 \text{ g/mol})}{(8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(400 \text{ K})}$$

$$\rho = \frac{3664.45}{3325.6} = 1.1019 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

(Or in g/L: $\rho = 1.1019 \times 10^{-3}$ g/L = $0.001102$ g/L.)

#### Part (b) — Mass Fraction to Mole Fraction

**Step 1 — Molecular weights:**
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$ g/mol
- $M_{CH_4} = 16.04$ g/mol
- $M_{CO} = 28.01$ g/mol
- $M_{N_2} = 28.02$ g/mol

**Step 2 — Average molecular weight using $\dfrac{1}{M_{avg}} = \sum \dfrac{w_i}{M_i}$:**

$$\frac{1}{M_{avg}} = \frac{0.10}{2.016} + \frac{0.20}{16.04} + \frac{0.30}{28.01} + \frac{0.40}{28.02}$$

$$\frac{1}{M_{avg}} = 0.04960 + 0.01247 + 0.01071 + 0.01428 = 0.08706$$

$$M_{avg} = \frac{1}{0.08706} = 11.486 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mole fractions using $y_i = w_i \cdot (M_{avg}/M_i)$:**

$$y_{H_2} = 0.10 \times \frac{11.486}{2.016} = 0.10 \times 5.6972 = 0.5697$$

$$y_{CH_4} = 0.20 \times \frac{11.486}{16.04} = 0.20 \times 0.7161 = 0.1432$$

$$y_{CO} = 0.30 \times \frac{11.486}{28.01} = 0.30 \times 0.4101 = 0.1230$$

$$y_{N_2} = 0.40 \times \frac{11.486}{28.02} = 0.40 \times 0.4099 = 0.1640$$

**Verification:** $0.5697 + 0.1432 + 0.1230 + 0.1640 = 0.9999 \approx 1.000$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $p_i / P_{total}$ with mole fraction** when components have different molecular weights. The ratio $p_i / P_{total}$ is the *mole fraction only for ideal gases* — wait, re-check: yes, $p_i / P_{total} = y_i$ always for ideal gases, since $p_i = y_i P_{total}$. However, the *mass fraction from partial pressures* requires $w_i = (p_i M_i) / \sum p_j M_j$. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to verify** $\sum y_i = 1$ or $\sum w_i = 1$ — KTU examiners reward this check with partial credit.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in $R$**: using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) with pressure in atm causes dimensional errors. Always match units: use $R = 0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K) for atm and L, or convert to SI.
> 4. **Arithmetic vs. weighted mean for $M_{avg}$**: a common wrong answer is $(M_1 + M_2 + ...)/n$, which ignores mole fractions. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **No verification of closure** ($\sum w_i = 1$ or $\sum y_i = 1$) at the end. Always include a check line; KTU values methodical presentation.
> 6. **Confusing partial pressure with partial volume**: they are numerically related through mole fraction but conceptually different — partial pressure has units of force/area; partial volume has units of volume.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Composition of Gaseous Mixtures**

- **Mole fraction** $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$; sums to 1; equals volume fraction for ideal gases.
- **Mass fraction** $w_i = m_i / m_{total}$; sums to 1.
- **Average molecular weight** $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i = 1 / \sum (w_i / M_i)$.
- **Conversion formulas:**
  - $w_i = y_i \cdot M_i / M_{avg}$
  - $y_i = w_i \cdot M_{avg} / M_i$
- **Dalton's Law:** $P_{total} = \sum p_i$, with $p_i = y_i P_{total}$.
- **Amagat's Law:** $V_{total} = \sum V_i$, with $V_i = y_i V_{total}$ (ideal gases).
- **Mixture density:** $\rho_{mix} = P M_{avg} / (R T)$.
- **Specific gravity** (vs air): $SG = M_{avg} / 28.97$.
- **Volume % ≡ Mole %** for ideal gases (Avogadro's principle at fixed $T, P$).
- **When partial pressures are given:** mole fraction = $p_i / P_{total}$, but mass fraction requires $w_i = (p_i M_i) / \sum (p_j M_j)$.
- **Closure check:** ALWAYS verify $\sum y_i = 1$ and $\sum w_i = 1$ at the end — KTU rewards systematic working.
- **Common molecular weights to memorize:** $H_2 = 2.016$, $N_2 = 28.02$, $O_2 = 32.00$, $CO_2 = 44.01$, $CH_4 = 16.04$, $CO = 28.01$, $C_2H_6 = 30.07$, $H_2O = 18.02$, air = 28.97 (all in g/mol).
- **Universal gas constant** $R$ values to remember:
  - $8.314$ J/(mol·K) [SI]
  - $0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K) [mixed practical]
  - $82.06$ cm³·atm/(mol·K) [CGS-volume]
  - $1.987$ cal/(mol·K) [thermal]
- **Engineering applications** to recall: combustion flue gas analysis, air separation, refrigerant blends, environmental stack gas monitoring, natural gas processing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Average Molecular Weight and Density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Average Molecular Weight and Density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Average Molecular Weight?

The **Average Molecular Weight** (also called the **Mean Molecular Mass**, $M_{avg}$ or $\bar{M}$) of a mixture is a single representative molar mass value assigned to a multi-component system so that the mixture behaves thermodynamically as if it were a single pure substance of that molecular weight.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCHT205 / Module 1):**
> "Average molecular weight of a gas mixture is the ratio of the total mass of the mixture to the total number of moles present. It is the mole-fraction-weighted sum of the individual component molecular weights."

Mathematically, for an ideal mixture:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{\text{Total Mass of Mixture}}{\text{Total Moles of Mixture}}$$

---

## 1.2 What is Density of a Mixture?

The **Density of a mixture** ($\rho_{mix}$) is the total mass per unit total volume of the combined system. For chemical engineering process calculations, density is required in material and energy balances, fluid flow, heat transfer, and reaction engineering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - For **GASES**, the standard reference basis for averaging is the **MOLE FRACTION ($y_i$)**.
> - For **LIQUIDS and SOLIDS**, the standard reference basis for averaging is the **MASS FRACTION ($w_i$)** or **VOLUME FRACTION ($\phi_i$)**, because liquid volumes are not always linearly additive.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — The Classroom of Students:**
> Imagine a classroom with 30 students: 10 of weight 40 kg, 10 of weight 50 kg, 10 of weight 60 kg. The *average weight* of the class is 50 kg. This is exactly the average molecular weight concept — the weighted average of individual values.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — Mixing Juices:**
> Suppose you mix 200 mL of honey (dense, $\rho = 1.4$ g/mL) with 200 mL of water ($\rho = 1.0$ g/mL). The total mass is $280 + 200 = 480$ g and total volume is $400$ mL, giving mixture density $\rho_{mix} = 1.2$ g/mL — a value *between* the two. Density of a mixture is a volume-weighted or mass-weighted compromise between the pure components.

**Standard physical constants** required in this module:

- **Universal Gas Constant, $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = 0.08206 \text{ L·atm/(mol·K)} = 10.732 \text{ psia·ft}^3\text{/(lbmol·°R)}$**
- **Standard Temperature, $T_0 = 273.15 \text{ K}$**
- **Standard Pressure, $P_0 = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 14.696 \text{ psia}$**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Choice of Basis

The fundamental reason a particular averaging basis is used lies in the **extensive property being conserved**:

| System Type | Conserved Extensive Property | Why? | Averaging Basis |
|-------------|------------------------------|------|-----------------|
| Ideal Gas Mixture | **Total Moles** ($n$) | Gases are perfectly miscible; volumes are additive by Avogadro's Law | **Mole Fraction** ($y_i$) |
| Liquid/Solid Solution | **Total Mass** ($m$) | Molecules of different sizes pack non-ideally; moles are not conserved on mixing | **Mass Fraction** ($w_i$) |

---

## 2.2 Average Molecular Weight — Logical Step Breakdown

**Step 1:** Define the mole fraction of component $i$ in a mixture of $c$ components:

$$y_i = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{c} n_j}$$

**Step 2:** The total mass is the sum of individual component masses:

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i M_i$$

**Step 3:** Divide total mass by total moles:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \frac{\sum n_i M_i}{\sum n_i}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $n_i = y_i \cdot n_{total}$:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{\sum (y_i n_{total}) M_i}{n_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i M_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result (Gas Mixture):**
> $$\boxed{\bar{M} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i M_i}$$

**For Liquid/Solid Mixtures** (using mass fraction $w_i$):

Since mass fractions add to 1, and $n_i = \frac{w_i}{M_i} \cdot m_{total}$:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{m_{total}}{\sum n_i} = \frac{m_{total}}{\sum (w_i m_{total}/M_i)} = \frac{1}{\sum w_i / M_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result (Liquid/Solid Mixture):**
> $$\boxed{\frac{1}{\bar{M}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{w_i}{M_i}}$$

---

## 2.3 Density of Mixtures — Logical Step Breakdown

### Case A: Ideal Gas Mixture

For each component behaving ideally: $\rho_i = \frac{P_i M_i}{RT} = \frac{y_i P M_i}{RT}$

Total mass per unit volume (densities are additive since volumes are additive for ideal gases):

$$\rho_{mix} = \sum \rho_i = \frac{P}{RT} \sum y_i M_i = \frac{P \bar{M}}{RT}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result — Ideal Gas Mixture Density:**
> $$\boxed{\rho_{mix} = \frac{P \bar{M}_{gas}}{RT}}$$

**Equivalent form** (using pure component densities at the same $T,P$):

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{y_i}{\rho_i}$$

### Case B: Liquid Mixture (Volumes Additive Assumption)

If the pure component volumes are assumed additive on mixing: $V_{mix} = \sum V_i$:

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{m_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{\sum \rho_i V_i}{\sum V_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \phi_i \rho_i$$

where $\phi_i$ is the **volume fraction** of component $i$.

If the masses are assumed additive (which they always are by definition): $m_{total} = \sum m_i$:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \frac{V_{total}}{m_{total}} = \frac{\sum m_i / \rho_i}{\sum m_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result — Liquid Mixture Density:**
> $$\boxed{\rho_{mix} = \sum \phi_i \rho_i \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \sum \frac{w_i}{\rho_i}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | System | Master Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|----------|--------|----------------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Avg. Mol. Wt. | Ideal Gas | $\bar{M} = \sum y_i M_i$ | $y_i$ = mole fraction | kg/kmol or g/mol |
| 2 | Avg. Mol. Wt. | Liquid/Solid | $1/\bar{M} = \sum (w_i/M_i)$ | $w_i$ = mass fraction | kg/kmol or g/mol |
| 3 | Density | Ideal Gas | $\rho_{mix} = P\bar{M}/(RT)$ | $P$ in Pa, $T$ in K | kg/m³ |
| 4 | Density | Ideal Gas (alt.) | $1/\rho_{mix} = \sum (y_i/\rho_i)$ | $\rho_i$ pure gas density | kg/m³ |
| 5 | Density | Liquid (vol. frac.) | $\rho_{mix} = \sum \phi_i \rho_i$ | $\phi_i$ = vol. fraction | kg/m³ |
| 6 | Density | Liquid (mass frac.) | $1/\rho_{mix} = \sum (w_i/\rho_i)$ | $w_i$ = mass fraction | kg/m³ |
| 7 | Mole Fraction | General | $y_i = n_i / \sum n_j$ | $n_i$ = moles | dimensionless |
| 8 | Mass Fraction | General | $w_i = m_i / \sum m_j$ | $m_i$ = mass | dimensionless |
| 9 | Volume Fraction | General | $\phi_i = V_i / \sum V_j$ | $V_i$ = volume | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often wrongly use $\bar{M} = \sum w_i M_i$ for *all* mixtures. This is INCORRECT. Mass fractions are only used in the *harmonic* form for liquid/solid mixtures, while gas mixtures always use the *linear* mole-fraction form.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Combustion Engineering:** Flue gas analysis (CO₂, H₂O, N₂, O₂) requires average molecular weight to compute air-fuel ratios.
- **Petroleum Refining:** Crude oil density and molecular weight govern distillation column design.
- **Refrigeration & Air-Conditioning:** Refrigerant mixture density (e.g., R-410A) is critical for compressor work.
- **Polymer Processing:** Polymer solution densities are used in spinning and extrusion.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Pollutant dispersion in air uses mixture density to determine buoyancy.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Basic Definition to Working Formula

### Derivation 1: Average Molecular Weight of an Ideal Gas Mixture

**Starting Premise:** A gas mixture contains $c$ components. The total mass and total moles are extensive properties.

**Step 1 — Define total mass:**
The mass of component $i$ in the mixture is $m_i = n_i \cdot M_i$. Summing over all components:

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i M_i$$

**Step 2 — Define total moles:**
By the principle of conservation, the total number of moles in the mixture is the arithmetic sum of individual moles (gases mix without reaction):

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i$$

**Step 3 — Apply the definition of average molecular weight:**

$$\bar{M} = \frac{m_{total}}{n_{total}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions from Steps 1 and 2:**

$$\bar{M} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i}$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $n_{total}$ to introduce mole fractions:**

$$\bar{M} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{c} \left(\frac{n_i}{n_{total}}\right) M_i}{\frac{1}{n_{total}}\sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i M_i}{1}$$

**Step 6 — Final simplified result:**

$$\bar{M} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i M_i \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Derivation 2: Average Molecular Weight of a Liquid/Solid Mixture

**Starting Premise:** For non-ideal condensed phases, *mole conservation* on mixing is not guaranteed. Therefore, we anchor on **mass** which is always strictly conserved.

**Step 1 — Express moles in terms of mass fraction:**
For component $i$, mass $m_i = w_i \cdot m_{total}$. Number of moles is:

$$n_i = \frac{m_i}{M_i} = \frac{w_i \, m_{total}}{M_i}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the moles:**

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i = m_{total} \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{w_i}{M_i}$$

**Step 3 — Apply definition of average molecular weight:**

$$\bar{M} = \frac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \frac{m_{total}}{m_{total} \sum (w_i/M_i)}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $m_{total}$:**

$$\bar{M} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{c} w_i / M_i}$$

**Step 5 — Final form:**

$$\frac{1}{\bar{M}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{w_i}{M_i} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### Derivation 3: Density of an Ideal Gas Mixture via Pure Component Densities

**Step 1 — Pure component density from ideal gas law:**
For pure gas $i$ at mixture temperature $T$ and mixture total pressure $P$:

$$\rho_i^{pure} = \frac{P M_i}{RT}$$

**Step 2 — Partial pressure of component $i$ in mixture:**
By Dalton's Law: $P_i = y_i P$. The density of component $i$ *contributing* to the mixture at the mixture $T,P$ is:

$$\rho_i = \frac{P_i M_i}{RT} = \frac{y_i P M_i}{RT} = y_i \cdot \rho_i^{pure}$$

**Step 3 — Total density is the sum of partial densities (volume additivity of ideal gases):**

$$\rho_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \rho_i = \sum y_i \cdot \rho_i^{pure}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent form using reciprocal summation:**

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \frac{1}{\sum y_i \rho_i^{pure}} \quad \text{(Not the standard KTU form)}$$

But, by substituting $\rho_i = y_i P M_i / (RT)$ in the original form:

$$\rho_{mix} = \sum y_i \cdot \frac{P M_i}{RT} = \frac{P}{RT} \sum y_i M_i = \frac{P \bar{M}}{RT} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 5 — Alternative reciprocal derivation:**
Starting from $\bar{M} = \sum y_i M_i$ and dividing both sides by $\bar{M}$:

$$1 = \sum y_i \frac{M_i}{\bar{M}}$$

Recognising that $\rho_i^{pure} = PM_i/(RT)$ and $\rho_{mix} = P\bar{M}/(RT)$, we have $M_i/\bar{M} = \rho_i^{pure}/\rho_{mix}$:

$$1 = \sum y_i \frac{\rho_i^{pure}}{\rho_{mix}} \implies \rho_{mix} = \sum y_i \rho_i^{pure}$$

And by algebraic rearrangement:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} \frac{y_i}{\rho_i^{pure}} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.2 Fully Worked KTU-Style Numerical Problems

### **Numerical Problem 1** — Average Molecular Weight of a Gas Mixture

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Pattern]**
> A flue gas from a combustion process has the following composition by volume: CO₂ = 12%, H₂O = 8%, O₂ = 5%, N₂ = 75%. Calculate the average molecular weight of the gas mixture.

**Given Data:**
- Mole fractions: $y_{CO_2} = 0.12$, $y_{H_2O} = 0.08$, $y_{O_2} = 0.05$, $y_{N_2} = 0.75$
- Molecular weights: $M_{CO_2} = 44.01$, $M_{H_2O} = 18.02$, $M_{O_2} = 32.00$, $M_{N_2} = 28.01$ g/mol

**Step 1 — Verify the sum of mole fractions:**

$$\sum y_i = 0.12 + 0.08 + 0.05 + 0.75 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula $\bar{M} = \sum y_i M_i$:**

$$\bar{M} = (0.12)(44.01) + (0.08)(18.02) + (0.05)(32.00) + (0.75)(28.01)$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term explicitly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Term 1 (CO}_2\text{):} \quad & (0.12)(44.01) = 5.2812 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Term 2 (H}_2\text{O):} \quad & (0.08)(18.02) = 1.4416 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Term 3 (O}_2\text{):} \quad & (0.05)(32.00) = 1.6000 \text{ g/mol} \\
\text{Term 4 (N}_2\text{):} \quad & (0.75)(28.01) = 21.0075 \text{ g/mol} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the contributions:**

$$\bar{M} = 5.2812 + 1.4416 + 1.6000 + 21.0075 = 29.33 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $\bar{M}_{flue\,gas} = 29.33 \text{ g/mol}$ (or equivalently $29.33$ kg/kmol)
> This is slightly less than air's $M = 28.97$ g/mol, which is physically reasonable for a slightly oxygen-depleted flue gas.

---

### **Numerical Problem 2** — Density of an Ideal Gas Mixture

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**
> A natural gas mixture has the composition: CH₄ = 80%, C₂H₆ = 15%, N₂ = 5% (mole basis). Calculate the density of this gas mixture at 30°C and 1.2 atm pressure.

**Given Data:**
- $T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \text{ K}$
- $P = 1.2 \text{ atm} = 1.2 \times 101.325 = 121.59 \text{ kPa} = 121590 \text{ Pa}$
- $M_{CH_4} = 16.04$, $M_{C_2H_6} = 30.07$, $M_{N_2} = 28.01$ g/mol

**Step 1 — Calculate the average molecular weight:**

$$\bar{M} = (0.80)(16.04) + (0.15)(30.07) + (0.05)(28.01)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_4: & \quad (0.80)(16.04) = 12.832 \\
\text{C}_2\text{H}_6: & \quad (0.15)(30.07) = 4.5105 \\
\text{N}_2: & \quad (0.05)(28.01) = 1.4005 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\bar{M} = 12.832 + 4.5105 + 1.4005 = 18.74 \text{ g/mol} = 0.01874 \text{ kg/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas density formula:**

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{P \bar{M}}{RT}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values with consistent SI units:**

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{(121590 \text{ Pa})(0.01874 \text{ kg/mol})}{(8.314 \text{ J/mol·K})(303.15 \text{ K})}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerator and denominator separately:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} & = 121590 \times 0.01874 = 2278.4 \text{ Pa·kg/mol} \\
\text{Denominator} & = 8.314 \times 303.15 = 2520.4 \text{ J/mol·K} = 2520.4 \text{ Pa·m}^3\text{/mol} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final computation:**

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{2278.4}{2520.4} = 0.904 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $\rho_{mix} = 0.904 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> This is lighter than air ($1.225$ kg/m³ at STP), as expected for a methane-rich natural gas.

---

### **Numerical Problem 3** — Density of a Liquid Mixture (Volume Additive)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
> A solution is prepared by mixing 200 L of ethanol ($\rho = 789$ kg/m³) with 300 L of water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) at 20°C. Assuming volumes are additive, calculate the density of the resulting mixture.

**Given Data:**
- $V_{ethanol} = 200 \text{ L} = 0.200 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho_{ethanol} = 789 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $V_{water} = 300 \text{ L} = 0.300 \text{ m}^3$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Compute individual masses:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{ethanol} & = \rho_{ethanol} \cdot V_{ethanol} = 789 \times 0.200 = 157.8 \text{ kg} \\
m_{water} & = \rho_{water} \cdot V_{water} = 1000 \times 0.300 = 300.0 \text{ kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass and total volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{total} & = 157.8 + 300.0 = 457.8 \text{ kg} \\
V_{total} & = 0.200 + 0.300 = 0.500 \text{ m}^3 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the mixture density formula:**

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{m_{total}}{V_{total}} = \frac{457.8}{0.500}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = 915.6 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the mass-fraction form:**

Mass fractions: $w_{ethanol} = 157.8/457.8 = 0.3447$, $w_{water} = 300.0/457.8 = 0.6553$

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \frac{0.3447}{789} + \frac{0.6553}{1000} = 4.369 \times 10^{-4} + 6.553 \times 10^{-4} = 1.0922 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{1}{1.0922 \times 10^{-3}} = 915.6 \text{ kg/m}^3 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $\rho_{mix} = 915.6 \text{ kg/m}^3$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Correct Averaging Basis

The following block diagram outlines the KTU-recommended decision tree for selecting the correct formula when computing average molecular weight or density.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given a MIXTURE] --> B{Physical State?}
    B -- GAS --> C[Basis: MOLE FRACTION yi]
    B -- LIQUID --> D{Basis Known?}
    B -- SOLID --> E[Basis: MASS FRACTION wi]

    C -- F1 --> F1[Average Mol. Wt.: Mavg = sum yi Mi]
    C -- F2 --> F2[Mixture Density: rho_mix = P*Mavg / RT]
    C -- F3 --> F3[Alt. Density: 1/rho_mix = sum yi/rhoi]

    D -- VOLUME FRACTION phi_i --> G1[rho_mix = sum phii * rhoi]
    D -- MASS FRACTION wi --> G2[1/rho_mix = sum wi/rhoi]

    E -- F4 --> E1[1/Mavg = sum wi/Mi]
    E -- F5 --> E2[1/rho_mix = sum wi/rhoi]

    F1 --> H[END: Apply Formula]
    F2 --> H
    F3 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    E1 --> H
    E2 --> H
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture: Computational Workflow for Mixture Properties

This block diagram represents the engineering workflow for determining mixture properties in a process calculation module — mimicking how a chemical engineer would chain the calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[/Input: Composition Data/] --> COMP{Composition Basis}
    COMP -- Mole percent --> CONV1[Convert to mole fraction yi]
    COMP -- Mass percent --> CONV2[Convert to mass fraction wi]
    COMP -- Volume percent --> CONV3[Convert to volume fraction phii]

    CONV1 --> MW1[Compute Mavg = sum yi Mi]
    CONV2 --> MW2[Compute 1/Mavg = sum wi/Mi]

    MW1 --> RHO1[Compute gas density: P*Mavg/RT]
    MW2 --> RHO2[Compute liquid density: sum phii*rhoi]

    CONV1 --> RHO3[Alt: 1/rhomix = sum yi/rhoi]
    CONV2 --> RHO4[Alt: 1/rhomix = sum wi/rhoi]

    RHO1 --> OUT[/Output: Mavg and rho_mix/]
    RHO2 --> OUT
    RHO3 --> OUT
    RHO4 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Conceptual Mapping of the Three Averaging Bases

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Bases [THREE AVERAGING BASES]
        B1[Mole Fraction yi - GAS MIXTURES]
        B2[Mass Fraction wi - LIQUID and SOLID MIXTURES]
        B3[Volume Fraction phii - LIQUID MIXTURES only]
    end

    subgraph Properties [RESULTING MIXTURE PROPERTIES]
        P1[Average Molecular Weight Mavg]
        P2[Density rho_mix]
    end

    B1 -- Linear sum --> P1
    B2 -- Harmonic sum --> P1
    B1 -- rho = P Mavg / RT --> P2
    B2 -- 1/rho = sum wi/rhoi --> P2
    B3 -- rho = sum phii*rhoi --> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each node in the Mermaid diagrams is named using a *purely alphanumeric identifier* (e.g., `node1`, `B1`, `P1`, `Bases`) to comply with the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards. Labels inside quoted text are kept raw, without markdown formatting, to prevent parser failures.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**
**Define average molecular weight of a gaseous mixture. Why are mole fractions used as the weighting basis for gases rather than mass fractions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The average molecular weight $\bar{M}$ of a gaseous mixture is defined as the ratio of the total mass of the mixture to the total number of moles present in it. Mathematically:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{\text{Total mass of mixture}}{\text{Total moles of mixture}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i M_i$$

Mole fractions are used as the weighting basis for gases (rather than mass fractions) because **gases mix ideally and the total number of moles is strictly conserved upon mixing** (no chemical reaction, no volume change due to molecular packing). Avogadro's Law guarantees that equal volumes of gases at the same $T$ and $P$ contain equal numbers of molecules, making mole fraction a natural measure of the *number* of molecules — which is what governs thermodynamic behaviour. For liquids and solids, volumes are not additive on mixing, so the harmonic mass-fraction formula is preferred.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — formula; 1 mark — reason for mole-fraction basis)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
**A gas mixture contains 40 mol% N₂ and 60 mol% CO₂. Calculate the average molecular weight of the mixture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Given:** $y_{N_2} = 0.40$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.60$
$M_{N_2} = 28.01$ g/mol, $M_{CO_2} = 44.01$ g/mol

**Formula:**

$$\bar{M} = \sum y_i M_i = y_{N_2} M_{N_2} + y_{CO_2} M_{CO_2}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\bar{M} = (0.40)(28.01) + (0.60)(44.01)$$

$$\bar{M} = 11.204 + 26.406 = 37.61 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $\bar{M} = 37.61$ g/mol (or equivalently 37.61 kg/kmol) *(Full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **average molecular weight of an ideal gas mixture** starting from the fundamental definition. Explain clearly why mole fractions are the natural weighting basis for gases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gaseous mixture at 2.5 atm and 80°C has the following composition by volume: H₂ = 30%, CH₄ = 20%, CO₂ = 15%, and the balance N₂. Calculate:
   (i) The average molecular weight of the mixture. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The density of the mixture in kg/m³. **(4 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider an ideal gas mixture containing $c$ components with $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_c$ moles of each, with molecular weights $M_1, M_2, \ldots, M_c$.

**Step 1:** The total mass of the mixture is the sum of the individual masses (mass is conserved):

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i M_i$$

**Step 2:** The total number of moles in the mixture is the sum of individual moles (mole conservation for ideal gas mixing without reaction):

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i$$

**Step 3:** By definition, the average molecular weight is total mass divided by total moles:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i M_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{c} n_i}$$

**Step 4:** Define the mole fraction as $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$, so $n_i = y_i n_{total}$:

$$\bar{M} = \frac{\sum y_i n_{total} M_i}{n_{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i M_i$$

**Step 5 (Physical Reasoning):** Mole fractions are the natural basis for gases because:
- Ideal gas behaviour follows Avogadro's hypothesis: equal volumes of gases at the same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules.
- For ideal gases, the partial pressure $P_i = y_i P$ is linearly related to $y_i$, making $y_i$ the natural measure of a component's contribution to the system.
- Mole fractions are conserved during ideal mixing (no volume change, no reaction), so they accurately represent the *intensive* composition of the gas phase.

$$\boxed{\bar{M} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} y_i M_i} \quad \text{(1 mark for final boxed result)}$$

*Mark distribution: 1 mark for step 1, 1 mark for step 2, 1 mark for step 3, 1 mark for step 4, 3 marks for the physical reasoning and final answer.*

---

#### **Part (b)(i) — Average Molecular Weight (3 marks):**

**Given composition by volume = mole fractions** for ideal gases:
- $y_{H_2} = 0.30$, $y_{CH_4} = 0.20$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.15$
- Balance $N_2$: $y_{N_2} = 1.00 - 0.30 - 0.20 - 0.15 = 0.35$
- $M_{H_2} = 2.016$, $M_{CH_4} = 16.04$, $M_{CO_2} = 44.01$, $M_{N_2} = 28.01$ g/mol

$$\bar{M} = (0.30)(2.016) + (0.20)(16.04) + (0.15)(44.01) + (0.35)(28.01)$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& = 0.6048 + 3.208 + 6.6015 + 9.8035 \\
& = 20.218 \text{ g/mol} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (b)(i):** $\bar{M} = 20.22$ g/mol = 0.02022 kg/mol *(3 marks: 1 mark for setup, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer)*

---

#### **Part (b)(ii) — Density of Mixture (4 marks):**

**Given:** $P = 2.5$ atm $= 2.5 \times 101325 = 253312.5$ Pa, $T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15$ K

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{P \bar{M}}{RT}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{253312.5 \times 0.02022}{8.314 \times 353.15}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} & = 253312.5 \times 0.02022 = 5122.0 \text{ Pa·kg/mol} \\
\text{Denominator} & = 8.314 \times 353.15 = 2936.5 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{5122.0}{2936.5} = 1.744 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (b)(ii):** $\rho_{mix} = 1.744$ kg/m³ *(4 marks: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for unit conversion, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final numerical result)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]*

**(a)** Discuss the various formulae for computing the **density of liquid and gaseous mixtures**. Show how the formula for a gas mixture can be derived from the ideal gas law. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid solution contains 30% by mass of sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄, $\rho = 1830$ kg/m³) and 70% by mass of water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) at 25°C. Calculate:
   (i) The average molecular weight of the solution. **(3 marks)**
   (ii) The density of the solution in kg/m³. **(4 marks)**

**Given:** $M_{H_2SO_4} = 98.08$ g/mol, $M_{H_2O} = 18.02$ g/mol

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Discussion & Derivation (7 marks):**

For an **ideal gas mixture**, the partial density of each component is:

$$\rho_i = \frac{P_i M_i}{RT} = \frac{y_i P M_i}{RT}$$

Summing all partial densities (volumes are additive for ideal gases):

$$\rho_{mix} = \sum \rho_i = \frac{P}{RT} \sum y_i M_i = \frac{P \bar{M}}{RT}$$

The alternative reciprocal form: $\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \sum \frac{y_i}{\rho_i^{pure}}$

For a **liquid mixture** with additive volumes (ideal solution assumption):

$$\rho_{mix} = \sum \phi_i \rho_i \quad \text{(volume-fraction form)}$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \sum \frac{w_i}{\rho_i} \quad \text{(mass-fraction form)}$$

The mass-fraction form is more commonly used because it avoids the need to know component volumes, and mass is always strictly conserved.

*Mark distribution: 2 marks for gas mixture derivation, 2 marks for liquid mixture formulas, 2 marks for comparison/justification, 1 mark for final neat summary.*

---

#### **Part (b)(i) — Average Molecular Weight of Solution (3 marks):**

**Given:** $w_{H_2SO_4} = 0.30$, $w_{H_2O} = 0.70$, $M_{H_2SO_4} = 98.08$ g/mol, $M_{H_2O} = 18.02$ g/mol

For a **liquid** mixture, use the harmonic formula:

$$\frac{1}{\bar{M}} = \frac{w_{H_2SO_4}}{M_{H_2SO_4}} + \frac{w_{H_2O}}{M_{H_2O}}$$

$$\frac{1}{\bar{M}} = \frac{0.30}{98.08} + \frac{0.70}{18.02}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{0.30}{98.08} & = 3.0586 \times 10^{-3} \\
\frac{0.70}{18.02} & = 38.846 \times 10^{-3} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\frac{1}{\bar{M}} = 41.905 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/g}$$

$$\bar{M} = \frac{1}{41.905 \times 10^{-3}} = 23.86 \text{ g/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (b)(i):** $\bar{M} = 23.86$ g/mol *(3 marks: 1 mark for choosing correct harmonic formula for liquids, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final result)*

---

#### **Part (b)(ii) — Density of Liquid Solution (4 marks):**

Using the mass-fraction form:

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \frac{w_{H_2SO_4}}{\rho_{H_2SO_4}} + \frac{w_{H_2O}}{\rho_{H_2O}}$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = \frac{0.30}{1830} + \frac{0.70}{1000}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{0.30}{1830} & = 1.6393 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/kg} \\
\frac{0.70}{1000} & = 7.0000 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/kg} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\frac{1}{\rho_{mix}} = 8.6393 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3\text{/kg}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = \frac{1}{8.6393 \times 10^{-4}} = 1157.5 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer (b)(ii):** $\rho_{mix} = 1157.5$ kg/m³ *(4 marks: 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for inversion, 1 mark for final answer)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost (PCCHT205 / Module 1):**
>
> 1. **Confusing mass and mole fractions (–2 marks):** Students often treat mass percent and mole percent as interchangeable. They are NOT. Mass percent is what you weigh on a balance; mole percent is the count of molecules.
>
> 2. **Using $\bar{M} = \sum w_i M_i$ for liquid mixtures (–3 marks):** This is the most common error. The correct formula for liquid/solid mixtures is the **harmonic** form: $1/\bar{M} = \sum (w_i/M_i)$.
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency in density problems (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert temperature to Kelvin, pressure to Pa, and $\bar{M}$ to kg/kmol leads to answers off by 1000×. Always write units explicitly.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Dalton's Law equivalence (–1 mark):** When a problem says "composition by volume", it is **equivalent to mole fraction** for ideal gases. Always state this in the solution.
>
> 5. **Mixing up volume-additive and mass-additive assumptions (–2 marks):** For liquids, the assumption of volume additivity may not always hold. The mass-additive form is the *safer* general approach.
>
> 6. **Not verifying the mole-fraction sum (–1 mark):** Always show that $\sum y_i = 1$ as a sanity check. KTU examiners award a free mark for this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Average Molecular Weight and Density):**
>
> - The average molecular weight of a **gas** is a **mole-fraction weighted LINEAR sum**: $\bar{M} = \sum y_i M_i$
> - The average molecular weight of a **liquid/solid** is a **mass-fraction weighted HARMONIC sum**: $1/\bar{M} = \sum (w_i / M_i)$
> - Volume percent = Mole percent (for ideal gases only).
> - The density of an **ideal gas mixture** is given by $\rho_{mix} = P\bar{M}/(RT)$. This is the *single most important* equation in the topic.
> - The alternative form $1/\rho_{mix} = \sum (y_i / \rho_i^{pure})$ holds for ideal gases and is useful when pure-component densities are tabulated.
> - For **liquid mixtures** (assuming volume additivity): $\rho_{mix} = \sum \phi_i \rho_i$ where $\phi_i$ is the volume fraction.
> - For **liquid mixtures** (mass-additive form): $1/\rho_{mix} = \sum (w_i / \rho_i)$ — the **most generally reliable** form.
> - The universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) in SI; or $0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K) in CGS.
> - Always convert to **Kelvin** for $T$ and **Pa (or atm)** for $P$ before applying the ideal gas density formula.
> - Mass fractions and mole fractions can be interconverted using: $w_i = \frac{y_i M_i}{\sum y_j M_j}$ and $y_i = \frac{w_i/M_i}{\sum (w_j/M_j)}$.
> - The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to *state* the assumption (ideal gas, additive volume) explicitly in long-answer solutions.
> - Always perform a **dimensional sanity check** on the final answer.
> - Memorise the molecular weights of the most common gases: H₂ = 2, He = 4, CH₄ = 16, N₂ = 28, O₂ = 32, CO₂ = 44, SO₂ = 64.
> - For a binary gas mixture: $\bar{M} = y_1 M_1 + (1-y_1) M_2 = M_2 + y_1 (M_1 - M_2)$ — a useful shortcut.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ideal Gas Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Ideal Gas Law — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Ideal Gas Law** (also called the **General Gas Equation** or **Equation of State for an Ideal Gas**) is a fundamental constitutive equation in chemical engineering thermodynamics that relates four state variables of a gaseous system — **Pressure (P)**, **Volume (V)**, **Temperature (T)**, and the **Amount of Substance (n)** — through a single universal proportionality constant.

In its canonical form, the law is expressed as:

$$PV = nRT$$

where **R** is the **Universal Gas Constant** (also called the *molar gas constant*).

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (CO1, Bloom Level: Remember/Understand):**
> An **ideal gas** is a hypothetical gas whose molecules occupy negligible space and have no intermolecular forces of attraction or repulsion. The ideal gas law is a limiting equation of state that real gases obey exactly only at **low pressures** and **high temperatures** (conditions under which intermolecular forces become insignificant and molecular volume is negligible compared to the container volume).

## 1.2 The Universal Gas Constant (R)

The constant **R** is *not* dimensionless — its numerical value depends on the system of units used. The CODATA recommended value is:

$$R = 8.314462618\ \text{J} \cdot \text{mol}^{-1} \cdot \text{K}^{-1}$$

For typical chemical engineering calculations, the following frequently used values of **R** must be memorized:

| Numerical Value | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **0.082057** | L·atm/(mol·K) | Volume in L, Pressure in atm |
| **0.083145** | L·bar/(mol·K) | Volume in L, Pressure in bar |
| **8.3145** | J/(mol·K) = Pa·m³/(mol·K) | SI Units |
| **1.987** | cal/(mol·K) | Thermochemistry |
| **10.7316** | ft³·psia/(lbmol·°R) | FPS Units |
| **1545.35** | ft·lbf/(lbmol·°R) | Energy form, FPS |

> [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic Tip for KTU Exams:**
> Remember "**0.0821**" for the L·atm form — it is the most common form in KTU numerical problems because pressures are often given in atm and volumes in litres.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **gymnasium full of bouncing rubber balls** (the gas molecules) that are thrown in randomly by a child standing at the door. The pressure exerted on the gym walls is the total force of all ball-wall collisions per unit area per unit time.

- **More balls (more moles, n) → more collisions → higher pressure (P)**
- **Bigger room (larger volume, V) → balls hit walls less frequently → lower pressure (P)**
- **Faster balls (higher temperature, T) → harder, more frequent collisions → higher pressure (P)**
- **Smaller room (compress volume) → balls collide more often → higher pressure (P)**

The Ideal Gas Law mathematically captures all four of these intuitive observations into a single elegant equation.

> [!TIP] > **Conceptual Hook:** Think of the ideal gas as a *perfect* gas — molecules are infinitely small (no volume) and infinitely polite (no attractive or repulsive forces). Real gases approach this behavior at *low P* and *high T*. The Ideal Gas Law is the **chemical engineer's first tool** before invoking more complex equations of state (van der Waals, Peng–Robinson, Redlich–Kwong, Virial, etc.).

## 1.4 The Five Pillars: Underlying Experimental Gas Laws

The Ideal Gas Law is a *combination* of five fundamental experimentally derived laws. Understanding each one is essential for both derivations and problem-solving.

### (a) Boyle's Law (1662) — Isothermal Process
At constant temperature and constant amount of gas, the volume of a gas is **inversely proportional** to its pressure.

$$V \propto \dfrac{1}{P} \quad \text{(at constant } T, n) \implies P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

### (b) Charles's Law (1787) — Isobaric Process
At constant pressure and constant amount of gas, the volume of a gas is **directly proportional** to its absolute temperature.

$$V \propto T \quad \text{(at constant } P, n) \implies \dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$$

### (c) Avogadro's Law (1811) — Isothermal–Isobaric Process
At constant temperature and pressure, the volume of a gas is **directly proportional** to the number of moles present.

$$V \propto n \quad \text{(at constant } T, P) \implies \dfrac{V_1}{n_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{n_2}$$

### (d) Gay-Lussac's Law (1809) — Isochoric Process
At constant volume and constant amount of gas, the pressure of a gas is **directly proportional** to its absolute temperature.

$$P \propto T \quad \text{(at constant } V, n) \implies \dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$$

### (e) Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures (1801)
The total pressure exerted by a non-reacting mixture of gases is equal to the **sum of the partial pressures** of the individual component gases.

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = P_A + P_B + P_C + \cdots$$

Each partial pressure is the pressure that the component would exert **if it alone occupied the entire volume** at the same temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:**
> Dalton's Law is *not* strictly derived from the Ideal Gas Law — it is an *assumption* used to extend the Ideal Gas Law to **non-reacting gas mixtures**. The combination $P_i V = n_i R T$ applied to each component in a mixture sharing the same V and T is the cornerstone of all combustion and gas-mixture calculations in Chemical Engineering.

## 1.5 Standard Reference Conditions (Critical for KTU Numerical Problems)

In all ideal gas problems, you will encounter reference states. Memorize these — examiners expect unit-conversion perfection.

| Standard State | Temperature | Pressure | Molar Volume |
|---|---|---|---|
| **STP (IUPAC, since 1982)** | **273.15 K** (0 °C) | **1 bar** (10⁵ Pa) | **22.711 L/mol** |
| **Old STP (often still used in textbooks)** | 273.15 K (0 °C) | 1 atm (101.325 kPa) | 22.414 L/mol |
| **NTP (Normal T & P)** | 293.15 K (20 °C) | 1 atm | 24.0 L/mol (approx.) |
| **SATP** | 298.15 K (25 °C) | 1 bar | 24.79 L/mol |
| **Old Standard (US)** | 60 °F = 520 °R | 1 atm | 379 ft³/lbmol |

> [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Pitfall:** If a problem says "at STP" without specifying which, *use 22.4 L/mol* — this is the convention in nearly all Indian engineering textbooks and previous KTU question papers. If the problem gives explicit P and T, **never** use the molar volume shortcut — always use PV = nRT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Module 1: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions of the Kinetic Theory (Why the Law Works)

The Ideal Gas Law is built on the **Kinetic Theory of Gases**, which assumes the following for an *ideal* gas:

1. **Negligible Molecular Volume:** The actual volume occupied by the gas molecules themselves is *infinitely small* compared to the volume of the container. The gas is essentially "all empty space" with point-mass molecules.
2. **No Intermolecular Forces:** There are *no* attractive or repulsive forces between molecules except during perfectly elastic collisions.
3. **Random Motion:** Molecules move in *completely random* directions with a Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution of speeds.
4. **Elastic Collisions:** All collisions (molecule–molecule and molecule–wall) are *perfectly elastic*, conserving kinetic energy and momentum.
5. **Newtonian Mechanics Apply:** At ordinary temperatures, relativistic effects are negligible.
6. **Mean Free Path >> Molecular Diameter:** Molecules travel very long distances between collisions.

> [!NOTE]
> These assumptions **break down** at *high pressures* (molecular volume becomes significant) and *low temperatures* (intermolecular forces become significant, eventually causing condensation). This is when we move to **Real Gas Equations of State** like van der Waals, Redlich–Kwong, Peng–Robinson, or the **Compressibility Factor (Z) Chart** approach.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic of the Ideal Gas Law (Step-by-Step Logic)

The ideal gas law is **derived** by combining the three primary gas laws:

**Step 1:** From **Boyle's Law** (at constant T, n):
$$V \propto \dfrac{1}{P}$$

**Step 2:** From **Charles's Law** (at constant P, n):
$$V \propto T$$

**Step 3:** From **Avogadro's Law** (at constant T, P):
$$V \propto n$$

**Step 4:** Combine all three proportionalities:
$$V \propto \dfrac{nT}{P}$$

**Step 5:** Introduce a proportionality constant **R**:
$$V = \dfrac{nRT}{P} \implies \boxed{PV = nRT}$$

**Step 6:** Verify the constant's value experimentally at known conditions (1 mol at STP):
$$R = \dfrac{PV}{nT} = \dfrac{(1\ \text{atm})(22.414\ \text{L})}{(1\ \text{mol})(273.15\ \text{K})} = 0.08206\ \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master This Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **complete formula bank** you need for any KTU question on Module 1 — Ideal Gas Law. Print it. Memorize it. Re-derive it on the day of the exam.

| # | Formula | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PV = nRT$ | Canonical ideal gas law | Most general form |
| 2 | $PV = \dfrac{m}{M}RT$ | Mass-based form (m = mass in g/kg, M = molar mass) | When mass is given |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{\rho RT}{M}$ | Density form ($\rho$ = mass/volume = m/V) | Density problems |
| 4 | $\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$ | Rearranged density form | Find density of gas at (P, T) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Combined Gas Law (fixed n) | Process changes (no moles added/removed) |
| 6 | $P_i = y_i P_{total} = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}} P_{total}$ | Partial pressure (Dalton's) | Gas mixtures |
| 7 | $V_i = y_i V_{total}$ | Partial volume (Amagat's) | Gas mixtures by volume |
| 8 | $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_j}$ | Mole fraction definition | Mixture composition |
| 9 | $M_{avg} = \sum_{i} y_i M_i$ | Average molecular weight of gas mixture | Mixture properties |
| 10 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{P M_{avg}}{RT}$ | Density of gas mixture | Density of gas mixtures |
| 11 | $\dfrac{n_A}{n_B} = \dfrac{P_A}{P_B} = \dfrac{V_A}{V_B}$ | Mole ratio from partial P or V | Stoichiometry from gas volumes |
| 12 | $Z = \dfrac{PV}{nRT}$ | Compressibility factor (real vs ideal) | Real gas corrections |
| 13 | $V_m = \dfrac{V}{n} = \dfrac{RT}{P}$ | Molar volume | Volume per mole |
| 14 | $N = n N_A = \dfrac{PV N_A}{RT}$ | Number of molecules | Statistical mechanics flavor |
| 15 | $\bar{u}^2 = \dfrac{3RT}{M}$ | RMS speed of gas molecules (M in kg/mol) | Kinetic theory extension |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Ideal Gas Law is **not just academic** — it is the workhorse of chemical engineering calculations. Applications include:

- **Reactor Design (PFR, CSTR):** Determining residence time $\tau = V/\dot{V}$ where gas volumetric flow $\dot{V}$ depends on T and P.
- **Compressors & Expanders:** Calculating work input/output using $\dot{W} = \dot{n} R (T_2 - T_1) / (\gamma - 1)$ for isentropic processes with ideal gas.
- **Storage Tank Sizing:** Computing the amount of gas (in moles or kg) stored in pressurized vessels.
- **Flow Meters (Orifice, Venturi):** Converting mass flow to volumetric flow using $\dot{V} = \dot{m} RT / (PM)$.
- **Combustion Analysis:** Calculating air-fuel ratios, flue gas composition, and excess air using partial pressures.
- **Distillation & Absorption:** Vapor-phase composition calculations in VLE.
- **Atmospheric & Environmental Engineering:** Stack gas analysis, emission calculations.
- **Petroleum Engineering:** Gas reservoir volumetrics, gas-oil ratio (GOR) calculations.
- **Cryogenics & Refrigeration:** Properties of gases near liquefaction.
- **Process Safety:** Pressure relief valve sizing for gas systems.

> [!TIP] > **Industry Note:** Over **95%** of all undergraduate chemical engineering process calculations use the Ideal Gas Law as a starting approximation. Only when the reduced pressure $P_r > 0.8$ or reduced temperature $T_r < 2$ do we move to a real gas equation of state (covered in Module 2 of the syllabus).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Module 1: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal Gas Law from First Principles

### Derivation Route 1: Combining Boyle's, Charles's, and Avogadro's Laws

**Given:**
- Boyle's Law: $V \propto 1/P$ (at constant T, n)
- Charles's Law: $V \propto T$ (at constant P, n)
- Avogadro's Law: $V \propto n$ (at constant T, P)

**Step 1: Combine the three proportionalities into a single relation.**

Since volume depends on three independent variables (P, T, n), we write:

$$V \propto f(P, T, n)$$

Each law tells us how V depends on one variable when the other two are fixed. By the principle of *separable proportionalities* (valid only when each proportionality is independent of the others), we combine them as a product:

$$V \propto \dfrac{nT}{P}$$

**Step 2: Introduce the proportionality constant R.**

The constant R must be a *universal* constant (same for all gases) because Avogadro's Law already says equal volumes contain equal moles. Hence R is the **Universal Gas Constant**.

$$V = R \cdot \dfrac{nT}{P}$$

**Step 3: Rearrange to the canonical form.**

$$\boxed{PV = nRT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensional Check:** [P] = Pa = N/m²; [V] = m³; [n] = mol; [T] = K. Product PV = N·m = J (energy). Product nRT = mol × (J/mol·K) × K = J. ✓ Units are consistent.

**Step 4: Determine the value of R at standard conditions.**

At STP (old convention): P = 1 atm = 101,325 Pa, T = 273.15 K, n = 1 mol, V = 22.414 L = 0.022414 m³.

$$R = \dfrac{PV}{nT} = \dfrac{(101325\ \text{Pa})(0.022414\ \text{m}^3)}{(1\ \text{mol})(273.15\ \text{K})}$$

$$R = \dfrac{2271.10\ \text{J/mol}}{273.15\ \text{K}} = 8.3145\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

In litre-atm units:

$$R = \dfrac{(1\ \text{atm})(22.414\ \text{L})}{(1\ \text{mol})(273.15\ \text{K})} = 0.08206\ \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$$

---

### Derivation Route 2: From the Mass-Based Form

Starting from $PV = nRT$, we use the relation $n = m/M$ (where m = mass, M = molar mass):

**Step 1:** Substitute $n = m/M$:

$$PV = \left(\dfrac{m}{M}\right) RT$$

**Step 2:** Define density $\rho = m/V$, so $m = \rho V$:

$$PV = \left(\dfrac{\rho V}{M}\right) RT$$

**Step 3:** Cancel V from both sides:

$$P = \dfrac{\rho RT}{M}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for density:

$$\boxed{\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This form is *extremely useful* in industry — given a gas's molecular weight, temperature, and pressure, you can directly compute its density. For example, at 1 atm and 25 °C (298.15 K), the density of nitrogen (M = 28.014 g/mol) is:

$$\rho_{N_2} = \dfrac{(1\ \text{atm})(28.014\ \text{g/mol})}{(0.08206\ \text{L·atm/mol·K})(298.15\ \text{K})} = 1.145\ \text{g/L}$$

This matches the experimental value within 0.1%, confirming the validity of the ideal gas law for N₂ at these conditions.

---

### Derivation Route 3: Derivation of Average Molecular Weight for Gas Mixtures

Consider a gas mixture containing components $i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, k$ with mole fractions $y_i$ and individual molar masses $M_i$.

**Step 1:** The total mass of the mixture is the sum of component masses:

$$m_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i M_i$$

**Step 2:** The total number of moles is:

$$n_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i$$

**Step 3:** The average (or *apparent*) molecular weight of the mixture is defined as:

$$M_{avg} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{\sum n_i M_i}{\sum n_i}$$

**Step 4:** Divide numerator and denominator by $n_{total}$. Recall that $y_i = n_i / n_{total}$:

$$M_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum (n_i/n_{total}) M_i}{\sum (n_i/n_{total})} = \dfrac{\sum y_i M_i}{\sum y_i}$$

**Step 5:** Since $\sum y_i = 1$ (definition of mole fraction), we obtain:

$$\boxed{M_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} y_i M_i}$$

This formula is the **standard tool** for solving all gas mixture problems in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Full Step-by-Step Solution (KTU Pattern)

### **Problem (KTU-Style):**
A gas mixture contains **60 mol% methane (CH₄)**, **30 mol% ethane (C₂H₆)**, and **10 mol% nitrogen (N₂)**. The mixture is contained in a 5.0 m³ vessel at a gauge pressure of 200 kPa and a temperature of 80 °C. The atmospheric pressure is 101.325 kPa.

**Calculate:**
1. The **average molecular weight** of the mixture.
2. The **total mass** of gas in the vessel.
3. The **density** of the gas mixture.

**Given Data:**
- $y_{CH_4} = 0.60$, $M_{CH_4} = 16.04\ \text{g/mol}$
- $y_{C_2H_6} = 0.30$, $M_{C_2H_6} = 30.07\ \text{g/mol}$
- $y_{N_2} = 0.10$, $M_{N_2} = 28.01\ \text{g/mol}$
- $V = 5.0\ \text{m}^3 = 5000\ \text{L}$
- $P_{gauge} = 200\ \text{kPa}$, $P_{atm} = 101.325\ \text{kPa}$
- $T = 80\ ^\circ\text{C} = 353.15\ \text{K}$

### **Part (a): Average Molecular Weight**

**Step 1:** Apply the mole-fraction-weighted average formula:

$$M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i = y_{CH_4} M_{CH_4} + y_{C_2H_6} M_{C_2H_6} + y_{N_2} M_{N_2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$M_{avg} = (0.60)(16.04) + (0.30)(30.07) + (0.10)(28.01)$$

$$M_{avg} = 9.624 + 9.021 + 2.801 = 21.446\ \text{g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_{avg} \approx 21.45\ \text{g/mol}}$$

### **Part (b): Total Mass of Gas**

**Step 1:** Convert pressure to absolute:

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm} = 200 + 101.325 = 301.325\ \text{kPa}$$

$$P_{abs} = 3.01325\ \text{bar} = 2.9733\ \text{atm}$$

(Recall: 1 atm = 101.325 kPa, so divide kPa by 101.325 to get atm)

**Step 2:** Use the mass-based form of the ideal gas law:

$$m_{total} = \dfrac{P V M_{avg}}{R T}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values (using R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K):

$$m_{total} = \dfrac{(2.9733\ \text{atm})(5000\ \text{L})(21.446\ \text{g/mol})}{(0.08206\ \text{L·atm/mol·K})(353.15\ \text{K})}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator and denominator separately:

$$\text{Numerator} = 2.9733 \times 5000 \times 21.446 = 318{,}810\ \text{atm·L·g/mol}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 0.08206 \times 353.15 = 28.977\ \text{L·atm/mol}$$

**Step 5:** Divide:

$$m_{total} = \dfrac{318{,}810}{28.977} = 11{,}002\ \text{g} \approx 11.0\ \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{total} \approx 11.00\ \text{kg}}$$

### **Part (c): Density of the Gas Mixture**

**Step 1:** Density is mass divided by volume:

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{V} = \dfrac{11.00\ \text{kg}}{5.0\ \text{m}^3} = 2.20\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Verify using the density form $\rho = PM_{avg}/RT$:

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{(301{,}325\ \text{Pa})(0.021446\ \text{kg/mol})}{(8.3145\ \text{J/mol·K})(353.15\ \text{K})}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{6462.5}{2936.0} = 2.201\ \text{kg/m}^3 \ \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{mix} \approx 2.20\ \text{kg/m}^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Self-Check Rule:** Always cross-verify using an alternative method. If the two methods give the same answer (within rounding), your calculation is almost certainly correct. This is a powerful KTU exam technique for partial-mark recovery if you make a small arithmetic slip.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Ideal Gas Formula to Use?

| Given in Problem | Find | Use This Formula |
|---|---|---|
| P, V, T | n | $n = PV/RT$ |
| P, V, T, M | m | $m = PVM/RT$ |
| P, T, M | $\rho$ | $\rho = PM/RT$ |
| m, V, T, M | P | $P = mRT/(VM)$ |
| State 1 (P, V, T) → State 2 | State 2 (one unknown) | $P_1V_1/T_1 = P_2V_2/T_2$ |
| Mole fractions $y_i$ and $M_i$ | $M_{avg}$ | $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ |
| Partial pressures $P_i$ | Mole fractions | $y_i = P_i/P_{total}$ |
| Partial volumes $V_i$ | Mole fractions | $y_i = V_i/V_{total}$ |
| P, T, mixture composition | $\rho_{mix}$ | $\rho_{mix} = PM_{avg}/RT$ |
| Gas at non-ideal conditions (high P, low T) | Correction | $Z = PV/(nRT)$ |

> [!TIP] > **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Note):**
> If $P_{reduced} = P/P_c < 0.5$ AND $T_{reduced} = T/T_c > 2.0$, the ideal gas law is accurate to within **1%**. Always check this before applying the Ideal Gas Law to real gas data. The quantities $P_c$ and $T_c$ are the *critical pressure* and *critical temperature* of the gas.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Module 1: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map: The Hierarchy of Gas Laws

The following Mermaid concept map shows how the five empirical gas laws (Boyle, Charles, Gay-Lussac, Avogadro, Dalton) combine and extend to form the **Ideal Gas Law** and its **mixture applications**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Empirical Gas Laws - 17th to 19th Century Experiments]
    A --> B[Boyle's Law 1662: V proportional to 1/P at constant T and n]
    A --> C[Charles's Law 1787: V proportional to T at constant P and n]
    A --> D[Gay-Lussac's Law 1809: P proportional to T at constant V and n]
    A --> E[Avogadro's Law 1811: V proportional to n at constant T and P]
    A --> F[Dalton's Law 1801: P total equals sum of Pi]

    B --> G[Combined: PV equals nRT divided by constant]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    G --> H[IDEAL GAS LAW: PV equals nRT]

    F --> I[Gas Mixture Applications]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Partial Pressure: Pi equals yi times P total]
    I --> K[Average Molecular Weight: M avg equals sum of yi times Mi]
    I --> L[Mixture Density: rho mix equals P M avg over RT]

    H --> M[Real Gas Corrections]
    M --> N[Compressibility Factor: Z equals PV over nRT]
    M --> O[Van der Waals Equation - Module 2]
    M --> P[Virial Equation - Module 2]

    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    style A fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this map:** The golden box (**IDEAL GAS LAW**) is the apex — it is the *integrated result* of the four blue boxes (empirical laws) above it. The yellow boxes (Mixture Applications) and red boxes (Real Gas Extensions) represent the *downstream* applications and corrections that KTU expects you to handle in Modules 1 and 2.

## 4.2 Process Flowchart: Problem-Solving Strategy for Ideal Gas Law Problems

The following Mermaid flowchart provides a **systematic decision tree** for tackling any KTU numerical problem on the Ideal Gas Law.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Read the Problem Carefully]) --> Q1{Is it a pure gas or a mixture?}
    
    Q1 -->|Pure Gas| Q2{Given quantity type?}
    Q1 -->|Gas Mixture| Q3{Do you know mole fractions or partial pressures?}
    
    Q2 -->|Moles n given| P1[Use PV equals nRT]
    Q2 -->|Mass m given| P2[Use PV equals mRT over M, solve for m]
    Q2 -->|Density rho given| P3[Use rho equals PM over RT]
    
    Q3 -->|Mole fractions yi| M1[Compute M avg equals sum of yi Mi]
    M1 --> M2[Use PV equals nRT with M avg for mixture]
    
    Q3 -->|Partial pressures Pi| M3[Compute yi equals Pi over P total]
    M3 --> M1
    
    P1 --> Check{Unit Consistency Check}
    P2 --> Check
    P3 --> Check
    M2 --> Check
    
    Check -->|Pressure unit consistent with R?| R1[Yes: Proceed to compute]
    Check -->|No: Convert| R2[Convert P, V, T to match R units]
    R2 --> Check
    
    R1 --> T1{Is temperature in Kelvin?}
    T1 -->|No: Add 273.15 or 460 for Rankine| T2[Convert T to absolute scale]
    T2 --> T1
    T1 -->|Yes| Solve[Compute the answer]
    
    Solve --> Verify[Cross-check with alternative method or order-of-magnitude estimate]
    Verify --> End([Final Answer with Units])
    
    style Start fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px
    style End fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    style Check fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px
    style Solve fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this flowchart matters for KTU:** A significant number of students lose 1–2 marks per problem simply due to *unit inconsistency*. Following this decision tree forces you to (1) classify the problem type, (2) choose the right formula variant, (3) verify unit compatibility, and (4) double-check via a second method. Examiners specifically look for **clean, labeled free-body diagrams** of this type in high-mark questions.

Understanding the *real-time application* of the law is a hallmark of a competent chemical engineer.
