# Module 1: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, CO1]`

**State the Ideal Gas Law. List the four assumptions of the kinetic theory that justify its use.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Ideal Gas Law states that for an ideal gas, the product of pressure and volume is directly proportional to the product of the number of moles and the absolute temperature:

$$PV = nRT$$

where **P** = absolute pressure, **V** = volume, **n** = number of moles, **T** = absolute temperature, and **R** = the universal gas constant (8.314 J/(mol·K)).

The four key assumptions of the kinetic theory are:
1. The gas molecules are point masses (negligible volume compared to container volume).
2. There are no intermolecular forces of attraction or repulsion.
3. Molecular motion is completely random, and all collisions (with walls and between molecules) are perfectly elastic.
4. The average kinetic energy of molecules is directly proportional to the absolute temperature.

> [!TIP] > **Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):** 1 mark for the correct equation with all terms defined; 2 marks for stating the four assumptions (½ mark each).

### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1, CO1]`

**A vessel contains 2.5 kg of nitrogen (N₂) gas at a temperature of 30 °C and a pressure of 5 bar. Calculate the volume of the vessel using the ideal gas law. Given: M(N₂) = 28 g/mol; R = 0.08314 L·bar/(mol·K).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert mass to moles:
$$n = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{2500\ \text{g}}{28\ \text{g/mol}} = 89.286\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2:** Convert temperature to Kelvin:
$$T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the ideal gas law:
$$V = \dfrac{nRT}{P} = \dfrac{(89.286)(0.08314)(303.15)}{5}$$

$$V = \dfrac{2250.05}{5} = 450.01\ \text{L}$$

$$\boxed{V \approx 0.450\ \text{m}^3}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B.1(a)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1, CO2, 7 marks]`

**A gaseous mixture has the following composition by volume: 40% H₂, 35% CH₄, 15% CO, and 10% CO₂. The mixture is at a pressure of 1.5 atm and a temperature of 100 °C. Calculate:**
**(i)** The **partial pressure** of each component.
**(ii)** The **average molecular weight** of the mixture.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:** Mole fractions (since volume% = mole% for ideal gases):
- $y_{H_2} = 0.40$, $M_{H_2} = 2.016\ \text{g/mol}$
- $y_{CH_4} = 0.35$, $M_{CH_4} = 16.04\ \text{g/mol}$
- $y_{CO} = 0.15$, $M_{CO} = 28.01\ \text{g/mol}$
- $y_{CO_2} = 0.10$, $M_{CO_2} = 44.01\ \text{g/mol}$
- $P = 1.5\ \text{atm}$, $T = 373.15\ \text{K}$

**Part (i): Partial Pressures** (apply $P_i = y_i P_{total}$):

$$P_{H_2} = 0.40 \times 1.5 = 0.60\ \text{atm}$$
$$P_{CH_4} = 0.35 \times 1.5 = 0.525\ \text{atm}$$
$$P_{CO} = 0.15 \times 1.5 = 0.225\ \text{atm}$$
$$P_{CO_2} = 0.10 \times 1.5 = 0.15\ \text{atm}$$

**Verification:** Sum = 0.60 + 0.525 + 0.225 + 0.15 = 1.5 atm = $P_{total}$ ✓

**Part (ii): Average Molecular Weight** (apply $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$):

$$M_{avg} = (0.40)(2.016) + (0.35)(16.04) + (0.15)(28.01) + (0.10)(44.01)$$

$$M_{avg} = 0.8064 + 5.614 + 4.2015 + 4.401 = 15.023\ \text{g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{M_{avg} \approx 15.02\ \text{g/mol}}$$

> [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark Loss (1 mark):** Students often confuse *volume percentage* with *mass percentage*. For ideal gas mixtures, **volume% = mole%**, but this is *only* true for ideal gases. Always check the problem statement carefully. If mass percentages are given, convert to mole fractions first: $y_i = (w_i/M_i) / \sum(w_j/M_j)$, where $w_i$ is the mass fraction.

### **Question B.1(b)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 1, CO2, 7 marks]`

**The mixture described in part (a) occupies a volume of 2.0 m³. Calculate:**
**(i)** The **total number of moles** in the mixture.
**(ii)** The **density** of the gas mixture in kg/m³.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 2.0\ \text{m}^3 = 2000\ \text{L}$, $P = 1.5\ \text{atm}$, $T = 373.15\ \text{K}$, $M_{avg} = 15.02\ \text{g/mol}$ (from part a).

**Part (i): Total Moles** (apply $PV = nRT$ with R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K):

$$n = \dfrac{PV}{RT} = \dfrac{(1.5\ \text{atm})(2000\ \text{L})}{(0.08206\ \text{L·atm/mol·K})(373.15\ \text{K})}$$

$$n = \dfrac{3000}{30.625} = 97.96\ \text{mol}$$

$$\boxed{n \approx 97.96\ \text{mol}}$$

**Part (ii): Density of the Mixture:**

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{PM_{avg}}{RT} = \dfrac{(1.5\ \text{atm})(15.02\ \text{g/mol})}{(0.08206\ \text{L·atm/mol·K})(373.15\ \text{K})}$$

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{22.53}{30.625} = 0.7357\ \text{g/L} = 0.7357\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{mix} \approx 0.736\ \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Alternative method (cross-check):**
$$\rho = \dfrac{m}{V} = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{avg}}{V} = \dfrac{(97.96)(15.02)}{2000} = 0.7356\ \text{g/L} \ \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B.2(a)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, CO2, 7 marks]`

**A 10 m³ tank contains a mixture of gases at 30 °C and 1 atm pressure. The mixture composition is as follows: 20 kg of O₂, 15 kg of N₂, and 5 kg of CO₂. Calculate:**
**(i)** The **mole fraction** of each component.
**(ii)** The **partial pressure** of each component.
**(iii)** The **density** of the mixture.
**Given:** M(O₂) = 32, M(N₂) = 28, M(CO₂) = 44 g/mol; R = 0.08206 L·atm/mol·K.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert mass to moles for each component:**

$$n_{O_2} = \dfrac{20000\ \text{g}}{32\ \text{g/mol}} = 625\ \text{mol}$$

$$n_{N_2} = \dfrac{15000\ \text{g}}{28\ \text{g/mol}} = 535.71\ \text{mol}$$

$$n_{CO_2} = \dfrac{5000\ \text{g}}{44\ \text{g/mol}} = 113.64\ \text{mol}$$

$$n_{total} = 625 + 535.71 + 113.64 = 1274.35\ \text{mol}$$

**Step 2: Compute mole fractions:**

$$y_{O_2} = \dfrac{625}{1274.35} = 0.4904\ (49.04\%)$$

$$y_{N_2} = \dfrac{535.71}{1274.35} = 0.4204\ (42.04\%)$$

$$y_{CO_2} = \dfrac{113.64}{1274.35} = 0.0892\ (8.92\%)$$

**Verification:** $0.4904 + 0.4204 + 0.0892 = 1.0000$ ✓

**Step 3: Partial pressures** (using $P_i = y_i P_{total}$, with $P_{total} = 1$ atm):

$$P_{O_2} = 0.4904\ \text{atm},\quad P_{N_2} = 0.4204\ \text{atm},\quad P_{CO_2} = 0.0892\ \text{atm}$$

**Step 4: Density of the mixture:**

Total mass = 20 + 15 + 5 = 40 kg

$$\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{m_{total}}{V} = \dfrac{40\ \text{kg}}{10\ \text{m}^3} = 4.0\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Alternate Density Verification (KTU Trick Question Defense):** Some students try to use $\rho = PM_{avg}/RT$ here, but $M_{avg}$ requires mole fractions in the same units as $M$. Let's verify:
> $M_{avg} = (0.4904)(32) + (0.4204)(28) + (0.0892)(44) = 15.69 + 11.77 + 3.92 = 31.39$ g/mol
> $\rho = (1\ \text{atm})(31.39)/(0.08206 \times 303.15) = 31.39/24.88 = 1.262$ g/L = 1.262 kg/m³... 
> **Discrepancy check:** Wait — there's a trap here! Let me recompute: $\rho V = m \Rightarrow \rho = 40/10 = 4$ kg/m³, but $PM_{avg}/RT = 1.262$ kg/m³. 
> **The discrepancy arises** because the problem states the pressure is 1 atm and the volume is 10 m³ at 30 °C — but this *implied* $n$ from PV=nRT is $n = PV/RT = (1)(10000)/[(0.08206)(303.15)] = 401.9$ mol, which is *less* than 1274.35 mol calculated. **This means the given data is internally inconsistent** — the tank cannot hold 40 kg of this mixture at 1 atm and 30 °C. Always state this in the exam if you spot it!

### **Question B.2(b)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, CO2, 7 marks]`

**Air can be approximated as a mixture of 21 mol% O₂ and 79 mol% N₂. Calculate:**
**(i)** The **average molecular weight** of air.
**(ii)** The **density of air** at 25 °C and 1 atm.
**(iii)** The **mass of air** in a room of dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 4 m at the same conditions.

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Part (i): Average Molecular Weight:**

$$M_{air} = y_{O_2} M_{O_2} + y_{N_2} M_{N_2} = (0.21)(32) + (0.79)(28)$$

$$M_{air} = 6.72 + 22.12 = 28.84\ \text{g/mol}$$

**Part (ii): Density of Air:**

$$T = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298.15\ \text{K},\quad P = 1\ \text{atm}$$

$$\rho_{air} = \dfrac{PM_{air}}{RT} = \dfrac{(1\ \text{atm})(28.84\ \text{g/mol})}{(0.08206\ \text{L·atm/mol·K})(298.15\ \text{K})}$$

$$\rho_{air} = \dfrac{28.84}{24.466} = 1.179\ \text{g/L} = 1.179\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Part (iii): Mass of Air in the Room:**

Volume of room: $V = 10 \times 8 \times 4 = 320\ \text{m}^3$

$$m_{air} = \rho_{air} \times V = (1.179\ \text{kg/m}^3)(320\ \text{m}^3) = 377.3\ \text{kg}$$

$$\boxed{m_{air} \approx 377.3\ \text{kg}}$$

> [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Full Marks Strategy):**
> 1. **Always show the unit at every step** — KTU examiners are instructed to deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 2. **Convert temperature to Kelvin** before substitution. Students commonly write T = 25 directly into PV = nRT — *instant loss of 1 mark*.
> 3. **State the assumption explicitly:** "Assuming the gas behaves ideally at the given conditions." This single sentence can earn you ½ mark that distinguishes a "good" answer from a "great" one.
> 4. **Box the final answer.** KTU scan sheets are graded quickly — boxed answers stand out and reduce valuation errors.
> 5. **Use significant figures consistent with the input data.** If the input has 3 sig figs (e.g., M = 28.84), retain 4 sig figs in intermediate steps but round to 3–4 sig figs in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ⚡ The 30-Second Mental Checklist Before Every KTU Ideal Gas Question

- [ ] **Identify the gas:** Pure gas, gas mixture, or component of a mixture?
- [ ] **Identify the unknowns:** What is being asked — n, m, V, P, T, ρ, yᵢ, M_avg?
- [ ] **Convert ALL temperatures to Kelvin** (or Rankine for FPS units).
- [ ] **Convert pressure to absolute** (gauge + atmospheric), not gauge.
- [ ] **Match R's units** to the units of P, V, T, and n in your problem.
- [ ] **For mixtures:** Compute $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ first, then treat the mixture as a single pseudo-gas.
- [ ] **For partial pressures:** $P_i = y_i P_{total}$ (Dalton's Law).
- [ ] **For density problems:** $\rho = PM/RT$ is the fastest route.
- [ ] **Always check** if the conditions justify the ideal gas assumption ($P_r < 0.5$ and $T_r > 2$).

### 🧠 The 10 "Must-Memorize" Items for KTU Module 1

1. $PV = nRT$ — the master equation.
2. $R = 0.08206\ \text{L·atm/(mol·K)} = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$.
3. STP molar volume = **22.414 L/mol** (old convention used in KTU).
4. Dalton's Law: $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ and $P_i = y_i P_{total}$.
5. Amagat's Law: $V_{total} = \sum V_i$ and $V_i = y_i V_{total}$.
6. Volume% = Mole% (only for ideal gases!).
7. $M_{avg} = \sum y_i M_i$ for gas mixtures.
8. $\rho = PM/RT$ (density form).
9. Compressibility factor $Z = PV/(nRT)$ — equals 1 for ideal gas.
10. RMS speed: $\bar{u}^2 = 3RT/M$ (with M in kg/mol for SI).

### 🎯 Common KTU Question Types (Practice These Patterns)

1. **Type 1:** Find moles/volume of a pure gas given P, T (and M if mass is given).
2. **Type 2:** Find the density of a gas at specified T and P.
3. **Type 3:** Compute partial pressures given mixture composition.
4. **Type 4:** Calculate $M_{avg}$ of a gas mixture.
5. **Type 5:** Combined gas law — process changes (P, V, T before/after).
6. **Type 6:** Reactor/storage tank sizing — find mass of gas held in a vessel.
7. **Type 7:** Real gas correction — calculate Z at given P, T for a known gas.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** Module 1 questions are *usually* direct applications of formulas. Marks are lost not because the student doesn't know the formula, but because of **unit conversion errors, missing temperature conversion, or inconsistent pressure reference**. Practice *unit consistency* — it is the single most important skill in Process Calculations.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** states that *the total pressure exerted by a gaseous mixture of non-reacting (ideal) gases is equal to the arithmetic sum of the partial pressures of the individual component gases*. The law was proposed by the English chemist and physicist **John Dalton** in **1801** and is rigorously valid only for **ideal gas mixtures** where intermolecular forces between unlike molecules are assumed to be negligible.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim)**
> *"The total pressure of an ideal gas mixture equals the sum of the partial pressures of its components. A partial pressure is the pressure that a component gas would exert if it alone occupied the entire volume of the mixture at the same temperature."*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$P_{total} = P_A + P_B + P_C + \cdots + P_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$$

Where:
- $P_{total}$ = Total absolute pressure of the gas mixture (in **Pa** or **N/m²** or **atm** or **bar** or **mm Hg**)
- $P_i$ = Partial pressure of the $i^{th}$ component

The corresponding **mole fraction relationship** (most important for KTU numericals):

$$P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad y_i = \frac{P_i}{P_{total}}$$

Where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$ in the gas phase.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a crowded classroom of **100 students** (gas molecules) standing together in a sealed room. Each student pushes on the walls of the room with some force, which adds up to a total force (total pressure). Now, suppose the room is divided into 4 different types of students: **Engineers (40), Doctors (30), Artists (20), and Lawyers (10)**. Even though they all push together, the *engineer's contribution* to total pressure is 40/100 of the total. In gas terms, the **partial pressure of Engineers** = (their mole fraction) × (total pressure).

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy: "Sharing the Pressure"**
> Think of partial pressure like a *joint account*. Dalton's Law says the total wealth (pressure) in the joint account is just the sum of what each partner (gas) deposited. The bank's total is simply owner-A's deposit + owner-B's deposit + owner-C's deposit.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant (R):** $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)} = 0.08206 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)} = 1.987 \, \text{cal/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Temperature and Pressure (STP):** $0^\circ \text{C}$ (**273.15 K**) and **1 atm** (**101.325 kPa**)
- **Normal Temperature and Pressure (NTP):** $20^\circ \text{C}$ (**293.15 K**) and **1 atm**
- **Mole Fraction Range:** $0 \le y_i \le 1$, and $\sum y_i = 1$ (fundamental closure property)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — "The Why" Behind Dalton's Law

Dalton's Law emerges as a direct consequence of the **Kinetic Theory of Gases** applied to ideal mixtures. The reasoning chain is as follows:

- **Step 1:** In an *ideal gas*, molecules experience no intermolecular forces and have negligible volume.
- **Step 2:** When two or more *ideal gases* are mixed at the same **T** and **V**, the molecules of one gas do not affect the motion of the molecules of another.
- **Step 3:** Therefore, the pressure exerted by each gas depends only on **its own number of moles, temperature, and the total volume** — not on the presence of other gases.
- **Step 4:** Each gas independently obeys the **Ideal Gas Law**: $P_i V = n_i R T$
- **Step 5:** Since volume **V** and temperature **T** are common to all gases in the mixture, partial pressures are simply additive.

### 2.2 Critical Assumptions of Dalton's Law (Board-Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Checklist — Validity Conditions**
> 1. The gases must be **non-reacting** (no chemical reaction between components).
> 2. The mixture must behave **ideally** (negligible intermolecular forces).
> 3. Temperature must be **uniform** throughout the mixture.
> 4. **Moderate-to-low pressures** (typically below **10 bar**) and **high temperatures** ensure ideal behavior.
> 5. Each gas must occupy the **same total volume** as the mixture.

### 2.3 Partial Pressure vs. Partial Volume — A Critical Distinction

| Property | Dalton's Law (Partial Pressure) | Amagat's Law (Partial Volume) |
|---|---|---|
| Variable held constant | **Volume & Temperature** | **Pressure & Temperature** |
| Mathematical form | $P = \sum P_i$ | $V = \sum V_i$ |
| Mole fraction relation | $P_i = y_i \cdot P$ | $V_i = y_i \cdot V$ |
| Application domain | Gas mixtures at fixed V, T | Gas mixtures at fixed P, T |
| KTU frequency | ★★★★★ (Extremely common) | ★★★☆☆ (Occasional) |

> [!NOTE] **Amagat's Law of Additive Volumes** states that the total volume of an ideal gas mixture equals the sum of the pure-component volumes (each at the mixture's T and P). This is the **volumetric twin** of Dalton's Law.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Dalton's Law — additivity of partial pressures | **Pa, atm, bar, mmHg** |
| 2 | $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ | Partial pressure in terms of mole fraction | Dimensionless $y_i$ |
| 3 | $y_i = \dfrac{P_i}{P_{total}}$ | Mole fraction from partial pressure | Sum equals **1** |
| 4 | $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{\sum n_i} = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$ | Mole fraction definition | Used when moles are given |
| 5 | $P_i = \dfrac{n_i R T}{V}$ | Partial pressure from ideal gas law | Common V and T |
| 6 | $P_{total} V = n_{total} R T$ | Mixture ideal gas law | $n_{total} = \sum n_i$ |
| 7 | $P_i = x_i \cdot P^{sat}_i$ | Raoult's Law (modified form) | Liquid-vapor equilibrium |
| 8 | $\displaystyle \sum y_i = 1$ | Mole fraction closure relation | Always true |
| 9 | $M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$ | Average molecular weight of gas mixture | kg/kmol or g/mol |
| 10 | $\rho_{mix} = \dfrac{P \cdot M_{mix}}{R T}$ | Density of ideal gas mixture | kg/m³ |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility in Industry**
> Dalton's Law is the *backbone* of **humidity measurement** (psychrometrics), **combustion analysis** (flue gas calculations), **gas absorption columns**, **distillation column design (Raoult's + Dalton)**, **partial pressure oxidation in catalytic reactors**, and **medical gas mixing** (anesthesia, oxygen therapy).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Rigorous Derivation of Dalton's Law from the Ideal Gas Equation

**Starting Premise:** Consider a mixture of **n** non-reacting ideal gases occupying a common volume **V** at a common temperature **T**.

For each individual gas component $i$ (treating it as if it alone occupies the entire volume V at temperature T), the **Ideal Gas Law** gives:

$$P_i V = n_i R T$$

Solving for the partial pressure $P_i$ of component $i$:

$$P_i = \frac{n_i R T}{V}$$

Now, summing the partial pressures of **all** components from $i = 1$ to $n$:

$$P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \cdots + P_n = \frac{n_1 R T}{V} + \frac{n_2 R T}{V} + \frac{n_3 R T}{V} + \cdots + \frac{n_n R T}{V}$$

Factoring out the common term $\dfrac{R T}{V}$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = \frac{R T}{V} \left( n_1 + n_2 + n_3 + \cdots + n_n \right) = \frac{R T}{V} \cdot n_{total}$$

The right-hand side is precisely the **Ideal Gas Law for the entire mixture**:

$$\frac{R T}{V} \cdot n_{total} = P_{total}$$

Therefore, by the **axiom of equality of transitivity**:

$$\boxed{\,P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \cdots + P_n\,}$$

This is the **exact form of Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures**, rigorously derived. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mole Fraction Form $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$

Starting from the two forms of the ideal gas law:

$$P_i V = n_i R T \quad \text{(for component } i \text{ alone)}$$

$$P_{total} V = n_{total} R T \quad \text{(for the entire mixture)}$$

Dividing the first equation by the second:

$$\frac{P_i V}{P_{total} V} = \frac{n_i R T}{n_{total} R T}$$

The terms $V$, $R$, and $T$ cancel out completely:

$$\frac{P_i}{P_{total}} = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}}$$

By definition, the mole fraction $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$. Substituting:

$$\frac{P_i}{P_{total}} = y_i$$

Multiplying both sides by $P_{total}$:

$$\boxed{\,P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}\,}$$

This elegant relationship is the **most frequently used form of Dalton's Law** in KTU numericals. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU-Style Comprehensive Example

**Problem Statement:** A gas cylinder of volume **$0.05 \, \text{m}^3$** contains a mixture of **0.5 mol of $\text{N}_2$**, **0.3 mol of $\text{O}_2$**, and **0.2 mol of $\text{CO}_2$** at a temperature of **$30^\circ \text{C}$**. Calculate:
- (a) The total pressure of the mixture.
- (b) The partial pressure of each component.
- (c) The mole fraction of each component.
- (d) The average molecular weight of the mixture.

**Given Data:**

$$V = 0.05 \, \text{m}^3, \quad n_{N_2} = 0.5 \, \text{mol}, \quad n_{O_2} = 0.3 \, \text{mol}, \quad n_{CO_2} = 0.2 \, \text{mol}$$

$$T = 30 + 273.15 = 303.15 \, \text{K}, \quad R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Step (a): Total Moles and Total Pressure**

Total moles:

$$n_{total} = n_{N_2} + n_{O_2} + n_{CO_2} = 0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \, \text{mol}$$

Applying the ideal gas law for the mixture:

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V} = \frac{(1.0)(8.314)(303.15)}{0.05}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$(1.0)(8.314)(303.15) = 2520.69 \, \text{J}$$

Dividing by volume:

$$P_{total} = \frac{2520.69}{0.05} = 50413.8 \, \text{Pa} \approx 50.41 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step (b): Partial Pressure of Each Component**

For $\text{N}_2$:

$$P_{N_2} = \frac{n_{N_2} R T}{V} = \frac{(0.5)(8.314)(303.15)}{0.05} = \frac{1260.35}{0.05} = 25206.9 \, \text{Pa}$$

For $\text{O}_2$:

$$P_{O_2} = \frac{(0.3)(8.314)(303.15)}{0.05} = \frac{756.21}{0.05} = 15124.1 \, \text{Pa}$$

For $\text{CO}_2$:

$$P_{CO_2} = \frac{(0.2)(8.314)(303.15)}{0.05} = \frac{504.14}{0.05} = 10082.7 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Verification via Dalton's Law (closure check):**

$$P_{N_2} + P_{O_2} + P_{CO_2} = 25206.9 + 15124.1 + 10082.7 = 50413.7 \, \text{Pa} \approx P_{total} \checkmark$$

**Step (c): Mole Fraction of Each Component**

$$y_{N_2} = \frac{0.5}{1.0} = 0.5, \quad y_{O_2} = \frac{0.3}{1.0} = 0.3, \quad y_{CO_2} = \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 0.2$$

Closure check: $0.5 + 0.3 + 0.2 = 1.0 \checkmark$

**Step (d): Average Molecular Weight**

$$M_{N_2} = 28 \, \text{g/mol}, \quad M_{O_2} = 32 \, \text{g/mol}, \quad M_{CO_2} = 44 \, \text{g/mol}$$

$$M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i = (0.5)(28) + (0.3)(32) + (0.2)(44)$$

$$M_{mix} = 14.0 + 9.6 + 8.8 = 32.4 \, \text{g/mol}$$

### 3.4 Final Results Summary Table

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $P_{total}$ | **50413.8 Pa (50.41 kPa)** |
| $P_{N_2}$ | **25206.9 Pa** |
| $P_{O_2}$ | **15124.1 Pa** |
| $P_{CO_2}$ | **10082.7 Pa** |
| $M_{mix}$ | **32.4 g/mol** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Dalton's Law Operational Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Mixture in Container] --> B[Identify Components: N2, O2, CO2...]
    B --> C[Determine Moles ni of Each Gas]
    C --> D[Compute Total Moles n_total = Sum ni]
    D --> E[Apply Ideal Gas Law: P_total = n_total R T / V]
    D --> F[Apply Ideal Gas Law: Pi = ni R T / V]
    E --> G[Compute Mole Fraction: yi = ni / n_total]
    F --> H[Verify Dalton Sum: Sum Pi = P_total]
    G --> I[Alternative Form: Pi = yi x P_total]
    H --> J[Final Pressure Profile]
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dalton's Law in Process Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        IN1[Volume V]
        IN2[Temperature T]
        IN3[Moles ni of Each Gas]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computational Engine]
        C1[n_total = Sum ni]
        C2[yi = ni / n_total]
        C3[Pi = ni R T / V]
        C4[P_total = n_total R T / V]
    end

    subgraph DALTON[Dalton Verification Layer]
        D1[Sum Pi = P_total Check]
        D2[Sum yi = 1 Check]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        OUT1[Partial Pressures Pi]
        OUT2[Mole Fractions yi]
        OUT3[Total Pressure P_total]
        OUT4[Mixture MW M_mix]
    end

    INPUT --> COMPUTE
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> DALTON
    C4 --> DALTON
    DALTON --> OUTPUT
```

### 4.3 Visual Representation — Pressure Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PT[Total Pressure P_total] --> PN2[Partial Pressure of N2]
    PT --> PO2[Partial Pressure of O2]
    PT --> PCO2[Partial Pressure of CO2]
    PN2 --> SUM[Dalton Sum]
    PO2 --> SUM
    PCO2 --> SUM
    SUM -->|must equal| PT
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation Guide**
> The three Mermaid diagrams above represent, in order: (1) the **sequential computational flow** when applying Dalton's Law to a numerical problem, (2) the **functional architecture** of how partial pressure data flows through a process calculation engine, and (3) the **circular closure** demonstrating that the sum of partial pressures must recover the total pressure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures. Is it applicable to a mixture of reacting gases? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures states that *the total pressure exerted by a gaseous mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of the individual component gases* in the mixture.

Mathematically: $P_{total} = P_A + P_B + P_C + \cdots$

**Justification on Reacting Gases (1 Mark):** No, Dalton's Law is **not strictly applicable** to a mixture of reacting gases. The derivation assumes each gas independently obeys the ideal gas law with **no interaction** between unlike molecules. In a reacting system, chemical interactions alter the number of moles of each species, and the partial pressure of a reactant changes as the reaction proceeds. The law holds only at a **frozen instant in time** (i.e., as an instantaneous composition snapshot) for reacting systems, not as a time-averaged description.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**A gas mixture contains 60% N₂ and 40% O₂ by volume at a total pressure of 2 atm. Calculate the partial pressure of each gas.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

By **Avogadro's Law**, volume percent = mole percent in gas mixtures.

Therefore: $y_{N_2} = 0.60$ and $y_{O_2} = 0.40$

Applying Dalton's Law: $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$

For N₂:

$$P_{N_2} = 0.60 \times 2 = 1.2 \, \text{atm}$$

For O₂:

$$P_{O_2} = 0.40 \times 2 = 0.8 \, \text{atm}$$

**Verification:** $P_{N_2} + P_{O_2} = 1.2 + 0.8 = 2.0 \, \text{atm} = P_{total}$ ✓

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** State Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures. A vessel of volume **$0.6 \, \text{m}^3$** contains a mixture of **1.5 kmol of methane ($\text{CH}_4$)** and **0.8 kmol of ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$)** at a temperature of **$60^\circ \text{C}$**. Determine:
- (i) The total pressure of the mixture.
- (ii) The partial pressure of each component.
- (iii) The average molecular weight of the mixture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ from the ideal gas law. Two gases A and B have partial pressures of **0.65 bar** and **0.35 bar** respectively in a mixture. If the total temperature is **$45^\circ \text{C}$** and the volume is **$0.02 \, \text{m}^3$**, calculate the total number of moles and the moles of each gas present. **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)

**Statement of Dalton's Law (1 Mark):**

> The total pressure of an ideal gas mixture is the sum of the partial pressures of its individual components. $P_{total} = P_1 + P_2 + \cdots + P_n$

**Given Data:**

$$V = 0.6 \, \text{m}^3, \quad n_{CH_4} = 1.5 \, \text{kmol}, \quad n_{C_2H_6} = 0.8 \, \text{kmol}, \quad T = 60 + 273.15 = 333.15 \, \text{K}$$

$$R = 8.314 \, \text{kJ/(kmol·K)} = 8.314 \, \text{kPa·m}^3/(\text{kmol·K})$$

**Step (i): Total Pressure (2 Marks)**

Total moles:

$$n_{total} = 1.5 + 0.8 = 2.3 \, \text{kmol}$$

Applying the ideal gas law:

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V} = \frac{(2.3)(8.314)(333.15)}{0.6}$$

Numerator:

$$(2.3)(8.314)(333.15) = 6371.27 \, \text{kPa·m}^3$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{6371.27}{0.6} = 10618.8 \, \text{kPa} \approx 10.62 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step (ii): Partial Pressures (2 Marks)**

$$P_{CH_4} = \frac{(1.5)(8.314)(333.15)}{0.6} = \frac{4154.27}{0.6} = 6923.8 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$P_{C_2H_6} = \frac{(0.8)(8.314)(333.15)}{0.6} = \frac{2215.61}{0.6} = 3692.7 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Verification:** $6923.8 + 3692.7 = 10616.5 \approx 10618.8$ ✓ (small rounding difference)

**Step (iii): Average Molecular Weight (2 Marks)**

$$M_{CH_4} = 16 \, \text{kg/kmol}, \quad M_{C_2H_6} = 30 \, \text{kg/kmol}$$

Mole fractions:

$$y_{CH_4} = \frac{1.5}{2.3} = 0.6522, \quad y_{C_2H_6} = \frac{0.8}{2.3} = 0.3478$$

$$M_{mix} = (0.6522)(16) + (0.3478)(30) = 10.435 + 10.435 = 20.87 \, \text{kg/kmol}$$

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

Starting with two equations of state:

$$P_A V = n_A R T \quad \text{and} \quad P_{total} V = n_{total} R T$$

Dividing the first by the second:

$$\frac{P_A}{P_{total}} = \frac{n_A}{n_{total}} = y_A$$

Therefore:

$$P_A = y_A \cdot P_{total} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Numerical Solution (4 Marks):**

Given: $P_A = 0.65$ bar, $P_B = 0.35$ bar, $T = 45 + 273.15 = 318.15$ K, $V = 0.02 \, \text{m}^3$

Total pressure:

$$P_{total} = 0.65 + 0.35 = 1.0 \, \text{bar} = 100 \, \text{kPa}$$

Mole fractions:

$$y_A = \frac{0.65}{1.0} = 0.65, \quad y_B = \frac{0.35}{1.0} = 0.35$$

Total moles:

$$n_{total} = \frac{P_{total} V}{R T} = \frac{(100)(0.02)}{(8.314)(318.15)} = \frac{2.0}{2645.10} = 7.56 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{kmol} = 0.756 \, \text{mol}$$

Moles of each gas:

$$n_A = 0.65 \times 0.756 = 0.4914 \, \text{mol}$$

$$n_B = 0.35 \times 0.756 = 0.2646 \, \text{mol}$$

**Verification:** $0.4914 + 0.2646 = 0.756$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the physical significance of Dalton's Law with reference to the kinetic theory of gases. A gas mixture at **10 atm** total pressure has the following composition by volume: **$\text{H}_2$ — 30%, $\text{CO}$ — 40%, $\text{N}_2$ — 20%, $\text{CO}_2$ — 10%**. Calculate the partial pressure of each component and the average molecular weight of the mixture. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 5-liter container holds **0.2 mol of $\text{O}_2$** and **0.3 mol of $\text{N}_2$** at **$27^\circ \text{C}$**. Using Dalton's Law:
- (i) Determine the total pressure and partial pressure of each gas.
- (ii) If helium is added until the total pressure becomes **double the original**, how many moles of He were added? **(7 Marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — (7 Marks)

**Physical Significance (3 Marks):**

From the **kinetic theory of gases**, the pressure exerted by a gas arises from the **collisions of its molecules with the container walls**. In an *ideal* gas mixture:

- The molecules of each gas are in constant, random motion.
- Molecules of *unlike* gases do not interact (no attractive or repulsive forces).
- The frequency of wall collisions by each species depends only on its own molecular density, temperature, and the volume.
- Therefore, the pressure contribution of each gas (its partial pressure) is **independent** of the presence of other gases.
- The total pressure is the **simple sum** of these independent contributions.

**Numerical Solution (4 Marks):**

Volume percent = mole percent (Avogadro's Law).

$$y_{H_2} = 0.30, \quad y_{CO} = 0.40, \quad y_{N_2} = 0.20, \quad y_{CO_2} = 0.10$$

Partial pressures using $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$:

$$P_{H_2} = 0.30 \times 10 = 3.0 \, \text{atm}$$

$$P_{CO} = 0.40 \times 10 = 4.0 \, \text{atm}$$

$$P_{N_2} = 0.20 \times 10 = 2.0 \, \text{atm}$$

$$P_{CO_2} = 0.10 \times 10 = 1.0 \, \text{atm}$$

**Verification:** $3.0 + 4.0 + 2.0 + 1.0 = 10.0$ atm ✓

Average molecular weight:

$$M_{H_2} = 2, \quad M_{CO} = 28, \quad M_{N_2} = 28, \quad M_{CO_2} = 44$$

$$M_{mix} = (0.3)(2) + (0.4)(28) + (0.2)(28) + (0.1)(44)$$

$$M_{mix} = 0.6 + 11.2 + 5.6 + 4.4 = 21.8 \, \text{g/mol}$$

#### Part (b) — (7 Marks)

**Step (i): Total and Partial Pressures (3 Marks)**

Given: $V = 5 \, \text{L} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^3$, $n_{O_2} = 0.2$ mol, $n_{N_2} = 0.3$ mol, $T = 300.15$ K

Total moles:

$$n_{total} = 0.2 + 0.3 = 0.5 \, \text{mol}$$

Total pressure:

$$P_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{V} = \frac{(0.5)(8.314)(300.15)}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{1247.72}{0.005} = 249544.5 \, \text{Pa} \approx 249.5 \, \text{kPa} \approx 2.463 \, \text{atm}$$

Partial pressures:

$$P_{O_2} = \frac{(0.2)(8.314)(300.15)}{0.005} = \frac{499.09}{0.005} = 99817.8 \, \text{Pa} \approx 99.82 \, \text{kPa}$$

$$P_{N_2} = \frac{(0.3)(8.314)(300.15)}{0.005} = \frac{748.63}{0.005} = 149726.6 \, \text{Pa} \approx 149.73 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Step (ii): Moles of Helium Added (4 Marks)**

New total pressure:

$$P_{new} = 2 \times P_{total} = 2 \times 249.5 = 499.0 \, \text{kPa}$$

Using the same V and T, new total moles:

$$n_{new} = \frac{P_{new} V}{R T} = \frac{(499000)(5 \times 10^{-3})}{(8.314)(300.15)} = \frac{2495.0}{2495.45} \approx 1.0 \, \text{mol}$$

Moles of He added:

$$n_{He} = n_{new} - n_{original} = 1.0 - 0.5 = 0.5 \, \text{mol}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the Closure Check:** Many students compute partial pressures but never verify $\sum P_i = P_{total}$. Examiners award **partial credit** for this verification — always include it.
> 2. **Unit Mismatch Errors:** Mixing **Pa, kPa, atm, bar, mm Hg** in the same problem without conversion. **Standardize units FIRST** before any calculation.
> 3. **Using Mass Fraction Instead of Mole Fraction:** Dalton's Law operates on **mole fractions**, not mass fractions. A common mistake is to use $\%w/w$ directly.
> 4. **Misapplying to Reacting Systems:** Dalton's Law is invalid for systems where reactions change the mole counts over time.
> 5. **Rounding Error Accumulation:** Round only at the **final step**; keep 4–5 significant figures during intermediate steps.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Dalton's Law: $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ — **valid only for non-reacting ideal gas mixtures**.
- ✅ Partial pressure = pressure a gas would exert if it **alone** occupied the total volume.
- ✅ Mole fraction form: $P_i = y_i \cdot P_{total}$ — **the most used equation** in KTU problems.
- ✅ Volume % = Mole % in **gas mixtures** (Avogadro's Law) — this shortcut is exam-friendly.
- ✅ Closure relations: $\sum y_i = 1$ and $\sum P_i = P_{total}$ — always include in your answers.
- ✅ Average molecular weight: $M_{mix} = \sum y_i M_i$ (use **mole** fractions, never mass fractions).
- ✅ Validity requires: **ideal behavior, non-reacting gases, uniform T, same V for all gases**.
- ✅ Dalton's Law is **NOT** applicable to reacting systems as a time-averaged description.
- ✅ Amagat's Law ($V = \sum V_i$) is the **volumetric counterpart** at constant P and T.
- ✅ Combined with **Raoult's Law**, it forms the foundation of **VLE** calculations in distillation.
- ✅ Critical engineering applications: **humidity, combustion, absorption, distillation, medical gases**.
- ✅ Always perform a **closure verification** ($\sum P_i \approx P_{total}$) at the end of your solution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 | Amagat's Law

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AMAGAT'S LAW OF PARTIAL VOLUMES (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Amagat's Law* states that the **total volume** occupied by a real gas mixture at a given temperature $T$ and pressure $P$ is equal to the **algebraic sum of the partial volumes** of its constituent components, where each partial volume $V_i$ is defined as the volume that pure component $i$ would occupy when subjected to the **same temperature and pressure** as the mixture.

Mathematically, the law is expressed as:

$$V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \dots + V_n$$

where each partial volume is:

$$V_i = \frac{n_i \, Z_i \, R \, T}{P}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The law was formulated by **Émile Hilaire Amagat** (1841–1915), a French physicist renowned for his high-pressure experimental studies on gases. Unlike Dalton's Law, which *adds pressures at constant V and T*, Amagat's Law *adds volumes at constant P and T*. The two laws are **identical for ideal gases** but **diverge for real (non-ideal) gases**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **symphony orchestra concert hall** with $200$ seats. Three sections of musicians arrive:

* **Strings:** 80 people (each occupies 1 "volume unit" of personal space)
* **Brass:** 60 people
* **Percussion:** 60 people

The **total physical space** occupied by the musicians equals the sum of the spaces occupied by each section, *as if each section were sitting in the same hall alone*. Amagat's Law applies this exact idea to gas molecules:

| Section | Molecules | "Space" each section would occupy ALONE at (T, P) |
|---|---|---|
| Strings ($N_2$) | 80% | 80 volume units |
| Brass ($O_2$) | 15% | 15 volume units |
| Percussion ($CO_2$) | 5% | 5 volume units |
| **TOTAL MIXTURE** | 100% | **100 volume units** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this single key sentence:** *"Amagat ADDS the VOLUMES; Dalton ADDS the PRESSURES."* Both hold exactly for ideal gases, both are *approximations* for real gases — but they give slightly *different numerical answers* for real mixtures, and the engineer must know which one to apply.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

* **Standard Temperature:** $T = 273.15 \text{ K}$ (**STP**)
* **Standard Pressure:** $P = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
* **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 0.082057 \text{ L·atm/(mol·K)} = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
* **Normal Molar Volume (ideal gas at STP):** $V_m = 22.414 \text{ L/mol}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Six Logical Steps

Amagat's Law is best understood as a sequential logical framework:

1. **Define the Mixture State:** Fix the mixture temperature $T$ and pressure $P$. These are the *reference* conditions for all subsequent calculations.

2. **Hypothetical Isolation:** For every component $i$, mentally isolate it from the mixture. Ask: *"What volume would component $i$ occupy if it ALONE existed at this same $T$ and $P$?"* This hypothetical volume is the **partial volume** $V_i$.

3. **Apply Real Gas Correction:** For a pure real gas at $(T, P)$, the actual volume is $V_i = \dfrac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}$, where $Z_i$ is the **compressibility factor of pure component $i$** at $(T, P)$.

4. **Additive Rule:** The total mixture volume is the **sum** of all partial volumes. This is the *additivity assumption* — equivalent to assuming each gas behaves as if the others were not affecting its volumetric contribution.

5. **Define Partial Volume Fraction:** The ratio $\dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}}$ is the **partial volume fraction** of component $i$, denoted $v_i$. By definition, $\sum v_i = 1$.

6. **Contrast with Dalton's Law:** Dalton's Law uses **partial pressures** $\left(P_i = y_i P\right)$ added at constant $V$. Amagat's uses **partial volumes** $\left(V_i = v_i V\right)$ added at constant $P$. For **ideal gases**, $v_i = y_i$. For **real gases**, $v_i \neq y_i$ in general.

### 2.2 The "Why" — Physical Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does Amagat's Law work?**
> For an **ideal gas**, intermolecular forces are zero and molecular volumes are negligible. Each gas therefore occupies its share of the container volume *independently* of the others. The total volume is the sum of these independent shares. This is the **same physical reasoning** that justifies Dalton's Law. Both laws are exact for ideal gases, and both are approximations for real gases.
>
> **Why is Amagat's Law *preferred* for real gases at HIGH PRESSURE?**
> Experimental data show that at high pressures, gases approach their **liquid-like densities** and molecular volumes become significant. The volume additivity assumption (Amagat) becomes a better approximation than the pressure additivity assumption (Dalton) for hydrocarbon mixtures, especially in petroleum and natural gas engineering. The **Kay's mixing rule** for pseudo-critical properties is closely linked to Amagat's Law.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Amagat's Law |
|---|---|
| **Natural Gas Industry** | Compressibility factor $Z_{mix}$ calculations for pipeline design |
| **Petroleum Refining** | Volume-based blending of refinery gas streams |
| **Cryogenics** | Liquefaction of air ($N_2 + O_2 + Ar$) — partial volumes of liquefied components |
| **Refrigeration** | Refrigerant mixtures (e.g., R-134a + R-1234yf) — volumetric capacity estimation |
| **Chemical Reaction Engineering** | Batch reactor design where volumetric feed rate of mixed gases is critical |
| **Compressed Air Systems** | Multi-component compressed gas storage tank sizing |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete formula toolkit** required for any Amagat's Law problem in KTU examinations. Master every row.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary Condition / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$ | Total volume = sum of partial volumes | Exact for **ideal gases**; approximate for real |
| 2 | $V_i = \dfrac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}$ | Partial volume of component $i$ at $(T,P)$ | Real gas; for ideal, set $Z_i = 1$ |
| 3 | $v_i = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}}$ | Partial volume fraction of $i$ | $\sum v_i = 1$ (always) |
| 4 | $V_i = v_i \, V_{total}$ | Partial volume from volume fraction | Definition |
| 5 | $v_i = \dfrac{y_i Z_i}{Z_{mix}}$ | Partial vol. fraction via compressibilities | **Real gas** relation — most-tested formula |
| 6 | $Z_{mix} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i Z_i$ | Kay's mixing rule for mixture $Z$ | Approximation; best at moderate pressures |
| 7 | $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}} = \dfrac{P_i}{P_{total}}$ | Mole fraction (ideal gas equality) | Ideal gas only |
| 8 | $v_i = y_i$ | Partial vol. fraction = mole fraction | **Ideal gas only** |
| 9 | $n_{total} = \dfrac{P V_{total}}{Z_{mix} R T}$ | Total moles from real-gas mixture equation | Universal real gas relation |
| 10 | $P_i = y_i P$ | Partial pressure from mole fraction | Dalton's Law; ideal gas only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical distinction — KTU examiners LOVE to test this:**
> * $y_i$ (mole fraction) is **always** a true property of the mixture.
> * $p_i$ (partial pressure fraction) = $y_i$ **only for ideal gases.**
> * $v_i$ (partial volume fraction) = $y_i$ **only for ideal gases.**
> * For real gases, **$p_i \neq v_i \neq y_i$** in general.

### 2.5 The Real-Gas Bridge Equation — The Most Tested Formula

Combining equations 5 and 6 above:

$$v_i = \frac{y_i Z_i}{\sum_j y_j Z_j}$$

This is the **single most important equation** for Amagat's Law problems in KTU. It allows you to convert *mole fractions* into *partial volume fractions* using the pure-component compressibility factors.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Amagat's Law from the Ideal Gas Law

**Starting Point:** For a pure component $i$ at temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, the ideal gas law gives:

$$\frac{P V_i^{*}}{n_i R T} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_i^{*} = \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 1 — Definition of Partial Volume:** The partial volume $V_i$ is defined as the volume that $n_i$ moles of pure gas $i$ would occupy at the *mixture's* $T$ and $P$:

$$V_i = \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Summing All Components:** For a mixture of $n$ components:

$$V_{total}^{Amagat} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{n_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Factoring Constants:** Since $R$, $T$, and $P$ are common to all terms:

$$V_{total}^{Amagat} = \frac{R T}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i = \frac{R T}{P} \cdot n_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Mixture Ideal Gas Law:** The total mixture, treated as a single ideal gas, gives:

$$V_{total}^{mixture} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{P}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** $V_{total}^{Amagat} = V_{total}^{mixture}$, confirming Amagat's Law is **exact for ideal gases**.

$$\boxed{V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \quad \text{(Exact for Ideal Gases)}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation II: Partial Volume Fraction in Terms of Compressibility Factors

**Starting Point:** For a real gas, the partial volume of component $i$ at mixture $(T, P)$ is:

$$V_i = \frac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 1 — Total Volume of the Real Mixture:** By the real-gas equation of state applied to the mixture as a whole:

$$V_{total} = \frac{n_{total} Z_{mix} R T}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Form the Partial Volume Fraction:**

$$v_i = \frac{V_i}{V_{total}} = \frac{\dfrac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}}{\dfrac{n_{total} Z_{mix} R T}{P}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel Common Terms:** The $R$, $T$, and $P$ cancel cleanly:

$$v_i = \frac{n_i Z_i}{n_{total} Z_{mix}}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce Mole Fraction:** Since $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$:

$$v_i = \frac{y_i Z_i}{Z_{mix}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Kay's Mixing Rule:** $Z_{mix} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j Z_j$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{v_i = \frac{y_i Z_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j Z_j} \quad \text{(Real Gas — Amagat's Law)}}$$

**Step 6 — Ideal Gas Limiting Case:** When all $Z_i = 1$ (ideal gas), the denominator becomes $\sum y_j (1) = 1$, and:

$$v_i = y_i \quad \text{(ideal gas limit)}$$

This confirms our earlier conclusion: **partial volume fraction = mole fraction for ideal gases only.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation III: Total Volume from Partial Volume Fractions

Starting from the real-gas mixture equation:

$$V_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{P} \cdot Z_{mix}$$

Substituting Kay's rule $Z_{mix} = \sum y_j Z_j$:

$$V_{total} = \frac{n_{total} R T}{P} \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j Z_j$$

We can also write this using partial volumes directly:

$$V_{total} = \frac{R T}{P} \sum_{i=1}^{n} n_i Z_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$$

This confirms consistency between the partial-volume formulation and the overall mixture formulation.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Board Examination Style

> **Problem:** A gas mixture contains **40 mol% N₂** and **60 mol% CO₂** at $T = 400$ K and $P = 50$ atm. The pure-component compressibility factors at these conditions are $Z_{N_2} = 1.10$ and $Z_{CO_2} = 0.62$. If the total mixture volume is $0.500 \text{ m}^3$, calculate:
> (a) The partial volume of each component using Amagat's Law.
> (b) The partial volume fractions.
> (c) Verify the result by summing partial volumes.

#### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Given:**
$y_{N_2} = 0.40$, $y_{CO_2} = 0.60$
$T = 400$ K, $P = 50$ atm
$Z_{N_2} = 1.10$, $Z_{CO_2} = 0.62$
$V_{total} = 0.500 \text{ m}^3 = 500$ L

**Step 1 — Compute Mixture Compressibility Factor (Kay's Rule):**

$$Z_{mix} = y_{N_2} Z_{N_2} + y_{CO_2} Z_{CO_2}$$

$$Z_{mix} = (0.40)(1.10) + (0.60)(0.62) = 0.440 + 0.372 = 0.812$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Moles:**

$$n_{total} = \frac{P V_{total}}{Z_{mix} R T} = \frac{(50)(500)}{(0.812)(0.08206)(400)}$$

$$n_{total} = \frac{25000}{26.6435} = 938.3 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Individual Moles:**
$n_{N_2} = (0.40)(938.3) = 375.3$ mol
$n_{CO_2} = (0.60)(938.3) = 563.0$ mol

**Step 4 — Partial Volumes (Amagat's Law):**

For $N_2$:

$$V_{N_2} = \frac{n_{N_2} Z_{N_2} R T}{P} = \frac{(375.3)(1.10)(0.08206)(400)}{50}$$

$$V_{N_2} = \frac{13550.2}{50} = 271.0 \text{ L}$$

For $CO_2$:

$$V_{CO_2} = \frac{n_{CO_2} Z_{CO_2} R T}{P} = \frac{(563.0)(0.62)(0.08206)(400)}{50}$$

$$V_{CO_2} = \frac{11456.2}{50} = 229.1 \text{ L}$$

**Step 5 — Partial Volume Fractions:**

$$v_{N_2} = \frac{V_{N_2}}{V_{total}} = \frac{271.0}{500} = 0.542$$

$$v_{CO_2} = \frac{V_{CO_2}}{V_{total}} = \frac{229.1}{500} = 0.458$$

**Step 6 — Verify via Real-Gas Formula $v_i = \dfrac{y_i Z_i}{Z_{mix}}$:**

$$v_{N_2} = \frac{(0.40)(1.10)}{0.812} = \frac{0.440}{0.812} = 0.542 \checkmark$$

$$v_{CO_2} = \frac{(0.60)(0.62)}{0.812} = \frac{0.372}{0.812} = 0.458 \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Verify Amagat's Additivity:**

$$V_{N_2} + V_{CO_2} = 271.0 + 229.1 = 500.1 \approx 500 \text{ L} = V_{total} \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Notice that $v_{N_2} = 0.542 > y_{N_2} = 0.40$, even though $N_2$ is the *minor* component. This is because $Z_{N_2} > Z_{mix}$ (N₂ is "more ideal" than the mixture), so it occupies a *larger share* of the total volume than its mole fraction would suggest. **This is the most common KTU conceptual trap.**

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Ideal vs. Real Gas Behaviour

| Property | Ideal Gas (Amagat = Dalton) | Real Gas via Amagat's Law |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{total}$ formula | $\dfrac{nRT}{P}$ | $\dfrac{nZ_{mix}RT}{P}$ |
| Partial volume $V_i$ | $\dfrac{n_i RT}{P}$ | $\dfrac{n_i Z_i RT}{P}$ |
| Partial volume fraction $v_i$ | $y_i$ | $\dfrac{y_i Z_i}{Z_{mix}}$ |
| Additive quantity | $V$ | $V$ |
| Constant condition | $T, P$ | $T, P$ |
| Validity | Exact | Approximate (better at high P for hydrocarbons) |
| Use of compressibility | Not required ($Z = 1$) | $Z_i$ for each pure component required |
