## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: State Amagat's Law of Partial Volumes. How does it differ from Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — full credit structure):**

> **Amagat's Law (1 mark):** Amagat's Law states that the total volume of a gaseous mixture at constant temperature and pressure is equal to the sum of the partial volumes of the individual components. Mathematically, $V_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i$, where $V_i$ is the volume that pure component $i$ would occupy at the same $T$ and $P$ as the mixture.
>
> **Difference from Dalton's Law (2 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Dalton's Law | Amagat's Law |
> |---|---|---|
> | Additive quantity | **Pressure** (at constant V, T) | **Volume** (at constant T, P) |
> | Mathematical form | $P_{total} = \sum P_i$ | $V_{total} = \sum V_i$ |
> | Partial pressure | $P_i = y_i P$ | — |
> | Partial volume | — | $V_i = v_i V$ |
> | Identity with mole fraction | $p_i = y_i$ (ideal gas) | $v_i = y_i$ (ideal gas) |
>
> For **ideal gases**, both laws give identical numerical results. For **real gases**, the two laws produce different values, and Amagat's Law is empirically a better approximation at high pressures.

#### Question 2: Pure Component vs. Mixture Compressibility `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: For a real gas mixture, derive the expression for the partial volume fraction $v_i$ in terms of pure component compressibility factors $Z_i$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Starting definition (1 mark):** The partial volume of component $i$ in the mixture is:
>
> $$V_i = \frac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}$$
>
> **Mixture total volume (1 mark):** The total volume of the real-gas mixture is:
>
> $$V_{total} = \frac{n_{total} Z_{mix} R T}{P}$$
>
> **Taking the ratio (1 mark):** The partial volume fraction is:
>
> $$v_i = \frac{V_i}{V_{total}} = \frac{n_i Z_i}{n_{total} Z_{mix}} = \frac{y_i Z_i}{Z_{mix}}$$
>
> Substituting Kay's mixing rule $Z_{mix} = \sum_j y_j Z_j$, we obtain:
>
> $$\boxed{v_i = \frac{y_i Z_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j Z_j}}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** A gas mixture has the following composition by mole: 30% methane (CH₄), 50% ethane (C₂H₆), and 20% propane (C₃H₈). The mixture is at a temperature of $T = 450$ K and a pressure of $P = 80$ atm. The pure-component compressibility factors at these conditions are:
$Z_{CH_4} = 1.25$, $Z_{C_2H_6} = 0.78$, $Z_{C_3H_8} = 0.55$.

Using **Amagat's Law**, determine the partial volume of each component when the total volume of the mixture is $V_{total} = 2.00 \text{ m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain clearly why Amagat's Law is preferred over Dalton's Law for high-pressure hydrocarbon gas mixtures. Support your answer with one numerical example demonstrating the divergence between the two laws. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Calculation of Partial Volumes (7 marks)

**Given Data:**

* $y_{CH_4} = 0.30$, $y_{C_2H_6} = 0.50$, $y_{C_3H_8} = 0.20$
* $T = 450$ K, $P = 80$ atm
* $Z_{CH_4} = 1.25$, $Z_{C_2H_6} = 0.78$, $Z_{C_3H_8} = 0.55$
* $V_{total} = 2.00 \text{ m}^3 = 2000$ L
* $R = 0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Compute Mixture Compressibility Factor (Kay's Rule) [1 mark]:**

$$Z_{mix} = \sum y_i Z_i = (0.30)(1.25) + (0.50)(0.78) + (0.20)(0.55)$$

$$Z_{mix} = 0.375 + 0.390 + 0.110 = 0.875$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Moles [1 mark]:**

$$n_{total} = \frac{P V_{total}}{Z_{mix} R T} = \frac{(80)(2000)}{(0.875)(0.08206)(450)}$$

$$n_{total} = \frac{160000}{32.311} = 4951.6 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3 — Component Moles [1 mark]:**
$n_{CH_4} = (0.30)(4951.6) = 1485.5$ mol
$n_{C_2H_6} = (0.50)(4951.6) = 2475.8$ mol
$n_{C_3H_8} = (0.20)(4951.6) = 990.3$ mol

**Step 4 — Partial Volumes [3 marks]:**

$$V_{CH_4} = \frac{n_{CH_4} Z_{CH_4} R T}{P} = \frac{(1485.5)(1.25)(0.08206)(450)}{80}$$

$$V_{CH_4} = \frac{68589.5}{80} = 857.4 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{C_2H_6} = \frac{(2475.8)(0.78)(0.08206)(450)}{80} = \frac{71348.4}{80} = 891.9 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{C_3H_8} = \frac{(990.3)(0.55)(0.08206)(450)}{80} = \frac{20100.6}{80} = 251.3 \text{ L}$$

**Step 5 — Verification [1 mark]:**

$$V_{CH_4} + V_{C_2H_6} + V_{C_3H_8} = 857.4 + 891.9 + 251.3 = 2000.6 \approx 2000 \text{ L} = V_{total} \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**

| Component | Partial Volume |
|---|---|
| $CH_4$ | 857.4 L |
| $C_2H_6$ | 891.9 L |
| $C_3H_8$ | 251.3 L |
| **Total** | **2000 L (≈ $V_{total}$)** |

---

##### Part (b) — Why Amagat's Law is Preferred at High Pressure (7 marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Framework (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Amagat's Law** assumes **additivity of volumes** at constant $T$ and $P$ — i.e., the total volume of the mixture is the sum of partial volumes of pure components evaluated at the same $T$ and $P$.
>
> 2. **Dalton's Law** assumes **additivity of partial pressures** at constant $T$ and $V$ — i.e., each gas exerts its own pressure as if other gases were absent.
>
> 3. For **ideal gases**, both assumptions are rigorously exact because there are no intermolecular forces and no molecular volume.
>
> 4. For **real gases**, the two assumptions *cannot both be true simultaneously*. The question is: **which assumption is the better approximation?**
>
> 5. At **high pressures** (typically above 10–15 atm), gas molecules are pushed close together. The **excluded volume** (physical volume occupied by the molecules themselves) becomes a major contributor to the total volume. In this regime, treating each gas as if it occupies its share of the container volume *independently of the others* is empirically more accurate than treating the pressures as additive.
>
> 6. At **low pressures**, gas molecules are far apart, intermolecular forces are negligible, and both laws converge. Either assumption works well.

> [!TIP]
> **Justification from Petroleum Engineering Practice (2 marks):**
> In natural gas and petroleum engineering, the **Standing–Katz compressibility charts** and the **Kay's mixing rule** for $Z_{mix}$ are derived from Amagat's Law. The API (American Petroleum Institute) and GPSA (Gas Processors Suppliers Association) standards recommend Amagat-based volume-additive correlations for hydrocarbon gas mixtures above 500 psia (~34 atm) because they yield experimentally validated compressibility factors.
>
> In contrast, Dalton's Law-based mixing tends to **overestimate** mixture compressibility for hydrocarbon systems at high pressure, leading to undersized separators and pipelines in real design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Demonstration (2 marks):**
> Consider a 50:50 molar mixture of methane and propane at $T = 400$ K and $P = 60$ atm. Given $Z_{CH_4} = 1.15$ and $Z_{C_3H_8} = 0.42$.
>
> **Amagat's Law (volume-additive):**
> $Z_{mix} = (0.5)(1.15) + (0.5)(0.42) = 0.785$
>
> **Equivalent Dalton-Based Estimation (pseudo-volume approach for comparison):**
> Dalton's Law of additive pressures gives $P_{mix} = \sum y_i P_i$, but for mixture $Z$ estimation it is customary to use:
> $Z_{mix}^{Dalton} \approx \sum y_i Z_i$ — same as Amagat at the simplest level.
>
> The divergence appears when comparing **partial volume fractions**:
>
> $v_{CH_4}^{Amagat} = \dfrac{y_{CH_4} Z_{CH_4}}{Z_{mix}} = \dfrac{(0.5)(1.15)}{0.785} = 0.732$
>
> Compare with $y_{CH_4} = 0.500$.
>
> The partial volume fraction of methane is **46% larger** than its mole fraction, which Dalton's Law would not capture in a partial-volume framework. This is the practical divergence that matters in design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the partial volume fraction $v_i$ in terms of mole fraction $y_i$, pure-component compressibility factor $Z_i$, and mixture compressibility factor $Z_{mix}$. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gaseous mixture contains nitrogen and oxygen in the molar ratio 79:21. The mixture occupies a volume of 5.0 L at 300 K and 10 atm. The compressibility factors of pure N₂ and O₂ at these conditions are $Z_{N_2} = 1.05$ and $Z_{O_2} = 1.03$. Calculate:

* (i) The mixture compressibility factor $Z_{mix}$.
* (ii) The partial volume of each gas using Amagat's Law.
* (iii) The partial volume fractions and verify they sum to unity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Derivation of Partial Volume Fraction (7 marks)

> **Assumption (1 mark):** Each component $i$ in the mixture behaves as a pure real gas at the mixture temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, with its own pure-component compressibility factor $Z_i$.

**Step 1 — Partial Volume of Component $i$ [1 mark]:** For $n_i$ moles of pure component $i$ at $(T, P)$:

$$V_i = \frac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Total Volume of the Real Mixture [1 mark]:** Applying the real gas law to the entire mixture:

$$V_{total} = \frac{n_{total} Z_{mix} R T}{P}$$

**Step 3 — Form the Ratio [2 marks]:** The partial volume fraction is:

$$v_i = \frac{V_i}{V_{total}} = \frac{n_i Z_i R T \, / \, P}{n_{total} Z_{mix} R T \, / \, P} = \frac{n_i Z_i}{n_{total} Z_{mix}}$$

Since mole fraction $y_i = \dfrac{n_i}{n_{total}}$:

$$v_i = \frac{y_i Z_i}{Z_{mix}}$$

**Step 4 — Kay's Mixing Rule [1 mark]:** $Z_{mix} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j Z_j$, giving:

$$v_i = \frac{y_i Z_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} y_j Z_j}$$

**Step 5 — Limiting Case (1 mark):** For ideal gases, all $Z_i = 1$, so $Z_{mix} = 1$ and $v_i = y_i$. The partial volume fraction equals the mole fraction, which is consistent with Dalton's Law for ideal gases.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $y_{N_2} = 0.79$, $y_{O_2} = 0.21$; $V_{total} = 5.0$ L; $T = 300$ K; $P = 10$ atm; $Z_{N_2} = 1.05$, $Z_{O_2} = 1.03$.

**(i) Mixture compressibility factor (2 marks):**

$$Z_{mix} = y_{N_2} Z_{N_2} + y_{O_2} Z_{O_2}$$

$$Z_{mix} = (0.79)(1.05) + (0.21)(1.03) = 0.8295 + 0.2163 = 1.0458 \approx 1.046$$

**(ii) Partial volumes (3 marks):**

First, total moles:

$$n_{total} = \frac{P V_{total}}{Z_{mix} R T} = \frac{(10)(5.0)}{(1.0458)(0.08206)(300)} = \frac{50}{25.74} = 1.943 \text{ mol}$$

Component moles:
$n_{N_2} = (0.79)(1.943) = 1.535$ mol
$n_{O_2} = (0.21)(1.943) = 0.408$ mol

Partial volumes:

$$V_{N_2} = \frac{n_{N_2} Z_{N_2} R T}{P} = \frac{(1.535)(1.05)(0.08206)(300)}{10}$$

$$V_{N_2} = \frac{39.69}{10} = 3.969 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{O_2} = \frac{(0.408)(1.03)(0.08206)(300)}{10} = \frac{10.345}{10} = 1.035 \text{ L}$$

**(iii) Partial volume fractions and verification (2 marks):**

$$v_{N_2} = \frac{3.969}{5.0} = 0.794$$

$$v_{O_2} = \frac{1.035}{5.0} = 0.207$$

Verification:

$$v_{N_2} + v_{O_2} = 0.794 + 0.207 = 1.001 \approx 1.000 \checkmark$$

Also verify using the direct formula:

$$v_{N_2} = \frac{y_{N_2} Z_{N_2}}{Z_{mix}} = \frac{(0.79)(1.05)}{1.046} = \frac{0.8295}{1.046} = 0.793 \checkmark$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Component | Mole Fraction $y_i$ | Compressibility $Z_i$ | Partial Volume $V_i$ (L) | Vol. Fraction $v_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N_2$ | 0.79 | 1.05 | 3.97 | 0.794 |
| $O_2$ | 0.21 | 1.03 | 1.04 | 0.207 |
| **TOTAL** | 1.00 | 1.046 | 5.01 | 1.000 |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on Amagat's Law Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use the mixture compressibility factor $Z_{mix}$ in the denominator** when computing $V_{total}$ from moles. Many students incorrectly use $Z_i = 1$ throughout. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Confusing mole fraction with partial volume fraction in real-gas problems.** Writing $V_i = y_i V_{total}$ for a real gas is **wrong** — this is only true for ideal gases. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Mixing up units of $R$** — using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) when the problem uses $P$ in atm and $V$ in litres. Always match units. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
>
> 4. **Forgetting to apply Kay's mixing rule** for $Z_{mix}$ when only pure-component $Z_i$ are given. Examiners explicitly test this. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Not verifying that partial volumes sum to the total volume.** This is a free 1-mark sanity check that 90% of students skip. **(−1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Stating Amagat's Law and Dalton's Law as identical for ALL gases.** They are identical for *ideal* gases only. For real gases, the difference is small but exam-relevant. **(−1 mark)**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — AMAGAT'S LAW**
>
> * **Core Statement:** Total volume = sum of partial volumes at constant $T$ and $P$.
> * **Mathematical Form:** $V_{total} = \sum V_i = \sum \dfrac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}$
> * **The Mnemonic:** *"Amagat Adds Volumes; Dalton Adds Pressures."*
> * **Partial Volume Fraction (Real Gas):** $v_i = \dfrac{y_i Z_i}{Z_{mix}}$
> * **Kay's Mixing Rule:** $Z_{mix} = \sum y_i Z_i$
> * **Ideal Gas Identity:** $v_i = y_i = p_i$ (all three are equal for ideal gas)
> * **Real Gas Divergence:** $v_i \neq y_i$ in general — they differ by the factor $Z_i / Z_{mix}$
> * **Pure-Component Volume:** $V_i = \dfrac{n_i Z_i R T}{P}$
> * **Mixture Total Volume:** $V_{total} = \dfrac{n_{total} Z_{mix} R T}{P}$
> * **Best Application Domain:** High-pressure hydrocarbon gas mixtures (natural gas, refinery gas, LNG)
> * **Total Moles Formula:** $n_{total} = \dfrac{P V_{total}}{Z_{mix} R T}$
> * **Verification Check:** Always confirm $\sum v_i = 1$ and $\sum V_i = V_{total}$.
> * **Common Mistake:** Setting $Z = 1$ for real gas problems → 2 marks lost.
> * **Common Mistake:** Equating $v_i = y_i$ for real gases → 2 marks lost.
> * **Common Mistake:** Using wrong $R$ for given $P$ and $V$ units → 1–2 marks lost.
> * **Distinguishing Feature:** $V_i$ is computed at the *mixture's* $T$ and $P$, NOT at the *pure component's* standard state.
> * **KTU Frequency:** Tested in **every Module 1 exam** — both Part A (definition) and Part B (numerical) consistently.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Van der Waals Equation, Vapour Pressure: Effect of Temperature on Vapour Pressure - Antoine Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Van der Waals Equation, Vapour Pressure: Effect of Temperature on Vapour Pressure - Antoine Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Van der Waals Equation of State

The **Van der Waals Equation of State (VDW EOS)** is a thermodynamic constitutive relation that models the pressure $(P)$, volume $(V)$, and temperature $(T)$ behaviour of a *real fluid* by introducing two correction factors to the ideal gas law. Proposed by Johannes Diderik van der Waals in his 1873 doctoral thesis (which earned him the 1910 Nobel Prize in Physics), it accounts for two physical realities that the ideal gas model ignores:

1. **Finite molecular volume** — gas molecules are not point particles; they occupy a real, finite excluded volume.
2. **Intermolecular attractive forces** — gas molecules pull on each other, reducing the *observed* pressure compared to an ideal gas at the same density.

The equation in molar form is:

$$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT$$

where $V_m$ is the molar volume (m³/mol), $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $a, b$ are fluid-specific empirical constants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Tag — Module 1:** The Van der Waals equation is the *primary cubic equation of state* students are expected to derive, expand, and apply to calculate molar volumes, residual properties, and compressibility factors in single-component and multi-component systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need VDW?** The ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ fails badly near the critical point, in the saturation region, and at high pressures (typically above 10 bar for most gases). VDW, being cubic in volume, can mathematically resolve into three real roots — one of which correctly represents the liquid-like phase, one the vapour-like phase, and one is unphysical (used to bracket the saturation region via Maxwell construction).

### 1.2 Vapour Pressure — Formal Definition

**Vapour pressure ($P^{sat}$ or $P^{vap}$)** of a pure substance is the absolute pressure exerted by the vapour of that substance when it is in *thermodynamic equilibrium* with its own condensed phase (liquid or solid) at a given temperature. It is an *intrinsic thermodynamic property* — it depends only on the substance and the temperature, not on the amount of material.

$$P^{sat} = P^{sat}(T) \quad \text{only}$$

A liquid *boils* when its vapour pressure equals the total pressure of the surrounding atmosphere. At the **normal boiling point** ($T_b$), $P^{sat} = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vapour Pressure and Phase Equilibrium:** Vapour pressure is the single most important property for designing distillation columns, evaporators, condensers, reboilers, and any mass-transfer equipment. Without an accurate $P^{sat}$ value, the entire VLE (Vapour–Liquid Equilibrium) calculation chain collapses.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Analogy — The Crowded Elevator (Van der Waals):**
> Imagine an *ideal gas* as ghosts floating in a vast empty hall — they pass through each other freely, occupy no space, and feel no attraction. Now think of a *real gas* as a *crowded elevator full of soft tennis balls*. The balls take up real space (the tennis balls are the **$b$** term — *excluded volume*). Also, near the walls of the elevator, the balls feel a slight inward pull from the other balls behind them, which *reduces* the impact force on the walls (this *reduction in pressure* is the **$a/V_m^2$** term — *cohesive attraction*). The Van der Waals equation simply says: the *measured* pressure is less than the ideal pressure, and the *available* volume is less than the container volume.

> **Analogy — The Lid on a Boiling Pot (Vapour Pressure):**
> Pour some water into a sealed pot with a pressure gauge. As you heat the pot, the water molecules at the surface escape into the vapour space, and the pressure inside the pot rises. The pressure at which the rate of evaporation exactly equals the rate of condensation is the *vapour pressure*. Heat the pot more → vapour pressure rises. Remove the lid (vent) → vapour escapes, pressure drops. This is exactly the *Clausius–Clapeyron* relationship: temperature drives vapour pressure exponentially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Recap (for KTU problems):**
> - Pressure $P$: **Pa (N/m²)**, or kPa, or bar, or atm. KTU expects SI.
> - Volume $V$: m³, or cm³ (1 m³ = 10⁶ cm³).
> - Temperature $T$: **K (Kelvin)** — always Kelvin, never Celsius, in any EOS or vapour pressure equation.
> - Universal gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Genesis of the Van der Waals Equation — Two Correction Layers

The ideal gas law arises from the kinetic theory of gases under two simplifying assumptions:

1. Gas molecules have **negligible volume** (point masses).
2. Gas molecules have **no intermolecular forces** (no attraction, no repulsion).

Real gases violate both. Van der Waals corrected them layer by layer:

#### Layer 1 — Excluded Volume Correction (`b`)

If each molecule has a finite volume, the *space available for molecular motion* is the container volume minus the volume already occupied by the molecules themselves. For one mole of gas with molecular volume $v_0$, the *effective free volume* is:

$$V_m^{free} = V_m - b$$

where $b$ is approximately four times the actual molecular volume (it accounts for the *exclusion zone* around each pair of colliding molecules). $b$ has units of m³/mol.

#### Layer 2 — Internal Pressure Correction (`a`)

Molecules near the container wall experience a net *inward* pull from the molecules behind them. This reduces the momentum transfer to the wall, hence the *observed* pressure is less than the ideal pressure. The *attraction pressure deficit* is proportional to the square of the number density (since both the impinging molecule and the molecule pulling it back are counted):

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}$$

where $a$ is the *cohesion parameter*, with units of Pa·m⁶/mol². Larger $a$ means stronger attraction (e.g., $a$ for water is much larger than $a$ for nitrogen because of hydrogen bonding).

#### Combined VDW Equation

Replacing $P \rightarrow (P + a/V_m^2)$ and $V_m \rightarrow (V_m - b)$ in the ideal gas law:

$$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cubic Nature of VDW:** Expanding the equation as a polynomial in $V_m$ yields a cubic equation. For a given $P$ and $T$ inside the two-phase dome, VDW produces *three real roots* — these are the *Maxwell construction* basis for phase equilibrium calculations.

### 2.2 The Van der Waals Constants — How Are They Determined?

The constants $a$ and $b$ are *not universal*; they are substance-specific. Two common methods to find them:

#### Method 1 — From Critical Properties

At the **critical point**, the isotherm has an *inflection point*, so both the first and second derivatives of pressure with respect to volume are zero:

$$\left(\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_{T_c} = 0, \qquad \left(\dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial V_m^{2}}\right)_{T_c} = 0$$

Solving these simultaneously with the VDW equation gives:

$$a = \dfrac{27 R^{2} T_c^{2}}{64 P_c}, \qquad b = \dfrac{R T_c}{8 P_c}, \qquad Z_c = \dfrac{P_c V_{m,c}}{R T_c} = \dfrac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

> [!NOTE]
> The *predicted* critical compressibility factor $Z_c = 0.375$ is the same for *all substances* under VDW — a well-known weakness. Real values range from 0.27 (for water) to 0.30 (for most hydrocarbons). More accurate EOS (Peng–Robinson, Soave–Redlich–Kwong) fix this.

#### Method 2 — From Tabulated Data

For more accuracy, $a$ and $b$ are fitted to experimental $P$–$V$–$T$ data. KTU problems usually provide $a$ and $b$ directly, or ask you to compute them from $T_c$ and $P_c$.

### 2.3 Effect of Temperature on Vapour Pressure — The Physics

The vapour pressure of any pure substance **increases monotonically with temperature**. The quantitative link is the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**, which for a liquid–vapour equilibrium (assuming the vapour is ideal gas and the liquid molar volume $V_m^{L}$ is negligible compared to $V_m^{V}$) reduces to:

$$\dfrac{d \ln P^{sat}}{d T} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T^{2}}$$

> **Why exponential?** Integrating this with the assumption that $\Delta H_{vap}$ is approximately constant over the temperature range gives:
> $$\ln P^{sat} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + C$$
> This is a *linear* relationship between $\ln P^{sat}$ and $1/T$. Since $1/T$ is non-linear in $T$, $P^{sat}$ vs $T$ is *exponential* — a hallmark curve in chemical engineering thermodynamics.

> [!WARNING]
> **Assumption Pitfall:** The simple integrated form $\ln P^{sat} = A - B/T$ is only accurate over a *narrow* temperature range (≈ 10–20 K), because $\Delta H_{vap}$ is itself temperature-dependent. For wide ranges, we need the **Antoine equation**.

### 2.4 The Antoine Equation — A Refined Vapour Pressure Model

The **Antoine equation** is a three-parameter empirical correlation that improves on the simple Clausius–Clapeyron form by introducing a third constant $C$ in the denominator of the temperature term:

$$\boxed{\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}}$$

where:
- $P^{sat}$ is in **mmHg** (most common), or kPa, or bar — **check the unit convention in the constant table**.
- $T$ is in **°C** (most common in tabulated data) or **K** — **check the unit convention**.
- $A, B, C$ are **substance-specific Antoine constants**, fitted to experimental data.

Some handbooks use the natural logarithm form:

$$\ln P^{sat} = A' - \dfrac{B'}{T + C'}$$

with different numerical values for the constants. **KTU problems always specify which form to use.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is the Antoine equation better?** The third parameter $C$ allows the correlation to fit the *curvature* of the $\ln P^{sat}$ vs $1/T$ plot accurately over a temperature range of typically 50–100 K. Most chemical engineering simulators (Aspen, HYSYS, ChemCAD) use Antoine as the *default* vapour pressure model for moderate temperature ranges.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference**. Print it, memorize it, and reproduce it in derivations.

| # | Equation / Relation | Mathematical Form | Units | Remarks / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | $P$:Pa, $V$:m³, $n$:mol | Reference baseline |
| 2 | Van der Waals (molar) | $\left(P + a/V_m^{2}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT$ | $a$:Pa·m⁶/mol², $b$:m³/mol | Cubic in $V_m$ |
| 3 | Van der Waals constant $a$ | $a = 27 R^{2} T_c^{2} / (64 P_c)$ | Pa·m⁶/mol² | From critical point |
| 4 | Van der Waals constant $b$ | $b = R T_c / (8 P_c)$ | m³/mol | From critical point |
| 5 | Critical Compressibility (VDW) | $Z_c = 3/8 = 0.375$ | dimensionless | Weakness of VDW |
| 6 | VDW expanded (cubic in $V_m$) | $V_m^{3} - \left(b + RT/P\right) V_m^{2} + \left(a/P\right) V_m - ab/P = 0$ | m³/mol | Solve for $V_m$ |
| 7 | Clausius–Clapeyron (differential) | $d \ln P^{sat}/dT = \Delta H_{vap} / (R T^{2})$ | Pa, K, J/mol | Exact form |
| 8 | Clausius–Clapeyron (integrated, $\Delta H =$ const) | $\ln\left(P_2^{sat}/P_1^{sat}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(1/T_2 - 1/T_1\right)$ | dimensionless ratio | Two-point form |
| 9 | Linear C–C form | $\ln P^{sat} = -\Delta H_{vap}/(RT) + C$ | dimensionless | Slope gives $\Delta H_{vap}$ |
| 10 | Antoine Equation (log₁₀) | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - B/(T + C)$ | depends on constants | Three-parameter fit |
| 11 | Antoine Equation (ln) | $\ln P^{sat} = A' - B'/(T + C')$ | dimensionless | $\ln = \log_{10} \cdot \ln 10$ |
| 12 | Universal Gas Constant | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | SI | Always use this in SI |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Conversion Quick-Reference:**
> - 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 760 torr
> - 1 bar = 100 kPa = 0.9869 atm
> - 1 mmHg = 133.322 Pa
> - $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Distillation Column Design** | Antoine $P^{sat}$ is the primary input for Raoult's law and relative volatility calculation. |
| **Compressor / Pump Sizing** | VDW predicts molar volume → fluid density → mass flow rate → power. |
| **Refrigeration Cycles** | Vapour pressure curve defines the saturation dome; refrigerant selection relies on $P^{sat}(T)$. |
| **Storage Tank Venting** | Vapour pressure determines the breathing losses and emission rates. |
| **Safety Relief Valve Sizing** | Fire-case scenarios use $P^{sat}$ at elevated temperatures to size PSV orifices. |
| **Polymer Devolatilization** | Vapour pressure of residual monomers dictates vacuum level and residence time. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Van der Waals Equation from the Ideal Gas Law

We begin with the **ideal gas law for one mole**:

$$P V_m = R T$$

**Step 1 — Identify the two assumptions of the ideal gas:**
Assumption (i): Molecules are *point particles* → no excluded volume.
Assumption (ii): No intermolecular forces → no cohesion correction.

**Step 2 — Apply the excluded-volume correction (`b`):**
Replace $V_m$ with $(V_m - b)$:

$$P (V_m - b) = R T \quad \text{...(i)}$$

> *Logic:* The molecules can only move in the free space, not in the space already occupied by themselves. $b$ is a substance-specific constant representing four times the actual volume of one mole of molecules (this factor of 4 comes from the integration of pairwise exclusion volumes).

**Step 3 — Apply the cohesion correction (`a`):**
Real molecules attract each other. A molecule striking the wall is *retarded* by the attraction of molecules behind it, so the measured pressure is *less* than the ideal pressure. The pressure *deficit* is proportional to the square of the molar density (proportional to $1/V_m^{2}$):

$$P_{ideal} = P_{measured} + \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}$$

Substitute $P_{ideal}$ in place of $P$ in equation (i):

$$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}\right)(V_m - b) = R T$$

> *Logic:* Both corrections act simultaneously — the molecules have finite volume AND they pull on each other. The two corrections cannot be added sequentially; they must be applied together as a single replacement.

**Step 4 — Identify VDW as a cubic equation in $V_m$:**
Expanding the product:

\begin{aligned}
P V_m - P b + \dfrac{a}{V_m} - \dfrac{a b}{V_m^{2}} &= R T \\
\text{Multiply through by } V_m^{2}: \quad P V_m^{3} - P b V_m^{2} + a V_m - a b &= R T V_m^{2}
\end{aligned}

Rearranging into a standard cubic form:

$$P V_m^{3} - (P b + R T) V_m^{2} + a V_m - a b = 0$$

Or, in terms of $Z = P V_m / (R T)$:

$$V_m^{3} - \left(b + \dfrac{R T}{P}\right) V_m^{2} + \left(\dfrac{a}{P}\right) V_m - \dfrac{a b}{P} = 0$$

This is a **cubic in $V_m$**, which is why VDW can yield up to three real roots in the two-phase region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Cubic" Matters:** Any cubic equation of state (VDW, Redlich–Kwong, Peng–Robinson, Soave) inherits this three-root property, which is mathematically essential for representing phase coexistence. Linear or quadratic EOS cannot model condensation.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Antoine Equation from the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

**Step 1 — Start with the differential Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**

$$\dfrac{d P^{sat}}{d T} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \Delta V_{vap}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the two simplifying assumptions:**
Assumption (i): The vapour behaves as an ideal gas, so $V_m^{V} = R T / P^{sat}$.
Assumption (ii): The molar volume of the liquid is much smaller than that of the vapour: $V_m^{V} \gg V_m^{L}$, hence $\Delta V_{vap} \approx V_m^{V} = R T / P^{sat}$.

Substitute:

$$\dfrac{d P^{sat}}{d T} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P^{sat}}{T \cdot R T} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P^{sat}}{R T^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Separate the variables and use logarithmic identity:**

$$\dfrac{d P^{sat}}{P^{sat}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{d T}{T^{2}}$$

$$\int \dfrac{d P^{sat}}{P^{sat}} = \int \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{d T}{T^{2}}$$

$$\ln P^{sat} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + \text{constant}$$

This is the **linear C–C form** — a straight line on a $\ln P^{sat}$ vs $1/T$ plot. The slope is $-\Delta H_{vap}/R$.

**Step 4 — Recognize the limitation of constant $\Delta H_{vap}$:**
In reality, $\Delta H_{vap}$ varies with temperature. The simplest empirical improvement is to add an *offset* to $T$ in the denominator, which Antoine proposed:

$$\ln P^{sat} = A' - \dfrac{B'}{T + C'}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to log₁₀ form for compatibility with handbooks:**
Since $\ln x = \ln 10 \cdot \log_{10} x$, divide through by $\ln 10$:

$$\log_{10} P^{sat} = \underbrace{\dfrac{A'}{\ln 10}}_{A} - \underbrace{\dfrac{B'/\ln 10}{T + C'}}_{B/(T+C)}$$

$$\boxed{\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 Insight:** The Antoine equation is *empirically derived*, not rigorously derived from first principles. It is a *functional fit* to experimental $P^{sat}$ data using three adjustable parameters. The fact that the functional form $1/(T+C)$ is *almost* derivable from a temperature-dependent $\Delta H_{vap}$ is what gives Antoine its wide applicability.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Solving VDW for Molar Volume

> **Problem:** Compute the molar volume of saturated steam at 100 °C and 101.325 kPa using the Van der Waals equation. Given: $a = 0.5536$ Pa·m⁶/mol², $b = 3.049 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/mol.

**Given:**
- $T = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15$ K
- $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 101325$ Pa
- $a = 0.5536$ Pa·m⁶/mol²
- $b = 3.049 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/mol
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Write the cubic in $V_m$:**

$$P V_m^{3} - (P b + R T) V_m^{2} + a V_m - a b = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute the coefficients:**

\begin{aligned}
P b &= 101325 \times 3.049 \times 10^{-5} = 3.090 \text{ Pa·m³/mol} \\
R T &= 8.314 \times 373.15 = 3102.5 \text{ J/mol} = 3102.5 \text{ Pa·m³/mol} \\
P b + R T &= 3.090 + 3102.5 = 3105.6 \text{ Pa·m³/mol} \\
a &= 0.5536 \text{ Pa·m⁶/mol²} \\
a b &= 0.5536 \times 3.049 \times 10^{-5} = 1.688 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·m⁹/mol³}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Substitute into the cubic:**

$$101325 \cdot V_m^{3} - 3105.6 \cdot V_m^{2} + 0.5536 \cdot V_m - 1.688 \times 10^{-5} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve numerically (iterative / Newton–Raphson).** A reasonable initial guess from the ideal gas law:

$$V_m^{ideal} = \dfrac{R T}{P} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 373.15}{101325} = 0.03062 \text{ m³/mol}$$

Apply Newton's method with $f(V_m) = P V_m^3 - (Pb+RT) V_m^2 + a V_m - ab$:

After convergence, the physical (largest) root is:

$$V_m^{VDW} \approx 0.03018 \text{ m³/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the ideal gas result:**

| Model | Molar Volume $V_m$ (m³/mol) | \% Deviation from Ideal |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal Gas | 0.03062 | — |
| Van der Waals | 0.03018 | -1.44 % |

> The VDW volume is *slightly less* than the ideal — physically correct because the molecules themselves take up space.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Antoine Equation Application

> **Problem:** The Antoine constants for water (with $P$ in mmHg and $T$ in °C) over the range 1–100 °C are: $A = 8.07131$, $B = 1730.63$, $C = 233.426$. Calculate the vapour pressure of water at 80 °C.

**Given:**
- $T = 80$ °C
- $A = 8.07131$
- $B = 1730.63$
- $C = 233.426$

**Step 1 — Write the Antoine equation:**

$$\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T + C$:**

$$T + C = 80 + 233.426 = 313.426$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio $B/(T+C)$:**

$$\dfrac{B}{T + C} = \dfrac{1730.63}{313.426} = 5.5214$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\log_{10} P^{sat}$:**

$$\log_{10} P^{sat} = 8.07131 - 5.5214 = 2.5499$$

**Step 5 — Take the antilog:**

$$P^{sat} = 10^{2.5499} = 354.8 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to SI (kPa) for KTU consistency:**

$$P^{sat} = 354.8 \times 0.13332 = 47.30 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with the experimental steam-table value at 80 °C:** $P^{sat}_{exp} = 47.39$ kPa.

> The Antoine prediction deviates by less than 0.2 % — excellent agreement over this range.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Two-Point Clausius–Clapeyron Interpolation

> **Problem:** Water has a vapour pressure of 1.0 kPa at 27.13 °C and 101.325 kPa at 100 °C. Estimate the latent heat of vaporisation of water.

**Given:**
- $P_1^{sat} = 1.0$ kPa, $T_1 = 27.13 + 273.15 = 300.28$ K
- $P_2^{sat} = 101.325$ kPa, $T_2 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)

**Step 1 — Apply the two-point C–C form:**

$$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the left side:**

$$\ln(101.325 / 1.0) = \ln(101.325) = 4.619$$

**Step 3 — Compute the right side bracket:**

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} = \dfrac{1}{373.15} - \dfrac{1}{300.28} = 0.002680 - 0.003330 = -6.500 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta H_{vap}$:**

$$\Delta H_{vap} = -\dfrac{R \cdot 4.619}{-6.500 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 4.619}{6.500 \times 10^{-4}}$$

\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{vap} &= \dfrac{38.405}{6.500 \times 10^{-4}} \\
\Delta H_{vap} &= 59,085 \text{ J/mol} \\
\Delta H_{vap} &\approx 59.1 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The accepted value for water's $\Delta H_{vap}$ at 100 °C is about 40.7 kJ/mol. Our estimate is *higher* because the two-point C–C form treats $\Delta H_{vap}$ as constant from 27 °C to 100 °C, but the *average* $\Delta H_{vap}$ over this range is higher than at 100 °C alone (where the latent heat is lower because of the higher temperature).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logical Block Diagram — From Ideal Gas to Van der Waals

This block diagram traces the *conceptual flow* of how we progressively refine the ideal gas law to obtain the VDW equation of state.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["IDEAL GAS LAW PV = nRT"]
    nodeB["ASSUMPTION 1 Molecules are point particles ZERO VOLUME"]
    nodeC["REALITY Molecules have FINITE VOLUME Excluded volume b"]
    nodeD["REPLACE V with V minus b P V minus b = nRT"]
    nodeE["ASSUMPTION 2 No intermolecular forces ZERO ATTRACTION"]
    nodeF["REALITY Molecules ATTRACT each other Cohesion parameter a"]
    nodeG["REPLACE P with P plus a over V squared"]
    nodeH["VAN DER WAALS EQUATION P plus a over V squared times V minus b = nRT"]
    nodeI["EXPAND Cubic in V Polynomial of degree 3"]
    nodeJ["SOLVE Three real roots in two phase region LIQUID VAPOUR UNPHYSICAL"]
    nodeK["APPLY MAXWELL CONSTRUCTION Phase equilibrium calculation"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

### 4.2 Vapour Pressure Concept Map — Clausius–Clapeyron to Antoine

This flowchart maps the *evolution* of vapour pressure correlations and their relationship to thermodynamic principles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["THERMODYNAMIC FIRST PRINCIPLES Gibbs Phase Rule and Chemical Potential Equality"]
    nodeB["CLAUSIUS CLAPEYRON EQUATION Differential Form d ln P by d T equals Delta H vap over R T squared"]
    nodeC["APPROXIMATION 1 Vapour is ideal gas V m V equals R T over P sat"]
    nodeD["APPROXIMATION 2 Liquid molar volume negligible V m V much greater than V m L"]
    nodeE["APPROXIMATION 3 Delta H vap is CONSTANT over temperature range"]
    nodeF["SIMPLE C C FORM ln P sat equals A minus Delta H vap over R times 1 over T"]
    nodeG["RECOGNITION Delta H vap VARIES with T Real curves deviate from straight line"]
    nodeH["EMPIRICAL REFINEMENT Add OFFSET C to temperature in denominator"]
    nodeI["ANTOINE EQUATION log base 10 P sat equals A minus B over T plus C"]
    nodeJ["DATA FIT Determine A B C from experimental P sat T data"]
    nodeK["APPLICATION Distillation design VLE calculation Safety valve sizing"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Vapour Pressure Model

This block-level decision tree helps students select the appropriate vapour pressure correlation for a given KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START Given a vapour pressure problem"]
    nodeB["What is the temperature range? Narrow less than 20 K"]
    nodeC["WIDE more than 20 K OR multiple components"]
    nodeD["Is Delta H vap given? YES"]
    nodeE["Use SIMPLE C C FORM ln P sat equals A minus Delta H vap over R T"]
    nodeF["NO Look up Antoine constants A B C in data table"]
    nodeG["Use ANTOINE EQUATION log base 10 P sat equals A minus B over T plus C"]
    nodeH["Check UNITS of A B C and T in the data table"]
    nodeI["Convert T to required unit Celsius or Kelvin"]
    nodeJ["Compute log base 10 P sat then take antilog for P sat"]
    nodeK["Convert P sat to required unit kPa or atm or bar"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — VDW Cubic Roots in the Two-Phase Region

This schematic illustrates how the cubic VDW equation produces three real roots inside the saturation dome and the role of the Maxwell construction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["GIVEN Pressure P and Temperature T INSIDE two phase dome"]
    nodeB["SOLVE VDW Cubic for V m Three Real Roots expected"]
    nodeC["ROOT 1 SMALLEST V m Liquid like volume"]
    nodeD["ROOT 2 MIDDLE V m UNPHYSICAL Discard"]
    nodeE["ROOT 3 LARGEST V m Vapour like volume"]
    nodeF["Apply MAXWELL CONSTRUCTION Area of curve 1 equals Area of curve 2"]
    nodeG["OUTPUT Two phase region bounded by saturation curve"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeD
    nodeB --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** — **Q1. (3 Marks)**
> **State and explain the Van der Waals equation of state. What are the physical significances of the constants `a` and `b`?**

**Model Answer:**

The Van der Waals equation of state for one mole of a real gas is:

$$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT$$

- The term $a/V_m^{2}$ is called the **cohesion pressure** or *internal pressure*. It represents the *reduction in observed pressure* caused by the intermolecular attractive forces between gas molecules. A molecule striking the container wall is pulled back by the molecules behind it, so the *measured* pressure is less than what an ideal gas would exert. The constant $a$ (with units Pa·m⁶/mol²) quantifies the strength of this attraction; larger $a$ means stronger intermolecular forces.
- The term $b$ (with units m³/mol) is the **excluded volume** per mole. It represents the *finite volume* actually occupied by the gas molecules themselves, which is therefore not available for molecular motion. The constant $b$ is approximately four times the actual molar volume of the molecules (the factor of 4 arises from pairwise exclusion integrals).

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — **Q2. (3 Marks)**
> **State the Antoine equation. Why is it preferred over the simple Clausius–Clapeyron equation for vapour pressure estimation?**

**Model Answer:**

The Antoine equation is a three-parameter empirical correlation for vapour pressure:

$$\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$$

where $A, B, C$ are substance-specific Antoine constants, $P^{sat}$ is the saturation pressure, and $T$ is the temperature (in the units specified by the constant table — usually °C or K, and $P^{sat}$ in mmHg or kPa).

It is preferred over the simple integrated Clausius–Clapeyron form $\ln P^{sat} = -\Delta H_{vap}/(RT) + C$ because:

1. The simple C–C equation assumes $\Delta H_{vap}$ is *constant* over the temperature range, which is a poor assumption for spans greater than 20 K. The third constant $C$ in the Antoine equation empirically absorbs the temperature dependence of $\Delta H_{vap}$.
2. The Antoine equation typically predicts vapour pressure within 1–2 % over a 50–100 K range, whereas the simple C–C equation can deviate by 5–10 % over the same range.
3. Antoine constants are tabulated in standard handbooks (Perry's, CRC, NIST) for thousands of substances, making the equation practically convenient for engineering calculations.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** — **Q3. (14 Marks)**
>
> **Question A:**
> **(a)** Derive the Van der Waals equation of state from the ideal gas law, clearly explaining the role of constants $a$ and $b$. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** For nitrogen, the Van der Waals constants are $a = 0.1408$ Pa·m⁶/mol² and $b = 3.913 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/mol. Calculate the pressure exerted by 1 mole of nitrogen gas confined in a 0.5 L vessel at 300 K using (i) the ideal gas law and (ii) the Van der Waals equation. Comment on the difference. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q3 — Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

The derivation proceeds in three conceptual steps:

**Step 1 — Start with the ideal gas law for one mole:**

$$P V_m = R T$$

**Step 2 — Apply the volume correction (finite size of molecules):**
Real molecules occupy a finite volume. The space available for molecular motion is the container volume minus the volume already occupied by the molecules themselves:

$$V_{available} = V_m - b$$

The ideal gas law, corrected for excluded volume, becomes:

$$P (V_m - b) = R T \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the pressure correction (intermolecular forces):**
Real molecules attract each other. A molecule striking the wall is *retarded* by the attraction of the bulk molecules behind it. The reduction in pressure is proportional to the square of the number density $\rho = N/V_m$, hence to $1/V_m^{2}$:

$$P_{ideal} = P_{observed} + \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}$$

Substituting $P_{ideal}$ in equation (i):

$$\left(P + \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}\right)(V_m - b) = R T$$

This is the **Van der Waals equation of state**. Constants $a$ and $b$ are as defined in Q1.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 1$ mol, $V = 0.5 \text{ L} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}$, so $V_m = V/n = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}\text{/mol}$
- $T = 300$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $a = 0.1408$ Pa·m⁶/mol², $b = 3.913 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/mol

**(i) Ideal gas pressure:**

$$P_{ideal} = \dfrac{R T}{V_m} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 300}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{2494.2}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.988 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 4.99 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) VDW pressure:**
The VDW equation solved for $P$ is:

$$P = \dfrac{R T}{V_m - b} - \dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}}$$

Compute $V_m - b$:

$$V_m - b = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} - 3.913 \times 10^{-5} = 4.609 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{mol}$$

Compute $RT/(V_m - b)$:

$$\dfrac{R T}{V_m - b} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 300}{4.609 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{2494.2}{4.609 \times 10^{-4}} = 5.412 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

Compute $a/V_m^{2}$:

$$\dfrac{a}{V_m^{2}} = \dfrac{0.1408}{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} = \dfrac{0.1408}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 5.632 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$$

VDW pressure:

$$P_{VDW} = 5.412 \times 10^{6} - 5.632 \times 10^{5} = 4.849 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 4.85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Comment on the difference:**

| Model | Pressure (MPa) |
|---|---|
| Ideal Gas | 4.99 |
| Van der Waals | 4.85 |
| Deviation | -2.8 % |

The VDW pressure is *lower* than the ideal gas pressure by 2.8 %. This is because the intermolecular attractive forces (the $a$ term) reduce the momentum transfer to the wall. At 4.99 MPa, the gas is at moderately high pressure; even nitrogen, normally near-ideal, begins to show non-ideality. The excluded-volume correction *alone* would *increase* the pressure (since the denominator is smaller), but it is more than offset by the attractive correction in this case.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** — **Q3. (14 Marks)**
>
> **Question B (Alternative Choice):**
> **(a)** Define vapour pressure. State and derive the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, clearly listing the assumptions involved. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** The Antoine constants for benzene are: $A = 6.90565$, $B = 1211.033$, $C = 220.790$ (with $P$ in mmHg and $T$ in °C). Calculate (i) the vapour pressure of benzene at 50 °C, and (ii) the temperature at which benzene's vapour pressure is exactly 1 atm (the normal boiling point). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for Q3 — Question B:**

**(a) Vapour Pressure Definition + Clausius–Clapeyron Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The vapour pressure of a pure substance is the absolute pressure exerted by its vapour when the vapour is in *thermodynamic equilibrium* with its own liquid (or solid) phase at a specified temperature.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1 — Start from the equality of chemical potentials** for the liquid and vapour phases at equilibrium:

$$\mu^{L}(T, P^{sat}) = \mu^{V}(T, P^{sat})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Gibbs–Helmholtz / Maxwell relation** for the change in chemical potential along the saturation curve. The rigorous derivation uses the entropy and molar volume difference:

$$\dfrac{d P^{sat}}{d T} = \dfrac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \Delta V_{vap}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Assumption 1: Vapour behaves as an ideal gas.**

$$V_m^{V} = \dfrac{R T}{P^{sat}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Assumption 2: Liquid molar volume is negligible compared to vapour molar volume.**

$$\Delta V_{vap} = V_m^{V} - V_m^{L} \approx V_m^{V} = \dfrac{R T}{P^{sat}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$\dfrac{d P^{sat}}{d T} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P^{sat}}{R T^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Separate variables:**

$$\dfrac{d P^{sat}}{P^{sat}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \cdot \dfrac{d T}{T^{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Integrate (assuming $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant over the range):**

$$\ln P^{sat} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T} + C$$

This is the **integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation**, which is linear in $1/T$ when $\ln P^{sat}$ is plotted against $1/T$.

**(b) Antoine Equation Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $A = 6.90565$, $B = 1211.033$, $C = 220.790$; $P$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C.

**(i) Vapour pressure at $T = 50$ °C:**

$$\log_{10} P^{sat} = 6.90565 - \dfrac{1211.033}{50 + 220.790} = 6.90565 - \dfrac{1211.033}{270.790}$$

$$\log_{10} P^{sat} = 6.90565 - 4.4722 = 2.4334$$

$$P^{sat} = 10^{2.4334} = 271.2 \text{ mmHg}$$

Convert to kPa:

$$P^{sat} = 271.2 \times 0.13332 = 36.16 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Normal boiling point ($P^{sat} = 760$ mmHg):**

$$\log_{10}(760) = 2.8808 = 6.90565 - \dfrac{1211.033}{T + 220.790}$$

$$\dfrac{1211.033}{T + 220.790} = 6.90565 - 2.8808 = 4.0248$$

$$T + 220.790 = \dfrac{1211.033}{4.0248} = 300.89$$

$$T = 300.89 - 220.790 = 80.10 \text{ °C}$$

**Verification:** The accepted normal boiling point of benzene is 80.1 °C — our calculation matches the experimental value to three significant figures, confirming the Antoine equation's accuracy.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic — Read Carefully Before Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Temperature in Wrong Units (–1 to –2 marks):** The most frequent error is plugging Celsius into an equation that requires Kelvin, or vice versa. The Antoine equation is *especially* prone to this because tabulated constants may be in °C or K — *always read the table header*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $\ln 10$ Conversion (–1 mark):** If a problem gives you $\Delta H_{vap}$ in kJ/mol and asks you to find $P^{sat}$ using the *linear* C–C form, you must convert $\Delta H_{vap}$ to J/mol *before* dividing by $R$ in the slope.
>
> 3. **Mismatched Pressure Units in the Antoine Equation (–2 marks):** If the constants are calibrated for mmHg, the computed $P^{sat}$ is in mmHg; you must explicitly convert to kPa or atm if the answer requires it. Simply writing the mmHg value *without unit conversion* when the question asks for kPa will lose full marks.
>
> 4. **Sign Error in the Cohesion Term (–1 mark):** In the VDW equation, the *attraction correction* is *added* to $P$ (because we are correcting the ideal pressure to obtain the observed pressure): $P = RT/(V_m - b) - a/V_m^{2}$. Some students write $+ a/V_m^{2}$ in the pressure expression, which is a *sign error*.
>
> 5. **Not Reporting the Physical Root of the VDW Cubic (–1 mark):** If asked to find the molar volume, you must explicitly identify which of the three cubic roots is the *physically meaningful* one and discard the other two (or two are complex and the third is the only real solution).
>
> 6. **Omitting the Maxwell Construction (–2 marks):** If the problem asks for liquid and vapour volumes in the two-phase region, you must mention or apply the Maxwell equal-area rule to locate the saturation volumes. Simply picking the smallest and largest roots is a *first-order approximation* and may not satisfy the equal-area condition.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section. Read it the night before the exam.**

- [ ] **Van der Waals Equation** (molar form): $\left(P + a/V_m^{2}\right)\left(V_m - b\right) = RT$. Cubic in $V_m$.
- [ ] **Constant $a$ (Pa·m⁶/mol²):** Cohesion / attraction. Larger $a$ = stronger intermolecular forces (e.g., water > nitrogen).
- [ ] **Constant $b$ (m³/mol):** Excluded volume. Approximately $4 \times$ the actual molar volume of the molecules.
- [ ] **From critical properties:** $a = 27 R^2 T_c^2 / (64 P_c)$, $b = R T_c / (8 P_c)$, $Z_c = 3/8$.
- [ ] **VDW limitations:** Predicts same $Z_c$ for all substances (weakness); poor near critical point; qualitative only.
- [ ] **Vapour pressure** is the saturation pressure of a pure liquid at a given temperature; depends only on substance and $T$.
- [ ] **Vapour pressure always increases monotonically with temperature.**
- [ ] **Clausius–Clapeyron (differential):** $d\ln P^{sat}/dT = \Delta H_{vap}/(RT^2)$.
- [ ] **Integrated C–C (constant $\Delta H_{vap}$):** $\ln P^{sat} = A - \Delta H_{vap}/(RT)$.
- [ ] **Two-point C–C form:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -(\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$.
- [ ] **Antoine Equation:** $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - B/(T + C)$. Three constants, fitted to data.
- [ ] **Antoine vs C–C:** Antoine is more accurate over a wider range (typically 50–100 K) because it captures the temperature dependence of $\Delta H_{vap}$ through the $C$ term.
- [ ] **Always check units** of Antoine constants — $P$ in mmHg/kPa/atm; $T$ in °C/K. The convention is given in the table header.
- [ ] **Conversion: $\ln x = 2.3026 \log_{10} x$.** Use this when switching between natural and base-10 forms of the Antoine or C–C equation.
- [ ] **KTU's favourite 7-mark sub-question:** Derive VDW from ideal gas, or derive Antoine from C–C, *then* solve a numerical. Both steps must be in your answer.
- [ ] **Sanity check:** VDW predicts $P_{VDW} < P_{ideal}$ at moderate pressures (attraction dominates); at very high pressures, the excluded volume term dominates and $P_{VDW} > P_{ideal}$.
- [ ] **The cubic nature of VDW** is mathematically essential for representing phase coexistence — three real roots in the two-phase dome, one unphysical (Maxwell construction).
- [ ] **Use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) in SI units** — converting to L·atm/(mol·K) when needed: $R = 0.08206$ L·atm/(mol·K).
- [ ] **Memorize the Antoine constants** for at least water, benzene, toluene, ethanol, and acetone — these are the five most-tested substances in KTU exams.
- [ ] **Final check before writing answer:** Sign of cohesion term, unit of temperature, unit of pressure, identification of the physical root.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clausius-Clapeyron Equation, Problem Solving in Equations of State

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Clausius-Clapeyron Equation — Formal Definition

The **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation** is a fundamental thermodynamic relationship that quantitatively describes how the **vapor pressure of a pure substance** varies with temperature along a **phase equilibrium curve** (typically the liquid–vapor coexistence curve). In the language of the KTU 2024 *Process Calculations* syllabus, it is the mathematical bridge that connects **thermal energy (temperature)**, **latent heat of vaporization**, and **saturation pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> The Clausius-Clapeyron equation expresses the slope of the saturation (vapor pressure) curve on a P–T diagram as a function of the **latent heat of phase change (ΔH)**, the **molar volume change (ΔV)**, and the **absolute temperature (T)**. It is the foundational equation for computing vapor pressures, boiling points, and condensation conditions in process equipment.

The differential form of the equation is:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T \cdot \Delta V}
$$

where:
- $\frac{dP}{dT}$ = slope of the vapor pressure curve
- $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ = molar latent heat of vaporization
- $\Delta V = V_g - V_l$ = difference between molar volumes of vapor and liquid
- $T$ = absolute temperature

## 1.2 The Equations of State — Formal Definition

An **Equation of State (EoS)** is a thermodynamic constitutive relation that relates the **state variables** of a pure substance or mixture — typically **Pressure (P), Volume (V), Temperature (T)**, and the **number of moles (n)** — through a single mathematical expression. The most celebrated EoS is the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$
PV = nRT
$$

In the context of *Process Calculations*, the KTU 2024 syllabus requires students to be proficient in applying several real-fluid equations of state to calculate P, V, T, or to estimate compressibility factors for non-ideal systems.

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Mountain Hiker and the Boiling Kettle"**
>
> Imagine you are a hiker climbing a mountain. As you climb higher, the atmospheric pressure drops. You notice that water in a kettle boils at a lower temperature (say 80°C instead of 100°C). Why? Because the boiling point is the temperature at which the **vapor pressure of water equals the surrounding pressure**. Lower surrounding pressure → easier to vaporize → lower boiling temperature. The Clausius-Clapeyron equation mathematically captures *exactly* this trade-off: **as pressure changes, so does the temperature at which two phases coexist**.
>
> For Equations of State, picture the EoS as a **"recipe book"** for gases. Just as a baker adjusts flour, water, and yeast ratios to get a particular cake, a chemical engineer adjusts P, V, and T using an EoS to predict gas behavior. The **Ideal Gas Law** is the simplest recipe (valid at low pressure, high temperature), while the **van der Waals EoS** is a more refined recipe that accounts for **molecular size** and **intermolecular attractions**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants to Memorize (Bolded for Quick Reference):**
> - **Universal Gas Constant, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K) = 1.987 cal/(mol·K)**
> - **Standard Pressure, P° = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psia**
> - **Normal Boiling Point, T_b** : the temperature at which vapor pressure equals **1 atm (101.325 kPa)**
> - **Critical Constants** (P_c, V_c, T_c) : the thermodynamic state beyond which the liquid and vapor phases are indistinguishable

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation Exists

The equation arises directly from the **Clapeyron theorem**, which is derived from the equality of the molar Gibbs free energy of two coexisting phases:

$$
G_l = G_v \quad \text{(at equilibrium along the saturation curve)}
$$

Differentiating along the curve yields the general **Clapeyron equation**:

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S_{\text{vap}}}{\Delta V} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T \cdot \Delta V}
$$

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Phase Equilibrium Condition:** Two phases coexist when their molar Gibbs free energies are equal.
- **Step 2 — Path Independence of State Functions:** Since G, S, H, and V are state functions, we can write $G = H - TS$, so $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S = 0$ → $\Delta S = \Delta H / T$.
- **Step 3 — Maxwell Relation Bridge:** The slope of the P–T coexistence line is $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V}$ from fundamental thermodynamic identities.
- **Step 4 — Combining:** Substituting $\Delta S = \frac{\Delta H}{T}$ gives the master **Clapeyron equation**.

### 2.1.2 The Clausius Simplification

Since $V_g \gg V_l$ at low to moderate pressures, we assume $V_l \approx 0$, so $\Delta V \approx V_g$.

Applying the **Ideal Gas Law** to the vapor phase:

$$
V_g = \frac{RT}{P}
$$

Substituting into the Clapeyron equation (and assuming $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ is roughly constant over the temperature range of interest — the **Trouton's rule** approximation):

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \cdot P}{R T^2}
$$

Rearranging into the **integrated (exponential) form**:

$$
\ln P = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R T} + C
$$

or, between two states:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Antoine Equation (Empirical Extension) — Frequently Tested in KTU:**
> For improved accuracy over wide temperature ranges, the three-parameter **Antoine equation** is widely used:
> $$\log_{10} P = A - \frac{B}{T + C}$$
> where A, B, C are substance-specific Antoine constants, P is in mmHg, and T is in °C.

## 2.2 Equations of State — A Structured Catalog

The KTU 2024 syllabus in *Process Calculations* emphasizes the following EoS, ordered by increasing physical realism:

### 2.2.1 Ideal Gas Equation

$$
PV = nRT
$$

- **Assumption:** Negligible molecular volume, no intermolecular forces.
- **Validity:** Low pressure, high temperature, non-polar gases (e.g., He, H₂, N₂ at ambient conditions).

### 2.2.2 Van der Waals Equation

$$
\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = RT
$$

Per mole:

$$
\left(P + \frac{a}{V_m^2}\right)(V_m - b) = RT
$$

- **'a' term:** Corrects for **attractive forces** between molecules (intermolecular cohesion).
- **'b' term:** Corrects for the **finite volume** occupied by the molecules themselves.
- **Critical Constants in terms of a and b:**
  $$P_c = \frac{a}{27 b^2}, \quad V_{c,m} = 3b, \quad T_c = \frac{8a}{27 R b}$$

### 2.2.3 Virial Equation of State (Truncated)

$$
Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT} = 1 + B'P + C'P^2 + \ldots
$$

or in density form:

$$
Z = 1 + B\rho + C\rho^2 + \ldots
$$

- **'B'** = second virial coefficient (accounts for pair-wise interactions).
- **Most accurate** near the critical region if higher terms are included.

### 2.2.4 Redlich-Kwong Equation

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{\sqrt{T} \, V_m(V_m + b)}
$$

- Improved over van der Waals for hydrocarbon systems.
- $a = 0.42748 \frac{R^2 T_c^{2.5}}{P_c}$, $b = 0.08664 \frac{RT_c}{P_c}$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Units | KTU Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T \Delta V}$ | Slope of coexistence curve | Pa/K | Boiler design, vacuum distillation |
| 2 | $\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Vapor pressure at T₂ | Pa or kPa | Single-component vapor pressure calc |
| 3 | $\log_{10} P = A - \frac{B}{T + C}$ | Empirical vapor pressure | mmHg | Steam tables, chemical data handbooks |
| 4 | $PV = nRT$ | Ideal gas state | Pa·m³, mol | Low-pressure gas calculations |
| 5 | $\left(P+\frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V-b) = nRT$ | Real gas state | Pa·m³, mol | Non-ideal gas processes, CO₂, NH₃ |
| 6 | $Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT}$ | Compressibility factor | dimensionless | Real-gas deviation measure |
| 7 | $P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}$, $T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$ | Critical constants | Pa, K | Determining a, b from critical data |
| 8 | $P_{R} = P/P_c$, $T_{R} = T/T_c$ | Reduced properties | dimensionless | Law of Corresponding States |
| 9 | $\Delta S_{\text{vap}} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T_b}$ | Trouton's rule (≈ 88 J/mol·K) | J/(mol·K) | Estimating ΔH from boiling point |
| 10 | $\rho_{\text{specific}} = \frac{PM}{RT}$ | Gas density | kg/m³ | Mass-volume conversions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Distillation Column Design:** Clausius-Clapeyron + Antoine → bubble/dew point calculations at each tray.
> - **Steam Boilers & Power Plants:** Predicting saturation pressure at operating temperature.
> - **Compressors & Pumps:** EoS to compute work of compression for non-ideal gases.
> - **Refrigeration Cycles:** Evaporator/condenser pressure selection.
> - **Process Safety:** Predicting vapor cloud formation in storage tanks under ambient temperature changes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Clausius-Clapeyron Equation

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Clapeyron Equation (Master Derivation)

**Goal:** Derive the master Clapeyron relation starting from fundamental thermodynamic principles.

**Step 1 — Phase Equilibrium:**
At the boundary where liquid and vapor coexist in equilibrium, the molar Gibbs free energy of each phase is equal:

$$
G_l(T, P) = G_v(T, P)
$$

**Step 2 — Differential Change Along the Coexistence Curve:**
Differentiating the equality:

$$
dG_l = dG_v
$$

Using the fundamental relation $dG = V\,dP - S\,dT$ (for closed, single-component system):

$$
V_l\,dP - S_l\,dT = V_v\,dP - S_v\,dT
$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging:**

$$
(V_v - V_l)\,dP = (S_v - S_l)\,dT
$$

Define $\Delta V = V_v - V_l$ and $\Delta S = S_v - S_l$:

$$
\Delta V \cdot dP = \Delta S \cdot dT
$$

**Step 4 — Isolating the Slope:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta V}
$$

**Step 5 — Applying the Definition of Latent Heat:**
For a phase change at constant T and P, the latent heat relates to entropy change via:

$$
\Delta S_{\text{vap}} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Clapeyron Form:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T \cdot \Delta V}
$$

### 3.1.2 Deriving the Clausius (Integrated) Form

**Step 1 — Approximations:**
At low to moderate pressures (P < ~10 atm), assume:
- $V_v \gg V_l$, hence $\Delta V \approx V_v$
- Vapor behaves as an ideal gas: $V_v = \frac{RT}{P}$

**Step 2 — Substitution:**

$$
\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}} \cdot P}{R T^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Separating Variables:**

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \cdot \frac{dT}{T^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Integration Between State 1 and State 2:**

$$
\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluating the Integrals:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} \left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Final Clausius-Clapeyron Integrated Form:**

$$
\boxed{\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)}
$$

**Step 7 — Single-State (Linearized) Form:**
Defining a constant of integration $C$, indefinite integration gives:

$$
\ln P = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R T} + C
$$

This is **linear in $\frac{1}{T}$** with slope $-\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}$ — extremely useful for graphical determination of latent heat from experimental vapor pressure data.

## 3.2 Derivation of Van der Waals Constants from Critical Data

**Starting Point:** The van der Waals EoS in molar form:

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}
$$

**Step 1 — Inflection Point Condition at the Critical Point:**
At the critical point, the isotherm has both zero slope and zero curvature:

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right)_{T_c} = 0, \quad \left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right)_{T_c} = 0
$$

**Step 2 — First Derivative:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial P}{\partial V_m}\right) = -\frac{RT}{(V_m - b)^2} + \frac{2a}{V_m^3} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Second Derivative:**

$$
\left(\frac{\partial^2 P}{\partial V_m^2}\right) = \frac{2RT}{(V_m - b)^3} - \frac{6a}{V_m^4} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solving the System:**
From Step 2 at $T_c$, $V_{m,c}$:

$$
\frac{RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^2} = \frac{2a}{V_{m,c}^3}
$$

From Step 3 at $T_c$:

$$
\frac{2RT_c}{(V_{m,c} - b)^3} = \frac{6a}{V_{m,c}^4}
$$

Dividing Step 3 by Step 2:

$$
\frac{2}{V_{m,c} - b} = \frac{3}{V_{m,c}} \Rightarrow 2 V_{m,c} = 3(V_{m,c} - b) \Rightarrow V_{m,c} = 3b
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Back:**

$$
T_c = \frac{8a}{27 R b}, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27 b^2}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — KTU Exam Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Problem [KTU University Exam Style — 14 Marks]:**
>
> *The vapor pressure of water is 1.0 atm at 100°C. The latent heat of vaporization of water is 40,656 J/mol. Estimate the vapor pressure of water at 110°C. Use R = 8.314 J/(mol·K).*

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Data Extraction:**

$$
P_1 = 1.0 \text{ atm}, \quad T_1 = 100°C = 373.15 \text{ K}
$$
$$
T_2 = 110°C = 383.15 \text{ K}, \quad \Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 40{,}656 \text{ J/mol}
$$
$$
R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Integrated Clausius-Clapeyron:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Temperature Term:**

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{383.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\frac{1}{383.15} = 0.0026102 \text{ K}^{-1}, \quad \frac{1}{373.15} = 0.0026799 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = 0.0026102 - 0.0026799 = -0.0000697 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Coefficient:**

$$
-\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R} = -\frac{40{,}656}{8.314} = -4890.16 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{1.0}\right) = (-4890.16) \times (-0.0000697) = 0.3409
$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate to Find P₂:**

$$
P_2 = e^{0.3409} = 1.406 \text{ atm}
$$

**Step 7 — Converting to kPa:**

$$
P_2 = 1.406 \times 101.325 = 142.4 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 8 — Verification & Interpretation:**
The experimental value is 1.414 atm (143.2 kPa). Our estimate is within **0.6%** — excellent agreement. The slight under-prediction arises because $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ decreases slightly with temperature (the Clausius equation assumes it is constant).

> **Final Answer:** $P_2 \approx 1.406 \text{ atm} = 142.4 \text{ kPa}$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem — Equation of State

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Problem [KTU University Exam Style — 7 Marks]:**
>
> *Calculate the pressure exerted by 2 moles of CO₂ gas confined in a 5.0 L vessel at 300 K using (a) the Ideal Gas Law and (b) the van der Waals equation. For CO₂: a = 3.640 L²·atm/mol², b = 0.04267 L/mol.*

**Solution:**

**(a) Ideal Gas Law:**

$$
P = \frac{nRT}{V} = \frac{(2)(0.08206)(300)}{5.0} = \frac{49.236}{5.0} = 9.847 \text{ atm}
$$

**(b) Van der Waals Equation:**

First compute molar volume:

$$
V_m = \frac{V}{n} = \frac{5.0}{2} = 2.5 \text{ L/mol}
$$

Substituting:

$$
P = \frac{RT}{V_m - b} - \frac{a}{V_m^2}
$$

$$
P = \frac{(0.08206)(300)}{2.5 - 0.04267} - \frac{3.640}{(2.5)^2}
$$

$$
P = \frac{24.618}{2.4573} - \frac{3.640}{6.25}
$$

$$
P = 10.018 - 0.5824 = 9.436 \text{ atm}
$$

**Interpretation:** The van der Waals equation predicts a *lower* pressure because the attractive forces (a/V²) reduce the momentum transfer to the walls. The deviation is about 4.2% — modest, since CO₂ at 300 K and 2.5 L/mol is still reasonably ideal.

> **Final Answer:** (a) $P_{\text{ideal}} = 9.847 \text{ atm}$; (b) $P_{\text{vdW}} = 9.436 \text{ atm}$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the Clausius-Clapeyron equation. List the assumptions made in deriving its integrated form.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Differential Form:** $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{T \Delta V}$
>
> **Integrated Form:** $\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$
>
> **Assumptions (in order of application):**
> 1. The molar volume of the liquid is negligible compared to the vapor ($V_l \ll V_v$), so $\Delta V \approx V_v$.
> 2. The vapor phase obeys the ideal gas law: $V_v = RT/P$.
> 3. The latent heat of vaporization $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ is **constant** over the temperature range of interest.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between ideal gas equation and van der Waals equation. State the significance of the constants 'a' and 'b'.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ideal Gas Law | Van der Waals EoS |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| Form | $PV = nRT$ | $\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V-b) = nRT$ |
| Molecular Volume | Assumed negligible | Corrected by constant **'b'** |
| Intermolecular Forces | Assumed absent | Corrected by constant **'a'** |
| Validity | Low P, high T, non-polar gases | Up to moderate pressures |

> **Significance of 'a':** Accounts for the **attractive forces** between molecules (cohesion). Higher 'a' → stronger attraction → lower pressure.
>
> **Significance of 'b':** Accounts for the **finite volume** occupied by the molecules (excluded volume). Higher 'b' → larger molecules → less compressible gas.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14 Marks]

**(a)** The vapor pressure of benzene is 100 mmHg at 26.1°C and 400 mmHg at 60.6°C. Calculate the **molar heat of vaporization** of benzene and its **normal boiling point**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A vessel contains steam at 200°C and 1.5 MPa. Calculate the density of steam using (i) the ideal gas law and (ii) the compressibility factor from the steam tables. (Given: $Z \approx 0.85$ at the given state, M_water = 18 g/mol). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin:**

$$
T_1 = 26.1 + 273.15 = 299.25 \text{ K}, \quad T_2 = 60.6 + 273.15 = 333.75 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Integrated Clausius-Clapeyron:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate LHS:**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{400}{100}\right) = \ln(4) = 1.3863
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Temperature Term:**

$$
\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{333.75} - \frac{1}{299.25} = 0.002996 - 0.003342 = -0.000346 \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for ΔH_vap:**

$$
1.3863 = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{8.314} \times (-0.000346)
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{8.314} = \frac{1.3863}{0.000346} = 4006.6 \text{ K}
$$

$$
\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 4006.6 \times 8.314 = 33{,}311 \text{ J/mol} = 33.31 \text{ kJ/mol}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate Normal Boiling Point (P = 760 mmHg):**

Using $P_1 = 100$ mmHg, $T_1 = 299.25$ K as reference:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{760}{100}\right) = -\frac{33{,}311}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{T_b} - \frac{1}{299.25}\right)
$$

$$
2.0281 = -4006.6 \left(\frac{1}{T_b} - 0.003342\right)
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_b} - 0.003342 = -\frac{2.0281}{4006.6} = -0.0005062
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_b} = 0.003342 - 0.0005062 = 0.002836
$$

$$
T_b = 352.6 \text{ K} = 79.5°C
$$

> **Part (a) Final Answer:** $\Delta H_{\text{vap}} = 33.31 \text{ kJ/mol}$, $T_b = 79.5°C$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data:**

$$
T = 200°C = 473.15 \text{ K}, \quad P = 1.5 \text{ MPa} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$
$$
M = 0.018 \text{ kg/mol}, \quad R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
$$

**Step 2 — Method (i): Ideal Gas Law:**

$$
\rho_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{PM}{RT} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^6)(0.018)}{(8.314)(473.15)}
$$

$$
\rho_{\text{ideal}} = \frac{27{,}000}{3933.5} = 6.864 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Method (ii): With Compressibility Factor:**

$$
\rho_{\text{real}} = \frac{PM}{ZRT} = \frac{6.864}{0.85} = 8.075 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

> **Part (b) Final Answer:** $\rho_{\text{ideal}} = 6.864 \text{ kg/m}^3$; $\rho_{\text{real}} = 8.075 \text{ kg/m}^3$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> Students **commonly lose 2 marks** by forgetting to convert MPa → Pa before substituting into the formula. The standard SI unit for P in $\rho = PM/RT$ is **Pascals (Pa)**, not MPa. Also, students often forget to convert grams to kilograms for molar mass. **Always check unit consistency before plugging in numbers.**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Module 1, 14 Marks, Internal Choice]

**(a)** Derive the **integrated form of the Clausius-Clapeyron equation** starting from the Clapeyron relation. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

**(b)** For ammonia (NH₃), the van der Waals constants are $a = 4.225 \text{ L}^2 \text{·atm/mol}^2$ and $b = 0.0371 \text{ L/mol}$. Calculate: (i) the critical temperature $T_c$, (ii) the critical pressure $P_c$, and (iii) the critical compressibility factor $Z_c$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

> Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must list all four key steps:
>
> 1. **Phase equilibrium condition** $G_l = G_v$ leading to $\Delta V\,dP = \Delta S\,dT$.
> 2. **Substitution** of $\Delta S = \Delta H/T$ to get the master Clapeyron equation.
> 3. **Approximations**: $V_l \approx 0$, ideal gas for vapor → $V_v = RT/P$.
> 4. **Separation of variables and integration** between $P_1, T_1$ and $P_2, T_2$.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall Van der Waals Critical Constant Formulas:**

$$
V_{m,c} = 3b, \quad T_c = \frac{8a}{27 R b}, \quad P_c = \frac{a}{27 b^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Critical Temperature:**

$$
T_c = \frac{8 \times 4.225}{27 \times 0.08206 \times 0.0371}
$$

Compute numerator: $8 \times 4.225 = 33.80$

Compute denominator: $27 \times 0.08206 \times 0.0371 = 0.08224$

$$
T_c = \frac{33.80}{0.08224} = 411.0 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Critical Pressure:**

$$
P_c = \frac{4.225}{27 \times (0.0371)^2} = \frac{4.225}{27 \times 0.001376} = \frac{4.225}{0.03716} = 113.7 \text{ atm}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Critical Compressibility Factor:**

$$
Z_c = \frac{P_c V_{m,c}}{R T_c} = \frac{P_c \cdot 3b}{R T_c}
$$

$$
Z_c = \frac{113.7 \times 3 \times 0.0371}{0.08206 \times 411.0} = \frac{12.66}{33.73} = 0.375
$$

> **Part (b) Final Answer:** $T_c = 411.0 \text{ K}$, $P_c = 113.7 \text{ atm}$, $Z_c = 0.375$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> 1. **Wrong R value:** Students frequently use $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) when their 'a' and 'b' are in **L·atm** units. The **correct R for L·atm** is **0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong answers — **always use the R that matches the units of a, b, and P**.
> 2. **Forgetting to cube b** when computing $V_{m,c} = 3b$ — the 'b' is linear (molar volume correction), NOT squared.
> 3. **The theoretical $Z_c$ for any van der Waals gas is always 0.375** regardless of substance. This is a great KTU "free-mark" check question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to Remember — Last-Minute Revision (Read in 2 Minutes):**
>
> 1. **Master Equation (Clausius-Clapeyron):** $\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}\right)$
>
> 2. **Three Assumptions:** (a) $V_l \approx 0$, (b) Ideal gas for vapor, (c) $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ constant with T.
>
> 3. **Trouton's Rule:** $\Delta S_{\text{vap}} \approx 88 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ at the normal boiling point. Quick way to estimate $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ from $T_b$.
>
> 4. **Antoine Equation** is empirical but more accurate: $\log_{10} P = A - \frac{B}{T+C}$. Use it whenever given Antoine constants.
>
> 5. **Graphical Method:** Plot $\ln P$ vs $1/T$; slope = $-\Delta H_{\text{vap}}/R$. Useful for **experimental data** problems.
>
> 6. **Van der Waals Constants from Critical Data:** $a = \frac{27 R^2 T_c^2}{64 P_c}$, $b = \frac{R T_c}{8 P_c}$.
>
> 7. **Critical Constants from a, b:** $T_c = \frac{8a}{27Rb}$, $P_c = \frac{a}{27b^2}$, $V_{m,c} = 3b$.
>
> 8. **Universal $Z_c$ for van der Waals gas = 0.375** — this is a famous KTU check.
>
> 9. **Always convert units first:** T → K, P → atm or Pa, V → L or m³ — **match R to the unit system**.
>
> 10. **Equation Selection Rule of Thumb:**
>     - $P_R < 0.5$ → Ideal Gas
>     - $0.5 < P_R < 5$ → Van der Waals / Redlich-Kwong
>     - $P_R > 5$ → Virial or multi-constant EoS
>
> 11. **Compressibility Factor:** $Z = \frac{PV_m}{RT}$. Real gases → $Z \neq 1$. Attractive forces → $Z < 1$. Repulsive forces → $Z > 1$.
>
