> 12. **Common Mistake:** Taking $V_l$ as nonzero. Unless the problem says "high pressure" or gives a specific $V_l$, assume $V_l \approx 0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapor Pressure Calculation using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vapor Pressure Calculation using Excel/Matlab/Scilab/Python etc.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Vapor Pressure** is defined as the pressure exerted by the vapor of a substance when it exists in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with its pure condensed phase (liquid or solid) at a given temperature, in a closed system. It is a direct measure of the **volatility** of a substance and is a fundamental property used in the design of distillation columns, evaporators, dryers, condensers, and flash vessels.

Mathematically, the vapor pressure of a pure component is denoted as $P^{sat}$ or $P^*$, and for a multi-component mixture, the partial vapor pressure of component $i$ is given by **Raoult's Law**:

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$$

where $x_i$ is the mole fraction in the liquid phase, and $P_i^{sat}(T)$ is the saturation (vapor) pressure of pure component $i$ at the system temperature $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must be able to estimate vapor pressure using empirical correlations such as the **Antoine Equation** and the **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation**, and verify these calculations using computational tools like **Excel, MATLAB, Scilab, or Python**.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Escaping Molecules" Concept

Imagine a sealed room full of dancing people. Some are pushing against the walls, trying to escape. The harder they push (the higher the temperature), the more force they exert on the walls. **Vapor pressure is exactly this — the "push" exerted by molecules escaping from a liquid into the vapor phase.**

- **Cool water in a sealed bottle (20 °C):** Molecules move slowly → low vapor pressure (~17.5 mmHg).
- **Hot water in a pressure cooker (120 °C):** Molecules move violently → very high vapor pressure (~2 atm).
- **Perfume bottle (volatile organic):** Even at room temperature, you can smell it — high vapor pressure.

## 1.3 Why Use Computational Tools?

Manual calculation of vapor pressure across **hundreds of temperatures and components** is tedious and error-prone. Computational tools (Excel, MATLAB, Scilab, Python) allow:

| Tool | Primary Strength in Vapor Pressure Calculations |
|---|---|
| **Excel** | Tabular lookups, chart plotting, Goal Seek for inverse problems |
| **MATLAB** | Vectorized operations, curve fitting, ODE solving |
| **Scilab** | Open-source alternative to MATLAB, similar syntax |
| **Python (NumPy/SciPy)** | High-level scripting, root finding, vectorized arrays |

> [!NOTE]
> All four tools accept the **same underlying physical equations** (Antoine, Clausius–Clapeyron). The difference lies in syntax and visualization capability — not in physics.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \; \text{kPa} = 760 \; \text{mmHg} = 1 \; \text{atm}$
- **Temperature Units:** Always convert to **Kelvin (K)** when using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, as it is derived from first principles.
- **Antoine Constants:** Reported in **NIST WebBook** for thousands of compounds; valid only within a specified temperature range.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Basis — Why Vapor Pressure Exists

Every molecule in a liquid has kinetic energy. At any temperature above absolute zero, some molecules near the surface possess enough energy to overcome the **intermolecular cohesive forces** and escape into the gas phase. Simultaneously, vapor molecules condense back into the liquid. **Dynamic equilibrium** is reached when the rate of evaporation equals the rate of condensation — this defines the saturation pressure.

## 2.2 Governing Equations (The KTU 2024 Tool-Kit)

### (A) The Clausius–Clapeyron Equation (First-Principles Model)

Derived from the equality of chemical potentials ($\mu^{liquid} = \mu^{vapor}$) and the assumption of constant latent heat of vaporization ($\Delta H_{vap}$):

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

Assuming the vapor behaves as an ideal gas and the liquid volume is negligible:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:** Valid for $P_1$ known at $T_1$; $\Delta H_{vap}$ assumed constant over the temperature interval. Accuracy degrades over wide temperature ranges (>50 K).

### (B) The Antoine Equation (Empirical Three-Constant Model)

$$\boxed{\;\log_{10}(P^{sat}) = A - \frac{B}{C + T}\;}$$

where:
- $P^{sat}$ = vapor pressure (mmHg by default, but verify units in data tables)
- $T$ = temperature in **°C**
- $A, B, C$ = Antoine constants, compound-specific

**Inverse form** (used to find boiling point at a given pressure):

$$T_b = \frac{B}{A - \log_{10}(P)} - C$$

### (C) Raoult's Law (For Mixtures)

$$P_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$

For an **ideal solution**, the partial pressure of each component is directly proportional to its mole fraction in the liquid.

### (D) Modified Antoine (for wider range)

$$\log_{10}(P) = A - \frac{B}{T + C} + D \cdot T + E \cdot T^2$$

> Used in process simulators like ASPEN and HYSYS for higher accuracy.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Form | Typical Use | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | Clausius–Clapeyron | Quick estimation; 2 known points | Narrow T-range |
| 2 | $\log_{10}(P^{sat}) = A - B/(C+T)$ | Antoine | Industry standard | 10–150 °C typically |
| 3 | $T_b = B/(A - \log_{10}P) - C$ | Inverse Antoine | Find normal boiling point | Same as above |
| 4 | $P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}$ | Raoult's Law | Ideal mixtures | All compositions |
| 5 | $P_{total} = \sum x_i P_i^{sat}$ | Dalton + Raoult | Bubble-point pressure | Ideal solution |
| 6 | $\Delta H_{vap} = R \cdot \text{slope}$ (from $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ plot) | Regression | Extract latent heat from data | Wide range |
| 7 | $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | Unit conversion | Mandatory for Clausius–Clapeyron | Always |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU):** When a question gives Antoine constants in **log₁₀ form with T in °C and P in mmHg**, you must NOT change units before applying the formula. Re-check the data source before substituting.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation Column Design:** Determines relative volatility $\alpha = P_A^{sat}/P_B^{sat}$, which dictates the number of theoretical stages.
- **Storage Tank Venting:** Inert gas blanketing pressure is set above the vapor pressure to prevent air ingress and moisture contamination.
- **Refrigeration Cycles:** Low-temperature vapor pressure curves define evaporator and condenser operating points.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Predicts VOC emissions from wastewater and contaminated soils.
- **Process Safety:** Used in **PSV (Pressure Safety Valve)** sizing for blocked-case heat exchanger scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

### Step 1: Start from the Equality of Chemical Potentials

At phase equilibrium between liquid (L) and vapor (V) of a pure component:

$$\mu^L(T,P) = \mu^V(T,P)$$

### Step 2: Differential Change Along the Saturation Curve

If we move infinitesimally along the saturation curve (so equilibrium is maintained), the change in $\mu$ for both phases must be equal:

$$d\mu^L = d\mu^V$$

### Step 3: Substitute the Fundamental Thermodynamic Relation

For a pure component, $d\mu = -S \, dT + V \, dP$ (where $S$ is molar entropy and $V$ is molar volume):

$$-S^L \, dT + V^L \, dP = -S^V \, dT + V^V \, dP$$

### Step 4: Rearrange to Isolate dP/dT

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{S^V - S^L}{V^V - V^L} = \frac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}}$$

### Step 5: Apply the Clapeyron Identity

At equilibrium, the molar entropy change of vaporization equals the latent heat divided by temperature:

$$\Delta S_{vap} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{vap}}$$

### Step 6: Apply Ideal-Gas Assumption to the Vapor Phase

Since $V^L \ll V^V$, we set $\Delta V \approx V^V$. Using ideal gas law $V^V = RT/P$:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap} \cdot P}{R \cdot T^2}$$

### Step 7: Separate Variables and Integrate

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** The derivation assumed $\Delta H_{vap}$ is **constant** with respect to temperature. For higher accuracy, use the **Watson correlation** or **Antoine equation**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Clausius–Clapeyron (KTU Style)

**Problem:** The vapor pressure of water at 100 °C is exactly 101.325 kPa. The enthalpy of vaporization is $\Delta H_{vap} = 40.656 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the vapor pressure of water at 120 °C using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation.

### Step 1: Convert Temperatures to Kelvin

$$T_1 = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \; \text{K}$$

$$T_2 = 120 + 273.15 = 393.15 \; \text{K}$$

### Step 2: Substitute into the Integrated Clausius–Clapeyron Equation

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{101.325}\right) = -\frac{40656}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{393.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}\right)$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the Bracket

$$\frac{1}{393.15} - \frac{1}{373.15} = 0.0025435 - 0.0026799 = -1.3637 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Exponent

$$\text{Exponent} = -\frac{40656}{8.314} \times (-1.3637 \times 10^{-4}) = 6.6684$$

### Step 5: Solve for P₂

$$P_2 = 101.325 \times e^{6.6684} = 101.325 \times 786.4 = 79679 \; \text{kPa}$$

Wait — this is unrealistic. Let's recheck: $e^{6.6684} \approx 786$, so $P_2 \approx 101.325 \times 1.247$ ... 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction:** $\Delta H_{vap}$ should be in **J/mol** consistently. Re-evaluating: $\Delta H/RT_1 = 40656/(8.314 \times 373.15) = 13.10$. Then $P_2/P_1 = \exp[13.10 \times (373.15-393.15)/393.15] = \exp[-0.6666] = 0.513$. Hence $P_2 \approx 52$ kPa, which is wrong direction.
>
> **The correct sign convention is:** $P_2/P_1 = \exp[-\Delta H/R \cdot (1/T_2 - 1/T_1)]$. Since $T_2 > T_1$, the bracket $(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ is **negative**, making the exponent **positive**. The correct exponent is $+0.6666$, giving $P_2/P_1 = 1.948$, so $P_2 \approx 197.4 \; \text{kPa}$. ✓ (Acceptable; experimental value at 120 °C is **198.5 kPa** — error ≈ 0.6%).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Antoine Equation

**Problem:** For **benzene**, Antoine constants are $A = 6.87987$, $B = 1196.760$, $C = 219.161$ (T in °C, P in mmHg). Calculate $P^{sat}$ at $T = 80.1 \; °C$ (the normal boiling point).

### Step 1: Substitute into the Antoine Equation

$$\log_{10}(P^{sat}) = 6.87987 - \frac{1196.760}{219.161 + 80.1}$$

### Step 2: Compute the Denominator

$$219.161 + 80.1 = 299.261$$

### Step 3: Compute the Fraction

$$\frac{1196.760}{299.261} = 3.9989$$

### Step 4: Final Subtraction

$$\log_{10}(P^{sat}) = 6.87987 - 3.9989 = 2.88097$$

### Step 5: Antilog

$$P^{sat} = 10^{2.88097} = 760.4 \; \text{mmHg} \approx 1 \; \text{atm} \; ✓$$

This confirms the normal boiling point of benzene (80.1 °C → 760 mmHg).

---

## 3.4 Computational Implementation — Conceptual Approach

Although the KTU syllabus is **non-CS**, students must understand the algorithmic structure used in Excel/MATLAB/Python.

### 3.4.1 Excel Implementation (Formula-Driven)

Assuming Antoine constants are in cells **B1, B2, B3** and temperature in cell **A1**:

| Cell | Formula (Excel Syntax) | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `B1` | `6.87987` | Constant A |
| `B2` | `1196.760` | Constant B |
| `B3` | `219.161` | Constant C |
| `A2` | `80.1` | Temperature (°C) |
| `B5` | `=10^(B1 - B2/(B3 + A2))` | Computes P (mmHg) |
| `B6` | `=B5/760` | Converts to atm |
| `B7` | `=B5*101.325/760` | Converts to kPa |

**For a range of temperatures** (A2:A20), drag the formula down → column B5 fills automatically → ready to plot a $P^{sat}$ vs $T$ chart.

### 3.4.2 MATLAB / Scilab Implementation (Vectorized Pseudocode)

The user provides a **temperature vector** `T` (in °C) and Antoine constants `A, B, C`. The computational steps are:

1. **Element-wise addition:** `denom = C + T`
2. **Division:** `term = B ./ denom`
3. **Subtraction:** `logP = A - term`
4. **Exponentiation:** `P_sat = 10.^logP`

These four vectorized operations replace a `for` loop entirely. Output is a vector of vapor pressures of the same length as `T`.

### 3.4.3 Python (NumPy Pseudocode)

The computational steps mirror MATLAB exactly: `denom = C + T`, `term = B / denom`, `logP = A - term`, `P_sat = 10**logP`. NumPy's element-wise operators eliminate the need for explicit loops.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** Exam questions will test the **equations, units, and numerical substitution** — not programming syntax. Mastering the four arithmetic operations above is sufficient.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Inverse Antoine (Boiling Point at Reduced Pressure)

**Problem:** A distillation column operates at 0.5 atm. At what temperature will **toluene** boil? Antoine constants (T in °C, P in mmHg): $A = 6.95087$, $B = 1342.31$, $C = 219.187$.

### Step 1: Convert Pressure to mmHg

$$P = 0.5 \times 760 = 380 \; \text{mmHg}$$

### Step 2: Apply Inverse Antoine

$$T_b = \frac{B}{A - \log_{10}(P)} - C$$

### Step 3: Compute $\log_{10}(P)$

$$\log_{10}(380) = 2.5798$$

### Step 4: Denominator

$$A - \log_{10}(P) = 6.95087 - 2.5798 = 4.3711$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$T_b = \frac{1342.31}{4.3711} - 219.187 = 307.10 - 219.187 = 87.91 \; °C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Toluene's normal boiling point is **110.6 °C** at 760 mmHg. At half-atmospheric pressure, it boils ~23 °C lower. This matches experimental data (89.5 °C — error 1.8%, attributable to the constant A,B,C calibration range).

---

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Raoult's Law with Antoine

**Problem:** A liquid mixture contains **50 mol% benzene** and **50 mol% toluene** at $T = 90 \; °C$. Calculate the total vapor pressure and the vapor-phase composition.

**Antoine constants (T in °C, P in mmHg):**

| Component | A | B | C |
|---|---|---|---|
| Benzene | 6.87987 | 1196.760 | 219.161 |
| Toluene | 6.95087 | 1342.31 | 219.187 |

### Step 1: Compute Vapor Pressures at 90 °C

$$P_B^{sat} = 10^{6.87987 - 1196.760/(219.161+90)} = 10^{2.4249} = 265.9 \; \text{mmHg}$$

$$P_T^{sat} = 10^{6.95087 - 1342.31/(219.187+90)} = 10^{2.2190} = 165.6 \; \text{mmHg}$$

### Step 2: Apply Raoult's Law for Total Pressure

$$P_{total} = x_B P_B^{sat} + x_T P_T^{sat} = 0.5(265.9) + 0.5(165.6) = 215.75 \; \text{mmHg}$$

### Step 3: Compute Vapor-Phase Composition (Dalton's Law)

$$y_B = \frac{x_B P_B^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \frac{132.95}{215.75} = 0.616$$

$$y_T = 1 - y_B = 0.384$$

> The vapor phase is **enriched in benzene** (more volatile) compared to the liquid — this is the physical basis of distillation.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Equation

| Scenario | Recommended Equation | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Quick estimate; 2 known data points | Clausius–Clapeyron | Only needs $\Delta H_{vap}$ and one (P,T) pair |
| Industry design; T range 10–150 °C | Antoine (3-parameter) | Excellent fit; constants widely tabulated |
| Wide T range (e.g., cryogenic to critical) | Modified Antoine / Wagner | Captures curvature near critical point |
| Multi-component ideal mixture | Raoult's Law + Antoine | Standard for VLE in distillation design |
| Non-ideal mixture (e.g., azeotropes) | Modified Raoult's + activity coefficient | Use Margules, Van Laar, Wilson, or NRTL models |
| Computational large dataset | All four vectorized in MATLAB/Python | Element-wise operations on arrays |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Computational Workflow for Vapor Pressure Calculation

The following flowchart illustrates the **decision pathway** a chemical engineer follows when computing vapor pressure using software tools.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Compound and Temperature] --> B{Is compound in NIST Database?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Lookup Antoine Constants A, B, C]
    B -- No --> D[Use Group Contribution Methods]
    D --> E[Estimate Constants from Joback or UNIFAC]
    C --> F{Choose Equation Type}
    E --> F
    F -- Narrow Range --> G1[Use Antoine Equation]
    F -- Wide Range --> G2[Use Modified Antoine]
    F -- Two Known Points --> G3[Use Clausius-Clapeyron]
    G1 --> H[Input T and Constants into Tool]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    H --> I{Which Computational Tool?}
    I -- Excel --> J1[Cell Formula: =10^A1 - B1 / C1 + D1]
    I -- MATLAB --> J2[Vectorized: P = 10.^A - B./C + T]
    I -- Scilab --> J3[Same syntax as MATLAB]
    I -- Python --> J4[NumPy: P = 10.0**A - B / C + T]
    J1 --> K[Output: Vapor Pressure Vector]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    K --> L[Plot Psat vs T Curve]
    L --> M[Cross-Check with Experimental Data]
    M --> N{Acceptable Error less than 5 percent}
    N -- Yes --> O[Report Final Result]
    N -- No --> P[Switch to Higher-Order Model]
    P --> F
    O --> Q[End]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Vapor Pressure Module

The following diagram represents the **internal architecture** of a vapor pressure calculation subroutine (as built in a process simulator or spreadsheet template).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input
        IN1[Compound Selection]
        IN2[Temperature Array]
        IN3[Constants Database]
    end
    subgraph Preprocessing
        PP1[Unit Conversion to K and Pa]
        PP2[Validate Constant Range]
    end
    subgraph Core Calculation
        CC1[Antoine Engine]
        CC2[Clausius-Clapeyron Engine]
        CC3[Raoult Mixer Module]
    end
    subgraph Postprocessing
        POST1[Vector Assembly]
        POST2[Error Estimation]
    end
    subgraph Output
        OUT1[Numeric P sat Table]
        OUT2[P sat vs T Plot]
        OUT3[Thermodynamic Report]
    end
    IN1 --> PP1
    IN2 --> PP1
    IN3 --> PP2
    PP1 --> CC1
    PP1 --> CC2
    PP1 --> CC3
    CC1 --> POST1
    CC2 --> POST1
    CC3 --> POST1
    POST1 --> POST2
    POST2 --> OUT1
    POST2 --> OUT2
    POST2 --> OUT3
```

## 4.3 Vapor Pressure Curve — Typical P vs T Behavior

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Vapor Pressure Curve - Water"
    x-axis "Temperature C" [0 20 40 60 80 100 120 140]
    y-axis "Vapor Pressure kPa" 0 500
    line [0.6 2.3 7.4 19.9 47.3 101.3 198.5 361.3]
```

This plot is typically produced in **Excel (Insert → Chart → Scatter with Smooth Lines)** or in **MATLAB** with the command `plot(T, P_sat)`. It illustrates the **exponential growth** of vapor pressure with temperature — a hallmark signature of the Clausius–Clapeyron behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define vapor pressure. State and explain the Clausius–Clapeyron equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Vapor pressure is the pressure exerted by a vapor when it is in thermodynamic equilibrium with its liquid phase at a given temperature in a closed system. It increases with temperature and is a key property indicating a substance's volatility. (1 mark)

The Clausius–Clapeyron equation, derived from the equality of chemical potentials across phases and assuming ideal gas behavior, relates vapor pressure at two temperatures through the latent heat of vaporization: (1 mark)

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This equation assumes constant $\Delta H_{vap}$ and is most accurate over narrow temperature ranges. It is widely used for quick vapor pressure estimates. (1 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**What is the Antoine equation? List its advantages over the Clausius–Clapeyron equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Antoine equation is a three-parameter empirical correlation for vapor pressure as a function of temperature: (1 mark)

$$\log_{10}(P^{sat}) = A - \frac{B}{C + T}$$

where A, B, C are substance-specific constants and T is in °C. (0.5 marks)

**Advantages over Clausius–Clapeyron:** (1.5 marks)
1. Three adjustable parameters give a much better fit to experimental data over a wide range.
2. Does not require explicit knowledge of $\Delta H_{vap}$ (which is temperature-dependent).
3. Constants are tabulated in databases (NIST, DIPPR) for thousands of compounds.
4. Easily invertible to compute boiling point at a given pressure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the Clausius–Clapeyron equation starting from the equality of chemical potentials at phase equilibrium. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The vapor pressure of **acetone** is 400 mmHg at 39.5 °C and 760 mmHg at 56.5 °C. Calculate **(i)** the enthalpy of vaporization of acetone, and **(ii)** its normal boiling point. Use the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

Refer to the **complete derivation** given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The key checkpoints examiners award marks for:

| Step | Concept | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Starting equation: $\mu^L = \mu^V$ | 1 |
| 2 | Differential equality: $d\mu^L = d\mu^V$ | 0.5 |
| 3 | Substitute $d\mu = -S\,dT + V\,dP$ | 1 |
| 4 | Arrive at $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S_{vap}}{\Delta V_{vap}}$ | 1 |
| 5 | Apply $\Delta S_{vap} = \Delta H_{vap}/T$ | 0.5 |
| 6 | Use ideal gas assumption: $V^V = RT/P$ | 1 |
| 7 | Separate variables and integrate to final form | 1.5 |
| 8 | State assumptions clearly (constant $\Delta H_{vap}$, ideal gas, $V^L \approx 0$) | 0.5 |

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 400 \; \text{mmHg}$ at $T_1 = 39.5 + 273.15 = 312.65 \; \text{K}$
- $P_2 = 760 \; \text{mmHg}$ at $T_2 = 56.5 + 273.15 = 329.65 \; \text{K}$

**(i) Enthalpy of vaporization:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{760}{400}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H_{vap}}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{329.65} - \frac{1}{312.65}\right)$$

$$\ln(1.9) = 0.6419$$

$$\frac{1}{329.65} - \frac{1}{312.65} = 0.003033 - 0.003199 = -1.654 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\Delta H_{vap} = -\frac{0.6419 \times 8.314}{-1.654 \times 10^{-4}} = 32{,}257 \; \text{J/mol} \approx 32.26 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

**(ii) Normal boiling point** (where $P = 760 \; \text{mmHg}$, but we need $T$ at exactly 760 mmHg using the first data point and computed $\Delta H_{vap}$):

Wait — since $P_2 = 760$ is given at $T_2 = 56.5 \; °C$, the normal boiling point is by definition **56.5 °C**. (1 mark for recognizing the definition; 1 mark for stating the answer.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students make sign errors in the bracket $\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$. If the bracket is positive (i.e., $T_2 < T_1$ in the data), the resulting $\Delta H_{vap}$ becomes **negative** — physically impossible. Always re-verify the order of $T_1$ and $T_2$ in your data.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With the help of the Antoine equation, calculate the vapor pressure of **n-hexane** at 50 °C, 70 °C, and 90 °C. Antoine constants (T in °C, P in mmHg): A = 6.87776, B = 1171.53, C = 224.00. Tabulate the results and comment on the trend. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid mixture contains 30 mol% **n-hexane** and 70 mol% **n-heptane** at 80 °C. Using Raoult's law and the Antoine equation, compute (i) the total vapor pressure of the mixture, and (ii) the composition of the vapor phase. For n-heptane: A = 6.89702, B = 1264.37, C = 216.64. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Vapor Pressures of n-Hexane (7 marks)

**Calculations:**

$$P^{sat}(50°C) = 10^{6.87776 - 1171.53/(224.00 + 50)} = 10^{6.87776 - 4.2757} = 10^{2.6021} = 400.0 \; \text{mmHg}$$

$$P^{sat}(70°C) = 10^{6.87776 - 1171.53/(294.00)} = 10^{2.3092} = 203.7 \; \text{mmHg} \quad \text{[recheck]}$$

Re-evaluating: $1171.53/294 = 3.985$, so $\log P = 6.87776 - 3.985 = 2.893$, giving $P = 781.3 \; \text{mmHg}$.

$$P^{sat}(90°C) = 10^{6.87776 - 1171.53/(314.00)} = 10^{6.87776 - 3.731} = 10^{3.1468} = 1401 \; \text{mmHg}$$

**Summary Table (2 marks):**

| Temperature (°C) | Vapor Pressure (mmHg) |
|---|---|
| 50 | 400.0 |
| 70 | 781.3 |
| 90 | 1401 |

**Trend comment (2 marks):** Vapor pressure increases **exponentially** with temperature. A 40 °C rise (50 → 90) increases $P^{sat}$ by a factor of ~3.5, confirming the non-linear behavior predicted by the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. Near the normal boiling point of n-hexane (68.7 °C), the pressure crosses 760 mmHg — consistent with our 70 °C entry.

### Part (b) — Binary Mixture (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute vapor pressures at 80 °C.**

For n-hexane: $\log P_H^{sat} = 6.87776 - 1171.53/(224+80) = 6.87776 - 3.850 = 3.028$

$$P_H^{sat} = 10^{3.028} = 1066 \; \text{mmHg}$$

For n-heptane: $\log P_{Hp}^{sat} = 6.89702 - 1264.37/(216.64+80) = 6.89702 - 4.2622 = 2.6348$

$$P_{Hp}^{sat} = 10^{2.6348} = 431.3 \; \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 2: Apply Raoult's Law for Total Pressure (2 marks)**

$$P_{total} = x_H P_H^{sat} + x_{Hp} P_{Hp}^{sat} = 0.30(1066) + 0.70(431.3) = 319.8 + 301.9 = 621.7 \; \text{mmHg}$$

**Step 3: Vapor-Phase Composition (2 marks)**

$$y_H = \frac{x_H P_H^{sat}}{P_{total}} = \frac{319.8}{621.7} = 0.514$$

$$y_{Hp} = 1 - 0.514 = 0.486$$

**Comment (1 mark):** The vapor is enriched in n-hexane (the more volatile component), as expected for an ideal binary mixture undergoing flash vaporization. The relative volatility is $\alpha = 1066/431.3 = 2.47$, indicating easy separation by distillation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Trap:** Students often forget to **convert temperature to the correct unit** specified in the Antoine constants. If A, B, C are tabulated for T in °C, students must NOT use Kelvin in the formula. Verify units **before** starting the calculation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Vapor Pressure Calculations**

- [x] **Definition:** Vapor pressure is the equilibrium pressure of vapor above a liquid at a given temperature.
- [x] **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation:** $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -(\Delta H_{vap}/R)(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ — requires T in **Kelvin**.
- [x] **Antoine Equation:** $\log_{10}(P) = A - B/(C+T)$ — uses T in **°C** (verify from data table).
- [x] **Inverse Antoine:** Used to find boiling point at a specified pressure.
- [x] **Raoult's Law:** $P_i = x_i P_i^{sat}$ — applies to **ideal solutions** only.
- [x] **Unit Awareness:** Always confirm whether Antoine constants expect P in **mmHg, kPa, or bar** before substituting.
- [x] **Computational Tools:** Excel uses cell formulas; MATLAB/Scilab use vectorized operations; Python uses NumPy element-wise operators. All four tools implement the **same underlying physics**.
- [x] **Plotting:** Always plot $\ln P$ vs $1/T$ — the slope gives $-\Delta H_{vap}/R$ (linear Clausius–Clapeyron check).
- [x] **Volatility Comparison:** Higher $P^{sat}$ at a given T means the substance is more volatile.
- [x] **Real-World Applications:** Distillation design (relative volatility), storage tank blanketing, refrigeration cycles, VOC emission modeling, PSV sizing.
- [x] **Common Exam Traps:** (1) Sign error in Clausius–Clapeyron bracket. (2) Mixing °C and K. (3) Forgetting unit conversion in P. (4) Using Antoine constants outside their valid temperature range.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Balance for Unit Operations - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Material Balance for Unit Operations - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Material Balance (Mass Balance)** is the systematic application of the **Law of Conservation of Mass** to a process or a piece of equipment. It quantitatively accounts for all materials entering, leaving, accumulating, generated, and consumed within a defined **system boundary**.

Mathematically, the most general statement is:

$$\text{Input} + \text{Generation} = \text{Output} + \text{Consumption} + \text{Accumulation}$$

In Chemical Engineering, a **Unit Operation** is any basic physical step (such as distillation, filtration, drying, evaporation, crystallization, mixing, etc.) in which mass, energy, or momentum is transferred between phases or within a single phase. A material balance on a unit operation tracks every kilogram of feed, product, recycle, purge, and waste to ensure that **mass is neither created nor destroyed** (in non-nuclear processes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The Law of Conservation of Mass is the foundational pillar of all chemical engineering calculations. Every unit operation — from a simple mixing tank to a multi-stage distillation column — must satisfy a material balance before any energy or equilibrium analysis is attempted.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Picture

Imagine a **leaky water tank** with an inlet pipe, an outlet pipe, and a small hole at the bottom that drips water (a chemical reaction producing a new compound):

| Real-World Part | Material Balance Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Water entering the inlet pipe | **Input** mass flow rate ($F_{in}$) |
| Water leaving the outlet pipe | **Output** mass flow rate ($F_{out}$) |
| Water dripping from the hole | **Generation** (or **Consumption**) term |
| Water level rising or falling | **Accumulation** term |

If the water level remains constant, then **input equals output** — this is the **steady state** condition. If the level changes, mass is being **accumulated** (level rising) or **depleted** (level falling).

## 1.3 Why Material Balances Matter

- **Design**: To size pipes, tanks, pumps, and reactors correctly.
- **Economics**: To estimate raw material requirements and product yields.
- **Safety & Environment**: To quantify waste streams and emissions.
- **Troubleshooting**: To identify unknown flow rates by measuring all other streams.
- **Process Control**: To set baseline operating conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Used:**
> All flows are conventionally expressed in **kg/h** (mass flow rate) or **kmol/h** (molar flow rate) in industrial practice. The **time unit must be consistent** throughout the calculation (e.g., never mix kg/h with kg/min).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Components of a Material Balance

Every material balance, regardless of the complexity of the unit operation, contains the same five conceptual terms:

1. **Input (I)** — Mass (or moles) that enters the system boundary.
2. **Output (O)** — Mass (or moles) that leaves the system boundary.
3. **Generation (G)** — Mass (or moles) of a *specific component* produced *within* the system (e.g., product of a chemical reaction).
4. **Consumption (C)** — Mass (or moles) of a *specific component* consumed *within* the system (e.g., reactant destroyed by reaction).
5. **Accumulation (A)** — Net change in the inventory of mass *inside* the system boundary.

## 2.2 Classification of Material Balances

### A. By System Behavior (Time Dependency)

- **Steady State Material Balance:** The accumulation term is **zero**. All flow rates, compositions, and inventories are constant with time. This is the most common type encountered in KTU problems.
  $$I + G = O + C$$
- **Unsteady State (Transient) Material Balance:** The accumulation term is **non-zero**. Inventories change with time — common during **start-up, shut-down, and batch operations**.
  $$I + G - O - C = \frac{dm}{dt}$$

### B. By Process Nature (Continuity)

- **Continuous Process:** Material enters and leaves continuously. Steady state is normally reached.
- **Batch Process:** Material is charged, processed, and discharged in discrete batches. Material balance is integrated over the batch time.

### C. By Number of Components

- **Total Material Balance (Overall Balance):** Tracks *all* mass, ignoring composition.
- **Component Material Balance:** Tracks *one specific chemical species* through the process.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure for Solving a Material Balance

> [!NOTE]
> **The 10-Step KTU Recommended Procedure (Memorize this!):**
> 1. Draw a clear, fully-labelled **process flow diagram (PFD)**.
> 2. Define the **system boundary** (a single unit, a group of units, or the entire process).
> 3. List all **known** and **unknown** variables.
> 4. Choose a **basis of calculation** (e.g., 100 kg of feed, 1 hour of operation, 1 kmol of key component).
> 5. Verify the **number of independent equations** vs. the number of unknowns — the problem must be **solvable**.
> 6. Convert all quantities to **consistent units**.
> 7. Write the **overall (total) material balance** equation.
> 8. Write the **component material balance** equation(s).
> 9. Solve the equations simultaneously.
> 10. Verify the solution by checking **units, limiting cases, and reasonableness** of the answer.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | When To Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | General Material Balance | $I + G = O + C + A$ | All processes (always valid) |
| 2 | Steady State, No Reaction | $I = O$ | Physical operations (distillation, filtration) |
| 3 | Steady State, With Reaction | $I + G = O + C$ | Reactors (CSTR, PFR, batch reactor) |
| 4 | Unsteady State, No Reaction | $I - O = \frac{dm}{dt}$ | Tank filling / emptying |
| 5 | Unsteady State, With Reaction | $I + G - O - C = \frac{dm}{dt}$ | Semi-batch reactors |
| 6 | Molar Flow Conversion | $\dot{n} = \dot{m} / M$ | Switching between mass and moles |
| 7 | Mass Fraction Closure | $\sum w_i = 1$ | Every stream composition must sum to 1 |
| 8 | Mole Fraction Closure | $\sum x_i = 1$ | Every stream mole fraction must sum to 1 |
| 9 | Average Molecular Weight | $M_{avg} = \sum x_i M_i$ | Converting mixture compositions |
| 10 | Degrees of Freedom | $DOF = \text{Unknowns} - \text{Independent Equations}$ | Must be $\geq 0$ for a solvable problem |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> Material balances are the *first calculation* performed in the design of **every** chemical plant — from petroleum refineries and pharmaceutical plants to wastewater treatment facilities. A process that violates the law of conservation of mass is physically impossible, so the material balance acts as a *consistency check* on all other (energy, momentum) calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Material Balance Equation

We start from the **Law of Conservation of Mass**, which states that matter can neither be created nor destroyed in a non-nuclear process. Consider a control volume (system boundary) at time $t$. Let $m(t)$ be the total mass contained within the boundary.

**Step 1 — Define the rate of accumulation.**

The rate at which mass changes inside the system is mathematically the time derivative of the inventory:

$$\text{Accumulation Rate} = \frac{dm}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Define the rate of mass entering the system.**

If $F_{in}$ is the total mass flow rate entering the system, then mass enters at a rate $F_{in}$:

$$\text{Input Rate} = F_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Define the rate of mass leaving the system.**

If $F_{out}$ is the total mass flow rate leaving the system:

$$\text{Output Rate} = F_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Define the rates of generation and consumption.**

If $r_G$ is the mass rate of generation of component $i$ by chemical reaction, and $r_C$ is the mass rate of consumption:

$$\text{Generation Rate} = r_G \quad ; \quad \text{Consumption Rate} = r_C$$

**Step 5 — Apply the conservation law.**

A mass balance on the control volume states that the net rate of mass addition must equal the rate of mass accumulation:

$$F_{in} + r_G - F_{out} - r_C = \frac{dm}{dt}$$

Rearranging into the standard textbook form:

$$\boxed{F_{in} + r_G = F_{out} + r_C + \frac{dm}{dt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:**
> The accumulation term $\frac{dm}{dt}$ is **positive** when mass is being stored (filling a tank), **negative** when mass is being removed (emptying a tank), and **zero** when the system is at steady state.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Steady-State Form

Setting the accumulation term to zero (steady state condition):

$$\frac{dm}{dt} = 0$$

Substituting into the general equation:

$$F_{in} + r_G = F_{out} + r_C$$

**Special Case 1 — Physical Unit Operations (No Reaction):**
For a distillation column, absorber, filter, dryer, evaporator, or crystallizer, no chemical reaction occurs, so $r_G = r_C = 0$:

$$F_{in} = F_{out}$$

**Special Case 2 — Reactive System at Steady State:**
For a chemical reactor operating continuously, generation and consumption must be retained:

$$F_{in} + r_G = F_{out} + r_C$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Component Material Balance

For a specific chemical species $j$ in a multi-component mixture, the conservation law is applied species-by-species. Let $F_{in}$ and $F_{out}$ be total flow rates, and $w_{in,j}$ and $w_{out,j}$ be the mass fractions of component $j$ in the inlet and outlet streams respectively. Then:

$$F_{in}\,w_{in,j} + r_{G,j} = F_{out}\,w_{out,j} + r_{C,j} + \frac{d(m\,w_{sys,j})}{dt}$$

Where:
- $F_{in}\,w_{in,j}$ = mass flow rate of species $j$ in the inlet.
- $F_{out}\,w_{out,j}$ = mass flow rate of species $j$ in the outlet.
- $w_{sys,j}$ = mass fraction of species $j$ inside the system.
- $r_{G,j}$ and $r_{C,j}$ = generation and consumption rates of species $j$ (from stoichiometry and reaction kinetics).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table

| Process Type | Reaction? | Steady State? | Simplified Balance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Distillation, Filtration, Drying | No | Yes | $I = O$ |
| Distillation, Filtration, Drying | No | No | $I - O = \frac{dm}{dt}$ |
| Continuous Reactor | Yes | Yes | $I + G = O + C$ |
| Continuous Reactor | Yes | No | $I + G - O - C = \frac{dm}{dt}$ |
| Batch Reactor | Yes | No (time-dependent) | $G - C = \frac{dm}{dt}$ (no flow) |
| Tank Filling / Emptying | No | No | $I - O = \frac{dm}{dt}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:**
> Examiners often test whether a student can **correctly identify the type of balance** to apply. A common error is using $I = O$ for a reactive system, which incorrectly ignores the generation and consumption terms. Always read the problem statement carefully for keywords like *"reactor"*, *"fermenter"*, *"combustor"*, or *"decomposer"*, which indicate the presence of a chemical reaction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Material Balance Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **five conceptual streams** entering and leaving a generic control volume (which represents any unit operation — reactor, mixer, separator, etc.):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[/Feed Stream F_in/] -->|w_in_j| node2{{System Boundary: Unit Operation}}
    node2 -->|w_out_j| node3[/Product Stream F_out/]
    node2 -.->|r_G, generation| node4((+ Generated))
    node2 -.->|r_C, consumption| node5((- Consumed))
    node2 -.->|dm/dt, accumulation| node6[(Inventory Change)]
    
    style node1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style node3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style node2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:3px
    style node4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px
    style node5 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px
    style node6 fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,stroke-width:2px
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Solid arrows** (Feed and Product) represent the primary material streams that physically cross the system boundary.
- **Dotted arrows** (Generated, Consumed, Accumulation) represent internal phenomena — they are *not* physical streams crossing the boundary, but mathematical terms in the balance equation.
- The **yellow diamond** in the center represents the **system boundary** — the imaginary envelope that defines what is "inside" the unit and what is "outside."

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Material Balance Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1: Read the Problem Statement] --> stepB[Step 2: Draw the Process Flow Diagram]
    stepB --> stepC[Step 3: Choose the System Boundary]
    stepC --> stepD[Step 4: Define Streams and Components]
    stepD --> stepE[Step 5: Select Basis of Calculation]
    stepE --> stepF{Step 6: Is Reaction Present?}
    stepF -->|No| stepG[Apply I = O or I - O = dm/dt]
    stepF -->|Yes| stepH[Apply I + G = O + C or full form]
    stepG --> stepI[Step 7: Check Degrees of Freedom]
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 8: Solve Equations]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 9: Verify Units and Reasonableness]
    stepK --> stepL[Step 10: Report Final Answer with Units]
    
    style stepA fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style stepB fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style stepC fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style stepD fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style stepE fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style stepF fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656
    style stepG fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style stepH fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
    style stepI fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style stepJ fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    style stepK fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    style stepL fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Architecture Diagram:**
> This flowchart represents the **standard 10-step algorithm** recommended by KTU examiners for solving any material balance problem. The **yellow decision diamond (Step 6)** is the most critical branching point — it determines whether reaction terms are required in the balance. Skipping this step leads to a wrong equation and zero marks in the valuation.

## 4.3 Steady-State vs Unsteady-State Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SS[Steady State Process]
        ss1[F_in] --> ss2[Unit Operation] --> ss3[F_out]
        ss2 -.->|dm/dt = 0| ss4[No Accumulation]
    end
    
    subgraph US[Unsteady State Process]
        us1[F_in] --> us2[Unit Operation] --> us3[F_out]
        us2 -.->|dm/dt ≠ 0| us4[Inventory Changes with Time]
    end
    
    style ss4 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    style us4 fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Law of Conservation of Mass. Write the general material balance equation and explain the significance of each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Law of Conservation of Mass** states that *matter can neither be created nor destroyed in any physical or chemical process (excluding nuclear reactions)*. The total mass of all inputs to a system must equal the total mass of all outputs plus any change in the mass accumulated within the system.

The general material balance equation is:

$$F_{in} + r_G = F_{out} + r_C + \frac{dm}{dt}$$

Significance of each term:
- $F_{in}$ — Mass flow rate entering the system (kg/h).
- $F_{out}$ — Mass flow rate leaving the system (kg/h).
- $r_G$ — Mass rate of generation by chemical reaction (kg/h).
- $r_C$ — Mass rate of consumption by chemical reaction (kg/h).
- $\frac{dm}{dt}$ — Rate of mass accumulation within the system (kg/h).

For a physical unit operation with no reaction and at steady state, this reduces to $F_{in} = F_{out}$. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between steady-state and unsteady-state material balances with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Steady State | Unsteady State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Accumulation term | $\frac{dm}{dt} = 0$ | $\frac{dm}{dt} \neq 0$ |
| Inventory with time | Constant | Changes continuously |
| Simplest equation | $F_{in} = F_{out}$ (no reaction) | $F_{in} - F_{out} = \frac{dm}{dt}$ |
| **Example** | Continuous distillation column in normal operation | A storage tank being filled from a tanker truck |
| Mathematical treatment | Algebraic equations | Differential equations |

A continuous distillation column operating at design conditions is a **steady-state** example. A process tank being filled or emptied, or a reactor during start-up, is an **unsteady-state** example. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Derive the general material balance equation for a single unit operation. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation.

(b) **[7 marks]** A continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) processes a feed stream of pure reactant A at a flow rate of **1000 kg/h** at 25% conversion. The molecular weight of A is 60 g/mol. The reaction is $A \rightarrow B$ (a single-component reaction). Calculate:
  (i) The mass flow rate of A in the product stream.
  (ii) The mass flow rate of product B in the product stream.
  (iii) The total mass flow rate of the product stream.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1:** Consider a system boundary enclosing the unit operation. Let $m(t)$ be the total mass of material contained within the boundary at time $t$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply the Law of Conservation of Mass over a differential time interval $dt$. The net mass entering the boundary must equal the mass accumulated within. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** The mass entering per unit time is the input flow rate $F_{in}$ plus any mass generated internally by chemical reaction, denoted $r_G$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** The mass leaving per unit time is the output flow rate $F_{out}$ plus any mass consumed internally by chemical reaction, denoted $r_C$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** The net imbalance is stored inside the system at a rate $\frac{dm}{dt}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Therefore, the general material balance is:

$$F_{in} + r_G = F_{out} + r_C + \frac{dm}{dt} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7 (Assumptions):** 
(i) The system is a macroscopic control volume. 
(ii) No nuclear reactions occur (mass is conserved). 
(iii) The system boundary is well-defined. 
**(1 mark for stating the assumptions)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Feed flow rate: $F_{in} = 1000 \text{ kg/h}$ of pure A.
- Conversion: $X = 25\% = 0.25$.
- Molecular weight of A: $M_A = 60 \text{ g/mol} = 60 \text{ kg/kmol}$.
- Reaction: $A \rightarrow B$.

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state component balance on A.**

At steady state, the accumulation term is zero. The general balance simplifies to:

$$F_{in}\,w_{in,A} + r_{G,A} = F_{out}\,w_{out,A} + r_{C,A}$$

Since A is consumed and not generated, $r_{G,A} = 0$ and $r_{C,A} = F_{in}\,w_{in,A} \cdot X$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Solve for the mass of A in the product.**

Since the feed is pure A, $w_{in,A} = 1$. The mass of A that reacts is:

$$\text{Mass of A reacted} = 1000 \times 0.25 = 250 \text{ kg/h}$$

Therefore, the mass flow rate of A in the product is:

$$F_{out,A} = 1000 - 250 = 750 \text{ kg/h} \quad \text{...(i) Answer: 750 kg/h}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Use stoichiometry to find the mass of B formed.**

From the reaction $A \rightarrow B$, 1 kmol of A produces 1 kmol of B. By mass balance on the reaction:

$$\text{Mass of B produced} = \text{Mass of A reacted} = 250 \text{ kg/h}$$

**(Note: This is true because the molecular weight of A and B would be required if it were specified. In this problem, since no $M_B$ is given, we assume the mass balance on the basis of mass conservation gives this answer. For rigor, $M_B$ must be specified.)** **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Calculate the total mass of the product stream.**

By overall mass balance at steady state (no net mass change due to reaction):

$$F_{out} = F_{in} = 1000 \text{ kg/h} \quad \text{...(iii) Answer: 1000 kg/h}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Verify component balance.**

Total out = A out + B out = $750 + 250 = 1000 \text{ kg/h} = F_{in}$. ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Final Answers (boxed for clarity):**

- (i) Mass flow rate of A in product = **750 kg/h**
- (ii) Mass flow rate of B in product = **250 kg/h**
- (iii) Total product flow rate = **1000 kg/h**

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative Choice) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

(a) **[7 marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the classification of material balance problems based on (i) the presence or absence of chemical reaction, and (ii) the time-dependence of the process (steady vs unsteady state). Give one example for each combination.

(b) **[7 marks]** An evaporator concentrates a **10000 kg/h** feed containing **15%** NaOH (sodium hydroxide) by mass to a product containing **40%** NaOH by mass. Calculate:
  (i) The mass flow rate of the concentrated product.
  (ii) The mass flow rate of water evaporated.
  (iii) Verify the result using both overall and component balances.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Classification Table and Sketches:** **(5 marks for table + 2 marks for sketches/examples)**

| Combination | Description | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **No Reaction, Steady State** | Physical separation; inputs = outputs | Continuous distillation column |
| **No Reaction, Unsteady State** | Physical process with changing inventory | Filling a storage tank |
| **Reaction Present, Steady State** | Continuous chemical reactor | CSTR in a petroleum refinery |
| **Reaction Present, Unsteady State** | Batch or semi-batch reactor | Batch fermentation in pharma |

The student is expected to draw a simple sketch for each case, showing:
- A box representing the unit.
- Arrows for inlet and outlet flows.
- Annotations indicating whether the reaction term and accumulation term are present.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- Feed flow rate: $F = 10000 \text{ kg/h}$.
- Feed composition: $w_F = 0.15$ (15% NaOH by mass).
- Product composition: $w_P = 0.40$ (40% NaOH by mass).
- Water is the other component.

**Step 1 — Write the overall (total) material balance.**

Let $P$ = mass flow rate of product (kg/h) and $W$ = mass flow rate of water evaporated (kg/h).

$$F = P + W$$
$$10000 = P + W \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Write the component balance on NaOH.**

NaOH is non-volatile and does not leave with the evaporated water, so all NaOH entering with the feed leaves with the product:

$$F \cdot w_F = P \cdot w_P$$
$$10000 \times 0.15 = P \times 0.40$$
$$1500 = 0.40\,P$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Solve for the product flow rate $P$.**

$$P = \frac{1500}{0.40} = 3750 \text{ kg/h} \quad \text{...(i) Answer: 3750 kg/h}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Solve for the water evaporated $W$ from equation (1).**

$$W = 10000 - 3750 = 6250 \text{ kg/h} \quad \text{...(ii) Answer: 6250 kg/h}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Verification using both balances.**

**Overall balance check:** $P + W = 3750 + 6250 = 10000 \text{ kg/h} = F$. ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Component (NaOH) check:** Mass of NaOH in product = $3750 \times 0.40 = 1500 \text{ kg/h}$ = Mass of NaOH in feed ($10000 \times 0.15 = 1500 \text{ kg/h}$). ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Final Answer Box:**

| Stream | Flow Rate (kg/h) | NaOH (kg/h) | Water (kg/h) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Feed (F) | 10000 | 1500 | 8500 |
| Product (P) | **3750** | 1500 | 2250 |
| Evaporated Water (W) | **6250** | 0 | 6250 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check degrees of freedom (DOF):** If the number of unknowns exceeds the number of independent equations, the problem cannot be solved. Always verify DOF before attempting to solve. **(–2 marks if skipped)**
> 2. **Using inconsistent units:** Mixing kg/h with kg/min or mol/h with kmol/h is a guaranteed mark-loser. Always convert all quantities to a single consistent system before writing any equation. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. **Ignoring the composition closure condition:** Every mass or mole fraction in a stream must sum to 1 (i.e., $\sum w_i = 1$). Forgetting this gives a "missing equation" in many problems. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. **Confusing total and component balances:** The total mass balance does not include the reaction term in the same way as the component balance. The overall mass balance is unaffected by chemical reactions (since $r_G - r_C = 0$ for total mass in non-nuclear processes), but the component balance must explicitly include $r_G$ and $r_C$. **(–3 marks if confused)**
> 5. **Not drawing a process flow diagram (PFD):** Examiners award 1–2 marks simply for a clear, well-labelled sketch. Skipping this step forfeits easy marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Material Balance for Unit Operations (Introduction):**
> - The **Law of Conservation of Mass** is the only physical principle you need. Always write it as: **Input + Generation = Output + Consumption + Accumulation**.
> - The **five terms** are: $F_{in}$, $F_{out}$, $r_G$, $r_C$, and $\frac{dm}{dt}$.
> - **Steady state** means $\frac{dm}{dt} = 0$. The vast majority of KTU problems are steady-state.
> - **Physical unit operations** (no reaction) reduce to $F_{in} = F_{out}$.
> - **Reactive systems** require $F_{in} + r_G = F_{out} + r_C$ (steady state).
> - The **overall mass balance** ignores composition and is unaffected by reactions.
> - The **component mass balance** tracks one chemical species at a time and is affected by reactions.
> - **Degrees of freedom (DOF)** must be $\geq 0$ for the problem to be solvable: $DOF = \text{Unknowns} - \text{Independent Equations}$.
> - **Units consistency** is non-negotiable. Pick a system (SI or CGS) and stick to it throughout.
> - **Composition closure:** $\sum w_i = 1$ for every stream; $\sum x_i = 1$ for mole fractions.
> - **Always draw a PFD** before writing any equation — it is worth 1–2 marks in the exam and prevents setup errors.
> - The **10-step algorithm** (draw PFD → define boundary → list unknowns → choose basis → check DOF → convert units → write overall balance → write component balance → solve → verify) is the gold standard.
> - **Batch processes** are inherently unsteady; integrate the balance over the batch time.
> - **Continuous processes** are typically at steady state, so all time derivatives vanish.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Key Component, Material Balance for Unit Operations like Mixing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Key Component, Material Balance for Unit Operations like Mixing ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition of a Key Component > [!IMPORTANT] > **KEY COMPONENT (Tie Component):** A *key component* is a constituent that is present in **both** the feed and product streams of a process unit, and which is used as a *mathematical reference* to relate the unknown flow rates of multiple streams when the number of independent material balance equations is insufficient to solve for all unknowns directly. Formally, in a multi-component, multi-stream system, if a process has **N** unknown flow rates (or compositions) but only **N − 1** independent material balance equations, the deficiency is overcome by writing a balance on **one selected component** — this is the *key component*. The component may be in the feed, in the product, or distributed between them. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2:** > The concept of the key (tie) component is introduced specifically to *bridge* the gap between the number of unknowns and the number of independent equations, and is the standard textbook technique for solving single-unit mixing, blending, and separation problems where the stream compositions are not all specified. ### 1.2 Definition of a Mixing Unit Operation **Mixing** is a fundamental physical unit operation in chemical engineering in which two or more streams (liquid–liquid, gas–gas, solid–solid, or any combination) of differing compositions are combined to yield a single, homogeneous product stream of intermediate or target composition. > [!NOTE] > **FORMAL DEFINITION (KTU textbook standard):** > A *mixer* is a process unit with **multiple inlet streams** and **one outlet stream** in which no chemical reaction occurs, no heat is transferred with the surroundings (unless otherwise stated), and only **physical homogenisation** takes place. Hence, *total mass* and *each component mass* are strictly conserved. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea-Cup" Analogy Imagine you are making a glass of *mixed fruit juice* at home. You pour: - **200 mL** of mango juice (**Stream 1**, 100 % mango) - **300 mL** of orange juice (**Stream 2**, 100 % orange) The combined drink (**Stream 3**) is **500 mL** of juice containing *all* the mango and *all* the orange you poured in. Nothing is lost, nothing is created — that is the **law of conservation of mass** at work. Now suppose the problem is reversed: a customer asks you to prepare **500 mL** of a juice blend that must contain **exactly 20 % mango**. You only know: - Stream 1 (mango juice) and Stream 2 (orange juice) compositions. - The required mango fraction in the product (20 %). - The total product flow (500 mL). You do **not** know *how much* of Stream 1 and Stream 2 to mix. Here, **mango is your key component** — it is the reference substance that lets you mathematically *tie* the two unknown flows (F₁ and F₂) into a single solvable equation. The orange component provides the **second equation** (the total mass balance), and together they form a **2 × 2 linear system** yielding unique values of F₁ and F₂. ### 1.4 Why Do We Need the Key Component Concept? In a generic mixing problem with two inlets (F₁, F₂) and one outlet (F₃) involving C components: | Quantity | Count | |---|---| | Unknowns (flows F₁, F₂ if F₃ is fixed) | 2 | | Independent material balances (total + C − 1 components) | C | | Specified compositions | varies | For a **binary system (C = 2)**: 1 total balance + 1 component balance = 2 equations, which is sufficient. But when a composition is *unspecified* or when the streams have more components, the key component method gives a **clean, single-equation shortcut**. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight:** The *key component* is not a special substance physically — it is a **mathematical anchor** we choose to write one specific component balance. Choosing it wisely (i.e., a component whose mass flow is conserved across all streams) simplifies the algebra dramatically. ### 1.5 Standard Metrics and Constants Used in Mixing Problems | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | **F** | Mass (or molar) flow rate of a stream | **kg/h** or **kmol/h** | | **x** | Mass (or mole) fraction of a component | dimensionless (0–1) | | **ρ** | Density of the stream | **kg/m³** | | **M** | Molar mass | **kg/kmol** | | **w** | Mass fraction (alternative to x) | dimensionless | | **N** | Number of moles | **kmol** | > [!WARNING] > Always keep the **basis (mass vs. mole)** consistent throughout a single problem. Mixing **mass fractions** with **mole fractions** in the same balance is the single most common error committed by KTU students in Module 2. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Degrees-of-Freedom Analysis — The Logic Behind Choosing a Key Component The rigorous procedure taught in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 is: 1. **List all unknowns** (stream flows and unknown compositions). 2. **List all independent equations** (material balances + specifications). 3. **Compute Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** $$ \text{DOF} = \text{Number of Unknowns} - \text{Number of Independent Equations} $$ 4. **If DOF = 0:** the system is exactly determined → solve directly. 5. **If DOF = 1 (or more):** the system is under-determined → introduce a **key component balance** to obtain the extra equation. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this happen in mixing?** Because mixing problems in Module 2 frequently give you the *product* composition and ask you to back-calculate the feed proportions. The product composition is essentially a **specification**, and the key component balance is the equation that *couples* the unknown feed flows to that specification. ### 2.2 The Mixing Material Balance Framework For a **generic mixer** with *m* inlet streams and *one* outlet stream: **Total Mass (or Mole) Balance:** $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_{j,\text{in}} = F_{\text{out}} $$ **Component Balance (for component *i*):** $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_{j,\text{in}} \cdot x_{i,j} = F_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{i,\text{out}} $$ **Key Component Balance (for component *k*):** $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_{j,\text{in}} \cdot x_{k,j} = F_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{k,\text{out}} \quad \text{(used as the bridging equation)} $$ ### 2.3 Logical Procedure — Step-by-Step Heuristic - **Step 1 — Identify the basis.** Choose **100 kg** of feed, **100 kmol** of feed, or any convenient value for the unknown stream. - **Step 2 — Identify the key component.** Pick a component that appears in all streams *and* whose composition is fully known in every stream. If multiple candidates exist, choose the one that gives the simplest algebra. - **Step 3 — Write the key component balance.** This single equation directly yields one unknown flow rate in terms of the others. - **Step 4 — Write the total mass balance** (or the second component balance) to obtain the second equation. - **Step 5 — Solve the resulting 2 × 2 linear system** simultaneously. - **Step 6 — Verify** by substituting back into all balances and checking that the closure (∑ xᵢ = 1) is satisfied in every stream. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why a Key Component Is Used | |---|---| | **Petroleum blending** (refinery gasoline pool) | A target octane number is fixed; the key component (e.g., iso-octane) is balanced to determine the proportion of each blend stock. | | **Pharmaceutical formulation** | Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient (API) must be a fixed mass fraction; the key component is the API. | | **Natural gas sweetening** (mixing of sweet + sour streams) | H₂S is the key component — its allowed concentration in the product dictates the blending ratio. | | **Soft-drink manufacturing** | Sugar concentration must hit a target Brix value; the key component (sugar) is balanced. | | **Alloy production** (metallurgical mixing) | A target alloying-element percentage (e.g., Cr in stainless steel) sets the proportions of scrap and ferro-chrome. | ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Description | Units / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_{1} + F_{2} = F_{3}$ | Total mass balance (two-inlet mixer) | F in kg/h or kmol/h | | 2 | $F_{1} x_{A,1} + F_{2} x_{A,2} = F_{3} x_{A,3}$ | Component A balance (A is the **key component**) | x in mass or mole fraction | | 3 | $\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j} = 1$ | Closure condition for stream j | Required for every stream | | 4 | $F_{1,\text{mass}} = F_{1,\text{mole}} \cdot \bar{M}_{1}$ | Mass–mole interconversion | $\bar{M}$ in kg/kmol | | 5 | $x_{i,\text{mass}} = \dfrac{x_{i,\text{mole}} \cdot M_{i}}{\sum_{j} x_{j,\text{mole}} \cdot M_{j}}$ | Mole-to-mass fraction conversion | dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{DOF} = U - E$ | Degrees of freedom | DOF = 0 ⇒ exactly determined | | 7 | $F_{1} = F_{3} \cdot \dfrac{x_{k,3} - x_{k,2}}{x_{k,1} - x_{k,2}}$ | **Key component shortcut** for two-inlet mixer where k is the key component | derived from (1) and (2) | > [!TIP] > **Formula 7 is the KTU board-exam "one-shot" formula.** If you memorise this single expression, you can solve the overwhelming majority of two-stream mixing problems in under three minutes. Verify it by deriving it yourself in the derivation section that follows. ### 2.6 Engineering Significance in the Process Industry Mixing is ubiquitous — from the **crude oil blending yards** of Kochi Refinery to the **reaction-feed preparation tanks** of every chemical plant in Kerala. The *key component method* is the workhorse calculation that allows process engineers to back-calculate feed proportions from product specifications. Mastery of this topic in Module 2 is a prerequisite for downstream modules on **reactor design (Module 4)**, **distillation (Module 3)**, and **energy balances (Module 5)**, where the same concept reappears as the *Dessler–Wankat key component* in staged separation. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the "Key Component Shortcut" Formula (Equation 7 of the Cheat Sheet) We consider a two-inlet, one-outlet adiabatic mixer with no reaction. Let: - $F_1, F_2$ = inlet mass (or mole) flow rates - $F_3$ = outlet mass (or mole) flow rate - $x_{k,1}, x_{k,2}, x_{k,3}$ = fraction of the key component *k* in streams 1, 2, and 3 respectively. **Step 1 — Total Mass Balance** (conservation of total mass entering must equal mass leaving): $$ F_1 + F_2 = F_3 $$ **Step 2 — Solve for F₂** in terms of F₁ and F₃: $$ F_2 = F_3 - F_1 $$ **Step 3 — Key Component Mass Balance** (component k is conserved): $$ F_1 \cdot x_{k,1} + F_2 \cdot x_{k,2} = F_3 \cdot x_{k,3} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute F₂** from Step 2 into Step 3: $$ F_1 \cdot x_{k,1} + (F_3 - F_1) \cdot x_{k,2} = F_3 \cdot x_{k,3} $$ **Step 5 — Expand the bracket**: $$ F_1 \cdot x_{k,1} + F_3 \cdot x_{k,2} - F_1 \cdot x_{k,2} = F_3 \cdot x_{k,3} $$ **Step 6 — Group the F₁ terms** on the left-hand side: $$ F_1 \cdot (x_{k,1} - x_{k,2}) = F_3 \cdot x_{k,3} - F_3 \cdot x_{k,2} $$ **Step 7 — Factor F₃ on the right-hand side**: $$ F_1 \cdot (x_{k,1} - x_{k,2}) = F_3 \cdot (x_{k,3} - x_{k,2}) $$ **Step 8 — Solve for F₁** by dividing both sides by $(x_{k,1} - x_{k,2})$: $$ \boxed{\,F_1 = F_3 \cdot \frac{x_{k,3} - x_{k,2}}{x_{k,1} - x_{k,2}}\,} $$ By symmetry, a similar expression holds for F₂: $$ F_2 = F_3 \cdot \frac{x_{k,1} - x_{k,3}}{x_{k,1} - x_{k,2}} $$ > [!NOTE] > This pair of equations is **algebraically exact** for any two-inlet, one-outlet mixer with no reaction. The same derivation extends to the **mass–mole interconversion** (Equation 4) and to the **multi-stream** case by repeated application. ### 3.2 Derivation of the Mass-to-Mole Fraction Conversion (Equation 5) Let stream *j* contain *C* components with mole fractions $y_{i,j}$ and molar masses $M_i$. The **average molar mass** of the stream is: $$ \bar{M}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{C} y_{i,j} \cdot M_i $$ The mass of 1 mole of mixture is $\bar{M}_j$ kg. The mass contributed by component *i* per mole of mixture is $y_{i,j} \cdot M_i$. Hence the **mass fraction** is: $$ w_{i,j} = \frac{y_{i,j} \cdot M_i}{\bar{M}_j} = \frac{y_{i,j} \cdot M_i}{\sum_{r=1}^{C} y_{r,j} \cdot M_r} $$ This is Equation 5 of the cheat sheet and is essential when a problem gives compositions in one basis (say mole %) but the answer must be in another (say mass %). ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Demonstration (Sanity Check) **Problem:** Stream 1 = 100 kg/h of pure A. Stream 2 = 200 kg/h of 40 % A (rest B). Mixer produces Stream 3. Verify the key-component shortcut. - Given: $F_1 = 100$ kg/h, $x_{A,1} = 1.00$, $F_2 = 200$ kg/h, $x_{A,2} = 0.40$, $F_3 = 300$ kg/h. - Predicted $x_{A,3}$ using key component shortcut (rearranged): $$ x_{A,3} = \frac{F_1 x_{A,1} + F_2 x_{A,2}}{F_3} = \frac{(100)(1.00) + (200)(0.40)}{300} $$ $$ x_{A,3} = \frac{100 + 80}{300} = \frac{180}{300} = 0.60 $$ - Verification: $x_{B,3} = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$. The closure condition $\sum x_i = 1$ is satisfied. > [!TIP] > This numerical example illustrates that the key component method is *self-consistent* — the algebra produces a unique, physically meaningful answer. KTU board examiners value this *closure check* as a sign of a thorough student. ### 3.4 Generalised Multi-Stream Mixing Equation For an *m*-inlet, one-outlet mixer, the key component balance generalises to: $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_j \cdot x_{k,j} = F_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{k,\text{out}} $$ Combined with the total mass balance: $$ \sum_{j=1}^{m} F_j = F_{\text{out}} $$ we obtain *m* unknowns and *m* equations. For $m \geq 3$, the system is solved as a **linear matrix** of the form $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where: $$ \mathbf{A} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & \cdots & 1 \\ x_{k,1} & x_{k,2} & \cdots & x_{k,m} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} F_1 \\ F_2 \\ \vdots \\ F_m \end{bmatrix} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 Module 2 syllabus caps the mixing problem at **two inlet streams**. The generalised $m$-stream case is shown here for *conceptual completeness* and appears as a Part B 14-mark question only in advanced sessions. ### 3.5 Mastery Table — When to Use Which Equation | Situation | Best Equation to Use | |---|---| | Two feeds, one product, all compositions known, find one flow | Key component shortcut (Equation 7) | | Two feeds, one product, one unknown composition | Total balance + component balance (Equations 1, 2) | | Three or more feeds | Matrix form of Equations 1 and 2 | | Compositions given in mole % but answer required in mass % | Convert using Equation 5 first | | Flow rate given in kg/h but compositions in mol % | Convert to kmol/h using Equation 4 first | | DOF = 1 and a specification exists (e.g., product spec) | Add the specification as a new equation, do NOT use key component shortcut | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Two-Inlet Mixer ```mermaid flowchart LR F1[Stream 1
Inlet F1 kg per h
Composition xA1 xB1] F2[Stream 2
Inlet F2 kg per h
Composition xA2 xB2] MIX((MIXER
Adiabatic
No Reaction)) F3[Stream 3
Outlet F3 kg per h
Composition xA3 xB3] F1 --> MIX F2 --> MIX MIX --> F3 MIX -. Key Component A balance .-> KB[FA1 xA1 + FA2 xA2 = FA3 xA3] MIX -. Total mass balance .-> TB[F1 + F2 = F3] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Key-Component Solution Method ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START: Mixing Problem Statement] --> B[Identify all inlet and outlet streams] B --> C[List all known flow rates and compositions] C --> D[Identify all unknown variables] D --> E[Count unknowns U and independent equations E] E --> F{DOF = U minus E = 0?} F -- YES --> G[Solve total mass balance and component balances directly] F -- NO --> H[Introduce Key Component balance as bridging equation] H --> I[Form the linear system of two equations in two unknowns] I --> J[Solve simultaneously for unknown flow rates] J --> K[Substitute back to compute all compositions] K --> L[Verify closure condition sum xi equals 1 in every stream] L --> M[Report final answer with units] ``` ### 4.3 Information-Flow Diagram for Mass–Mole Conversion in Mixing ```mermaid flowchart LR MOLE[Mole fractions yi and total molar flow Fmole] --> CALC[Compute average molar mass Mbar = sum yi times Mi] CALC --> MASS1[Mass flow Fmass = Fmole times Mbar] MASS1 --> MASS2[Mass fractions wi = yi times Mi divided by Mbar] MASS2 --> MIX[Feed into mixing material balances] MOLE --> MIX MIX --> OUT[Outlet stream flow and composition] ``` ### 4.4 Decision Tree — Choosing the Key Component ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Choose Key Component] --> Q{Does component appear in ALL streams?} Q -- YES --> R{Is its composition known in EVERY stream?} R -- YES --> S[Select this component as Key] R -- NO --> T[Look for another component] Q -- NO --> U[Not a candidate for key component] T --> Q S --> V[Write Key Component balance] V --> W[Combine with total mass balance to solve the system] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each node is an *alphanumeric identifier* with a clean uppercase label — no markdown bold or italics inside the quoted labels, as required by the Mermaid compiler safeguard protocol. Arrows indicate the *direction of information or material flow*. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1 — 3 Marks]** **Q1.** Define a *key component* in the context of material balance calculations on a mixing unit. Under what circumstances is the key component method preferred over writing a direct component balance? **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **key component** is a constituent that is present in *all* the streams of a process unit and is selected as a *mathematical reference* for writing an additional material balance equation when the number of independent equations is insufficient to determine all unknowns. **(1 mark)** The key component method is preferred when: **(2 marks)** - The number of unknowns exceeds the number of independent material balance equations (DOF = 1 or more). - The product stream composition is *specified* as a target, while the feed proportions are unknown. - A direct, single-equation "shortcut" is required to minimise algebra in two-inlet, one-outlet mixing problems. > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Q2 — 3 Marks]** **Q2.** A mixing tank receives 500 kg/h of a 20 % (by mass) NaCl solution and 300 kg/h of a 50 % (by mass) NaCl solution. The mixer is adiabatic and operates at steady state. Calculate the mass fraction of NaCl in the product stream. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - Total mass balance: $F_3 = F_1 + F_2 = 500 + 300 = 800$ kg/h. **(1 mark)** - NaCl component balance (NaCl is the key component): $F_1 x_1 + F_2 x_2 = F_3 x_3$. **(1 mark)** - Solving: $x_3 = \dfrac{(500)(0.20) + (300)(0.50)}{800} = \dfrac{100 + 150}{800} = \dfrac{250}{800} = 0.3125$. **(1 mark)** **Answer:** The product stream contains **31.25 % (by mass) NaCl**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, Q4]** #### **Question A — 14 Marks** A refinery needs to blend **two crude oil streams** to produce a final blend with a **sulphur content of 1.5 % (by mass)**. - **Stream 1:** Available at 1000 kg/h, contains 0.8 % sulphur. - **Stream 2:** Available in unlimited quantity, contains 2.5 % sulphur. **(a)** Identify the **key component** for this mixing problem and justify your choice. **(3 marks)** **(b)** Determine the flow rate of Stream 2 and the total product flow rate. **(7 marks)** **(c)** If the plant instead needs the product at 2000 kg/h with the same 1.5 % sulphur, calculate the required flow rates of Streams 1 and 2. **(4 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Key Component Identification (3 Marks):** The **key component is sulphur**, because: - Sulphur is present in **both inlet streams** and in the **outlet product stream**. **(1 mark)** - The composition of sulphur in **every stream is known** (0.8 %, 2.5 %, and 1.5 % respectively). **(1 mark)** - A single sulphur balance provides the *bridging equation* needed to solve for the unknown flow rate of Stream 2. **(1 mark)** **Part (b) — Flow Rate Calculations (7 Marks):** Given: $F_1 = 1000$ kg/h, $x_{S,1} = 0.008$, $x_{S,2} = 0.025$, $x_{S,3} = 0.015$, $F_2 = ?$, $F_3 = ?$. **Step 1 — Total Mass Balance:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_3 = F_1 + F_2 $$ **Step 2 — Key Component (Sulphur) Balance:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_1 \cdot x_{S,1} + F_2 \cdot x_{S,2} = F_3 \cdot x_{S,3} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute F₃ from Step 1 into Step 2:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_1 \cdot x_{S,1} + F_2 \cdot x_{S,2} = (F_1 + F_2) \cdot x_{S,3} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_2 (x_{S,2} - x_{S,3}) = F_1 (x_{S,3} - x_{S,1}) $$ **Step 5 — Solve for F₂:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{x_{S,3} - x_{S,1}}{x_{S,2} - x_{S,3}} = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.015 - 0.008}{0.025 - 0.015} $$ $$ F_2 = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.007}{0.010} = 700 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 6 — Compute F₃:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_3 = 1000 + 700 = 1700 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 7 — Verification (closure check):** **(1 mark)** $$ \text{Sulphur in product} = 1700 \times 0.015 = 25.5 \text{ kg/h} $$ $$ \text{Sulphur in feeds} = (1000 \times 0.008) + (700 \times 0.025) = 8 + 17.5 = 25.5 \text{ kg/h} \;\;\checkmark $$ **Final Answer:** $F_2 = 700$ kg/h, $F_3 = 1700$ kg/h. **Part (c) — Reverse Calculation (4 Marks):** Now $F_3 = 2000$ kg/h, $x_{S,3} = 0.015$, $x_{S,1} = 0.008$, $x_{S,2} = 0.025$. The product is a *blend* of the same two streams. **Step 1 — Apply the key component shortcut formula:** **(2 marks)** $$ F_1 = F_3 \cdot \frac{x_{S,3} - x_{S,2}}{x_{S,1} - x_{S,2}} = 2000 \cdot \frac{0.015 - 0.025}{0.008 - 0.025} = 2000 \cdot \frac{-0.010}{-0.017} $$ $$ F_1 = 2000 \cdot \frac{10}{17} = \frac{20000}{17} \approx 1176.47 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 2 — Compute F₂:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_2 = F_3 - F_1 = 2000 - 1176.47 = 823.53 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 3 — Sanity check:** **(1 mark)** $$ \text{Sulphur balance: } (1176.47)(0.008) + (823.53)(0.025) = 9.412 + 20.588 = 30.0 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \text{Sulphur in product: } 2000 \times 0.015 = 30.0 \text{ kg} \;\;\checkmark $$ **Final Answer:** $F_1 \approx 1176.47$ kg/h, $F_2 \approx 823.53$ kg/h. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative)** A pharmaceutical company must mix **three aqueous solutions** to obtain **1000 kg/h** of a product containing **30 % solute S**: - **Stream 1:** 10 % S, supply limit 500 kg/h. - **Stream 2:** 40 % S, supply limit 800 kg/h. - **Stream 3:** 60 % S, unlimited. **(a)** Identify the **key component** and set up the system of material balance equations. **(4 marks)** **(b)** Assuming all streams are used at their maximum available rates (Stream 1 at 500 kg/h, Stream 2 at 800 kg/h), determine the required flow rate of Stream 3 and check whether the resulting product composition equals 30 % S.
