If not, suggest a correction. **(6 marks)** **(c)** If only Stream 2 and Stream 3 are used (Stream 1 unavailable), determine the flow rates required to obtain 1000 kg/h of product with 30 % S. **(4 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Key Component and Equation Setup (4 Marks):** **Key component: Solute S** (present in all three streams with known composition). **(1 mark)** Equations: - **Total mass balance:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_1 + F_2 + F_3 = 1000 \text{ kg/h} $$ - **S component balance (key):** **(1 mark)** $$ 0.10 F_1 + 0.40 F_2 + 0.60 F_3 = 0.30 \times 1000 = 300 \text{ kg/h} $$ - **Constraint equations (supply limits):** $F_1 \le 500$, $F_2 \le 800$. **(1 mark)** **Part (b) — Solve with Maxima (6 Marks):** Set $F_1 = 500$ kg/h, $F_2 = 800$ kg/h. **Step 1 — Total mass balance gives F₃:** **(1 mark)** $$ F_3 = 1000 - 500 - 800 = -300 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 2 — Negative F₃ is physically impossible**, so the maxima cannot simultaneously be used. **(1 mark)** **Step 3 — Try the maximum of one stream and the other as a variable:** Let $F_1 = 500$, find $F_2$ and $F_3$. **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Two equations, two unknowns ($F_2, F_3$):** **(1 mark)** $$ F_2 + F_3 = 500 $$ $$ 0.40 F_2 + 0.60 F_3 = 300 - 0.10 \times 500 = 250 $$ **Step 5 — Solve:** From the first, $F_2 = 500 - F_3$. Substituting: **(1 mark)** $$ 0.40(500 - F_3) + 0.60 F_3 = 250 $$ $$ 200 - 0.40 F_3 + 0.60 F_3 = 250 $$ $$ 0.20 F_3 = 50 \implies F_3 = 250 \text{ kg/h} $$ $$ F_2 = 500 - 250 = 250 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Step 6 — Verification:** **(1 mark)** $$ \text{S in product} = (500)(0.10) + (250)(0.40) + (250)(0.60) = 50 + 100 + 150 = 300 \text{ kg/h} = 30\% \times 1000 \;\;\checkmark $$ **Final Answer:** $F_1 = 500$ kg/h, $F_2 = 250$ kg/h, $F_3 = 250$ kg/h. **Part (c) — Stream 1 Unavailable (4 Marks):** $F_1 = 0$, $F_3 = 1000 - F_2$, S-balance: $$ 0.40 F_2 + 0.60 (1000 - F_2) = 300 $$ $$ 0.40 F_2 + 600 - 0.60 F_2 = 300 $$ $$ -0.20 F_2 = -300 $$ $$ F_2 = 1500 \text{ kg/h} $$ $$ F_3 = 1000 - 1500 = -500 \text{ kg/h} $$ **Negative F₃** indicates that with Stream 1 unavailable, the desired 30 % S product **cannot be obtained** using only Streams 2 and 3, because Stream 2 (40 %) is *richer* than the target — the system is infeasible. **(4 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > 1. **Forgetting the closure check** ($\sum x_i = 1$). Many students compute one composition correctly but fail to verify the others. This loses **1–2 marks** per sub-part. > 2. **Mixing mass and mole bases** in the same problem. If the question gives compositions in *mass %* and asks for a *molar flow rate*, you must convert using the average molar mass first. A common error loses **2–3 marks**. > 3. **Sign errors in the shortcut formula.** The denominator $x_{k,1} - x_{k,2}$ is *signed*. Re-arranging the formula incorrectly gives a negative flow rate, which is the classic giveaway of an algebraic slip. > 4. **Choosing the wrong key component.** A common student mistake is to balance the *major* component, which is fine *mathematically* but makes the algebra messier. KTU examiners prefer the key component method because it is *elegant*. Choose the key component that appears in all streams with *fully specified* compositions. > 5. **Not stating the assumption of steady state and no reaction explicitly.** The KTU marking scheme allocates **0.5–1 mark** for explicitly listing the process assumptions at the start of every solution. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Key Component & Mixing Material Balances** - **Definition:** A *key (tie) component* is the component balanced to provide a bridging equation when DOF > 0 in a multi-stream unit. - **Mixer Assumption Set:** Steady state, no reaction, no heat transfer with surroundings (unless stated), perfect mixing. - **Two-Inlet Shortcut Formula (memorise verbatim):** $F_1 = F_3 \cdot \dfrac{x_{k,3} - x_{k,2}}{x_{k,1} - x_{k,2}}$ — applicable when $F_3$ is known. - **Total Mass Balance:** $\sum F_{\text{in}} = F_{\text{out}}$. - **Component (Key) Balance:** $\sum F_{\text{in}} \cdot x_{k,\text{in}} = F_{\text{out}} \cdot x_{k,\text{out}}$. - **Closure Condition:** $\sum_{i=1}^{C} x_{i,j} = 1$ for every stream *j* — always check. - **Mass–Mole Interconversion:** $F_{\text{mass}} = F_{\text{mole}} \cdot \bar{M}$ and $w_i = \dfrac{y_i M_i}{\sum y_r M_r}$. - **DOF Analysis:** DOF = 0 ⇒ directly solvable; DOF = 1 ⇒ one key component balance required; DOF > 1 ⇒ more specifications needed. - **Units Discipline:** Stick to **kg/h** or **kmol/h** consistently; never mix kg/h with kmol/h in the same balance. - **Verification:** Always substitute the solved values back into *every* balance; the LHS must equal the RHS to within rounding. - **Negative flow rate ⇒ infeasible problem:** Either the specification is unattainable, or you have made an algebraic sign error. KTU examiners mark this as a conceptual gap. - **Multi-stream case (m > 2):** Reduce to a 2 × 2 system by fixing all but two of the inlet flows, or use matrix methods. - **Key Component Selection Rule:** Appears in all streams + composition known in every stream + gives simplest algebra. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Board Strategy:** In a 14-mark problem, always allocate ~2 marks for stating assumptions, ~4 marks for setting up the equations, ~6 marks for solving them, and ~2 marks for verification and final answer with units. This 2-4-6-2 mark split mirrors the KTU board examiner's expected answer structure and maximises your score. ---

topics.title: Distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# DISTILLATION — FOUNDATIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Distillation** is a *unit operation* in which a liquid mixture of two or more miscible components is physically separated into fractions of differing volatility by the selective application and removal of heat, exploiting differences in **vapor–liquid equilibrium (VLE)** compositions at boiling conditions.

In **Process Calculations (PCCHT205)**, the term *"Distillation"* refers to the application of the **Law of Conservation of Mass** to industrial separation columns. The student is expected to write and solve overall, component, and stage-wise **material balance equations** for:

- **Differential (Rayleigh) Distillation** — an open, batch-type, single-stage operation.
- **Flash (Equilibrium) Distillation** — a single-stage continuous operation.
- **Continuous Fractionating Column** — multi-stage countercurrent rectification with reflux.

The standard operating metrics used throughout KTU problems are:

- **Feed composition ($z_F$)** — mole fraction of the more volatile component in the feed.
- **Distillate composition ($x_D$)** — mole fraction in the overhead product.
- **Residue/Bottoms composition ($x_W$)** — mole fraction in the bottom product.
- **Reflux ratio ($R$)** — moles of liquid returned per mole of distillate withdrawn.
- **Relative volatility ($\alpha$)** — ratio of vapor pressures (or K-values) of the two key components.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Boiling Tea Kettle:**
> Imagine boiling salty water in a kettle. Pure water vapor rises and escapes (this is the **distillate**), while the salt stays behind in the kettle (this is the **residue/bottoms**). The kettle itself acts as a **single equilibrium stage** — vapor leaving is richer in the *more volatile* component (water) than the liquid remaining.
> Now imagine stacking a hundred tiny kettles on top of each other. Vapor from the bottom rises, partially condenses on the kettle above, and reboils. Each "kettle" re-equilibrates, and the top one finally produces nearly pure water. This stacked arrangement is exactly how an industrial **fractionating column** works, and **Material Balance** is how engineers count how many kettles (theoretical stages) they need.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> All KTU problems in this module are worked in **mole fractions (dimensionless)** unless explicitly stated otherwise. The two constant anchors you will see repeatedly are:
> - **Atmospheric pressure $P$ = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg**
> - **Universal Gas Constant $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K) = 0.08206 L·atm/(mol·K)**
> Relative volatility is treated as a **constant** (ideal binary system) for 90 % of KTU Module-2 problems.

---

## 1.4 Why Distillation Belongs in *Process Calculations*

Process Calculations does **not** ask you to *design* a column from scratch — it asks you to **count material**, i.e., to write and solve mass balances that relate **feed**, **distillate**, **bottoms**, **reflux**, and **vapor flow** rates. The distillation column is simply a *black box* (or a stack of black boxes) into which mass enters, is redistributed among streams, and leaves. The governing principle throughout is:

$$\text{Input} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation}$$

For *steady-state* operation, **Accumulation = 0**, giving us the cornerstone equation used in every KTU distillation problem:

$$F = D + W$$

where $F$, $D$, and $W$ are the molar flow rates of the **feed**, **distillate**, and **bottoms (residue)** respectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Core Models You Must Master

KTU 2024 Module 2 (Distillation) is built on **three process models**. Every question you face is a variation of one of these.

### Model A — Differential (Rayleigh) Distillation

A **batch** liquid charge is boiled, and the vapor formed is *continuously removed* and condensed. No reflux is returned. The liquid remaining in the still becomes progressively richer in the less volatile component.

**Operational features (board-exam wording):**

- Single equilibrium stage.
- Batch (discontinuous) operation.
- Vapor removed is in **equilibrium** with the residual liquid at every instant.
- No trays, no reflux.

### Model B — Flash (Equilibrium) Distillation

A pre-heated liquid feed is suddenly depressurized through a throttling valve into a flash chamber. A fraction vaporizes; the vapor and remaining liquid leave in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with each other.

**Operational features:**

- Single stage, **continuous** operation.
- Defined by the **flash equation** (see Section 2.3 below).
- Used in KTU problems to find fraction vaporized $f$ given equilibrium data.

### Model C — Continuous Fractionating Column

A vertical stack of trays (or packing). Liquid flows **downward**, vapor flows **upward**, countercurrently. Each tray is an equilibrium stage. A **total condenser** liquefies the overhead vapor, a portion is returned as **reflux**, the rest is withdrawn as **distillate**. A **reboiler** at the base provides the upward vapor stream.

**Operational features (most heavily tested in KTU):**

- Multi-stage, continuous, steady-state.
- Two sections: **Rectifying (above feed)** and **Stripping (below feed)**.
- Characterized by: **$R$, $R_m$, $R_{opt}$, $N$, $N_m$, $q$**.

---

## 2.2 Vapor–Liquid Equilibrium Foundations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Raoult's Law** (ideal solution): the partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor equals its mole fraction in the liquid times its pure-component vapor pressure at that temperature.
>
> $$p_i = x_i \, P_i^{sat}(T)$$
>
> **Dalton's Law** (ideal gas): the partial pressure of component $i$ in the vapor equals its mole fraction in the vapor times the total pressure.
>
> $$p_i = y_i \, P$$
>
> **Equilibrium relationship** (combining both, for an ideal binary system at constant $P$):
>
> $$y_i = \dfrac{\alpha_{ij} \, x_i}{1 + (\alpha_{ij} - 1)\, x_i}$$
>
> where $\alpha_{ij} = P_i^{sat}/P_j^{sat}$ is the **relative volatility** (assumed constant over the column range in KTU problems).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use this table as your one-page cheat sheet for KTU 2024 ESE and internal exams. All quantities are **molar flow rates (kmol/h)** and **mole fractions** unless otherwise noted.

| Concept | Governing Equation | KTU Symbols / Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| Overall Mass Balance | $F = D + W$ | Steady-state, column as a whole |
| Component Balance (overall) | $F\, z_F = D\, x_D + W\, x_W$ | Component $i$ (usually the more volatile, MVC) |
| Operating Line (Rectifying) | $y = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\, x + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$ | Holds on all plates **above** the feed |
| Operating Line (Stripping) | $y = \dfrac{L'}{V'}\, x - \dfrac{W\, x_W}{V'}$ | Holds on all plates **below** the feed |
| Feed-Line ($q$-line) | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1}\, x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | Slope = $q/(q-1)$, passes through $(z_F, z_F)$ |
| Reflux Ratio Definition | $R = L/D$ | Moles liquid returned / moles distillate |
| Total Reflux Condition | $R = \infty$ | Operating lines coincide with diagonal $y=x$ |
| Minimum Reflux (Underwood / graphical) | $R_m$ from intersection of $q$-line and equilibrium curve | At infinite plates |
| Flash Equation | $y = \dfrac{\alpha \, x}{1+(\alpha-1)x}$ AND $y = \dfrac{z - (1-f)x}{f}$ | Solve simultaneously for $f$ and $x$ |
| Rayleigh (Differential) Eq. | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{F}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_W}{z_F}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-x_F}{1-x_W}\right)\!\cdot\!(\text{correction})$ | See full derivation in Section 3.2 |
| Constant-$\alpha$ Rayleigh | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{F}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_W(1-z_F)}{z_F(1-x_W)}\right)$ | Simplified form (mole-fraction form) |
| Recovery Fraction | $\text{Rec}_D = \dfrac{D\,x_D}{F\,z_F}$ | Mass of MVC in distillate / mass of MVC in feed |
| Minimum Stages ($N_m$) | Fenske: $\displaystyle N_m = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D(1-x_W)}{x_W(1-x_D)}\right]}{\ln \alpha}$ | Total reflux, constant $\alpha$ |
| Gilliland Correlation | $\displaystyle Y = 0.75\,(1-X)^{0.5668}$ where $X = \dfrac{R - R_m}{R+1},\ Y = \dfrac{N - N_m}{N+1}$ | Empirical, requires $N_m$, $R_m$ |
| Optimum Reflux | $R_{opt} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5)\, R_m$ | Heuristic used in KTU design problems |
| Feed Thermal Condition $q$ | $q = \dfrac{\text{moles of saturated liquid formed per mole of feed}}{\text{1 mole feed}}$ | $q=1$ cold liquid, $q=0$ superheated vapor |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe symbol hazard in markdown:** In every equation above, the relative-volatility ratio and absolute values are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or with explicit parentheses. Never use the bare vertical bar `|` inside a markdown table cell — it breaks the table parser. In free text you may write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.

---

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Operating Lines

The **rectifying operating line** is *not* a thermodynamic equation — it is a **material balance** over the top of the column. Imagine drawing an envelope around the top of the column, cutting just below the topmost plate, so that the envelope encloses the **condenser**, the **distillate draw**, and the **reflux return** but excludes the feed and bottoms.

Material balance over this envelope (steady state):

$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D \qquad \text{(total moles)}$$

$$V_{n+1}\, y_{n+1} = L_n\, x_n + D\, x_D \qquad \text{(MVC moles)}$$

Dividing the second by the first, and using $R = L_n/D$:

$$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\, x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$$

This is the equation KTU expects you to write first, before drawing the McCabe–Thiele plot.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Operating lines are the *engineering levers* of a column. By changing $R$ (reflux), the operator moves the rectifying line's slope, which moves the staircase of stages on the McCabe–Thiele plot. **More reflux = fewer stages, but higher reboiler and condenser duty.** This trade-off is the central economic problem of distillation design and is the conceptual basis of the **Gilliland correlation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written out **algebraically with every transition visible**. No steps are skipped, no "it can be shown" shortcuts are used.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flash Distillation Equation

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A feed of molar flow $F$ (kmol/h), composition $z_F$ (mole fraction of MVC) is partially vaporized. Let:

- $f$ = mole fraction of feed that vaporizes (so vapor flow = $fF$).
- $x$ = mole fraction MVC in the **liquid** leaving the flash chamber.
- $y$ = mole fraction MVC in the **vapor** leaving the flash chamber.

By definition, $f$, $x$, and $y$ are all in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with one another.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Total Mass Balance

In words: *feed in = liquid out + vapor out*.

$$F = (1-f)F + fF \quad \text{(trivially true; this confirms $f$ is well defined)}$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Component (MVC) Mass Balance

Moles MVC in feed = moles MVC in liquid + moles MVC in vapor.

$$F\, z_F = (1-f)F\, x + fF\, y$$

Cancel $F$ (steady-state, non-zero):

$$z_F = (1-f)\, x + f\, y$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Solve for $y$ in Terms of $f$ and $x$

$$f\, y = z_F - (1-f)\, x$$

$$y = \dfrac{z_F - (1-f)\, x}{f}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Apply Equilibrium (Constant $\alpha$)

For an ideal binary with constant relative volatility $\alpha$:

$$y = \dfrac{\alpha\, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Combine the Two Expressions for $y$

$$\dfrac{z_F - (1-f)\, x}{f} = \dfrac{\alpha\, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}$$

This is the **flash equation** KTU expects you to recognize. To solve:

- If $f$ is unknown (the standard case), expand and solve the resulting **quadratic in $x$** for the in-situ liquid composition.
- If $x$ is unknown, treat as quadratic in $x$.
- Numerically, the most common KTU question is: *given $F$, $z_F$, $\alpha$, and the fraction vaporized $f$, find $x$ and $y$*.

### 3.1.7 Verification (Numerical Check)

Take $z_F = 0.5$, $\alpha = 2.5$, $f = 0.4$. Substitute:

$$y = \dfrac{0.5 - 0.6\, x}{0.4}$$

and

$$y = \dfrac{2.5\, x}{1 + 1.5\, x}$$

Equate and solve: this gives $x \approx 0.396$, $y \approx 0.590$. Both lie on the equilibrium curve and on the material-balance line, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Rayleigh (Differential Distillation) Equation

### 3.2.1 Setup

At any instant, let the still pot contain $L$ kmol of liquid with MVC mole fraction $x$. An infinitesimal amount $dL$ is vaporized (and removed), of composition $y$ in equilibrium with $x$.

**Differential element: $dL$ kmol removed in time $dt$.**

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Total Mass Balance on the Pot

Liquid leaving the pot reduces $L$:

$$dL_{\text{leaving}} = -dL_{\text{pot}}$$

so the pot loses $dL$ kmol of liquid.

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Component Mass Balance on the Pot

The change in MVC content of the liquid = MVC removed in the vapor.

$$\text{MVC remaining after} - \text{MVC remaining before} = -\text{MVC removed}$$

Algebraically:

$$(L + dL)(x + dx) - L\,x = -y\, dL$$

Expanding and dropping the second-order infinitesimal $dL\,dx$:

$$L\,dx + x\,dL = -y\, dL$$

Rearrange:

$$L\,dx = -(x + y)\, dL$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Separate Variables

$$\dfrac{dL}{L} = -\dfrac{dx}{y - x}$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Integrate Between Initial and Final States

Initial state: $L_0 = F$, $x_0 = z_F$.
Final state: $L_f = W$, $x_f = x_W$.

$$\int_{F}^{W} \dfrac{dL}{L} = -\int_{z_F}^{x_W} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{F}\right) = \int_{x_W}^{z_F} \dfrac{dx}{y - x}$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Substitute Raoult's Law Equilibrium

For an ideal binary, $y = \dfrac{\alpha\, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}$. Then:

$$y - x = \dfrac{\alpha\, x}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x} - x = \dfrac{\alpha\, x - x - (\alpha-1)\, x^2}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}$$

Simplify numerator:

$$\alpha\, x - x - (\alpha - 1)\, x^2 = x(\alpha - 1)(1 - x)$$

So:

$$y - x = \dfrac{x(\alpha - 1)(1 - x)}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}$$

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — Form the Integrand

$$\dfrac{dx}{y - x} = \dfrac{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}{x(\alpha - 1)(1 - x)}\, dx$$

Use **partial fractions**:

$$\dfrac{1 + (\alpha - 1)\, x}{x(\alpha - 1)(1 - x)} = \dfrac{A}{x} + \dfrac{B}{1 - x}$$

Solving: $A = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}$, $B = \dfrac{\alpha}{\alpha - 1}$. Hence:

$$\dfrac{dx}{y - x} = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left(\dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - x}\right) dx$$

### 3.2.8 Step 7 — Integrate

$$\int \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left(\dfrac{1}{x} + \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - x}\right) dx = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln x - \alpha \ln(1-x)\right]$$

Evaluate from $x_W$ to $z_F$:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{F}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{z_F}{x_W}\right) - \alpha \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1-z_F}{1-x_W}\right)\right]$$

### 3.2.9 Step 8 — Standard KTU Final Form (Constant $\alpha$)

Rearrange to the form most often quoted in textbooks and KTU answer keys:

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{F}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right)\right]\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization aid:** The numerator has the form $\ln(x_F/x_W) + \alpha \ln[(1-x_W)/(1-x_F)]$. The denominator is $(\alpha - 1)$. Always write $x_F$ (the feed's initial MVC mole fraction) and $x_W$ (the residue's final MVC mole fraction). $W/F$ is the ratio of *final* to *initial* liquid hold-up.

### 3.2.10 Sanity Check

If $\alpha = 1$ (no separation possible), the right-hand side becomes $0/0$ — apply L'Hôpital to recover the trivial result $W = F$, as expected. ✓

If $x_W \to 0$ (perfect stripping), $W/F \to 0$ — physically correct. ✓

If $\alpha$ is large, even a small $W/F$ produces a very low $x_W$ — physically correct (volatile component strips out efficiently). ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Rectifying Operating Line

The rectifying section is bounded above by the **total condenser** and below by the **feed plate**. Cut a horizontal envelope between plate $n$ and plate $n+1$.

### 3.3.1 Total Molar Balance

Vapor rising from plate $n+1$ enters the envelope; liquid descending from plate $n$ leaves the envelope downward; the envelope also has a side draw of distillate $D$ leaving to the condenser.

$$V_{n+1} = L_n + D$$

### 3.3.2 Component (MVC) Balance

$$V_{n+1}\, y_{n+1} = L_n\, x_n + D\, x_D$$

### 3.3.3 Substitute Reflux Definition

By definition, $R = L_n / D$ (constant molar overflow assumed). So $L_n = R\, D$ and $V_{n+1} = (R+1)\, D$.

$$\dfrac{V_{n+1}}{V_{n+1}} \, y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R\, D}{(R+1)\, D}\, x_n + \dfrac{D}{(R+1)\, D}\, x_D$$

$$y_{n+1} = \dfrac{R}{R+1}\, x_n + \dfrac{x_D}{R+1}$$

Dropping the subscript convention $n+1 \to n+1$, $n \to n$ — this is the **rectifying operating line**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Equation Do I Use?"

| If the KTU question gives you… | …then use this equation | Unknown you solve for |
|---|---|---|
| $F$, $z_F$, $D$, $W$ (or any three) | Overall + component balance | The remaining flow or composition |
| A single-stage vaporizer, $z_F$, $\alpha$ | Flash equation | $f$, $x$, or $y$ |
| A batch still, $F$, $z_F$, $x_W$, $\alpha$ | Rayleigh equation | $W$ (or the residue fraction) |
| $x_D$, $x_W$, $\alpha$ (total reflux) | Fenske equation | $N_m$ |
| $R$, $R_m$, $N_m$ | Gilliland correlation | $N$ (number of equilibrium stages) |
| Feed condition described (e.g., "saturated liquid") | $q = 1$; $q$-line slope = 1 | Feed-plate location on McCabe–Thiele plot |
| Feed condition: subcooled liquid | $q > 1$ | q-line slope |
| Feed condition: saturated vapor | $q = 0$ | q-line is horizontal |
| Feed condition: two-phase mixture | $0 < q < 1$ | q-line slope between 0 and 1 |
