# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme** assessment pattern: Part A (3 marks) tests recall and short derivation; Part B (14 marks) tests full problem-solving with internal choice. Mark splits and the Valuation Warning callouts are aligned with the **Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels** (Apply / Analyze / Evaluate).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series 1]
**Define distillation. Differentiate clearly between differential distillation and flash distillation, citing the model equation used for each.**

**Model Answer (board-exam phrasing):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** Distillation is the separation of a liquid mixture into fractions of differing volatility by selective vaporization and condensation, exploiting vapor–liquid equilibrium.
>
> **Differentiation Table:**
>
> | Aspect | Differential (Rayleigh) | Flash (Equilibrium) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Operation | Batch | Continuous |
> | Stages | One, but instantaneous | One, but steady |
> | Equation | $\ln(W/F) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\ln(x_F/x_W) + \alpha\ln[(1-x_W)/(1-x_F)]\right]$ | $z_F = (1-f)x + fy$ combined with $y = \alpha x/[1+(\alpha-1)x]$ |
> | Output | Pot residue $W$ | Vapor $V=fF$ and Liquid $L=(1-f)F$ leaving together |

**Marks split (3 M):** 1 M for definition, 1 M for Rayleigh equation statement, 1 M for flash equation statement.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series 2]
**What is relative volatility? Why is the assumption of *constant* relative volatility useful in KTU-level distillation problems?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Relative volatility $\alpha_{ij}$** is the ratio of the K-values (or pure-component vapor pressures) of two components $i$ and $j$:
>
> $$\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{K_i}{K_j} = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}}{P_j^{sat}}$$
>
> In an **ideal binary system** at modest pressure, $\alpha$ is approximately **constant** across the composition range. This lets us use the closed-form equilibrium relation $y = \alpha x/[1+(\alpha-1)x]$ and closed-form solutions like the **Fenske** and **Rayleigh** equations, which would otherwise require numerical integration over a tabulated equilibrium curve.

**Marks split (3 M):** 1 M for the definition + formula, 1 M for "useful because it gives a closed-form VLE", 1 M for naming the Fenske / Rayleigh consequence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfalls:**
> - **Unit consistency (–1 M):** KTU values strictly in *mole fractions* and *kmol/h*. Mixing mass fractions with molar flow rates = 1-mark penalty.
> - **Sign of $\alpha$ in Rayleigh (–1 M):** Always subtract 1 in the denominator. A common slip is to write $1/(\alpha + 1)$.
> - **Constant molar overflow assumption (–0.5 M):** State it explicitly before writing operating lines. KTU examiners explicitly look for this phrase.
> - **Rounding (–0.5 M):** Carry 4 significant figures until the final answer.
> - **Drawing the envelope first (–0.5 M):** Always state what the control volume encloses *before* writing the balance.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series 1]

> A feed of **100 kmol/h** containing **benzene (MVC) — toluene (LVC)** with mole fraction $z_F = 0.50$ is subjected to **differential (Rayleigh) distillation**. The residue composition is required to be $x_W = 0.30$. The relative volatility of benzene to toluene may be taken as **$\alpha = 2.47$** (constant).
>
> **(a)** Calculate the amount of distillate collected and the fraction of benzene recovered in the distillate. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** If the same feed is instead processed in a **single-stage flash** with fraction vaporized $f = 0.40$, find the equilibrium liquid and vapor compositions $x$ and $y$ leaving the flash chamber. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Identify unknowns and apply Rayleigh equation.** (1 M)
The Rayleigh equation with constant $\alpha$ is:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{F}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_F}{x_W}\right) + \alpha \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - x_W}{1 - x_F}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.** (1 M)

$$x_F = z_F = 0.50, \quad x_W = 0.30, \quad \alpha = 2.47, \quad F = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the bracket.** (2 M)

$$\ln(0.50 / 0.30) = \ln(1.6667) = 0.5108$$

$$\ln[(1 - 0.30)/(1 - 0.50)] = \ln(0.70/0.50) = \ln(1.40) = 0.3365$$

$$\text{Bracket} = 0.5108 + 2.47 \times 0.3365 = 0.5108 + 0.8311 = 1.3419$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $(\alpha - 1) = 1.47$.** (1 M)

$$\ln(W/F) = 1.3419 / 1.47 = 0.9129$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $W/F$ and $W$.** (1 M)

$$W/F = e^{0.9129} = 2.4912$$

Wait — this gives $W > F$, which is **physically impossible** (residue cannot exceed feed). Re-check: the bracket should be **negative** for the residue to be smaller. The correct form is:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{W}{F}\right) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_F}{x_W}\right) - \alpha \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 - x_F}{1 - x_W}\right)\right]$$

**Step 3 (corrected) — Re-evaluate.** (1 M)

$$\ln(0.50/0.30) = 0.5108$$

$$\ln[(1 - 0.50)/(1 - 0.30)] = \ln(0.50/0.70) = \ln(0.7143) = -0.3365$$

$$\text{Bracket} = 0.5108 - 2.47 \times (-0.3365) = 0.5108 + 0.8311 = 1.3419$$

This still gives $W > F$, indicating that with $z_F = 0.50$ and $x_W = 0.30$, the separation is **not achievable** in a single batch still using $\alpha = 2.47$. KTU will award **full process marks** for the correct equation setup and **partial credit** for the numerical recognition that this configuration is infeasible without changing $x_W$ or $F$.

**Practical resolution:** Lower $x_W$ further (e.g., $x_W = 0.10$) or assume a smaller $\alpha$ for the exercise.

**Step 6 — Fraction of benzene in distillate.** (1 M for attempt)
For a *feasible* numerical case, the recovery fraction is:

$$\text{Rec}_{\text{benzene}} = \dfrac{F z_F - W x_W}{F z_F} = 1 - \dfrac{W x_W}{F z_F}$$

For the corrected feasible case ($x_W = 0.10$):

$$\ln(W/F) = \dfrac{1}{1.47}\left[\ln(5.0) - 2.47\ln(0.50/0.90)\right] = \dfrac{1}{1.47}\left[1.6094 - 2.47 \times (-0.5878)\right]$$

$$= \dfrac{1.6094 + 1.4519}{1.47} = \dfrac{3.0613}{1.47} = 2.0825$$

$$W/F = e^{2.0825} = 8.025 \implies W = 80.25 \text{ kmol/h (still infeasible — illustrative only)}$$

**Conclusion for the answer key:** A clean KTU-graded problem must specify consistent $z_F$ and $x_W$ such that $W < F$. The model answer is the **equation, the substitution, and the recognition of feasibility**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Flash equation: material balance.** (1 M)

$$z_F = (1 - f)\, x + f\, y \quad \Rightarrow \quad y = \dfrac{z_F - (1-f)x}{f} = \dfrac{0.5 - 0.6\, x}{0.4}$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium with constant $\alpha = 2.47$.** (1 M)

$$y = \dfrac{2.47\, x}{1 + 1.47\, x}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve.** (2 M)

$$\dfrac{0.5 - 0.6\, x}{0.4} = \dfrac{2.47\, x}{1 + 1.47\, x}$$

$$(0.5 - 0.6\, x)(1 + 1.47\, x) = 0.4 \times 2.47\, x$$

$$0.5 + 0.735\, x - 0.6\, x - 0.882\, x^2 = 0.988\, x$$

$$0.5 + 0.135\, x - 0.882\, x^2 = 0.988\, x$$

$$-0.882\, x^2 - 0.853\, x + 0.5 = 0$$

$$0.882\, x^2 + 0.853\, x - 0.5 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Quadratic formula.** (1 M)

$$x = \dfrac{-0.853 \pm \sqrt{0.853^2 + 4 \times 0.882 \times 0.5}}{2 \times 0.882}$$

$$= \dfrac{-0.853 \pm \sqrt{0.7276 + 1.764}}{1.764} = \dfrac{-0.853 \pm \sqrt{2.4916}}{1.764}$$

$$= \dfrac{-0.853 \pm 1.5785}{1.764}$$

Taking the positive root:

$$x = \dfrac{0.7255}{1.764} = 0.4113$$

**Step 5 — Compute $y$.** (1 M)

$$y = \dfrac{0.5 - 0.6 \times 0.4113}{0.4} = \dfrac{0.5 - 0.2468}{0.4} = \dfrac{0.2532}{0.4} = 0.6330$$

**Step 6 — Verify with equilibrium.** (1 M)

$$y_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{2.47 \times 0.4113}{1 + 1.47 \times 0.4113} = \dfrac{1.0159}{1.6046} = 0.6331 \; \checkmark$$

**Final Answer (Part b):** $x = 0.411$, $y = 0.633$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Wrong root rejection (–1 M):** The negative root $x = (-0.853 - 1.5785)/1.764 = -1.378$ is *unphysical* (mole fraction cannot be negative). Always reject negative or >1 roots and state why.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Series 2]

> A continuous fractionating column is fed with **100 kmol/h** of a benzene–toluene mixture with $z_F = 0.50$ (mole fraction benzene). The distillate must have $x_D = 0.95$ and the bottoms $x_W = 0.05$. The relative volatility $\alpha = 2.47$ is constant. A **total condenser** is used, and the feed is a **saturated liquid** ($q = 1$).
>
> **(a)** Determine the **minimum number of theoretical stages $N_m$** using the Fenske equation. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** If the operating reflux ratio is $R = 1.5\, R_m$, and $R_m = 0.88$ (from the McCabe–Thiele construction at the $q$-line–equilibrium intersection), determine the **actual number of stages $N$** using the Gilliland correlation. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Fenske equation.** (2 M)

$$N_m = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D}{1 - x_D} \cdot \dfrac{1 - x_W}{x_W}\right]}{\ln \alpha}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.** (1 M)

$$x_D = 0.95, \quad x_W = 0.05, \quad \alpha = 2.47$$

**Step 3 — Compute the argument of the outer log.** (2 M)

$$\dfrac{x_D}{1 - x_D} = \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.0$$

$$\dfrac{1 - x_W}{x_W} = \dfrac{0.95}{0.05} = 19.0$$

$$\text{Product} = 19.0 \times 19.0 = 361.0$$

**Step 4 — Take logs and divide.** (2 M)

$$\ln(361.0) = 5.888$

Wait — compute precisely:

$$\ln(361) = \ln(19^2) = 2\ln 19 = 2 \times 2.9444 = 5.8889$$

$$\ln \alpha = \ln 2.47 = 0.9046$$

$$N_m = 5.8889 / 0.9046 = 6.51 \approx 7 \text{ stages (round up)}$$

**Final Answer (Part a):** $N_m = 7$ equilibrium stages at total reflux.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $R$.** (1 M)

$$R = 1.5 \times R_m = 1.5 \times 0.88 = 1.32$$

**Step 2 — Gilliland dimensionless groups.** (2 M)

$$X = \dfrac{R - R_m}{R + 1} = \dfrac{1.32 - 0.88}{1.32 + 1} = \dfrac{0.44}{2.32} = 0.1897$$

**Step 3 — Apply Gilliland empirical equation.** (2 M)

$$Y = 0.75\, (1 - X)^{0.5668} = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.1897)^{0.5668} = 0.75 \times (0.8103)^{0.5668}$$

$$(0.8103)^{0.5668} = e^{0.5668 \ln 0.8103} = e^{0.5668 \times (-0.2103)} = e^{-0.1192} = 0.8876$$

$$Y = 0.75 \times 0.8876 = 0.6657$$

**Step 4 — Recover $N$ from $Y$.** (2 M)

Recall $Y = (N - N_m)/(N + 1)$, so:

$$N = \dfrac{N_m + Y}{1 - Y} = \dfrac{6.51 + 0.6657}{1 - 0.6657} = \dfrac{7.176}{0.3343} = 21.46$$

Round up: $N = 22$ equilibrium stages.

**Final Answer (Part b):** $N \approx 22$ theoretical stages.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Gilliland is empirical (–0.5 M):** Always state "using the Gilliland correlation" and identify which form (Eduljee's, Molokanov's, or the original 1940 graphical form). For the 2024 scheme, KTU expects the form $Y = 0.75(1-X)^{0.5668}$. Mentioning the assumption that $R > R_m$ is mandatory for partial credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and tape it inside your calculator cover for the day of the exam.**

- **Three core models:** Rayleigh (batch, single stage, no reflux) · Flash (continuous, single stage, no reflux) · Fractionating column (continuous, multi-stage, with reflux).
- **Two universal balances for any distillation model:** $F = D + W$ (total) and $F z_F = D x_D + W x_W$ (component). Always start with these.
- **Constant relative volatility assumption** turns VLE into the closed form $y = \alpha x / [1 + (\alpha-1)x]$ — the workhorse of KTU problems.
- **Raoult + Dalton** together give the equilibrium relationship. Raoult governs the liquid, Dalton governs the vapor.
- **Flash equation:** $z_F = (1-f)x + fy$. Always pair it with the equilibrium equation before solving.
- **Rayleigh equation** (constant $\alpha$): $\ln(W/F) = \dfrac{1}{\alpha-1}\left[\ln(x_F/x_W) - \alpha\ln[(1-x_F)/(1-x_W)]\right]$ — the sign of the second log term is **negative**, not positive. (Memorize the form; double-check on derivation day.)
- **Operating lines are mass balances, not thermodynamic relations.** Always state the control-volume envelope *before* writing the equation.
- **Rectifying line slope = $R/(R+1)$, y-intercept = $x_D/(R+1)$.** Passes through $(x_D, x_D)$.
- **Stripping line slope = $L'/V'$.** Passes through $(x_W, x_W)$.
- **q-line slope = $q/(q-1)$**, passing through $(z_F, z_F)$. Saturated liquid → slope = 1. Saturated vapor → slope = 0.
- **Total reflux** = operating lines coincide with the diagonal $y = x$ → *minimum* number of stages, infinite reflux.
- **Fenske equation** (minimum stages, total reflux, constant $\alpha$): $N_m = \ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_D(1-x_W)}{x_W(1-x_D)}\right] \big/ \ln \alpha$.
- **Gilliland correlation** (between $N_m$ and infinite reflux, and $N$ at finite $R$): use $Y = 0.75(1-X)^{0.5668}$.
- **Optimum reflux heuristic:** $R_{opt} \approx 1.3 R_m$ (range 1.2–1.5).
- **Reflux ratio** is a *design variable*; increasing $R$ reduces $N$ but increases reboiler and condenser duty — there is an economic optimum.
- **Constant molar overflow** is the assumption that lets us treat $L$ and $V$ as constant in each section. State it explicitly in every operating-line derivation.
- **Recovery fraction** $\text{Rec}_D = D x_D / (F z_F)$ — appears in nearly every KTU Part A and is often the *first checkpoint* a Part B examiner marks.
- **Negative or > 1 mole fractions** in any root are *always* unphysical — reject them and say so.

---

> **END OF KTU 2024 MODULE 2 — DISTILLATION NOTES**
> *Aligned to PCCHT205 Process Calculations | Outcome-Based Education | Revised Bloom's Taxonomy*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drying** is a **mass transfer operation** involving the **simultaneous transfer of heat and mass** (moisture), in which the moisture content of a wet solid is reduced by evaporation into a hot, dry gas stream (usually air). It is classified as a **diffusion-based separation process** governed by the principles of convective heat transfer, vapor-liquid equilibrium, and interphase mass transfer.

In the context of **PCCHT205 – Process Calculations**, drying is treated as a **binary system (dry air + water vapour)** where we apply material and energy balances to a control volume enclosing the dryer.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — Washing clothes on a sunny day:**
> Imagine a wet shirt hung on a clothesline. Three things happen simultaneously:
> 1. **Sunlight (heat)** strikes the fabric and warms it up.
> 2. **Water molecules** at the surface gain enough energy to **escape as vapour** into the surrounding air.
> 3. The **breeze (air)** sweeps the vapour away, preventing it from re-condensing.
>
> If the air is very dry and moving fast, drying is rapid. If the air is humid and stagnant, drying slows down. The shirt dries from the **surface first**, then moisture from **inside migrates outward** to replace the evaporated surface moisture. This two-stage behaviour — **fast surface drying** followed by **slow internal diffusion** — is exactly the physics of the **constant-rate** and **falling-rate** drying periods we will study.

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Units (SI) | Quick Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture content (wet basis) | $w$ | kg water / kg wet solid | Most common in industry |
| Moisture content (dry basis) | $X$ | kg water / kg dry solid | Most common in calculations |
| Bone-dry solid mass | $S$ | kg | Mass of solid without any moisture |
| Critical moisture content | $X_c$ | kg water / kg dry solid | Point where surface dries out |
| Equilibrium moisture content | $X^*$ | kg water / kg dry solid | Minimum moisture achievable in given air |
| Free moisture content | $X_f$ | kg water / kg dry solid | $X - X^*$ (the removable part) |
| Humidity (absolute) | $\mathcal{H}$ | kg water / kg dry air | Same as $Y$ in many texts |
| Wet-bulb temperature | $T_{wb}$ | K or °C | Used for adiabatic drying approximation |
| Drying rate | $N$ or $R$ | kg water / (m²·h) | Mass evaporated per unit area per time |
| Latent heat of vaporization | $\lambda_s$ | kJ / kg | Energy to vaporize 1 kg water at solid T |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **fundamental metric** in all KTU drying problems is **moisture content on a DRY basis ($X$)**, because the mass of *dry solid* is invariant during drying, while the total mass changes. Mixing wet-basis and dry-basis formulas is the **#1 source of numerical errors** in the KTU exam.

## 1.4 Interconversion Between Wet and Dry Basis

> [!NOTE]
> The two definitions are related by simple algebra. Let $m_t$ = total mass, $m_s$ = mass of dry solid, $m_w$ = mass of water.

$$X = \frac{m_w}{m_s} \quad \text{and} \quad w = \frac{m_w}{m_t} = \frac{m_w}{m_s + m_w}$$

Conversion formulas (memorize these — they appear in **almost every KTU numerical**):

$$X = \frac{w}{1 - w} \quad \text{and} \quad w = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

**Limiting behaviour check:** as $w \to 0$, $X \to w$ (small moistures are nearly equal); as $w \to 1$, $X \to \infty$ (wet basis is bounded, dry basis is unbounded — this is why dry basis is preferred for concentrated systems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Logical Steps of a Drying Calculation

A KTU drying problem is always solved in this fixed sequence. Master the **order**, not just the formulas.

**Step 1 — Convert all moistures to DRY basis ($X$).**
*Why?* Because the mass of bone-dry solid $S$ is the conserved quantity. Every subsequent balance is built on $X$.

**Step 2 — Calculate the bone-dry solid mass $S$.**
*How?* From the initial condition:
$$S = m_{\text{initial}} \cdot (1 - w_1) = \frac{m_{\text{initial}}}{1 + X_1}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the water to be removed $\Delta m_w$.**
$$\Delta m_w = S(X_1 - X_2) = m_{\text{initial}} (w_1 - w_2) \cdot \frac{1}{1 - w_2} \cdot \frac{1-w_2}{1} \quad \text{(verify per problem)}$$

A cleaner form:
$$\Delta m_w = S(X_1 - X_2)$$

**Step 4 — Apply mass balance on air (humidity change).**
For a continuous adiabatic dryer:
$$\Delta m_w = L_s (\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1)$$
where $L_s$ = mass flow of *bone-dry air* (kg dry air / s) and $\mathcal{H}_1, \mathcal{H}_2$ are inlet and outlet humidities.

**Step 5 — Apply energy balance (heat supplied = water evaporated × latent heat + sensible heating of solid + losses).**
$$Q = L_s \cdot c_{s,\text{air}} (T_2 - T_1) + \Delta m_w \cdot \lambda_s + m_s c_{p,s} (T_{s,2} - T_{s,1}) + Q_{\text{loss}}$$

## 2.2 The Drying Rate Curve — Heart of the Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **drying rate curve** is a plot of **drying rate $N$ (kg/m²·h)** on the y-axis versus **moisture content $X$** (or sometimes time $t$) on the x-axis. It is the single most important graph in Module 2. The KTU board expects students to **label both periods, identify $X_c$, and compute drying time from the area under the curve**.

### 2.2.1 Region-by-region breakdown

- **A → B : Initial adjustment (transient).** Solid heats up to wet-bulb temperature. Short, often neglected in KTU.
- **B → C : Constant Rate Period (CRP).** Surface is saturated. Drying rate is governed purely by **external mass transfer** to the air (heat and mass transfer coefficients $h, k_y$). The rate is **constant** because surface conditions remain at wet-bulb.
- **Point C : Critical Point.** The surface just dries out. Moisture content here is $X_c$.
- **C → D : First Falling Rate Period (FFRP).** The wet surface area shrinks; dry patches appear. Rate falls roughly **linearly** with $X$.
- **D → E : Second Falling Rate Period (SFRP).** Internal diffusion controls. Often **curvilinear**; sometimes modelled as proportional to $(X - X^*)$.

### 2.2.2 Bound vs Unbound Moisture (the equilibrium story)

- **Unbound moisture** = water that exerts the **full vapour pressure of pure water** at the given temperature. It can be removed by simple evaporation; behaviour is independent of the solid structure.
- **Bound moisture** = water held by **physical/chemical forces** (capillaries, adsorption, cell walls). Vapour pressure is *less* than that of pure water; cannot be fully removed by air of given humidity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equilibrium moisture content $X^*$** is the *minimum* $X$ achievable when the solid is exposed to air of given temperature and humidity **for infinite time**. Below $X^*$, no drying is possible with that air. This is why a *humidifier* of sorts can never completely dry a solid.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Memorize this table — these 14 equations account for ~80% of marks in any KTU drying question.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = w/(1-w)$ | Wet → Dry basis | kg/kg |
| 2 | $w = X/(1+X)$ | Dry → Wet basis | kg/kg |
| 3 | $S = m_1(1-w_1) = m_1/(1+X_1)$ | Bone-dry solid mass | kg |
| 4 | $m_w = S(X_1 - X_2)$ | Water removed | kg |
| 5 | $L_s(\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1) = S(X_1 - X_2)$ | Air-side mass balance | kg/s |
| 6 | $\mathcal{H} = 0.622 \dfrac{p_w}{P - p_w}$ | Humidity from partial pressure | kg/kg |
| 7 | $V_{H} = V_{\text{dry}}(T_{sp}/T_{std})$ | Humid volume conversion | m³/kg dry air |
| 8 | $t_{\text{CRP}} = \dfrac{S(X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c}$ | Time in constant rate period | h or s |
| 9 | $t_{\text{FRP}} = \dfrac{S(X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$ | Time in falling rate period (linear model) | h or s |
| 10 | $N_c = \dfrac{h(T - T_{wb})}{\lambda_s}$ | Drying rate from heat transfer | kg/(m²·s) |
| 11 | $Q = \Delta m_w \cdot \lambda_s + m_s c_{ps} \Delta T_s$ | Heat duty (adiabatic) | kW |
| 12 | $\lambda_s = \lambda_{s,0} - 2.0\,T$ (approx.) | Latent heat at solid T (°C) | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $c_{s,\text{air}} = 1.005 + 1.88 \mathcal{H}$ | Humid air heat capacity | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 14 | $\% \text{Humidity} = \dfrac{\mathcal{H}}{\mathcal{H}_s} \times 100$ | Relative saturation | — |

## 2.4 Real-world Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Drying calculations are the **backbone of the following industries** (this is what makes it a high-weightage KTU topic):
> - **Cement & ceramics** — rotary dryers calcine moisture out of slurry.
> - **Pharma** — fluidized bed dryers (FBD) for granules; controlled to avoid product degradation.
> - **Food** — spray drying of milk, coffee, detergents (atomization of liquid feed).
> - **Pulp & paper** — Yankee and multi-cylinder dryers.
> - **Fertilizers** — co-current rotary dryers for prill drying.
> - **Crops & grains** — flat-bed batch dryers.
>
> **Why process engineers care:** A 1% error in moisture specification can mean **tonnes of energy wasted per day** at plant scale. The latent heat of vaporization of water ($\approx 2260$ kJ/kg) is the largest single energy cost in such plants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section contains three **exhaustive worked derivations** that are *guaranteed* to appear (in some form) in a KTU Part B (14-mark) question. Every algebraic step is shown, with the engineering logic of each transition.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Constant-Rate Drying Equation (CRP)

### 3.1.1 Starting point (physical setup)
During CRP, the solid surface is completely wet. The rate of evaporation is controlled by **convective heat transfer** from the hot air to the wet surface, because the surface temperature is pinned at the **wet-bulb temperature $T_{wb}$**.

### 3.1.2 Heat transferred to the wet surface
For a surface of area $A$ and a convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$ (W/m²·K), the heat flux is:

$$q = h\,A\,(T - T_{wb})$$

where $T$ is the bulk air dry-bulb temperature (K).

### 3.1.3 Mass evaporated (latent heat balance)
The heat supplied is consumed almost entirely in vaporizing water at rate $\dot{m}_w$ (kg/s) with latent heat $\lambda_s$ (J/kg) at the surface temperature:

$$q = \dot{m}_w \cdot \lambda_s$$

### 3.1.4 Drying rate $N$ (kg/m²·s) — define and substitute
By definition, $N = \dot{m}_w / A$. Equating the two expressions for $q$:

$$h\,A\,(T - T_{wb}) = (N A)\,\lambda_s$$

The area $A$ cancels out — *this is why CRP rate is independent of the amount of solid in the dryer*:

$$\boxed{\,N_c = \frac{h\,(T - T_{wb})}{\lambda_s}\,}$$

### 3.1.5 Engineering insight
> [!TIP]
> This equation is **dimensionally consistent** ($W/m^2K \cdot K / (J/kg) = kg/m^2s$) and shows that to maximize CRP drying rate, the engineer should maximize $(T - T_{wb})$ (raise inlet air temperature) and minimize $\lambda_s$ (use higher surface T — though this risks product damage).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time of Drying in the Falling-Rate Period (Linear Model)

### 3.2.1 Physical model
Once the surface dries out, the wet area shrinks. The drying rate becomes **proportional to the free moisture content** $(X - X^*)$ — this is the standard linear-falling-rate assumption used in **McCabe–Smith–Harriott** and **Geankoplis** (the two references KTU examiners lean on).

$$N = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

### 3.2.2 Mass balance in differential form
For a thin slice of drying time $dt$, the moisture content changes by $dX$. The water leaving per unit area is:

$$N = -\frac{S}{A}\,\frac{dX}{dt}$$

(The minus sign is because $X$ decreases as $t$ increases.)

### 3.2.3 Combine the two expressions
$$-\frac{S}{A}\,\frac{dX}{dt} = N_c \cdot \frac{X - X^*}{X_c - X^*}$$

### 3.2.4 Separate variables
Rearrange so that all $X$-terms are on the left and $t$ on the right:

$$\frac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\frac{N_c \cdot A}{S\,(X_c - X^*)}\,dt$$

### 3.2.5 Integrate from $X_c$ (at $t=0$ in this period) to $X_2$ (at $t = t_{\text{FRP}}$)
$$\int_{X_c}^{X_2} \frac{dX}{X - X^*} = -\frac{N_c \cdot A}{S\,(X_c - X^*)} \int_{0}^{t_{\text{FRP}}} dt$$

The left side gives $\ln(X - X^*)$ evaluated between the limits:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{X_2 - X^*}{X_c - X^*}\right) = -\frac{N_c \cdot A}{S\,(X_c - X^*)}\,t_{\text{FRP}}$$

### 3.2.6 Final closed-form expression
Solving for $t_{\text{FRP}}$:

$$\boxed{\,t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{S\,(X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)\,}$$

### 3.2.7 Total drying time
$$t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{CRP}} + t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{S(X_1 - X_c)}{A N_c} + \frac{S(X_c - X^*)}{A N_c}\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Material and Energy Balance on a Continuous Adiabatic Dryer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **standard 14-mark KTU Part B derivation**. Master every step.

### 3.3.1 System diagram (control volume)
- Inlet: wet solid (mass $m_{s1}$ at $X_1$, temperature $T_{s1}$) + fresh air (mass $L_s$, humidity $\mathcal{H}_1$, temperature $T_1$).
- Outlet: dried solid (mass $m_{s2}$ at $X_2$, temperature $T_{s2}$) + exhaust air (humidity $\mathcal{H}_2$, temperature $T_2$).
- Heat input: $Q$ (kW) from an external heater.

### 3.3.2 Steady-state total material balance
$$\underbrace{L_s + m_{s1}(1+X_1)}_{\text{in}} = \underbrace{L_s + m_{s1}(1+X_2)}_{\text{out}}$$

This is trivially satisfied because $m_{s1} = m_{s2} = S$ (bone-dry solid is conserved).

### 3.3.3 Water (moisture) balance
Water IN (with solid + with air) = Water OUT (with solid + with air):

$$L_s \mathcal{H}_1 + S X_1 = L_s \mathcal{H}_2 + S X_2$$

Rearranging (this is the most-tested equation):

$$L_s (\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1) = S(X_1 - X_2)$$

### 3.3.4 Air balance (dry component)
$$L_{s,\text{in}} = L_{s,\text{out}} = L_s \quad \text{(dry air is unchanged — only humidity changes)}$$

### 3.3.5 Energy balance (adiabatic, no work)
Total enthalpy IN + $Q$ = Total enthalpy OUT. Choose reference: liquid water at 0 °C, dry air at 0 °C, dry solid at 0 °C. Then:

$$L_s c_{s1} T_1 + S c_{ps} T_{s1} + S X_1 c_{pw} T_{s1} + Q = L_s c_{s2} T_2 + S c_{ps} T_{s2} + S X_2 c_{pw} T_{s2} + L_s (\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1)\,\lambda_0$$

(Here $\lambda_0$ is the latent heat at 0 °C ≈ 2501 kJ/kg; the last term accounts for the energy carried out as water vapour in the exhaust.)

### 3.3.6 Approximation (for KTU problems)
For the **simplified adiabatic saturation assumption** that KTU almost always uses:
- $T_{s1} = T_{s2} = T_{wb}$ (solid and outlet air are close to wet-bulb)
- Sensible heat of water is small compared with latent heat

$$Q = L_s (\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1)\,\lambda_s$$

Substituting $S(X_1 - X_2)$ from the moisture balance:

$$\boxed{\,Q = S(X_1 - X_2)\,\lambda_s\,}$$

This compact result says: **the heat supplied equals the water removed times its latent heat at the surface temperature**. This is the **single most tested result** in Module 2.

### 3.3.7 Sanity-check (units)
kg × (kg/kg) × kJ/kg = **kJ**, i.e., energy per batch. For continuous operation, divide by time → kW. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style, 7 marks)

> **Problem:** A wet solid enters a continuous dryer at $500$ kg/h with $w_1 = 0.40$ and leaves at $w_2 = 0.05$ (wet basis). Hot air enters at $T_1 = 95\,°C$ with $\mathcal{H}_1 = 0.010$ kg/kg and leaves at $T_2 = 65\,°C$, $\mathcal{H}_2 = 0.028$ kg/kg. Find: (a) water removed per hour, (b) bone-dry air required, (c) heat duty assuming $\lambda_s = 2400$ kJ/kg.

### Solution

**(a) Convert to dry basis and find water removed:**

Using $X = w/(1-w)$:
$$X_1 = \frac{0.40}{1-0.40} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.6667 \text{ kg/kg}$$
$$X_2 = \frac{0.05}{1-0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg}$$

Bone-dry solid:
$$S = 500 \times (1 - 0.40) = 500 \times 0.60 = 300 \text{ kg/h}$$

Water removed:
$$\Delta m_w = S(X_1 - X_2) = 300 \times (0.6667 - 0.0526) = 300 \times 0.6141 = 184.2 \text{ kg/h}$$

**(b) Bone-dry air required** (from $L_s(\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1) = \Delta m_w$):
$$L_s = \frac{184.2}{0.028 - 0.010} = \frac{184.2}{0.018} = 10{,}233 \text{ kg dry air / h}$$

**(c) Heat duty** (from $Q = \Delta m_w \cdot \lambda_s$):
$$Q = 184.2 \times 2400 = 442{,}080 \text{ kJ/h} = 122.8 \text{ kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validation by energy balance** (the more rigorous form):
> Sensible heat of air = $L_s c_{s,\text{air}}(T_1 - T_2)$ with $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.019 \approx 1.041$ kJ/kg·K.
> $Q_{\text{air}} = 10{,}233 \times 1.041 \times 30 = 319{,}572$ kJ/h ≈ 88.8 kW.
>
> The slight difference (≈ 30 kW) is the sensible heat taken up by the wet solid heating up — a perfectly normal and expected result. In KTU answers, mention this for **bonus marks**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Continuous Adiabatic Dryer (Co-current)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Wet Solid Feed]:::input -->|ms1, X1, Ts1| MIX[Co-current Dryer Unit]:::unit
    A2[Fresh Air Inlet]:::input -->|Ls, H1, T1| HTR[Heater Q kW]:::heater
    HTR -->|Ls, H1, T1 hot| MIX
    MIX -->|ms2, X2, Ts2| B1[Dried Product]:::output
    MIX -->|Ls, H2, T2| B2[Exhaust Air to Atmosphere]:::output
    MIX -. Heat + Mass Transfer .-> MIX

    classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef unit fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#000
    classDef heater fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Wet solid (top) and hot air (right) enter the same end of the dryer — this is *co-current* flow. They exchange heat and mass; the wet solid dries, the air picks up moisture. Dried product leaves one end; humid exhaust leaves the other.

## 4.2 Mermaid Graph — The Drying Rate Curve with All Features Labelled

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Solid is wet, X = X1] --> B[Initial Adjustment: Heats up to Twb]
    B --> C[Constant Rate Period: N = Nc constant]
    C --> D[Critical Point: X = Xc, surface dries out]
    D --> E[Falling Rate Period 1: Linear drop in N]
    E --> F[Falling Rate Period 2: Internal diffusion, curvilinear]
    F --> G[End: X approaches Xstar equilibrium]

    A -. X1, t=0 .-> G
    C -. N = Nc .-> D
    D -. Xc .-> E
    E -. Slope dN/dX .-> F
    F -. X -> Xstar .-> G

    classDef period fill:#e7f1ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef point fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#000
    classDef endnode fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000
    class A,G endnode
    class C,D,E,F period
    class B point
```

**Engineering reading:** The drying rate is **constant** from A through D (i.e., region B→C and the constant-rate plateau), then it **falls** from D onwards. The total drying time is the area under the *N vs. t* plot (or equivalently, integrated using the formulas in Section 3).

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Dryers (High-Yield Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Industrial Dryers]:::root --> BATCH[Batch Dryers]:::cat
    ROOT --> CONT[Continuous Dryers]:::cat
    BATCH --> B1[Tray / Cabinet Dryer]:::type
    BATCH --> B2[Agitated Pan Dryer]:::type
    BATCH --> B3[Vacuum Shelf Dryer]:::type
    CONT --> C1[Rotary Dryer]:::type
    CONT --> C2[Fluidized Bed Dryer]:::type
    CONT --> C3[Spray Dryer]:::type
    CONT --> C4[Drum Dryer]:::type
    CONT --> C5[Belt / Conveyor Dryer]:::type
    C1 --> C1A[Co-current or Counter-current]:::mode
    C2 --> C2A[For Granules Pharma Food]:::mode
    C3 --> C3A[Liquid Feed Atomized]:::mode
    C4 --> C4A[Slurry or Paste]:::mode

    classDef root fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000
    classDef cat fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef type fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000
    classDef mode fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Name two continuous dryers suitable for heat-sensitive materials"* — the answer is **spray dryer** (very short residence time) and **fluidized bed dryer** (good heat transfer, low T). This block diagram is the **mental model** to recall during the exam.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Material & Energy Flow in a Dryer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1[Inlet Air]:::stream -->|Ls, H1, T1| F1[FAN / BLOWER]:::equip
    F1 -->|Flow Control| H[PRE-HEATER]:::heater
    H -->|Ls, H1, T1 heated| CV[DRYER CHAMBER - Control Volume]:::cv
    IN2[Inlet Wet Solid]:::stream -->|ms, X1, Ts1| CV
    CV -->|ms, X2, Ts2| OUT1[Dried Solid Out]:::stream
    CV -->|Ls, H2, T2| OUT2[Exhaust Air Out]:::stream
    CV -. Q supplied by heater .-> H
    OUT2 -. Optionally .-> REC[Recycle Loop Partial]:::opt
    REC -. H2 mixed with H1 .-> H

    classDef stream fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000
    classDef equip fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#146c43,color:#000
    classDef heater fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#b02a37,color:#000
    classDef cv fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc9a06,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **The control volume is the dryer chamber.** Everything entering the chamber — air, solid, heat — must equal everything leaving — dried solid, exhaust, losses. The KTU "control volume" method (used in Treybal, McCabe) is identical to the mass/energy balance we derived in Section 3.3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define the following terms with their units: (i) Moisture content on dry basis, (ii) Humidity, (iii) Wet-bulb temperature.

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Moisture content on dry basis ($X$):** Mass of moisture (kg) per unit mass of *bone-dry* solid (kg). Units: **kg water / kg dry solid** (dimensionless ratio).
>
> (ii) **Humidity ($\mathcal{H}$ or $Y$):** Mass of water vapour (kg) carried per unit mass of *bone-dry* air. Units: **kg water / kg dry air**. Sometimes called *absolute humidity* or *humidity ratio*.
>
> (iii) **Wet-bulb temperature ($T_{wb}$):** The temperature reached by a water surface when exposed to a stream of unsaturated air under adiabatic conditions, with the air–vapour system at steady state. It is the **surface temperature** of water (or of a saturated solid) during the constant-rate drying period.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] Differentiate between bound moisture and unbound moisture. Give one example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bound Moisture | Unbound Moisture |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Water held by physical/chemical forces within the solid; exerts a vapour pressure **less than** that of pure water at the same T. | Water that exerts the **full vapour pressure** of pure water at the given T; behaves independently of the solid. |
| **Removal** | Cannot be removed by drying in air of given T & H; only by more drastic means. | Easily removed by evaporation in any air. |
| **Equilibrium** | Contributes to the *equilibrium moisture content* $X^*$. | No equilibrium limit; all removable. |
| **Example** | Water adsorbed in cell walls of wood (the *fibre-saturation* region). | Free water in a wet sand pile; surface water on a wet cloth. |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]

**A wet solid is dried from an initial moisture content of $40\%$ (wet basis) to a final moisture content of $5\%$ (wet basis). The initial mass of the wet solid is $1000$ kg. Hot air enters the dryer at $90\,°C$ with humidity $0.015$ kg/kg dry air and leaves at $55\,°C$ with humidity $0.030$ kg/kg dry air. The solid enters at $30\,°C$ and leaves at $50\,°C$. The specific heat of the dry solid is $1.5$ kJ/(kg·K), and latent heat of vaporization at solid temperature is $2400$ kJ/kg.**

**(a)** Calculate the mass of water removed per kg of dry solid, and the bone-dry air required per hour. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the total heat to be supplied per hour, taking into account the sensible heating of the solid and the humid air. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert moistures to dry basis:**

$$X_1 = \frac{0.40}{1-0.40} = \frac{0.40}{0.60} = 0.6667 \text{ kg/kg}$$

$$X_2 = \frac{0.05}{1-0.05} = \frac{0.05}{0.95} = 0.0526 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Bone-dry solid mass:**

$$S = 1000 \times (1 - 0.40) = 1000 \times 0.60 = 600 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Water removed per hour:**

$$\Delta m_w = S \times (X_1 - X_2) = 600 \times (0.6667 - 0.0526) = 600 \times 0.6141 = 368.5 \text{ kg water / h}$$

**Step 4 — Bone-dry air required** (from moisture balance $L_s(\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1) = \Delta m_w$):

$$L_s = \frac{\Delta m_w}{\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1} = \frac{368.5}{0.030 - 0.015} = \frac{368.5}{0.015} = 24{,}567 \text{ kg dry air / h}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 5 — Sensible heat of the dry solid:**

$$Q_{\text{solid}} = S \cdot c_{ps} \cdot (T_{s2} - T_{s1}) = 600 \times 1.5 \times (50 - 30) = 600 \times 1.5 \times 20 = 18{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 6 — Humid air heat capacity (using inlet humidity for the entering stream):**

$$c_{s,\text{air,in}} = 1.005 + 1.88 \times 0.015 = 1.033 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 7 — Sensible heat of inlet air** (using $\Delta T = T_1 - T_2 = 90 - 55 = 35\,°C$):

$$Q_{\text{air,sensible}} = L_s \cdot c_{s,\text{air}} \cdot (T_1 - T_2) = 24{,}567 \times 1.033 \times 35 = 888{,}191 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 8 — Latent heat for evaporation:**

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = \Delta m_w \times \lambda_s = 368.5 \times 2400 = 884{,}400 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 9 — Total heat to be supplied** (sum of all three):

$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{air,sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent}} + Q_{\text{solid}} = 888{,}191 + 884{,}400 + 18{,}000 = 1{,}790{,}591 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Convert to kW:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = \frac{1{,}790{,}591}{3600} = 497.4 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** If we had used the simplified formula $Q = \Delta m_w \cdot \lambda_s = 884{,}400$ kJ/h ≈ 245.7 kW, this would have given us only the *latent* component — about **half** the true value. The 252 kW extra covers the sensible heating of air + solid, which is a significant fraction. **KTU examiners reward students who do the rigorous energy balance.**

### Question B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style — Alternative Choice]

**A batch dryer dries $500$ kg of a wet granular solid from $w_1 = 0.30$ to $w_2 = 0.02$ (wet basis). The constant-rate drying rate is $N_c = 1.5$ kg/(m²·h) and the critical moisture content (dry basis) is $X_c = 0.18$ kg/kg. The equilibrium moisture content is $X^* = 0.005$ kg/kg. The drying tray area is $4$ m².**

**(a)** Calculate the total drying time, showing the contribution of the constant-rate and falling-rate periods separately. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the inlet air temperature is $80\,°C$ and the wet-bulb temperature is $40\,°C$, estimate the convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$ (use $\lambda_s = 2400$ kJ/kg). Also state the assumption made regarding the area under the drying rate curve. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis:**

$$X_1 = \frac{0.30}{0.70} = 0.4286 \text{ kg/kg}, \quad X_2 = \frac{0.02}{0.98} = 0.0204 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Bone-dry solid:**

$$S = 500 \times (1 - 0.30) = 500 \times 0.70 = 350 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Constant-rate period time** ($X_1 \to X_c$):

$$t_{\text{CRP}} = \frac{S(X_1 - X_c)}{A \cdot N_c} = \frac{350 \times (0.4286 - 0.18)}{4 \times 1.5} = \frac{350 \times 0.2486}{6.0} = \frac{87.01}{6.0} = 14.50 \text{ h}$$

**Step 4 — Falling-rate period time** ($X_c \to X_2$, linear model):

$$t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{S(X_c - X^*)}{A \cdot N_c}\ln\!\left(\frac{X_c - X^*}{X_2 - X^*}\right)$$

Compute the inner terms:
- $X_c - X^* = 0.18 - 0.005 = 0.175$
- $X_2 - X^* = 0.0204 - 0.005 = 0.0154$
- Ratio: $0.175 / 0.0154 = 11.36$
- $\ln(11.36) = 2.430$

$$t_{\text{FRP}} = \frac{350 \times 0.175}{4 \times 1.5} \times 2.430 = \frac{61.25}{6.0} \times 2.430 = 10.208 \times 2.430 = 24.81 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Total drying time:**

$$t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{CRP}} + t_{\text{FRP}} = 14.50 + 24.81 = 39.31 \text{ h}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 6 — Heat-transfer coefficient from $N_c$:**

Rearrange $N_c = h(T - T_{wb}) / \lambda_s$:

$$h = \frac{N_c \cdot \lambda_s}{T - T_{wb}} = \frac{1.5 \, \text{kg/(m}^2\text{·h)} \times 2400 \, \text{kJ/kg}}{(80 - 40)\,°C}$$

Convert units consistently: $N_c = 1.5 / 3600 = 4.167 \times 10^{-4}$ kg/(m²·s); $\lambda_s = 2.4 \times 10^6$ J/kg.

$$h = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.4 \times 10^6}{40} = \frac{1000}{40} = 25 \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**Step 7 — Area under the drying-rate curve assumption:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption:** The total drying time equals the area under the *drying rate vs. moisture content* curve divided by $(A \cdot N_c)$, **or** equivalently, the area under the *N vs. t* curve. For the **constant-rate period**, the area is a rectangle: $N_c \times t_{\text{CRP}} = N_c (X_1 - X_c) S / (A N_c) = S(X_1 - X_c)/A$. For the **falling-rate period (linear model)**, the area is a triangle plus a rectangle, leading to the logarithmic form shown in Step 4.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing wet & dry basis in the same line** is the #1 error. Always convert to dry basis first, then keep using $X$ throughout.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $S$** in the drying-time equation — students often write $\frac{X_1 - X_c}{A N_c}$ which is *dimensionally wrong* (units: m²·h/kg, not h). The dry-solid mass $S$ is mandatory.
> 3. **Sign errors in energy balance** — heat IN and heat OUT must be in the same direction. Tabulate before substituting.
> 4. **Using $T$ in °C in energy-balance formulas that need K** — for differences this is fine, but for gas-law conversions ($PV = nRT$) use K.
> 5. **Ignoring the sensible heat of the solid** in part (b) type questions — this costs 2–3 marks in KTU evaluations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this list to your wall the night before the exam.**

- [ ] Always convert to **dry basis** $X$ before any calculation. Memorize: $X = w/(1-w)$, $w = X/(1+X)$.
- [ ] Bone-dry solid mass is **conserved**: $S = m_{\text{initial}}(1 - w_1) = m_{\text{initial}}/(1+X_1)$.
- [ ] Water removed: $\Delta m_w = S(X_1 - X_2)$.
- [ ] Air-side mass balance: $L_s(\mathcal{H}_2 - \mathcal{H}_1) = S(X_1 - X_2)$.
- [ ] **Humidity formula:** $\mathcal{H} = 0.622\, p_w/(P - p_w)$ — the constant **0.622** is the ratio of molar masses of water (18) to air (29).
- [ ] **Drying rate in CRP:** $N_c = h(T - T_{wb})/\lambda_s$. Rate is **independent of the amount of solid**.
- [ ] **Time in CRP:** $t_{\text{CRP}} = S(X_1 - X_c)/(A N_c)$.
- [ ] **Time in FRP (linear model):** $t_{\text{FRP}} = [S(X_c - X^*)/(A N_c)] \cdot \ln[(X_c - X^*)/(X_2 - X^*)]$.
- [ ] **Total drying time** = $t_{\text{CRP}} + t_{\text{FRP}}$.
- [ ] **Heat duty (simplified):** $Q = \Delta m_w \cdot \lambda_s$.
- [ ] **Heat duty (rigorous):** $Q = L_s c_s \Delta T_{\text{air}} + \Delta m_w \lambda_s + m_s c_{ps} \Delta T_s$.
- [ ] **Humid air heat capacity:** $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88\,\mathcal{H}$ (kJ/(kg·K)).
- [ ] **Latent heat variation (approx.):** $\lambda_s = 2501 - 2.38\,T$ (T in °C) or $\lambda_s = \lambda_{s,0} - 2.0\,T$.
- [ ] **Equilibrium moisture $X^*$**: minimum achievable; depends on T, H of air.
- [ ] **Free moisture** = $X - X^*$; **Bound moisture** = $X^*$ at a given humidity.
- [ ] **Critical moisture $X_c$**: marks transition from CRP to FRP. Higher for thick beds, finer particles, lower air velocity.
- [ ] **Drying rate curve** has FOUR regions: AB (adjustment), BC (constant rate), CD (1st falling), DE (2nd falling).
- [ ] The **constant rate** is limited by **external** heat/mass transfer. The **falling rate** is limited by **internal** diffusion.
- [ ] **Co-current** dryers: solid meets hottest air first → good for **heat-sensitive** materials. **Counter-current**: solid meets coolest air last → lower exit moisture, higher thermal efficiency, but solid may overheat.
- [ ] **Spray dryer** residence time ~ 5–30 s; used for milk, coffee, detergent.
- [ ] **Fluidized bed dryer** — used in pharma for granules; excellent heat transfer.
- [ ] **Rotary dryer** — used for bulk solids, ores, fertilizers.
- [ ] Common unit conversion: $1$ ton refrigeration ≈ $3.517$ kW; $1$ kcal = $4.184$ kJ.
- [ ] In **KEE (Kerala Engineering Entrance)** & KTU: never mix S.I. and CGS units in the same line — convert first.
- [ ] Always quote answers to **3–4 significant figures** with **correct units** in the box.

> **End of Module 2 — Drying notes. All the best for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaporation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaporation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Evaporation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evaporation** is a unit operation in chemical engineering that involves the **concentration of a non-volatile solute** by the **removal of the volatile solvent (usually water)** through vaporization, using a heat source. The process is fundamentally a heat-transfer operation coupled with simultaneous mass transfer of the vapor.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, evaporation is defined as the process of **vaporizing a portion of a liquid from a solution, suspension, or emulsion to produce a more concentrated product**. The volatile component is removed as vapor, and the non-volatile solute is retained in the remaining liquid phase.

### Key Distinguishing Features
- The **vapor** produced is usually a single-component system (pure solvent vapor).
- The **liquid phase** is a multi-component mixture of the solute and the remaining solvent.
- Heat is supplied externally, typically by **saturated steam** condensing in a heating medium (calandria, jacket, or coils).
- The process is generally carried out at the **boiling point** of the solution.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Saucepan" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of boiling a pot of milk on a stove to make reduced, thicker *khoya* or condensed milk. The water in the milk escapes as steam, and the milk becomes thicker and more concentrated. The stove provides heat (like steam in an industrial evaporator), the pot holds the liquid (like the evaporator body), and the steam escaping is the vapor product.

In a **kitchen saucepan (single-effect)**:
- Heat is supplied once from a stove.
- Vapor is removed and lost to the atmosphere (no reuse).
- Only **one** boiling stage occurs.

In an **industrial chemical plant (multiple-effect)**:
- Heat from one evaporator's vapor is reused to boil the next stage.
- This cascading approach dramatically improves **steam economy** — the kg of water evaporated per kg of steam consumed.

## 1.3 Terminology Used in Evaporator Calculations

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Feed** | $F$ | Solution entering the evaporator (kg/h) |
| **Concentrated product (liquor)** | $L$ | Concentrated solution leaving the evaporator (kg/h) |
| **Vapor** | $V$ | Solvent vapor produced (kg/h) |
| **Steam** | $S$ | Heating steam supplied to the calandria (kg/h) |
| **Feed concentration** | $x_F$ | Mass fraction of solute in feed |
| **Product concentration** | $x_L$ | Mass fraction of solute in product |
| **Feed temperature** | $T_F$ | Temperature of incoming solution (°C) |
| **Boiling temperature** | $T_B$ | Temperature of solution in the evaporator (°C) |
| **Steam temperature** | $T_S$ | Temperature of saturated heating steam (°C) |
| **Vapor temperature** | $T_V$ | Temperature of vapor leaving the evaporator (°C) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity** of an evaporator is defined as the **kilograms of water (vapor) produced per unit time** (typically **kg/h** or **kg/s**).
>
> **Economy** of an evaporator is defined as the **kilograms of water vaporized per kilogram of steam supplied** (dimensionless ratio).

## 1.4 Classification of Evaporators

> [!NOTE]
> Evaporators are classified based on the **method of heat supply** and the **configuration of the heating surface**.

### 1.4.1 Based on Method of Heat Supply
1. **Direct-fired evaporators** — Heat supplied by combustion gases in direct contact.
2. **Steam-heated evaporators** — Heat supplied indirectly by saturated steam (most common in industry).

### 1.4.2 Based on Construction
- **Open-pan evaporators** — Simplest design, low cost, used in sugar and salt industries.
- **Natural circulation evaporators** — e.g., *Short-tube*, *Long-tube vertical* (Calandria type).
- **Forced circulation evaporators** — Liquid is pumped through the tubes, used for viscous or scaling liquids.
- **Falling film evaporators** — Liquid flows as a thin film down the inside of heated tubes.
- **Rising film (LTV) evaporators** — Liquid rises as a film due to boiling action.
- **Agitated thin-film evaporators** — Used for highly viscous, heat-sensitive materials.

## 1.5 Single-Effect vs. Multiple-Effect Evaporators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **single-effect evaporator** uses steam in **one** vessel to produce vapor only **once**.
>
> A **multiple-effect evaporator** uses a series of vessels (effects) operating at successively **lower pressures**, where the vapor from one effect serves as the heating medium for the next effect.

The driving force for evaporation in each effect is the **temperature difference** $(\Delta T)$ between the heating medium and the boiling liquid. In a multiple-effect system, the total temperature difference is distributed across the effects.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Balance in an Evaporator

The fundamental material balance is based on **conservation of mass** for both the **total solution** and the **solute**.

### 2.1.1 Total Material Balance

> [!NOTE]
> The total mass entering the evaporator must equal the total mass leaving.

$$
F = L + V
$$

Where:
- $F$ = mass flow rate of feed (kg/h)
- $L$ = mass flow rate of concentrated product (kg/h)
- $V$ = mass flow rate of vapor produced (kg/h)

### 2.1.2 Solute (Solid) Material Balance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The solute is **non-volatile**, so it does not leave with the vapor. All the solute entering must leave in the concentrated product.

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L
$$

Combining the two material balance equations:

$$
V = F - L = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

## 2.2 Energy Balance in an Evaporator

> [!NOTE]
> The energy balance follows the **First Law of Thermodynamics** under steady-state conditions: *Energy in = Energy out*.

The general energy balance for a single-effect evaporator is:

$$
F \cdot h_F + S \cdot h_S = L \cdot h_L + V \cdot h_V + S \cdot h_{S,\text{cond}} + Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

In its standard simplified form (neglecting heat losses), and using the latent heat of condensation of steam, $S \cdot \lambda_S$, and the latent heat of vaporization of the product vapor, $V \cdot \lambda_V$:

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda_V + L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_B - T_F) + Q_{\text{loss}}
$$

The term $L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_B - T_F)$ represents the **sensible heat** required to raise the feed from its initial temperature to the boiling point.

## 2.3 Boiling Point Elevation (BPE) and Dühring's Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **boiling point of a solution is always higher** than the boiling point of the pure solvent at the same pressure. This is called **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**, and it is a colligative property.

$$
\text{BPE} = T_B - T_{\text{solvent}}
$$

Where $T_{\text{solvent}}$ is the boiling point of pure water at the operating pressure of the evaporator.

### 2.3.1 Dühring's Rule

Dühring's rule states that the **boiling point of a solution is a linear function of the boiling point of pure water at the same pressure**:

$$
T_B^{\text{solution}} = m \cdot T_B^{\text{water}} + c
$$

Where $m$ and $c$ are constants determined experimentally for each solute at a given concentration.

## 2.4 Effect of Boiling Point Elevation on Capacity and Area

### 2.4.1 Temperature Drop in Each Effect
For an $N$-effect evaporator with total temperature drop $\Delta T_{\text{total}}$:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i
$$

If BPE is neglected, the temperature drop is equally divided:
$$
\Delta T_i = \frac{\Delta T_{\text{total}}}{N}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> As $N$ increases, $\Delta T_i$ per effect **decreases**, requiring **larger heat transfer area** for the same capacity. This explains why the **optimum number of effects is finite** (usually 3–5 for steam economy balance).

### 2.4.2 Capacity Equation
The heat transfer rate in each effect is given by:

$$
Q_i = U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T_i
$$

Where $U_i$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient, and $A_i$ is the heat transfer area.

If all effects have **equal area** ($A_i = A$) and **equal heat transfer coefficient** ($U_i = U$):

$$
A = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{U \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i}
$$

## 2.5 Steam Economy and Capacity of Multiple-Effect Evaporator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an $N$-effect evaporator:
> - **Capacity** = Total water evaporated = $V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_N$
> - **Economy** = (Total water evaporated) / (Steam supplied) = $V_{\text{total}} / S$

In an ideal $N$-effect evaporator, approximately $N$ kg of water is evaporated per kg of steam supplied. So:

$$
\text{Economy}_{\text{ideal}} \approx N
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all the high-yield formulas required for the KTU examination on Evaporation.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Material Balance | $F = L + V$ | kg/h | Conservation of mass |
| 2 | Solute Material Balance | $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ | kg/h | Solute is non-volatile |
| 3 | Vapor produced | $V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | kg/h | Derived from (1) and (2) |
| 4 | Steam required (single effect) | $S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_V + L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_B - T_F)}{\lambda_S}$ | kg/h | From energy balance |
| 5 | Steam Economy | $\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S}$ | dimensionless | kg vapor / kg steam |
| 6 | Capacity | $\text{Capacity} = V$ | kg/h | Mass of water evaporated |
| 7 | Boiling Point Elevation | $\text{BPE} = T_B - T_{\text{pure}}$ | °C or K | $T_{\text{pure}}$ = pure water BP |
| 8 | Dühring's rule | $T_B^{\text{soln}} = m \cdot T_B^{\text{water}} + c$ | °C | Linear relation |
| 9 | Heat transfer rate (effect $i$) | $Q_i = U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T_i$ | W or kW | $U$ in W/m²K |
| 10 | Total temperature drop | $\Delta T_{\text{total}} = \sum \Delta T_i$ | °C or K | Sum across all effects |
| 11 | Equal area, equal $U$ | $A = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{U \cdot \sum \Delta T_i}$ | m² | Single area for all effects |
| 12 | Economy of $N$-effect | $\text{Economy} \approx N$ | dimensionless | Ideal upper limit |
| 13 | Optimum $N$ (Kremser eqn.) | $N_{\text{opt}} = \left(\frac{\Delta T_{\text{total}}}{34.9 \cdot \frac{T_B^2}{K}}\right)^{0.5}$ | dimensionless | Where $K$ is a constant |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Evaporation is one of the **largest energy-consuming operations** in the chemical process industry. It is used in:
> - **Sugar industry** — concentrating cane juice from 14% to 70% solids.
> - **Salt production** — concentrating brine.
> - **Dairy industry** — milk concentration, whey evaporation.
> - **Pulp & paper** — black liquor concentration.
> - **Desalination** — producing fresh water from seawater.
> - **Caustic soda / Chlor-alkali** — concentrating NaOH solutions.

The design of evaporators is a **classical exercise in process calculations** involving simultaneous material and energy balances, heat transfer, and vapor-liquid equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material and Energy Balance of a Single-Effect Evaporator

### 3.1.1 System Setup

Consider a **single-effect evaporator** with the following inputs and outputs:
- **Feed** ($F$, kg/h) at concentration $x_F$ (mass fraction of solute) and temperature $T_F$.
- **Steam** ($S$, kg/h) at temperature $T_S$, with latent heat of condensation $\lambda_S$.
- **Concentrated liquor** ($L$, kg/h) at concentration $x_L$ and temperature $T_B$ (boiling point).
- **Vapor product** ($V$, kg/h) at temperature $T_V$ and latent heat $\lambda_V$.
- **Steam condensate** leaving at temperature $T_S$.

### 3.1.2 Total Material Balance

> Derivation: Under steady-state, mass in = mass out.

$$
F + S = L + V + S_{\text{condensate}}
$$

But the steam condensate mass equals the steam mass supplied ($S$). Therefore:

$$
F = L + V
$$

### 3.1.3 Solute (Solid) Material Balance

> Derivation: The solute is non-volatile. Hence, no solute leaves with the vapor stream.

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L + V \cdot 0
$$

$$
F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L
$$

### 3.1.4 Combined Mass Balance

From $L = F - V$ and $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$:

$$
F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L
$$

$$
F \cdot x_F = F \cdot x_L - V \cdot x_L
$$

$$
V \cdot x_L = F \cdot x_L - F \cdot x_F = F \cdot (x_L - x_F)
$$

$$
V = F \cdot \frac{(x_L - x_F)}{x_L} = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)
$$

### 3.1.5 Energy Balance

> Derivation: At steady state, the total enthalpy entering equals the total enthalpy leaving, plus any heat loss.

Energy entering:
- Feed enthalpy: $F \cdot h_F$
- Steam enthalpy: $S \cdot h_S$ (saturated steam enthalpy)

Energy leaving:
- Concentrated liquor enthalpy: $L \cdot h_L$
- Vapor product enthalpy: $V \cdot H_V$ (superheated or saturated vapor enthalpy)
- Steam condensate enthalpy: $S \cdot h_{\text{cond}}$ (saturated liquid at $T_S$)

$$
F \cdot h_F + S \cdot h_S = L \cdot h_L + V \cdot H_V + S \cdot h_{\text{cond}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
S \cdot (h_S - h_{\text{cond}}) = L \cdot h_L + V \cdot H_V - F \cdot h_F
$$

The term $(h_S - h_{\text{cond}}) = \lambda_S$ is the **latent heat of condensation** of the heating steam. Therefore:

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S = L \cdot h_L + V \cdot H_V - F \cdot h_F
$$

### 3.1.6 Approximate Form (Boiling-Point Feed)

> If the feed enters at the boiling point of the solution ($T_F = T_B$), then $h_F = h_L$ (approximate if the concentration change is small). The energy balance simplifies to:

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda_V
$$

$$
S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_V}{\lambda_S}
$$

If $\lambda_V \approx \lambda_S$ (which is often a reasonable assumption for dilute solutions), then:

$$
S \approx V
$$

This means **1 kg of steam evaporates approximately 1 kg of water** in a single-effect evaporator — hence the **economy ≈ 1**.

### 3.1.7 General Form (Cold Feed)

> If the feed enters cold (below boiling), the sensible heat of the feed and the concentrated liquor must be considered:

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S = V \cdot \lambda_V + L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_B - T_F)
$$

Rearranging for steam:

$$
S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_V + L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_B - T_F)}{\lambda_S}
$$

This is the **standard KTU formula for steam consumption** in a single-effect evaporator.

## 3.2 Derivation: Multiple-Effect Evaporator Economy and Capacity

### 3.2.1 Setup

Consider an $N$-effect evaporator. The total vapor produced is the sum of vapors from each effect:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_i = V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \cdots + V_N
$$

### 3.2.2 Mass Balance for the Entire System

For the entire $N$-effect system, the material balance is identical to a single-effect:

$$
F = L_N + V_{\text{total}}
$$

$$
F \cdot x_F = L_N \cdot x_{L_N}
$$

### 3.2.3 Energy Balance for the Entire System

The total steam supplied is $S$, and the total heat delivered equals:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = S \cdot \lambda_S = V_{\text{total}} \cdot \lambda + L_N \cdot c_p \cdot (T_B - T_F) + \text{heat losses}
$$

### 3.2.4 Derivation of Steam Economy

By definition:

$$
\text{Economy} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S}
$$

If we **neglect** sensible heat effects and assume no heat losses:

$$
S \cdot \lambda_S \approx V_{\text{total}} \cdot \lambda
$$

For dilute aqueous solutions, $\lambda_S \approx \lambda$, hence:

$$
\text{Economy} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} \approx \frac{S \cdot \lambda / \lambda}{S} = 1
$$

Wait — this seems to suggest economy is always 1, which is **wrong**. The key insight is that in a multiple-effect evaporator, **vapor from effect $i$ is the heating steam for effect $(i+1)$**. So although the total energy delivered to the system is $S \cdot \lambda_S$, this energy is **cascaded** through the effects.

### 3.2.5 The Correct Cascading Logic

In a multiple-effect evaporator:
- The steam supplied to effect 1 vaporizes $V_1$ kg of water.
- The vapor $V_1$ becomes the heating medium for effect 2, which vaporizes $V_2$ kg of water.
- The vapor $V_2$ becomes the heating medium for effect 3, and so on.

Under ideal conditions (equal latent heats, no BPE, no sensible heat effects):

$$
V_1 = S, \quad V_2 = V_1 = S, \quad \ldots, \quad V_N = S
$$

Hence:

$$
V_{\text{total}} = N \cdot S
$$

$$
\text{Economy} = \frac{V_{\text{total}}}{S} = N
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **theoretical maximum economy** of an $N$-effect evaporator. In practice, the economy is **less than $N$** because of BPE, sensible heat effects, and heat losses.

## 3.3 Derivation: Heat Transfer Area for Multiple Effects (Equal Area Condition)

### 3.3.1 Total Heat Transfer

The total heat transferred across all effects must equal the heat supplied by steam:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} Q_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i \cdot A_i \cdot \Delta T_i
$$

### 3.3.2 Equal Area Assumption

If $A_1 = A_2 = \ldots = A_N = A$ and $U_1 = U_2 = \ldots = U_N = U$:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = U \cdot A \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i
$$

### 3.3.3 Solving for Area

$$
A = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{U \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta T_i}
$$

### 3.3.4 What if Areas are NOT Equal?

For unequal $U_i$ or specified different $A_i$, the iterative procedure is:

1. Assume a temperature distribution $\Delta T_i$ (e.g., in proportion to latent heats).
2. Calculate $V_i$ from material balance.
3. Calculate $A_i = \frac{V_i \cdot \lambda}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$.
4. Iterate until convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU examination, the **equal area** assumption is the **standard simplification** used unless otherwise stated.

## 3.4 Derivation: Dühring's Rule and BPE

### 3.4.1 Theoretical Basis (Raoult's Law)

For an ideal dilute solution, the vapor pressure of the solvent over the solution is reduced according to Raoult's law. From the Clausius-Clapeyron equation, the boiling point of the solution is higher than the pure solvent.

For a solution containing a non-volatile solute at mole fraction $x_1$ (solvent):

$$
\ln(x_1) = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_{\text{pure}}} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{solution}}}\right)
$$

For dilute solutions, this simplifies to the elevation:

$$
\Delta T_B \approx \frac{R \cdot T^2 \cdot x_2}{\Delta H_v}
$$

This shows that BPE depends on the **mole fraction of solute**.

### 3.4.2 Dühring's Empirical Form

Since rigorous calculation of BPE is complex, Dühring proposed:

$$
T_{\text{solution}} = m \cdot T_{\text{water}} + c
$$

Where $T_{\text{water}}$ is the boiling point of pure water at the **same pressure** as the solution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **multiple-effect evaporator**, the vapor from effect $i$ is superheated relative to the boiling liquid in effect $(i+1)$. The effective temperature driving force in effect $(i+1)$ is reduced by the BPE of the solution in that effect.

### 3.4.3 Effect of BPE on Economy

If the total BPE across all effects is $\sum \text{BPE}_i$, then the **available temperature difference** for heat transfer is:

$$
\Delta T_{\text{available}} = \Delta T_{\text{total}} - \sum \text{BPE}_i
$$

The **true economy** is reduced from $N$ to:

$$
\text{Economy}_{\text{true}} < N
$$

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Single-Effect Evaporator

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A feed of 10,000 kg/h containing 10% NaOH (by mass) is concentrated to 50% NaOH in a single-effect evaporator. The feed enters at 30 °C, and the solution boils at 110 °C. Saturated steam at 1.5 bar (latent heat 2226 kJ/kg) is used. The latent heat of vaporization at 110 °C is 2230 kJ/kg. The specific heat of the feed can be taken as $c_p = 3.77$ kJ/(kg·K). Calculate the (a) kg/h of water evaporated, (b) kg/h of concentrated liquor, (c) kg/h of steam required, and (d) economy.

### Step 1: Vapor Produced (Material Balance)

$$
V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 10{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right)
$$

$$
V = 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.2) = 10{,}000 \times 0.8 = 8{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 2: Concentrated Liquor

$$
L = F - V = 10{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 3: Steam Required (Energy Balance)

The feed enters at 30 °C and is heated to 110 °C. The sensible heat required:

$$
Q_{\text{sensible}} = L \cdot c_p \cdot (T_B - T_F) = 2{,}000 \times 3.77 \times (110 - 30)
$$

$$
Q_{\text{sensible}} = 2{,}000 \times 3.77 \times 80 = 603{,}200 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

The latent heat to vaporize water:

$$
Q_{\text{latent}} = V \cdot \lambda_V = 8{,}000 \times 2{,}230 = 17{,}840{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

Total heat:

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 17{,}840{,}000 + 603{,}200 = 18{,}443{,}200 \text{ kJ/h}
$$

Steam required:

$$
S = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{\lambda_S} = \frac{18{,}443{,}200}{2{,}226} = 8{,}285.5 \text{ kg/h}
$$

### Step 4: Economy

$$
\text{Economy} = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{8{,}000}{8{,}285.5} = 0.966
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The economy is slightly less than 1 because the feed enters cold (30 °C) and must be heated to the boiling point, requiring extra steam.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on Capacity | Effect on Economy | Effect on Heat Transfer Area |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Increased steam pressure** | Increases (higher $\Delta T$) | No direct effect | Decreases (higher $Q$ per unit area) |
| **Increased number of effects ($N$)** | No direct effect | **Increases** (toward $N$) | **Increases** (per effect) |
| **Higher feed temperature** | Slight increase | Slight increase | Slight decrease |
| **Higher feed concentration** | Decreases (less water to vaporize) | No direct effect | No direct effect |
| **Lower operating pressure** | Increases (lower $T_B$) | No direct effect | Increases |
| **Higher BPE** | Decreases | Decreases | Increases |
| **Forward feed** | Easy operation | Lower economy | Larger area for last effect |
| **Backward feed** | Higher economy | Higher economy | Smaller area |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Material and Energy Flows in a Single-Effect Evaporator

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a single-effect evaporator, mapping the flow of material and energy streams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedA[FEED F] --> evaporatorA[EVAPORATOR BODY]
    steamA[STEAM S] --> coilA[STEAM COIL CALANDRIA]
    coilA --> evaporatorA
    evaporatorA --> productA[CONCENTRATED LIQUOR L]
    evaporatorA --> vaporA[SECONDARY VAPOR V]
    coilA --> condensateA[CONDENSATE]
    
    style feedA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style steamA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style evaporatorA fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900
    style productA fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
    style vaporA fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066
    style coilA fill:#ff9966,stroke:#663300
    style condensateA fill:#dddddd,stroke:#333333
```

### Stream Description
- **FEED F**: Dilute solution entering the evaporator.
- **STEAM S**: Saturated heating steam entering the calandria/coil.
- **CALANDRIA**: Heat exchanger where steam condenses and transfers latent heat.
- **CONCENTRATED LIQUOR L**: Concentrated product leaving the bottom.
- **SECONDARY VAPOR V**: Solvent vapor leaving the top (condensed or sent to next effect).
- **CONDENSATE**: Steam condensate leaving the calandria.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Multiple-Effect Evaporator (Forward Feed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feedB[FEED F] --> eff1[EFFECT 1 HIGH PRESSURE]
    steamB[STEAM S] --> eff1
    eff1 --> interm1[INTERMEDIATE V1]
    eff1 --> eff2[EFFECT 2 MEDIUM PRESSURE]
    interm1 --> eff2
    eff2 --> interm2[INTERMEDIATE V2]
    eff2 --> eff3[EFFECT 3 LOW PRESSURE]
    interm2 --> eff3
    eff3 --> productB[CONCENTRATED LIQUOR L]
    eff3 --> vaporB[FINAL VAPOR V3]
    
    style feedB fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style steamB fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style eff1 fill:#ffddcc,stroke:#993300
    style eff2 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900
    style eff3 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
    style productB fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366
    style vaporB fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066
```

### Operating Principle
- **Effect 1** operates at the **highest pressure** (lowest vacuum).
- **Effect $N$** operates at the **lowest pressure** (highest vacuum).
- **Vapor from effect $i$** is the **heating medium for effect $(i+1)$**.
- **Forward feed**: Feed and vapor flow in the **same direction** (high pressure to low pressure).

## 4.3 Comparison of Feed Arrangements — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    arr[FEED ARRANGEMENTS] --> fwd[FORWARD FEED]
    arr --> bwd[BACKWARD FEED]
    arr --> par[PARALLEL FEED]
    
    fwd --> fwd1[Feed and vapor flow in same direction]
    fwd --> fwd2[Highest T feed meets highest T steam]
    fwd --> fwd3[Most common arrangement]
    
    bwd --> bwd1[Feed and vapor flow in opposite direction]
    bwd --> bwd2[Cold feed enters last effect]
    bwd --> bwd3[Higher economy for viscous liquids]
    
    par --> par1[Feed enters each effect separately]
    par --> par2[Used for crystallizing solutions]
    par --> par3[Saturated feed to each effect]
    
    style arr fill:#ffff99,stroke:#666600
    style fwd fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300
    style bwd fill:#ccffff,stroke:#003366
    style par fill:#ff99cc,stroke:#990066
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — Heat and Mass Transfer in Multiple Effect

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[HEAT FROM STEAM Q1] --> T1[EFFECT 1]
    T1 --> V1[VAPOR V1]
    V1 --> H2[HEAT FROM VAPOR V1]
    H2 --> T2[EFFECT 2]
    T2 --> V2[VAPOR V2]
    V2 --> H3[HEAT FROM VAPOR V2]
    H3 --> T3[EFFECT 3]
    T3 --> V3[VAPOR V3]
    V3 --> cond[CONDENSER]
    cond --> vacuum[VACUUM PUMP]
    
    style H1 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#990000
    style H2 fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300
    style H3 fill:#ffff99,stroke:#666600

    style T2 fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099
    style T3 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#003366
    style cond fill:#cccccc,stroke:#333333
    style vacuum fill:#999999,stroke:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The cascade of vapor from one effect to the next is the key to high steam economy. Each kg of steam supplied can ultimately evaporate approximately $N$ kg of water in an $N$-effect system.
