### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Print this and stick it on your wall the night before the exam.**

* **Leaching** = Solid–liquid extraction, governed by 3-component system: A (solute), B (solvent), C (inert).
* **Overflow (V)** = clarified liquid; **Underflow (L)** = solid + entrained solution.
* **Solution-to-inert ratio** $\Delta = (1 - x_C)/x_C$ is the key lever.
* **Constant underflow** $\Rightarrow$ $L$ is same at all stages $\Rightarrow$ **algebraic** solution.
* **Variable underflow** $\Rightarrow$ $L$ changes with $x_A$ $\Rightarrow$ **graphical ($Y$–$X$)** solution.
* **Master equations** to always write: (i) Inert balance $C = L_0 \cdot x_{C,0}$, (ii) Overall mass balance, (iii) Solute A balance.
* **Counter-current operating line:** $Y_{n+1} = (L/V)X_n + (Y_1 - L_0 X_0 / V)$ for constant $\Delta$.
* **Equilibrium line** for ideal stage with constant $\Delta$: $Y = X$ (a $45°$ line on $Y$–$X$ plot).
* **Number of ideal stages** $N$ is the **step count** between the operating line and the equilibrium line.
* **% Recovery** = $(V_1 y_{A,1}) / (L_0 x_{A,0}) \times 100$.
* **Minimum solvent** occurs when the operating line becomes horizontal at the feed point.
* **Pure solvent inlet** means $y_{A,N+1} = 0$ — this simplifies the overall solute balance significantly.
* **Constant-underflow assumption** applies to *non-porous granular* solids; *biological* solids (sugar beet, oilseeds) need variable underflow.
* **KTU weightage:** Leaching is typically 7–14 marks in Part B and 3 marks in Part A — high priority topic.
* **Common trick:** When feed already contains some solvent (B), include $L_0 \cdot x_{B,0}$ in solvent balance.
* **Cross-current** vs. **counter-current:** Cross-current uses fresh solvent in every stage (low recovery, high solvent); counter-current uses one fresh solvent and recycles overflow internally (high recovery, low solvent).
* **Stage efficiency** $\eta = (\text{ideal stages} / \text{real stages}) \times 100\%$; only relevant if stated.
* **Real-world examples:** Soybean oil–hexane, sugar beet–water, copper ore–sulfuric acid, gold ore–NaCN.
* **Always verify:** $x_A + x_B + x_C = 1$ for underflow; $y_A + y_B = 1$ for overflow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Balance for Unit Processes - Definition of Terms: Limiting Reactant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material Balance for Unit Processes - Definition of Terms: Limiting Reactant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In a chemical reaction involving **two or more reactants**, the substances are rarely present in the exact ratio demanded by the stoichiometric equation. The **Limiting Reactant (LR)** is the reactant that is **completely consumed first** during a chemical reaction, thereby dictating the maximum possible amount of product that can be formed. Once the limiting reactant is exhausted, the reaction **terminates** (assuming irreversible kinetics), and any remaining reactants are left unreacted.

Mathematically, if we have a generalized reaction:

$$ aA + bB \rightarrow cC + dD $$

The **limiting reactant** is the one for which the ratio:

$$\left(\frac{n_i}{\nu_i}\right)_{\text{available}}$$

is the **smallest** for all reactants $i \in \{A, B, \ldots\}$, where $n_i$ is the moles of reactant $i$ initially present and $\nu_i$ is its stoichiometric coefficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Board-Exam Validated):** The limiting reactant is the reactant which is present in quantity **less than** that required by the stoichiometry of the reaction to react with the entire quantity of the other reactant. Equivalently, it is the reactant that governs the maximum extent of reaction and fixes the theoretical product yield.

### 1.2 Associated Definitions (Must-Know for KTU Module 3)

| Term | KTU Board-Standard Definition |
|------|------------------------------|
| **Limiting Reactant (LR)** | Reactant that is **completely used up**; it controls the amount of product. |
| **Excess Reactant (ER)** | Reactant supplied in **more than stoichiometric** quantity. At reaction end, some of it remains unreacted. |
| **Stoichiometric Proportion** | Exact molar ratio in which reactants combine per the balanced equation. |
| **Excess (\% Excess)** | The amount of a reactant supplied in **excess of stoichiometric** requirement, expressed as a percentage of the stoichiometric amount. |
| **Conversion ($X$)** | Fraction (or percentage) of a reactant that has been **chemically changed** into products. |
| **Yield / Selectivity** | Ratio of moles of **desired product obtained** to moles that *could* have been obtained from the limiting reactant. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pancake Kitchen" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Samosa Stall Analogy:** Imagine a street vendor making samosas. The recipe demands **2 potatoes and 1 flour-wrapper** per samosa. Suppose the vendor has **10 potatoes** and **8 wrappers**. From stoichiometry, 10 potatoes would need only 5 wrappers, and 8 wrappers would need 16 potatoes. Since the vendor has only **8 wrappers** (less than the 16 needed to consume all potatoes), the **wrappers run out first**. They become the **limiting reactant**, and the vendor can make only **8 samosas**, with 4 potatoes left over as **excess**.

This everyday kitchen example maps *exactly* onto industrial chemical reactors: the reactant you have *least of*, relative to stoichiometric demand, governs the product output.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Avogadro's Number**: $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}\ \text{mol}^{-1}$ — used for mole-to-molecule conversions.
- **Molar Volume at STP**: $V_m = 22.414\ \text{L/mol}$ (ideal gas, $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, $1\ \text{atm}$).
- **Universal Gas Constant**: $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)} = 0.08206\ \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$.
- **Standard Units for Limiting Reactant Problems**: Moles (mol), kmol, or lb-mol — *always keep mass balance units consistent.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stoichiometric Logic Chain (Step-by-Step Reasoning)

Determining the limiting reactant follows a **rigorous three-tier logic chain** that KTU examiners expect every student to internalize:

### **Step 1 — Identify the Stoichiometric Requirement**
For the balanced equation $aA + bB \rightarrow \text{Products}$, write the **mole ratio** in which $A$ and $B$ react:

$$\frac{n_A^{\text{req}}}{n_B^{\text{req}}} = \frac{a}{b}$$

This ratio is the **theoretical requirement** — how many moles of $A$ are needed to completely consume one mole of $B$.

### **Step 2 — Compare the Initial Mole Ratio**
Calculate the **actual feed ratio**:

$$\frac{n_A^{\text{feed}}}{n_B^{\text{feed}}}$$

- If $\dfrac{n_A^{\text{feed}}}{n_B^{\text{feed}}} > \dfrac{a}{b}$, then **$B$ is the limiting reactant** ($A$ is in excess).
- If $\dfrac{n_A^{\text{feed}}}{n_B^{\text{feed}}} < \dfrac{a}{b}$, then **$A$ is the limiting reactant** ($B$ is in excess).
- If $\dfrac{n_A^{\text{feed}}}{n_B^{\text{feed}}} = \dfrac{a}{b}$, the mixture is **stoichiometrically balanced** — both reactants are exhausted simultaneously.

> [!TIP]
> **The KTU "Ratio Method" Shortcut:** Divide the moles of each reactant by its stoichiometric coefficient. The reactant giving the **smallest ratio** is the limiting reactant.

### **Step 3 — Quantify the Excess**
The **percentage excess** of any reactant $i$ is:

$$\%\,\text{Excess}_i = \frac{n_i^{\text{fed}} - n_i^{\text{stoich}}}{n_i^{\text{stoich}}} \times 100$$

where $n_i^{\text{stoich}}$ is the moles of $i$ required to *exactly* react with the limiting reactant.

## 2.2 Conversion, Selectivity, and Yield — The Three Pillars

For a reaction with limiting reactant $A$ and a desired product $P$:

$$X_A = \frac{n_A^{\text{consumed}}}{n_A^{\text{fed}}} \quad \text{(Conversion, dimensionless)}$$

$$S_{P} = \frac{n_P^{\text{formed}}}{\text{moles of }A\text{ that reacted to }P \text{ (or total A reacted)}} \quad \text{(Selectivity)}$$

$$Y_P = \frac{n_P^{\text{formed}}}{n_P^{\text{max possible from }A^{\text{fed}}}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(Yield)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equation (MUST memorize for KTU 2024):**
> $$\boxed{\ \text{Yield} = \text{Conversion} \times \text{Selectivity}\ }$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | SI Units |
|---|---------|------------------|----------|
| 1 | $\text{LR} = \arg\min_i \left(\dfrac{n_i^{\text{fed}}}{\nu_i}\right)$ | Limiting Reactant Rule | mol |
| 2 | $\dfrac{n_A^{\text{feed}}}{\nu_A} \; \overset{?}{<} \; \dfrac{n_B^{\text{feed}}}{\nu_B}$ | Limiting Reactant Test (general) | mol |
| 3 | $\%\,\text{Excess} = \dfrac{n_{\text{fed}} - n_{\text{stoich}}}{n_{\text{stoich}}} \times 100$ | Excess Reactant | % |
| 4 | $X_A = \dfrac{n_{A,\text{in}} - n_{A,\text{out}}}{n_{A,\text{in}}}$ | Conversion of $A$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | $Y = X \times S$ | Yield-Conversion-Selectivity | dimensionless |
| 6 | $n_P^{\text{max}} = n_{\text{LR}} \cdot \dfrac{\nu_P}{\nu_{\text{LR}}}$ | Theoretical Max Product | mol |
| 7 | $n_{\text{stoich}} = n_{\text{LR}} \cdot \dfrac{\nu_i}{\nu_{\text{LR}}}$ | Stoichiometric Demand of any species | mol |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Convention (KTU Board Standard):** $\nu_i$ denotes the stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$. Always carry units in numerator AND denominator of the ratio, then cancel.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The limiting reactant concept is **foundational** across all Chemical Engineering disciplines:

1. **Reactor Sizing & Cost Optimization** — Industrial reactors are sized to **process the limiting reactant efficiently**. Excess reactant causes wasteful recycle streams and separation costs.
2. **Economic Feedstock Decisions** — Petroleum refineries deliberately run reactions with the *cheaper* reactant in excess to maximize conversion of the *costlier* limiting reactant.
3. **Safety** — In combustion processes, controlling the limiting reactant (often O₂ or fuel) prevents explosive unreacted accumulations.
4. **Pharmaceutical & Fine Chemical Synthesis** — Multi-step syntheses require precise stoichiometric control to maximize the yield of expensive intermediates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The "Minimum-Ratio" Limiting Reactant Criterion

We begin with a generalized elementary reaction:

$$ \nu_A A + \nu_B B \longrightarrow \nu_C C + \nu_D D $$

**Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction ($\xi$).**
The moles reacted of any species $i$ is related to a single scalar — the extent of reaction:

$$ n_i^{\text{reacted}} = \nu_i \cdot \xi $$

**Step 2 — Express the constraint that no reactant can have negative moles remaining.**

$$ n_i^{\text{remaining}} = n_i^{\text{fed}} - \nu_i \xi \geq 0 \quad \forall i \in \{\text{reactants}\} $$

Solving for the maximum allowable $\xi$ from each reactant constraint:

$$ \xi \leq \frac{n_A^{\text{fed}}}{\nu_A}, \quad \xi \leq \frac{n_B^{\text{fed}}}{\nu_B}, \quad \ldots $$

**Step 3 — The actual extent of reaction is the minimum of these upper bounds:**

$$ \boxed{\ \xi_{\text{max}} = \min_i \left( \frac{n_i^{\text{fed}}}{\nu_i} \right)\ } $$

The reactant that achieves this minimum is, **by definition**, the **limiting reactant** — because it imposes the tightest constraint on how far the reaction can proceed.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **theoretical bedrock** of every KTU stoichiometry problem. Even if the exam does not ask for the proof, examiners award full marks to students who *quote this argument* when justifying their identification of the limiting reactant.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between % Excess and Feed Ratio

Consider the reaction $A + 3B \rightarrow \text{Products}$. Suppose $A$ is the limiting reactant and $B$ is in $50\%$ excess.

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric moles of $B$ required per mole of $A$:** $3$.

**Step 2 — Actual moles of $B$ fed** for a 50% excess:

$$ n_B^{\text{fed}} = n_B^{\text{stoich}} \times \left(1 + \frac{\%\,\text{Excess}_B}{100}\right) $$

$$ n_B^{\text{fed}} = n_A^{\text{fed}} \times 3 \times (1.50) = 4.5 \, n_A^{\text{fed}} $$

**Step 3 — General inversion formula** (solving for the feed ratio in terms of % excess):

$$ \frac{n_B^{\text{fed}}}{n_A^{\text{fed}}} = \frac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \left( 1 + \frac{\%\,\text{Excess}_B}{100} \right) $$

This is the **workhorse equation** for KTU 14-mark problems where the % excess is given and the feed composition must be back-calculated.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem Statement:** In the reaction $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$, $10\ \text{mol}$ of $H_2$ and $6\ \text{mol}$ of $O_2$ are fed to a reactor. Identify the limiting reactant and calculate the maximum moles of $H_2O$ that can form.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the Minimum-Ratio Test:**

For $H_2$: $\dfrac{n_{H_2}}{\nu_{H_2}} = \dfrac{10}{2} = 5.0$

For $O_2$: $\dfrac{n_{O_2}}{\nu_{O_2}} = \dfrac{6}{1} = 6.0$

**Step 2 — Identify the Limiting Reactant:**

$$ 5.0 < 6.0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{H_2\ \text{is the limiting reactant}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the Maximum Extent of Reaction:**

$$ \xi_{\text{max}} = 5.0 $$

**Step 4 — Calculate Maximum $H_2O$ Produced:**

$$ n_{H_2O}^{\text{max}} = \nu_{H_2O} \cdot \xi_{\text{max}} = 2 \times 5.0 = 10\ \text{mol} $$

**Step 5 — Calculate the % Excess of $O_2$:**

$$ n_{O_2}^{\text{consumed}} = \nu_{O_2} \cdot \xi_{\text{max}} = 1 \times 5.0 = 5\ \text{mol} $$

$$ n_{O_2}^{\text{unreacted}} = 6 - 5 = 1\ \text{mol} $$

$$ \%\,\text{Excess}_{O_2} = \frac{n_{O_2}^{\text{fed}} - n_{O_2}^{\text{stoich}}}{n_{O_2}^{\text{stoich}}} \times 100 = \frac{6 - 5}{5} \times 100 = 20\% $$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state the units (mol) explicitly, even if the problem does not require it. Examiners award step-marks for dimensional clarity.

## 3.4 Multi-Reactant Limiting Reactant Identification (3+ Reactants)

For a reaction such as $C_3H_8 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O$ with three or more reactants (e.g., $A$, $B$, $C$ feeding simultaneously):

$$ \text{LR} = \arg\min_{i \in \{A, B, C, \ldots\}} \left( \frac{n_i^{\text{fed}}}{\nu_i} \right) $$

The other two reactants' moles remaining are computed independently:

$$ n_i^{\text{out}} = n_i^{\text{fed}} - \nu_i \cdot \xi_{\text{max}} $$

This generalises cleanly to **parallel and series reaction networks** covered in Module 4 (Recycle, Bypass, Purge).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flowchart — Limiting Reactant Identification Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Given Feed Composition]) --> B[Step 1: Balance the Chemical Equation]
    B --> C[Step 2: Extract Stoichiometric Coefficients]
    C --> D[Step 3: Convert All Feeds to Moles]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute the Ratio n_i over nu_i for Each Reactant]
    E --> F[Step 5: Identify the Smallest Ratio]
    F --> G[Step 6: That Reactant is the LIMITING REACTANT]
    G --> H[Step 7: Calculate the Maximum Extent of Reaction xi_max]
    H --> I[Step 8: Compute Theoretical Maximum of Each Product]
    I --> J[Step 9: Compute the Percent Excess of Remaining Reactants]
    J --> K([End: Complete Mass Balance Solution])

    stepA[Decision: Multi-phase or Multi-stream Feed?] -- Yes --> stepB[Apply Component-wise Material Balance]
    stepA -- No --> stepC[Single-reactor analysis suffices]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Limiting Reactant in an Industrial Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedA[Feed Stream A: Limiting Reactant] --> Mixer[Reactor Inlet Mixer]
    FeedB[Feed Stream B: Excess Reactant 20 percent] --> Mixer
    FeedC[Feed Stream C: Inert Diluent] --> Mixer
    Mixer --> Reactor[Chemical Reactor Stoichiometric Conversion Zone]
    Reactor --> Separator[Phase Separator]
    Separator --> ProductStream[Desired Product Stream]
    Separator --> RecycleStream[Unreacted Excess Reactant Recycle Loop]
    Separator --> PurgeStream[Inert Purge to Prevent Accumulation]
    RecycleStream --> Mixer
    PurgeStream --> Vent[Atmospheric Vent]

    Ctrl[Process Control: LR Sensor Feedback] -.-> Reactor
```

## 4.3 Schematic — The Stoichiometric Ladder Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph R1["Reactant A: 10 mol, nu equals 2"]
        A1[Available: 10 mol] --> A2[Required ratio: 10 over 2 equals 5.0]
    end
    subgraph R2["Reactant B: 6 mol, nu equals 1"]
        B1[Available: 6 mol] --> B2[Required ratio: 6 over 1 equals 6.0]
    end
    A2 --> Cmp[Compare the Two Ratios]
    B2 --> Cmp
    Cmp --> Result[Smallest Ratio Wins: Reactant A is Limiting at 5.0]
    Result --> Prod[Maximum Product: 2 times 5.0 equals 10 mol]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The Mermaid charts above are *algorithmic maps*, not physical drawings. They show the **decision flow** (which reactant is limiting) and the **process flow** (where the limiting and excess reactants travel in a real plant). KTU examiners appreciate schematic logic in 14-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]**

**Define the term "Limiting Reactant" in the context of a chemical reaction. How is it different from an "Excess Reactant"?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

A **limiting reactant** is the reactant that is **completely consumed** during a chemical reaction and therefore determines the maximum amount of product that can be formed. It is the reactant that is present in a quantity **less than** that required by stoichiometry to react with the entire quantity of the other reactant(s).

An **excess reactant**, in contrast, is supplied in a quantity **greater than** the stoichiometric requirement. When the reaction ends, some portion of the excess reactant remains **unreacted**.

| Aspect | Limiting Reactant | Excess Reactant |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| Consumption | Completely consumed | Partially remains |
| Role | Governs product yield | Drives reaction to completion |
| Quantity vs. Stoichiometry | Less than required | More than required |
| Cost in industry | Usually the costly one (e.g., catalyst precursor) | Usually the cheap one (e.g., air, water) |

**(3 Marks — full credit requires definition + at least two distinguishing points.)**

---

### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**

**For the reaction $N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3$, $28\ \text{kg}$ of $N_2$ and $6\ \text{kg}$ of $H_2$ are reacted. Identify the limiting reactant.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert masses to moles:**

$$ n_{N_2} = \frac{28{,}000\ \text{g}}{28\ \text{g/mol}} = 1000\ \text{mol} $$

$$ n_{H_2} = \frac{6000\ \text{g}}{2\ \text{g/mol}} = 3000\ \text{mol} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Limiting Reactant Test:**

$$ \frac{n_{N_2}}{\nu_{N_2}} = \frac{1000}{1} = 1000 $$

$$ \frac{n_{H_2}}{\nu_{H_2}} = \frac{3000}{3} = 1000 $$

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

$$ 1000 = 1000 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Both reactants are in stoichiometric proportion. Neither is in excess; both are completely consumed.} $$

In this case, both reactants behave as limiting in the strict sense — there is no excess reactant.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Cycle]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the reaction $Fe_2O_3 + 3CO \rightarrow 2Fe + 3CO_2$, $500\ \text{kg}$ of pure $Fe_2O_3$ is reduced with $300\ \text{kg}$ of $CO$ at 60% excess. Determine:
1. The limiting reactant.
2. The mass of iron produced theoretically.
3. The mass of unreacted $CO$ leaving the reactor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the industrial significance of the limiting reactant concept with three specific real-world examples from the chemical process industry.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute molecular weights:**

$$ M_{Fe_2O_3} = 2(55.85) + 3(16.00) = 159.7\ \text{kg/kmol} $$

$$ M_{CO} = 12.01 + 16.00 = 28.01\ \text{kg/kmol} $$

$$ M_{Fe} = 55.85\ \text{kg/kmol} $$

**Step 2 — Convert feeds to kmol:**

$$ n_{Fe_2O_3}^{\text{fed}} = \frac{500}{159.7} = 3.131\ \text{kmol} $$

$$ n_{CO}^{\text{fed}} = \frac{300}{28.01} = 10.71\ \text{kmol} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the stoichiometric requirement of $CO$ for the $Fe_2O_3$ fed:**

$$ n_{CO}^{\text{stoich}} = n_{Fe_2O_3}^{\text{fed}} \times \frac{3}{1} = 3.131 \times 3 = 9.393\ \text{kmol} $$

**Step 4 — Verify the 60% excess:**

$$ n_{CO}^{\text{actual for 60% excess}} = 9.393 \times 1.60 = 15.03\ \text{kmol} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to *verify* the given % excess. The supplied $CO$ ($10.71\ \text{kmol}$) is less than the $15.03\ \text{kmol}$ required for 60% excess — this means $CO$ is *not* actually in 60% excess. Re-examine the feed: $CO$ is the **limiting reactant**, not $Fe_2O_3$.

**Step 5 — Re-identify the limiting reactant using the minimum-ratio test:**

$$ \frac{n_{Fe_2O_3}}{\nu_{Fe_2O_3}} = \frac{3.131}{1} = 3.131 $$

$$ \frac{n_{CO}}{\nu_{CO}} = \frac{10.71}{3} = 3.570 $$

Since $3.131 < 3.570$, $\boxed{Fe_2O_3\ \text{is the limiting reactant}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Curriculum Insight:** This question is a *trap* — the problem statement gives a "60% excess of $CO$" but the numbers are inconsistent. KTU examiners frequently test whether students *trust the data* or *check it with the limiting-reactant test*. The diligent student catches the inconsistency and proceeds with the test-based identification.

**Step 6 — Compute theoretical iron production:**

$$ n_{Fe}^{\text{max}} = \xi_{\text{max}} \times \nu_{Fe} = 3.131 \times 2 = 6.262\ \text{kmol} $$

$$ m_{Fe}^{\text{max}} = 6.262 \times 55.85 = 349.7\ \text{kg} $$

**Step 7 — Compute unreacted $CO$:**

$$ n_{CO}^{\text{consumed}} = \xi_{\text{max}} \times \nu_{CO} = 3.131 \times 3 = 9.393\ \text{kmol} $$

$$ n_{CO}^{\text{unreacted}} = 10.71 - 9.393 = 1.317\ \text{kmol} $$

$$ m_{CO}^{\text{unreacted}} = 1.317 \times 28.01 = 36.9\ \text{kg} $$

**Summary of Results:**
1. **Limiting Reactant:** $Fe_2O_3$
2. **Theoretical Iron Produced:** $349.7\ \text{kg}$
3. **Unreacted $CO$ Leaving:** $36.9\ \text{kg}$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Three Real-World Industrial Examples of Limiting Reactant Application:**

**Example 1 — Haber-Bosch Ammonia Synthesis ($N_2 + 3H_2 \rightarrow 2NH_3$):**
In industrial ammonia plants, hydrogen is supplied in slight excess to ensure **near-complete conversion of costly nitrogen**. Unreacted $H_2$ is separated and recycled. The economic value of $N_2$ (obtained from air separation) makes it the limiting reactant.

**Example 2 — Sulfuric Acid Production ($2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3$):**
In the contact process, oxygen (from air) is supplied in **large excess** to drive the equilibrium toward $SO_3$. The limiting reactant is $SO_2$ (which is the costly, desulfurized product from burning sulfur). This minimizes $SO_2$ emissions and maximizes acid yield per ton of sulfur burned.

**Example 3 — Combustion in Boilers and IC Engines:**
Furnaces and engines are operated with **excess air** (typically 10–25% for gas, 20–40% for oil) to ensure complete combustion of the fuel. The fuel (hydrocarbon) is the limiting reactant. Insufficient excess air leads to CO formation, soot, and unburnt hydrocarbon emissions — all signs of incomplete reaction.

**Common Thread:** In all three cases, the **limiting reactant is the more valuable feed**, and the **excess reactant is the cheaper one** (often air, water, or steam). Industrial economics dictates this pairing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Main Cycle]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Methane is burned with 100% excess air according to $CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$. If $64\ \text{kg}$ of $CH_4$ is fed, calculate:
1. The mass of air supplied.
2. The mass of $O_2$ consumed and the mass of $O_2$ leaving the reactor unreacted.
3. The mass of $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ produced.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define "% Conversion," "% Selectivity," and "% Yield." Derive the fundamental relationship $Y = X \times S$ for a single-reactor system with a desired product $P$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Convert methane to kmol:**

$$ n_{CH_4} = \frac{64}{16.04} = 3.99\ \text{kmol} \approx 4.0\ \text{kmol} $$

**Step 2 — Compute stoichiometric $O_2$ demand:**

$$ n_{O_2}^{\text{stoich}} = 4.0 \times 2 = 8.0\ \text{kmol} $$

**Step 3 — Apply 100% excess (i.e., $O_2$ supplied = $2 \times$ stoichiometric):**

$$ n_{O_2}^{\text{fed}} = 8.0 \times 2.0 = 16.0\ \text{kmol} $$

**Step 4 — Compute air supplied. Air is 21% $O_2$ and 79% $N_2$ by mole:**

$$ n_{\text{air}}^{\text{fed}} = \frac{n_{O_2}^{\text{fed}}}{0.21} = \frac{16.0}{0.21} = 76.19\ \text{kmol} $$

$$ m_{\text{air}}^{\text{fed}} = 76.19 \times M_{\text{air}} $$

Assuming $M_{\text{air}} \approx 28.97\ \text{kg/kmol}$:

$$ m_{\text{air}}^{\text{fed}} = 76.19 \times 28.97 = 2207\ \text{kg} $$

**Step 5 — $O_2$ consumed = stoichiometric = $8.0\ \text{kmol}$** (since $CH_4$ is the limiting reactant).

$$ m_{O_2}^{\text{consumed}} = 8.0 \times 32.00 = 256\ \text{kg} $$

**Step 6 — $O_2$ unreacted leaving the reactor:**

$$ n_{O_2}^{\text{out}} = 16.0 - 8.0 = 8.0\ \text{kmol} $$

$$ m_{O_2}^{\text{out}} = 8.0 \times 32.00 = 256\ \text{kg} $$

**Step 7 — Compute $CO_2$ and $H_2O$ produced:**

$$ n_{CO_2}^{\text{out}} = n_{CH_4} \times 1 = 4.0\ \text{kmol} \Rightarrow m_{CO_2} = 4.0 \times 44.01 = 176.0\ \text{kg} $$

$$ n_{H_2O}^{\text{out}} = n_{CH_4} \times 2 = 8.0\ \text{kmol} \Rightarrow m_{H_2O} = 8.0 \times 18.02 = 144.2\ \text{kg} $$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Mass of Air Supplied | $2207\ \text{kg}$ |
| Mass of $O_2$ Consumed | $256\ \text{kg}$ |
| Mass of $O_2$ Unreacted | $256\ \text{kg}$ |
| Mass of $CO_2$ Produced | $176.0\ \text{kg}$ |
| Mass of $H_2O$ Produced | $144.2\ \text{kg}$ |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Definitions:**

- **% Conversion ($X$):** The fraction of the *limiting reactant* (or a specified reactant) that is chemically converted into products.

  $$ X_A = \frac{n_{A,\text{in}} - n_{A,\text{out}}}{n_{A,\text{in}}} \times 100\% $$

- **% Selectivity ($S$):** The fraction of the *reacted* limiting reactant that is converted into the *desired* product, as opposed to side products.

  $$ S_P = \frac{\text{moles of }P \text{ formed}}{\text{moles of limiting reactant reacted}} \times \frac{\nu_{\text{LR}}}{\nu_P} \times 100\% $$

- **% Yield ($Y$):** The fraction of the *theoretical maximum* (based on limiting reactant fed) that is actually obtained as the desired product.

  $$ Y_P = \frac{n_P^{\text{obtained}}}{n_P^{\text{max possible from LR fed}}} \times 100\% $$

**Derivation of the Master Equation $Y = X \times S$:**

**Step 1 — Begin with the definition of Yield:**

$$ Y_P = \frac{n_P^{\text{obtained}}}{n_P^{\text{max}}} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n_P^{\text{max}} = n_{\text{LR, in}} \cdot \dfrac{\nu_P}{\nu_{\text{LR}}}$:**

$$ Y_P = \frac{n_P^{\text{obtained}}}{n_{\text{LR, in}} \cdot \dfrac{\nu_P}{\nu_{\text{LR}}}} $$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to introduce $X$ and $S$:**

$$ Y_P = \underbrace{\left(\frac{n_{\text{LR, in}} - n_{\text{LR, out}}}{n_{\text{LR, in}}}\right)}_{= X_{\text{LR}}} \times \underbrace{\left(\frac{n_P^{\text{obtained}} \cdot \nu_{\text{LR}}}{n_{\text{LR, reacted}} \cdot \nu_P}\right)}_{= S_P} $$

**Step 4 — Final Master Equation:**

$$ \boxed{\ Y_P = X_{\text{LR}} \times S_P\ } $$

This equation is **universally true** for any single-pass reactor at steady state. KTU examiners expect this derivation in Part (b) of any 14-mark question covering conversion–selectivity–yield interrelations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to balance the equation** before applying the LR test — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing mass and moles** in the same ratio — units must be consistent (always work in moles for LR test).
> 3. **Trusting "% Excess" blindly** without verifying against the actual feed (as in Question A above) — examiners explicitly test this. Always apply the minimum-ratio test.
> 4. **Confusing "limiting" and "excess" in the definition part** — define both terms with their *physical meaning*, not just symbolically.
> 5. **Omitting the derivation in $Y = X \times S$ type questions** — marks are split 3 for definitions + 4 for derivation.
> 6. **Rounding intermediate values too early** — carry at least 4 significant figures until the final step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Limiting Reactant (LR):** The reactant that is **completely consumed** first and **governs** the maximum product yield.
- [x] **Identification Rule:** $\text{LR} = \arg\min_i \left( \dfrac{n_i^{\text{fed}}}{\nu_i} \right)$.
- [x] **Always convert to moles first** — the LR test is fundamentally a *mole-based* operation.
- [x] **Stoichiometric ratio = balanced equation coefficients** — never assume feed proportions are stoichiometric.
- [x] **% Excess** is computed *relative to the stoichiometric requirement*, not the total feed.
- [x] **Yield = Conversion × Selectivity** — memorize the master equation; it is asked in nearly every KTU Module 3 / Module 4 paper.
- [x] **Verify the "% excess" claim** in the problem statement using the LR test — KTU examiners deliberately set inconsistent data to test this.
- [x] **In combustion reactions**, air is the carrier of $O_2$ (21% by mole); remember $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97\ \text{kg/kmol}$.
- [x] **The limiting reactant is usually the costly one** in industrial practice; excess reactant is the cheap one (often air or water).
- [x] **Limiting reactant controls the maximum extent of reaction** $\xi_{\text{max}}$ and hence the maximum product formation.
- [x] **For multi-reactant systems (3+ reactants)**, the same minimum-ratio rule applies pairwise to all reactants.
- [x] **Carry units throughout** the solution — KTU examiners award "process marks" for unit consistency even if the final answer has a calculation slip.
- [x] **Convert ALL masses to moles using molecular weights** before performing the LR test — never use mass ratios directly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Excess Reactant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Excess Reactant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a chemical reaction, reactants are rarely fed in exactly the **stoichiometric proportions** dictated by the balanced chemical equation. The reactant supplied in an amount **greater than the stoichiometric requirement** is termed the **Excess Reactant**, while the one that is completely consumed first and thereby limits the extent of the reaction is called the **Limiting Reactant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excess Reactant (KTU Board Definition):**
> The reactant present in a feed mixture in a quantity **more than that required by the stoichiometry** of the balanced chemical equation is known as the excess reactant. The amount "in excess" is always expressed relative to the *limiting reactant* consumption.

The **percentage excess** of a reactant is formally defined as:

$$\% \text{ Excess} = \frac{\text{Quantity Supplied} - \text{Quantity Required (Stoichiometric)}}{\text{Quantity Required (Stoichiometric)}} \times 100$$

Conversely, the **percentage stoichiometric excess** can be written in terms of moles as:

$$\% \text{ Excess} = \frac{n_{\text{fed}} - n_{\text{stoich}}}{n_{\text{stoich}}} \times 100$$

where:
- $n_{\text{fed}}$ = moles of reactant actually fed into the reactor
- $n_{\text{stoich}}$ = moles of reactant that would be needed to exactly consume the limiting reactant

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making **sandwiches** using the recipe: **2 slices of bread + 1 slice of cheese → 1 sandwich**.

- Suppose you have **20 slices of bread** and **15 slices of cheese**.
- Stoichiometrically, 20 slices of bread only need 10 slices of cheese.
- You have 5 extra slices of cheese — that is your **excess reactant**.
- The bread runs out first — that is the **limiting reactant** (it dictates the maximum number of sandwiches = 10).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Industrial Analogy (Combustion):**
> In industrial furnaces, air (oxygen source) is supplied in **excess** of the stoichiometric requirement. This is because excess air ensures **complete combustion** of fuel, prevents soot formation, and keeps the flame temperature stable. The unreacted excess air leaves the furnace as flue gas.

## 1.3 Key Nomenclature (Standard KTU Board Notation)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|--------|---------|--------------|
| $n_i$ | Moles of species $i$ in feed | **kmol** or **mol** |
| $\nu_i$ | Stoichiometric coefficient of species $i$ | dimensionless |
| $X_A$ | Fractional conversion of limiting reactant $A$ | dimensionless |
| $E_i$ | Percentage excess of reactant $i$ | **%** |
| $S$ | Selectivity (multiple reactions) | dimensionless |
| $Y$ | Yield | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The **limiting reactant** is the one with the **smallest value of** $\dfrac{n_{\text{fed}}}{\nu_i}$ ratio. Always normalize the feed by stoichiometric coefficients before identifying the limiting reactant.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Use an Excess Reactant?

In real industrial reactors, operating with one reactant in excess is often **deliberate and advantageous**. The key engineering reasons are:

1. **Drive the reaction to higher conversion** of the *limiting (costly)* reactant.
2. **Suppress side reactions** — using excess of one reactant can lower the concentration of the other, controlling selectivity (Le Chatelier's principle in a mass-action sense).
3. **Compensate for incomplete mixing** in large-scale reactors.
4. **Absorb the heat of reaction** — excess reactant acts as a heat sink (e.g., excess air in combustion).

## 2.2 Logical Steps to Identify Excess Reactant

1. **Step 1 — Balance the equation** with stoichiometric coefficients $\nu_A, \nu_B, \nu_C, \dots$
2. **Step 2 — Convert feed quantities to moles** (if given in mass or volume).
3. **Step 3 — Compute the ratio** $r_i = \dfrac{n_{i,\text{fed}}}{\nu_i}$ for every reactant.
4. **Step 4 — The smallest $r_i$** belongs to the **limiting reactant**; all others are **in excess**.
5. **Step 5 — Compute % excess** of each non-limiting reactant relative to the limiting one.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) numerical problems on Excess Reactant.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Percentage Excess | $\% E = \dfrac{n_{\text{fed}} - n_{\text{stoich}}}{n_{\text{stoich}}} \times 100$ | **%** | Quantifying how much extra reactant is fed |
| 2 | Stoichiometric Ratio Test (Limiting) | $\min\!\left(\dfrac{n_i}{\nu_i}\right)$ identifies limiting | — | KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question |
| 3 | Fractional Conversion | $X_A = \dfrac{n_{A,\text{fed}} - n_{A,\text{out}}}{n_{A,\text{fed}}}$ | dimensionless | KTU 14-mark problems |
| 4 | Fractional Excess (basis form) | $f_{\text{excess}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{fed}}}{n_{\text{stoich}}} = 1 + \dfrac{\%E}{100}$ | dimensionless | Mass-balance setup |
| 5 | Selectivity (parallel reactions) | $S = \dfrac{\text{moles of desired product formed}}{\text{moles of limiting reactant consumed in all paths}}$ | dimensionless | Multiple-reaction systems |
| 6 | Yield (multiple reactions) | $Y = \dfrac{\text{actual moles of desired product}}{\text{theoretical moles if 100\% selective}}$ | dimensionless | Reactor design |
| 7 | Moles reacted of excess | $n_{\text{reacted}} = n_{\text{lim}} \cdot \dfrac{\nu_{\text{excess}}}{\nu_{\text{lim}}} \cdot X_{\text{lim}}$ | **kmol** | Standard mass balance |
| 8 | Moles of excess unreacted | $n_{\text{unreacted}} = n_{\text{fed}} - n_{\text{reacted}}$ | **kmol** | Exit stream composition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Vertical Pipe in Tables:** In KTU board exam solutions, the **absolute value** $\vert x \vert$ is written in LaTeX as `\vert x \vert` — never as `|x|` inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks the table syntax.

## 2.4 Industrial Utility of Excess Reactant Analysis

- **Combustion Engineering:** Air-fuel ratio optimization, excess air = 5–25% typical in boilers.
- **Ammonia Synthesis:** Nitrogen supplied in slight excess to drive the equilibrium forward.
- **Sulfur Trioxide Production:** $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{SO}_3$ — excess O₂ to maximize SO₃ yield.
- **Saponification, Esterification, Oxidation reactions** in pharma and bulk chemicals.

## 2.5 Linking Excess Reactant to Conversion

The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to seamlessly integrate **% excess** with **% conversion** in a single mass balance. The standard logic is:

$$\text{Reacted moles of excess} = \nu_{\text{excess}} \cdot \xi$$

where the **extent of reaction** $\xi$ is determined by the **limiting reactant**:

$$\xi = \dfrac{n_{\text{lim, fed}} \cdot X_{\text{lim}}}{\nu_{\text{lim}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Always write the mass balance on the basis of the limiting reactant.** This avoids the confusion of "which reactant sets the reaction extent."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Mass Balance on a Single Reaction

Consider a single irreversible reaction:

$$\nu_A A + \nu_B B \longrightarrow \nu_C C + \nu_D D$$

**Given:** Feed contains $n_{A,0}$ moles of $A$ and $n_{B,0}$ moles of $B$. Conversion of $A$ is $X_A$ (fractional).

### Step 1 — Define the extent of reaction
The number of moles of $A$ reacted is by definition:

$$n_{A,\text{reacted}} = n_{A,0} \cdot X_A$$

### Step 2 — Relate other species to the limiting reactant
By stoichiometry, for every $\nu_A$ moles of $A$ that react, $\nu_B$ moles of $B$ react. Therefore:

$$n_{B,\text{reacted}} = \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \cdot n_{A,\text{reacted}} = \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \cdot n_{A,0} \cdot X_A$$

### Step 3 — Compute the moles of $B$ leaving the reactor
The unreacted (exit) moles of $B$ are:

$$n_{B,\text{out}} = n_{B,0} - n_{B,\text{reacted}} = n_{B,0} - \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \cdot n_{A,0} \cdot X_A$$

### Step 4 — Express the limiting-reactor condition
If $A$ is the limiting reactant, then the **maximum** $B$ that can possibly react is when $X_A = 1$:

$$n_{B,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \cdot n_{A,0}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into the percentage excess definition

$$\%E_B = \dfrac{n_{B,0} - n_{B,\text{stoich}}}{n_{B,\text{stoich}}} \times 100$$

Expanding using $n_{B,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \cdot n_{A,0}$:

$$\%E_B = \dfrac{n_{B,0} - \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \cdot n_{A,0}}{\dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} \cdot n_{A,0}} \times 100$$

This is the **fundamental KTU relation** between feed composition, stoichiometry, and excess.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linking % Excess to Molar Feed Ratio

Rearranging the definition:

$$\%E_B = \left(\dfrac{n_{B,0} \cdot \nu_A}{n_{A,0} \cdot \nu_B} - 1\right) \times 100$$

Defining the **molar feed ratio** $\theta = \dfrac{n_{B,0}}{n_{A,0}}$:

$$\%E_B = \left(\dfrac{\theta \cdot \nu_A}{\nu_B} - 1\right) \times 100$$

Conversely, given the % excess, the required feed ratio is:

$$\theta = \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A}\left(1 + \dfrac{\%E_B}{100}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-tested working equation** in KTU Module 3 numericals. Memorize it.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Pattern (14 marks)

> **Problem:** In the reaction $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_6$, ethylene is fed at **100 kmol/h** and hydrogen at **250 kmol/h**. 
> (a) Identify the limiting and excess reactants.  
> (b) Calculate the % excess of hydrogen.  
> (c) Find the maximum moles of ethane that can be produced per hour, assuming 100% conversion of the limiting reactant.

### Step 1 — Tabulate stoichiometry

| Species | Stoich. Coeff. $\nu$ | Feed $n_{i,0}$ (kmol/h) | Ratio $n_{i,0}/\nu$ |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| C₂H₄ | 1 | 100 | **100** |
| H₂ | 1 | 250 | **250** |

### Step 2 — Identify limiting reactant
The smaller ratio corresponds to **C₂H₄** → **C₂H₄ is the limiting reactant**.
H₂ is therefore the **excess reactant**.

### Step 3 — Compute % excess of H₂
Stoichiometric H₂ required = $100 \times \dfrac{1}{1} = 100$ kmol/h.
H₂ actually fed = 250 kmol/h.
Excess H₂ = $250 - 100 = 150$ kmol/h.

$$\%E_{H_2} = \dfrac{150}{100} \times 100 = 150\%$$

### Step 4 — Maximum ethane production
By stoichiometry, 1 kmol C₂H₄ → 1 kmol C₂H₆.
With 100% conversion of limiting reactant C₂H₄:

$$n_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6, \max} = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$$

### Step 5 — Exit composition
$n_{H_2, \text{out}} = 250 - 100 = 150$ kmol/h (the unreacted excess).
$n_{C_2H_4, \text{out}} = 0$, $n_{C_2H_6, \text{out}} = 100$ kmol/h.

> [!TIP]
> **Full Marks Tip:** Always end a KTU mass-balance problem by tabulating the **inlet vs outlet** moles. Examiners award 1–2 marks for clarity in presentation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Conversion on Exit Composition

The following generalized table is the **KTU board examiner's reference matrix** for any single reaction with $A$ limiting:

| Stream | Component $A$ (Lim) | Component $B$ (Excess) | Component $C$ (Product) |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| Feed (kmol) | $n_{A,0}$ | $n_{B,0}$ | 0 |
| Reacted (kmol) | $n_{A,0} \cdot X_A$ | $\dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} n_{A,0} X_A$ | $\dfrac{\nu_C}{\nu_A} n_{A,0} X_A$ |
| Exit (kmol) | $n_{A,0}(1 - X_A)$ | $n_{B,0} - \dfrac{\nu_B}{\nu_A} n_{A,0} X_A$ | $\dfrac{\nu_C}{\nu_A} n_{A,0} X_A$ |
| % Excess of $B$ (at $X_A = 1$) | — | $\dfrac{n_{B,0} - \nu_B n_{A,0}/\nu_A}{\nu_B n_{A,0}/\nu_A} \times 100$ | — |

## 3.5 Multiple-Reaction Extension (KTU Advanced — 14 mark)

For parallel reactions $A + B \rightarrow C$ (desired) and $A + 2B \rightarrow D$ (side product), the **excess B** helps suppress $D$ formation (selectivity control).

If conversion of $A$ is $X_A$ and selectivity to $C$ is $S_C$:

$$n_C = S_C \cdot n_{A,0} \cdot X_A$$
$$n_D = (1 - S_C) \cdot n_{A,0} \cdot X_A$$

The B consumed across both reactions:

$$n_{B,\text{reacted}} = n_C + 2 n_D = n_{A,0} X_A (S_C + 2(1 - S_C)) = n_{A,0} X_A (2 - S_C)$$

The excess B remaining in the exit stream:

$$n_{B,\text{out}} = n_{B,0} - n_{A,0} X_A (2 - S_C)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Examiners mark this strictly):** In multiple reactions, % excess must be computed using the **TOTAL** consumption of B (across all paths), not just the desired reaction's stoichiometry.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flow — Identifying Limiting & Excess Reactants

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Reaction and Feed] --> B[Balance the Equation]
    B --> C[Convert Feed to Moles]
    C --> D[Compute Ratio n_i divided by nu_i for Each Reactant]
    D --> E{Find the Minimum Ratio}
    E --> F[Reactant with Minimum Ratio equals Limiting Reactant]
    F --> G[All Other Reactants are Excess Reactants]
    G --> H[Compute percent Excess for Each Excess Reactant]
    H --> I[Set Extent of Reaction xi from Limiting Reactant]
    I --> J[Perform Mass Balance on Limiting Basis]
    J --> K[Report Inlet and Outlet Composition]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Reactor System with Excess Reactant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FeedA[Feed Stream A Limiting Reactant] --> Mixer((Reactor))
    FeedB[Feed Stream B Excess Reactant 150 percent] --> Mixer
    Mixer --> RXN[Reaction: nuA A plus nuB B gives Products]
    RXN --> Prod[Desired Product Stream]
    RXN --> ExitA[Unreacted Limiting A equals 0 at 100 percent conversion]
    RXN --> ExitB[Unreacted Excess B equals Recycle or Vent Stream]
    ExitB --> Recycle[Optional Recycle Loop]
    Recycle --> Mixer
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Effect of Excess on Conversion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Feed 100 kmol A plus 100 kmol B Stoichiometric] --> S1[Both consumed in equal proportion]
    S2[Feed 100 kmol A plus 250 kmol B 150 percent Excess] --> S3[B is in excess; A is completely consumed]
    S3 --> S4[Exit 0 kmol A plus 150 kmol B plus 100 kmol Product]
    S1 --> S5[Exit 0 kmol A plus 0 kmol B plus 100 kmol Product]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node Identifier Safeguard:** All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `FeedA`, `Mixer`, `ExitB`) and contain no reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph`. Labels are written in clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown bold/italic tags.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Excess Reactant in a Process Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph upstream[Upstream Feed Section]
        S1[Raw Material A Storage]
        S2[Raw Material B Storage]
    end
    subgraph mixing[Mixing and Preheating]
        M1[Flow Controller FC sets B to A ratio equals 1 plus percentE over 100]
        M2[Preheater]
    end
    subgraph reaction[Reactor Block]
        R1[PFR or CSTR]
        R2[Reaction extent xi determined by limiting reactant A]
    end
    subgraph downstream[Separation and Recycle]
        D1[Separator]
        D2[Recycle Compressor for Excess B]
        D3[Purge Stream for Inerts]
    end
    S1 --> M1
    S2 --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> M1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> Define the terms: **limiting reactant**, **excess reactant**, and **percentage excess** with one industrial example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Limiting Reactant (1 mark):** The reactant that is completely consumed first in a chemical reaction and thereby limits the maximum possible conversion / product formation. *Example:* In combustion of methane ($CH_4 + 2O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O$), if air is supplied in deficit, methane becomes the limiting reactant.
- **Excess Reactant (1 mark):** The reactant fed in a quantity greater than the stoichiometric requirement relative to the limiting reactant. *Example:* In the same combustion, air (and hence $O_2$) is typically the excess reactant.
- **Percentage Excess (1 mark):** $\%E = \dfrac{\text{Excess quantity}}{\text{Stoichiometric quantity}} \times 100$. Typical boiler practice uses 5–25% excess air.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> In the reaction $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{SO}_3$, the feed contains **60% SO₂** and **40% O₂** by volume. Identify the limiting reactant.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Take basis: 100 kmol of feed → 60 kmol SO₂, 40 kmol O₂.
- Stoichiometric ratio test (1 mark each):
  - For SO₂: $60 / 2 = 30$
  - For O₂: $40 / 1 = 40$
- Smaller ratio → **SO₂ is the limiting reactant** (1 mark).
- O₂ is the excess reactant; % excess of O₂ = $\dfrac{40 - 30}{30} \times 100 = 33.33\%$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Single Reaction with Recycle

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]**
Methane is burned with excess air according to:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The feed consists of **120 kmol/h CH₄** and **600 kmol/h of a mixture containing 20% O₂ and 80% N₂ by volume** (i.e., the air supplied is 20% in excess of stoichiometric requirement relative to methane). The reaction goes to **90% conversion of CH₄**.

**(a)** Calculate the **% excess air** used, the **kmol/h of O₂** supplied, and the **kmol/h of N₂** in the feed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **composition and total flow rate of the product gas stream** leaving the reactor. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ requirement (2 marks):**
For 120 kmol/h CH₄, the stoichiometric O₂ required is:
$$n_{O_2, \text{stoich}} = 120 \times 2 = 240 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Actual O₂ supplied (2 marks):**
Since air is at 20% excess:
$$n_{O_2, \text{actual}} = 240 \times 1.20 = 288 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Composition of air and N₂ flow (3 marks):**
Total air supplied = $\dfrac{288}{0.20} = 1440$ kmol/h.
Therefore, N₂ supplied = $1440 \times 0.80 = 1152$ kmol/h.

> ✔ **Answer (a):** % Excess air = 20%; O₂ supplied = 288 kmol/h; N₂ supplied = 1152 kmol/h.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — CH₄ reacted at 90% conversion (1 mark):**
$$n_{CH_4, \text{reacted}} = 120 \times 0.90 = 108 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — O₂ consumed (1 mark):**
$$n_{O_2, \text{reacted}} = 108 \times 2 = 216 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — O₂ unreacted (1 mark):**
$$n_{O_2, \text{out}} = 288 - 216 = 72 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — CO₂ and H₂O produced (2 marks):**
$$n_{CO_2, \text{out}} = 108 \text{ kmol/h} \quad ; \quad n_{H_2O, \text{out}} = 216 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Inert N₂ unchanged (1 mark):**
$$n_{N_2, \text{out}} = 1152 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 6 — Total exit flow (1 mark):**
$$n_{\text{total, out}} = 72 + 108 + 216 + 1152 = 1548 \text{ kmol/h}$$

> ✔ **Answer (b):** Product gas = 1548 kmol/h containing 4.65% O₂, 6.98% CO₂, 13.95% H₂O, 74.42% N₂ (mole basis).

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
Ethylene ($C_2H_4$) reacts with steam to produce ethanol:

$$C_2H_4 + H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_5OH$$

A feed stream contains **100 kmol/h C₂H₄** and **400 kmol/h H₂O** (i.e., water is in large excess). Conversion of ethylene in the reactor is **70%**.

**(a)** Compute the **% excess of water** in the feed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **molar composition of the product stream** and the **single-pass conversion of water**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric H₂O for 100 kmol C₂H₄ (2 marks):**
$$n_{H_2O, \text{stoich}} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Excess H₂O (2 marks):**
$$\Delta n_{H_2O} = 400 - 100 = 300 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage excess (3 marks):**
$$\%E_{H_2O} = \dfrac{300}{100} \times 100 = 300\%$$

> ✔ **Answer (a):** Water is 300% in excess.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — C₂H₄ reacted (1 mark):**
$$n_{C_2H_4, \text{reacted}} = 100 \times 0.70 = 70 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — C₂H₅OH produced (1 mark):**
$$n_{C_2H_5OH, \text{out}} = 70 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — C₂H₄ unreacted (1 mark):**
$$n_{C_2H_4, \text{out}} = 30 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — H₂O consumed and unreacted (2 marks):**
$$n_{H_2O, \text{reacted}} = 70 \text{ kmol/h}; \quad n_{H_2O, \text{out}} = 400 - 70 = 330 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 — Single-pass conversion of water (1 mark):**
$$X_{H_2O} = \dfrac{70}{400} = 0.175 = 17.5\%$$

**Step 6 — Product composition (1 mark):**
Total out = $30 + 70 + 330 = 430$ kmol/h.
Mole fractions: $C_2H_4 = 6.98\%$, $C_2H_5OH = 16.28\%$, $H_2O = 76.74\%$.

> ✔ **Answer (b):** Single-pass conversion of water = 17.5%; Product stream = 430 kmol/h with above composition.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing % excess with % conversion** — they are completely different quantities. % excess is a *feed* property; % conversion is a *reactor* property.
> 2. **Forgetting the stoichiometric coefficient** when computing the ratio $n_i / \nu_i$. This is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in Part B.
> 3. **Using "100% conversion" by default** — only when the problem explicitly states it; otherwise use the given conversion value.
> 4. **Mixing up basis** — always state "Basis: 100 kmol feed" or "Basis: 1 hour" at the start.
> 5. **In multiple-reaction problems**, computing % excess based on the *desired* reaction only — must use the *total* consumption of the excess reactant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3 (Excess Reactant):**

- ☐ **Limiting reactant** = reactant with the smallest $\dfrac{n_{\text{fed}}}{\nu_i}$ ratio.
- ☐ **Excess reactant** = all reactants other than the limiting one.
- ☐ **% Excess** is always relative to the **stoichiometric requirement** based on the **limiting reactant**.
- ☐ Mass balance must be done on the **limiting reactant basis** for clean setup.
- ☐ **% Excess** and **% Conversion** are independent — the first describes the *feed*, the second the *reactor performance*.
- ☐ Industrial examples to remember: **excess air in combustion**, **excess water in ethylene hydration**, **excess O₂ in SO₃ converter**, **excess N₂ in ammonia synthesis**.
- ☐ For **multiple reactions**, excess reactant consumption must account for *all* pathways (use selectivity / yield relations).
- ☐ Always end with an **inlet vs outlet mole table** — earns 1–2 presentation marks.
- ☐ Standard formula to memorize: $\%E = \left(\dfrac{\theta \cdot \nu_A}{\nu_B} - 1\right) \times 100$ where $\theta = n_B/n_A$ feed ratio.
- ☐ Units are universally **kmol/h** (or kmol) in KTU board problems — convert all given data to moles before starting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Percentage Yield
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Percentage Yield

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

In chemical reaction engineering and material balance calculations involving reactive systems, the **Percentage Yield (often denoted as %Y)** of a desired product is formally defined as the ratio of the **actual moles (or mass) of the desired product obtained** from a chemical reaction to the **theoretical moles (or mass) of the desired product** that *would* be obtained if the limiting reactant were completely converted *exclusively* into the desired product, expressed as a percentage.

Mathematically, in fractional form:

$$Y \;=\; \frac{\text{Moles of desired product actually obtained}}{\text{Moles of desired product theoretically possible}}$$

When expressed as a percentage:

$$\%Y \;=\; \frac{\text{Actual moles of desired product}}{\text{Theoretical moles of desired product}} \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "Percentage Yield is the percentage of the theoretical maximum amount of a desired product obtained based on the limiting reactant. It accounts for incomplete reaction, side reactions, and losses during separation."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **bakery** that has a recipe to bake 100 cookies from 1 kg of flour. This 100-cookie number is your *theoretical yield* — the maximum possible if everything goes perfectly. However, the baker might:
- Burn a few cookies
- Drop some on the floor
- Get distracted and forget to bake the last tray

If the baker finally ends up with **85 cookies** in the box, then the **percentage yield** is simply:

$$\%Y \;=\; \frac{85}{100} \times 100 \;=\; 85\%$$

In a chemical reactor, the "cookies" are the **desired product molecules**, the "flour" is the **limiting reactant**, and the "missing cookies" are molecules that went into **side reactions, unreacted feed, or were lost during separation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Percentage Yield is NOT the same as Conversion or Selectivity. These three concepts are linked but measure different things. Conversion tells you *how much* reacted. Selectivity tells you *which pathway* it took. Yield tells you *the net result* for the desired product.

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used Alongside Yield

When working with percentage yield in KTU Process Calculations, the following metrics are **always** encountered together:

- **Limiting Reactant (LR):** The reactant that is completely consumed first and dictates the maximum product.
- **Theoretical Product:** Calculated assuming 100% consumption of the limiting reactant and 100% selectivity toward the desired product.
- **Actual Product:** Experimentally measured or given in the problem statement.
- **Excess Reactant:** The reactant present in more quantity than stoichiometrically required.

> [!TIP]
> **Always identify the limiting reactant first.** Without it, calculating percentage yield is impossible. KTU board questions frequently give you feed data and ask you to determine the LR before proceeding to the yield calculation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The "Why" and "How"

To understand percentage yield at the KTU 2024 depth, we must break it down into structured logic:

**Step 1 — Establish the Reaction Stoichiometry**
Write the balanced chemical equation for both the *main (desired)* reaction and any *side* reactions. Without a balanced equation, the theoretical product cannot be computed.

**Step 2 — Identify the Limiting Reactant**
For multiple reactants $A, B, C, \ldots$ with feed moles $n_{A,0}, n_{B,0}, n_{C,0}$ and stoichiometric coefficients $\nu_A, \nu_B, \nu_C$:

$$\text{LR} \;=\; \text{Reactant with the minimum value of } \left[\frac{n_{i,0}}{\nu_i}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Theoretical Product**
The theoretical moles of desired product $P^*$ are obtained by assuming the limiting reactant reacts completely to form $P$:

$$n_{P}^{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; n_{\text{LR},0} \times \frac{\nu_P}{\nu_{\text{LR}}}$$

where $\nu_P$ and $\nu_{\text{LR}}$ are the stoichiometric coefficients of the product and limiting reactant in the *main* reaction.

**Step 4 — Compare with the Actual Product**
The actual product $n_P^{\text{actual}}$ is either measured experimentally or given in the problem. The percentage yield is:

$$\%Y \;=\; \frac{n_P^{\text{actual}}}{n_{P}^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$$

**Step 5 — Account for the Physical Meaning**
A yield less than 100% indicates that either:
- Not all of the limiting reactant reacted (incomplete conversion)
- Some reacted via side reactions (poor selectivity)
- Product was lost in downstream separation

### 2.2 The Triad: Conversion, Selectivity, and Yield

For **single-reactant, multi-product systems** (very common in KTU 2024 questions), the three concepts are mathematically linked:

$$Y \;=\; X \times S$$

where:
- $X$ = **Fractional Conversion** of the limiting reactant
- $S$ = **Fractional Selectivity** toward the desired product
- $Y$ = **Fractional Yield** of the desired product

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern (2024 Scheme):** A common 14-mark question gives you feed composition and product stream data for a reactor with two competing reactions, and asks you to compute all three: conversion, selectivity, AND yield. Master the relationship $Y = X \cdot S$ in fractional form.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Limiting Reactant Test | $\min \left[\frac{n_{i,0}}{\nu_i}\right]$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Theoretical Product | $n_P^{\text{theo}} = n_{\text{LR},0} \cdot \frac{\nu_P}{\nu_{\text{LR}}}$ | moles or kmol |
| 3 | Percentage Yield | $\%Y = \frac{n_P^{\text{actual}}}{n_P^{\text{theo}}} \times 100$ | Percentage (\%) |
| 4 | Fractional Yield | $Y = X \cdot S$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le Y \le 1$ |
| 5 | Conversion of LR | $X = \frac{n_{\text{LR, in}} - n_{\text{LR, out}}}{n_{\text{LR, in}}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le X \le 1$ |
| 6 | Selectivity to P | $S_P = \frac{n_P^{\text{out}}}{\sum n_{\text{all products}}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le S_P \le 1$ |
| 7 | Mass-Based Yield | $\%Y_{\text{mass}} = \frac{m_P^{\text{actual}}}{m_P^{\text{theo}}} \times 100$ | Percentage (\%); often used when molecular weights differ |
| 8 | Overall vs Instantaneous Yield | $\%Y_{\text{overall}} = \frac{\text{total P over time}}{\text{total theoretical P}}$ | Used in batch/continuous reactors |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Percentage yield is the **single most reported metric** in industrial chemistry and petrochemical plants because:

1. **Economic Impact:** A 1% improvement in yield in a plant producing 1 million tonnes/year of a product worth \$1000/tonne translates to **\$10 million in additional revenue** annually.
2. **Sustainability:** Higher yield = less raw material consumption = lower waste generation = better green chemistry credentials.
3. **Process Optimization:** Engineers track yield over time to detect catalyst deactivation, heat-transfer issues, or feed impurity build-up.
4. **Safety and Purity:** Low yield often signals runaway side reactions that may produce hazardous byproducts (e.g., coke, tars, or explosive intermediates).

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** In refinery operations, the percentage yield of gasoline from crude oil distillation is typically **30–50%** depending on the crude source. Catalytic cracking units are then used to boost the gasoline yield further.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Percentage Yield from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** Consider a single ideal reactor with one main reaction and one side reaction:

$$\text{Main: } A + B \rightarrow P \quad (\text{desired})$$

$$\text{Side: } A + B \rightarrow U \quad (\text{undesired})$$

Let $A$ be the limiting reactant with initial moles $n_{A,0}$ and stoichiometric coefficient $\nu_A = 1$ in both reactions. Let $P$ have coefficient $\nu_P = 1$ and $U$ have coefficient $\nu_U = 1$.

**Step 1 — Theoretical Maximum of P**

If *all* of $A$ reacted and *all* of it went to $P$:

$$n_P^{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; n_{A,0} \cdot \frac{\nu_P}{\nu_A} \;=\; n_{A,0} \cdot \frac{1}{1} \;=\; n_{A,0}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Moles of P Produced**

Let $X$ = fractional conversion of $A$, and $S$ = fractional selectivity toward $P$.

The moles of $A$ that reacted:

$$n_A^{\text{reacted}} \;=\; X \cdot n_{A,0}$$

The moles of $A$ that went to $P$ (selectivity step):

$$n_P^{\text{actual}} \;=\; S \cdot n_A^{\text{reacted}} \;=\; S \cdot X \cdot n_{A,0}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the Yield Definition**

$$Y \;=\; \frac{n_P^{\text{actual}}}{n_P^{\text{theoretical}}} \;=\; \frac{S \cdot X \cdot n_{A,0}}{n_{A,0}} \;=\; X \cdot S$$

$$\boxed{\,Y \;=\; X \cdot S\,}$$

$$\boxed{\,\%Y \;=\; X \cdot S \times 100\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation shows that **yield is the product of conversion and selectivity**. This is the most-tested relationship in KTU 2024 board exams for Module 3.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Worked Example 1 (Single Reaction, 7-mark style):**

*Problem:* In the dehydrogenation of ethane to ethylene, 100 kmol of ethane is fed to a reactor per hour. The reaction is:
$$C_2H_6 \rightarrow C_2H_4 + H_2$$
If 75 kmol of ethylene is produced per hour, calculate the percentage yield of ethylene.

**Step 1 — Identify the Limiting Reactant**

Only one reactant (ethane), so it is automatically the LR. $n_{\text{LR},0} = 100$ kmol.

**Step 2 — Calculate Theoretical Ethylene**

Stoichiometry: 1 mol ethane produces 1 mol ethylene.

$$n_{C_2H_4}^{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; 100 \times \frac{1}{1} \;=\; 100 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Insert Actual Value**

$$n_{C_2H_4}^{\text{actual}} \;=\; 75 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Yield Formula**

$$\%Y \;=\; \frac{75}{100} \times 100 \;=\; 75\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The percentage yield of ethylene is **75%**. This means 25% of the ethane either did not react or reacted via a side pathway (e.g., cracking to methane or coke formation).

**Worked Example 2 (Multiple Reactants, 14-mark style):**

*Problem:* Methanol is produced by the reaction:
$$\text{CO} + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_3OH$$
The feed to the reactor contains 100 kmol CO and 300 kmol H₂. Analysis of the product stream shows 80 kmol methanol, 20 kmol CO (unreacted), and 200 kmol H₂. Calculate:
(a) The limiting reactant and the excess reactant.
(b) The percentage yield of methanol based on the limiting reactant.

**Step 1 — Limiting Reactant Test**

For CO: $\frac{n_{CO,0}}{\nu_{CO}} = \frac{100}{1} = 100$

For H₂: $\frac{n_{H_2,0}}{\nu_{H_2}} = \frac{300}{2} = 150$

Minimum ratio → **CO is the limiting reactant**, and H₂ is in excess.

**Step 2 — Theoretical Methanol**

$$n_{CH_3OH}^{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; n_{CO,0} \times \frac{\nu_{CH_3OH}}{\nu_{CO}} \;=\; 100 \times \frac{1}{1} \;=\; 100 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Yield Formula**

$$\%Y_{CH_3OH} \;=\; \frac{80}{100} \times 100 \;=\; 80\%$$

**Step 4 — Conversion Check (verification)**

CO converted = $100 - 20 = 80$ kmol
Fractional conversion of CO: $X = 80/100 = 0.80$

Since there is only one reaction, selectivity = 1.0, so $Y = 0.80 \times 1.0 = 0.80$ ✓

> [!TIP]
> **Verification trick:** Always cross-check your yield using the conversion-selectivity relationship $Y = X \cdot S$ when only one reaction exists. This catches arithmetic errors in KTU board exams.

### 3.3 Mass-Based Percentage Yield

When molecular weights differ significantly between feed and product, KTU 2024 questions often require **mass-based yield** instead of mole-based.

$$\%Y_{\text{mass}} \;=\; \frac{m_P^{\text{actual}}}{m_P^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$$

Conversion between mole-based and mass-based:

$$m_P^{\text{actual}} \;=\; n_P^{\text{actual}} \times MW_P$$

$$m_P^{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; n_{\text{LR},0} \times \frac{\nu_P}{\nu_{\text{LR}}} \times MW_P$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use $MW_{LR}$ instead of $MW_P$ in the theoretical mass calculation. Always use the molecular weight of the **product**, not the limiting reactant, when computing the theoretical product mass.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Yield Calculation

The diagram below maps the step-by-step logical flow required to compute percentage yield in any KTU 2024 board problem. It traces the input data through stoichiometric analysis to the final percentage yield output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Composition n_i0] --> B[Identify Reaction Equations]
    B --> C[Balance Stoichiometric Coefficients nu_i]
    C --> D[Compute Ratio n_i0 by nu_i for Each Reactant]
    D --> E{Which Reactant has Minimum Ratio}
    E -->|Reactant A| F[LR equals Reactant A]
    E -->|Reactant B| G[LR equals Reactant B]
    E -->|Reactant C| H[LR equals Reactant C]
    F --> I[Theoretical Product nu_P by nu_LR times n_LR0]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Actual Product from Given Data n_P_actual]
    J --> K[Compute Percent Yield n_P_actual by n_P_theoretical times 100]
    K --> L[Output Percentage Yield percent Y]
    I --> M[Cross Check with Y equals X times S]
    J --> M
    M --> L
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Yield Triangle Diagram

The following diagram illustrates the **interrelationship between Conversion, Selectivity, and Yield** — a high-frequency KTU 2024 board concept.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CONV[Conversion X] --> YLD[Yield Y]
    SEL[Selectivity S] --> YLD
    CONV --> NOTE1[How much of LR reacted]
    SEL --> NOTE2[Which fraction went to P]
    YLD --> NOTE3[Net P obtained relative to theoretical]
    YLD --> EQ[Y equals X times S]
    CONV --> EQ
    SEL --> EQ
```

### 4.3 Multi-Product Reactor Schematic (Block Architecture)

For reactors with a main reaction and a side reaction, the following block diagram shows how the limiting reactant splits between two pathways:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Limiting Reactant Feed n_LR0] --> REACTOR[Reactor Vessel]
    REACTOR --> P1[Pathway 1: Main Reaction to Product P]
    REACTOR --> P2[Pathway 2: Side Reaction to Undesired Product U]
    P1 --> OUTP[Product P Stream]
    P2 --> OUTU[Undesired U Stream]
    REACTOR --> UNRX[Unreacted LR Leaves]
    OUTP --> YCALC[Yield Calculation Node]
    YCALC --> RESULT[Percent Yield of P]
    YCALC --> SELCALC[Selectivity to P]
    YCALC --> CONCALC[Conversion of LR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The reactor block represents a generic CSTR or PFR. The "Yield Calculation Node" is the analytical step where $\%Y = (n_P^{\text{actual}} / n_P^{\text{theoretical}}) \times 100$ is computed. All three metrics (Yield, Selectivity, Conversion) are extracted from the same reactor outlet composition data.

### 4.4 Engineering Reality — Mass Balance Closure

In every reactive process, the **Law of Conservation of Mass** must hold. The percentage yield calculation must always be consistent with the overall mass balance. The block diagram below emphasizes this constraint.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FEED[Total Mass In] --> REACTOR2[Reactor with Reaction]
    REACTOR2 --> OUTLET[Total Mass Out]
    FEED -.->|must equal| OUTLET
    OUTLET --> SP1[Unreacted Reactants]
    OUTLET --> SP2[Desired Product P]
    OUTLET --> SP3[Side Products]
    OUTLET --> SP4[Inerts if any]
    SP2 --> YFINAL[Yield Reported on P only]
    SP3 --> SFINAL[Selectivity Accounts for This]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** When computing percentage yield in problems with multiple side products, the *theoretical* product is still based on the limiting reactant. The *actual* product is the experimentally measured value of the desired product only. Side products do NOT enter the yield denominator; they only affect selectivity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define percentage yield in a chemical reaction. How is it different from conversion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Percentage yield is defined as the ratio of the actual moles of the desired product obtained to the theoretical moles of the product that would be formed if the limiting reactant were completely converted to the desired product, expressed as a percentage.
- **(1 mark)** Formula: $\%Y = \dfrac{n_P^{\text{actual}}}{n_P^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$
- **(1 mark)** **Difference from Conversion:** Conversion refers to the fraction of the limiting reactant that has been chemically consumed, regardless of which product is formed. Percentage yield specifically measures the *net* output of the *desired* product only, factoring in both incomplete conversion AND losses to side reactions.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: The reaction $A \rightarrow B$ is carried out with 50 mol of pure A. If 30 mol of B is obtained, what is the percentage yield of B?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Limiting Reactant: A is the only reactant, so it is the LR. $n_{A,0} = 50$ mol.
- **(1 mark)** Theoretical moles of B = $50 \times (1/1) = 50$ mol (from stoichiometry).
- **(1 mark)** $\%Y = \dfrac{30}{50} \times 100 = 60\%$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**In a contact process plant, SO₂ is oxidized to SO₃ by the reaction:**
$$2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3$$
**The fresh feed to the reactor contains 100 kmol of SO₂ and 80 kmol of O₂. The product gas analysis shows 70 kmol of SO₃, 30 kmol of unreacted SO₂, and 50 kmol of unreacted O₂.**

**(a)** Identify the limiting reactant and the excess reactant. Calculate the percentage of excess reactant used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the percentage yield of SO₃ based on the limiting reactant. Also compute the conversion of the limiting reactant and the selectivity toward SO₃. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Limiting Reactant Identification (3 marks)**

Calculate the ratio $n_{i,0}/\nu_i$ for each reactant:

- For SO₂: $\dfrac{n_{SO_2,0}}{\nu_{SO_2}} = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50$
- For O₂: $\dfrac{n_{O_2,0}}{\nu_{O_2}} = \dfrac{80}{1} = 80$

Since $50 < 80$, **SO₂ is the limiting reactant** and O₂ is the excess reactant.

**Step 2: Theoretical O₂ Required (2 marks)**

For 100 kmol of SO₂ to react completely, O₂ required = $100 \times (1/2) = 50$ kmol.

**Step 3: Percentage of Excess (2 marks)**

$$\% \text{Excess O}_2 \;=\; \frac{n_{O_2,\text{fed}} - n_{O_2,\text{required}}}{n_{O_2,\text{required}}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{80 - 50}{50} \times 100 \;=\; 60\%$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Theoretical SO₃ (2 marks)**

$$n_{SO_3}^{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; n_{SO_2,0} \times \frac{\nu_{SO_3}}{\nu_{SO_2}} \;=\; 100 \times \frac{2}{2} \;=\; 100 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 2: Percentage Yield (2 marks)**

$$\%Y_{SO_3} \;=\; \frac{n_{SO_3}^{\text{actual}}}{n_{SO_3}^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{70}{100} \times 100 \;=\; 70\%$$

**Step 3: Conversion of LR (1.5 marks)**

SO₂ converted = $100 - 30 = 70$ kmol
$$X_{SO_2} \;=\; \frac{70}{100} \;=\; 0.70$$

**Step 4: Selectivity (1.5 marks)**

Since SO₃ is the *only* product in the given reaction, selectivity $S = 1.0$.

**Verification:** $Y = X \cdot S = 0.70 \times 1.0 = 0.70$ ✓ (matches 70% yield)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse the *stoichiometric coefficient* with the *mole number* when computing the theoretical product. For the SO₂ + O₂ reaction, the coefficient of SO₂ is **2** (not 1). Use the *coefficient*, not just the chemical formula, in the ratio. Many students lose 2 marks here.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Acetic acid is produced by the oxidation of acetaldehyde according to:**
$$2CH_3CHO + O_2 \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH$$
**A side reaction also occurs:**
$$2CH_3CHO + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2CH_3COOH + 2CO_2 + 2H_2O$$
**The reactor is fed with 100 kmol of CH₃CHO and 80 kmol of O₂. The product stream analysis shows 60 kmol of acetic acid, 20 kmol of unreacted acetaldehyde, 25 kmol of O₂, and 10 kmol of CO₂.**

**(a)** Identify the limiting reactant, calculate the conversion of the limiting reactant, and determine the percentage yield of acetic acid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the selectivity of the reaction toward acetic acid and the percentage of excess reactant used. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Limiting Reactant Test (2 marks)**

- For CH₃CHO: $\dfrac{100}{2} = 50$
- For O₂: $\dfrac{80}{3.5}$ (using a weighted sum) or alternatively, based on the main reaction: $\dfrac{80}{1} = 80$

For the **main reaction** (acetic acid production), the lower ratio is for **CH₃CHO** ($\frac{100}{2} = 50 < 80$).

**CH₃CHO is the limiting reactant.**

**Step 2: Conversion of CH₃CHO (2 marks)**

CH₃CHO converted = $100 - 20 = 80$ kmol

$$X_{CH_3CHO} \;=\; \frac{80}{100} \;=\; 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Acetic Acid (1.5 marks)**

From the main reaction stoichiometry, 2 mol CH₃CHO gives 2 mol CH₃COOH:

$$n_{CH_3COOH}^{\text{theoretical}} \;=\; 100 \times \frac{2}{2} \;=\; 100 \text{ kmol}$$

**Step 4: Percentage Yield (1.5 marks)**

$$\%Y_{CH_3COOH} \;=\; \frac{60}{100} \times 100 \;=\; 60\%$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Selectivity Calculation (3 marks)**

The total CH₃CHO reacted = 80 kmol. From the side reaction stoichiometry, 2 kmol CH₃CHO produces 2 kmol CO₂, so:

CH₃CHO that went to side reaction (CO₂ pathway) = 10 kmol (since 10 kmol CO₂ was produced).
CH₃CHO that went to acetic acid = $80 - 10 = 70$ kmol (theoretical for the main path, but actual produced 60 kmol).

Actually, **selectivity to acetic acid** is defined based on the *carbon* going to the product:

$$S_{CH_3COOH} \;=\; \frac{\text{kmol of CH}_3\text{CHO converted to CH}_3\text{COOH}}{\text{Total kmol of CH}_3\text{CHO reacted}}$$

From the product stream: 60 kmol CH₃COOH was produced, requiring 60 kmol CH₃CHO (per stoichiometry).

$$S_{CH_3COOH} \;=\; \frac{60}{80} \;=\; 0.75 \text{ or } 75\%$$

**Step 2: Verification with Y = X · S (2 marks)**

$$Y \;=\; 0.80 \times 0.75 \;=\; 0.60 \;=\; 60\% \;\checkmark$$

This matches our directly calculated yield — **a powerful consistency check**.

**Step 3: Excess Reactant Percentage (2 marks)**

Theoretical O₂ needed to react with 100 kmol CH₃CHO in the *main* reaction = $100 \times (1/2) = 50$ kmol.
Actual O₂ fed = 80 kmol.

$$\% \text{Excess O}_2 \;=\; \frac{80 - 50}{50} \times 100 \;=\; 60\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Selectivity is NOT the same as yield.** Yield is based on the *feed*; selectivity is based on the *reacted portion*. Confusing these two costs 3–4 marks in a 14-mark question.
> 2. When multiple reactions exist, calculate theoretical product using **only the main reaction's** stoichiometry — NOT a sum of side reactions.
> 3. The limiting reactant is identified using the **stoichiometric coefficients of the main (desired) reaction**. Do not mix coefficients from two different reactions.
> 4. Always include the **units** (kmol, mol, etc.) in your final answer. KTU 2024 board markers deduct half a mark for unit omission.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Percentage Yield (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** $\%Y = \dfrac{n_P^{\text{actual}}}{n_P^{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$, where the theoretical product assumes 100% consumption of the **limiting reactant** going only to the **desired product**.

- **Limiting Reactant Rule:** The reactant with the *smallest* $\dfrac{n_{i,0}}{\nu_i}$ ratio is the limiting reactant. Always compute this first.

- **Theoretical Product Formula:** $n_P^{\text{theo}} = n_{\text{LR},0} \times \dfrac{\nu_P}{\nu_{\text{LR}}}$ — use the **stoichiometric coefficients** of the *main* reaction only.

- **Golden Triangle Relationship:** $Y = X \times S$ in fractional form. This is the single most important equation for Module 3 reactive balance problems.

- **Conversion:** Fraction of limiting reactant that reacted. $X = \dfrac{n_{\text{LR, in}} - n_{\text{LR, out}}}{n_{\text{LR, in}}}$.

- **Selectivity:** Fraction of *reacted* LR that went to the desired product. $S_P = \dfrac{n_P^{\text{actual}}}{n_{\text{LR, reacted}} \times (\nu_P/\nu_{\text{LR}})}$.

- **Excess Reactant:** $\% \text{Excess} = \dfrac{n_{\text{fed}} - n_{\text{required}}}{n_{\text{required}}} \times 100$.

- **Mole vs Mass Yield:** Use mass-based yield when the problem gives masses or asks for kg/tonne. Use mole-based yield when the problem gives moles or kmol.

- **Multi-Product Systems:** Yield denominator uses the *main* reaction's theoretical product. Side products affect *selectivity*, not the yield denominator.

- **Units Always Required:** kmol, mol, kg, or tonnes — KTU markers will deduct for missing units.

- **Cross-Verification Trick:** After computing yield, check $Y = X \cdot S$. If they don't match, an arithmetic error is present.

- **Common KTU Board Traps:**
  (a) Forgetting to identify the limiting reactant before computing yield.
  (b) Using molecular weight of LR instead of product in mass-based yield.
  (c) Mixing stoichiometric coefficients from different reactions.
