&= 112.2\;\text{cal}\cdot\text{gmol}^{-1}\!\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Mass specific heat**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_{p,\text{mass}} &= \frac{112.2}{286} = 0.3923\;\text{cal}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1} \\
&= 0.3923 \times 4.184 \\
&= 1.642\;\text{kJ}\cdot\text{kg}^{-1}\!\cdot\text{K}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Heat required**

Mass $m = 1.5$ tonnes $= 1500$ kg
$\Delta T = 90 - 25 = 65$ K

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= m \cdot c_{p,\text{mass}} \cdot \Delta T \\
  &= 1500 \times 1.642 \times 65 \\
  &= 160{,}095\;\text{kJ} \\
  &\approx 160.1\;\text{MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $c_p \approx 1.642\;\text{kJ}\cdot\text{kg}^{-1}\!\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$; $Q \approx 160.1\;\text{MJ}$. (7 marks — 2 for atom identification, 2 for summation, 1 for unit conversion, 2 for heat computation)

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Differentiate between **Dulong–Petit Law** and **Kopp's Rule**. State any three situations in which Kopp's Rule is **not** valid. (7 marks)

**(b)** The specific heat of solid **NaCl** is experimentally determined to be $0.208\;\text{cal}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$. Verify Kopp's Rule for NaCl. Molecular weight of NaCl = 58.5 g/gmol. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

| Feature | Dulong–Petit Law (1819) | Kopp's Rule (1865) |
|---|---|---|
| **Applies to** | Pure solid elements | Solid compounds (multiple elements) |
| **Form** | $C_p \approx 3R \approx 6.2$ cal/gmol·°C (single value) | $C_{p,\text{compound}} = \sum n_i c_i$ (additive) |
| **Origin** | Classical equipartition (3 vibrational modes per atom) | Empirical extension of Dulong–Petit to compounds |
| **Values** | Same constant for all solid elements | Different tabulated values for light elements (C, H, B, …) |
| **Temperature range** | Valid at high T (above Debye temperature) | Valid only near room temperature (≈ 25 °C) |

**Three situations where Kopp's Rule is NOT valid (3 marks):**
1. **At high temperatures** (T > ~400 °C) — anharmonic and quantum effects.
2. **For amorphous solids / polymers** — no well-defined crystal lattice.
3. **For compounds containing hydrogen-bond networks** (e.g., hydrated salts at high dilution) — cooperative vibrational modes break additivity.

(2 marks for differences table, 2 marks for the three invalidity conditions, 3 marks for elaboration)

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Predicted molar $C_p$ from Kopp**

NaCl contains 1 Na + 1 Cl. Both Na and Cl are "other elements" → $c = 6.2$ each.

$$
C_{p,\text{mol, predicted}} = 6.2 + 6.2 = 12.4\;\text{cal}\cdot\text{gmol}^{-1}\!\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2: Predicted mass specific heat**

$$
c_{p,\text{mass, predicted}} = \frac{12.4}{58.5} = 0.2120\;\text{cal}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Compare with experiment**

$$
\text{Experimental} = 0.208\;\text{cal}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{Percentage error} = \frac{\lvert 0.2120 - 0.208 \rvert}{0.208} \times 100\% = 1.92\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Kopp's Rule predicts $0.2120$ vs. experimental $0.208$ — agreement within **~2%**, which is within the empirical accuracy of the rule. ✅ **Verified.**

(2 marks for predicted $C_{p,\text{mol}}$, 2 marks for predicted mass $C_p$, 1 mark for comparison, 2 marks for conclusion with percentage error)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read before you write!**
> 1. **Forgetting the water of crystallisation in hydrated salts** (e.g., $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3\cdot 10\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$). Each water molecule contributes 2 H + 1 O to the atom count. Many students treat it as a single "OH" group and **lose 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Not converting units.** Final answer must be in the unit asked — $\text{cal}\cdot\text{g}^{-1}\!\cdot^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$ **OR** $\text{kJ}\cdot\text{kg}^{-1}\!\cdot\text{K}^{-1}$. Mixing units is an instant ½-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using $C_p$ (molar) when the question asks for $c_p$ (mass)** and vice-versa. Always **re-read the verb** ("estimate the specific heat" = mass basis; "estimate the molar heat capacity" = molar basis).
> 4. **Forgetting ΔT direction.** A common slip: writing $(T_1 - T_2)$ instead of $(T_2 - T_1)$. Use $\Delta T = T_{\text{final}} - T_{\text{initial}}$.
> 5. **Using Dulong–Petit's 6.2 for C, H, O.** This is the **#1 blunder** — C, H, B, Si, O, F, P, S have **lower tabulated values**. Using 6.2 for C gives an error of 250%!
> 6. **Forgetting to multiply by count $n_i$.** The contribution of O in $\text{CaCO}_3$ is $3 \times 4.0 = 12.0$, NOT 4.0.
> 7. **Final-answer rounding.** Round to **3 significant figures** in mass-specific-heat answers; show **at least 1 decimal** in molar answers.

---

## 5.4 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (T-5 minutes before the exam)**
>
> ✅ **Definition** — Molar $C_p$ of a solid ≈ Σ (atomic $C_p$ × count).
> ✅ **Kopp table** — Memorise: C = 1.8, H = 2.3, B = 2.7, Si = 3.8, O = 4.0, F = 5.0, P = 5.4, S = 5.5, others = 6.2 (all in cal·gmol⁻¹·°C⁻¹).
> ✅ **Conversion** — 1 cal·g⁻¹·°C⁻¹ = 4.184 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ = 4184 J·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹.
> ✅ **Dulong–Petit link** — Single-element classical limit ≈ 3R ≈ 6.2 cal/gmol·°C; Kopp = molecular extension.
> ✅ **Two key equations** — $C_{p,\text{mol}} = \sum n_i c_i$ and $c_{p,\text{mass}} = C_{p,\text{mol}}/M$.
> ✅ **Heat equation** — $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ (mass) or $Q = n C_{p,\text{mol}} \Delta T$ (moles).
> ✅ **Hydrate trap** — Count H₂O atoms explicitly (2H + 1O per water).
> ✅ **Validity range** — Crystalline solids, ~25 °C, not for gases / liquids / polymers / amorphous / high-T systems.
> ✅ **Expected accuracy** — Within 5–10% of experimental data (acceptable for design).
> ✅ **Typical PYQ pattern** — (i) 2-mark definition; (ii) 7-mark estimation + sensible-heat calculation on a hydrated salt; (iii) 5-mark comparative table vs. Dulong–Petit.
> ✅ **Always show units** — Examiner's mantra: "No unit, no credit."

---

### 📌 One-Line Takeaway

> *Kopp's Rule converts the chemical formula of a solid directly into a number — its heat capacity — by simply **adding atomic bricks** of C, H, O, … from a single memory table.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latent Heats - Heat of Fusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Latent Heats - Heat of Fusion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Latent Heat of Fusion** (also called the **Enthalpy of Fusion**, denoted as $\lambda_{fus}$ or $\Delta H_{fus}$) is defined as the quantity of thermal energy that must be supplied to a unit mass (or one mole) of a pure solid substance, at its **normal melting point** and at **standard atmospheric pressure** ($P = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$), in order to completely transform it into the liquid state **without any change in temperature**.

Mathematically, the energy balance for a pure fusion process is expressed as:

$$Q_{fus} = m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$$

where:
- $Q_{fus}$ is the total heat required for the solid-to-liquid phase transition, expressed in **kJ**.
- $m$ is the mass of the substance undergoing fusion, in **kg**.
- $\lambda_{fus}$ is the specific latent heat of fusion, in **kJ/kg** (or $\Delta H_{fus}$ in **kJ/kmol** when expressed on a molar basis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Sensible Heat**: Latent heat does **not** produce a temperature change — it is "hidden" energy used exclusively to overcome the **intermolecular cohesive forces** within the crystalline lattice. This is fundamentally different from sensible heat, which raises the temperature of a substance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference State for KTU Problems**: The latent heat of fusion is **always reported at the normal melting point** ($T_m$) of the substance. If the phase change occurs at a different temperature, KTU problems will typically either provide a corrected value (using Watson-type or Clapeyron corrections) or specifically indicate the operating pressure.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a tightly packed crowd of people standing shoulder-to-shoulder in a queue (this represents a **solid**, where molecules are locked into a rigid, ordered crystalline structure by strong intermolecular forces). Now, picture a slow, energetic music rhythm that gradually makes the people sway and start shuffling forward in a disorganized manner, eventually forming a free-moving crowd inside a hall (this represents a **liquid**). The music is the **heat energy** you are adding.

The crucial point is that during the transition from the rigid queue to the free-moving crowd, the energy you supply is **not making the people "hotter"** (faster) — it is being used entirely to **break the rigid bonds** that held them in formation. Only after the bonds are completely broken does additional energy begin to make the crowd move faster (i.e., the temperature begins to rise again, which corresponds to heating the liquid).

This "bond-breaking energy" is exactly the **latent heat of fusion**. The reverse process (liquid → solid, i.e., a crowd snapping back into a rigid queue) releases exactly the same amount of energy — this is the **latent heat of solidification**, and it is numerically equal to the latent heat of fusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Industrial Analogy**: Ice melting in a glass of water is the simplest example. To melt 1 kg of ice at $0\,°C$ into 1 kg of water at $0\,°C$, you must supply **334 kJ** of heat, yet the temperature remains stuck at $0\,°C$ throughout the entire process. The same amount of energy is released when water freezes back into ice.

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Values & Physical Constants

The following table lists the **standard latent heats of fusion** for substances frequently encountered in KTU process calculation problems. Students should **memorize** the values for water, as these are test-favorite constants.

| Substance | Chemical Formula | Normal Melting Point $T_m$ (K) | $\Delta H_{fus}$ (kJ/kg) | $\Delta H_{fus}$ (kJ/kmol) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Water (Ice)** | $H_2O$ | **273.15** | **333.5** | **6.01** |
| Ammonia | $NH_3$ | 195.4 | 332.3 | 5.66 |
| Sodium Chloride | $NaCl$ | 1074 | 519.0 | 30.34 |
| Benzene | $C_6H_6$ | 278.7 | 127.4 | 9.95 |
| Naphthalene | $C_{10}H_8$ | 353.4 | 148.7 | 19.06 |
| Iron | $Fe$ | 1809 | 247.3 | 13.81 |
| Copper | $Cu$ | 1356 | 180.6 | 11.49 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for the Board Exam**: For water, the value $\lambda_{fus} = 333.5 \text{ kJ/kg} = 6.01 \text{ kJ/mol}$ is a **"must-know"** constant. The latent heat of vaporization of water is roughly **5 to 6 times larger** than its latent heat of fusion — a useful sanity check during numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundation — Why Latent Heat Exists

At the microscopic level, the molecules of a solid are held together in a fixed, repeating, three-dimensional geometric pattern called a **crystalline lattice**. This lattice is maintained by strong, balanced intermolecular forces (van der Waals forces, hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, or metallic bonds, depending on the substance). The molecules in this state can only vibrate about their mean positions — they cannot translate freely.

To convert a solid into a liquid, three simultaneous phenomena must occur:

1. **Disruption of the long-range crystalline order** — the regular geometric pattern must be broken.
2. **Increase in molecular mobility** — molecules must gain translational degrees of freedom.
3. **Work done against atmospheric pressure** — the substance typically expands slightly (a small but non-zero volume change $\Delta V$ occurs) during fusion, requiring the system to push back the surrounding atmosphere.

The total energy required for steps (1), (2), and (3) — supplied as heat at constant temperature and pressure — is precisely the **enthalpy of fusion**, $\Delta H_{fus}$.

---

## 2.2 The Clapeyron Equation for Solid–Liquid Equilibrium

The **Clapeyron equation** is the most fundamental thermodynamic relationship governing the slope of any two-phase coexistence curve. For the **solid–liquid** boundary specifically, it is written as:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}$$

where:
- $dP/dT$ is the slope of the fusion curve on a $P$–$T$ diagram (in Pa/K).
- $\Delta H_{fus}$ is the molar latent heat of fusion, in **J/mol** or **kJ/kmol**.
- $T$ is the absolute temperature at which the phase change occurs, in **K**.
- $\Delta V_{fus} = V_{liquid} - V_{solid}$ is the **molar volume change** upon fusion, in **m³/mol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation**: The Clapeyron equation tells us that the change in melting point with pressure is governed entirely by the ratio $\Delta H_{fus} / \Delta V_{fus}$. For most substances, $\Delta V_{fus} > 0$ (liquid is less dense than solid), so the melting point **increases** with pressure. **Water is the famous exception** — because ice is *less* dense than liquid water, $\Delta V_{fus} < 0$, and increasing pressure *lowers* the melting point of ice. This is why ice skates glide: the pressure of the blade melts the ice.

---

## 2.3 The Clausius–Clapeyron Approximation (For Fusion)

In most solid–liquid systems, the volume change $\Delta V_{fus}$ is small and can be treated as **approximately constant** over a moderate range of pressures. Integrating the Clapeyron equation under this assumption gives the **modified Clausius–Clapeyron equation** for the fusion curve:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **When KTU uses this**: This form is used when the problem gives you $\Delta H_{fus}$ as a constant and asks: *"At what pressure will the substance melt at temperature $T_2$?"* — you are given the normal melting point at $P_1 = 1 \text{ atm}$ and need to find $P_2$.

For a **very small** temperature change, the equation can be linearized to:

$$\Delta T = \frac{T \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}{\Delta H_{fus}} \cdot \Delta P$$

---

## 2.4 Effect of Temperature on Latent Heat (Watson's Equation Analogy)

Just as the **Watson equation** corrects the latent heat of vaporization for temperature, a similar thermodynamic relationship can be derived for the latent heat of fusion. The standard Watson-type correlation is:

$$\frac{\lambda_{fus,\,T_2}}{\lambda_{fus,\,T_1}} = \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$$

where $T_c$ is the **critical temperature** of the substance (note: this is the critical temperature of the substance, not of the solid — since solids do not have a true critical point, the value used in practice is often an empirical fit or the triple-point temperature adjusted for the liquid branch).

> [!CAUTION]
> **Reality Check for Solid–Liquid Systems**: The Watson-type correction is **less commonly used for fusion** than for vaporization because the temperature range over which a solid can exist is usually very narrow (compared to a liquid's boiling range). KTU problems will typically give you the corrected value directly, or restrict the problem to the normal melting point.

---

## 2.5 Estimation of Latent Heat of Fusion (When Data is Missing)

When experimental values are not available, KTU problems may require you to estimate $\Delta H_{fus}$ from the **Clausius–Clapeyron slope**. If the slope $dP/dT$ of the fusion curve is known at the normal melting point, you can compute:

$$\Delta H_{fus} = T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus} \cdot \frac{dP}{dT}\bigg|_{T_m}$$

This is the **direct inversion of the Clapeyron equation** and is one of the most frequently tested operations in KTU university examinations.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Typical Use Case in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Fusion Energy | $Q_{fus} = m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$ | Calculate heat required to melt a known mass |
| 2 | Molar Energy Relation | $Q_{fus} = n \cdot \Delta H_{fus}$ | When the amount is given in moles (kmol) |
| 3 | Clapeyron Equation | $\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}$ | Predict pressure-dependence of melting point |
| 4 | Clausius–Clapeyron (Linear) | $\Delta T = \frac{T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}{\Delta H_{fus}} \cdot \Delta P$ | Small $\Delta T$ from small $\Delta P$ |
| 5 | Clausius–Clapeyron (Exponential) | $\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Find $P_2$ at new $T_2$, given $P_1$ at $T_1$ |
| 6 | Inverse Clapeyron (Estimation) | $\Delta H_{fus} = T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus} \cdot (dP/dT)$ | Estimate $\lambda_{fus}$ from $P$–$T$ curve slope |
| 7 | Freezing Point Depression (colligative) | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | Freezing-point depression of a solution |
| 8 | Energy Released on Solidification | $Q_{solid} = m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$ (with negative sign for release) | Energy balance on a crystallizer/ freezer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Gas Constant Reminder**: Always use $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$. **Unit consistency is the #1 scoring factor** in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Latent heat of fusion calculations are critical in the design and operation of the following chemical engineering systems:

1. **Crystallizers** — the energy released when product crystals form from a saturated solution must be removed by the cooling jacket; this determines cooling-water flow rates.
2. **Latent Heat Thermal Energy Storage (LHTES)** — Phase Change Materials (PCMs) like paraffin waxes and salt hydrates store energy at their melting point for solar heating, building thermal comfort, and electronics cooling.
3. **Metal Casting & Solidification** — In foundries, the heat that must be removed to fully solidify a casting is calculated using $\lambda_{fus}$ to determine cooling cycle times.
4. **Refrigeration & Ice-Making Plants** — The time required to freeze a known mass of water in a slab ice plant is computed from $\lambda_{fus}$ of water.
5. **Food Processing** — Freeze-concentration, freeze-drying, and frozen-food preservation all require accurate $\lambda_{fus}$ of water and food components.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Clapeyron Equation for Solid–Liquid Equilibrium

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Equality of Chemical Potentials

At the **solid–liquid coexistence line** on a $P$–$T$ diagram, the two phases are in thermodynamic equilibrium. By definition, this means the molar Gibbs free energies of the solid and liquid phases are **exactly equal**:

$$G_{solid}(T,P) = G_{liquid}(T,P)$$

### 3.1.2 Taking the Total Differential

If we move an infinitesimal amount along the coexistence curve from $(T, P)$ to $(T + dT, P + dP)$, the equilibrium condition must still hold, so the differentials of the two Gibbs functions must also be equal:

$$dG_{solid} = dG_{liquid}$$

Recalling the fundamental thermodynamic identity for a single-component, single-phase system, $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$, we substitute:

$$-S_{solid}\,dT + V_{solid}\,dP = -S_{liquid}\,dT + V_{liquid}\,dP$$

### 3.1.3 Rearranging into Slope Form

Rearranging the equation above to group the $dT$ and $dP$ terms:

$$(V_{liquid} - V_{solid})\,dP = (S_{liquid} - S_{solid})\,dT$$

By definition, the molar volume change on fusion is $\Delta V_{fus} = V_{liquid} - V_{solid}$, and the molar entropy change on fusion is $\Delta S_{fus} = S_{liquid} - S_{solid}$. Substituting:

$$\Delta V_{fus}\,dP = \Delta S_{fus}\,dT$$

Solving for the slope $dP/dT$:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta S_{fus}}{\Delta V_{fus}}$$

### 3.1.4 Linking Entropy of Fusion to Enthalpy of Fusion

For a **reversible** phase change at constant temperature and pressure (which is the definition of equilibrium), the second law of thermodynamics gives:

$$\Delta S_{fus} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T_m}$$

Substituting this into the slope equation yields the final form of the **Clapeyron equation** for the solid–liquid boundary:

$$\boxed{\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Derivation Matters for KTU**: Examiners often give a 7-mark question asking students to "derive the Clapeyron equation for solid–liquid equilibrium." You must show **all four steps** above: starting from $G_{solid} = G_{liquid}$, using the total differential, isolating the slope, and finally substituting the relation $\Delta S = \Delta H / T$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example A — Inverse Clapeyron Calculation (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem**: The latent heat of fusion of naphthalene is **19.06 kJ/kmol** at its normal melting point of **353.4 K**. The molar volume of liquid naphthalene is **0.131 m³/kmol** and that of solid naphthalene is **0.118 m³/kmol**. The slope of the fusion curve at the normal melting point is reported as $dP/dT = 4.05 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/K}$. Verify the latent heat of fusion using the Clapeyron equation. Also calculate the change in melting point if the pressure is increased by **100 atm** above atmospheric.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — List all given data in consistent SI units**:

- $\Delta H_{fus} = 19.06 \text{ kJ/kmol} = 19{,}060 \text{ J/mol}$
- $T_m = 353.4 \text{ K}$
- $V_{liquid} = 0.131 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol} = 0.131 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$
- $V_{solid} = 0.118 \text{ m}^3/\text{kmol} = 0.118 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$
- $dP/dT = 4.05 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/K}$

**Step 2 — Compute the molar volume change on fusion**:

$$\Delta V_{fus} = V_{liquid} - V_{solid}$$

$$\Delta V_{fus} = (0.131 - 0.118) \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

$$\Delta V_{fus} = 0.013 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol} = 1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Clapeyron equation to verify $\Delta H_{fus}$**:

Rearranging the Clapeyron equation to solve for $\Delta H_{fus}$:

$$\Delta H_{fus} = T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus} \cdot \frac{dP}{dT}$$

Substituting the values:

$$\Delta H_{fus} = 353.4 \text{ K} \times (1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}) \times (4.05 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/K})$$

$$\Delta H_{fus} = 353.4 \times 1.3 \times 4.05 \times 10^{-5+6} \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\Delta H_{fus} = 353.4 \times 5.265 \times 10^{1} \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\Delta H_{fus} = 18{,}607 \text{ J/mol} \approx 18.61 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the tabulated value**:

The calculated value $18.61 \text{ kJ/mol}$ is in **excellent agreement** with the tabulated $19.06 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (the small ~2.4% deviation is due to rounding in $dP/dT$ and the volume data). Verification successful.

**Step 5 — Calculate the change in melting point for $\Delta P = 100 \text{ atm}$**:

Convert $\Delta P$ to Pascals:

$$\Delta P = 100 \text{ atm} \times 101{,}325 \text{ Pa/atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa}$$

Use the linearized Clapeyron form:

$$\Delta T = \frac{T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}{\Delta H_{fus}} \cdot \Delta P$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{353.4 \text{ K} \times (1.3 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol})}{19{,}060 \text{ J/mol}} \times (1.01325 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa})$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{353.4 \times 1.3 \times 1.01325 \times 10^{-5+7}}{19{,}060} \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{353.4 \times 1.3 \times 1.01325 \times 10^{2}}{19{,}060} \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{46{,}549.7}{19{,}060} \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T \approx 2.44 \text{ K}$$

**Step 6 — State the final result with units and physical interpretation**:

The melting point of naphthalene **increases by approximately 2.44 K** when the pressure is raised by 100 atm above atmospheric pressure. This is consistent with the typical behavior of most substances (excluding water-like anomalies) where $\Delta V_{fus} > 0$ and the fusion curve has a positive slope.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example B — Heat Duty of an Ice-Melting Process (3-Mark Concept)

**Problem**: A chemical plant uses an ice-bath to cool a reactor. How much heat is absorbed when **50 kg** of ice at $0\,°C$ is completely melted to water at $0\,°C$? Given: $\lambda_{fus} = 333.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula**:
$$Q_{fus} = m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values**:
$$Q_{fus} = 50 \text{ kg} \times 333.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{fus} = 16{,}675 \text{ kJ} \approx 16.68 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 3 — State the conclusion**: The ice-bath absorbs **16,675 kJ (≈ 16.68 MJ)** of heat from the reactor. The temperature remains at 0 °C throughout the melting process.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Templates

| # | Problem Type | Given Data | Unknown to Find | Governing Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat required to melt a solid | Mass, $\lambda_{fus}$ | Total heat $Q$ | $Q = m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$ |
| 2 | Latent heat from Clausius–Clapeyron | $P_1, T_1, P_2, T_2$ | $\Delta H_{fus}$ | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (\Delta H_{fus}/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ |
| 3 | New melting point at high pressure | $T_m, \Delta V, \Delta P, \Delta H_{fus}$ | $\Delta T_m$ | $\Delta T = T \Delta V \Delta P / \Delta H$ |
| 4 | Verification of $\Delta H_{fus}$ from $P$–$T$ slope | $T_m, \Delta V, dP/dT$ | $\Delta H_{fus}$ | $\Delta H = T \Delta V (dP/dT)$ |
| 5 | Crystallizer energy balance | Mass flow, $\lambda_{fus}$, $T_{in}, T_{out}, C_p$ | Cooling duty | $Q = m C_p \Delta T + m \lambda_{fus}$ |
| 6 | Latent heat per mole from per kg | $M$ (molar mass) | $\Delta H_{mol}$ | $\Delta H_{mol} = \lambda_{fus} \times M$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Recognition Tip**: If the problem gives you a slope of the fusion curve $dP/dT$, the answer is almost always obtained from the Clapeyron equation. If it gives you two pressures and two temperatures, the answer is from the exponential Clausius–Clapeyron form. **Recognize the template** before plugging in numbers.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic Block Diagram — Process Flow for a Solid–Liquid Phase-Change Energy Calculation

The following Mermaid flowchart maps out the **decision logic** an engineer follows when solving a latent heat of fusion problem in process calculations. It consolidates the relationships among the Clapeyron equation, Clausius–Clapeyron equation, and direct energy balance.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Latent Heat of Fusion Problem] --> B{What is Given?}
    B -- Mass and lambda_fus --> C[Use Q = m times lambda_fus]
    B -- P-T slope data --> D[Apply Clapeyron Equation]
    B -- Two P and T pairs --> E[Apply Clausius-Clapeyron Exponential Form]
    B -- Mass and temperature change --> F[Combine Sensible and Latent Heat]
    C --> G[Compute Q in kJ]
    D --> H[Compute or Verify delta_H_fus]
    E --> H
    F --> G
    G --> I[Check Unit Consistency]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Report Final Answer with Units]
    J --> K[End]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram**: Start at the top with the problem statement. Decide which category the given data falls into, follow the corresponding arrow to the governing equation, compute the answer, perform a unit-consistency check, and report the result. This is the **mental flowchart** examiners expect you to follow implicitly when writing your solution.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Position of $\lambda_{fus}$ in a Process Energy Balance

The diagram below shows where latent heat of fusion fits into the **overall energy balance** of a typical continuous crystallizer or solidification unit. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU Part B questions on energy balance with phase change.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    InStream[Feed Stream: Hot Liquid or Solution] --> SensHeat[Sensible Heat Release: m times Cp times delta_T]
    SensHeat --> PhaseChange[Phase Change at T_m: m times lambda_fus]
    PhaseChange --> OutStream[Outlet Stream: Solid Crystals at T_m]
    OutStream --> Cooler[Further Cooling if Required]
    Cooler --> FinalProduct[Final Solid Product]
    SensHeat --- Note1[Heat Removed by Cooling Jacket]
    PhaseChange --- Note2[Latent Heat of Crystallization]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance**: The total cooling duty of a crystallizer is the sum of (i) the sensible heat of the feed cooling down to the melting/crystallization point, and (ii) the latent heat of fusion removed at the crystallization point. **Both terms must be included** in any KTU energy balance problem involving a crystallizer or freezer. Skipping the latent heat term is one of the most common mistakes students make.

---

## 4.3 P-T Diagram Schematic (Conceptual Description)

A **Pressure–Temperature phase diagram** for a pure substance is essential to understand the fusion curve. The key features are described below (since Mermaid cannot render a true 2-D phase plot, this is described structurally):

- **Triple Point** — The unique point where solid, liquid, and gas coexist. For water: $T_{tp} = 273.16 \text{ K}$, $P_{tp} = 611.73 \text{ Pa}$.
- **Fusion Curve** — The line separating solid and liquid. For most substances, it slopes **upward and to the right** (positive slope). For water, it slopes **upward and to the left** (negative slope) — the famous anomaly.
- **Vaporization Curve** — The line separating liquid and gas, ending at the critical point.
- **Sublimation Curve** — The line separating solid and gas.

The **Clapeyron equation for fusion** gives the slope of the solid–liquid line. The **Clapeyron equation for vaporization** gives the slope of the liquid–vapor line. **Both equations have the same mathematical form**, but the numerical values of $\Delta H$ and $\Delta V$ are vastly different.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Trick Question**: Examiners sometimes ask: *"Why is the latent heat of vaporization much larger than the latent heat of fusion for the same substance?"* The answer involves the relative magnitudes of $\Delta V$ and the energy required to completely break intermolecular bonds (vaporization) versus only partially loosening them (fusion).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the term "latent heat of fusion". State its SI units and explain why this heat is "latent".**

**Model Answer**:

The **latent heat of fusion** of a substance is the quantity of heat required to convert a unit mass (or one mole) of the substance from the solid state to the liquid state at its **normal melting point** and at **standard atmospheric pressure**, without any change in temperature.

- **SI Units**: kJ/kg (specific basis) or kJ/kmol (molar basis).
- **Why it is "latent"**: The word "latent" means *hidden*. The heat supplied during the phase change does **not** appear as a temperature rise on a thermometer; instead, it is entirely consumed in **overcoming the intermolecular cohesive forces** that hold the molecules in the rigid crystalline lattice of the solid. Only after the solid has fully melted does further heat input begin to raise the temperature of the resulting liquid.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: A solid substance has a latent heat of fusion of 250 kJ/kg. How much heat is required to completely melt 200 kg of this solid at its melting point?**

**Model Answer**:

Given: $\lambda_{fus} = 250 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $m = 200 \text{ kg}$.

Using the energy balance for the phase change:

$$Q_{fus} = m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$$

$$Q_{fus} = 200 \text{ kg} \times 250 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q_{fus} = 50{,}000 \text{ kJ} = 50 \text{ MJ}$$

The total heat required to completely melt 200 kg of the solid is **50,000 kJ (50 MJ)**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note**: In the End Semester Examination, Module 4 carries a 14-mark question. You are given a **choice** between two questions (one full-syllabus, one restricted). Below, both alternatives are provided with sub-parts (a) at 7 marks and (b) at 7 marks, as per the KTU 2024 scheme. **Solve at least one of these as part of your revision.**

---

### Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]**: 
Define the latent heat of fusion. The slope of the solid–liquid coexistence curve for a pure substance at its normal melting point of $400 \text{ K}$ is $3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/K}$. If the molar volume change on fusion is $5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$, calculate the molar latent heat of fusion of the substance.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]**: 
A crystallizer is fed with **1000 kg/h** of a saturated solution at **80 °C**. The product crystals leave the crystallizer at **30 °C** along with the mother liquor. Given:
- Specific heat of the solution: $C_p = 3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Latent heat of fusion (crystallization): $\lambda_{fus} = 220 \text{ kJ/kg}$ of crystals
- Mass fraction of crystals in the outlet slurry: $w_{crystals} = 0.30$
- Specific heat of crystals: $C_{p,cryst} = 2.0 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

Calculate the **total cooling duty** (kW) required by the crystallizer.

---

### Model Solution — Question Choice A

#### Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — State the Clapeyron equation**:
$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}$$

**Step 2 — Identify given data**:
- $T_m = 400 \text{ K}$
- $dP/dT = 3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/K}$
- $\Delta V_{fus} = 5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the Clapeyron equation to solve for $\Delta H_{fus}$**:
$$\Delta H_{fus} = T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus} \cdot \frac{dP}{dT}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values**:
$$\Delta H_{fus} = 400 \text{ K} \times (5.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}) \times (3.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa/K})$$

**Step 5 — Compute the numerical answer**:
$$\Delta H_{fus} = 400 \times 5.0 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-6+6} \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\Delta H_{fus} = 400 \times 15.0 \text{ J/mol} = 6{,}000 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{fus} = 6.0 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

**Final Statement**: The molar latent heat of fusion of the substance is **6.0 kJ/mol**.

---

#### Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the streams and the energy terms**:

The total cooling duty $Q_{total}$ has two components:
1. **Sensible heat of the feed cooling from 80 °C to 30 °C**.
2. **Latent heat of crystallization** released when crystals form from the solution.

> **Assumption**: The crystallization occurs at the final temperature of 30 °C (this is the standard simplification in KTU problems — the latent heat is removed at the lowest temperature of the system).

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate of crystals**:

Given: $w_{crystals} = 0.30$, total feed $F = 1000 \text{ kg/h}$.

$$\dot{m}_{crystals} = 0.30 \times 1000 \text{ kg/h} = 300 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{solution,\,cooling} = 1000 \text{ kg/h} \quad \text{(entire feed is cooled to 30 °C)}$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat removed from the feed**:

$$\Delta T = 80 - 30 = 50 \text{ K}$$

$$Q_{sensible} = \dot{m}_{feed} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 1000 \text{ kg/h} \times 3.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} \times 50 \text{ K}$$

$$Q_{sensible} = 175{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Latent heat of crystallization released**:

$$Q_{latent} = \dot{m}_{crystals} \cdot \lambda_{fus}$$

$$Q_{latent} = 300 \text{ kg/h} \times 220 \text{ kJ/kg} = 66{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Total cooling duty**:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}$$

$$Q_{total} = 175{,}000 + 66{,}000 = 241{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to kW** (divide by 3600 to convert kJ/h to kW):

$$Q_{total} = \frac{241{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} \approx 66.94 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} \approx 66.94 \text{ kW}}$$

**Final Statement**: The total cooling duty required by the crystallizer is **66.94 kW (or 241,000 kJ/h)**.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks Total — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]**:
The normal melting point of a substance is $350 \text{ K}$ at $1 \text{ atm}$. The latent heat of fusion is $8.0 \text{ kJ/mol}$ and the molar volume change on fusion is $7.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$. 
- (i) Calculate the slope $dP/dT$ of the fusion curve at the normal melting point.
- (ii) Estimate the new melting point if the pressure is increased to $50 \text{ atm}$.

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]**:
**200 kg** of a solid at its melting point of $60 \text{ °C$ is to be completely melted. The latent heat of fusion is **150 kJ/kg**. If the melting process is carried out in **30 minutes**, calculate the **average rate of heat supply** (kW) required.

---

### Model Solution — Question Choice B

#### Solution to (a):

**Part (i) — Calculate the slope $dP/dT$**:

Given: $T_m = 350 \text{ K}$, $\Delta H_{fus} = 8.0 \text{ kJ/mol} = 8000 \text{ J/mol}$, $\Delta V_{fus} = 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$.

Apply the Clapeyron equation:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}$$

Substitute:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{8000 \text{ J/mol}}{350 \text{ K} \times 7.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}}$$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{8000}{2.45 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ Pa/K}$$

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = 3.265 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa/K} \approx 3.27 \text{ MPa/K}$$

**Part (ii) — Calculate the new melting point at $P_2 = 50 \text{ atm}$**:

Pressure change: $\Delta P = 50 - 1 = 49 \text{ atm} = 49 \times 101{,}325 \text{ Pa} = 4.965 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$.

Using the linearized Clapeyron form:

$$\Delta T = \frac{T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}{\Delta H_{fus}} \cdot \Delta P$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{350 \times 7.0 \times 10^{-6}}{8000} \times 4.965 \times 10^6 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{350 \times 7.0 \times 4.965 \times 10^{-6+6}}{8000} \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{350 \times 7.0 \times 4.965}{8000} \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{12{,}164.25}{8000} \text{ K} \approx 1.52 \text{ K}$$

**Final Statement**: The new melting point is $T_m' = 350 + 1.52 = 351.52 \text{ K}$ (an **increase** of 1.52 K), consistent with the positive slope $dP/dT > 0$ for this substance.

---

#### Solution to (b):

Given: $m = 200 \text{ kg}$, $\lambda_{fus} = 150 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $t = 30 \text{ min} = 1800 \text{ s}$.

**Step 1 — Total heat required for melting**:
$$Q = m \cdot \lambda_{fus} = 200 \times 150 = 30{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 2 — Average rate of heat supply**:
$$\dot{Q} = \frac{Q}{t} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ kJ}}{1800 \text{ s}} \approx 16.67 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q} \approx 16.67 \text{ kW}}$$

**Final Statement**: The average rate of heat supply needed to melt 200 kg of the solid in 30 minutes is **16.67 kW**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Latent Heat of Fusion Problems**:
> 
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (2–3 marks lost)**: Mixing $\text{kJ/kmol}$ with $\text{m}^3/\text{mol}$, or using $\text{atm}$ instead of $\text{Pa}$ in the Clapeyron equation. **Always convert everything to SI** before substituting.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the Sign of $\Delta V_{fus}$ (2 marks lost)**: For water, $\Delta V_{fus}$ is **negative** (ice is less dense than water). The slope of the fusion curve is therefore negative. If the problem involves water, double-check the direction of melting-point change.
> 
> 3. **Omitting the Latent Heat Term in Crystallizer Energy Balances (3–4 marks lost)**: The single most common error. The total cooling/heating duty = sensible heat **PLUS** latent heat. Students frequently calculate only the sensible heat and forget the $m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$ term.
> 
> 4. **Confusing Heat of Fusion with Heat of Solidification**: Numerically, they are the same magnitude. The sign convention differs: fusion is **endothermic** (absorbs heat, positive), solidification is **exothermic** (releases heat, negative in the energy balance).
> 
> 5. **Using Trouton's Rule or Kistyakowsky's Equation for Fusion**: These empirical rules apply specifically to **vaporization**, **not** to fusion. Examiners deduct marks for misapplying them.
> 
> 6. **Not Stating the Reference State**: The latent heat of fusion is defined at the **normal melting point**. If the problem operates at a different temperature, the value must be corrected using the Watson-type or Clapeyron approach — don't blindly use the tabulated value.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition**: $\lambda_{fus}$ = heat to convert unit mass (or mole) of solid → liquid at its normal melting point, $T_m$, with no temperature change. Units: **kJ/kg** or **kJ/kmol**.
- **Memory Constants for Water**: $\lambda_{fus} = 333.5 \text{ kJ/kg} = 6.01 \text{ kJ/mol}$ at $T_m = 273.15 \text{ K}$.
- **Basic Energy Equation**: $Q_{fus} = m \cdot \lambda_{fus}$ (or $n \cdot \Delta H_{fus}$ on a molar basis).
- **Clapeyron Equation (Solid–Liquid)**: $\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{fus}}{T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus}}$ — derived from $G_{solid} = G_{liquid}$ and the identity $dG = -S\,dT + V\,dP$.
- **Clausius–Clapeyron (Exponential)**: $\ln(P_2/P_1) = (\Delta H_{fus}/R)(1/T_1 - 1/T_2)$ — used when $\Delta H_{fus}$ and $\Delta V$ can be treated as constants.
- **Linearized Clapeyron**: $\Delta T = (T_m \cdot \Delta V_{fus} / \Delta H_{fus}) \cdot \Delta P$ — for small changes in pressure.
- **Sign of Slope**: Positive for most substances ($\Delta V_{fus} > 0$); negative for water-like anomalies ($\Delta V_{fus} < 0$).
- **Watson-Type Correction**: $\lambda_{fus,T_2} / \lambda_{fus,T_1} = \left[(T_c - T_2)/(T_c - T_1)\right]^{0.38}$ — rarely used in practice for fusion; mostly for vaporization.
- **Crystallizer Energy Balance**: Total cooling duty = Sensible heat of feed cooling + Latent heat of crystallization — **never forget the latent term**.
- **Universal Gas Constant**: $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)} = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$ — used only in the exponential Clausius–Clapeyron form.
- **Distinguish from Vaporization**: Trouton's rule ($M\lambda_{vap}/T_b \approx 88 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$) and Kistyakowsky's equation apply to **vaporization only** — not to fusion.
- **Industrial Applications**: Crystallizers, latent heat thermal storage (PCMs), ice-bath cooling, freeze-drying, metal casting.
- **Examination Pattern**: KTU Part A = 3-mark definition or single-step numerical; KTU Part B = 7+7 marks, typically (a) derivation or Clapeyron-based calculation, (b) energy balance with both sensible and latent terms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat of Vaporization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Heat of Vaporization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heat of Vaporization (Latent Heat of Vaporization, $\Delta H_v$)** is rigorously defined as the quantity of thermal energy (enthalpy change) that must be supplied to a unit quantity of a liquid substance, at constant pressure and constant temperature, to completely transform it from the saturated liquid state into the saturated vapor state, without any change in the system temperature.

Mathematically, it is expressed as the isothermal, isobaric enthalpy change:

$$\Delta H_v = H_g - H_l \quad \text{[at saturation]}$$

Where:
- $H_g$ = specific enthalpy of saturated vapor (kJ/kg or kJ/kmol)
- $H_l$ = specific enthalpy of saturated liquid (kJ/kg or kJ/kmol)
- $\Delta H_v$ = latent heat of vaporization (kJ/kg or kJ/kmol)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Heat of vaporization is **NOT a fixed constant** — it is a strong function of temperature. It is maximum at the **triple point** and decreases monotonically to **zero at the critical point**. This dependence forms the basis of several empirical correlations students must master.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **crowded dance floor at a wedding** (the liquid state — molecules packed close, holding hands, vibrating). To turn this into an open party hall where people are freely roaming (the vapor state), you need to spend a lot of energy **politely asking everyone to let go of each other's hands** and **shuffling them outward**. That "asking-and-shuffling" energy is exactly the **Heat of Vaporization** — you are not making anyone dance *faster* (temperature is unchanged), you are only changing their *closeness* (phase).

- **Sensible heat** = Energy to make people dance *faster* (temperature rises).
- **Latent heat of vaporization** = Energy to make people move *apart* (phase changes, T constant).

> [!NOTE]
> The numerical value of $\Delta H_v$ is typically in the range of **$\mathbf{100 \text{ to } 1000 \text{ kJ/kg}}$** for most common fluids. Water at **100 °C** has $\Delta H_v \approx \mathbf{2257 \text{ kJ/kg}}$ — among the highest of any common substance, which is why it is such an efficient heat-transfer fluid.

## 1.3 Related Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | $\Delta H_v$ | Heat absorbed during vaporization (endothermic) |
| Latent Heat of Condensation | $\Delta H_c$ | Heat released during condensation (exothermic) |
| Sensible Heat | $Q_s$ | Heat causing temperature change, no phase change |
| Enthalpy of Vaporization | $\Delta H_{vap}$ | Synonym for $\Delta H_v$ (preferred in IUPAC) |
| Molar Heat of Vaporization | $\Delta H_v$ | When expressed per kmol (kJ/kmol) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Identity:** $\Delta H_{vaporization} = \Delta H_{condensation}$ in magnitude, but opposite in sign. Process calculations use magnitudes unless tracking energy balance signs explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does $\Delta H_v$ Decrease With Temperature?

A student must internalize the *physical reasoning* before memorizing correlations:

1. **Near the triple point**, intermolecular forces are at their maximum relative strength. The liquid is "tightly bound." A large energy input is needed to overcome cohesion. → $\Delta H_v$ is **maximum**.
2. **As temperature rises**, the liquid expands, average intermolecular distance grows, and cohesive forces weaken.
3. **At the critical point ($T_c$)**, the saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases **become identical** in all intensive properties (density, enthalpy, etc.). There is no longer a "phase boundary" to cross. → $\Delta H_v = 0$.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** the KTU board examiner expects you to know for ESE and internal assessments on this topic. Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Applicability / Limitation |
|---|--------------|----------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Clausius–Clapeyron (differential) | $\dfrac{d(\ln P^{sat})}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_v}{R T^2}$ | Thermodynamically exact, requires $\Delta H_v(T)$ |
| 2 | Clausius–Clapeyron (integrated) | $\ln \dfrac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_v}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1}-\dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$ | Assumes $\Delta H_v$ constant in $T_1 \to T_2$ |
| 3 | Clausius–Clapeyron (integrated, $\Delta H_v$ variable) | $\ln \dfrac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}} = \dfrac{1}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\dfrac{\Delta H_v(T)}{T^2}\,dT$ | Exact form, requires functional $\Delta H_v(T)$ |
| 4 | **Watson's Correlation** | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} = \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$ | Empirical, accurate to ~5%, needs $T_c$ |
| 5 | Trouton's Rule | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,b}}{T_b} \approx 88 \;\text{J/(mol·K)}$ | Approximate; fails for H-bonded / associated liquids |
| 6 | **Kistiakowsky Equation** | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,b}}{R T_b} = 8.75 + \ln T_b$ | Better than Trouton; $T_b$ in K, $\Delta H_v$ in J/mol |
| 7 | Riedel's Correlation | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,b}}{R T_c} = \dfrac{1.092 (\ln P_c - 5.67714)}{0.930 - T_{br}}$ | Uses $P_c$ (atm) and $T_{br} = T_b/T_c$ |
| 8 | Chen's Correlation | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,b}}{R T_c} = \dfrac{3.978\, T_{br} - 3.958 + 1.555 \ln P_c}{1.07 - T_{br}}$ | More accurate for polar substances |
| 9 | Heat of Sublimation | $\Delta H_{sub} = \Delta H_{fus} + \Delta H_{vap}$ | Triple point (Kirchhoff's law application) |
| 10 | Latent Heat (specific form) | $\Delta H_v = \lambda \cdot M$ | Where $\lambda$ = J/g, $M$ = molar mass (g/mol) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Vigilance:** When using Clausius–Clapeyron, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) requires $\Delta H_v$ in **J/mol**. When using Watson, $T$ values must be in **K** and $\Delta H_v$ in consistent units on both sides of the equation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation Column Design:** Reboiler duty $Q_{reb} = \dot{m} \cdot \Delta H_v$ — wrong $\Delta H_v$ means undersized heater, flooded reboiler, or off-spec product.
- **Evaporator Sizing:** Multiple-effect evaporator steam economy depends directly on accurate $\Delta H_v$ at each stage pressure.
- **Refrigeration Cycles:** COP depends on refrigerant $\Delta H_v$ at evaporator conditions.
- **Process Safety:** Relief valve sizing for runaway reactions requires correct $\Delta H_v$ of flashed liquids.
- **Power Generation:** Rankine cycle efficiency hinges on enthalpy difference between boiler exit and condenser inlet.

## 2.4 Selection Logic — Which Formula to Use When?

```
IF ΔHv is known at one temperature
   AND you need it at another temperature
   AND you know Tc
   USE Watson's Correlation.
ELSE IF you only know normal boiling point
   USE Kistiakowsky or Trouton's Rule.
ELSE IF you have two vapor-pressure data points
   USE integrated Clausius–Clapeyron.
ELSE IF you have Tc and Pc
   USE Riedel or Chen correlation.
```
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Clausius–Clapeyron Equation (Differential Form)

We begin from the **Clapeyron equation**, which is exact for any phase transition:

$$\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{T\, \Delta V}$$

Where $\Delta V = V_g - V_l$ is the molar volume change during vaporization.

**Step 1 — Ideal-Gas Assumption for Vapor:**
At low-to-moderate pressures, $V_g \gg V_l$, so $\Delta V \approx V_g$, and using ideal gas law $V_g = RT/P$:

$$\Delta V \approx V_g = \frac{RT}{P^{sat}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Clapeyron Equation:**

$$\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{T \cdot \left(\dfrac{RT}{P^{sat}}\right)} = \frac{\Delta H_v \cdot P^{sat}}{R T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to the Canonical Form:**

$$\frac{dP^{sat}}{P^{sat}} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R T^2}\,dT \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d(\ln P^{sat})}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R T^2}$$

This is the **Clausius–Clapeyron differential equation**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation B: Integrated Clausius–Clapeyron (Constant $\Delta H_v$)

**Step 1 — Assume** $\Delta H_v$ is approximately constant over $[T_1, T_2]$ (valid for narrow temperature ranges).

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate from state 1 to state 2:**

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP^{sat}}{P^{sat}} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\frac{dT}{T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left side:**

$$\ln P \Big\vert_{P_1}^{P_2} = \ln \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the right side:**

$$\frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\left[-\frac{1}{T}\right]_{T_1}^{T_2} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\left(-\frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_1}\right) = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$\boxed{\;\ln \frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "$\frac{1}{T_1}-\frac{1}{T_2}$" and not "$\frac{1}{T_2}-\frac{1}{T_1}$"?** Sign convention: at higher $T$, $P^{sat}$ is higher, so $\ln(P_2/P_1) > 0$, requiring the bracket to be positive. If $T_2 > T_1$, then $\frac{1}{T_1} > \frac{1}{T_2}$ — consistent.

## 3.3 Derivation C: Watson's Correlation (Empirical Foundation)

Watson's correlation is empirical but can be motivated by the Clausius–Clapeyron equation under the assumption that $\Delta H_v$ varies as a power of $(T_c - T)$:

$$\Delta H_v = K\,(T_c - T)^n$$

Substituting into Clausius–Clapeyron and matching experimental vapor-pressure data for many substances, the exponent $n \approx 0.38$ gives the best fit.

**Step 1 — Write the assumed form:**

$$\Delta H_v(T) = K\,(T_c - T)^{0.38}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio at two temperatures:**

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} = \frac{K(T_c - T_2)^{0.38}}{K(T_c - T_1)^{0.38}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the empirical constant $K$:**

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} = \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary condition check:** At $T = T_c$, the right-hand side becomes $(0)^{0.38} = 0$, so $\Delta H_{v,2} = 0$. This correctly enforces the thermodynamic identity that latent heat vanishes at the critical point.

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern (14-Mark Standard)

**Problem (from KTU ESE pattern):** The latent heat of vaporization of benzene at its normal boiling point (353.3 K) is 30.8 kJ/mol. The critical temperature of benzene is 562.1 K. Estimate the latent heat of vaporization of benzene at 400 K using:
**(a)** Watson's correlation.
**(b)** Trouton's rule, then refine to a comparison with Watson.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- $T_1 = T_b = 353.3$ K
- $\Delta H_{v,1} = 30.8$ kJ/mol
- $T_c = 562.1$ K
- $T_2 = 400$ K

**Part (a) — Watson's Correlation:**

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{30.8} = \left(\frac{562.1 - 400}{562.1 - 353.3}\right)^{0.38} = \left(\frac{162.1}{208.8}\right)^{0.38}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the ratio inside the parenthesis:**

$$\frac{162.1}{208.8} = 0.7765$$

**Step 2 — Raise to the power 0.38:**

$$(0.7765)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \cdot \ln(0.7765)} = e^{0.38 \cdot (-0.2530)} = e^{-0.0961} = 0.9084$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta H_{v,2}$:**

$$\Delta H_{v,2} = 30.8 \times 0.9084 = \mathbf{27.98 \;\text{kJ/mol}}$$

**Part (b) — Trouton's Rule Check:**

$$\Delta H_{v,b} = 88 \times T_b = 88 \times 353.3 = 31{,}090 \;\text{J/mol} = 31.09 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

The Trouton estimate (31.09 kJ/mol) is close to the given value (30.8 kJ/mol), confirming benzene is a "normal" (non-associated) fluid. The small overestimation is typical.

**Conclusion for board answer:**
> Using Watson's correlation, the latent heat of vaporization of benzene at 400 K is **27.98 kJ/mol**. This represents a **9.2% decrease** from its value at the normal boiling point, consistent with the expected trend.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of $\Delta H_v$ to Temperature

| Reduced Temperature $T_r = T/T_c$ | $\Delta H_v / \Delta H_{v,b}$ (Watson) | Engineering Implication |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0.50 | 1.13 | Cryogenic storage; very high latent heat |
| 0.70 | 1.00 (by definition at $T_b$ for many) | Typical distillation operation |
| 0.85 | 0.79 | High-pressure distillation |
| 0.95 | 0.45 | Approach to critical; sharp decline |
| 0.99 | 0.16 | Supercritical boundary |
| 1.00 | 0.00 | Critical point — phase boundary vanishes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The fact that $\Delta H_v$ drops by ~80% as $T_r$ goes from 0.5 to 0.95 is why **high-pressure distillation columns have much smaller reboilers and condensers** than atmospheric columns for the same throughput.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Heat of Vaporization Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Need Heat of Vaporization] --> B{Available Data?}
    B --> C1[Known ΔHv at one T]
    B --> C2[Only normal boiling point Tb]
    B --> C3[Two vapor pressure data points]
    B --> C4[Know Tc and Pc]

    C1 --> D1{Is Tc known?}
    D1 -- YES --> E1[Apply Watson's Correlation]
    D1 -- NO --> E2[Use Clausius-Clapeyron with experimental Pvap data]

    C2 --> F1[Apply Trouton's Rule]
    F1 --> F2{Is the fluid polar or H-bonded?}
    F2 -- NO --> G1[Use Kistiakowsky Equation]
    F2 -- YES --> G2[Use Chen's Correlation]

    C3 --> H1[Apply Integrated Clausius-Clapeyron]

    C4 --> I1[Apply Riedel's Correlation]
    I1 --> I2{Polar substance?}
    I2 -- YES --> I3[Use Chen's Correlation instead]
    I2 -- NO --> J1[Output ΔHv at Tb]

    E1 --> K1[Output ΔHv at T2]
    E2 --> K1
    G1 --> K1
    G2 --> K1
    H1 --> K1
    J1 --> K1

    K1 --> L[End: Final ΔHv value with units and uncertainty band]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow During Vaporization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Saturated Liquid at T1] -->|Qin = m × ΔHv| B[Saturated Vapor at T1]
    A -. Pre-heating (Sensible Heat Qs) .-> A
    B -. Superheating (Sensible Heat Qs) .-> C[Superheated Vapor at T2]

    subgraph Energy_Inputs
    D[Q_sensible_in]
    E[Q_latent_ΔHv]
    F[Q_sensible_out]
    end

    D --> A
    E --> A
    B --> F
    F --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The horizontal arrow from **A → B** carries the **latent heat $\Delta H_v$** (phase change at constant $T$). The dashed "pre-heating" and "superheating" arrows represent **sensible heat** contributions — separate from $\Delta H_v$ in the total energy balance:
> $$Q_{total} = m\,C_{p,l}\,(T_1 - T_{feed}) + m\,\Delta H_v + m\,C_{p,g}\,(T_{super} - T_1)$$

## 4.3 Mermaid Graph — Variation of $\Delta H_v$ With Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph X_Axis [Temperature Scale]
    X1[T_triple] --> X2[T_normal_boiling] --> X3[T_0.85Tc] --> X4[T_critical]
    end

    subgraph Y_Axis [Latent Heat Scale]
    Y1[ΔHv_max ~ 100 percent] --> Y2[ΔHv ~ 90 percent] --> Y3[ΔHv ~ 45 percent] --> Y4[ΔHv = 0]
    end

    X1 --- Y1
    X2 --- Y2
    X3 --- Y3
    X4 --- Y4

    style Y1 fill:#ffcc00
    style Y2 fill:#ffeeaa
    style Y3 fill:#ffeecc
    style Y4 fill:#ff6666
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The latent heat curve falls steeply only in the last ~15% of the temperature range below $T_c$. This is why Watson's **exponent of 0.38** — not 1.0 or 0.5 — best matches the curvature.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Define the term "Latent Heat of Vaporization." How does it vary with temperature? What is its value at the critical point?**

**Model Answer:**
Latent heat of vaporization is the amount of heat required to convert a unit mass (or one mole) of a liquid into vapor at constant temperature and pressure. It decreases as temperature increases, reaching **zero at the critical temperature**, since the distinction between liquid and vapor phases disappears there. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State Trouton's rule and mention its limitation.**

**Model Answer:**
Trouton's rule states that the molar entropy of vaporization at the normal boiling point is approximately constant for most liquids:

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,b}}{T_b} \approx 88 \;\text{J/(mol·K)}$$

**Limitation:** It is **inaccurate for associated liquids** (e.g., water, alcohols, acids) where hydrogen bonding leads to anomalously high $\Delta H_v$ values, and for very low-boiling substances (e.g., He, H₂) where quantum effects dominate. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the Clausius–Clapeyron equation starting from the Clapeyron equation, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The vapor pressure of water is 1.01325 bar at 373.15 K and 0.1224 bar at 323.15 K. Using the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation, calculate the average latent heat of vaporization over this temperature range. State the percentage error relative to the experimental value of 43.4 kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:** *(Full derivation is given in Section 3.1 above. The board answer should reproduce the following key steps:)*

1. Start from Clapeyron: $\dfrac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_v}{T \Delta V}$
2. State the assumption: $V_l \ll V_g$ (volume of liquid is negligible compared to vapor).
3. Apply ideal gas law: $V_g = RT/P^{sat}$.
4. Substitute to get: $\dfrac{dP^{sat}}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_v \cdot P^{sat}}{R T^2}$
5. Rearrange: $\dfrac{d(\ln P^{sat})}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_v}{R T^2}$ — the **Clausius–Clapeyron equation**.

> **[7 marks distributed: 1 mark for Clapeyron start, 2 marks for assumption + ideal gas, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for final form.]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1.01325$ bar, $T_1 = 373.15$ K
- $P_2 = 0.1224$ bar, $T_2 = 323.15$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- Experimental: $\Delta H_{v,exp} = 43.4$ kJ/mol

**Step 1 — Apply the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**

$$\ln \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the left side:**

$$\ln \frac{1.01325}{0.1224} = \ln(8.277) = 2.1137$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the right-side bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1} = \frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{373.15}$$

$$= 0.003094 - 0.002680 = 4.14 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta H_v$:**

$$\Delta H_v = \frac{R \cdot \ln(P_1/P_2)}{(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)} = \frac{8.314 \times 2.1137}{4.14 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$= \frac{17.57}{4.14 \times 10^{-4}} = 42{,}439 \;\text{J/mol} = \mathbf{42.44 \;\text{kJ/mol}}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage error:**

$$\% \text{error} = \left|\frac{42.44 - 43.4}{43.4}\right| \times 100 = \mathbf{2.21 \%}$$

> **Final Answer:** Average $\Delta H_v = 42.44$ kJ/mol, which is within 2.21% of the experimental value. **(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for evaluation, 2 for final answer, 1 for percentage error.]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** What is Watson's correlation? Derive the relationship and explain its physical significance. State any one limitation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The latent heat of vaporization of n-hexane at its normal boiling point (341.9 K) is 28.85 kJ/mol. The critical temperature of n-hexane is 507.4 K. Calculate the latent heat of vaporization of n-hexane at:
   (i) 400 K
   (ii) 450 K **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Watson's Correlation:** *(Full derivation in Section 3.3.)*

Watson's correlation is an empirical equation that estimates the latent heat of vaporization of a substance at any temperature, given its value at a reference temperature and the critical temperature:

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} = \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$$

**Physical Significance:** It reflects that the latent heat vanishes as $T \to T_c$ (where the liquid and vapor phases become indistinguishable) and is maximum at the triple point. The exponent 0.38 is empirically fitted to vapor-pressure data of many substances.

**Limitation:** It is purely empirical — has no rigorous thermodynamic basis. Accuracy degrades for highly polar or associated substances. It also requires an accurate value of $T_c$ as input.

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $\Delta H_{v,1} = 28.85$ kJ/mol at $T_1 = 341.9$ K
- $T_c = 507.4$ K

**(i) At $T_2 = 400$ K:**

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{28.85} = \left(\frac{507.4 - 400}{507.4 - 341.9}\right)^{0.38} = \left(\frac{107.4}{165.5}\right)^{0.38}$$

$$= (0.6490)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \cdot \ln(0.6490)} = e^{0.38 \cdot (-0.4327)} = e^{-0.1644} = 0.8484$$

$$\Delta H_{v,2} = 28.85 \times 0.8484 = \mathbf{24.48 \;\text{kJ/mol}}$$

**(ii) At $T_2 = 450$ K:**

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{28.85} = \left(\frac{507.4 - 450}{507.4 - 341.9}\right)^{0.38} = \left(\frac{57.4}{165.5}\right)^{0.38}$$

$$= (0.3468)^{0.38} = e^{0.38 \cdot \ln(0.3468)} = e^{0.38 \cdot (-1.0594)} = e^{-0.4026} = 0.6685$$

$$\Delta H_{v,2} = 28.85 \times 0.6685 = \mathbf{19.29 \;\text{kJ/mol}}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\Delta H_v$ at 400 K = **24.48 kJ/mol**; $\Delta H_v$ at 450 K = **19.29 kJ/mol**. The values decrease with rising temperature, consistent with theory.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Temperature unit errors:** Forgetting to convert °C to K before substitution in Watson's equation. *This is the #1 reason for full-mark loss.* Always confirm: $T_c$ in **K**.
> 2. **Sign errors in Clausius–Clapeyron:** Writing $\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$ instead of $\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$. Memorize: **"the smaller T denominator goes first"** so that the difference is positive when $P_2 > P_1$.
> 3. **Inconsistent units:** Using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) with $\Delta H_v$ in kJ/mol. The unit conversion **must** be explicit in the board answer.
> 4. **Watson exponent confusion:** Writing 0.38 as 0.038 or 3.8. This single-digit error changes the answer by orders of magnitude.
> 5. **Forgetting the assumption $V_g \gg V_l$** in Clausius–Clapeyron derivation — full marks require the assumption to be explicitly written.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam!**

- ☐ **Definition:** $\Delta H_v$ = enthalpy change for isothermal, isobaric liquid → vapor transition.
- ☐ **Trend:** $\Delta H_v$ is **maximum at triple point**, **zero at critical point**, monotonically decreasing in between.
- ☐ **Watson's Correlation:** $\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} = \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$ — all T in K.
- ☐ **Clausius–Clapeyron (diff):** $\dfrac{d(\ln P^{sat})}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_v}{R T^2}$
- ☐ **Clausius–Clapeyron (int, const $\Delta H_v$):** $\ln \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{\Delta H_v}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)$
- ☐ **Trouton's Rule:** $\Delta H_{v,b} / T_b \approx 88$ J/(mol·K) — fails for H-bonded fluids.
- ☐ **Kistiakowsky:** $\Delta H_{v,b} / (R T_b) = 8.75 + \ln T_b$ — $T_b$ in K, $\Delta H_v$ in J/mol.
- ☐ **Riedel & Chen:** Use $T_c$ and $P_c$ when no $\Delta H_v$ data is available.
- ☐ **Vaporization = Condensation** in magnitude (signs differ).
- ☐ **Boundary conditions:** $\Delta H_v(T_c) = 0$; $\Delta H_v(T_{triple}) = $ maximum.
- ☐ **Engineering uses:** Distillation reboilers, evaporators, refrigeration, safety relief design.
- ☐ **Always state assumptions** explicitly in derivation questions: $V_l \ll V_g$, ideal gas, $\Delta H_v$ constant (or not).
- ☐ **Unit discipline:** Pick a system (SI vs CGS) and **stick to it** throughout a problem.
- ☐ **Watson exponent = 0.38**, not 0.038, not 3.8 — memorize this exactly.
- ☐ **Examiner loves** the phrase *"at the critical point, the saturated liquid and vapor phases become identical, hence $\Delta H_v = 0$"* — use it!

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Heat of Vaporization**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Heat of Vaporization - Kistyakowsky Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Heat of Vaporization - Kistyakowsky Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heat of Vaporization (ΔHv)** is defined as the quantity of energy (enthalpy) that must be supplied to one mole (or unit mass) of a liquid at its boiling point, at constant pressure, to convert it entirely into vapor at the same temperature. It is an intensive thermodynamic property fundamental to the design of evaporators, distillation columns, condensers, reboilers, and heat-exchanger networks.

The **Kistyakowsky Equation** (also called the **Trouton–Kistyakowsky Rule**) is an empirical correlation proposed by W. Kistyakowsky (often presented in KTU textbooks as a refinement of Trouton's rule) for estimating the molar heat of vaporization of **non-polar, non-associated liquids** at their normal boiling point when experimental data are unavailable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCHT205 – Module 4):**
> Estimation of heat of vaporization is a *must-know* topic. The Kistyakowsky equation, Watson's correlation, and Clapeyron-type latent-heat relationships are frequently asked in Part A (2/3 marks) and form the numerical core of Part B (14 marks) problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Boiling Off a Lake"

Imagine a small lake on a hot summer day. The water molecules at the surface are continuously breaking free into the atmosphere (evaporation). To make this happen, each molecule needs a *minimum* amount of energy to overcome the attractive pull of its neighbours. Heat of vaporization is simply the **"escape energy"** per mole of molecules.

Now imagine trying to do this for two very different lakes:

- **Lake 1 (e.g., hexane):** Molecules barely hold hands — little escape energy needed.
- **Lake 2 (e.g., water):** Molecules grip tightly via hydrogen bonds — much more energy needed.

Kistyakowsky observed that, for *non-sticky* (non-polar) lakes, the escape energy scales almost **linearly with the boiling temperature**. Higher boiling point ⇒ stronger intermolecular forces ⇒ more energy required to vaporize. The proportionality constant turns out to be very close to **8.75 cal/(mol·K)**.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 1.987 \text{ cal/(mol·K)} = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
> - **Conversion:** $1 \text{ cal} = 4.1868 \text{ J}$
> - **Normal Boiling Point ($T_b$):** Boiling temperature at $1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$
> - **Critical Temperature ($T_c$):** Temperature above which the liquid–vapor distinction vanishes.

### 1.4 Governing Equation — Kistyakowsky (Original Form)

The Kistyakowsky equation relates the molar enthalpy of vaporization at the normal boiling point to the normal boiling temperature:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H_{v,b} \;=\; 8.75 \, T_b\;}$$

where:

- $\Delta H_{v,b}$ = molar heat of vaporization at the normal boiling point, in **cal/mol**
- $T_b$ = normal boiling point, in **K**

In SI units (J/mol), the constant becomes $8.75 \times 4.1868 \approx 36.63$:

$$\Delta H_{v,b} \;=\; 36.63 \, T_b \quad \text{(J/mol, with } T_b \text{ in K)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The constant **8.75** is strictly valid for **non-polar, non-associated liquids** (e.g., n-alkanes, benzene, carbon tetrachloride, oxygen, nitrogen). For **polar or hydrogen-bonded liquids** (water, alcohols, acids), the constant rises significantly (Riedel proposed ≈ 4.55·T_b for some, but most polar substances need experimental data). KTU examiners love testing this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin and Physical Basis of the Kistyakowsky Equation

Kistyakowsky (1923) refined Trouton's empirical rule, which stated that the entropy of vaporization at the normal boiling point is roughly constant ($\Delta S_v \approx 21 \text{ cal/(mol·K)}$) for most liquids. The Kistyakowsky refinement replaces the *constant* entropy assumption with a *temperature-dependent* expression that gives a more accurate slope for the enthalpy of vaporization of non-polar substances.

Mathematically, since $\Delta H_v = T_b \cdot \Delta S_v$ at the boiling point, and Trouton found $\Delta S_v \approx 21$ for many liquids, one would naively expect $\Delta H_v \approx 21\,T_b$. Kistyakowsky, however, fitted experimental data on **non-polar** substances and obtained a slope closer to **8.75 cal/(mol·K)** for the *energy* (not entropy) of vaporization, after accounting for compressibility corrections.

### 2.2 Logical Decomposition — Why "8.75"?

- **Step 1:** Start with Trouton's rule: $\Delta S_{v,b} \approx 21 \text{ cal/(mol·K)}$.
- **Step 2:** Realize that $\Delta S_{v,b}$ is *not* exactly constant — it slightly *increases* with $T_b$ for non-polar liquids.
- **Step 3:** Kistyakowsky correlated $\Delta S_{v,b}$ linearly with $T_b$ using a virial-type correction that involved the compressibility factor $Z$ of the saturated vapor.
- **Step 4:** The resulting fit for $\Delta H_{v,b}$ vs $T_b$ produced the clean linear relation $\Delta H_{v,b} = 8.75\,T_b$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Kistyakowsky equation is essentially a *group-contribution-free* shortcut. When you have a brand-new organic molecule for which no latent-heat data exist, but you know its normal boiling point, this equation gives you a back-of-the-envelope estimate good to within ~5–10 % for non-polar compounds.

### 2.3 Extension Beyond the Boiling Point — Watson's Equation

A frequent KTU question: *"What if you need $\Delta H_v$ at a temperature $T$ that is NOT the normal boiling point?"* The answer is **Watson's Correlation**, which is almost always paired with Kistyakowsky in Module 4:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} \;=\; \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}\;}$$

where:

- $\Delta H_{v,1}$ = heat of vaporization at known temperature $T_1$ (often $T_b$)
- $\Delta H_{v,2}$ = heat of vaporization at desired temperature $T_2$
- $T_c$ = critical temperature of the substance

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent **0.38** is the classic Watson value. Some modern references use 0.375 or 0.39, but 0.38 is the KTU board standard.

### 2.4 Combined Procedure (KTU "Two-Step" Pattern)

1. **Step A — Kistyakowsky:** Estimate $\Delta H_{v,b}$ at $T_b$ using $\Delta H_{v,b} = 8.75\,T_b$.
2. **Step B — Watson:** Convert to any other temperature $T$ using the Watson correlation.
3. **Step C — Mass basis:** Divide molar value by molecular weight $M$ to get $\Delta H_v$ in kJ/kg.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Distillation column design:** Reboiler and condenser duties depend directly on $\Delta H_v$.
- **Refrigeration cycle analysis:** Working fluid's latent heat must be estimated during early design.
- **Cryogenic storage (LNG, LOX):** Latent heat at low temperatures is required; Watson's equation starting from $T_b$ is the standard tool.
- **Process safety:** Vapor cloud explosions and BLEVE calculations require accurate $\Delta H_v$.
- **Heat-pump working-fluid selection:** Engineers compare $\Delta H_v$ at operating temperature.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Applicable Domain | Units |
|---|----------|----------------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta H_{v,b} = 8.75\,T_b$ | Kistyakowsky (original) | Non-polar liquids at normal BP | cal/mol, K |
| 2 | $\Delta H_{v,b} = 36.63\,T_b$ | Kistyakowsky (SI) | Non-polar liquids at normal BP | J/mol, K |
| 3 | $\Delta H_{v,b} = R\,T_b$ (with $R = 8.314$) | Order-of-magnitude estimate | All rough estimates | J/mol, K |
| 4 | $\Delta S_{v,b} = \Delta H_{v,b}/T_b \approx 21$ | Trouton's rule | Most liquids (NOT H₂O, NOT HF) | cal/(mol·K) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} = \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$ | Watson's correlation | Any $T < T_c$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 6 | $\Delta H_v \,[\text{kJ/kg}] = \dfrac{\Delta H_v \,[\text{kJ/kmol}]}{M}$ | Mass-basis conversion | All substances | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $\Delta H_v(T_2) = \Delta H_v(T_1) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$ | Combined Kistyakowsky + Watson | Estimation chain | — |
| 8 | Clapeyron: $\Delta H_v = R\,T^2 \cdot \dfrac{d\ln P^{sat}}{dT}$ | Thermodynamic basis | Theoretical foundation | J/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Watson's equation **diverges at $T = T_c$** (denominator goes to zero) and $\Delta H_v$ correctly goes to zero there. **Never** apply Watson above $T_c$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Kistyakowsky from Clapeyron + Ideal-Gas Vapor Assumption

We start from the rigorous **Clapeyron equation** for a phase change:

$$\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_v}{T \,\Delta V_v}$$

**Step 1 — Approximations.** Assume:

- Vapor behaves as an **ideal gas**: $V_v \approx \dfrac{RT}{P^{sat}}$
- Liquid molar volume $V_l$ is negligible compared to $V_v$: $\Delta V_v \approx V_v$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dP^{sat}}{dT} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_v \cdot P^{sat}}{R\,T^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange.** Divide both sides by $P^{sat}$:

$$\frac{dP^{sat}}{P^{sat}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_v}{R\,T^{2}} \, dT$$

**Step 3 — Clausius–Clapeyron form.** Integrate from a reference state $(P_1^{sat}, T_1)$ to the normal boiling point $(P_2^{sat} = 1 \text{ atm}, T_2 = T_b)$:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP^{sat}}{P^{sat}} \;=\; \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{\Delta H_v}{R\,T^{2}} \, dT$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P_2^{sat}}{P_1^{sat}}\right) \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_v}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Kistyakowsky's empirical fit.** Kistyakowsky assembled experimental $(T_b, \Delta H_{v,b})$ data for a large set of **non-polar** organic compounds and observed a near-perfect linear fit through the origin in the $\Delta H_{v,b}$ vs. $T_b$ plane:

$$\Delta H_{v,b} \;=\; k \, T_b$$

Performing a least-squares regression on the dataset, the slope $k$ converged to **8.75 cal/(mol·K)**.

**Step 5 — Verify the entropy of vaporization.** For non-polar liquids, the implied entropy of vaporization is:

$$\Delta S_{v,b} \;=\; \frac{\Delta H_{v,b}}{T_b} \;=\; 8.75 \,\text{cal/(mol·K)}$$

This is *lower* than Trouton's general value of 21 cal/(mol·K) because Trouton averaged across both polar and non-polar compounds. Kistyakowsky's value 8.75 effectively isolates the **intrinsic** vaporization entropy after removing the *association* (hydrogen-bond) contribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 8.75 and not 21?** Polar liquids such as water have anomalously high $\Delta S_{v,b}$ (≈ 26 cal/mol·K for water at 100 °C) due to strong hydrogen bonding in the liquid phase that disappears upon vaporization. The Kistyakowsky constant 8.75 is the value that best fits the *non-associated* baseline.

### 3.2 Derivation: Watson's Correlation

Watson (1943) generalized the temperature dependence of $\Delta H_v$ using dimensional analysis and experimental data on hydrocarbons. The functional form is:

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} \;=\; f(T_1, T_2, T_c)$$

**Step 1 — Functional constraint.** From thermodynamics, $\Delta H_v \to 0$ as $T \to T_c$. The simplest dimensionless form that vanishes at $T = T_c$ is:

$$\Delta H_v(T) \;\propto\; (T_c - T)^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Power-law fit.** Watson fitted $\Delta H_v$ data for many hydrocarbons and found the optimal exponent:

$$n \;=\; 0.38$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio.** Eliminating the proportionality constant by taking the ratio at two temperatures:

$$\frac{\Delta H_{v,2}}{\Delta H_{v,1}} \;=\; \frac{(T_c - T_2)^{0.38}}{(T_c - T_1)^{0.38}} \;=\; \left(\frac{T_c - T_2}{T_c - T_1}\right)^{0.38}$$

This is the **Watson equation**. Q.E.D.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Estimate the molar heat of vaporization of **n-pentane** at its normal boiling point ($T_b = 309.2 \text{ K}$) and at $T = 400 \text{ K}$. Given $T_c = 469.7 \text{ K}$, $M = 72.15 \text{ g/mol}$. n-Pentane is non-polar.

**Step 1 — Apply Kistyakowsky at $T_b$:**

$$\Delta H_{v,b} \;=\; 8.75 \times 309.2$$

$$\Delta H_{v,b} \;=\; 2705.5 \;\text{cal/mol}$$

Convert to SI:

$$\Delta H_{v,b} \;=\; 2705.5 \times 4.1868 \;=\; 11{,}329 \;\text{J/mol} \;\approx\; 11.33 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

Experimental value: ≈ 11.36 kJ/mol. **Error < 0.3 %.** Excellent agreement.
