# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Hess's Law Connection

The standard heat of combustion is **not** an independent thermodynamic property. It is rigorously calculated using **Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation**, which states that the enthalpy change of a reaction is independent of the pathway taken — only the initial and final states matter.

There are two principal calculation routes:

### Route A: From Standard Heats of Formation ($\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}$)

For any chemical reaction occurring at standard state:

$$\Delta \bar{H}_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ} = \sum_i n_i \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum_j n_j \Delta \bar{H}_{f,j}^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$$

Applied specifically to the combustion of a fuel $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c\text{N}_d\text{S}_e$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \sum_i n_i \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^{\circ}(\text{combustion products}) - \left[\Delta \bar{H}_{f,\text{fuel}}^{\circ} + n_{\text{O}_2} \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_{f,\text{O}_2}^{\circ}\right]\;}$$

Since $\Delta \bar{H}_{f}^{\circ}(\text{O}_2) = 0$ (oxygen is the reference element), this simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[\sum_i n_i \Delta \bar{H}_{f,i}^{\circ}(\text{products})\right] - \Delta \bar{H}_{f,\text{fuel}}^{\circ}\;}$$

### Route B: From Standard Heats of Combustion of Constituents (Group Contribution)

For a complex fuel whose individual bonds/groups are tabulated, the heat of combustion can be estimated by summing contributions (Dulong's formula, etc.). This is **less common** in KTU exams but appears in advanced fuel analysis.

## 2.2 Operational Logic: Why This Works (The "Why" Behind the Math)

- **Step 1 — Choose the reaction:** Write a stoichiometrically balanced combustion equation with integer coefficients for **one mole of fuel**.
- **Step 2 — Identify products in standard states:** $\text{CO}_2(g)$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, $\text{SO}_2(g)$, $\text{N}_2(g)$ (always gaseous since reference).
- **Step 3 — Look up standard heats of formation** from the table (the KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with these values).
- **Step 4 — Apply the formula:** $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \sum n \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum n \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$.
- **Step 5 — Interpret the sign:** The result is **negative** thermodynamically (exothermic); flip the sign to report the conventional "heat of combustion" as a **positive** value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Standard heat of combustion is the **backbone of boiler design, fuel calorific value specification, internal combustion engine analysis, refinery feedstock evaluation, and chemical process safety calculations** (e.g., fire/explosion hazard assessment). It is the single most important property used in **heating value calculations** for gaseous, liquid, and solid fuels.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Standard heat of combustion from $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ | $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \sum n \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum n \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$ | kJ/mol fuel |
| 2 | Sign convention (KTU standard) | $\text{Heat of Combustion} = -\Delta \bar{H}_{\text{rxn}}^{\circ}$ | Reported as **positive** number |
| 3 | Stoichiometric $\text{O}_2$ for $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c$ | $n_{\text{O}_2} = a + \dfrac{b}{4} - \dfrac{c}{2}$ | mol $\text{O}_2$ per mol fuel |
| 4 | HHV – LHV relationship | $\Delta H_{\text{HHV}} - \Delta H_{\text{LHV}} = n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_{\text{vap}}^{\circ}$ | $\Delta \bar{H}_{\text{vap}}^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}) = 44.01$ kJ/mol |
| 5 | Theoretical air requirement | $n_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{O}_2}}{0.21}$ | mol air per mol fuel |
| 6 | Air–fuel ratio (mass) | $\text{AFR} = \dfrac{n_{\text{air}} \cdot M_{\text{air}}}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kg air / kg fuel, $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ |
| 7 | Dulong's formula (for solid fuels, % by mass) | $\text{HHV} = 33{,}823 \cdot \text{C} + 144{,}250\left(\text{H} - \dfrac{\text{O}}{8}\right) + 9{,}270 \cdot \text{S}$ | J/g of solid fuel |
| 8 | Heat released in combustion | $Q = m_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kJ released per unit mass |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Safety Rule:** In all KTU exam scripts, never write absolute value using the `|` symbol if it could be confused with the table delimiter. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ explicitly.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linking $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}$ to Standard Heats of Formation

We will derive the master formula for the standard heat of combustion from **first principles** using Hess's Law and the construction of a closed thermochemical cycle.

**Consider the combustion of one mole of methane:**

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

We construct two pathways from reactants ($\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2$) to products ($\text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$):

- **Pathway I (Direct Combustion):** $\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, with $\Delta H = \Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}$ (the unknown we seek).
- **Pathway II (Stepwise via Elements):** Decompose the reactants into their constituent elements in their standard states, then recombine those elements into the products.

**Pathway II consists of two sub-steps:**

**Step II(a):** Decompose $\text{CH}_4(g)$ into its elements:

$$\text{CH}_4(g) \longrightarrow \text{C}(s,\text{graphite}) + 2\,\text{H}_2(g) \quad \Delta H_{a} = -\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4)$$

(Note: Reversing the formation reaction changes the sign.)

For $\text{O}_2$, the decomposition is trivial:

$$2\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{O}_2(g) \quad \Delta H = 0$$

(since $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2) = 0$)

**Step II(b):** Form the combustion products from their elements:

$$\text{C}(s) + \text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) \quad \Delta H_b = \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2)$$

$$2\,\text{H}_2(g) + \text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) \quad \Delta H_c = 2 \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)$$

**By Hess's Law** (state function property of enthalpy):

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \Delta H_{a} + \Delta H_{b} + \Delta H_c$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = -\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4) + \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)$$

**Generalizing** to any fuel $i$ with products $P_j$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{fuel}) = \sum_j \nu_j \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(P_j) - \sum_i \nu_i \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{reactant}_i)\;}$$

Since $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2) = 0$ and for one mole of fuel, $\nu_{\text{fuel}} = 1$, this reduces to the previously stated compact form.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Standard Heat of Combustion of Methane (HHV)

**Problem:** Calculate the standard heat of combustion of methane gas at 25 °C and 1 atm, given standard heats of formation: $\text{CH}_4(g) = -74.85$ kJ/mol, $\text{CO}_2(g) = -393.51$ kJ/mol, $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) = -285.83$ kJ/mol.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion equation:**

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master formula:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2) + 2 \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)\right] - \left[\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4) + 2 \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[(-393.51) + 2 \cdot (-285.83)\right] - \left[(-74.85) + 2 \cdot (0)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the brackets:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[-393.51 - 571.66\right] - \left[-74.85\right]$$

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = (-965.17) - (-74.85)$$

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = -965.17 + 74.85 = -890.32\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Report as conventional positive value:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4, \text{HHV}) = +890.32\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — LHV for Methane

**Problem:** Find the Lower Heating Value (LHV) for methane given the same data plus $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -241.82$ kJ/mol.

**Step 1 — Re-write equation with vapor water:**

$$\text{CH}_4(g) + 2\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(g) + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master formula:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{LHV}) = \left[(-393.51) + 2 \cdot (-241.82)\right] - \left[(-74.85) + 0\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{LHV}) = \left[-393.51 - 483.64\right] - (-74.85)$$

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{LHV}) = -877.15 + 74.85 = -802.30\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Report:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4, \text{LHV}) = +802.30\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via HHV – LHV relation:**

$$\text{HHV} - \text{LHV} = 2 \times 44.01 = 88.02\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$890.32 - 802.30 = 88.02\ \text{kJ/mol} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Combustion of Liquid Octane (HHV)

**Problem:** Calculate the standard heat of combustion of liquid n-octane. Given: $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}, l) = -249.95$ kJ/mol; other values as tabulated above.

**Step 1 — Balanced equation:**

$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}(l) + \dfrac{25}{2}\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2(g) + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

(Multiply through by 2 if integer coefficients are desired.)

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[8 \cdot (-393.51) + 9 \cdot (-285.83)\right] - \left[(-249.95) + 0\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate term by term:**

$$8 \times (-393.51) = -3148.08\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$9 \times (-285.83) = -2572.47\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{Products sum} = -3148.08 - 2572.47 = -5720.55\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract reactants:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = -5720.55 - (-249.95) = -5720.55 + 249.95 = -5470.60\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Report:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}, l, \text{HHV}) = +5470.60\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Combustion of Ethanol (HHV)

**Problem:** Determine the standard heat of combustion of liquid ethanol. Given: $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}, l) = -277.0$ kJ/mol.

**Step 1 — Balanced equation:**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[2 \cdot (-393.51) + 3 \cdot (-285.83)\right] - \left[(-277.0) + 0\right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$2 \times (-393.51) = -787.02\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$3 \times (-285.83) = -857.49\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{Products sum} = -787.02 - 857.49 = -1644.51\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract:**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = -1644.51 - (-277.0) = -1367.51\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Report:**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}, l, \text{HHV}) = +1367.51\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Standard Heat of Combustion Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Fuel Molecule] --> B[Write Balanced Combustion Equation]
    B --> C{Choose Product Water State}
    C --> D[Liquid H2O = HHV Path]
    C --> E[Vapor H2O = LHV Path]
    D --> F[Look Up Standard Heats of Formation]
    E --> F
    F --> G[List All Reactants and Products]
    G --> H[Apply Hess Law Formula]
    H --> I[Sum nu_i times dHf products]
    I --> J[Subtract Sum nu_j times dHf reactants]
    J --> K[Compute Net Enthalpy Change]
    K --> L{Is Delta H Negative?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Combustion Exothermic Confirmed]
    L -->|No| N[Verify Stoichiometry and Signs]
    M --> O[Report as Positive Heat of Combustion]
    N --> P[Recheck Tabulated Data]
    P --> O
    O --> Q[Convert to kJ per kg if Required]
    Q --> R[Final Answer Ready for KTU Submission]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Thermochemical Cycle (Hess's Law Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InitialState[Reactants at 298.15 K, 1 atm]
        R1[Fuel plus Stoichiometric O2]
    end
    subgraph FinalState[Products at 298.15 K, 1 atm]
        P1[CO2 plus H2O liquid or vapor]
    end
    subgraph DirectPath[Pathway 1: Direct Combustion]
        D1[Combustion Reaction in Calorimeter]
    end
    subgraph IndirectPath[Pathway 2: Stepwise via Elements]
        I1[Step A: Decompose Fuel to Elements]
        I2[Step B: Form Products from Elements]
    end
    R1 --> D1
    D1 --> P1
    R1 --> I1
    I1 --> I2
    I2 --> P1
    D1 -.Heat equals.-> H1[Standard Heat of Combustion]
    I1 -.Negative dHf fuel.-> H1
    I2 -.Sum of dHf products.-> H1
    H1 --> R1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Relationships Between Heats of Reaction, Formation, and Combustion

```mermaid
graph TD
    H1[Standard Heat of Formation dHf] --> HESS[Hess Law of Constant Heat Summation]
    H2[Standard Heat of Combustion dHc] --> HESS
    H3[Standard Heat of Reaction dHrxn] --> HESS
    HESS --> APP[Applied to Combustion Reactions]
    APP --> FORM[Formula: dHc = sum nu dHf products minus sum nu dHf reactants]
    FORM --> HHV[Higher Heating Value if H2O is liquid]
    FORM --> LHV[Lower Heating Value if H2O is vapor]
    HHV --> CONV1[HHV minus LHV = nH2O times 44.01 kJ per mol]
    LHV --> CONV1
    CONV1 --> ENG[Engineering Use in Boiler and Engine Design]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Sankey-Style Flow — Energy Released in Combustion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel: 1 mole of CH4] -->|Energy In: 74.85 kJ/mol as chemical potential| B(Combustion Reactor)
    B -->|Heat Released: 890.32 kJ/mol| C[Useful Thermal Energy Output]
    B -->|Energy in Products| D[CO2 plus 2 H2O liquid]
    D -->|Reheat Losses| E[Flue Gas Sensible Heat]
    D -->|Latent Loss if vapor| F[Uncondensed H2O vapor: 88.02 kJ/mol]
    C --> G[Boiler Steam Generation]
    C --> H[Internal Combustion Engine Work]
    C --> I[Process Heating in Reactor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Sankey-style block above illustrates how, in a **real industrial combustor**, not all the calculated 890.32 kJ/mol of heat is captured — some leaves with hot flue gases, and some is lost as latent heat when $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ exits as vapor. This is why **LHV is often used in practice** (since most exhaust gases leave above 100 °C, water does not condense).

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define the term "Standard Heat of Combustion". What is the difference between Higher Heating Value (HHV) and Lower Heating Value (LHV)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The standard heat of combustion of a substance is the enthalpy change accompanying the complete combustion of one mole of the substance with oxygen at **25 °C (298.15 K)** and **1 atm**, with all reactants and products in their **standard states** and combustion products being fully oxidized ($\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{N}_2$).

> **HHV vs LHV (1 mark):** HHV assumes product water is in the **liquid** state (latent heat of condensation is recovered), while LHV assumes product water is in the **vapor** state (latent heat is lost). They differ by $n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times 44.01$ kJ/mol.

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Write the stoichiometric combustion equation for propane ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$). State the standard state of water used for calculating HHV.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Equation (2 marks):** $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g) + 5\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2(g) + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$

> **Standard state of water (1 mark):** For HHV, product water is taken as **liquid $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$** at 25 °C and 1 atm (the reference standard state).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive an expression for the standard heat of combustion of a fuel $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b$ in terms of standard heats of formation of the products and the fuel. State clearly any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the standard heat of combustion (HHV) of **propane gas** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Given data: $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8, g) = -103.85$ kJ/mol; $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2, g) = -393.51$ kJ/mol; $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.83$ kJ/mol. Also report the LHV. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Write the generic combustion reaction (1 mark):**

$$\text{C}_a\text{H}_b + \left(a + \dfrac{b}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow a\,\text{CO}_2(g) + \dfrac{b}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Step 2 — State the assumptions (1 mark):**
- Combustion is **complete** (no CO, soot, or unburnt hydrocarbons).
- Reactants enter and products leave at **25 °C and 1 atm** (standard state).
- Reactants and products behave ideally.
- Kinetic and potential energy changes are **negligible**.
- $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{O}_2) = 0$ (reference element).

**Step 3 — Construct the Hess's Law thermochemical cycle (2 marks):**

Pathway 1 (Direct): $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b + (a + b/4)\text{O}_2 \to a\,\text{CO}_2 + (b/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$, with $\Delta H_1 = \Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}$.

Pathway 2 (Indirect):
- Step 2a: $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b \to a\,\text{C}(s) + (b/2)\text{H}_2(g)$ with $\Delta H_{2a} = -\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_a\text{H}_b)$.
- Step 2b: $a\,\text{C}(s) + a\,\text{O}_2(g) \to a\,\text{CO}_2(g)$ with $\Delta H_{2b} = a \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2)$.
- Step 2c: $(b/2)\text{H}_2(g) + (b/4)\text{O}_2(g) \to (b/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$ with $\Delta H_{2c} = (b/2) \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)$.

**Step 4 — Apply Hess's Law (2 marks):**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \Delta H_{2a} + \Delta H_{2b} + \Delta H_{2c}$$

**Step 5 — Final expression (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = a \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2) + \dfrac{b}{2} \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) - \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_a\text{H}_b)\;}$$

---

#### **Solution to B-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Balanced equation (1 mark):**

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_8(g) + 5\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 3\,\text{CO}_2(g) + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master formula (1 mark):**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[3 \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{CO}_2) + 4 \cdot \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l)\right] - \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values (2 marks):**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \left[3 \cdot (-393.51) + 4 \cdot (-285.83)\right] - (-103.85)$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term (1 mark):**

$$3 \times (-393.51) = -1180.53\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$4 \times (-285.83) = -1143.32\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\text{Products sum} = -1180.53 - 1143.32 = -2323.85\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Subtract and report (1 mark):**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = -2323.85 - (-103.85) = -2323.85 + 103.85 = -2220.00\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8, \text{HHV}) = +2220.00\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

**Step 6 — LHV calculation (1 mark):**

For LHV, use $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -241.82$ kJ/mol:

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{LHV}) = \left[3 \cdot (-393.51) + 4 \cdot (-241.82)\right] - (-103.85)$$

$$= \left[-1180.53 - 967.28\right] + 103.85$$

$$= -2147.81 + 103.85 = -2043.96\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{C}_3\text{H}_8, \text{LHV}) \approx +2044.00\ \text{kJ/mol}\;}$$

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks — Internal Choice]

**(a)** A gas mixture containing 80 mol% methane ($\text{CH}_4$) and 20 mol% ethane ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$) is burned completely with 100% excess air. Calculate the standard heat of combustion per mole of fuel mixture. Use HHV basis. Standard heats of formation: $\text{CH}_4 = -74.85$ kJ/mol; $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 = -84.68$ kJ/mol; $\text{CO}_2 = -393.51$ kJ/mol; $\text{H}_2\text{O}(l) = -285.83$ kJ/mol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A boiler burns **500 kg/h of liquid ethanol** ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}$) at 25 °C. Calculate the rate of heat release (in kW) if combustion is complete and water leaves as vapor. Use $\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}, l) = -277.0$ kJ/mol. Molecular weight of ethanol = 46 g/mol. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B-B(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}$ for methane (1 mark):**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \to \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4) = (-393.51) + 2(-285.83) - (-74.85) = -890.32\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}$ for ethane (2 marks):**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 3.5\text{O}_2 \to 2\text{CO}_2 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{C}_2\text{H}_6) = 2(-393.51) + 3(-285.83) - (-84.68)$$

$$= -787.02 - 857.49 + 84.68 = -1559.83\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Weighted average for 1 mole of mixture (2 marks):**

For 1 mole of mixture: 0.80 mol $\text{CH}_4$ + 0.20 mol $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$.

$$\Delta \bar{H}_{c,\text{mix}}^{\circ} = 0.80 \times 890.32 + 0.20 \times 1559.83$$

$$= 712.26 + 311.97 = 1024.23\ \text{kJ/mol mixture}$$

**Step 4 — Note on excess air (2 marks):** With 100% excess air, the **thermodynamic heat released** is still the same 1024.23 kJ/mol (the excess air is inert and absorbs no heat in the reaction stoichiometry). However, in a real combustor, the excess air would absorb some of the released heat, lowering the **flame temperature**. The standard heat of combustion is an **intrinsic property** and is **independent of excess air**.

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \bar{H}_{c,\text{mix}}^{\circ} = 1024.23\ \text{kJ/mol fuel mixture (HHV basis)}\;}$$

---

#### **Solution to B-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Balanced equation (1 mark):**

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}(l) + 3\,\text{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2(g) + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$$

(Note: water as vapor since LHV basis is specified.)

**Step 2 — Apply master formula for LHV (2 marks):**

$$\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}(\text{LHV}) = \left[2 \cdot (-393.51) + 3 \cdot (-241.82)\right] - \left[(-277.0) + 0\right]$$

$$= \left[-787.02 - 725.46\right] + 277.0$$

$$= -1512.48 + 277.0 = -1235.48\ \text{kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Compute molar flow rate of ethanol (1 mark):**

$$\dot{n} = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{M} = \dfrac{500\ \text{kg/h}}{0.046\ \text{kg/mol}} = 10869.57\ \text{mol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat release rate (2 marks):**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{n} \times |\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}| = 10869.57 \times 1235.48\ \text{kJ/h}$$

$$\dot{Q} = 13{,}430{,}830\ \text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kW (1 mark):**

$$\dot{Q} = \dfrac{13{,}430{,}830}{3600} = 3730.79\ \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\;\dot{Q} \approx 3731\ \text{kW}\;}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error:** Reporting $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}$ as negative. The convention is **positive** for the "heat of combustion" quantity. (−1 to −2 marks if sign is wrong.)
> 2. **Product Water State:** Forgetting to switch to $\text{H}_2\text{O}(g)$ for LHV. (−2 marks.)
> 3. **Skipping the Balanced Equation:** KTU examiners specifically award **1 mark** for writing the balanced combustion equation. Never skip it.
> 4. **Not Stating Assumptions:** "Complete combustion" and "products in standard states" must be stated explicitly for full marks on derivations. (−1 to −2 marks.)
> 5. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing kcal and kJ (1 kcal = 4.184 kJ). The KTU 2024 scheme primarily uses kJ; conversions from older kcal data are sometimes needed.
> 6. **Mole vs. Mass Confusion:** The heat of combustion is **per mole of fuel**, not per mole of mixture or per kg. Clearly state the basis in your final answer.
> 7. **Excess Air Trap:** Standard heat of combustion is **independent of excess air** — students often incorrectly divide by total air supplied.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Standard Heat of Combustion (Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}$ = enthalpy change for complete combustion of **1 mole of fuel** at **298.15 K, 1 atm**, products in **standard states**.
>
> ✅ **Standard State:** $T = 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, $P = 1\,\text{atm}$, reference elements in their most stable form.
>
> ✅ **Sign Convention:** Combustion is exothermic ($\Delta H < 0$); but the **reported heat of combustion is positive**.
>
> ✅ **Master Formula:** $\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ} = \sum n \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{products}) - \sum n \Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{reactants})$
>
> ✅ **HHV:** Water in products is **liquid** ($\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, l) = -285.83$ kJ/mol).
>
> ✅ **LHV:** Water in products is **vapor** ($\Delta \bar{H}_f^{\circ}(\text{H}_2\text{O}, g) = -241.82$ kJ/mol).
>
> ✅ **HHV – LHV = $n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} \times 44.01$ kJ/mol.**
>
> ✅ **Hess's Law** is the underlying principle — the calculation is just a thermochemical cycle via elemental reference states.
>
> ✅ **Stoichiometric $\text{O}_2$ for $\text{C}_a\text{H}_b\text{O}_c$:** $n_{\text{O}_2} = a + b/4 - c/2$
>
> ✅ **Theoretical air:** $n_{\text{air}} = n_{\text{O}_2} / 0.21$; with $x\%$ excess air: $n_{\text{air,actual}} = n_{\text{air,theoretical}} \times (1 + x/100)$.
>
> ✅ **Heat Released in Industrial Combustor:** $Q = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \times \dfrac{\Delta \bar{H}_c^{\circ}}{M_{\text{fuel}}}$
>
> ✅ **Independent of excess air:** The standard heat of combustion is a thermodynamic property of the fuel; excess air affects **flame temperature**, not the heat of combustion itself.
>
> ✅ **Always balance first**, then look up $\Delta H_f^{\circ}$ values, then apply the formula.
>
> ✅ **Engineering Applications:** Boiler design, IC engine analysis, fuel specification, refinery operations, process heating calculations, fire/explosion safety indices.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: and Standard Heat of Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** and Standard Heat of Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the rigorous framework of **thermochemistry** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme for *Process Calculations (PCCHT205)*, the **Standard Heat of Formation** (also written as the **Standard Enthalpy of Formation** and denoted as $\Delta H_f^\circ$) is formally defined as the quantity of heat that is either evolved (released, exothermic, $\Delta H_f^\circ < 0$) or absorbed (endothermic, $\Delta H_f^\circ > 0$) when **one mole** of a chemical compound is synthesized **directly from its constituent elements**, where:

1. Each reactant and product exists in its **standard state** (the most stable physical form of the pure substance at **1 atm pressure** — and, in the IUPAC 2024 modern convention, **1 bar** — and conventionally at a reference temperature of **25 °C (298.15 K)**).
2. The reaction proceeds such that the stoichiometric coefficient of the product formed is exactly **unity (1 mole)**.
3. The standard enthalpy change of the formation reaction is reported at the standard reference conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** *"The standard heat of formation of a compound is the enthalpy change accompanying the formation of one mole of the compound from its elements in their standard states."*

The standard state of an element is its **most stable thermodynamic form** at 1 atm and 25 °C. For example:
- Carbon exists as **graphite (C, s)** — not diamond.
- Oxygen exists as diatomic gas **O₂(g)** — not ozone (O₃) or atomic oxygen.
- Hydrogen exists as diatomic gas **H₂(g)** — not atomic H.
- Bromine exists as liquid **Br₂(l)**.
- Sulfur exists as **rhombic S₈(s)**.

By a strict consequence of this definition, the standard heat of formation of **every element in its own standard state is, by convention, exactly equal to zero**:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ \big(\text{Element in its standard state}\big) = 0 \;\; \text{kJ/mol}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Highlight:** Values of $\Delta H_f^\circ$ are extensively **tabulated** in standard thermodynamic data tables (such as those in *Smith, Van Ness & Abbott*, *Felder & Rousseau*, and KTU recommended references). Students are expected to memorize a small set of common values (e.g., $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of CO₂, H₂O, CH₄, NH₃) and know how to *interpret and apply* tabulated values rather than memorize the entire table.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **chemical architect**, and every compound is a "building" you wish to construct from raw materials. The "raw materials" are pure elements in their most natural, stable form — like a chef who insists on using flour, water, and yeast in their freshest state to bake bread.

The **standard heat of formation** is simply the "energy receipt" of that construction job at standard weather conditions (25 °C, 1 atm):

- **Exothermic (negative ΔH):** The building "gives off" heat as it is built — like pouring quick-set cement that warms the surroundings. The product is in a **lower energy state** than the elements.
- **Endothermic (positive ΔH):** You must "inject" energy into the building site to assemble the molecule — like a refrigeration process. The product is in a **higher energy state** than the elements.

**Why is the standard state of an element defined as $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$?** This is a clever choice of a **reference point** (a thermodynamic "sea level"). Just as we define the height of Mt. Everest relative to mean sea level (height = 0), we define the energy content of all pure elements in their most stable form as the zero baseline. All other compounds' energies are then expressed **relative to this baseline** — positive means "above sea level," negative means "below sea level."

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Bank Account:** Think of $\Delta H_f^\circ$ as the "account balance" of a compound. Pure elements in their standard state are the "account holders with a zero balance." Compounds are like savings accounts — some are in **debt** (negative, exothermic — energy was paid out to form them, so they sit in a low-energy, stable state), while others have a **positive balance** (endothermic — energy was deposited to form them, so they are in a higher-energy, less stable state).

## 1.3 Common KTU-Listed Standard Heats of Formation (Memorize These)

The following table summarizes frequently-tested compounds. These values are the **essential reference data** for the vast majority of KTU 2024 problems:

| Compound | Formula | State | $\Delta H_f^\circ$ (kJ/mol) |
|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| Water (vapor) | H₂O | g | $-241.8$ |
| Water (liquid) | H₂O | l | $-285.8$ |
| Water (liquid) | H₂O | l | $-285.83$ (more precise) |
| Carbon dioxide | CO₂ | g | $-393.5$ |
| Carbon monoxide | CO | g | $-110.5$ |
| Methane | CH₄ | g | $-74.8$ |
| Ethane | C₂H₆ | g | $-84.7$ |
| Propane | C₃H₈ | g | $-103.8$ |
| n-Butane | C₄H₁₀ | g | $-124.7$ |
| Ethylene | C₂H₄ | g | $+52.3$ |
| Propylene | C₃H₆ | g | $+20.4$ |
| Acetylene | C₂H₂ | g | $+226.7$ |
| Ammonia | NH₃ | g | $-46.0$ |
| Nitrogen dioxide | NO₂ | g | $+33.9$ |
| Nitric oxide | NO | g | $+90.4$ |
| Sulfur dioxide | SO₂ | g | $-296.9$ |
| Sulfur trioxide | SO₃ | g | $-395.0$ |
| Hydrogen chloride | HCl | g | $-92.3$ |
| Hydrogen sulfide | H₂S | g | $-20.2$ |
| Methanol | CH₃OH | l | $-238.7$ |
| Ethanol | C₂H₅OH | l | $-277.0$ |
| Glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ | s | $-1273$ |
| Calcium carbonate | CaCO₃ | s | $-1206.9$ |
| Calcium oxide | CaO | s | $-635.1$ |
| Sodium chloride | NaCl | s | $-411.2$ |
| Sodium hydroxide | NaOH | s | $-425.9$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Warning:** Notice the **state of aggregation matters enormously**. H₂O(g) and H₂O(l) have *different* $\Delta H_f^\circ$ values. The difference between them is precisely the **latent heat of vaporization** at 25 °C ($+44.0$ kJ/mol). Always verify whether the problem specifies gas, liquid, or solid.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Hess's Law as the Foundation

The **entire** utility of the standard heat of formation rests upon a cornerstone principle formulated in 1840 by the Russian-born Swiss chemist **Germain Henri Hess** (1802–1850):

> **Hess's Law of Constant Heat Summation:**
> *"The total enthalpy change accompanying a chemical reaction is independent of the pathway by which the reaction is carried out, provided the initial and final states (and the conditions of temperature and pressure) are identical."*

In simpler words: **Energy is a state function.** A reaction's heat effect depends only on where you **start** (reactants) and where you **finish** (products) — not on the route taken.

### The Pathway Argument (Logical Breakdown)

Consider a general chemical reaction:
$$A + B \longrightarrow C + D \qquad \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = ?$$

We can imagine an **alternative two-step pathway** to reach the same products:

**Pathway 1 (Direct):** $A + B \rightarrow C + D$ (this is the unknown we seek)

**Pathway 2 (Indirect, through elements in their standard states):**
- **Step 2a:** Decompose $A$ and $B$ into their constituent elements. The heat required is $-\Delta H_f^\circ(A) - \Delta H_f^\circ(B)$ (negative sign because we are running the *formation* reaction in reverse).
- **Step 2b:** Form $C$ and $D$ from these elements. The heat evolved is $+\Delta H_f^\circ(C) + \Delta H_f^\circ(D)$.

By Hess's law, both pathways must yield identical net enthalpy change:
$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum_{i} n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum_{j} n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{reactants})$$

This is the **central operational equation** of this module, and it is the single most-tested formula in this section of the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every high-yield formula** required for the KTU 2024 board exam on this topic. The use of `\vert` (or `\mid`) instead of vertical pipes ensures the markdown table syntax is not broken.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | When to Use | Standard Units |
|---|--------------|--------------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Standard Heat of Formation Definition** | $\Delta H_f^\circ = \sum_{\text{products}} \nu_i \cdot H_i^\circ - \sum_{\text{reactants}} \nu_j \cdot H_j^\circ$ | For a formation reaction of 1 mole of compound from elements in standard states | kJ/mol |
| 2 | **Heat of Reaction from Heats of Formation (Hess's Law)** | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \displaystyle\sum_i n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ \big(\text{products}\big) - \sum_j n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ \big(\text{reactants}\big)$ | To calculate the standard heat of ANY chemical reaction from tabulated $\Delta H_f^\circ$ data | kJ |
| 3 | **Heat of Reaction per mole of a specific reactant or product** | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \dfrac{1}{\nu_k} \left[ \sum_i n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{prod}) - \sum_j n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{react}) \right]$ | When the problem asks for "heat of reaction per mole of limiting reagent" or "per mole of product formed" | kJ/mol |
| 4 | **Heat of Combustion Relationship** | $\Delta H_{\text{comb}}^\circ = \sum_i n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum_j n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{reactants})$ | Combustion is just a special case of the heat of reaction equation | kJ/mol |
| 5 | **Heat of Formation of Elements (Convention)** | $\Delta H_f^\circ \big(\text{element in standard state}\big) = 0$ | Always — for bookkeeping in Hess's law calculations | kJ/mol |
| 6 | **Kirchhoff's Law (Temperature Correction)** | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T_2) = \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | When the reaction occurs at a temperature $T_2$ different from 25 °C (298 K) | kJ/mol |
| 7 | **Mean Heat Capacity Approximation** | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T_2) \approx \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(T_1) + \overline{\Delta C_p}(T_2 - T_1)$ | When $\Delta C_p$ is treated as constant over the temperature range | kJ/mol |
| 8 | **Standard State of Water Reference** | $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, \text{l}) = -285.83$ kJ/mol | Use this for reactions producing liquid water | kJ/mol |
| 9 | **Heat of Formation from Heats of Combustion** | $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{compound}) = \sum_i n_i \Delta H_{c,i}^\circ(\text{constituent elements}) - \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{compound})$ | When $\Delta H_f^\circ$ is not tabulated but $\Delta H_c^\circ$ is given | kJ/mol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention to Memorize for the KTU Board Exam:** The standard state of water at 25 °C is **liquid** (H₂O, l), NOT gas. The standard state of bromine is **liquid**, not gas. The standard state of mercury is **liquid**. The standard state of carbon is **graphite**, not diamond.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The standard heat of formation is not merely an academic bookkeeping tool — it has profound engineering applications:

1. **Combustion Engineering:** In designing furnaces, boilers, internal combustion engines, and gas turbines, the heat released per unit mass of fuel is computed from $\Delta H_f^\circ$ values of reactants and products. This determines fuel efficiency and pollutant formation.

2. **Chemical Reactor Design:** The energy balance on any industrial reactor (e.g., an ammonia synthesis reactor, sulfuric acid contactor, or steam reformer) requires the standard heat of reaction. Reactor sizing, cooling/heating duty, and catalyst-bed temperature profiles all depend on it.

3. **Process Safety & Hazard Analysis:** Exothermic reactions with large negative $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ$ can become **runaway reactions** if heat removal fails. Standard heat of formation data is essential for sizing relief valves and emergency cooling systems.

4. **Environmental Engineering:** Standard heats of formation are used to compute **standard Gibbs free energy** of formation ($\Delta G_f^\circ$) and equilibrium constants, which determine pollutant distribution in the atmosphere (e.g., formation of NOₓ in combustion).

5. **Refrigeration and Cryogenics:** Endothermic reactions and phase changes (both tabulated using standard reference data) are used in absorption refrigeration cycles (e.g., the ammonia–water cycle).

6. **Biochemical Engineering:** Metabolic heat generation in bioreactors (microbial fermentations) is computed using formation enthalpies of substrates and biomass.

7. **Petroleum Refining:** Hydrocracking, catalytic reforming, and coking unit energy balances all require $\Delta H_f^\circ$ of hydrocarbon species.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Master Derivation: Heat of Reaction from Standard Heats of Formation

We will now rigorously derive the operational formula using **Hess's Law** as our thermodynamic foundation.

### Setup of the Derivation

Consider a general chemical reaction at standard conditions:
$$\underbrace{a A + b B}_{\text{Reactants}} \longrightarrow \underbrace{c C + d D}_{\text{Products}} \qquad \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = ?$$

Where $a, b, c, d$ are the stoichiometric coefficients. We wish to find $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ$.

### Step 1 — Identify the Standard Heat of Formation Reactions

By definition, the standard heat of formation of each species is the enthalpy change for the reaction forming it from its constituent elements in their standard states:

$$\text{Elements} \longrightarrow A \qquad \Delta H_1^\circ = \Delta H_f^\circ(A)$$

$$\text{Elements} \longrightarrow B \qquad \Delta H_2^\circ = \Delta H_f^\circ(B)$$

$$\text{Elements} \longrightarrow C \qquad \Delta H_3^\circ = \Delta H_f^\circ(C)$$

$$\text{Elements} \longrightarrow D \qquad \Delta H_4^\circ = \Delta H_f^\circ(D)$$

### Step 2 — Construct the Two Pathways

**Pathway 1 (Direct, the unknown we seek):**

$$a A + b B \longrightarrow c C + d D \qquad \Delta H_{\text{path 1}}^\circ = \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ$$

**Pathway 2 (Indirect, the constructed route):**

- **Sub-step 2a — Decompose the reactants into their elements** (run the formation reaction in reverse, hence negate the enthalpy and multiply by the stoichiometric coefficient):

$$a A \longrightarrow a \cdot (\text{Elements of } A) \qquad \Delta H_{2a}^\circ = -a \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(A)$$

$$b B \longrightarrow b \cdot (\text{Elements of } B) \qquad \Delta H_{2b}^\circ = -b \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(B)$$

- **Sub-step 2b — Form the products from these elements** (run the formation reaction forward, multiply by the stoichiometric coefficient):

$$c \cdot (\text{Elements of } C) \longrightarrow c C \qquad \Delta H_{2c}^\circ = +c \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(C)$$

$$d \cdot (\text{Elements of } D) \longrightarrow d D \qquad \Delta H_{2d}^\circ = +d \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(D)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Hess's Law

Since the **initial state** of Pathway 1 ($aA + bB$) and the **initial state** of Pathway 2 (the elements) are connected by the decomposition steps, and the **final state** of both pathways is the same ($cC + dD$), Hess's Law states:

$$\Delta H_{\text{path 1}}^\circ = \Delta H_{\text{path 2}}^\circ$$

Substituting the expressions:

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \left[-a \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(A) - b \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(B)\right] + \left[+c \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(C) + d \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(D)\right]$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange into the Standard Form

Rearranging to bring products to the front:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \underbrace{\left[c \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(C) + d \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(D)\right]}_{\text{Sum over products}} - \underbrace{\left[a \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(A) + b \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(B)\right]}_{\text{Sum over reactants}}\;}$$

Or, in the more compact summation form that you must write in your KTU answer script:

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum_{i=\text{products}} n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ - \sum_{j=\text{reactants}} n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ$$

This completes the derivation. The result is the **operational master equation** for computing heats of reaction from tabulated standard heats of formation.

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Example (Illustrative Derivation Walkthrough)

**Problem:** Calculate the standard heat of combustion of **methane (CH₄)** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Use the following data:

- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CH}_4, \text{g}) = -74.8$ kJ/mol
- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2, \text{g}) = 0$ kJ/mol (element in standard state)
- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2, \text{g}) = -393.5$ kJ/mol
- $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, \text{l}) = -285.8$ kJ/mol

**Step 1 — Write the balanced combustion reaction:**

$$\text{CH}_4(\text{g}) + 2\,\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(\text{g}) + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

**Step 2 — Apply the master equation:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = \sum n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{reactants})$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the products' contribution:**

$$\sum n_i \Delta H_{f,i}^\circ(\text{products}) = (1) \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CO}_2) + (2) \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{H}_2\text{O}, \text{l})$$

Substituting the values:

$$= (1) \cdot (-393.5) + (2) \cdot (-285.8)$$

$$= -393.5 - 571.6$$

$$= -965.1 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the reactants' contribution:**

$$\sum n_j \Delta H_{f,j}^\circ(\text{reactants}) = (1) \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{CH}_4) + (2) \cdot \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{O}_2)$$

Substituting the values:

$$= (1) \cdot (-74.8) + (2) \cdot (0)$$

$$= -74.8 + 0$$

$$= -74.8 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the difference:**

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = (-965.1) - (-74.8)$$

$$\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ = -965.1 + 74.8$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H_{\text{comb}}^\circ(\text{CH}_4) = -890.3 \text{ kJ/mol}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation:**

The **negative sign** indicates the reaction is **exothermic** (releases heat). At 25 °C and 1 atm, the complete combustion of one mole of methane produces liquid water and releases **890.3 kJ** of heat. This is the famous "heating value" of methane used in calorific calculations for natural gas burners and combined-cycle power plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Subtle Point — Phase of Water Matters:** If the problem had specified H₂O(g) instead of H₂O(l), the answer would change by $2 \times 44.0 = 88$ kJ (the latent heat of vaporization for 2 moles of water). The "lower heating value" (LHV, with H₂O as vapor) would be $-802.3$ kJ/mol, and the "higher heating value" (HHV, with H₂O as liquid) is $-890.3$ kJ/mol. The KTU board frequently tests this distinction.

## 3.3 Second Worked Example: Formation from Combustion Data

**Problem:** The standard heat of combustion of **ethylene (C₂H₄, g)** is $-1411$ kJ/mol. The standard heats of combustion of **carbon (graphite)** and **hydrogen (H₂, g)** are $-393.5$ kJ/mol and $-285.8$ kJ/mol, respectively. Calculate the **standard heat of formation** of ethylene.

**Step 1 — Write the combustion reactions:**

$$(i)\;\;\text{C}_2\text{H}_4(\text{g}) + 3\,\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2(\text{g}) + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l}) \qquad \Delta H_c^\circ = -1411 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$(ii)\;\;\text{C}(\text{s, graphite}) + \text{O}_2(\text{g}) \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2(\text{g}) \qquad \Delta H_c^\circ = -393.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$(iii)\;\;\text{H}_2(\text{g}) + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2(\text{g}) \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l}) \qquad \Delta H_c^\circ = -285.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the target reaction (formation of ethylene from elements):**

$$2\,\text{C}(\text{s}) + 2\,\text{H}_2(\text{g}) \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4(\text{g}) \qquad \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_4) = ?$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hess's Law using combustion data:**

We observe that reaction (i) is the **combustion of the product** (C₂H₄) of our target reaction, while reactions (ii) and (iii) are the **combustions of the reactants** (C and H₂). The relationship is:

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_4) = \sum \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{constituent elements}) - \Delta H_c^\circ(\text{compound})$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the values:**

$$\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_4) = \left[2 \cdot (-393.5) + 2 \cdot (-285.8)\right] - (-1411)$$

$$= \left[-787.0 - 571.6\right] - (-1411)$$

$$= -1358.6 + 1411$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_4) = +52.4 \text{ kJ/mol}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:**

The **positive sign** indicates that the formation of ethylene from its elements is **endothermic** — energy must be supplied. The C=C double bond in ethylene is a high-energy, reactive functionality, which is why ethylene is so important in petrochemical polymerization (the energy stored in the double bond is released when it opens up to form the polymer chain).

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-Verification:** The result ($+52.4$ kJ/mol) matches the tabulated value of $+52.3$ kJ/mol from Section 1.3, with the tiny discrepancy due to rounding in the given combustion data. This kind of cross-check is highly recommended in the KTU board exam to catch sign errors and unit mistakes.

## 3.4 Third Worked Example: Multi-Component Combustion (Gas Mixture)

**Problem:** A fuel gas mixture has the following composition by volume: **CH₄ = 80%, C₂H₆ = 15%, N₂ = 5%**. Calculate the **higher heating value (HHV)** of the fuel mixture in kJ per standard cubic meter (Nm³) of fuel, with water produced as liquid.

**Step 1 — Write balanced combustion reactions:**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + \tfrac{7}{2}\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}(\text{l})$$

**Step 2 — Recall that 1 mole of ideal gas at STP occupies 22.4 L = 0.0224 m³.**

For 1 Nm³ of fuel gas (1000 L at STP), the number of moles is:

$$n_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{22.4 \text{ L/mol}} = 44.64 \text{ mol per Nm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Calculate moles of each component per Nm³ of fuel:**

$$n_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.80 \times 44.64 = 35.71 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_6} = 0.15 \times 44.64 = 6.70 \text{ mol}$$

$$n_{\text{N}_2} = 0.05 \times 44.64 = 2.23 \text{ mol (inert — does not combust)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat of combustion per mole of each component (HHV):**

For CH₄: We already calculated $\Delta H_{\text{comb}}^\circ(\text{CH}_4) = -890.3$ kJ/mol

For C₂H₆: Using $\Delta H_f^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_6, \text{g}) = -84.7$ kJ/mol:

$$\Delta H_{\text{comb}}^\circ(\text{C}_2\text{H}_6) = [2 \cdot (-393.5) + 3 \cdot (-285.8)] - [(-84.7) + 0]$$

$$= [-787.0 - 857.4] - (-84.7)$$

$$= -1644.4 + 84.7$$

$$= -1559.7 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 5 — Total heat released per Nm³ of fuel:**

$$Q_{\text{HHV}} = (35.71)(-890.3) + (6.70)(-1559.7)$$

$$= -31{,}793 - 10{,}450$$

$$= -42{,}243 \text{ kJ/Nm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\;Q_{\text{HHV}} \approx -42{,}243 \text{ kJ/Nm}^3 \approx -42.24 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3\;}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Sanity Check:** Natural gas typically has an HHV of 35–45 MJ/Nm³, so our value of 42.24 MJ/Nm³ is physically reasonable for a methane-rich fuel. ✓

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Hess's Law Pathway Argument

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **Hess's Law pathway construction** that underpins the master equation for computing heats of reaction. The diagram shows two alternative routes from the same initial state (reactants) to the same final state (products) — one direct, one through the standard-state "element reference."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Reactants A plus B<br/>Standard State 25 C 1 atm"]:::reactNode
    Z["Products C plus D<br/>Standard State 25 C 1 atm"]:::prodNode
    E["Element Pool<br/>Standard State Reference<br/>Delta Hf equals zero"]:::elemNode
    P1["Pathway 1 Direct Route<br/>Delta H reaction equals unknown"]:::pathNode
    P2["Pathway 2 Indirect Route<br/>Reactants to Elements to Products"]:::pathNode2
    S1["Step 1 Decompose A and B<br/>to elements<br/>Delta H equals minus sum of Delta Hf reactants"]:::stepNode
    S2["Step 2 Form C and D<br/>from elements<br/>Delta H equals plus sum of Delta Hf products"]:::stepNode
    S3["Hess Law Equivalence<br/>Pathway 1 equals Pathway 2"]:::hessNode
    M["MASTER EQUATION<br/>Delta H reaction equals products minus reactants"]:::masterNode

    A --> P1
    P1 --> Z
    A --> S1
    S1 --> E
    E --> S2
    S2 --> Z
    P1 --> S3
    P2 --> S3
    S3 --> M

    classDef reactNode fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prodNode fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elemNode fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pathNode fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pathNode2 fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stepNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hessNode fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef masterNode fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> **How to Read This Diagram:** Start at the top-left "Reactants" block. There are two arrows leaving it: one (Pathway 1) goes directly to the top-right "Products" block — this is the unknown heat of reaction. The other (Pathway 2) goes down to decompose the reactants into the "Element Pool" (a state where all compounds have been broken into their elements), and then up to the Products. Hess's Law asserts that the two pathways are energetically equivalent, and from this equivalence we obtain the master equation.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Calculation Workflow

The following diagram shows the **step-by-step procedure** that a KTU student should follow when solving a heat-of-reaction problem using standard heats of formation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1 Read the problem<br/>Identify the balanced reaction<br/>and all compounds involved"]:::step
    S2["STEP 2 Look up Delta Hf values<br/>for each compound from<br/>tabulated thermodynamic data"]:::step
    S3["STEP 3 Verify the state<br/>gas liquid solid<br/>matches the data table"]:::step
    S4["STEP 4 Multiply each Delta Hf<br/>by its stoichiometric coefficient"]:::step
    S5["STEP 5 Sum the products side<br/>Sum prod equals sum n i times Delta Hf i"]:::step
    S6["STEP 6 Sum the reactants side<br/>Sum react equals sum n j times Delta Hf j"]:::step
    S7["STEP 7 Apply master equation<br/>Delta H reaction equals Sum prod minus Sum react"]:::step
    S8["STEP 8 Interpret the result<br/>Check sign exothermic endothermic<br/>Verify units kJ or kJ per mol"]:::step
    S9["STEP 9 If T is not 25 C<br/>Apply Kirchhoff Law correction"]:::step
    S10["STEP 10 Report final answer<br/>with proper units and sign"]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10

    classDef step fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Energy Level Diagram for an Exothermic Reaction

The following diagram represents the **energy landscape** of an exothermic reaction. The reactants sit at a higher energy level than the products, and the "drop" between them is the standard heat of reaction:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Reactants<br/>Energy = E_R<br/>Sum n_j times H_f reactants"]:::reactE
    P["Products<br/>Energy = E_P<br/>Sum n_i times H_f products"]:::prodE
    DROP["Delta H reaction<br/>equals E_P minus E_R<br/>NEGATIVE for exothermic"]:::deltaH
    AXIS["Energy Axis y"]:::axis
    R -->|"Energy release"| DROP
    DROP --> P
    AXIS -.-> R
    AXIS -.-> P

    classDef reactE fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef prodE fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5C20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef deltaH fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef axis fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Energy Diagram:** The horizontal axis represents the progress of the reaction (reaction coordinate), and the vertical axis represents the enthalpy. For an **exothermic** reaction, the products lie **below** the reactants on the energy axis, and the magnitude of the vertical drop equals $\vert \Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ \vert$. For an **endothermic** reaction, the products would lie *above* the reactants, and the vertical climb equals $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}^\circ$ (which would be positive).

## 4.4 Schematic — The "Element Reference" Concept

The following diagram illustrates why the standard heat of formation of elements is conventionally set to zero — it serves as a **thermodynamic reference baseline**:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE["THERMODYNAMIC SEA LEVEL<br/>All Pure Elements in Standard State<br/>Delta Hf equals 0 by definition"]:::baseNode
    L1["Energy Level LESS than Zero<br/>Exothermic Compounds<br/>Examples CO2 H2O CH4<br/>Stable low energy products"]:::lowNode
    L2["Energy Level GREATER than Zero<br/>Endothermic Compounds<br/>Examples C2H4 C2H2 NO<br/>Unstable high energy products"]:::highNode
    AX["Energy Axis"]:::axNode

    BASE --> L1
    BASE --> L2
    AX -.-> L1
    AX -.-> L2
    AX -.-> BASE

    classDef baseNode fill:#CFD8DC,stroke:#263238,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef lowNode fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef highNode fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef axNode fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> **Interpretation:** Think of pure elements as living at "sea level" (zero altitude). Some compounds are "below sea level" (exothermic, stable, low energy — like water, carbon dioxide). Other compounds are "above sea level" (endothermic, energy-rich, less stable — like acetylene, which is famously used in welding precisely because of its high stored energy). The **vertical distance** from sea level to a compound's energy is exactly the standard heat of formation.
