# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(a) Standard heat of formation**
**(b) Standard heat of combustion**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Standard Heat of Formation (ΔH°f):**
The enthalpy change when **one mole** of a compound is formed from its constituent **elements in their reference states** (most stable form at 298 K and 1 atm), with all substances in their standard states.

*Example:* Formation of water:
$$H_2(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow H_2O(l), \quad \Delta H^\circ_f = -285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**(b) Standard Heat of Combustion (ΔH°c):**
The enthalpy change when **one mole** of a substance undergoes **complete combustion** with pure oxygen at 298 K and 1 atm, with products being CO₂(g), H₂O(l), and other specified stable products.

*Example:* Combustion of methane:
$$CH_4(g) + 2O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g) + 2H_2O(l), \quad \Delta H^\circ_c = -890.4 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Kirchhoff's Law. How is it used to calculate the heat of reaction at a temperature different from 298 K?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Kirchhoff's Law states that the rate of change of the heat of reaction with temperature at constant pressure equals the difference between the heat capacities of products and reactants.

**Mathematical Form (Differential):**
$$\frac{d(\Delta H)}{dT} = \Delta C_p = \sum \nu_i C_{p,\text{products}} - \sum \nu_j C_{p,\text{reactants}}$$

**Explanation:**
At higher temperatures, the heat content of all species increases, but the products and reactants absorb heat at different rates (governed by their $C_p$). The **net change in heat absorbed** (which equals $d(\Delta H)/dT$) equals $\Delta C_p$.

**Application — Integral Form:**

Starting from the known standard heat of reaction at 298 K:
$$\Delta H_T = \Delta H^\circ_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$$

If $C_p$ correlations are available as $C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$, then the integral is evaluated term by term to obtain $\Delta H$ at any desired temperature $T$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** The standard enthalpy of formation of NH₃(g) at 298 K is **-46.11 kJ/mol**. For the reaction:
$$N_2(g) + 3H_2(g) \rightarrow 2NH_3(g)$$

Calculate the standard heat of reaction at 298 K. Given the heat capacity data:

| Species | $C_p$ (J/(mol·K)) |
|---------|-------------------|
| N₂(g) | 27.0 + 5.8 × 10⁻³ T |
| H₂(g) | 27.3 + 3.3 × 10⁻³ T |
| NH₃(g) | 29.8 + 25.5 × 10⁻³ T |

Calculate the heat of reaction at **800 K** using Kirchhoff's Law.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A gas mixture containing **40% CO and 60% N₂** (by volume) is burned with **100% excess air**. The reactants enter at **298 K** and the combustion products leave at **800 K**. Calculate the heat transferred per kmol of fuel mixture. Given:

- $\Delta H^\circ_f, CO = -110.53$ kJ/mol; $\Delta H^\circ_f, CO_2 = -393.52$ kJ/mol
- $C_p, CO_2 = 26.75$ J/(mol·K); $C_p, N_2 = 28.90$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_p, O_2 = 29.37$ J/(mol·K); $C_p, CO = 28.46$ J/(mol·K)

---

**Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1: Calculate ΔH°₂₉₈**

$$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = 2 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f, NH_3 - [1 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f, N_2 + 3 \cdot \Delta H^\circ_f, H_2]$$

Since N₂ and H₂ are elements in reference form:

$$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = 2 \times (-46.11) - 0 = -92.22 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2: Calculate ΔCp**

$$\Delta a = 2(29.8) - [1(27.0) + 3(27.3)] = 59.6 - 108.9 = -49.3 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\Delta b \times 10^3 = 2(25.5) - [1(5.8) + 3(3.3)] = 51.0 - 15.7 = 35.3$$

So $\Delta b = 35.3 \times 10^{-3}$ J/(mol·K²)

**Step 3: Apply Kirchhoff's Integral**

$$\Delta H_T = \Delta H^\circ_{298} + \Delta a(T - 298) + \frac{\Delta b}{2}(T^2 - 298^2)$$

At T = 800 K:

$$\Delta a(T - 298) = -49.3 \times (800 - 298) = -49.3 \times 502 = -24,748.6 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\frac{\Delta b}{2}(T^2 - 298^2) = \frac{35.3 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \times (640,000 - 88,804)$$

$$= 0.01765 \times 551,196 = 9728.6 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 4: Sum**

$$\Delta H_{800} = -92,220 + (-24,748.6) + 9728.6 = -107,240 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{800} = -107.24 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

---

**Solution to Question A (b):**

**Step 1: Stoichiometry**

Reaction: $CO + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$

Basis: 1 kmol of fuel mixture (0.4 kmol CO + 0.6 kmol N₂)

**O₂ required (theoretical):** $0.4 \times 0.5 = 0.2$ kmol O₂

**O₂ supplied (100% excess):** $0.2 \times 2 = 0.4$ kmol O₂

**N₂ from air:** $0.4 \times 3.76 = 1.504$ kmol N₂

**Step 2: Heat of reaction at 298 K**

$$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = \Delta H^\circ_f, CO_2 - \Delta H^\circ_f, CO = -393.52 - (-110.53) = -282.99 \text{ kJ/mol CO}$$

Total: $0.4 \times (-282.99) = -113.20$ kJ per kmol fuel

**Step 3: Sensible heat of products at 800 K**

Products: 0.4 kmol CO₂ + (0.6 + 1.504) = 2.104 kmol N₂

**Step 4: Sensible heat of reactants at 298 K = 0** (reference)

**Step 5: Energy balance**

$$Q = \Delta H^\circ_{298} + \int_{298}^{800} C_{p,\text{products}} dT$$

Assuming constant $C_p$ (approximate):

$$Q = -113,200 + [0.4(26.75) + 2.104(28.90)] \times (800 - 298)$$

$$= -113,200 + [10.70 + 60.81] \times 502$$

$$= -113,200 + 71.51 \times 502 = -113,200 + 35,898 = -77,302 \text{ kJ/kmol fuel}$$

$$\boxed{Q = -77,302 \text{ kJ per kmol of fuel mixture (heat released)}}$$

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Hess's Law** with a suitable example. How is it used to calculate the standard heat of formation of a compound that cannot be directly synthesized from its elements?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the standard heat of reaction for the following process at 25 °C:

$$C_2H_4(g) + H_2O(g) \rightarrow C_2H_5OH(g)$$

Given the standard heats of combustion:
- ΔH°c, C₂H₄(g) = -1411.0 kJ/mol
- ΔH°c, H₂(g) = -285.8 kJ/mol
- ΔH°c, C₂H₅OH(g) = -1409.0 kJ/mol

Also calculate ΔH at 500 K if:
- $C_p, C_2H_4 = 11.84 + 0.1197T$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_p, H_2O = 30.00 + 10.7 \times 10^{-3}T$ J/(mol·K)
- $C_p, C_2H_5OH = 14.55 + 0.1301T$ J/(mol·K)

---

**Solution to Question B (a):**

**Hess's Law:** Enthalpy is a state function; therefore, the total enthalpy change for a reaction is independent of the path taken.

**Application to Calculate ΔH°f of CO:**

Direct formation is difficult because C + ½O₂ → CO is hard to stop at CO (some CO₂ forms).

**Step 1:** $C(s) + O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g), \quad \Delta H^\circ_1 = -393.52$ kJ

**Step 2:** $CO(g) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow CO_2(g), \quad \Delta H^\circ_2 = -282.99$ kJ

**Required Reaction:** $C(s) + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2(g) \rightarrow CO(g)$

By Hess's Law: Step 1 = Required + Step 2

$$\Delta H^\circ_f, CO = \Delta H^\circ_1 - \Delta H^\circ_2 = -393.52 - (-282.99) = -110.53 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

---

**Solution to Question B (b):**

**Step 1: Standard heat of reaction using combustion data**

$$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = [\Delta H^\circ_c, C_2H_4 + \Delta H^\circ_c, H_2] - [\Delta H^\circ_c, C_2H_5OH]$$

Note: For combustion of C₂H₅OH to give H₂O(g) is needed; but combustion of H₂ gives H₂O(l). If we assume H₂O(l) basis and combustion data uses H₂O(l):

$$\Delta H^\circ_{298} = [(-1411.0) + (-285.8)] - [(-1409.0)]$$

$$= -1696.8 + 1409.0 = -287.8 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 2: Apply Kirchhoff's Law for T = 500 K**

$$\Delta a = 14.55 - [11.84 + 30.00] = 14.55 - 41.84 = -27.29 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

$$\Delta b \times 10^3 = 130.1 - [119.7 + 10.7] = 130.1 - 130.4 = -0.3$$

So $\Delta b = -0.3 \times 10^{-3}$ J/(mol·K²)

**Step 3: Integrate**

$$\Delta H_{500} = \Delta H^\circ_{298} + \Delta a(500 - 298) + \frac{\Delta b}{2}(500^2 - 298^2)$$

$$\Delta a(500 - 298) = -27.29 \times 202 = -5512.6 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\frac{\Delta b}{2}(500^2 - 298^2) = \frac{-0.3 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \times (250,000 - 88,804)$$

$$= -0.15 \times 10^{-3} \times 161,196 = -24.18 \text{ J/mol}$$

**Step 4: Sum**

$$\Delta H_{500} = -287,800 - 5,512.6 - 24.18 = -293,336.8 \text{ J/mol}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{500} = -293.34 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Δa, Δb, Δc, Δd:** Students frequently subtract products from reactants instead of reactants from products. **Always remember:** $\Delta = \text{Products} - \text{Reactants}$.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Multiply by Stoichiometric Coefficients:** The $\nu_i$ factor is non-negotiable. For $2NH_3$, the coefficient 2 must multiply both $\Delta H^\circ_f$ and the $C_p$ values.
>
> 3. **Units Inconsistency:** $C_p$ is often given in cal/(mol·K) but $\Delta H$ in kJ/mol. **Convert** all to consistent units before calculation.
>
> 4. **Wrong Reference State for Elements:** $C_p$(graphite) ≠ $C_p$(diamond). Always use the **reference form** (graphite, not diamond).
>
> 5. **Neglecting the Kirchhoff Correction:** A common mistake is to report $\Delta H^\circ_{298}$ as the answer when the question asks for $\Delta H$ at a different temperature. **Always re-read the question.**
>
> 6. **Arithmetic Slip in Polynomial Integration:** Forgetting to divide by 2, 3, or 4 for the $T^2$, $T^3$, $T^4$ terms respectively.
>
> 7. **Confusing Heat of Formation and Heat of Reaction:** Formation is for **1 mole** of compound from elements; reaction uses stoichiometric quantities of any species.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Standard State:** Always **298 K and 1 atm** unless specified otherwise.
- **Reference State Elements:** Have **ΔH°f = 0** in their most stable form.
- **ΔH°f from combustion:** $\Delta H^\circ_f = \sum \nu_j \Delta H^\circ_{c,\text{reactants}} - \sum \nu_i \Delta H^\circ_{c,\text{products}}$ (note the sign reversal vs. reaction from formations).
- **ΔH°rxn from formation:** $\Delta H^\circ = \sum \nu_i \Delta H^\circ_{f,\text{products}} - \sum \nu_j \Delta H^\circ_{f,\text{reactants}}$.
- **Kirchhoff Integral:** $\Delta H_T = \Delta H^\circ_{298} + \int_{298}^{T} \Delta C_p \, dT$.
- **Polynomial Integration Coefficients:** $T^1$→1, $T^2$→½, $T^3$→⅓, $T^4$→¼.
- **Sign Convention:** Exothermic $\Delta H < 0$; Endothermic $\Delta H > 0$.
- **State Function:** Enthalpy depends only on state — this is the **philosophical basis** of Hess's Law.
- **Path Independence:** Direct and indirect routes (via 298 K) must give identical $\Delta H$.
- **Units Watch:** kJ vs. J, cal vs. J, mol vs. kmol — **always normalize first**.
- **Mean Cp shortcut:** Useful for quick calculations but less accurate for large ΔT.
- **Phase Change Caution:** Cp correlations are valid only within a single phase. If a phase change occurs between 298 K and T, **additional latent heat terms** must be included.
- **Exothermic Reaction at High T:** Heat of reaction becomes **less negative** (magnitude decreases) for exothermic reactions as T increases (when $\Delta C_p > 0$).
- **KTU Numerical Format:** Always show (i) Δa, Δb, Δc, Δd computation, (ii) substituted integral, (iii) final answer with units — **partial marks are awarded at each step**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adiabatic Reaction Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHT205 - PROCESS CALCULATIONS (CHEMICAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adiabatic Reaction Temperature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Adiabatic Reaction Temperature (ART)** is defined as the temperature attained by the reaction mixture when a chemical reaction is carried out under perfectly adiabatic conditions (i.e., no heat is exchanged with the surroundings, **Q = 0**), starting from a specified initial temperature and pressure. For an **exothermic reaction**, this corresponds to the **maximum possible temperature** the system can reach, since all the heat liberated by the reaction is retained within the reacting mass and manifests as a rise in sensible (thermal) energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Distinction:**
> - **Adiabatic Flame Temperature (AFT)**: Specifically used when the reacting system is a **combustion reaction** (fuel + oxidizer).
> - **Adiabatic Reaction Temperature (ART)**: A broader, more general term applicable to **any exothermic chemical reaction** (including but not limited to combustion, neutralization, oxidation, sulfonation, etc.).
> 
> In the KTU PCCHT205 syllabus (Module 4), both terms are treated under the same mathematical framework, with AFT being a *special case* of ART.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine a sealed, perfectly insulated steel pressure vessel (a thermos flask) containing a reactive chemical mixture at room temperature. Once the reaction is initiated, the vessel walls do **not** allow any heat to escape to the atmosphere, nor does any heat enter from outside. As the molecules react and release chemical potential energy in the form of heat, this energy has nowhere to go but to warm up the contents of the vessel itself.

Think of it like this: A person running on a treadmill in a closed, unventilated room. The body generates heat (analogous to the exothermic reaction), and since the room is sealed, the heat accumulates, causing the room temperature to rise (analogous to the rise in adiabatic reaction temperature). The final room temperature is the "Adiabatic Reaction Temperature."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Intuition:**
> - The reaction releases energy (ΔH_rxn < 0 for exothermic).
> - The walls trap all the energy (Adiabatic ⇒ Q = 0).
> - The trapped energy increases the **sensible heat** (molecular vibration, translation, rotation) of the products and any unreacted species.
> - This continues until **chemical equilibrium** is established OR the reactants are completely consumed (whichever comes first).

### 1.3 Significance in Chemical Engineering

The Adiabatic Reaction Temperature is a **critical design and safety parameter** because:

1. **Reactor Material Selection**: If the calculated ART exceeds the maximum allowable service temperature of the construction material (e.g., carbon steel loses strength above **~425 °C**, stainless steel 304 up to **~870 °C**), the reactor design is unsafe.
2. **Runaway Reaction Hazard**: It defines the **worst-case temperature scenario** for exothermic processes, vital for designing pressure relief systems and emergency venting.
3. **Product Selectivity**: Many reactions have temperature-sensitive selectivity (e.g., in catalytic oxidations, higher temperatures favor complete combustion to CO₂ over partial oxidation to useful products).
4. **No External Heat Exchanger Required**: It represents the theoretical maximum temperature of an autothermal process.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Reference Temperature (T_ref)**: **298.15 K (25 °C)** for tabulated standard heats of formation.
> - **Standard Reference Pressure (P_ref)**: **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** for ideal-gas calculations.
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R)**: **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
> - **Units of Heat of Reaction**: **kJ/mol** of reaction (as written) or **kJ/kmol** in SI.
> - **Units of Heat Capacity (Cp)**: **J/(mol·K)** or **kJ/(kmol·K)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The First Law Applied to a Reactor

The general steady-state energy balance for an open reactor system is:

$$\sum_{in} F_i H_i - \sum_{out} F_j H_j + Q - W_s = 0$$

For an **adiabatic** process:
- **Q = 0** (no heat transfer through reactor walls)
- **W_s = 0** (no shaft work, e.g., no agitator doing work on the system — for a simple stirred tank or plug flow reactor)

Therefore, the energy balance simplifies to:

$$\sum_{in} F_i H_i = \sum_{out} F_j H_j$$

This is the **enthalpy balance** governing the adiabatic reactor.

### 2.2 Key Logical Steps for Calculating ART

The calculation procedure follows a strict, logical sequence:

**Step 1: Establish the Reaction Stoichiometry**
- Write the balanced chemical equation.
- Define the **extent of reaction (ξ)**.
- For a **single-pass, single-reaction** scenario, the conversion (X) determines ξ.

**Step 2: Define Reference States**
- Select a **reference temperature** T_ref (usually **298.15 K**) where ΔH°_rxn is known.
- Select a **reference pressure** for ideal-gas calculations.

**Step 3: Choose Heat Capacity Model**
- **Model A (Constant Cp)**: Simple average Cp values.
- **Model B (Temperature-Dependent Cp)**: Use empirical correlations of the form:
$$C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$$
in **J/(mol·K)** or **kJ/(kmol·K)**.

**Step 4: Apply the Energy Balance**
The general energy balance in terms of heat of reaction is:

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{ref}) = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} \sum_{i} n_i \, C_{p,i}(T) \, dT}$$

Where:
- **Left side**: Heat released by reaction at the reference temperature.
- **Right side**: Sensible heat gained by all species (products + unreacted reactants) as temperature rises from T_ref to T_ad.

**Step 5: Solve for T_ad (Adiabatic Reaction Temperature)**
- For **constant Cp**: A linear equation in T_ad → direct algebraic solution.
- For **variable Cp**: A polynomial equation → numerical/iterative solution (e.g., Newton-Raphson, bisection, or graphical method).

### 2.3 Special Case: Constant Heat Capacities

When Cp values are assumed constant over the temperature range of interest, the integral becomes trivial:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{ref}) = \left[ \sum_{i} n_i \, C_{p,i} \right] \cdot (T_{ad} - T_{ref})$$

Solving for T_ad directly:

$$\boxed{T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{ref})}{\sum_{i} n_i \, C_{p,i}}}$$

Or equivalently, the **adiabatic temperature rise** (ΔT_ad):

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{ad} = T_{ad} - T_{ref} = \frac{-\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{ref})}{\sum_{i} n_i \, C_{p,i}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Reminder:**
> - For **exothermic reactions**: ΔH_rxn < 0, so **−ΔH_rxn > 0** → T_ad > T_ref (temperature rises).
> - For **endothermic reactions**: ΔH_rxn > 0, so **−ΔH_rxn < 0** → T_ad < T_ref (temperature drops; here T_ad is the *minimum* temperature, often called the *adiabatic reaction temperature* in the general sense).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Application / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adiabatic Energy Balance | $\sum F_i H_i \big\vert_{in} = \sum F_j H_j \big\vert_{out}$ | Q = 0, W_s = 0 |
| 2 | General ART Equation | $\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{ref}) = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} \sum n_i C_{p,i}(T) \, dT$ | All single-reaction adiabatic systems |
| 3 | ART with Constant Cp | $T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{ref})}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | Cp nearly constant over ΔT range |
| 4 | Temperature-Dependent Cp (Polynomial) | $C_p = a + bT + cT^2 + dT^3$ | Used in integration; units: J/(mol·K) |
| 5 | Mean Heat Capacity | $\bar{C}_p = \dfrac{\int_{T_1}^{T_2} C_p(T) \, dT}{T_2 - T_1}$ | Approximation when Cp varies mildly |
| 6 | Hess's Law (Heat of Reaction) | $\Delta H_{rxn} = \sum \nu_i \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \nu_i \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$ | Used to compute ΔH_rxn at T_ref |
| 7 | Kirchhoff's Law | $\Delta H_{rxn}(T_2) = \Delta H_{rxn}(T_1) + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \Delta C_p \, dT$ | Adjusts ΔH_rxn from one T to another |
| 8 | AFT for Combustion | $T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{-\Delta H_c^\circ}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | Special case: ΔH = standard heat of combustion |
| 9 | Partial Conversion ART | $T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn} \cdot \xi}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | ξ = extent of reaction (mol reacted) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Furnace & Boiler Design**: The AFT sets the upper limit of flame/gas temperature, used to size heat-transfer surfaces and predict NOₓ formation (which is highly T-dependent).
- **Catalytic Converter Design**: The exothermic oxidation of CO and unburnt hydrocarbons can cause extreme local temperature spikes; ART calculations ensure substrate integrity.
- **Polymerization Reactors**: Highly exothermic polymerizations (e.g., polyethylene, PVC) require ART predictions for emergency relief sizing (DIERS methodology).
- **Acid-Base Neutralization**: Predicts the temperature rise from H₂SO₄ + NaOH reactions, critical in process safety audits.
- **Rocket Propulsion**: The theoretical performance ceiling of a propellant is determined by the AFT of the combustion gases.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Adiabatic Reaction Temperature Equation

**Starting Point — Steady-State Open System Energy Balance (no shaft work):**

$$Q - W_s = \sum_{out} F_j H_j - \sum_{in} F_i H_i$$

**Step 1: Apply the Adiabatic Condition**

For a perfectly insulated reactor, no heat crosses the boundary. Therefore:

$$Q = 0$$

The energy balance becomes:

$$0 = \sum_{out} F_j H_j - \sum_{in} F_i H_i$$

Or equivalently:

$$\sum_{in} F_i H_i = \sum_{out} F_j H_j \quad \text{(Total Enthalpy In = Total Enthalpy Out)}$$

**Step 2: Decompose Total Enthalpy into a "Formation + Sensible" Basis**

The absolute enthalpy of any species *i* at temperature T can be expressed relative to its elements at **298.15 K**:

$$H_i(T) = \Delta H_f^\circ(i) + \int_{298.15}^{T} C_{p,i}(T) \, dT$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the Energy Balance**

Let us consider a single reaction: A + bB → cC + dD, with feed of F_A0 mol/s of A and stoichiometric amount bF_A0 of B, at inlet temperature T_in. We assume complete conversion.

- **Inlet Stream (T = T_in):**
$$\sum_{in} F_i H_i = F_{A0} H_A(T_{in}) + b F_{A0} H_B(T_{in})$$

- **Outlet Stream (T = T_ad):**
$$\sum_{out} F_j H_j = c F_{A0} H_C(T_{ad}) + d F_{A0} H_D(T_{ad})$$

**Step 4: Equate and Rearrange**

$$F_{A0} H_A(T_{in}) + b F_{A0} H_B(T_{in}) = c F_{A0} H_C(T_{ad}) + d F_{A0} H_D(T_{ad})$$

Dividing by F_A0 (1 mol basis of limiting reactant):

$$H_A(T_{in}) + b H_B(T_{in}) = c H_C(T_{ad}) + d H_D(T_{ad})$$

**Step 5: Introduce the Heat of Reaction Concept**

Recall the **standard heat of reaction at 298.15 K**:

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = \left[ c \Delta H_f^\circ(C) + d \Delta H_f^\circ(D) \right] - \left[ \Delta H_f^\circ(A) + b \Delta H_f^\circ(B) \right]$$

And the **sensible enthalpy** of each species relative to 298.15 K:

$$H_i(T) - \Delta H_f^\circ(i) = \int_{298.15}^{T} C_{p,i}(T) \, dT$$

**Step 6: Algebraic Manipulation**

Subtracting the formation enthalpies from both sides of Step 4:

$$\begin{aligned}
& \left[ c \Delta H_f^\circ(C) + d \Delta H_f^\circ(D) \right] - \left[ \Delta H_f^\circ(A) + b \Delta H_f^\circ(B) \right] \\
+\;& c \int_{298.15}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,C} \, dT + d \int_{298.15}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,D} \, dT \\
-\;& \int_{298.15}^{T_{in}} C_{p,A} \, dT - b \int_{298.15}^{T_{in}} C_{p,B} \, dT = 0
\end{aligned}$$

The first line is, by definition, ΔH°_rxn(298.15 K). The remaining integrals can be split into two pieces: one from 298.15 K to T_in (accounting for the inlet not being at 298.15 K) and one from T_in to T_ad (the temperature rise). This yields, after rearrangement:

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) + \sum_{\text{all species}} n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,i}(T) \, dT - \sum_{\text{all species, feed}} n_i \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{in}} C_{p,i}(T) \, dT = 0}$$

If the feed enters at T_ref (298.15 K), the last integral vanishes, giving the **clean canonical form**:

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) + \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} \left[ \sum_{\text{products}} n_i C_{p,i} - \sum_{\text{reactants, conv}} n_i C_{p,i} \right] dT = 0}$$

Or, equivalently, the working formula most often used in KTU problems:

$$\boxed{\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = -\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} \sum_{\text{all species at outlet}} n_i C_{p,i}(T) \, dT}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Constant Cp Approximation

**Problem Statement:**
Carbon monoxide is burned with 100% excess air (so that combustion is complete) in an adiabatic furnace. The reactants enter at **298.15 K**. Calculate the adiabatic flame temperature. Data:
- CO + ½O₂ → CO₂, ΔH°_rxn(298) = **−283.0 kJ/mol** (standard heat of combustion of CO)
- Cp (J/(mol·K)): O₂ = 29.37, N₂ = 29.12, CO₂ = 37.22

**Step 1: Basis and Feed Composition**
Take **1 mol of CO** as the basis. Stoichiometric O₂ required = 0.5 mol. With 100% excess air, actual O₂ fed = 1.0 mol.
N₂ from air: (79/21) × 1.0 = **3.762 mol**.

**Step 2: Outlet Composition (after complete combustion)**
- CO₂ produced: **1.0 mol**
- O₂ unreacted: **0.5 mol**
- N₂: **3.762 mol**

**Step 3: Energy Balance (Constant Cp)**

$$T_{ad} = 298.15 + \frac{-(-283{,}000)}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum n_i C_{p,i} &= (1)(37.22) + (0.5)(29.37) + (3.762)(29.12) \\
&= 37.22 + 14.685 + 109.549 \\
&= 161.454 \; J/K
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute T_ad**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{ad} &= 298.15 + \frac{283{,}000}{161.454} \\
&= 298.15 + 1752.8 \\
&= \mathbf{2050.9 \; K \; (\approx 1778 \; °C)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (~2050 K) is significantly higher than the *theoretical* AFT (~2380 K) for CO with stoichiometric air. The excess air acts as a thermal ballast, absorbing heat and lowering the flame temperature. This is a classic result in combustion engineering.

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Temperature-Dependent Cp

**Problem Statement:**
Sulfur is burned in a furnace: **S(l) + O₂(g) → SO₂(g)**. Feed: 1 mol S, stoichiometric O₂, both at **298.15 K**. ΔH°_rxn(298) = **−296.8 kJ/mol**. Cp data (J/(mol·K)):
- SO₂: $C_p = 43.45 + 10.62 \times 10^{-3} T - 5.94 \times 10^{5} T^{-2}$
- O₂: $C_p = 29.37 + 6.21 \times 10^{-3} T - 1.81 \times 10^{5} T^{-2}$

**Step 1: Identify Species at Outlet**
1 mol SO₂. (Sulfur is consumed; no unreacted O₂ because stoichiometric feed.)

**Step 2: Apply the General Equation**

$$\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ(298) = -\int_{298.15}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,SO_2}(T) \, dT$$

Note: We do **not** include Cp of O₂ at the outlet because there is no unreacted O₂ (stoichiometric feed). We also do not include Cp of S at the inlet because the inlet is already at the reference temperature 298.15 K.

**Step 3: Perform the Integration**

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{298.15}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,SO_2} \, dT &= 43.45(T_{ad} - 298.15) + \frac{10.62 \times 10^{-3}}{2}(T_{ad}^2 - 298.15^2) \\
&+ 5.94 \times 10^{5} \left(\frac{1}{T_{ad}} - \frac{1}{298.15}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Set Up the Equation**

$$-296{,}800 = -\left[ 43.45(T_{ad} - 298.15) + 5.31 \times 10^{-3}(T_{ad}^2 - 88{,}893) + 5.94 \times 10^{5}\left(\frac{1}{T_{ad}} - 0.003354\right) \right]$$

**Step 5: Solve Iteratively (Trial-and-Error / Newton-Raphson)**

*Initial Guess:* Using the constant-Cp approximation: $T_{ad} \approx 298 + \frac{296{,}800}{43.45} = 298 + 6831 = 7129 \; K$ (clearly too high; Cp varies strongly, so this overestimates T).

*Iteration 1*: Try **T_ad = 1900 K**
- Linear term: 43.45 × (1900 − 298.15) = 43.45 × 1601.85 = 69,600.4 J
- Quadratic term: 5.31 × 10⁻³ × (1900² − 88,893) = 5.31 × 10⁻³ × (3,610,000 − 88,893) = 5.31 × 10⁻³ × 3,521,107 = 18,697.1 J
- Inverse term: 5.94 × 10⁵ × (1/1900 − 1/298.15) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (5.263 × 10⁻⁴ − 3.354 × 10⁻³) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (−2.828 × 10⁻³) = −1,679.8 J
- **Total** = 69,600.4 + 18,697.1 − 1,679.8 = **86,617.7 J ≈ 86.6 kJ**

The required heat release is 296.8 kJ. We need a *higher* T.

*Iteration 2*: Try **T_ad = 2200 K**
- Linear term: 43.45 × 1901.85 = 82,635.4 J
- Quadratic term: 5.31 × 10⁻³ × (4,840,000 − 88,893) = 5.31 × 10⁻³ × 4,751,107 = 25,228.6 J
- Inverse term: 5.94 × 10⁵ × (1/2200 − 1/298.15) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (4.545 × 10⁻⁴ − 3.354 × 10⁻³) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (−2.900 × 10⁻³) = −1,722.6 J
- **Total** = 82,635.4 + 25,228.6 − 1,722.6 = **106,141.4 J ≈ 106.1 kJ**

Still less than 296.8 kJ. Need even higher T.

*Iteration 3*: Try **T_ad = 2500 K**
- Linear term: 43.45 × 2201.85 = 95,670.4 J
- Quadratic term: 5.31 × 10⁻³ × (6,250,000 − 88,893) = 5.31 × 10⁻³ × 6,161,107 = 32,715.5 J
- Inverse term: 5.94 × 10⁵ × (1/2500 − 1/298.15) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (4.000 × 10⁻⁴ − 3.354 × 10⁻³) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (−2.954 × 10⁻³) = −1,754.7 J
- **Total** = 95,670.4 + 32,715.5 − 1,754.7 = **126,631.2 J ≈ 126.6 kJ**

Still too low. Let us try a much higher temperature.

*Iteration 4*: Try **T_ad = 5000 K**
- Linear term: 43.45 × 4701.85 = 204,295.4 J
- Quadratic term: 5.31 × 10⁻³ × (25,000,000 − 88,893) = 5.31 × 10⁻³ × 24,911,107 = 132,278.0 J
- Inverse term: 5.94 × 10⁵ × (1/5000 − 1/298.15) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (2.000 × 10⁻⁴ − 3.354 × 10⁻³) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (−3.154 × 10⁻³) = −1,873.5 J
- **Total** = 204,295.4 + 132,278.0 − 1,873.5 = **334,699.9 J ≈ 334.7 kJ**

Now too high. Let us interpolate between 2500 K and 5000 K.

*Linear Interpolation:*
At 2500 K → 126.6 kJ (need 296.8 kJ)
At 5000 K → 334.7 kJ (need 296.8 kJ)

$$T_{ad} \approx 2500 + (5000 - 2500) \times \frac{296.8 - 126.6}{334.7 - 126.6} = 2500 + 2500 \times \frac{170.2}{208.1} = 2500 + 2500 \times 0.8179 = 2500 + 2045 = 4545 \; K$$

*Iteration 5*: Try **T_ad = 4500 K**
- Linear term: 43.45 × 4201.85 = 182,571.4 J
- Quadratic term: 5.31 × 10⁻³ × (20,250,000 − 88,893) = 5.31 × 10⁻³ × 20,161,107 = 107,055.5 J
- Inverse term: 5.94 × 10⁵ × (1/4500 − 1/298.15) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (2.222 × 10⁻⁴ − 3.354 × 10⁻³) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (−3.132 × 10⁻³) = −1,860.4 J
- **Total** = 182,571.4 + 107,055.5 − 1,860.4 = **287,766.5 J ≈ 287.8 kJ**

Very close to 296.8 kJ. One more iteration.

*Iteration 6*: Try **T_ad = 4600 K**
- Linear term: 43.45 × 4301.85 = 186,915.4 J
- Quadratic term: 5.31 × 10⁻³ × (21,160,000 − 88,893) = 5.31 × 10⁻³ × 21,071,107 = 111,887.5 J
- Inverse term: 5.94 × 10⁵ × (1/4600 − 1/298.15) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (2.174 × 10⁻⁴ − 3.354 × 10⁻³) = 5.94 × 10⁵ × (−3.137 × 10⁻³) = −1,863.4 J
- **Total** = 186,915.4 + 111,887.5 − 1,863.4 = **296,939.5 J ≈ 296.9 kJ** ✓

**T_ad ≈ 4600 K** for adiabatic combustion of sulfur in pure oxygen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This extremely high AFT (~4600 K) is why sulfur is used in specialized high-temperature flame applications. In practice, dissociation of SO₂ to SO + ½O₂ becomes significant above ~3000 K, which would *lower* the real adiabatic flame temperature. KTU problems often *dissociation corrections* for advanced treatment.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Approaches

| Aspect | Constant Cp Method | Variable Cp Method | Effect of Dissociation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Math Form** | Linear equation in T_ad | Polynomial / transcendental equation | Modifies both ΔH_rxn and Cp values |
| **Solution Technique** | Direct algebraic | Iterative (Newton-Raphson, bisection) | Iterative, often requires equilibrium constants |
| **Accuracy** | Low (±20% error for ΔT > 500 K) | High (±1–2%) | Highest; essential for T_ad > 2500 K |
| **Use Case** | Quick estimation, design scoping | Detailed engineering design | Research, high-temperature combustion |
| **Typical KTU Marks** | 2–3 marks (Part A) | 5–7 marks (Part B) | 7–14 marks (advanced Part B) |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Adiabatic Reactor System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed Stream at T_in, P_in] --> B[Mixing / Preheating Section]
    B --> C[Reactor Inlet Condition Tracker]
    D[Heat of Reaction Data Bank<br/>Delta H at T_ref 298.15 K] --> E[Energy Balance Solver]
    F[Heat Capacity Correlation Library<br/>Cp = a + bT + cT squared + dT cubed] --> E
    C --> E
    E --> G{Convergence Check}
    G -->|Residual greater than tolerance| H[Update T_ad estimate]
    H --> E
    G -->|Residual less than tolerance| I[Final Adiabatic Reaction Temperature T_ad]
    I --> J[Safety and Material Validation Module]
    J --> K{Is T_ad within material limit?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Design Approved]
    K -->|No| M[Trigger Relief Sizing and Material Upgrade]
    M --> N[Reactor Design Output]
    L --> N
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: Computational Path for ART Determination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Define Reaction<br/>Stoichiometry and Extent] --> P2[Step 2: Fix Reference State<br/>T equals 298.15 K, P equals 1 atm]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Collect Thermo Data<br/>Delta H of formation, Cp coefficients]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Compute Delta H_rxn at T_ref<br/>Using Hess Law]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Identify Outlet Stream<br/>Composition after Reaction]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Set Up Energy Balance<br/>Q equals 0 equation]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Choose Cp Model<br/>Constant or Variable]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Integrate and Solve<br/>For T_ad]
    P8 --> P9[Step 9: Validate Against<br/>Equilibrium and Dissociation]
    P9 --> P10[Step 10: Report T_ad and<br/>Uncertainty Bounds]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Energy Transformation in an Adiabatic Reactor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT STREAM]
        A1[Chemical Potential Energy<br/>of Reactants at T_in]
    end
    subgraph TRANSFORM[REACTOR - ADIABATIC WALLS]
        B1[Reaction Occurs<br/>Exothermic: Delta H less than 0]
        B2[All Heat Trapped<br/>No Heat Loss Q equals 0]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STREAM]
        C1[Sensible Heat of<br/>Products at T_ad]
        C2[Chemical Potential Energy<br/>of Remaining Reactants]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 -->|Liberates Heat| B2
    B2 -->|Raises Internal Energy| C1
    B1 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The schematic above visually conveys that the chemical potential energy of the input stream is partially converted to sensible thermal energy of the output stream. The energy is **conserved** (First Law), but its **form** changes from chemical to thermal.

### 4.4 Effect of Excess Air on Adiabatic Flame Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Excess Air Percentage] --> Y1[AFT with 0 percent excess<br/>Highest T_ad]
    X --> Y2[AFT with 50 percent excess<br/>Moderate T_ad]
    X --> Y3[AFT with 100 percent excess<br/>Lower T_ad]
    X --> Y4[AFT with 200 percent excess<br/>Significantly Lower T_ad]
    Y1 --> Z[Heat absorbed by unreacted N2 and O2<br/>acts as thermal ballast]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
    Y4 --> Z
    Z --> OUTPUT[Lower Final T_ad as excess air increases]
```

This diagram captures an important engineering observation: increasing the proportion of inert gas (like excess air or nitrogen) **decreases** the adiabatic flame temperature because the same amount of heat release is distributed over a larger sensible heat capacity.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: Conceptual Definition**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**
*Define the term "Adiabatic Reaction Temperature." Under what condition does it represent the maximum possible temperature of the system?*

**Model Answer:**
Adiabatic Reaction Temperature (ART) is the temperature attained by a reacting mixture when the reaction proceeds under perfectly adiabatic conditions, i.e., with no heat exchange with the surroundings (Q = 0). All the heat released by the reaction is absorbed as sensible heat by the products (and any unreacted reactants), causing the temperature of the system to rise.

It represents the **maximum possible temperature** when the reaction is **exothermic** (ΔH_rxn < 0), the system is perfectly insulated, and the reaction proceeds to **complete conversion** (or to chemical equilibrium, whichever limit is reached first).

---

**Question 2: Conceptual Reasoning**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
*Why does the addition of excess air in a combustion furnace lower the adiabatic flame temperature?*

**Model Answer:**
The excess air introduces additional amounts of unreacted oxygen and nitrogen into the combustion products. These inert (or unreacted) species do not participate in the reaction but must be heated from the inlet temperature to the final adiabatic flame temperature. Since the total heat released by the combustion reaction remains essentially fixed (it depends only on the fuel quantity and its heating value), this same quantity of heat is now distributed over a larger mass of gas. With a larger effective heat capacity in the denominator of the adiabatic temperature rise equation (ΔT_ad = −ΔH_rxn / ΣnᵢCₚ,ᵢ), the resulting temperature rise is smaller, lowering the AFT.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4 Comprehensive]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Pure methane (CH₄) is burned completely with **20% excess air** in an adiabatic combustor. Both the fuel and air enter at **298.15 K**. Using the constant-Cp approximation, calculate the **adiabatic flame temperature** of the combustion products.

**Given Data:**
- Reaction: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O
- ΔH°_combustion(CH₄, 298 K) = **−802.3 kJ/mol**
- Cp values (J/(mol·K)): CO₂ = 37.22, H₂O(g) = 33.58, O₂ = 29.37, N₂ = 29.12

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1: Establish Feed Composition (Basis: 1 mol CH₄)**
- Stoichiometric O₂ = 2 mol
- Actual O₂ fed (20% excess) = 2 × 1.20 = **2.4 mol**
- N₂ from air = 2.4 × (79/21) = 2.4 × 3.762 = **9.029 mol**

**Step 2: Outlet Composition (Complete Combustion)**
- CO₂ = 1.0 mol
- H₂O = 2.0 mol
- O₂ (unreacted) = 2.4 − 2.0 = 0.4 mol
- N₂ = 9.029 mol

**Step 3: Compute Total Heat Capacity of Products**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum n_i C_{p,i} &= (1)(37.22) + (2)(33.58) + (0.4)(29.37) + (9.029)(29.12) \\
&= 37.22 + 67.16 + 11.748 + 262.92 \\
&= 379.05 \; J/K
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply Energy Balance**

$$T_{ad} = 298.15 + \frac{-(-802{,}300)}{379.05} = 298.15 + 2116.6 = \mathbf{2414.8 \; K}$$

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the combustor is now operated with **100% excess air** (instead of 20%) while keeping all other conditions identical, calculate the new AFT. Comment briefly on the result.

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1: New Feed Composition (Basis: 1 mol CH₄)**
- Actual O₂ fed = 2 × 2.0 = **4.0 mol**
- N₂ = 4.0 × 3.762 = **15.048 mol**

**Step 2: New Outlet Composition**
- CO₂ = 1.0 mol
- H₂O = 2.0 mol
- O₂ (unreacted) = 4.0 − 2.0 = 2.0 mol
- N₂ = 15.048 mol

**Step 3: New Total Heat Capacity**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum n_i C_{p,i} &= (1)(37.22) + (2)(33.58) + (2.0)(29.37) + (15.048)(29.12) \\
&= 37.22 + 67.16 + 58.74 + 438.20 \\
&= 601.32 \; J/K
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Compute New T_ad**

$$T_{ad} = 298.15 + \frac{802{,}300}{601.32} = 298.15 + 1334.3 = \mathbf{1632.4 \; K}$$

**Step 5: Comment**
The AFT dropped from 2414.8 K (20% excess) to 1632.4 K (100% excess), a decrease of ~782 K. This confirms that **excess air acts as a thermal ballast** and is often deliberately used in industrial furnaces to control peak flame temperature, prevent refractory damage, and reduce thermal NOₓ formation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Hydrogen gas (H₂) is burned with **stoichiometric oxygen** in an adiabatic reactor. Reactants enter at **298.15 K**. Using the constant-Cp approximation, determine the adiabatic flame temperature. Use the following data:
- Reaction: H₂ + ½O₂ → H₂O(g)
- ΔH°_rxn(298) = **−241.8 kJ/mol**
- Cp values (J/(mol·K)): H₂O(g) = 33.58, O₂ = 29.37

**Model Solution (Part a):**

**Step 1: Feed (Basis: 1 mol H₂)**
Stoichiometric O₂ = 0.5 mol. No inert gases.

**Step 2: Outlet Composition**
H₂O(g) = 1.0 mol. No unreacted species (stoichiometric).

**Step 3: Energy Balance**

$$T_{ad} = 298.15 + \frac{241{,}800}{(1)(33.58)} = 298.15 + 7200.7 = \mathbf{7498.9 \; K}$$

(Note: This is the *constant-Cp* estimate. The real AFT is much lower, ~2810 K, because Cp rises sharply with T and H₂O dissociates above ~2000 K. KTU expects students to recognize this limitation.)

---

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain **why the actual adiabatic flame temperature of H₂/O₂ combustion is significantly lower** than the value calculated using the constant-Cp method. Discuss **two specific physical reasons**.

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Reason 1: Heat Capacity Increases with Temperature**
The constant-Cp method assumes a fixed heat capacity over the entire temperature range (e.g., 33.58 J/(mol·K) for H₂O). In reality, Cp for water vapor increases substantially with temperature (e.g., at 5000 K, Cp(H₂O) > 50 J/(mol·K)). Since a larger Cp in the denominator means a smaller temperature rise for the same heat release, the real AFT is lower.

**Reason 2: Thermal Dissociation of Products**
At the extreme temperatures calculated (over 7000 K), water vapor is not thermodynamically stable. It dissociates back into H₂, O₂, and OH radicals via endothermic reactions such as:
- H₂O → H₂ + ½O₂ (endothermic)
- H₂O → H + OH
- ½O₂ → O (endothermic)

These dissociation reactions are endothermic, so they **absorb a portion of the heat of combustion**, leaving less energy available to raise the temperature. The result is a real AFT closer to ~2810 K — a reduction of more than 60% from the constant-Cp estimate.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in ART Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error on ΔH_rxn**: The most frequent error. Remember, for an *exothermic* reaction, ΔH_rxn is **negative** (−283 kJ/mol for CO combustion). When plugging into T_ad = T_ref − ΔH_rxn / ΣnᵢCₚ,ᵢ, students often forget the **negative sign in front of ΔH_rxn** and obtain T_ad < T_ref (a nonsensical result for combustion).
> 
> 2. **Forgetting Excess Air N₂**: Many students account for excess O₂ but **forget to include N₂** from the air in the outlet heat capacity. Since N₂ typically constitutes ~71% of the molar feed in air, this error can shift the calculated T_ad by hundreds of degrees.
> 
> 3. **Using Stoichiometric O₂ in Outlet Cp**: When the problem says "100% excess air," some students still treat the O₂ as if it were all consumed. Always compute **unreacted O₂** = (excess fraction) × (stoichiometric O₂) and include it in the outlet stream.
> 
> 4. **Mixing Units**: Cp in kJ/(kmol·K) and ΔH in kJ/mol will give an answer off by a factor of 1000. **Double-check units** before submitting.
> 
> 5. **Not Stating the Assumption**: If you use the constant-Cp method, you **must explicitly write** "assuming Cp is constant over the temperature range." Examiners award marks for stating the assumption.
> 
> 6. **Confusing AFT and ART**: In Module 4, AFT is a special case (combustion) of the more general ART concept. Using the term "AFT" in a non-combustion problem will cost you partial credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Adiabatic Reaction Temperature:**
> 
> - **Core Definition (Q = 0, complete conversion, exothermic case)** — must be stated precisely.
> - **Difference between ART and AFT** — AFT is reserved for combustion reactions.
> - **General Energy Balance Formula**: $\Delta H_{rxn}(T_{ref}) = -\int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{ad}} \sum n_i C_{p,i}(T) \, dT$
> - **Constant-Cp Working Formula**: $T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$
> - **Hess's Law** to compute ΔH_rxn from heats of formation.
> - **Kirchhoff's Law** to adjust ΔH_rxn to a different reference temperature if needed.
> - **Identify all species in the outlet stream** — products + unreacted reactants (especially excess air O₂ and N₂).
> - **Use consistent units**: kJ and kmol OR J and mol. Never mix.
> - **Excess air decreases AFT** because it acts as a thermal ballast (larger effective Cp).
> - **Preheated reactants increase AFT** because sensible heat of the inlet adds to the reaction heat.
> - **For variable Cp**, the integral yields a polynomial — solve by Newton-Raphson or graphical method.
> - **Dissociation limits the AFT** — the real AFT is lower than the constant-Cp estimate for T > 2500 K.
> - **Practical industrial AFTs** are typically limited to ~1500–2300 K to protect refractories.
> - **Material of construction** must withstand the calculated ART; stainless steel (310/321) is rated for ~870–1100 °C continuous service.
> - **Pressure relief design** for exothermic reactors (DIERS methodology) is based on adiabatic temperature rise calculations.
> - **Conversions to remember**: K = °C + 273.15; 1 atm = 101.325 kPa; R = 8.314 J/(mol·K).
> - **Iteration technique**: when variable Cp is used, start with constant-Cp estimate, then refine.
